question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 42110/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reason why she informed the Ulster Farmers Union that she was delaying work on the A5 Western Transport Corridor until 2027, whatever the outcome of the appeal; and (ii) why a statement was not made MLAs first.
answer_text: I remain absolutely committed to the A5 scheme. It was my predecessor and party colleague John O’Dowd who made the decision to proceed with the scheme, and I am appealing the judgement and doing all I can to ensure that this scheme goes ahead and saves lives.This timescale recognises the duration of the legal proceedings and the further governance and contractual steps that would be required before construction could properly commence in the event of a successful outcome. It is also intended to provide landowners with greater clarity and reassurance during this period of uncertainty and a notification letter has been issued.I recognise the importance of keeping Members informed of significant matters, and I appreciate the strong interest in the A5 scheme. In this instance, the matter relates to an operational issue affecting a number of identified landowners, and it was appropriate that those directly impacted were informed at the earliest opportunity. I remain committed to engagement with the Assembly and will continue to provide updates as matters progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration her Department has taken of the comments made by the Ulster Farmers Union, that the proposed firearms fee increase is not justified for farm businesses.
answer_text: Firearms Certificates are held for various reasons such as pest control, recreational purposes and by those who wish to hold firearms as part of a collection.  While mindful of the impact on those who rely on firearms for legitimate purposes including agricultural and pest control activities, the licensing fee is the same for all applicants, and each applicant must be assessed to be a “fit” person and have good reason for each firearm or ammunition holding applied for.  Firearms fees were last increased in Northern Ireland in 2016 and are now significantly less than licensing fees in neighbouring jurisdictions. The current fees do not reflect the cost of delivering the firearms licensing service by the PSNI. There is a clear challenge in ensuring that the service is adequately resourced to meet demand and most importantly, maintain public safety.The Department’s consultation on proposals to amend firearms licensing fees and the “banded system” is open to 29th May 2026.  I encourage members of the public, and any representative organisation, to share their views on the social, economic, environmental or rural impacts. The consultation responses will play an important role in developing the Department’s final proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he raised an exemption in immigration permissions for fishing crew rights in Isle of Man waters at his recent meeting with their Ministers, including their response.
answer_text: I met with Chris Thomas, Isle of Man Minister for The Treasury and Clare Barber, Isle of Man Minister for Environment, Food and Agriculture on Wednesday 15th April 2026 to discuss Isle of Man immigration rules affecting NI fishing vessels access to Manx waters. The purpose of this meeting was, inter alia, to seek arrangements that would allow our vessels continued operation in Manx waters with overseas crew.Discussions were constructive and helpfully provided more information on the immigration rules in relation to Isle of Man waters. I suggested potential exemptions for overseas fishing crew on NI vessels from requiring a work permit. It was explained that exemptions from requiring an Isle of Man work permit have arisen in the past due to recognised skills shortages and specific purposes for the economy of the Island. The Isle of Man Government outlined the significant resource implications involved in monitoring an exemption of this kind and that this would need to be first agreed by the Home Office. Currently available routes of access by the NI fleet were identified, including the ability of vessel owners to register as a foreign business, which would enable overseas crew to apply for an Isle of Man Worker Migrant visa.  Officials have briefed the industry on the outcome of the meeting and industry are considering the potential applicability of the Isle of Man Worker Migrant visa as a means of addressing crewing challenges within the NI fleet. It was agreed that we would keep lines of engagement open with the Isle of Man Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the cyber attack on C2K on 2 April 2026.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) has confirmed that an individual was arrested on 15 April in relation to the cyber-attack on the C2K system. The Education Authority (EA) continues to assist the PSNI in their enquiries.In respect of operational impact, the cyber-attack is contained and all users of the C2k system (circa 440,000) have been supported to change their passwords and the system is fully operational.  The EA continues to work with Capita and National Cyber Centre, and additional security measures have been implemented. Further to the ongoing investigation, it was identified that a small number of schools have had some of their records accessed.  EA has written to the affected schools to notify them of this and is working closely with each school to provide ongoing support and assess the impact of the attack. At this stage, these assessments indicate that no further action is required in the vast majority of cases, as the information involved is of minimal consequence, for example material already in the public domain.  In a very small number of cases where personal data has been compromised, the EA will contact parents/carers directly with further information, advice and support as required. The EA will continue to take all appropriate steps in response to the incident, including continued engagement with the PSNI and the Information Commissioner’s Office.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has made any assessment of the Ulster Farmer's Union's call for support for farmers facing rising fuel, energy and fertiliser costs.
answer_text: I understand the challenges that farmers are facing, and I share the concerns of the Ulster Farmer’s Union regarding rising fuel prices, energy and fertiliser costs. These market developments are outside the control of the NI Executive and whilst, at this stage, a support package for farm businesses affected by the current energy crisis and increase in fuel and fertiliser costs is not planned,  I am continuing to monitor developments, assess the local impacts, and liaise with my NI Executive colleagues and the UK Government.The implementation of any future financial support package would be dependent on the allocation of additional funds to my Department.Existing support is available within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) schemes, which provide a degree of stability and help support resilience in the local agriculture sector. The Farm Sustainability Payment, as part of the SAP, is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and help withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively and encouraging businesses to become more environmentally sustainable and efficient.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any sharing of best practice that has taken place between her Department and local councils over the last three years, regarding achieving the statutory processing targets for planning applications and those that don’t meet the target, to try and improve performance.
answer_text: The sharing of best practice is a core component of the planning improvement programme and plays a key role in supporting continuous improvement in performance against statutory processing targets for planning applications, including where targets are not being met.  A central theme within this programme has been an ongoing, structured engagement and collaboration between my Department and local government over the past 3 years. This regular engagement focuses on development management and local development plan processes and provides a consistent forum to examine the operational challenges affecting processing times, understand the issues impacting performance, and share practical approaches that have proven effective in improving performance across local planning authorities.Best practice has also been shared through regular engagement with key stakeholders, for example, through the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum, in wider forums such as the Planning Round Table Event last year, and ongoing engagement with other jurisdictions.  These forums and engagements have provided opportunities for all key stakeholders to share learning from different perspectives, discuss operational challenges, and exchange examples of good practice and effective approaches, with the shared aim of improving planning performance.It is worth noting there was a significant improvement in the average processing time for major applications in 2024/25, which saw the lowest processing times since the transfer of planning powers in 2015. This is in addition to improving statutory consultee response times with the latest figures for Q2 2025/26 showing that 79% were responded to on time.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any changes to the Fisheries and Water Environment Bill he plans to make following the consultation on the Bill.
answer_text: The Department has taken into the consideration the issues raised during the public consultation in respect of the Aquaculture policy proposals. The Bill will now include amendments to Fish Culture Licences (FCL) to achieve the policy intent but the requirement to hold a FCL will not be removed. This licence will be renamed as an Aquaculture licence.The Department intends to define Aquaculture clearly within the Bill, to allow all non-fish/shellfish aquaculture operations, e.g. seaweed, to be licensed, thereby supporting future development of these industry sub-sectors.  Clear provisions for transfer of licences will also be included. These amendments continue to align with the policy intent, ensuring a transparent licensing system for aquaculture, which supports the sustainable development of all aquaculture sub-sectors in NI, and will allay concerns that have been raised through the consultation by some regarding the removal of the FCL.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to increase capacity at Knockavoe School in Strabane.
answer_text: In April 2024 I established the SEN Capital Investment Programme (CIP) and asked the Education Authority (EA) to assess the accommodation needs across all special schools.  The EA published the Special Schools Plans of Action on 16 September 2025.  These plans identify the short, medium and long-term strategies for all special schools; the medium and long-term strategy for Knockavoe School is the development of a new school building/second campus for up to 110 pupils.  I have set out to the Executive the £1.7 billion investment required over the next decade to modernise the special school estate. This level of funding cannot be met from my department’s capital budget, and Executive agreement to treat the SEN CIP as a strategic, crossgovernment priority is essential. While the need for urgent SEN capital investment is widely recognised, the Executive has not yet had a full discussion on my proposals.The expansion of Knockavoe School is a priority project in the SEN CIP. However, without Executive support and a confirmed longterm funding commitment, I am unable to progress the next phase of the SEN CIP.

answer_text: There are a range of cardiac diagnostics and procedures that are provided within the Western Health and Social Care Trust, such as cardiac MRI, cardiac CT, coronary angiogram, etc.  According to the Department of Health’s Official Statistics for diagnostic services for the quarter ending December 2025, the Western Trust does not hold the longest waiting times for cardiac diagnostics (see link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/diagnostic-waiting-times).With regard to procedures undertaken in the cardiac catheterisation laboratory, while waiting times in the Western Trust are outside of expected timeframes, the most recent inpatient and day-case waiting times statistics confirm that the Western Trust does not hold the longest waiting in this category (see link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/inpatient-waiting-times).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to the latest NI Composite Economy Index, to detail (i) any reasons behind the 0.8 per cent decreased activity in the Services sector; and (ii) any mitigations her Department plan to put into place to support the Services sector.
answer_text: The NI Composite Economic Index increased by 0.2% over the latest quarter. The main contributions to growth came from increased activity in the Construction sector (0.6pps) which was offset by decreased activity in the Services sector (0.8 pps). When examined in isolation, the services sector weakened in Quarter 4 2025, with the Index of Services falling by 1.5%. This decline was broadbased across the three main sectors. Wholesale & retail, motor vehicle repair, and accommodation & food services fell by 0.6%, while Business services & finance recorded a sharper contraction of 5.1%. Transport, storage, and information & communication also declined, falling by 3.4%. The only sub-sector that reported growth was Other Services, which grew by 5.5% over Q4 2025. Despite this quarterly decline, the Index of Services still posted annual growth of 0.6% and is now 10.3% above its pre-pandemic level seen in Q4 2019.Supporting the Services sector remains a priority for my Department given its central role in employment and output across the North. Since my predecessor, Conor Murphy, announced a new Economic Vision in February 2024, significant progress has been made against the four key priorities of: Good Jobs, Productivity, Regional Balance, and Decarbonisation all of which are relevant to service-based industries. I intend to sustain and build on the North’s economic performance through a continued sharp focus on the delivery of these priorities for the remainder of this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the Health Inequalities Annual Report 2026, to detail any (i) reasons why inequalities regarding female life expectancy have widened; (ii) reasons why the female healthy life expectancy decreased; (iii) actions his Department is taking to reverse these trends.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the Health Inequalities Annual Report 2026, to detail (i) the reasons why large inequality gaps continue for mental health indicators; and (ii) any actions his Department is taking to reverse these trends.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the Health Inequalities Annual Report 2026, to detail the reasons why the rate of dental extractions in the most deprived areas was more than double that in the least deprived areas.
answer_text: The two most common reasons for dental extractions are because of dental decay and / or periodontal (gum) disease.  Oral health across the United Kingdom, including Northern Ireland, has shown marked overall improvement over decades, but there are persistent oral health inequalities that follow socioeconomic deprivation.  While this published data does not include teeth extracted by dentists under private arrangements, rates of dental decay among both children and adults are highest in the most deprived areas of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the reason why there is a significant gender gap in favour of men regarding those accessing Legal Aid; and (ii) any action her Department is taking to narrow that gap.
answer_text: The latest Official Statistics published by the Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland in September 2025, detail the 2024/25 legal aid grant rate for males and females. When disaggregated by type of legal aid, statistics show that civil legal aid grant rates were similar for males and females – at 10.3 and 10.5 grants per 1,000 population (respectively). However, in criminal cases, males were over four times more likely than females to be recipients of legal aid, displaying grant rates of 35.6 per 1,000 population compared to 8.1 per 1,000 population for females.This difference in criminal legal aid uptake is reflective of trends across Northern Ireland criminal justice organisations – for example, Public Prosecution Service Official Statistics that show males are statistically more likely to be involved in criminal proceedings that result in subsequent grants of criminal legal aid.In Northern Ireland, civil legal aid is granted by the Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland under a statutory framework designed to ensure access to justice for individuals who are deemed eligible through a combination of means testing and merits assessment. In contrast, criminal legal aid in Northern Ireland is granted by the judiciary if the individual has insufficient means to fund their own defence and if it desirable in the interests of justice to do so. In both of these scenarios, the sex of any applicant is irrelevant to the adjudication

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any assessment that has been made on whether the budget for social farms is sufficient; and (ii) any actions his Department is taking to support social farms.
answer_text: My Department supports social farms through the Social Farming Capital Grant Scheme. Since its inception in 2016, the scheme has distributed over £350,000 to successful applicants. The budget available has been sufficient to meet the needs of the applicant farms and the scheme has not been over-subscribed.My Department also provides ongoing financial support to Rural Support to deliver the Social Farming Support Service (SFSS) which supports farmers delivering Social Farming and promoting the service for those who could benefit from participation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the date on which (i) she received the Planning Appeals Commission report into Planning Application LA11/2018/0186/O; and (ii) she will publish the report and her response to it.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to increase the numbers of pupils cycling safely to school.
answer_text: My Department recognises behavioural change work as a key element to maximising the benefits of increased investment in active travel infrastructure. To this end, we work in partnership with schools on the delivery of a number of road safety and active travel programmes, such as Child Pedestrian Road Safety Training, Junior Road Safety Officer Training, and the Cycling Proficiency Scheme.Additionally, my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) jointly fund the Active School Travel programme which is delivered by the Walk Wheel Cycle Trust (formerly Sustrans). Schools apply to join the programme for an initial three-year period with the aim of encouraging children to walk, wheel or cycle to school. Sixty new schools joined the Programme for the 2025-26 school year, and work is already underway to agree the next cohort of schools to join the programme in 2026-27, with the deadline for applications having recently just passed.In addition, I hope to finalise the Active Travel Delivery Plan later this year which prioritises routes to key destinations, including schools, and seeks to complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan in the provision of improved active travel infrastructure within our towns and cities.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the timetable for the introduction of the Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill.
answer_text: The Bill was introduced on Monday 16th March 2026. The second reading is provisionally scheduled to take place on Monday 20th April 2026, after which it will be referred to the Committee for Finance. I would expect it to complete its passage in the Assembly during 2026.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any priority system that the NI Environment Agency have agreed with the 11 local councils in relation to planning application responses.
answer_text: Since 28 February 2025, NIEA’s Natural Environment Division has implemented a Prioritisation Pilot with local councils through the councils’ Heads of Planning Group. This pilot enables councils to collectively consider cases which merit prioritisation or are deemed exceptional, with the NIEA accepting five cases at a time. As of 24 March 2026, there have been 80 requests for prioritisation with 2 cases outstanding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment been made on the impact of the Electronic Travel Authorisation scheme on trips from overseas visitors, travelling from the Republic of Ireland to the north.
answer_text: Our all-island economy is vital, and seamless all-island travel is key to building on the growth we have seen in our tourism sector. I have engaged with the British Government to highlight the challenges and negative impacts which the introduction of the Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) scheme presents to the visitor economy here. Officials from my Department are currently engaged with Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) to compile evidence to the extent of this impact. Preliminary work including stakeholder engagement and comparative policy analysis is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 42410/22-27, to detail a timescale for consideration and action as a result of this report.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Executive’s Online Safety Strategy, cross-departmental implementation group (CDIG) is due to consider the report by Ulster University on ‘Understanding Risk, Barriers and Facilitators to Reporting Technology-Assisted Child Sexual Abuse’ which was commissioned by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) and discuss what actions are required, at its next meeting on 20 April 2026.The group, of which the Department of Justice is a member, is chaired by the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he had in the United States of America, promoting Strabane native John Dunlap.
answer_text: The 250th anniversary provides an important opportunity to strengthen links between Northern Ireland and the United States, while highlighting our shared history and contribution.During my recent visit to Washington DC and Savannah, Georgia I ensured that the legacy of John Dunlap from Strabane was recognised during my engagements and speeches.I was pleased to launch a new publication “Ulster American Treasures” at a joint reception with the America250 Commission, attended by senior representatives from across political, business, tourism, and cultural sectors. The publication produced by the Public Record Office of Northern Ireland (PRONI) features a selection of reprinted original documents from PRONI’s collections and includes a letter from John Dunlap to his family back in Strabane.John Dunlap will continue to feature prominently in our USA–NI 250 programme including a PRONI exhibition (Autumn 2026) in Titanic Quarter, Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any updated guidance issued to parents from the Public Health Agency regarding meningitis.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) is leading the operational response to meningitis, working closely with the Department of Health.  The PHA is closely linked into the UKHSA response to the outbreak in Kent, attending their Incident Management Team meetings, and is keeping the situation in Northern Ireland under close review.  To date there is no evidence of spread beyond the South East of England.The PHA has been working closely with a school in Belfast that had a suspected case which proved to be negative, providing advice to parents and staff.  The Agency also met with the Department of Education, Education Authority and the two NI universities to provide advice and public health information.  The public have been urged to remain vigilant for the signs and symptoms of meningitis and to act fast if they suspect they may be symptomatic.  Public health information is available on the PHA website at: PHA statement - meningitis | HSC Public Health Agency and on NI Direct at: Meningitis | nidirect

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of the labour market statistics, published on 19 March 2026, which indicate that Northern Ireland's labour market continues to lose momentum; and (ii) any action she plans to take to reverse this loss of momentum.
answer_text: The latest statistics from NISRA show our labour market continuing to show resilience as we face an increasingly uncertain economic situation as a result of the conflict in the Middle East. I was encouraged to see median monthly pay increasing by 8.2% over the year, more than in any region in Britain.  Furthermore, the number of payrolled employees in the North increased by 1.4% over the year - the only region to see an annual increase in payrolled employees.  The Quarterly Employment Survey also reported a new series high with employee jobs increasing over the quarter and over the year in all sectors.Although some indicators weakened over the year, none of these changes were statistically significant, meaning they may reflect normal variation rather than a genuine shift in underlying trends.Through my Economic Vision I am seeking to develop the right context for businesses and workers to thrive.  I intend to sustain and build on the North’s recent positive economic performance through a continued sharp focus on the delivery of my Economic Vision priorities for the remainder of this mandate. We are pursuing a once in a generation reform to employment rights through the development of our Employment Rights Bill. We have made significant and targeted investments in addressing priority skills gaps through the implementation of the Skills Action Plan and Skills Fund. We have made more progress on the longstanding commitment to the expansion of university provision in Magee in the last two years than in the previous ten. And we have clear plans in place to grow priority sectors, raise our productivity, and deliver better regional balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has made any assessment of correspondence from the Commissioner for Older People in NI to MLAs, regarding the exclusion of older people from the protected characteristics in the hate crime element of the Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc.) Bill.
answer_text: I am due to meet the Commissioner for Older People NI in April to discuss the omission of hate crime protections for older people in the Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc.) Bill.The Department provided a detailed response to the Justice Committee on 4 February outlining the current policy development undertaken by officials and details of engagement the Department has carried out with reference to age as a protected characteristic.In summary, Judge Marrinan’s recommendation on the inclusion of age (to include all ages) as a protected characteristic is still under consideration with further policy work needed in this area. A final decision on whether to include age as a protected characteristic will be informed by findings from the Phase 2 Consultation which will be progressed in the next mandate. In considering the need for legislative protections for older persons who are victims of crime, one of the risks that the Department needs to consider in future policy development for this recommendation is the risk of duplication of legislative provisions.Sentencing guidance provisions already require the courts to take into consideration the vulnerability of older people (victims) when sentencing offenders. In addition, two key provisions are due to be passed by the end of this mandate. These are:The introduction of a vulnerability aggravator in the Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc.) BillOffences in the forthcoming Department of Health [DoH] Adult Protection BillI remain committed to delivering the best outcomes for all victims of hate crime in this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41289/22-27, whether any active travel stakeholders were consulted before her Department determined its interpretation of overall transport budgets and active travel spend, under Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Equality Act 2010 does not apply in the north. The equality process in the north is provided for via Section 75 of the NI Act (1998).I recognise the importance of stakeholder engagement and my Department has accepted the recommendations of the NIAO report related to the establishment of a Stakeholder Engagement Forum for Active Travel. Invitations to sit on the Forum have been issued to key stakeholders and I expect the first meeting to be held in the coming weeks.The Forum will provide an opportunity for regular engagement on a range of issues that affect the delivery of active travel in the north, and will provide a structured, inclusive platform for ongoing collaboration between the relevant officials and stakeholders. It will be co-designed to reflect the diversity of the active travel community and will ensure that a wide range of stakeholder voices are represented and heard. The comprehensive assessment of overall spend on active travel referred to in AQW 41289/22-27 was not subject to separate formal consultation. It was necessary to undertake the exercise in order to track progress towards meeting a legal obligation, and the outcome reflects my Department’s considered interpretation of the Act. Going forward, officials will gather views from the Stakeholder Engagement Forum should material changes to financial reporting methodologies arise. These views will help inform future reporting, while ensuring the approach remain aligned with financial and legal requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of recommendations contained in the October 2024 report Enabling Safe Quality Midwifery Services and Care, including (i) the setting up of the Maternity and Neonatal Partnership; and (ii) the setting up of an Oversight Group.
answer_text: My Department established a Maternity and Neonatal Services Safety Oversight Group in December 2023 to receive assurance on the safety of maternity and neonatal services. The Group has been overseeing a broad programme of work to improve maternity and neonatal care - this includes the independent review of midwifery services by Professor Renfrew, Enabling Safe Quality Midwifery Services and Care in Northern Ireland, which was published on 22 October 2024. Other areas include a review of governance and safety arrangements within maternity services by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority, as well as a gap analysis of other relevant professional guidance, audits and reports. Given the large volume of recommendations which collectively shape this important programme of work, a regional action plan is being developed.Following the publication of Professor Renfrew’s report, the Department wrote to Trusts to outline the critical priorities contained in the report and the immediate actions the Trusts needed to take - there have been no immediate major safety concerns identified.With regards to the other recommendations falling out of the wider programme of work overseen by the Oversight Group, while there is clear evidence of commitment and progress across all Trusts, it is evident that some areas will require further action.  I announced plans to establish a Maternal and Newborn Partnership to provide leadership and lead improvement across maternity and neonatal services. Whilst the current funding position has placed constraints on progress, plans are now in progress to establish the Partnership, with recruitment to key positions in the coming months.

answer_text: The Equality Act 2010 does not apply in the north. The equality process in the north is provided for via Section 75 of the NI Act (1998).I recognise the importance of stakeholder engagement and my Department has accepted the recommendations of the NIAO report related to the establishment of a Stakeholder Engagement Forum for Active Travel. Invitations to sit on the Forum have been issued to key stakeholders and I expect the first meeting to be held in the coming weeks.The Forum will provide an opportunity for regular engagement on a range of issues that affect the delivery of active travel in the north, and will provide a structured, inclusive platform for ongoing collaboration between the relevant officials and stakeholders. It will be co-designed to reflect the diversity of the active travel community and will ensure that a wide range of stakeholder voices are represented and heard. The comprehensive assessment of overall spend on active travel referred to in AQW 41289/22-27 was not subject to separate formal consultation. It was necessary to undertake the exercise in order to track progress towards meeting a legal obligation, and the outcome reflects my Department’s considered interpretation of the Act. Going forward, officials will gather views from the Stakeholder Engagement Forum should material changes to financial reporting methodologies arise. These views will help inform future reporting, while ensuring the approach remain aligned with financial and legal requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 41644/22-27, whether the increase in court actions is due to (i) a change in guidance issued by his Department; and (ii) a change in enforcement policy by Land and Property Services.
answer_text: Money collected from rates is used to fund frontline public services such as healthcare and education.The increase in Magistrates’ Court process notices during these years was not the result of any change in guidance or enforcement policy. The increase in volume reflects Land & Property Services’ (LPS) focus on strengthening rate collection where rates remained unpaid despite repeated reminders, prompts and offers of support. LPS is committed to offering support to ratepayers who need help to pay, however, where rates are not paid, legal recovery action will be progressed against those who won’t pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail Translink's plans for new bus purchases, for each of the next three years.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering a business case that will determine future bus procurement. I am therefore not able to provide further information at this stage.

answer_text: My Department does not hold this information. Records of the number of wildfires is held by the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of his Department's arm's-length bodies he plans to (i) remove; and (ii) create, during the 2022- 27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: (i) I have no plans to remove any existing Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs) during this mandate; however, the performance of all my Department’s ALBs is kept under regular review through a range of governance and oversight mechanisms.(ii) The outcome of my Department’s public consultation demonstrated an unequivocal call for improved support for controlled schools and provided a clear mandate for my proposal to establish a dedicated statutory body to manage and support the controlled sector.  The Executive has very recently agreed that my officials can engage with the Office of the Legislative Council to draft the necessary legislation to create a new body which, when established, will subsume the Controlled Schools Support Council (CSSC).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients (i) not admitted due to availability of beds; (ii) placed on trollies; and (iii) discharged due to a lack of beds, in each of the last twelve months, broken down by acute hospital.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of potholes that have been (i) reported; and (ii) repaired, in West Tyrone, in each month of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.  Defects are categorised using a range of options and a priority is set based on the severity of safety risk to the public.This information is published and can be viewed using the link: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/surface-defects. The information can be sorted by year, by section office, and to show if the defect has been instructed for repair and completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of suicides in West Tyrone, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  Deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision).  Data on the numbers of deaths from suicide from 2015 to 2024, broken down by parliamentary constituency (including West Tyrone) can be found in Table 9 of the publication ‘NI Suicide Statistics 2004-2024’. There are some situations where the registration of the death can be delayed, specifically where the death has been accidental, unexpected, or suspicious, and must be referred to the Coroners Service, only to be registered after completion of the investigation. It should be noted that suicides registered during a particular time will therefore not be reflective of suicides that occurred in that period.In 2019 NISRA and the Coroners Service commenced a review of suicide statistics following the identification of a classification issue in published statistics which concluded in 2022. This marked a major revision to this statistical series with a break in the time series being introduced from 2015. Comparisons with data prior to 2015 should therefore be made with caution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on capital build projects in West Tyrone.
answer_text: The current position of the Major Capital Programme projects is as detailed below:St Catherine’s Primary School, Strabane, is in the early design stages. Drumragh Integrated College has design work completed, however requires additional funding to move to the contractor procurement and construction stage. Dean Maguirc College, Carrickmore, was paused in 2023 and remains so.The current position of the School Enhancement Programme projects is as detailed below:St Mary’s Primary School, Cloughcor, is at the early design stage.  The proposed project is for an extension to the main school building, the refurbishment of the existing main school building and site works to provide hard play areas, traffic management and car parking facilities. Land and Property Services are negotiating a second plot of land to facilitate the project.Strabane Primary School, is in the Business Case development stage.  The proposed project is for the refurbishment of the existing building and a two- storey extension.In addition, construction began on site for the Strule Shared Education Campus in early February 2025. Full opening of the Campus is planned for September 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to extend the use of Temporary Exemption Certificates, beyond 2026-27.
answer_text: On 12 March 2026, I announced an extension of Temporary Exemption Certificates (TECs) for a further year to help ensure MOT waiting times remain manageable during 2026-27, until the new test centres at Hydebank and Mallusk become operational, which is expected within the next year.The use of TECs will remain under review, and an assessment on any further extension will be provided in early 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has made any assessment of the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland and the Ulster University study report, Understanding Risk, Barriers and Facilitators to Reporting Technology Assisted Child Sexual Abuse.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the recent report by the Ulster University which was commissioned by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland entitled ‘Understanding Risk, Barriers and Facilitators to Reporting TechnologyAssisted Child Sexual Abuse’.  The report will be considered by the crossdepartmental Online Safety Strategy Group, which is chaired by the Department of Health and on which my Department is represented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current reporting and monitoring processes used regarding restrictive interventions in schools.
answer_text: Restrictive interventions are interventions that limit a person’s movement, activity or liberty. Restrictive interventions in educational settings can sometimes involve the use of reasonable force. The use of reasonable force is a specific and legally defined subset of restrictive intervention permitted in certain circumstances under Article 4(1) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998. The use of reasonable force within education settings is specifically defined under Article 4 (1) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 and can be summarised as being such force as is reasonable in the circumstances for the purpose of preventing injury, damage, committing an offence, or serious disruption. The Department’s current arrangements for the reporting and monitoring of the use of reasonable force are set out in existing guidance. Circular 1999/09 advises that schools should have a policy on the use of reasonable force and that any incidents should be recorded.Circular 2021/13, the Interim Guidance on the Use of Restraint and Seclusion, outlines the Department’s expectations and recommended good practice specifically in relation to the use of restraint and seclusion in educational settings, and specifies that all instances of the use of reasonable force/restraint should be recorded, parents/carers should be informed and follow up support provided to the pupil and staff involved.At present, therefore, reporting and monitoring of the use of reasonable force, restraint and seclusion occurs at school level.As part of its wider review, the Department has committed to defining the types of restrictive interventions which are used in our educational settings, and introducing a framework for the recording, reporting and monitoring of certain restrictive interventions (to include incidents of serious physical restraint, the use of mechanical restraint outside an agreed plan, and all instances of seclusion) through forthcoming guidance. The Department is currently consulting on this draft guidance, having launched a public consultation on the draft Restrictive Interventions: Guidance for Educational Settings. The consultation runs from 12 March to 4 June 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's targets of by how much it estimates the new Graduated Driver Licensing scheme will reduce accidents.
answer_text: GDL is  one of a number of strategic interventions I have set out in in the Road Safety Strategy Action Plan to 2027.  It will be the combined effect of such planned measures along with existing measures that will help us achieve the target of reducing the number of young people killed or seriously injured in road collisions by 60% by 2030.  However, I believe that GDL has the potential to deliver significant road safety benefits.   The report ‘Potential Impact of Graduated Driver Licensing in Northern Ireland’ produced by Transport Research Laboratory (TRL) for DVA in 2019/20, considered international evaluations and found that while introducing GDL can reduce collisions generally, effectiveness varies across jurisdictions ranging between 5% to 40%, depending on factors such as the baseline licensing system and the strength of GDL measures.In terms of collisions involving young people and fatalities, research carried out by the TRL show GDL systems reduce collisions involving young people and fatalities by 19–21%.The report considered the greatest potential impact of introducing GDL to lie in the core learner and probationary components, particularly night-time and passenger restrictions. It concluded that GDL offers substantial potential to improve road safety and that strong probationary restrictions, supported by complementary measures, could deliver casualty reductions in line with international best practice. The report can be read in full at https://www.trl.co.uk/uploads/trl/documents/PPR934---The-potential-impact-of-Graduated-Driver-Licensing-in-Northern-Ireland.pdf    The GDL Monitoring Report, which is published annually, provides a statistical baseline so that when GDL is introduced its impact and effectiveness can be monitored and assessed.  This includes collision and casualty statistics. The most recent report for 2024 can be found at  https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/system/files/2026-03/graduated-driver-licensing-gdl-monitoring-report-2024.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the timescale for providing households a £30 per year reduction in electricity bills, as part of the UK Government scheme.
answer_text: The Treasury has confirmed it will provide funding for the North, ensuring that domestic electricity customers here receive comparable benefit in respect of their contribution to the Northern Ireland Renewables Obligation (NIRO).  Domestic electricity consumers here will receive around £30 reduction per year on their electricity bills, over three years.My officials worked to secure agreement from the British government for it to  amend the Energy Prices Act 2022 and extend powers to the North as the fastest way to deliver this support. The necessary Statutory Instrument was laid in Westminster on 16th March and I expect the new legislation to be in place before the summer recess. As soon as this legislation is passed my Department will be ready to ensure local electricity consumers benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the initial budget for the Residents’ Parking Zones scheme; and (ii) the estimated number of schemes that budget will support.
answer_text: I recently set out to the Assembly how my Department will assess and deliver future residents’ parking schemes across the North through a demand-led application process, which I intend to launch in April 2026. As the process is consensus-based, securing sufficient resident buy-in for any schemes proposed will influence how many schemes are within my Department’s scope to take forward. In line with this approach, funding will be allocated as and when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any schemes supported through Northern Ireland Screen's creative education and careers pipeline, in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department’s investment in Northern Ireland Screen supports numerous creative education, digital skills and screen careers initiatives for teachers, youth leaders and young people across Northern Ireland at all stages of Primary and Post Primary education as well as community and youth settings. Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 42197/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timetable for (i) responding to; and (ii) implementing, the recommendations contained within the Independent Review of Assessment report.
answer_text: On 5 March, I welcomed the publication of the Independent Review of Assessment Report which I commissioned in June 2025 as part of my TransformED strategy. The report delivers on my commitment to fundamentally review assessment arrangements in Northern Ireland. It provides a strong evidence base to develop an assessment system for Northern Ireland that sets high standards and supports progress for every child. I intend to publish my Department’s response to the report and proposals for implementation in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the recommendations in the March 2026 Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman follow-up report Healthcare Waiting List Communications.
answer_text: My Department continues to assess the progress made following the March 2026 publication of the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman (NIPSO) follow-up report, Forgotten. While the majority of recommendations have been assessed as either met or partially met, my Department will continue to monitor compliance through ongoing audits of the Health and Social Care Trusts.  This will ensure regional consistency and confirm that appropriate processes are in place. Due to existing system limitations, some recommendations cannot be fully met at this time.  However, my Department will continue to monitor system capabilities and work towards improving levels of compliance wherever possible.My Department remains committed to working closely with the Health and Social Care Trusts to continually improve communication methods with all patients, ensuring that they have the best possible experience and access to care.The Encompass system is now fully operational across Northern Ireland, allowing patients and service users to securely access a subset of their Health and Social Care record through the My Care application, providing secure online access to appointment letters, medication details, and some test results. Patients can also view appointments and track the progress of their treatment, therefore enhancing overall communication with patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) number of; and (ii) nature of, adverse drug reaction reports submitted from Northern Ireland to the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency related to GLP-1 medication, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of and nature of suspected adverse drug reactions reports submitted from Northern Ireland to the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust is not information held by my department.It is the role of the MHRA as the UK’s regulator for medicines to monitor the safety, quality and efficacy of medicines through a comprehensive pharmacovigilance strategy. An important part of this strategy is the MHRA’s Yellow Card scheme which collects and investigates reports of suspected adverse drug reactions, enabling the MHRA to identify potential safety issues promptly and take any necessary actions to protect public health.The Department recognises the importance of reporting to the Yellow Card scheme to help to improve the safety of medicines and medical devices for all patients. In support of this, in 2023 the Department worked with the MHRA and the Belfast HSC Trust to launch the regional Yellow Card Centre for Northern Ireland which works to raise awareness of reporting adverse drug reactions. The Yellow Card Centre in Northern Ireland has published two annual reports for 2023/24 and 2024/25 since its launch, both available at https://belfasttrust.hscni.net/services/yellow-card-centre-northern-ireland/It is important to stress that a reported reaction does not necessarily mean it has been caused by the medicine, only that the reporter had a suspicion it may have. Many factors have to be considered when assessing whether the medicine has caused a reported adverse reaction. When monitoring the safety of medicines, MHRA experts carry out careful analysis of all these factors.The Department continues to encourage patients, healthcare professionals and carers to report any suspected side effects to the Yellow Card scheme. In addition, the Department engages regularly with the MHRA and disseminates information on drug safety to healthcare professionals, including recent MHRA guidance on the safe use of GLP-1 medicines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the (i) number of; and (ii) nature of, any investigations made by the NI Environment Agency into sites at Bonds Glen Road, Derry, including the outcomes of those investigations.
answer_text: I can advise you that NIEA have completed two site inspections at Bonds Glen Road, Derry, following a report from the Bonds Glen Environment Group on 24 September 2025. During a joint site inspection, a Wildlife Officer from the Natural Environment Division (NED) as well as an Investigating Officer from the Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) completed a visit of the site in question on 10 October 2025. A Water Quality Inspector (WQI) from the Water Catchment Unit (WCU) also visited the site on 23 October 2025 and checked the local waterways for pollution. Upon inspecting the site, no breaches of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985, the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 or the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 were identified.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to the Electricity Consumption and Renewable Generation Statistics, to detail any action she is taking to encourage renewable energy sources, such as solar, landfill gas, and hydro tidal generation, which currently account for just 8 per cent of the total renewable energy total.
answer_text: My Department is taking action to increase renewable electricity generation capacity through the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG).		The Final Scheme Design for the REPG scheme was published by my Department in September 2025. I have also submitted a paper to the Executive to agree the final policy.		Once Executive approval is secured, my department aims to introduce the required primary legislation for the REPG to the Assembly before summer recess 2026. Once the Primary Bill is in place, subordinate legislation will be taken forward to set out the detailed arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to the Electricity Consumption and Renewable Generation Statistics, whether her Department will meet the targets set in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, that at least 80 per cent of electricity consumption is from renewable sources, by 2030.
answer_text: Delivering additional renewable electricity generation is complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance. It requires joined-up working, across government and with our delivery partners. My Department does not hold all the levers, so collaboration is essential to unlock progress.  Grid capacity remains a constraint, so new and upgraded transmission infrastructure is essential. My Department continues to work closely with the System Operator, and other partners, on the measures needed to accommodate more renewable generation, including actions set out in their Dispatch Down Plan and Operational Policy Roadmap. I also welcome the revised Strategic Planning Policy Statement on Renewable and Low Carbon Energy that offers a more supportive planning framework for renewable deployment. My Department is focused on working collectively to deliver the transition in a way that ensures value for money for consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the avian flu outbreak near Omagh, West Tyrone.
answer_text: A suspect case of notifiable Avian Influenza was reported on Tuesday 03 March 2026 at a commercial poultry premises near Omagh, County Tyrone. A Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) was established, from Wednesday 04 March at 17:00 hrs, following preliminary positive results for Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI).  The decision was taken based on a number of factors including the clinical signs and preliminary results provided by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI).  All poultry at the site have been humanely culled as a disease control measure.  The TCZ comprises a 3km TCZ-A and a 10km TCZ-B.  The declaration which details all the measures that must be adhered to within the 3km and 10km zones can be found on the DAERA website at:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/disease-control-zone-declarations 	Updates where appropriate will be made on the DAERA website at:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/latest-situation
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the budget for the Early Years SEN Assistant Training Project for the next three years.
answer_text: The current indicative budget for the Early Years Assistants – SEN Training Project is £556k over three years, funded by the Public Sector Transformation Board.This is subject to change as the project evolves.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans she has to help mitigate against the rising price of home heating oil.
answer_text: Many of our households and businesses are already under pressure from the cost-of-living crisis, and this new energy shock, caused by conflict in the Middle East, is compounding those pressures.When the conflict in the Middle East, began, the reduction in electricity bills of around £30 per year had already been announced and delivery was being progressed by my Department.The £17 million subsequently announced by the British government is clearly inadequate, therefore, it is important that whatever support is available, or becomes available going forward, is targeted at those in most need. I am committed to engaging with my Executive colleagues to ensure that support is delivered as quickly as possible.	I met with the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) in advance of the launch of its formal study into the oil retail market on 20 March. The study is expected to conclude within approximately three months. My officials will provide relevant data to support this work, and I will give careful consideration to any recommendations arising from the findings.		The Consumer Council provides invaluable information for consumers by monitoring heating oil prices and publishing a weekly home heating oil price checker for the average price for 300 litres, 500 and 900 litres. A daily price checker began on 2nd March 2026 to help consumers who are monitoring heating oil prices and looking to make a more informed decision during this period of oil price volatility Home Heating Oil Daily Price Checker | Consumer Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reason why the proportion of patients waiting for over 9 and 26 weeks for a diagnostic test has increased.
answer_text: Although the proportion of patients waiting over 9 and 26 weeks has increased, it is encouraging to note that Department of Health statistics show a reduction in the total number of patients waiting for a diagnostic test at 31 December 2025, compared with 30 September 2025. It is also positive that the actual number of patients waiting longer than 9 and 26 weeks has decreased over the same period.The recently published Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan sets out clear proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards and how we will transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in the future.As part of the Implementation and Funding Plan, £12m has been secured to expand diagnostic imaging capacity across the region.  This includes increasing capacity for CT, MRI and ultrasound. An additional £13m has also been provided to expand endoscopy capacity.  While plans are being finalised to invest funding on a recurrent basis, it is being utilised to deliver additional non-recurrent capacity to reduce waiting list backlogs. As this backlog is resolved, patients may receive their test quicker than others due to clinical prioritisation, which can impact the proportion of patients waiting over 9 and 26 weeks, despite overall patient numbers reducing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any action by the NI Environment Agency to address issues around foul odours in Artigarvan, West Tyrone.
answer_text: Lacpatrick Dairies (NI) Ltd, Artigarvan is permitted under the Pollution Prevention and Control (Industrial Emissions) Regulations (NI) 2013 (PPC regulations).NIEA has issued one enforcement notice to the company since September 2025 relating to odour and breaches of the emissions limits in their permit covering discharges from their effluent treatment plant.This notice has led to the company carrying out a strategic effluent review on site by two external consultants and have contracted an additional consultant to review the site’s odour management plan. Alongside these reviews, improvement works continue on site and NIEA officials are in regular contact with LacPatrick Dairies about this ongoing work. NIEA officials continue to take necessary and proportionate enforcement action in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy where appropriate. NIEA also regularly engages with local stakeholders about their concerns, including residents and elected representatives.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reason why no Health and Social Care Trust is meeting his targets for (i) waiting time for a first outpatient appointment; (ii) waiting time for inpatient and day case admission; and (iii) waiting times for a diagnostic service.
answer_text: There are ongoing challenges meeting waiting time targets across the Health and Social Care system, which have been driven by historical underfunding and a widening gap between rising service demand and funded capacity. The absence of multi-year budgets has also impacted on the ability to plan and deliver long-term service reform.Despite the current financial constraints, my Department has made significant progress in relation to the number of patients who are projected to be waiting four years by the end of March 2026. Between March 2025–January 2026, there has been a reduction of 47% for outpatients, 61% for inpatient and day-cases, and 79% for the named procedures in the Elective Care Framework.Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and it will not be resolved in a year or two, and investment will need to be sustained for up to five years to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any JobStart opportunities for young people in West Tyrone.
answer_text: Since the JobStart Scheme launched on 6th October 2025 there have been 71 approved JobStart opportunities in West Tyrone.  Positions that are still available are advertised on https://www.jobapplyni.com/. Anyone interested in taking part in the Scheme can speak to a Work Coach in their local Jobs & Benefits office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to introduce a community-based audiology service for adults to improve access to assessment and treatment.
answer_text: At present the level of demand for audiology services exceeds the capacity available in the HSC leading to unacceptable waiting times for some patients.  A number of factors have contributed to this, including funding pressures and missed opportunities in the past to modernise how health and social care services are delivered.As demand, in the form of referrals into Audiology Services in Northern Ireland has increased, the number of audiologists available within HSC has reduced restricting capacity. The reasons for this are complex but officials in my Department have been working closely with stakeholders to identify how rising demand can be met in the short, medium and longer term.This engagement has included discussions with HSC Trusts, workforce leads, the British Association of Audiologists, and community and primary care providers. While some of these discussions have not resolved the challenges as yet, they have been constructive and are helping to shape thinking around a more sustainable model of care. A key consideration is maintaining the right balance between hospitalbased audiology services and those that may be safely and effectively delivered in community settings, while also ensuring workforce capacity is protected.Officials are currently exploring the development of a pilot proposal for an alternative communitybased audiology model to complement existing HSC services. This pilot would allow a small group of patients to be seen in a new way in the short term, with the intention of gathering evidence to inform a longerterm solution.  Further work on this model of care will be taken forward later this month to fully scope the opportunities available.  Should the pilot commence and be positively evaluated, any further roll-out of such an initiative will need underpinned by an extensive tendering exercise and additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the organisations active in West Tyrone which have received Core Grant funding in 2026-27.
answer_text: While the Core Grant Scheme does not currently hold information relating to the specific constituencies in which applicants are operating, the following organisations have progressed to Stage 2 of the assessment process and have listed office locations within Tyrone; Willowbank Limited, Tobin Centre, Lilac Cancer Support and Coalisland Training Services (CTS).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to reduce audiology waiting times for adults with age-related hearing loss, in West Tyrone.
answer_text: As demand, in the form of referrals into Audiology Services in Northern Ireland has increased, the number of audiologists available within HSC has reduced and is restricting capacity. The reasons for this are complex but officials in my Department have been working closely with stakeholders to identify how rising demand can be met in the short, medium and longer term. Officials are currently scoping the potential of a pilot proposal for an alternative communitybased audiology model to complement existing HSC services. This potential approach would allow a small group of patients to be seen in a new way in the short term, with the intention of gathering evidence to inform a longerterm solution.  The outcome of the scoping exercise will inform the next steps and any associated funding implications of service extension.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the process for a dentist practice to remove patients from its list; and (ii) the number of patients that have been taken off dentist's practice lists, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Under Schedule 2, paragraphs 11 and 11A of the General Dental Services Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1993, General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) have the right to withdraw from continuing care and capitation arrangements with a patient.Where a Dental Practitioner chooses to end a continuing care or capitation arrangement for an individual patient, or for a small number of patients, a WCA 996 form must be submitted to the Business Services Organisation (BSO) for each patient. The practitioner may choose either to withdraw from the arrangement with immediate effect in specific circumstances or to provide the patient with three months’ notice.For Dental Practitioners wishing to terminate arrangements for a larger cohort of patients, this may be undertaken through a ‘block deregistration’ process. Practices are required to contact the BSO, which will provide guidance to support the electronic termination of the continuing care or capitation arrangements. In this instance three months’ notice of withdrawal will be given to the patient.Patients are also automatically deregistered from continuing care and capitation arrangements with a Health Service dentist if they have not attended for an appointment within the previous two years. Dentists, in consultation with their patient, may also opt to not renew a patient’s continuing care or capitation arrangement at the end of the 24-month arrangement which is the standard registration period with a Health Service dentist. Tables 1 to 6 below provide details on the number of health service registrations that had a registration lapse, a manual de-registration or a block de-registration from a health service dentist in Northern Ireland in the financial years 2020/21 to 2024/25 broken down by the Assembly Area in which the dental practice is located.All count figures in Tables 1 to 5 represent activity across the full financial year (1st April to 31st March). question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 40483/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 40806/22-27, to detail (i) the area of Derry and Strabane District Council that this scheme covers; and (ii) any actions that are being taken to encourage those farmers who have not yet given consent for his Department to carry out a badger sett survey on their land to do so.
answer_text: (i) The bTB regionalisation research pilot scheme encompasses approximately 235 km² within the Derry and Strabane District Council area. It is located in the council’s border region, running north along border from Strabane and bounded to the east by line running south, roughly through Maydown, Drumahoe and Dunnamanagh. (ii) My Department has written to those farmers and landowners in the area who have not yet given permission to carry out the sett survey. These letters included a list of Frequently Asked Questions with more details about what the project involves and what to expect. A number of information events have been held in early March to which all farmers in the pilot area were invited. DAERA staff are also visiting the remaining farmers in the area to impress upon them the importance of taking part in the survey and seeking their permission to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 41112/22-27, to detail a timescale for these potential changes to the Civil Service Pension Scheme to be considered.
answer_text: Subject to the necessary approvals and completion of Assembly procedures on progressing draft delegated legislation, it is currently anticipated that the Department will progress with making these potential changes in May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on any work her Department is undertaking to improve mobile phone coverage on the (i) A5; and (ii) A6.
answer_text: As you are aware mobile infrastructure and associated services are currently provided on a wholly commercial basis in the North, however my officials are involved in a number of initiatives, such as the Mobile Action Plan and City and Growth Deals digital projects, that should help improve mobile connectivity here. I understand the importance of good quality mobile connectivity and remain committed to encouraging the rollout of mobile infrastructure to improve these services. My officials will continue to engage and collaborate with the mobile industry and other partners to ensure that citizens and businesses throughout the North can fully avail of the benefits that wireless technology can offer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she will take to deliver the actions not delivered within the Social Enterprise Action Plan One year update, particularly (i) Action 6, in relation to Invest NI leadership and development; and (ii) Action 9, regarding the resource challenges within the Social Enterprise Growth Fund.
answer_text: (i) Action 6Action 6 is a follow-on action to build on the learning from Action 2.  Invest NI made available two places for social enterprises in the Leader Programme Autumn/ Winter 2025 cohort, funding 100% of workshop participation costs and 50% financial support towards leadership coaching and business mentoring.  One participant accepted a place, and a second place was offered and declined.  Two places will be made available again in the Autumn/ Winter 2026 cohort. Intertrade Ireland’s Leadership Programme for social enterprises was launched on 26 February 2026.  This will make available up to five places for social enterprises across the North this year. Once the participation by social enterprises on both of those courses has been evaluated, Action 6 can be taken forward fully.  My officials, along with Invest NI and Intertrade Ireland have also worked to refine the recruitment process for social enterprise organisations to access future Leadership and Management opportunities.  (ii) Action 9A capital funding bid was submitted as part of the budget process to progress Action 9.  My Department is currently assessing its overall spending priorities.  It is also worth noting that recent research highlighted the need for social enterprise organisations to become better informed about existing funding provisions. Therefore, through the Social Enterprise Work Programme my Department is supporting organisations to improve their capacity to access existing external funding by enhancing their understanding of social value, marketing potential and providing exposure to new or reopened funding opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's work to reduce the minimum age for bowel cancer screening to 60.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland bowel screening is currently offered to people aged between 60 and 74 years old, every 2 years.  Work to expand the bowel screening programme down to 50 years of age, in line with a UK NSC recommendation, is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services. Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of staff dealing with McCloud payments, in each of the past 3 years; and (ii) the number of staff vacancies in that section.
answer_text: There are 2 categories of McCloud remedy payments. Deferred Choice Underpin (DCU) – applies to active members who receive their remedy at retirement. These payments have been processed as part of business as usual from 1 October 2023, when the legislation took effect. Immediate Choice (IC) – applies to Civil Service Pension Scheme members who left the service before the remedy was implemented. The number of staff dealing with McCloud payments in each of the last 3 years are:23/24*24/25*25/26** 710 16* DCU payments only.** IC payments did not commence until September 2025. The priority for McCloud IC team is to issue Remedial Service Statements (RSSs) to pensioner and partially retired members affected by the McCloud Judgement by the legislative deadline of 31 March 2027.There are currently no vacancies in the DCU team and two vacancies within the McCloud IC team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the average time taken from an application being made for a McCloud payment to the payment being issued, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: There are 2 categories of McCloud remedy payments. Deferred Choice Underpin (DCU) – applies to active members who receive their remedy at retirement. These payments have been processed as part of business as usual from 1 October 2023, when the legislation took effect.  In the last 3 years, 99.8% of DCU payments have been made within 6 days of the members last day of service.Immediate Choice (IC) – applies to Civil Service Pension Scheme members who left the service before the remedy was implemented. Civil Service Pensions commenced issuing IC applications from October 2024 and processing returned choices from September 2025. The current average time for processing returned IC remedy choices is 12 months. During this period members continue to receive their unadjusted pension each month. The Department’s priority remains the provision of Remedial Service Statements (RSSs) to all affected members by the legislative deadline of 31 March 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance issued to schools to ensure that all defibrillators are registered on The Circuit, the national defibrillator network.
answer_text: As part of the rollout of the defibrillators to schools in Northern Ireland, officials from my Department have worked in conjunction with the British Heart Foundation and the Education Authority (EA) to compiled guidance for schools. This strongly recommends that schools register their defibrillator on The Circuit.  A copy of the guidance provided can be found at Department Of Education - Provision Of Automated External Defibrillators - Advice for NI Schools | Education Authority Northern Ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the defibrillators in West Tyrone schools that are placed in external cabinets at school boundaries.
answer_text: From the recent rollout of defibrillators under my Curriculum-Led Capital Programme, no defibrillators have been placed in external cabinets outside West Tyrone schools at this time.However, officials are working in conjunction with the British Heart Foundation to identify schools in areas with little or no existing access to a defibrillator, where an external cabinet would facilitate wider community use.Where a need is identified, officials will work with schools to have external cabinets provided.
