answer_text: The programme of works will be developed for each Council area, reflective of the relative priorities of schemes, and published in my Department’s 2026/27 annual report to Council.The highway network is published as a live link on: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/highway-network which can be formatted to show total digital length for each of my Department’s section offices which are broadly equivalent to local council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any actions taken by his Department since the first incident of blue-green algae in Moorlough Lake, West Tyrone, to prevent a re-occurrence.
answer_text: I am concerned of the occurrence of blue-green algal blooms in Moorlough Lake and various other sites across Northern Ireland.My Department has received a total of eleven reports of potential blue-green algae in Moorlough Lake, with the first on 28th August 2021. Five of these reports have been confirmed as blue-green algae. Following the 2025 monitoring season, a review of the Inter-Agency Blue-Green Algae Monitoring Protocol has been completed, which provides clarity on Departmental actions and the roles and responsibilities of partners and stakeholders in addressing reports of blue-green algal blooms.I remain committed to improving water quality throughout Northern Ireland to reduce the impacts to society such have been witnessed by blue green algae blooms in recent years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average turnaround time for a blue badge application being submitted, to the issuing of the badge, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Records relating to Blue Badge applications are retained only for the lifetime of the badge, which is a maximum of three years. As a result, it is not possible to provide average turnaround times for the last five years.Based on the information available, over 98% of applications have been processed within the 6 to 8 week advised timeframe over the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will be requesting that the Executive allocate any Barnett consequentials coming from the reforms to the special educational needs and disabilities system in England, be earmarked for those services here.
answer_text: Yes, I will be making the strongest case possible that any Barnett consequential resulting from funding to address the needs of children with Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) should be used to address SEND in Northern Ireland.My officials have already raised the matter of a Barnett consequential with their counterparts in the Department of Finance (DoF).  DoF is currently engaging with HM Treasury in relation to whether Northern Ireland will receive any Barnett Consequential as a result of the £5bn in England for SEND.  If Northern Ireland does receive a Barnett Consequential, it should be assumed that this would be un-hypothecated and that it would ultimately be for the Executive to decide how this funding would be allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of pets that were abandoned following Christmas, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not collect this type of data nor is there any statutory requirement to do so. Nevertheless, I am conscious that rescue and rehoming organisations can experience pressure in the post-Christmas period, which is why I recently highlighted the need for pet owners to understand and honour their legal obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with stakeholders regarding the Active Travel budget, over the last three years.
answer_text: Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 places a statutory requirement on my Department to set a minimum level of spend on active travel of 10% of overall transport budgets. While the Act does not define “overall transport budgets” or “active travel spend”, active travel is generally understood to include walking, wheeling and cycling. Prior to the Act, reported active travel expenditure was largely focused on capital investment in new schemes and on road safety education programmes delivered in schools.In order to transparently demonstrate progress towards meeting the statutory target, my Department undertook, for the first time, a comprehensive assessment of overall spend on active travel. This work concluded in June 2025 and reflects my Department’s interpretation of Section 22 of the Act. It recognises the wide range of activities that contribute to maintaining safe, accessible and wellfunctioning footways and cycleways, while also highlighting the importance of continued and increased investment in new active travel infrastructure. In that context, I have increased funding for new active travel projects from between £10m and £12m per year in recent years to £18m in 2025/26.Details of overall active travel expenditure are now published on my Department’s website and will be updated annually to ensure transparency and allow progress against the statutory target to be tracked over time.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/topics/departmental-spend-active-travel Engagement with stakeholders on transport funding, including active travel, is undertaken primarily through the public consultation on the Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) of draft budgets, which my Department carries out annually. This process provides stakeholders with the opportunity to comment on budget proposals, including active travel allocations, and to highlight how funding decisions may impact on equality outcomes.I welcome the publication of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report, Active Travel in Northern Ireland. My Department has carefully considered the findings of the report and has accepted all of the recommendations contained within it. The Northern Ireland Audit Office was formally notified of this on 27 November 2025. A detailed response to each recommendation, including target implementation dates during 2026/27, has been deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on her Department's consideration of the NI Audit Office report, Active Travel in Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail the timetable for responding to the recommendations.
answer_text: Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 places a statutory requirement on my Department to set a minimum level of spend on active travel of 10% of overall transport budgets. While the Act does not define “overall transport budgets” or “active travel spend”, active travel is generally understood to include walking, wheeling and cycling. Prior to the Act, reported active travel expenditure was largely focused on capital investment in new schemes and on road safety education programmes delivered in schools.In order to transparently demonstrate progress towards meeting the statutory target, my Department undertook, for the first time, a comprehensive assessment of overall spend on active travel. This work concluded in June 2025 and reflects my Department’s interpretation of Section 22 of the Act. It recognises the wide range of activities that contribute to maintaining safe, accessible and wellfunctioning footways and cycleways, while also highlighting the importance of continued and increased investment in new active travel infrastructure. In that context, I have increased funding for new active travel projects from between £10m and £12m per year in recent years to £18m in 2025/26.Details of overall active travel expenditure are now published on my Department’s website and will be updated annually to ensure transparency and allow progress against the statutory target to be tracked over time.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/topics/departmental-spend-active-travel Engagement with stakeholders on transport funding, including active travel, is undertaken primarily through the public consultation on the Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) of draft budgets, which my Department carries out annually. This process provides stakeholders with the opportunity to comment on budget proposals, including active travel allocations, and to highlight how funding decisions may impact on equality outcomes.I welcome the publication of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report, Active Travel in Northern Ireland. My Department has carefully considered the findings of the report and has accepted all of the recommendations contained within it. The Northern Ireland Audit Office was formally notified of this on 27 November 2025. A detailed response to each recommendation, including target implementation dates during 2026/27, has been deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Care Leaver Statistics 2024/25, in particular the 48 per cent of men and 37 per cent of women leaving care without any qualifications, up from 26 per cent and 18 per cent respectively in 2015; and (ii) to detail any actions his Department plans to take to reduce these figures.
answer_text: Between 2015 and 2025, the proportion of young people leaving care with no qualifications increased for both groups. For females, rates rose from 18% to 37% (a 19point increase), while for males, rates increased from 30% to 48% (an 18point increase). My department is focused on improving outcomes for young people transitioning out of care, with a key priority placed on strengthening their educational attainment and overall educational outcomes.  Each HSC Trust has their own employability service whose responsibility is to support care leavers to navigate their ETE pathway from 16 onwards until the age out of services.From a strategic perspective The New Foundations programme, launched by the NI Minister for Communities and by My Department represents a major crossdepartmental systems reform aimed at improving outcomes for care leavers, including reducing NETE (Not in Education Training and Employment) rates.  The programme explicitly aims to embed pathways to work and study, connecting young people with training providers, further education, skills programmes, and employers.  There have already been relationships established and young people engaged in pathways with Hope Project (Lloyds Bank), Project Flourish (Hays recruitment) and NICSHR colleagues are developing a proofofconcept employability scheme for care leavers aged 16–30. Operationally, building on the success of last year’s event for young people, carers and key stakeholders, my Department alongside Ulster University Widening Participation Team are providing a 3x day ‘Raising Educational Aspirations’ event between 02nd June to 04th June 2026 for young people Year 9 and above at the Derry/Londonderry Campus.  This event will focus on enhancing the knowledge of young people, carers and wider professional networks on the pathways available for young people into education, training and employment, and involve key stakeholders such as DfE Careers department, Money Ready, UU academics, and Homewards NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 36151/22-27, to detail the reason why there was a significant increase in dismissals in recent years.
answer_text: HSC Trusts report several factors contributing to the increase in dismissals in recent years. COVID19 led to a substantial rise in longterm sickness absence, including cases of Long COVID. In some Trusts, COVID19related absences were temporarily excluded from the Managing Attendance Protocol, which resulted in delays in case progression and a number of historic cases reaching formal stages later than usual.Trusts also reported delays in the processing of Ill Health Retirement applications by the HSC Pension Service during the pandemic. The subsequent clearance of this backlog created a clustering effect, with more cases reaching their conclusion in the same year.In parallel, Occupational Health services identified higher numbers of staff who were permanently unfit for work. This led either to Ill Health Retirement or, where pension criteria were not met, termination on the grounds of illhealth capability.Trusts emphasise that dismissal on illhealth grounds is a last resort and is only undertaken after all reasonable supports, adjustments and interventions have been exhausted.Trusts also advise that the renewed focus on supporting consistent attendance management, including the work of dedicated Attendance Management Teams, has helped ensure cases were progressed in line with policy. As a result, a number of cases that had been delayed during the pandemic were able to reach their appropriate conclusion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support that will be given to young farmers in the 2026/27 financial year.
answer_text: While 2025 was the final year for applications to the YFP Scheme, successful applicants to the YFP scheme in 2025 will receive their top-up payment for five years. The Farming for the Generations Scheme, included within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, is the policy instrument to support generational renewal within the industry. This new scheme, rather than focusing on purely financial incentives, is designed to take a strategic and holistic approach to generational renewal which supports the intergenerational needs of the entire farm family, thereby facilitating a smooth and planned transition and supporting the long-term sustainability of the farm business. The pilot Farming for the Generations Scheme is scheduled to conclude in March 2026. The main Farming for the Generations Scheme is being developed in conjunction with stakeholders and future support for young farmers is being considered as part of the development of this Scheme. Subject to all necessary approvals being in place this Scheme is planned for launch in November 2026.The College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs (CAFRE) provides knowledge and innovation schemes and I would encourage young farmers to engage with these schemes and avail of the benefits that they can deliver for their farm businesses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action he is taking to increase enforcement against fly-tipping.
answer_text: The NIEA’s Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) investigates and prosecutes serious waste crimes in Northern Ireland, using its enforcement powers under the Waste and Contaminated Land Order 1997. Criminal prosecutions may include confiscation under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 to ensure offenders do not profit and to enforce the ‘Polluter Pays’ principle. To strengthen its enforcement capability, the ECU is recruiting specialist staff and investing in accredited training to embed best practice. The NIEA also works closely with local councils through an established flytipping protocol.Both the NIEA and District Councils have access to various provisions under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, enabling them to undertake enforcement action where appropriate. A multiagency approach remains central to tackling illegal waste activity. NIEA collaborates with organisations including the Joint Unit on Waste Crime and the Organised Crime Task Force, ensuring coordinated, proactive action across agencies.My Department is also seeking to increase the maximum penalties associated with waste offences which impact the water environment. The draft Fisheries and Water Environment Bill provides the legislative solution to ensure there are consistent penalties across a range of pieces of legislation including the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 and the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, which is necessary to deliver penalties that are robust, fair and proportionate to the offences committed. This supports action that has been committed to within the Lough Neagh Action plan and ensures that there is a consistent enforcement regime with appropriate powers to ensure that aquatic and ecosystem health is prioritised and supported. The public consultation on policies for inclusion in the draft Bill ended on 6th November and officials incorporated analysis into the Policy Memorandum, which was agreed by the Executive in late December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the cross-border pilot regional cooperation programme on tackling bovine TB.
answer_text: In December I secured €6.4 million of Shared Island Funding to support this cross-border regionalisation research project, with its initial focus in a cross-border area covering north east Donegal and part of the area covered by the Derry City and Strabane Council.  On Thursday 19 February I, along with Minister Heydon, officially launched the joint bovine TB Eradication Pilot Project which is intended to run from 2025 to 2030 and will include a number of measures including, wildlife intervention, additional cattle measures and people measures.To date, 89% of local farmers have granted permission for DAERA to carry out a badger sett survey on their land which commenced in January this year.  Wildlife intervention and cattle measures are due to follow in the summer, subject to necessary statutory approvals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of nurse recruitment issues at Glenside Adult Training Centre, Strabane, which has led to a reduction of services.
answer_text: The Western HSC Trust have advised that Glenside Day Centre has two full-time nursing posts, delivered by three staff who provide specific nursing care to a proportion of the service users, with relevant needs attending day care at the Centre.  The Trust has been experiencing nursing staff sick leave since November 2025, which has unfortunately impacted on the Centre's ability to facilitate full attendance for five service users. The Trust have engaged an Agency nurse to provide temporary capacity and all necessary training/support has been provided to ensure that the available nursing staff can meet and support the presenting nursing needs of service users. The Trust have advised that this is a temporary situation with staff due to return from sick leave on a phased basis from March 2026. The Trust regret this temporary impact on service delivery but want to offer assurance that no permanent nursing vacancies exist at the Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Labour Market Statistics February 2026, in particular the Labour Force Survey which shows a decrease in the employment rate and an increase in the unemployment rate.
answer_text: While I note the latest Labour Market Statistics released by NISRA reported a decrease in the employment rate over the year, the rate did increase by 0.5pps over the quarter.  That said, both of these changes were not statistically significant.  It was encouraging to see the number of payrolled employees in the North increasing by 1.2% over the year especially given all regions in Britain saw a decline in payrolled employees over the year. In relation to the unemployment rate, while it did increase by 0.6pps over the year, there was a 0.2pps decrease over the quarter.  Again, both of these changes were not statistically significant.  It is worth highlighting that at 2.2%, our unemployment rate is lower than any of the regions in Britain.  Overall, NISRA noted that the latest labour market results for the North show that across all sources, changes in the employment levels over the year have been relatively small.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to introduce a scheme similar to the Republic of Ireland’s Basic Income for the Arts project.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 40364 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on any discussions with the Minister for the Economy, and Executive Colleagues, in relation to the Independent Review of Education recommendation for a Single Education Department, including post-16 education.
answer_text: I have met with the Minister for the Economy on several occasions to discuss strengthening the 14–19 and post16 system, enhancing departmental collaboration, supporting early learning and childcare, and improving post-16 SEN provision, careers advice, pathways, and learner mobility.I have not had any substantive discussions regarding the Independent Review of Education’s recommendation to establish a single education department, including responsibility for post16 education. As set out in my department’s response to the Independent Review on 21 October 2024, this recommendation is primarily a matter for the Executive.My department continues to work closely with the Department for the Economy on areas of shared interest, including post16 education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) economic benefits of the Ireland’s Hidden Heartlands campaign, to the Fermanagh economy; and (ii) 45 local businesses currently engaged in the campaign.
answer_text: A £300,000 marketing campaign launched on 16th February across Ireland. It showcases a range of County Fermanagh businesses, including the Killyhevlin Lakeside Hotel, Lough Erne Resort, Boatyard Distillery, Marble Arch Caves and Erne Water Taxi. The campaign encourages visitors to book a short break in County Fermanagh.It is envisioned that the campaign will generate almost 27 million opportunities to see or hear the activity, reaching 58% of adults across Ireland. The campaign will run until 31st March. The economic impact of the campaign will be evaluated after completion. Evidence from previous Tourism NI marketing campaigns indicates that for every £1 spent, £60 is spent in the local economy.The campaign is underpinned by 26 offers recruited from the County Fermanagh tourism industry. These promotional offers are showcased throughout press, paid digital and the discovernorthernireland.com website. To date, 45 businesses have applied to use the Ireland's Hidden Heartlands brand. Due to GDPR guidelines, the list cannot be shared, but it includes a broad range of businesses from right across County Fermanagh, including hotels, restaurants, visitor attractions and experiences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the timescale for the accelerated review to resolve the current impasse within the criminal justice system.
answer_text: The scope, terms of reference and overarching conditions for the accelerated review are a matter of ongoing discussion with the Criminal Bar Association and Solicitors Criminal Bar Association. Those issues will necessarily determine the timescale for initiation and completion, but I expect work to proceed and conclude at pace and am deploying Departmental resources to that end. It is critical that the Criminal Bar Association quickly return to full service in the interests of the victims and witnesses who suffering additional stress and uncertainty as the result of the service withdrawal. They remain my focus and priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has given formal confirmation of his Department's position on the introduction of Minimum Unit Pricing for alcohol.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the disposal of the former Strabane Grammar School site on Liskey Road, Strabane.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) open market disposal of the site was paused in late 2023 at Derry City and Strabane District Council’s request to allow Council officials to carry out the PAD process and to obtain Land & Property Services’ (LPS) valuation.  The Council has just recently confirmed that these steps have finally been completed and EA has subsequently instructed LPS to proceed with the disposal.  EA will continue to work with LPS in prioritising this and has set a target completion of early in the 2026/27 financial year.  However, completing the disposal is subject to LPS’ advice as the honest broker between the Council and EA.  If LPS requires additional information to support the process, this may impact on the target completion date and would be outside EA’s control.In the two-year period since the Council asked for a pause in the open market disposal, EA has continued to manage this high-risk, vacant site as best it can within its exceptionally constrained budget.  Despite these endeavours, EA has been the victim of break-ins, anti-social behaviour, criminal damage and arson.  With this in mind, the EA has confirmed it remains exceptionally keen to complete this disposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the October 2025 Patient and Client Council reports (i) People to Partners; and (ii) What the Public Think.
answer_text: I welcome the Patient and Client Council’s recent reports: People to Partners and What the Public Think. The reports provide timely and important insight into how people in Northern Ireland experience health and social care services and how they want to be involved in shaping them. My Department values the PCC’s role in representing the interests of patients, carers and communities. The findings of both reports will inform the work set out in the Health and Social Care Reset Plan published in July 2025 and how we can work collectively to deliver on its ambition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the research report, Transform: Exploring Pathways and Barriers for Young People with Disabilities; and (ii) whether her Department plans to implement any of its recommendations.
answer_text: I welcome the Transform: Exploring Pathways and Barriers for Young People with Disabilities research report, which aligns with and reinforces many of the findings emerging from my Department’s own review into provision and support for young people with Special Educational Needs (SEN).In June 2025, I outlined my proposals for reform which, subject to investment, will enhance the education, skills and training provision and support for school leavers with SEN. These are referenced in the report and cover issues such as expanded capacity and pathways, better information and guidance, more effective transitions and joined up approaches. My officials have been making steady progress across the work strands, including leading the development of a crossdepartmental bid for funding through the Transformation Fund.  This collaborative bid is specifically focused on improving pathways and transitions for SEN school leavers as they move from school into adulthood. The outcome of the bid is expected to be confirmed shortly. My officials have met with Stranmillis University College to discuss their research, particularly the mapping of postschool provision.  We recognise the value of this work and officials will explore further with Stranmillis how it could be sustained and further developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Strategy to 2030 Annual Statistical Report 2025, particularly the (i) seven per cent increase in serious road accidents; (ii) 12 per cent increase in children, killed or seriously injured; and (iii) 8 per cent rise in 16 to 24-year-olds, killed or seriously injured.
answer_text: Road Safety is a key priority for me, one death on our roads is one too many. On the 27th of January 2026 I launched my Department’s latest Road Safety Action Plan to 2027, that supports the ongoing work of the Road Safety Strategy, a key element within the Programme for Government. This action plan has been developed in collaboration with a wide range of road safety partners including Executive Departments and Blue Lights organisations and faces into priority and emerging road safety issues. The resulting action plan outlines specific steps and initiatives, to be progressed until the end of March 2027, which will contribute to the aim of reducing the number of people killed or seriously injured on our roads. This plan includes a number of Strategic Interventions, acknowledged to be long-term policy developments and actions. These contribute to the strategic aims of Safe Roads, Safe Vehicles and Safe People. Alongside our road safety partners, we focus on priority areas including, amongst others, policy and legislation, enforcement, communication and awareness and active travel.I also announced one of the key actions of the plan, the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing for later this year.  Other key actions are the introduction of a lower legal alcohol limit, examining options for increasing speeding fines, and the development of new scheme proposals on the Regional Strategic Transport Network, targeting those routes with highest collision rates where engineering-based solutions would mitigate risks.My Department has already implemented part-time speed limits at 233 schools. In June I announced a further 40 schools to benefit from this safety intervention.  I also announced that I am putting measures in place to reinforce existing policy around the proper use of signs and lights on buses carrying children to and from school.  My Department continues to deliver targeted and relevant road safety campaigns and educational programmes, including the new ‘Road Wise Up’ - Drug Driving Campaign and ‘Be Bold, Be Seen’.  I continue to work closely with other Departments and agencies to explore further opportunities to work together on road safety issues. In addition, through the Road Safety Strategic Forum, my officials will collaborate with ‘Blue Light’ organisations and other departments to progress the actions of the Road Safety Strategy.While many of these interventions target specific aspects of road safety, we all need to have honest conversations about our personal responsibility on the road, and the things we can all do to keep ourselves and others safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38838/22-27, to detail the reason for the significant reduction in organ donations in 2024-25.
answer_text: In 2024/25 Northern Ireland experienced a 19% decrease in the eligible donor population.  A range of factors can contribute to declines in donor numbers, including the patient’s medical suitability to donate and cause of death.The decline in the eligible donor population, combined with a drop in the consent rate for donation after circulatory death, led to the decrease in the number of proceeding donors in 2024/25.Year-to-date donation rates in Northern Ireland for 2025/26 have increased and are now ahead of target, with 48 proceeding donors recorded to 31 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the January 2026 Ulster University research A Systematic Review of the Perceived Barriers to and Facilitators of Technology Assisted–Child Sexual Abuse Disclosure will influence his Department's policy development in this area.
answer_text: A Systematic Review of the Perceived Barriers to and Facilitators of Technology Assisted–Child Sexual Abuse Disclosure is a journal article based on the findings of the research report Understanding Risk, Barriers and Facilitators to Reporting Technology Assisted Child Sexual Abuse. This research was commissioned by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland under the Northern Ireland Executive’s Keeping Children and Young People Safe: An Online Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland. The findings presented in the report offer a significant contribution to our understanding of online harm and will help to inform my Department’s policy development in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the January 2026 Ulster University research A Systematic Review of the Perceived Barriers to and Facilitators of Technology Assisted–Child Sexual Abuse Disclosure will influence his Department's policy development in this area.
answer_text: My department welcomes the report as an important source of Northern Ireland specific evidence, which officials will consider as part of any refreshed and updated ‘Safeguarding in Education Guidance’ and other cross-departmental policy work in respect of healthy relationships, mobile phone use in schools and online safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the £214.6 million allocated through the reserve claim will be used.
answer_text: The allocation of £214.6m from the Reserve Claim will enable my Department to address a number of the unavoidable financial pressures faced this year, including pay, Special Educational Needs, and essential schools’ maintenance. However, even with this allocation, a funding gap remains, leaving the Department, the EA and schools needing to make savings of £23.4m between now and the end of the current financial year.  Both my Department and the EA have made and continue to make every effort to realise savings at pace.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the £185.4 million allocated through Reserve Claim will be used.
answer_text: My Department is currently facing a funding gap of some £211.6 million. The allocation of £185.4 million from the Reserve Claim is very welcome as it will make a substantial contribution towards reducing our projected overspend. While it will remain considerably challenging to breakeven this year, I will continue to make every effort to achieve this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when he will answer AQW 39186/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 39186/22-27 was answered on 23 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of current Housing Executive Homes in West Tyrone that have two forms of heating for water, broken down by estate.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has assumed that this query does not mean immersion heaters, which most of our properties would have in addition to their primary heating systems. We have based our response on our low carbon systems.The Housing Executive has six properties in West Tyrone with two forms of heating for water:Three homes in Hunters Crescent estate, Omagh with Air Source Heat Pumps and thermal battery heating water.Three homes in Strathroy estate, Omagh with hybrid gas boilers and Air Source Heat PumpsIt should be noted that these were installed as part of energy efficiency pilot projects and are not as yet standard installations by the Housing Executive.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people in West Tyrone who have been struck off their GP register, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of patient removal request transactions by West Tyrone Assembly Area GP Practices, by subcategory, can be found in Table 1 below.Table 1 – Patient removal request transactions by subcategory – by West Tyrone Assembly Area GP Practices.Description20212022202320242025Outside area removal requests127129300260268Removal at Surgery Request91610108Para21 (Removal as the result of an incident reported to the police) Removal43***GP Practices have been assigned to Assembly Area 'West Tyrone’ based on Practice Postcode.Removals information is based on data held by BSO relating to registered patients.‘Outside area removal requests’ are submitted when a patient moves to an address outside the Practice catchment area.‘Removal at Surgery Request’ refers to removal requests submitted after a breakdown of the Doctor/Patient relationship’.Counts of less than 3 have been replaced with an * in order to protect patient confidentiality.Removal request transactions do not always result in a patient being removed from a GP Practice via the same transaction type.  For example, in the case of outside area removal requests a patient may have registered with another GP Practice before the removal request has been actioned.Patients removed at doctor's request retain entitlement to healthcare but are not immediately re-registered with a GP practice.Individual Patients may have more than one removal transaction request over the period.Removal figures have been derived from a live administrative database updated as new information is received and hence may not perfectly reconcile with published statistics.Patients may be removed for reasons other than at Doctors request e.g. Death, moving to another part of the UK, leaving the UK, whereabouts unknown.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the businesses in West Tyrone that have received support from Invest NI, in each of the last five years, broken down by business and the amount provided.
answer_text: Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of support offered to businesses over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal. The portal contains the following information which will facilitate access to the data requested:		Portal | Invest NI Total Tables 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025	Provides a complete picture of Invest NI support through summary tables that aggregate both published and non-published data (indirect support, like advice and guidance, and offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues). This information is broken down into several different variables, including council and constituency areas.		Portal | Invest NI Financial Offers of Assistance (Detail)	Provides detail on Individual offers of direct financial assistance issued by Invest NI to businesses between 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025. Does not include indirect support, like advice and guidance, or offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues.		Portal | Invest NI Open Data Guidance	Provides guidance on the data contained in 1 and 2 above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 39400/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 39400/22-27 was answered on 10 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of compensation that her Department has paid for damage to cars, due to potholes or other road defects, in West Tyrone, for each of last five years.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 38929/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Irish Farm Journal's Land Report 2025, in particular the implication of rising land prices on inheritance tax obligations.
answer_text: The Irish Farmers Journal Land Price Report has stated that the average price per acre in Northern Ireland in 2025 was £15,202/acre compared to £14,736/acre in 2024.  This is in line with my Department’s analysis on the impact of the changes to inheritance tax being introduced on 6 April 2026.  This analysis used an average land price of £15,000/acre (prior to adding the valuations of buildings and working capital) and showed that around 5% of farms would be impacted by the limitation of 100% agricultural and business property relief combined to £2.5m, transferable between spouses.I remain strongly of the view that the inheritance tax changes should be reversed completely and have written to the Defra Secretary of State to state this.  I have also asked that if the changes remain in place, the threshold for agricultural and business property relief at a rate of 100% be kept under review.  If the threshold remains frozen and land values continue to rise, the number of farms impacted by inheritance tax will also increase. I hope you find this information helpful.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37128/22-27, for an update on the Strule Shared Education Campus project, in particular the £6.4million reduction in capital spend announced.
answer_text: Significant progress continues on the Strule Shared Education Campus (SSEC) Main Works Construction with just under 27% of the Main Works now complete. All works remain within the Full Business Case approval and programme limits. The construction is due to be completed by November 2027 with full campus opening by September 2028.The reduced requirement of £6.4m reflects some minor impacts on the contract programme due to the exceptional weather conditions which has meant that the projected expenditure profile would not be realised for this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department is taking to introduce safeguards for children with allergies, similar to the principles of Benedict’s Law.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 41034/22-27 published in the Weekly Answer Booklet for week ending 6th March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his department is taking to provide additional safeguards for children with allergies in schools.
answer_text: Schools have a responsibility to follow the “Supporting pupils with Medication Needs” guidance and make reasonable adjustments to ensure that the needs of pupils with medical needs, including severe allergies/anaphylaxis, are met. First Aid and Administration of Medication policies should be in place.The relevant health and social care professional involved in supporting the medical needs of a child or young person will provide a detailed plan, agreed with the parents and school, which outlines the allergies and the medication and actions required for both mild and severe reactions.  These Individual Healthcare Plans are vital to helping school staff understand the pupil’s anaphylaxis, ensure their safety and inclusion in school activities.   Schools can seek training, with a school nurse and/or relevant medical professional, relating to the individual child’s needs.   EA Catering Service operate a range of measures to safeguard children with allergies who use the school meals service including:screening of menus for allergenic content and documentation of sameannual allergen training for catering staffthe operation of a special diets process to support children and young people with medical conditions, including food allergies.The Catering Service continue to review their allergen management processes to ensure they are fit for purpose, compliant, and meet the needs of children and young people and is finalising a review of the Special Diet Application form.The Education Authority (EA) Health and Safety Service provides training for school staff to qualify in First Aid at Work. First aiders will be competent to recognise symptoms and deliver appropriate first aid to those children suffering a severe allergic reaction until the arrival of qualified medical personnel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any process by which the Legal Services Agency can use the Criminal Legal Aid (Recovery of Defence Costs Orders) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2012, to recover legal aid costs; (ii) the number of times the Agency has used these orders including the amount recovered by the Agency.
answer_text: (i) The Criminal Legal Aid (Recovery of Defence Costs Orders) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2012 provide for Recovery of Defence Costs Orders to be made against legally-aided defendants who have been convicted in the Crown Court. An order will be sought if it comes to the attention of the Legal Services Agency that the convicted defendants financial circumstances could result in such an order. An order cannot be made against a convicted defendant who: (a) is under 18 years of age; (b) is directly or indirectly in receipt of prescribed benefits; (c) does not have capital over £3,000; equity in their principal residence of over £100,000; or a gross annual income of over £22,235. It is for the court to decide whether an order should be made and the sum which should be recovered. The court will not make an order if it is satisfied that it would be unreasonable to do so on the basis of the information and evidence available, and if the payment of such an order would, owing to the exceptional circumstances of the case, involve undue financial headship in respect of the assisted person, their dependants or immediate family. (ii) The Agency has been granted 5 recovery of defence costs orders and has recovered £87,237.17. In addition, a further £383,585.44 is subject to enforcement proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road defects that can be repaired through the £7.85 million Winter Recovery Road Fund.
answer_text: I am aware that the recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have all taken their toll on our roads, and I have been working to identify and secure funding to address the problem. I can confirm that I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects which are causing the most concern. It will go towards a mixture of  patching and  resurfacing in areas of greatest need across the north and where those works can commence without delay.As the materials, specifications, quantities and methods used vary from location to location there is no single method or technique used in the repair of surface defects and as such it is not possible to provide a single unit cost for repairs carried out by either our internal or external contractors.For the same reason, it is not possible to determine how many potholes the recently announced additional funding of £7.85m will address, but the funding will be used on a priority basis, targeting the most severe potholes causing the highest safety concerns for the Department.Given the scale of the damage caused, it is not possible to address every issue in the short-term. However, I have also asked my officials to also explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible.I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads and the Winter Recovery Road Fund will allow vital repairs to the worst affected areas to be carried out in a focused way and completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the budget for the Warm Healthy Homes Fund, for each of the next five years; and (ii) the number of properties that will be upgraded.
answer_text: My Officials are currently developing the final scheme design for the Fund, and this will be further shaped by public consultation in the next few months.(i) Budget: My Department has submitted a bid of £150m to support the Warm Healthy Homes Fund, enabling it to deliver energy efficiency improvements at scale and increase investment for low income and vulnerable households most affected by fuel poverty. (ii) Number of properties: The number of homes that can be upgraded each year will be informed by both the final budget allocation alongside modelling to identify the most cost-effective measures and appropriate grant limit per home. These elements will be informed by the upcoming public consultation. The policy intent remains to prioritise support for low-income and vulnerable households living in the poorest-performing properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amounts contained in the offer letters sent to the 20 performance club projects that are being progressed under the Northern Ireland Football Fund’s Performance Programme, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Ten clubs have previously received business case funding from the Football Ground Business Case Development Fund and have been offered up to £10,000 to update their business cases.The remaining 10 clubs have been offered up to £30,000 to develop a new business case from scratch.Please see below table showing a breakdown of resource funding offered by constituency.ConstituencyNumber of applicants offered up to £10K resource funding Number of applicants offered up to £30K resource funding Total (£)North Antrim1010,000North Down1010.000East Antrim2020,000Fermanagh & South Tyrone1140,000Upper Bann2280,000Newry & Armagh1140,000Belfast North110,000Belfast East110,000Lagan Valley390,000West Tyrone130,000Belfast South & Mid Down130.000South Down130.000

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW38956/22-27, to detail (i) whether the minutes of the discussions on Education Authority’s performance at the Governance and Accountability Review meetings have been published; (ii) the dates on which these meeting have taken place; (iii) the attendees at these meetings.
answer_text: (i) Minutes from the Education Authority (EA) Governance and Accountability (GAR) Meetings are not routinely published. The Department monitors the performance of the EA through a range of mechanisms including at the GAR meetings, quarterly business plan monitoring, and a range of policy oversight meetings. The EA also publishes its performance in a number of ways, including its Corporate Business Plans and Annual Reports, available within the performance analysis section at How we do it | Education Authority Northern Ireland and Annual Reports. Discussion of the EA’s performance also takes place at its Performance & Engagement Standing Committee, the Minutes for which are available at Performance & Engagement Standing Committee Minutes | Education Authority Northern Ireland.(ii)  The EA GAR meeting takes place three times each financial year. For the 2025/26 Financial Year, these were held on 30 June and 19 November 2025 and the next GAR is due to be held on 26 March 2026.(iii)  The EA GAR meetings are attended by the EA Chief Executive and Chairperson and supported by relevant members of its Corporate Leadership Team. The GAR is chaired by the Permanent Secretary and supported by the relevant Grade 3 or Grade 5, as dictated by the agenda items. Beginning in 2025/26, I have chaired one EA GAR per year, the first of which took place on 19 November 2025, which was also attended by my Special Advisor. Officials from both EA and DE will also attend GAR meetings to provide administrative support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) monitoring; and (ii) quality assurance, that takes place to ensure that the fish stocked in our rivers and lakes by his Department are healthy.
answer_text: Daily monitoring at Movanagher fish farm assesses fish health and condition and identifies any need for intervention. Additionally, two independent veterinary inspections take place annually. 	Quality assurance is maintained by verifying the condition of fish prior to stocking and by ensuring strict compliance with the Movanagher stocking protocol before any fish are transferred to the Public Angling Estate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the timescale for the formation of the Just Transition Commission.
answer_text: Following engagement with my Executive colleagues the draft Regulations to establish the Commission were shared with the AERA Committee on 5 February 2026, and officials are scheduled to brief the Committee on 12 March 2026 following which arrangements will be made to lay the draft Regulations in the Assembly for further scrutiny and debate. It is anticipated that the debate, subject to other Assembly business, should take place during April / May.Once the Regulations are agreed by the Assembly and become law my Department will then be able to commence the process to appoint members to the Commission in line with the Commissioner for Public Appointments Northern Ireland (CPANI) guidance. Given this appointment’s process will be to appoint all members it will take several months to complete from start to finish but I am committed to having the Commission formed within the current mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who will be eligible for the respiratory syncytial virus vaccine, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are several RSV vaccination programmes in operation in Northern Ireland, this includes:the high-risk infant vaccination programme,a vaccination programme for older adults aged 75 to 79 years of age, anda vaccination programme for all pregnant women.It is not possible to detail the number of eligible patients broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, mainly because we cannot quantify how many children may come into the at-risk group nor can we predict the exact number of pregnant women.I have also recently announced the expansion of the RSV vaccination programme for older adults, which from 1 April 2026 will be extended to all individuals aged 80 years of age and over, as well as all residents in care homes for older adults.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the process for elected representatives to engage with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency on planning applications in their area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons why the Planning Appeals Commission has not accepted updated information and survey data, relevant to the determination of the consent and abstraction applications, as part of the Dalradian Public Local Inquiry.
answer_text: The Planning Appeals Commission is an independent body responsible for managing the procedural aspects of the Public Local Inquiry, including decisions on the acceptance of evidence. Any determination on whether updated information or survey data should be admitted falls within the PAC’s remit and not my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the medicine lines that are currently in short supply; (ii) any actions he plans to take to mitigate medicine shortages.
answer_text: There are over 14,000 licensed medicines in the UK, and the majority are in good supply and are available in sufficient quantities to meet patient needs. However, medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons such as manufacturing issues or increases in demand.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK. Under the Health Service Products (Provision and Disclosure of Information) Regulations 2018, marketing authorisation holders are obliged to share information with DHSC about the discontinuation of any medicine, or any anticipated shortages that will directly affect UK patients. Most medicine supply notifications to DHSC are risk assessed as having a low impact on UK patients or the healthcare system as alternative manufacturers are able to meet the supply gap in full, and so no clinical review or amendment to prescriptions is required. Marketing authorisation holders share this information in confidence and therefore, it is not possible to list all the medicines that are experiencing supply disruptions. However, where supply disruptions are identified as having a potential impact on patients or the healthcare system, advice and guidance is provided to healthcare professionals on the steps they can take to ensure patients have access to appropriate treatments. Advice and guidance can be issued to HSC healthcare professionals via National Patient Safety Alerts (NatPSA) or Medicine Supply Notifications (MSN) depending on the assigned risk associated with the medicine shortage. NatPSAs are publicly available via my Department’s website, https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/hssmd-letters-and-urgent-communications, while MSNs are publicly available via the Business Services Organisation’s (BSO) website,  https://bso.hscni.net/directorates/operations/family-practitioner-services/pharmacy/contractor-information/drug-tariff-and-related-materials/shortages-information/. The information shared via NatPSAs and MSNs are correct at the time of issue and publication, however, HSC healthcare professionals can register with the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) and access the SPS medicines supply tool which provides the most up-to-date information on medicines that are in short supply including, actions they can take, anticipated resupply dates, available alternatives and other clinical information. Information on the SPS medicines supply tool is not publicly available.Communicating to healthcare professionals is one of the well-established tools and processes that can be applied to ensure patients have access to appropriate treatments. Other actions that can be applied to mitigate the impact of medicine shortages include placing items on a list of medicines that cannot be exported from the UK or hoarded. This ensures all medicines within the UK can be made avai

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timescale for the replacement pitch at Castlederg High School, as part of the Your School Your Club programme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI has made an award of £270,726 under the Your School Your Club programme for a replacement pitch surface at Castlederg High School and are currently in the process of appointing a specialist provider to manage the delivery of the project on behalf of the school.“The expectation, at this stage and subject to any unforeseen delays, is that the project will be substantially complete by 31 March 2026.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the February 2026 Northern Ireland Public Service Ombudsman report Investigation of a Complaint against a GP Practice; and (ii) whether he has any plans to review the current guidelines.
answer_text: I welcome the Northern Ireland Public Service Ombudsman’s publication of the Overview Report on the Removal of Patients from their General Practice Service and the consideration given to this important issue.I fully recognise the importance of appropriate access to General Medical Services provided by GP practices under the GMS Contract, and the vital service delivered by GPs across Northern Ireland.I acknowledge NIPSO’s recognition of steps already taken by my Department to address some of the concerns identified in the report. My Department will give due regard to the three recommendations detailed in the NIPSO report as part of its ongoing work to ensure that all patients have access to the General Practice services they need

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any schemes and level of funding in NI through the PEAT+ programme over the next three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Peat Plus programme is funded through the SEUPB PEACEPLUS programme and provides an opportunity for collaboration between landowners, project partners, and statutory agencies across Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.		The programme will primarily focus on peatland restoration in Northern Ireland and the border counties of the Republic of Ireland.  Specific funding amounts by constituency are not available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Business Demography Northern Ireland 2024 statistics, in particular the high number of business deaths in Production and Retail.
answer_text: The latest Business Demography statistics were released on 03 February by NISRA and indicate that in 2024 there were 6,240 business births in the North, which is a large increase of 16.2% on the previous year, and 4,790 business deaths, a decrease of 3.6% over the same period. In relation to Production and Retail, while these sectors did report a high number of business deaths, these were offset in both instances by the number of business births, with the increases in births for both sectors among the highest reported across the broad industry groups reported on.Business demography statistics are closely monitored by my department and are important economic indicators of business dynamism, which itself is a key measure of the health of an economy and a key contributor to productivity, one of my four economic priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the reason why public sector bodies are excluded from drawing down the UK wide apprenticeship in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether she intends to change this policy during the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Whilst eligible private and public sector organisations in the North are liable to pay the British Government’s Apprenticeship Levy, apprenticeship policy and spending matters are devolved. My Department funds the off the job training element of an apprenticeship to the value of almost £50 million per year.  Departmental budget pressures mean that it is not possible to fund both private and public sector apprenticeships (PSAs).  Nevertheless, I will continue to make the case for increased funding for skills and apprenticeships here.While my Department does not currently fund the training aspect of PSAs, we have committed to exploring what is possible in the Apprenticeship Action Plan, building on a small number of successful public sector apprenticeship programmes. My officials are working with public sector employers to support the development of new apprenticeship programmes. We are keen to collaborate, to build the skills and talent pipeline for all public service employers here through the implementation of an effective PSA programme. My officials are keen to further engage with public sector employers to explore how we can maximise apprenticeship opportunities within the current funding framework and shape our policy in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of complaints received by the NI Ambulance Service, broken down by stage; and (ii) the number of complaints upheld, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The number of complaints received by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), over the last five years is outlined in Table 1.Table 1Year	2021/22	2022/23	2023/24	2024/25	2025/26*Number of complaints received	266	208	241	276	247Source: Northern Ireland Ambulance Service*2025/26 includes complaints reported between 1 April 2025 to 31 January 2026NIAS does not record/hold complaint information by constituency area, and therefore it is not possible to provide a breakdown by constituency.The Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman’s (NIPSO) Model Complaints Handling Procedure (MCHP), implemented on 1 January 2026, introduced a two-stage approach, therefore no data exists to distinguish stages prior to this date.  Further details on the MCHP is available on the NIPSO website: https://www.nipso.org.uk/service-providers/complaints-standards/model-complaints-handling-procedures Since the implementation of the MCHP on 1 January 2026, NIAS have received 30 complaints, of which eight are currently at stage one and eight at stage two. However, it should be noted that as the investigation progresses, if it is assessed as more complex or the complainant remains dissatisfied after stage one, the complaint will proceed to stage two. With regard to the number of complaints upheld, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency, prior to the introduction of MCHP there was no requirement to record whether a complaint was upheld and therefore this information is also only available from 1 January 2026. From this date, up until 31 January 2026, 30 complaints were received. 14 were closed, of which four were upheld. As previously noted, NIAS is unable to provide a breakdown by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to Habitats Regulations Overview Report for the Reporting Period 2019-2024 Northern Ireland, to detail (i) the 48 bird species on the Red List; and (ii) any plans he has to reduce this number.
answer_text: The 48 species on the UK Birds of Conservation Concern (BoCC) Red list which currently regularly occur in Northern Ireland are listed here:SpeciesScientific nameArctic Skua Stercorarius parasiticusArctic Tern Sterna paradisaeaBlack-tailed Godwit Limosa limosaCommon Gull Larus canusCommon Scoter Melanitta nigraCorncrake Crex crexCuckoo Cuculus canorusCurlew Numenius arquataDunlin Calidris alpinaFieldfare Turdus pilarisGoldeneye Bucephala clangulaGrasshopper Warbler Locustella naeviaGreat Black-backed Gull Larus marinusGreat Skua Stercorarius skuaGreenfinch Carduelis chlorisGreenland White-fronted Goose Anser albifronsHen Harrier Circus cyaneusHerring GullLarus argentatusHouse Martin Delichon urbicumHouse Sparrow Passer domesticusKittiwake Rissa tridactylaLapwing Vanellus vanellusLinnet Carduelis cannabinaLong-tailed Duck Clangula hyemalisMerlinFalco columbariusMistle Thrush Turdus viscivorusPochard Aythya ferinaPuffin Fratercula arcticaPurple Sandpiper Calidris maritimaRedpoll Acanthis flammeaRinged Plover Charadrius hiaticulaRoseate Tern Sterna dougalliiRuffCalidris pugnaxScaup Aythya marilaSkylark Alauda arvensisSlavonian Grebe Podiceps auritusSmew Mergellus albellusSpotted Flycatcher Muscicapa striataStarlingSturnus vulgarisSwift Apus apusTree Sparrow Passer montanusTwite Carduelis flavirostrisVelvet Scoter Melanitta fuscaWhimbrel Numenius phaeopusWhinchatSaxicola rubetraWood Warbler Phylloscopus sibilatrixWoodcock Scolopax rusticolaYellowhammer Emberiza citrinellaProactive actions have been taken forward through agri-environment schemes and other delivery mechanisms including the Environment Fund. DAERA recently launched a consultation on a draft Nature Recovery Strategy, to help drive the restoration and regeneration of biodiversity; this strategy will complement delivery of my Environmental Improvement Plan and successful implementation will help improve the conservation status of many of the birds on the UK Birds of Conservation Concern Red list. Officials are also considering how to optimise delivery of elements of the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995 with respect to wild birds. It should be noted that BoCC conservation status is a UK-wide assessment and actions in Northern Ireland alone may not necessarily improve that overall status.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 24040/22-27, for an update on discussions regarding a Park and Ride facility at Bradley Way, Strabane.
answer_text: My Department has identified the potential for further Park and Ride facilities in the vicinity of the existing Bus Station in Strabane on Bradley Way where the existing parking is often at capacity.My officials continue to consider options for Park and Ride sites as part of the development of the draft Regional Strategic Transport Plan and the priority for further sites including Strabane will be influenced by the outcome of the Plan.  Development of future priority sites identified as part of this process will be dependent on the availability of appropriate funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to introduce restrictions on the sale and use of glue-type traps.
answer_text: During the development of my Animal Welfare Pathway, I met with representatives from animal welfare organisations and they were asked to highlight areas where they felt reform was most needed. None of these organisations highlighted a pressing requirement to prohibit the use of glue traps.I fully empathise with the concern over the use of glue-type traps. However, unfortunately I have no plans to legislate to restrict their sale and use in Northern Ireland within the remainder of this, already constrained, mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Habitats Regulations Overview Report for the Reporting Period 2019-2024 Northern Ireland, to detail (i) the 30 Annex I habitats being attributed Unfavourable to Bad Conservation Status; and (ii) any plans he has to reduce this number.
answer_text: i. 39 Annex I habitats (37 terrestrial, 2 marine) have been reported as having Unfavourable-Bad Conservation Status in the 2019-2024 Habitats Regulations Reports for Northern Ireland. These are summarised in table form in Appendix 1 – Summary Results (from page 204) of the Habitats Regulations Overview Report for the Reporting Period 2019-2024 Northern Ireland https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2026-01/Habitats%20Regulations%20Overview%20Report%20for%20the%20Reporting%20Period%202019-2024%20Northern%20Ireland%20-%20Terrestrial%20and%20Marine%20Habitats%20and%20non-bird%20Species_1.PDF  ii. The Habitats Regulations reports are one of the most comprehensive assessments of the UK's protected habitats and species. They provide a national context of how these natural assets are faring and Northern Ireland’s progress in conserving them. The reports both look at what has been achieved and point to where further action is needed. Over the 2019-2024 reporting cycle, proactive actions have included: co-ordination and funding of agri-environment schemes, Conservation Management Plans, and cross border nature restoration initiatives. Moving forward particular emphasis will be placed on the draft Nature Recovery Strategy which is currently out for consultation. The draft Strategy contains five strategic objectives and clear actions to help deliver them over and above existing targets within other policies and strategies such as my Environmental Improvement Plan. Successful implementation of these plans will result in improvement in reported Conservation Status in the future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the reason why only 28 per cent of apprentices are women, up from 26.7 per cent since 2019; and (ii) any steps she will take to increase the number of women doing apprenticeships.
answer_text: While women remain underrepresented in a number of sector apprenticeships, my Department is actively addressing this imbalance. The latest published statistics show that female participation in the ApprenticeshipsNI programme more than doubled between October 2020 and October 2025, rising from 1,804 to 3,756.Female participation in Higher Lever Apprenticeships is higher, with 35% of females undertaking a HLA at Further Education college and 39% at a Higher Education institution. One key intervention is the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund which specifically promotes uptake from three underrepresented groups across apprenticeship programmes, including women.  Projects under this Challenge Fund include Women’s Tec, which is targeting low female representation in sectors like construction, engineering and mechanics, using a holistic cross-sectoral approach. Workplus leads a second project focused on supply and demand barriers to greater female participation for women in software apprenticeships. Both projects run until late summer 2026 and will be evaluated to inform future apprenticeships policy.The introduction of All Age Apprenticeships in September 2023 has provided flexible entry points for individuals of all ages, helping more women return to the workforce and consider careers in skilled trades. In addition, the Department is increasing the visibility of female apprentices in promotional materials to challenge stereotypes and encourage broader participation. Careers Advisers also play a key role by providing impartial advice and guidance, encouraging individuals to explore all career options and actively challenge gender stereotypes in occupational choices.These actions are underpinned by the Department’s recently published three-year Apprenticeship Action Plan, which includes a specific focus on inclusion, ensuring that all sectors of society, including women, are supported to access and benefit from apprenticeship opportunities. While gender representation in apprenticeships often reflects wider employment trends, the Department remains committed to promoting diversity and inclusion across all apprenticeship pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to reform or review the law, regarding the statue of limitations, during the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: I am responding on the assumption that the question relates to the six-month time limit (from the commission of the offence), within which summary offences can be brought to the magistrates’ court, under the Magistrates’ Court (Northern Ireland) Order 1981. Article 19(1)(a) of the Magistrates’ Court (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 states:“a magistrates' court shall not have jurisdiction to hear and determine a complaint charging the commission of a summary offence other than an offence which is also triable upon indictment unless the complaint was made within six months from the time when the offence was committed or ceased to continue…;”The same position generally applies in the rest of the UK.Whilst sympathetic to recent representations received with regards prosecution of potential instances of historical physical abuse, I do not currently have the resources to undertake such a review or reform in the current Assembly mandate, given the significant resource and time constraints faced by my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail for how long has he halted the NI Reval2026 process.
answer_text: As I set out in my statement to the Assembly on 2 February 2026, I decided to stop Reval2026 for a full examination of the issues. I will take time to consider evidence before deciding next steps. My full statement is available at:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions his Department has had with UK Government Ministers regarding women from Northern Ireland who were born in the 1950s, and who were impacted by increases to their retirement age.
answer_text: On 17 December 2024, I had a call with Emma Reynolds MP (Parliamentary Secretary at the Department of Work and Pensions) on this matter. 		This was in advance of the Secretary of State, Liz Kendall MP, making her announcement in the House of Commons that whilst the UK Government accepted the Ombudsman’s finding of maladministration, they would not be awarding compensation to the women affected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of statutory planning consultation requests that are currently outstanding within the NI Environment Agency; (ii) the number of these that have exceeded the statutory consultation period.
answer_text: Across the NIEA and MFD, from 01 April 2025 – 03 February 2026, there are currently 576 consultations awaiting a response, including 526 Statutory and 50 Non-Statutory planning consultations. As of the 03 February 2026, there are 364 statutory planning consultation that have exceeded the statutory consultation period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any reasons why the recycling rate for household waste reduced by 0.2 per cent in the latest figure produced by his Department.
answer_text: The recently released quarterly Local Authority Collected Municipal (LACM) Waste Statistics highlight a slight fall in our household recycling rate, although quarterly provisional estimates are subject to change. The LACM Annual Reports contain finalised data and supersede provisional quarterly estimates.In recent years, our recycling rates have stagnated. However, my proposals under the two workstreams of the ‘Rethinking Our Resources programme (Waste and Recycling Reforms and the draft Waste Management Strategy) aim to elevate recycling rates across households, businesses and public and third sectors.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to encourage local councils to meet the target of 35 per cent of 1995 levels of biodegradable waste going to landfill, which currently no local council achieves.
answer_text: The EU Landfill Directive set a target of 35 per cent of 1995 levels of biodegradable waste going to landfill by 2020. This equated to 220,000 tonnes.The actual figure for biodegradable waste going to landfill in 2019/20 was 126,286 tonnes, passing the target set.Progress is continuing; 71,828 tonnes of biodegradable waste were sent to landfill in 2024/2025.DAERA continues to work with councils to accelerate diversion from landfill and support Northern Ireland’s wastesector climate commitments.My officials are exploring options to further limit the amount of biodegradable waste entering landfill sites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: At present there are 40 whole-time equivalent staff that are currently employed by the NI Environment Agency on statutory planning consultation work. This total fluctuates over time due to maternity leave, temporary promotions, availability of temporary workers and other factors. Teams have a complement of 7 agency staff overall.
