question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) any discussions between his Department and Independent Health Care Providers; and (ii) any plans he has to fund a real living wage as part of his budget planning for 2026/27.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of teachers that have used Article 22 of The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 to withdraw from (i) teaching Religious Education; and (ii) attending collective worship, such as assemblies, since the order was enacted, broken down by year.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.Requests of this nature must be made at school level.  Therefore, any records would be held by the Boards of Governors of individual schools rather than centrally by Education Authority or my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the recent HM Fire Service Inspectorate Report on the Independent Inspection of the Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue Service (NIFRS), to detail any further actions his Department will take to improve on the 37-40 per cent compliance in the alignment of NIFRS policy, and associated training, to National Operational Guidance.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) to improve Service compliance.  NIFRS 3-year alignment schedule aims for 90% compliance with National Guidance. This is planned for achievement by the end of the 2027 business planning year.  This schedule is risk based and prioritised, ensuring that work is focused on those areas which carry the greatest risk to firefighter and public safety. The review process for Operational Guidance is also integrated into the continued improvement of operational training programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the recent HM Fire Service Inspectorate Report on the Independent Inspection of the Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue Service, why his Department has not complied with recommendation 6, that a clear set of priorities should be issued by Government in the form of a Fire Framework that sets expectations and priorities for the coming years.
answer_text: My department is compliant with recommendation 6.  An advanced draft Fire Framework is under active review.  The recent HM Fire Service Inspectorate Report acknowledged that whilst this recommendation has not yet been fully actioned, the department’s intention is to publish within this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his assessment of the PIPS Suicide Prevention Ireland letter sent to MLAs that claims only 16 per cent of the 10-year Mental Health Strategy has been resourced; and (ii) how he is going to fully fund the strategy.
answer_text: I acknowledge the concerns raised by PIPS and other stakeholders regarding the findings of the Mental Health Strategy Review of Deliverability for 2026–2029. The review confirmed that no additional funding had been provided to the Department to implement the Strategy and that, as at the end of the 2024/25 financial year, only 16% of the funding expected at that point had been allocated from existing resources.  The review was therefore undertaken to help us refocus and prioritise actions in the context of the Department’s current funding constraints, while reaffirming that the full Strategy remains essential and is a key vehicle for bringing about changes to mental health services.This review does not represent a dilution of commitment to delivering for mental health, but rather a sharpening of focus to maximise impact within the resources available.Mental Health Workforce and the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service have been identified as immediate priorities for 2026/27 with both seen as crucial enablers for broader system improvement.Whilst longer term planning has commenced, the quantum of the funding gap in 2025-26 has meant there has been no capacity to direct additional funding to mental health services. A bid to secure additional recurrent funding for mental health services has been submitted by the Department to the Department of Finance as part of the multi-year budget exercise.However, it is expected that the 2026-27 funding position will be as challenging as the current year with the funding position in the following two years expected to improve.I have been clear that delivering the Strategy requires sustained and additional investment and I will continue to make the case for mental health until the ambition so clearly set out in 2021 is fully realised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of existing co-operatives, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the specific information you requested. The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) serves as the registrar for co-operatives in Britain and the North, maintaining a mutuals register on their website where you can find details about co-operatives, including their locations.According to the latest report from Co-operatives UK, there are 241 registered co-operatives in the North. They also offer a directory featuring an interactive map that displays the locations of active co-operative businesses throughout the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Environmental Improvement Plan Annual Progress Report September 2024-September 2025, to detail any actions he plans to take to reduce the seven indicator trends where there is currently no data or indicator available.
answer_text: My Department will continue to implement the actions detailed within the Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP). It should be noted that the Annual Progress Report (APR) contains several newly established environmental schemes and therefore data will not be available until a later point in the EIP’s delivery. My officials are undertaking a lessons learned exercise of the APR process to identify improvements to data collation and reporting. My officials will also be cognisant of any recommendations or indicators made by the Office for Environmental Protection (OEP), following their review of the APR by summer 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Environmental Improvement Plan Annual Progress Report September 2024-September 2025, to detail any actions he plans to take in relation to the 23 indicators where the environment has had little or no change.
answer_text: My Department will continue to implement the actions detailed within the Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP). The Annual Progress Report (APR) better enables the Department to identify areas of limited change and focus delivery in future years. It is important to note the change across some indicators will take a longer period to be observable throughout the lifetime of the EIP. My officials will also be cognisant of any recommendations made by the Office for Environmental Protection, following their review of the APR by summer 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Environmental Improvement Plan Annual Progress Report September 2024-September 2025, to detail any actions he plans to take in relation to the seven indicator trends where there has been a decline in the environment.
answer_text: My Department will continue to implement the actions detailed within the Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP). It is a long-term plan and forms the basis for a coherent and effective set of interventions that can deliver real improvements in the quality of the environment.It is important to note the change across some indicators will take a longer period to be observable throughout the lifetime of the EIP. Equally, it will take a longer period to reverse trends that have been in prolonged environmental decline. My officials will also be cognisant of any recommendations made by the Office for Environmental Protection, following their review of the Annual Progress Report by summer 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of staff who will be responsible for dealing with enquires about the NI Reval26 programme; and (ii) the number of those staff who have experience of previous revaluations.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has a dedicated team handling Reval2026 enquiries, including experienced valuers who have worked on previous revaluations.Since publication of the Reval 2026 draft values I have listened carefully to the concerns of the hospitality sector about the implications of implementing the revised values from 1 April 2026.  I have taken the decision to stop Reval 2026 to create space for sectors to provide the evidence that will allow a full examination of the issues raised.  Until then rate bills will continue to be based on the 2023 Valuation List.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any lessons learnt by his Department from recent bird flu outbreaks; and (ii) the level of compliance by farmers and the public when restrictions have been put in place.
answer_text: My Department is currently reviewing its response to the recent bird flu outbreaks.  Any lessons learnt will be implemented, where appropriate, to ensure the continued effectiveness in our preparations and response to epizootic disease incursions. 	I welcome the high compliance by farmers and the public when restrictions have been put in place due to bird flu outbreaks.  The commercial poultry industry in Northern Ireland is known to practice high standards of biosecurity and this is reflected in the prompt reporting and quick actions progressed to stamp our incursions of the disease when it has occurred.  		The Department is aware of a small number of complaints about non-compliances and where this was notified to officials, they were quickly investigated and compliance was achieved.  No prosecutions have been taken forward by my Department in relation to non-compliance with the first steps being education and encouragement to adhere to the necessary standards to protect individual flocks and the wider industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of grit boxes in West Tyrone, broken down by District Electoral Area.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining over 5,800 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.The table below provides details of the number of salt boxes maintained within the last year broken down by section office which broadly aligns to local council area. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to lever in finance from emerging private markets, to assist with restoring nature.
answer_text: My officials are engaging with partners across the public and private sector to make best use of available sources of funding and will continue to develop links with the private sector as we progress climate objectives.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the responsibilities of (a) his Department; and (b) the Education Authority (EA), for ensuring that EA-funded Special Educational Needs Classroom Assistant support is delivered by trained and competent staff; (ii) his assessment of whether there is adequate oversight within this process.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) does not have direct responsibility for the recruitment or training of school staff however DE and the Education Authority (EA) work collaboratively to ensure that Special Educational Needs (SEN) Classroom Assistant staff are both competent and appropriately trained to carry out their duties. The SEN Reform Agenda contains a range of actions which will stabilise and professionalise the Classroom Assistant workforce through the development of a new employment model, a professional learning programme, and the establishment of career pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any sanctions faced by her Department for not acting within the boundaries of its licence, as arbitrated by the Office of Environmental Protection.
answer_text: I welcome the Office for Environmental Protection’s (OEP) investigation into the regulation of sewage discharges into Belfast Lough. This investigation is ongoing and my Department will fully co-operate with the OEP.  Once the investigation has completed, I will consider the recommendations and take forward any necessary actions. However, it is important to note that it is the Department of Environment, Agriculture and Rural Affairs’ NI Environment Agency that regulates the sewage discharges into Belfast Lough and can and has prosecuted NI Water for water pollution offences.I will of course continue to address wastewater issues through the three-pronged approach of securing adequate funding for water and wastewater infrastructure from the Executive, bringing forward the Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill and the consideration of developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the application by Artigarvan Primary School for a new school roof.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that the roof upgrade at Artigarvan Primary School has been scheduled for the 2026/27 financial year, contingent upon the availability of capital funding.The project involves the removal of asbestos-containing materials. These works will be scheduled during the summer when the school is vacant to minimise disruption and ensure safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any mechanisms his Department has in place to monitor the performance of the Education Authority; and (ii) any assessment that has been made of the adequacy of these mechanisms.
answer_text: My Department has a number of mechanisms in place for monitoring the performance of the Education Authority (EA) in delivering its key functions.  Annually the EA is asked to develop a Corporate Business Plan which sets out its strategic priorities for the forthcoming year.  The Plan, which I approve, provides a key mechanism for the EA to report on how it is progressing against its priorities at in-year and end-year review stages.In addition, the EA has been providing the Department with a quarterly Corporate Performance Report, which contains the latest data for a number of key metrics which EA considers to be the most high-impact, child-centric measures available. This is supported by a much wider range of metrics across all areas of EA’s delivery. In 2025-26, the EA has been developing a comprehensive corporate performance reporting system which aims to bring together Business Plan and Corporate Performance reporting. My Department has been engaging closely with the EA in this process to ensure that future reporting provides sufficient assurances (for both the Department and the EA internally) on delivery of all key priorities.At the end of each financial year the EA’s Annual Report and Accounts is laid before the Northern Ireland Assembly, outlining the organisation's performance.  This is also summarised in the Department’s Annual Report and Accounts.Discussions on EA’s performance take place at the Governance and Accountability Review meetings, which take place three times per year. These meetings are Chaired either by me or the Departmental Accounting Officer.The 2022 EA Landscape Review considered performance reporting and the mechanisms my Department use to derive assurances from them. Overall, the Review provided an assurance that these were appropriate and noted the improvements made to performance reporting in recent years. Nonetheless, my Department and EA officers continue to work together to refine and improve these mechanisms following the Review’s recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Cancer Research UK document No Time To Wait.
answer_text: I welcome Cancer Research UK’s publication of the ‘No Time to Wait’ report and acknowledge their continued engagement and strong commitment to improving cancer outcomes in Northern Ireland.While my Department remains focused on improving cancer waiting times, current performance is affected by increasing referral volumes, constraints in diagnostic and treatment capacity, and workforce pressures across key specialties. Departmental officials are working closely with Health and Social Care Trusts to address these challenges through proactive patient tracking, targeted pathway optimisation, performance oversight, workforce planning, system collaboration and improved data transparency.Significant investment is being delivered through the Elective Care Framework and the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032. This includes expanded endoscopy and diagnostic capacity, alongside targeted support across priority tumour sites. These measures, delivered in partnership with system stakeholders, support sustainable service transformation of cancer services.The Cancer Research UK report highlights the complexity of cancer care and reinforces the need for sustained system-wide action. There are no quick fixes; however reducing long waits, prioritising patients at highest risk, and redesigning services to meet future demand remain clear priorities for my Department and the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to upgrade (i) Strabane; and (ii) Omagh, bus centre.
answer_text: (i) Strabane; Strabane Bus Depot has recently had a new site wide emergency lighting system installed. There were also significant safety upgrades complete in December 2021 which included resurfacing of the car park, installation of safety protection bollards, new automated barrier installations, drainage replacement and upgrades, increased lighting levels in line with current safety standards, new line markings and updated signage.   (ii) Omagh, Omagh Bus Centre has recently had a new site wide emergency lighting system installed. Translink have initiated a project for potential enhancement works to Omagh Bus Station.   In addition to the above, Translink have recently received planning permission for a new £19m south western regional engineering depot on the existing site at Tamlaght Road. Subject to the availability of funding, work could commence on the new deport during 2027.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the Delivery Unit.
answer_text: Tackling waiting lists is an Executive priority which is why the Delivery Unit has been focussed on ongoing work with the Department of Health to reduce hospital waiting times.The Unit has also been supporting the implementation of the Programme for Government commitment to the reform and transformation of public services. The Unit has been overseeing delivery of six funded transformation projects and has been supporting the Public Sector Transformation Board in assessing a range of additional proposals. The Unit will continue to play a central role in ensuring that public sector transformation projects fulfil their potential and that connections are made across individual projects. The Unit has also been engaging with a range of other stakeholders to identify projects where it can assist and make a positive difference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have consented to donate organs, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Data on organ donation is collated by NHS Blood and Transplant, and they have confirmed that 165 out of 272 deceased donors in Northern Ireland consented to organ donation in the last five years, as follows:FinancialYear	2020/21	2021/22	2022/23	2023/24	2024/25	TotalExpressed consent	26	31	32	45	31	165Total donors	51	54	59	64	44	272It should be noted that expressed consent is defined as when the individual expressed a decision to donate via any method, either verbally or via the Organ Donor Register and it was therefore their last known decision on organ donation.In cases where an individual had not expressed their decision to donate, a discussion would always have taken place with the family and donation would only have proceeded if consent was given by the family.  Since the introduction of legislation in 2023, consent can either be expressed or deemed; however, a discussion would still take place with the family to try to establish the last known decision of the individual.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the 2,350 organisations that received £7.91million in grants through the (i) Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme; and (ii) Rural Business Development Grant Scheme, broken down by amount and constituency.
answer_text: 1,048 voluntary and community organisations were awarded grants totalling £1.74m through the 2025/26 Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme. A full list of these organisations and funding award, broken down by constituency, has been placed in the Assembly Library.£6.17m funding was also awarded to 1,310 businesses through the 2025/26 Rural Business Development Grant Scheme.  A full list of these businesses and funding award, broken down by constituency, has been placed in the Assembly Library.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of complaints received by her Department, regarding unofficial billboards on her Department's land, for each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold records on the number of complaints specifically in relation to unofficial billboards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35346/22-27, (i) for an update on the evaluation of the site inspection on Kelvin Road and Kelvin Gardens; and (ii) whether remedial action is needed to mitigate against flooding.
answer_text: My officials have inspected Kelvin Road and Kelvin Gardens on three separate occasions during wet weather conditions and no evidence of flooding was observed. However, my officials would be happy to meet with you on site to discuss the matter further.  This can be arranged by contacting them at FermanaghandOmaghEast@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the current average business rates level for each local council area; and (ii) the estimated average rates levels for the next three years should his Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30 recommendations be followed.
answer_text: There is no meaningful concept of an “average” non-domestic rate bill, as the non-domestic sector covers a wide and diverse range of property classifications. This includes properties ranging from utility infrastructure and industrial premises to small retail units.Rate liabilities vary significantly depending on property classification, rateable value, location, and usage. In addition, a range of reliefs and exemptions apply differently across sectors, resulting in wide variation in actual bills payable. Taken together, these factors mean that average figures would not be representative.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients waiting for (i) hip; and (ii) knee, replacement surgery for (a) over two years; (b) two to four years; (c) four to six years; and (d) over six years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department is unable to provide a breakdown of the requested figures by electoral boundary. However, waiting list statistics broken down by specialty, Trust, and waiting time cohort are published each quarter on my Department’s webpage. This information can be accessed using the following address:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/hospital-waiting-times-statistics.The Elective Care Framework sets out the strategic roadmap for tackling the backlog of patients waiting for surgery and, while core capacity is being developed, my Department will continue to ensure that additional capacity is secured to help address our current backlog. This will include the funding of additional in-house waiting list initiative work and the ongoing partnership arrangement with the independent sector for primary joint replacement surgery.It should be noted that even within the current constrained financial environment, my Department has made significant progress since March 2025 with the number of patients waiting more than four years for a hip or knee procedure reduced by approximately 66% from 1,400 to 490. Further reductions are expected before the end of March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 37795/22-27, to detail any current policies in relation to spinal muscular atrophy in (i) Scotland; (ii) Wales; (iii) England; and (iv) the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: As outlined in AQW 37795/22-27, screening policy across the UK is informed by the recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee which advises the four UK countries on screening matters.  Currently, the UK NSC does not recommend screening for Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) but has asked for more evidence to be gathered to address gaps in the evidence.  To do this, Scotland and England are carrying out an In-Service Evaluation (ISE). The results of the ISEs will support a future UK NSC recommendation. More information on the ISE process can be found here: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/uk-nsc-evidence-review-process/uk-nsc-evidence-review-process.In the Republic of Ireland, I understand they plan to add screening for SMA to its’ National Newborn Bloodspot Screening Programme.  My Department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on screening matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the figures contained in the latest Labour Force Survey, which shows a yearly 0.4 per cent increase in seasonally adjusted unemployment rate; and (ii) to detail any plans she can take to lower this figure.
answer_text: The latest NISRA Labour Market Report shows that the North’s unemployment rate for September - November 2025, decreased over the quarter but increased over the year, to 2.1%. An unemployment rate of this level is considered exceptionally low, especially compared to other jurisdictions. Economic inactivity has been a long-standing issue for the North, and the latest NISRA Labour Market Report shows that the inactivity rate increased over the year but decreased over the quarter, to 26.4% for September – November 2025. This remains high compared to other jurisdictions. It is important to note that NISRA has stated that none of these quarterly or annual changes were statistically significant. 	While the Department for Communities leads on employability and economic inactivity, I am aware that addressing these issues requires collaboration across multiple departments and agencies - my department is already contributing significantly on these issues, for example through commitments outlined in the draft Disability and Work Strategy, which was recently consulted upon. 	My Department also offers a range of programmes aimed at supporting people to thrive in training or employment. These include programmes such as Skills for Life & Work and Traineeships, both of which provide clear pathways into employment, particularly for young people who face additional barriers. My flagship Apprenticeships NI programme opens up employment opportunities for people of all ages, while key programmes, such as Skill Up and Step Up, aim to broaden access, especially for those facing additional barriers.Alongside these programmes, work continues to enhance my department’s support for school leavers and others with special educational needs and disabilities, and Higher and Further Education institutions and other training providers offer a range of opportunities which have disability and childcare support available.	Funding however remains a significant challenge - the sustained reduction in support following the loss of the European Social Fund and the transition to the UK Shared Prosperity Fund and now the Local Growth Fund, has had a considerable impact. This is especially acute for partners in the Voluntary and Community Sector, who deliver so much of the vital support in this area.		In addition to the programmes outlined above, increasing the proportion of our working-age population in Good Jobs is a key priority outlined in both my Economic Vision and the Programme for Government, and my department has also been progressing a wide range of initiatives to deliver that. 		The Employment Rights Bill for example, will represent the biggest, most complex modernisation of employment law since the Good Friday Agreement, raising minimum standards for all workers, and helping more people engage with the labour market. 		A fuller picture of my department’s plans can be found in the 2025-26 Business Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 37226/22-27, for his assessment of that decision.
answer_text: The Minister of Finance set out in his 9 December 2025 oral statement on the December Monitoring Round that the Executive had agreed on 16 October 2025 that the Departments of Health, Education, Justice and Infrastructure would have first call on available funding up to £150.4 million in total for pay awards.  He also advised that his Department did not seek any Resource DEL bids from departments in that exercise.  Given the scale of the funding gap at that time in the overall Resource DEL budget, this decision was prudent.In relation to Capital DEL, my Department's assessment of the position across DAERA was also prudent at that time and the focus continues to be on spending out the existing allocation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the figures contained in the latest Labour Force Survey, which show an increase of 0.2 per cent to 26.4 per cent in the proportion of economically inactive people; and (ii) to detail any plans she has to lower this figure.
answer_text: The latest NISRA Labour Market Report shows that the North’s unemployment rate for September - November 2025, decreased over the quarter but increased over the year, to 2.1%. An unemployment rate of this level is considered exceptionally low, especially compared to other jurisdictions. Economic inactivity has been a long-standing issue for the North, and the latest NISRA Labour Market Report shows that the inactivity rate increased over the year but decreased over the quarter, to 26.4% for September – November 2025. This remains high compared to other jurisdictions. It is important to note that NISRA has stated that none of these quarterly or annual changes were statistically significant. 	While the Department for Communities leads on employability and economic inactivity, I am aware that addressing these issues requires collaboration across multiple departments and agencies - my department is already contributing significantly on these issues, for example through commitments outlined in the draft Disability and Work Strategy, which was recently consulted upon. 	My Department also offers a range of programmes aimed at supporting people to thrive in training or employment. My flagship Apprenticeships NI programme opens up employment opportunities for people of all ages, while key programmes, such as Skill Up and Step Up, aim to broaden access, especially for those facing additional barriers.Alongside these programmes, work continues to enhance my department’s support for school leavers and others with special educational needs and disabilities, and Higher and Further Education institutions and other training providers offer a range of opportunities which have disability and childcare support available.	Funding however remains a significant challenge - the sustained reduction in support following the loss of the European Social Fund and the transition to the UK Shared Prosperity Fund and now the Local Growth Fund, has had a considerable impact. This is especially acute for partners in the Voluntary and Community Sector, who deliver so much of the vital support in this area. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farmers taking part in the Pesticide Usage Monitoring Programme for (i) grassland and fodder crops; and (ii) edible protected crops, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In the past 5 years there has been one survey carried out on grassland and fodder crops, which took place in 2021. Over this same period there have been two surveys carried out on edible protected crops: in 2021 and 2023.Grassland and fodder crops 282 responses were received from 397 holdings contacted - 200 responses from grassland growers and 82 responses from fodder crop growers.Edible protected crops In 2021, 7 responses were received from 25 relevant holdings contacted. In 2023, 10 responses were received from 18 relevant holdings contacted.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 27616/22-27, for an update on discussions between his Department and the Northern Ireland Museums Council regarding the establishment of a Harry Ferguson Museum.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with the NIMC in relation to the requirements and processes associated with the establishment of any new Harry Ferguson museum. This engagement has been regarding the provision of high-level advice on accreditation, governance and collections development. No formal proposals have been submitted to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policy of each Health and Social Care Trust in relation to continuing healthcare.
answer_text: From 2010 until the policy was revised in 2021, my Department had an extant Continuing Healthcare (CHC) policy in place, issued to Trusts to guide the assessment and delivery of CHC. During that period, it was the responsibility of each Health and Social Care Trust to implement and comply with that policy in carrying out their statutory functions. My Department set the policy framework within which Trusts were to operate and implement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when new guidance will be issued to Health and Social Care Trusts following a recent High Court judgement on an individual continuing healthcare case.
answer_text: My Department is currently reviewing the implications of the recent High Court judgment on an individual continuing healthcare case. Officials remain in close contact with Senior Crown Counsel, who is currently finalising advice following the appeal judgment. Once this advice has been received, my Department will consider any updates required to existing guidance. Health and Social Care Trusts will be issued with the revised guidance as soon as possible after this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of Assembly Written Questions she has been asked, in each year during the 2022-27 mandate; and (ii) the proportion she has answered within the designated timescale.
answer_text: Since taking office on 3 February 2025, I have received 1,552 AQWs for answer and responded to 75% within the designated timescale.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 35343/22-27, and given that the preliminary 2023 House Condition Survey has now been published, to detail when the new estimates on the number of children living in fuel poor households will be published.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the NIHE, as they carry out the data collection for the Northern Ireland House Condition Survey. She has advised me as follows:	The 2023 House Condition Survey Preliminary Report was published 13 January 2026. Unlike previous years, this will not be followed by one Main Report. Rather, a series of shorter, thematic reports will be published. These thematic reports will be published throughout 2026. Fuel poverty figures are modelled, and this work is currently underway with a scheduled completion date of May. A report on the findings will be produced on completion – estimating June/July 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the three core targets for 2030, contained in the Energy Strategy, The Path to Net Zero Energy, will be met.
answer_text: Since taking on the Economy portfolio in February 2024, former Minister Murphy and I have led the Department to effectively deliver key commitments within the Executive’s Energy Strategy.My Department’s Mid-Term Review of the Energy Strategy was published on 18th December 2025, and highlights the significant progress achieved to date. Later this year an Energy Policy Position Statement and milestones will be published that set out a clear direction for delivery through to 2030. I remain committed to the delivery of a net zero energy system powered by local renewables, smart infrastructure, and shaped by the people who live and work here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the benefits of; and (ii) any discussions between his Department and CCEA regarding, the introduction of an app to receive exam results.
answer_text: CCEA Regulation advised that consideration had previously been given to a similar approach to GCSE results publication here. However, concerns were raised about the appropriateness of students aged 15 or 16, receiving their GCSE results potentially on their own, without the opportunity to receive on-site pastoral, academic and careers support from school staff.   As with all aspects of examinations service delivery, the Department and CCEA will keep the matter under review.  It should be noted that AS level and A level students in Northern Ireland, are able to access their results digitally through an online system. It was judged to be appropriate given the age profile of pupils who would be availing of this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Hospital Statistics: Acute Episode-Based Activity Statistics 2024-25, in particular the decrease in the average number of available beds of 5.2 per cent.
answer_text: It is important to note that statistics on available beds are contained within the Hospital Statistics: Inpatient and Day Case Activity release. The figures reported are based on data provided quarterly by Health and Social Care Trusts through regular bed returns.The methodology for calculating average available beds represents the total number of beds available in wards open overnight at midnight each day of the year, divided by the number of days in the year. These figures include both occupied and unoccupied commissioned beds. A commissioned bed is one with full recurrent funding for all associated costs. Beds reserved solely for day care admissions or regular day admissions are excluded, as are escalation spaces used temporarily in place of commissioned beds. Only beds used for consultant-led care are counted.Where a commissioned bed becomes unavailable, for example, due to infection control measures, staffing reductions, damage, maintenance, or cleaning, the count of available beds is reduced accordingly. This may help explain the observed decrease in the average number of available beds between 2023/24 and 2024/25. During this period, the average number of available beds fell by 5.2% (316.9 beds), from 6,152 to 5,835.1. However, between 2020/21 and 2024/25, there was an increase of 2.9% (162.5), from 5,672.6 to 5,835.1.Over the last number of years there has also been investment to increase ambulatory hubs and Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) beds which are designed to manage more patients without the need for admission to an overnight bed. Any reduction in inpatient beds has to be taken in the overall context of a changing service model where the aim is to maximise these short stay beds as opposed to admit patients to overnight inpatient beds.It should also be noted that figures extracted from the Encompass system, which has been rolled out across Health and Social Care Trusts since 2023/24, are considered “official statistics in development.” Therefore, caution should be exercised when interpreting these figures.Despite the reduction in available beds, occupancy rates increased from 84% in 2023/24 to 85.4% in 2024/25, while the average length of stay remained stable at 7.4 days. This suggests that demand for inpatient care has remained high.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) a timetable for enhancements to the Lough Neagh Water Quality Dashboard; and (ii) any data those enhancements will produce.
answer_text: The Lough Neagh Water Quality Dashboard is the first step in my Department’s development of the Science Platform as set out within the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan.Short term deliveryAdditional NIEA data, including details on water chemistry and algal composition, from nine shore sites around Lough Neagh will be incorporated through a dashboard update in the summer of 2026. To provide additional warning regarding algal movements towards the North Coast, additional bathing water algal toxin data and a further telemetered buoy in the Lower Bann will be added. Medium term deliveryDAERA and UKRI-NERC (UK Research and Innovation, Natural Environment Research Council) are co-funding a significant new microbial source tracking project to trace and quantify microbial communities and provide an insight into the phytoplankton, zooplankton and cyanobacterial species composition and the range of invasive species residing in the Lough. An update to the dashboard with data outputs from this project is planned for early 2027. Longer term deliveryDAERA has also commissioned additional research to enhance understanding of Lough Neagh with science partners, AFBI. This includes high frequency water quality monitoring, bathymetric and sediment mapping, zebra mussel surveys, analysis of the role of aquatic plants in nutrient cycling, enhanced toxin analysis, and testing for taste and odour compounds. Outputs will be integrated into the dashboard on a rolling basis to provide an expanded evidence base.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support provided to farmers to assist in identifying and managing Bovine TB: Resolved Inconclusive Animals.
answer_text: My officials have recently written to farmers with regards identifying and managing animals regarded as “resolved inconclusives” (resolved ICs) under the bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) programme. 		Research evidence points to these resolved IC animals, those which have initially tested inconclusive to the tuberculin skin test but have subsequently tested negative, as posing an increased risk of infection compared to cattle that have consistently tested negative for bTB. This scientific evidence shows resolved ICs can act as a hidden reservoir of disease, threatening herd health and prolonging breakdowns.		To address this, my officials have recently written to herd keepers to inform them of the presence of such resolved ICs in their herds along with advice on the elevated risk such cattle pose.  This guidance advises farmers to:	finish and slaughter resolved ICs promptly in non-breeding herds;	in breeding herds, consider voluntary culling at the earliest opportunity, especially in herds with a history of bTB;	prioritise removal if these animals have other health issues such as lameness or fertility problems; and	note that direct movement to slaughter is the safest option to reduce bTB risk.		Should farmers have further queries on the effective management of resolved ICs, they are advised to contact their local DAERA Direct office.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the estimated cost of screening newborns for spinal muscular atrophy; and (ii) whether he has any plans to introduce such screening.
answer_text: Screening policy across the UK is informed by the recommendations of the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent committee which advises the four UK health Departments on screening matters.  The UK NSC does not currently recommend screening for spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) in newborns. In the summer of 2025 UK NSC published four documents reviewing the latest evidence:https://view-health-screening-recommendations.service.gov.uk/sma/.The Committee concluded that an In-Service Evaluation (ISE) is required to provide the UK NSC with critical evidence on the feasibility, safety and cost effectiveness of screening for SMA, to inform a future recommendation. Planning to commence the ISE has begun. Once completed, the UK NSC will review the evidence gathered   My department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the main changes contained in the New Land Eligibility Rules for the 2026 Farm Sustainability Payment.
answer_text: The Land Eligibility rules for FSP 2026 have been simplified, making them easier to understand and reducing their associated bureaucracy.  Broadly, all land is now eligible except for hard/man-made features. Eligible land now includes land with soft vegetative features, such as, rush, scrub, bracken, heather and bogs, and land where the density of scattered stones, scree or rock make up equal to or less than 70%. Woodland parcels, which are less than 5ha, discrete and mapped in the Land Parcel Identification System (LPIS), are also now eligible.Importantly, the simplifications reduce the need for activity to be undertaken to maintain eligibility, such as burning heather or removing scrub by mechanical means which could cause environmental damage, or controlling rush by spraying, which may impact on biodiversity and water quality.Full details are in the Guide to Land Eligibility on the Department’s website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department is explaining the benefits and encouraging farmers to use the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise Benchmarking Tool.
answer_text: The CAFRE financial benchmarking Tool is available to all farm business owners and can be accessed either via the CAFRE website or the DAERA Online Services Portal.CAFRE highlights the benefits and encourages farmers to use the financial benchmarking Tool through the Knowledge Transfer and Innovation programmes that are an integral part of the new Sustainable Agriculture Programme.In addition, the benefits of using the benchmarking tool are highlighted in the Farming Press and through ongoing engagement with key agri-food stakeholders, who provide financial support to farm business owners.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of nursing and residential care homes that were the subject of more than one complaint recorded by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority, in each of the last five financial years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Responsibility for dealing with complaints about health and social care services in Northern Ireland is placed with the provider of the service itself, for example a HSC Trust or care home, and therefore Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) does not routinely capture data relating to complaints about nursing and residential care homes.  RQIA will routinely receive information and intelligence from a range of services relating to health and social care services and can record and treat these as a “concern” on its internal information systems. RQIA’s latest published Annual Report (for 2024/25) indicates that approximately one third of the total 2996 contacts /concerns that were recorded by RQIA in that year related to Nursing and Residential Care Homes. This information can be found on the RQIA website (see page 11):RQIA_Annual_Report_Accounts_2024-25_Final_CAG-Certificate_21072025.pdfRQIA responds to any concerns brought to its attention. Upon receipt of such information RQIA may take a range of actions for example signposting individuals toward the correct complaints procedure; where required, RQIA may share the information with the relevant HSC Trust for follow-up action to address issues raised, or may choose to follow-up directly itself. Where appropriate, information received may also be used to inform future inspections, may be included in inspection reports and may help determine the outcomes of an inspection.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 32092/22-27, to detail any work that will be carried out in this mandate to regulate property management companies.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer I recently gave in response to AQO 2770/22-27, which sets out the current position in relation to this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any vacancies to be filled by the recent Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment process, broken down by (i) department; and (ii) location.
answer_text: As at the end of December 2025, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 338 vacancies available to be filled from the recent Civil Service Administrative Officer, North West pilot competition which launched in October 2025. There are 92 vacancies in Ballykelly and 246 vacancies in Derry (Area 2).  All of the current vacancies are Alternative Working Pattern (AWP) in the Department for Communities.My Department is finalising the first round of allocations from this merit list; a total of 218 candidates who indicated a preference for AWP in Area 2 have been made offers of employment and are due to take up post from 26 January 2026 onwards (54 for Ballykelly and 164 for Derry).A further round of allocations to fill any remaining current and future vacancies will be considered in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of nursing and residential care home generated complaints recorded by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority in more than one financial year during the last five financial years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Responsibility for dealing with complaints about health and social care services in Northern Ireland is placed with the provider of the service itself, for example a HSC Trust or care home, and therefore Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) does not routinely capture data relating to complaints about nursing and residential care homes.  RQIA will routinely receive information and intelligence from a range of services relating to health and social care services and can record and treat these as a “concern” on its internal information systems. RQIA’s latest published Annual Report (for 2024/25) indicates that approximately one third of the total 2996 contacts /concerns that were recorded by RQIA in that year related to Nursing and Residential Care Homes. This information can be found on the RQIA website (see page 11):RQIA_Annual_Report_Accounts_2024-25_Final_CAG-Certificate_21072025.pdfRQIA responds to any concerns brought to its attention. Upon receipt of such information RQIA may take a range of actions for example signposting individuals toward the correct complaints procedure; where required, RQIA may share the information with the relevant HSC Trust for follow-up action to address issues raised, or may choose to follow-up directly itself. Where appropriate, information received may also be used to inform future inspections, may be included in inspection reports and may help determine the outcomes of an inspection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with NIE Networks, regarding the review of the connection charging framework, over the last three years.
answer_text: My Department and NIE Networks, along with the Utility Regulator, collaborated significantly in the development of the Fairer Grid Connection costs policy. My Department continues to work with the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks to bring this change through as quickly as possible, with change expected to take effect in the first half of 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether NIE Networks plan to amend the connection agreement charging structure, in the near future.
answer_text: Following the publication of my decision on Fairer Grid Connection Costs on 26 November 2025, the Utility Regulator is currently working to modify NIE Networks’ licence which will be followed by the publication of a new Statement of Connection Charges. Implementation of the policy change is expected to take effect in the first half of 2026.		The Department will continue to work with the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks bring this change through as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will expand the winter gritting schedule for times of adverse weather.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by my Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes.The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. Once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is assured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion.In addition to the gritting schedule, my Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,850 salt bins and 52,300 grit piles for use by the public on a self-help basis on selected routes not included on the salting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met. During the winter months, grit piles and salt bins are inspected during inclement weather and replenished from our reserves as necessary to maintain supply.The Winter Service Policy is available to view on my Department’s website at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 36412/22-27, to detail how his Department can (i) monitor; and (ii) reduce, the number of agency staff employed by Health and Social Care Trust, given the Western Health and Social Care Trust and Northern Health and Social Care Trust do not collate the number of agency staff and hours.
answer_text: The Department monitors Health and Social Care Trusts’ expenditure on agency and bank staff on a quarterly basis for specific staffing categories.  In addition, the Regional Agency Reduction Implementation and Planning Group (ARIG) has been established to co-ordinate the improvement of staff utilisation, with the aim of reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. The main function of the Group is to develop and implement a plan, with local and regional commitment and coordination, to safely facilitate, and manage the service impact of, reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. The ARIG also regularly monitors agency and locum expenditure across all HSC Trusts.The Northern HSC Trust currently have a ‘Non - Medical, Non- Nursing (Multi-Disciplinary) Agency Reduction Group’ the purpose of which is to develop, implement, and monitor strategies to reduce the reliance of Multi-Disciplinary Framework agency workers across the Trust, with the goal of eliminating their use where feasible. As part of a phased approach the Northern HSC Trust is focusing on eliminating the use of non-contract agency. As part of this work the levels of spend and trend are monitored through financial information relating to invoices for non-contract staff use.  The Western HSC Trust was not able to provide a response to AQW 36412/22-27 on the number of agency staff employed because this information is recorded as costs, however, the WHSCT Trust can provide a notional WTE agency figure converted from monthly invoices to an average notional WTE.  These figures are provided below;Trust2022/232023/242024/25WHSCT1,2361,1101,011

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the latest Northern Ireland Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste Management Statistics; and (ii) to detail any further action that can be taken to reduce waste going to landfill and increase recycling.
answer_text: NI Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste Management Statistics released on 11 December 2025 show a landfill rate of 14% for 2024/2025, compared to 18.4% for 2023/2024. This is the lowest rate ever recorded. The quantity of waste sent to landfill decreased by 22.8% from 183,748 tonnes in 2023/2024 to 141,836 tonnes in 2024/2025.  However, the latest statistics show that our recycling rate is stagnating. 	The upcoming Waste Management Strategy (WMS) will include proposals and planned actions aimed at reducing consumption and waste prevention.  Improving the quality as well as the quantity of materials collected for reuse/recycling is a key aim of both the WMS and the Departmental Response on waste and recycling reforms in Northern Ireland, which I hope to publish shortly following Executive approval. 	My Department has responsibility for the £23m Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme, which provides capital funding assistance for local councils to transform kerbside recycling and Household Recycling Centre (HRC) infrastructure and services. Increasing the quantity of household waste recycling collected by local councils will in turn reduce reliance on landfill. The programme emphasises quality of recycling, as well as increasing the quantity of recyclates collected, in order to realise the economic potential of recycling to the economy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36938/22-27, to detail (i) the rationale for this development proposal being agreed for part-time hours, despite asking for full-time hours; and (ii) any steps he is taking to ensure that rural communities receive equitable access to full-time places.
answer_text: The rationale for part-time provision in respect of DP 750 is detailed in the report available on the Department’s website which can be accessed here.  I have set out my intention, subject to Executive agreement and funding, to transition, over time, all funded pre-school education provision within the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) to full-time. Approximately 50% of PSEP provision in all areas of Northern Ireland is now full-time. The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for identifying pre-school settings suitable for transition and seeks to ensure all areas are equitably represented among the settings put forward for consideration at each stage, taking into account the relevant statutory duties and readiness of individual settings to transition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Agricultural Census in Northern Ireland 2025, including (i) the reduction in the number of farms; and (ii) the reduction of sheep and cattle numbers.
answer_text: The Agricultural Census reports 25,834 active farms in Northern Ireland in June 2025.  This is a reduction of 356 farms from the 26,190 reported in June 2024, equating to a 1 percent decrease. The observed reduction in total number of farms between 2024 and 2025 is not considered to be an indicator of a recent trend and is not a significant change. The number of farms has been relatively stable over the last 20 years. Between 2024 and 2025 total sheep numbers declined by 8 percent.  This continues a downward trend since 2022.  Total cattle numbers decreased by 2 percent between 2024 and 2025, having remained relatively stable from 2021 to 2024. It is not possible to determine if this is a temporary deviation from the general pattern of stability in total cattle or the beginning of a change in trend. The Agricultural Census figures show variation within the cattle sector. Dairy Cow numbers increased by 2 percent between 2024 and 2025 to 330,673, continuing an increasing trend seen since 2013.  Beef Cow numbers decreased by 5 percent to 213,744 between 2024 and 2025.  Beef cow numbers have been trending downwards since 2015. Changes in farm numbers and livestock figures over time are influenced by numerous factors, many of which are outside of the control of DAERA such as world market conditions, wider economic policy, and individual business decisions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 36835/22-27, to detail any efforts his Department is making on domestic recruitment of seasonal workers.
answer_text: While I recognise the critical importance of seasonal workers for the agri-food sector, recruitment is undertaken by individual businesses and employers and it is not within the remit of my Department to get involved in the direct employment of workers for the sector. As immigration policy is not a devolved matter, the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme (SAWS) is administered by the Home Office. I continue to urge the UK Government to consider the needs of Northern Ireland in how the scheme operates, including our unique position regarding the land border with the Republic of Ireland. Liaison continues with NI industry stakeholders, Defra, the Migration Advisory Committee, the Labour Market Evidence Group (LMEG), and with the Home Office to clarify the implementation of the UK immigration policy changes.  I remain committed to supporting the NI agri-food sector by continuing to raise these issues with the UK Government and to put forward the case for those sectors facing workforce shortages while working with Defra to explore efforts on domestic recruitment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 31162/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the reason he bid for £13million for priority adaptations to Housing Executive properties for tenants living with a disability; (ii) how the £4million allocated in the December Monitoring Round will reduce waiting times.
answer_text: (i)    A bid was submitted for £13m in the monitoring round, as this was the maximum amount of expenditure which could be delivered at that point in the financial year.(ii) is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The £4m allocated in the December Monitoring Round will have no impact on the waiting times for the adaptations. Current programme expenditure of £13m in 2025/26, is the maximum level of programme that can be delivered taking into account existing contractors’ capacity. The £4m of funding received from DfC will simply reduce the need for NIHE to fully fund this programme from rental income receipts – which will, in turn, provide additional capacity for other stock maintenance and improvement initiatives. This is welcomed given the wider funding challenges that NIHE currently faces.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any discussions with the GB Government about the GB Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) when it comes into effect in January 2027, particularly in relation to the interaction with the EU CBAM, which is due to come into effect from 1 January 2026.
answer_text: The UK Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) is designed to prevent carbon leakage from UK businesses to other jurisdictions, ensure fair competition for UK producers, and encourage global emission reductions by applying a carbon price to imported goods equivalent to that faced by locally produced goods. The EU Mechanism works on a similar principle.Responsibility for the development of UK CBAM policy rests with the UK Government, however both I and my officials liaise closely with, and receive regular updates from, our counterparts across the relevant Whitehall Departments. Through this engagement both I and my officials have stressed the importance of considering the interaction with the EU CBAM.As you are aware, the EU CBAM will apply from January 2026. It will not apply in Northern Ireland as the regulation has not been added to the scope of the Windsor Framework.On 26 November, the UK Government announced its intention to legislate in the 2025-26 Finance Bill to introduce the UK CBAM from 1 January 2027, and they have confirmed that it will apply across the whole of the UK, including Northern Ireland. They have stated their intention to continue to work with international partners, including the EU, to ensure implementation in a way that works for businesses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how she will spend the additional £12.6 million, allocated in the 2025-26 December Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The additional £12.6 million Capital DEL funding allocated to my Department in the December Monitoring Round will be used as follows:£4.5 million to address a capital overcommitment arising from the June Monitoring Round, when I committed to fund an additional £9.0 million for quality-related research in Higher Education Institutions (HEIs). This step was necessary to provide financial stability for HEIs after the Executive provided only £2.5 million in extra funding at that time;£6.3 million to provide further support for Higher Education; and£1.8 million for minor capital works across the Further Education estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the current value of requests from schools for essential school maintenance; (ii) how he will spend the £5.2 million allocated for essential school maintenance; and (iii) how he will spend the £3.2 million allocated for schools’ minor works and energy-saving solutions, as designated in the December Monitoring Round.
answer_text: (i) £9million is the current value of essential school maintenance requests. Maintenance of schools in the Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated sectors is the direct responsibility of the schools themselves, with the cost for this being met from within the schools' existing recurrent budget.(ii) The £5.2million capital funding allocated for essential school maintenance will be used to meet various inescapable minor works pressures, enabling the Education Authority (EA) to maintain operations, continue service delivery and mitigate the risk of closures across the schools’ estate.(iii) The £3.2million capital funding allocated for schools’ minor works and energy-saving solutions will be used to provide solar panels, Automatic Meter Reading systems and LED lighting in various controlled and maintained schools that will not only enhance the schools but result in both energy and cost savings in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rationale behind his Department not making any significant bids in the 2025-26 December Monitoring Round, for additional resources.
answer_text: The Executive agreed on 16 October 2025 that the Departments of Health, Education, Justice and Infrastructure would have first call on available funding up to £150.4 million in total for pay awards.  Consequently, no other Resource DEL bids were sought from departments in the December Monitoring Round exercise.  In terms of Capital DEL, a full and detailed exercise was undertaken internally as part of this Monitoring Round to ensure that the most up to date financial forecasts could be assessed.  Given the late stage of the year to spend additional Capital DEL, no significant bids were identified and my Department's focus is now on spending out its existing allocation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he will spend the additional £19.7 million for IT equipment, allocated in the December Monitoring Round.
answer_text: This funding is to refresh out of warranty and unsupported end user devices across all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts and Arms-Length Bodies (ALBs). The investment will enable the replacement of ageing equipment, such as computers, laptops, and tablets that are no longer covered by warranty or vendor support. By updating these devices, the Department aims to improve reliability, performance, and security for frontline staff and administrative teams alike.Modernising end user equipment will help ensure that Trusts and ALBs can maintain operational continuity and safeguard against potential technical failures or cyber security risks. The new devices will support staff in delivering clinical services more efficiently and securely, while also facilitating staff training and access to essential systems. This refresh is an important step in strengthening the overall IT infrastructure within Northern Ireland's Health and Social Care sector, helping to mitigate issues such as those experienced during recent IT outages and ensuring resilient and effective service delivery for patients and staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how he will spend the £8.6 million allocated to the Cladding Safety Scheme in the December Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The money will be used for works completed to date in this financial year as well as work that will be undertaken by the end of March 2026 under the Northern Ireland Cladding Safety Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Strule Shared Education Campus project, in particular any reason for the reduction in need for capital spend of £18.4million this financial year, as identified in the December Monitoring Round.
answer_text: Significant progress continues on the Strule Shared Education Campus (SSEC) Main Works Construction with 25% of the Main Works now complete. All works remain within the Full Business Case approval and programme limits. Planned construction completion is November 2027 with full campus opening by September 2028.The £18.4m reduced requirement submitted as part of the December Monitoring Round takes account of a revised anticipated expenditure profile to the end of March 2026 which reflects unexpected programme delays (largely relating to challenging ground conditions encountered at the Shared Education Centre). It also takes account of an assessment of the current position in terms of Contractor procurement activities and the estimated cost of implemented and expected change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeframe and scope of the review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 and all relevant legislation, to assess whether legislative reform is required.
answer_text: My Department recently completed a 14-week public consultation on the Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) and the Service Delivery Plan (SDP) which closed on 25 November 2025. The LDSM sets out clear ambitions for young people with a learning disability, which includes individuals with complex needs, to receive lifelong, co-ordinated support, tailored to their needs and enabling choice and control. Next steps will include publishing a consultation summary report by mid-January and an analysis of feedback to further refine the LDSM and SDP by early Spring 2026. Once finalised, they will inform decisions on the future reconfiguration of services for people with a learning disability and any additional investment required. Delivery of the ambitions outlined within the Model will be subject to funding made available to my Department. I am committed to reviewing the Chronically Sick & Disabled Persons (NI) Act 1978 and all appropriate legislation during the first year of implementing the three-year SDP for the LDSM, to determine whether there is a need for legislative change, which better reflects today's needs and modern models of support for people with lifelong complex needs.Until the LDSM and SDP are finalised, it is not possible to detail an exact timeframe for this review or to assess whether legislative reform will be required. With the legislation cutting across several programmes of care, a working group will need to be established to determine the terms of reference, scope and timeframe for the review.

There are 84 vacancies in Ballykelly and 236 vacancies in Derry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the latest Statistical Bulletin, Cyber Crime, Modern Slavery and Sentencing: Findings from the 2023/24 Northern Ireland Safe Community Telephone Survey, in particular the reduction in numbers who felt the courts and police were effective in deal with cases; and (ii) to detail any additional action she will take to reverse these figures.
answer_text: Building public confidence in the justice system remains a key priority for my Department. The Northern Ireland Safe Community Telephone Survey 2023/24 indicates a slight decline in the proportion of respondents who consider the police and courts effective in dealing with certain crimes, including modern slavery, trafficking and cybercrime. These findings underline the importance of continued focus on prevention, enforcement and victim support. While these findings are disappointing, the survey also highlights positive trends. Awareness of where to seek advice on cyber security has increased, and reporting of cybercrime incidents has risen significantly. This indicates improved public engagement and willingness to report, despite reduced confidence in outcomes.	The context for these findings is important. Modern offending, particularly scams and cyber-enabled crime, has become increasingly sophisticated and often targets vulnerable groups, including older people. Under-reporting remains a challenge, notwithstanding improvements in reporting routes such as Report Fraud, PSNI and Crimestoppers. The Scamwise NI Partnership, chaired by the PSNI and supported by over 45 partners, also provides advice and delivers awareness campaigns to keep the public informed and vigilant.	Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) funded by my Department and the NI Policing Board, help address cyber-enabled crime by delivering local initiatives to address scams and cybercrime, including work with older and vulnerable people, school-based cyber safety activity, and community awareness events. My Department also provides core funding to Victim Support NI to ensure victims receive emotional support, advocacy and assistance through the justice process.On 10 July 2024, I published the Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024–2027, which underpins Northern Ireland’s strategic response to these crimes. Developed with input from statutory partners and NGOs, the Strategy supports the effective use of existing and new regulatory tools to pursue offenders and promotes continued early engagement between the PSNI and the Public Prosecution Service in cases concerning slavery and trafficking. Implementation of the Strategy is overseen on a cross-government basis by the Organised Crime Task Force Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Sub-Group, which will consider the survey findings and any actions required. I welcome the increased public awareness of the signs and indicators of modern slavery, reflecting awareness-raising activity delivered by my Department and partners across social media and in public spaces.The above actions aim to reduce harm, improve detection and prosecution, and enhance the experience of victims, which are key drivers of public confidence in policing and the courts
