answer_text: The Pre-School Inclusion Fund (PSIF) supported seven settings in the parliamentary constituency of West Tyrone in 2024-25, namely: Jack and Jill Community Playgroup (Plumbridge) Omagh Integrated Primary School Omagh North Nursery School St Catherine's Primary School and Nursery Unit (Strabane)  St Lawrence's Primary School and Nursery Unit (Fintona) St Mary's Primary School and Nursery Unit (Killyclogher) St Patrick's Primary School and Nursery Unit (Castlederg).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on Donemana Community Nursery Development Proposal.
answer_text: Officials have finalised their assessment, and a report has been passed to me for my consideration.I will take a decision at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of Religion and Worldviews Education for All (RWE for All) research conducted by Queen's University, Belfast.
answer_text: The Religion and Worldviews Education for All (RWE for All) research is a report produced by Queen’s University Belfast. While this report has not been formally shared with my Department for comment, officials are aware of its publication and the issues it raises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his assessment of his Department’s research on the demand for Integrated Education; and (ii) any actions his Department will take following on from that report.
answer_text: The Department is currently finalising its first report on the demand for Integrated Education, which will be published early in the new year. Initial findings indicate that societal openness to Integrated Education remains relatively stable, with approximately 66% of survey respondents expressing a positive inclination towards Integrated Education. However, the research also highlights that parental behaviour at the point of school preference is influenced more strongly by factors such as school quality and proximity to home than by Integrated Education ethos alone.Once the report is complete, the Department will consider its recommendations carefully and identify appropriate actions to support informed parental choice and the continued development of Integrated Education within the wider education system. This work will be taken forward in line with the Department’s statutory duties under the Integrated Education Act, ensuring that policy and practice continue to reflect the principles set out in that legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the latest Quarterly Northern Ireland Outpatient, Inpatient and Day Case, and Diagnostic Waiting Times Statistics; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to reduce waiting lists.
answer_text: The revised Elective Care Framework (ECF), which was published on 24 May 2025, sets out a five-year roadmap to reduce waiting times.As you are aware only £50m of the allocation referenced within the Programme for Government was new money.Even with these financial pressures, there has been significant progress since March 2025 with the number of patients waiting over four years for an outpatient assessment reduced by 35% and the number of patients waiting for an inpatient or day case procedure reduced by 47%. Endoscopy waits have also reduced by 62% over the same time period.Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix, and investments at a sustained level will need to be sustained to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the latest Further Education Sector Activity in Northern Ireland 2020-21 to 2024-25 report, in particular the (a) 96 per cent male enrolment in construction, planning and the built environment; and (b) 90 per cent in engineering and manufacturing technologies; and (ii) to detail any steps she is taking to encourage women into these careers.
answer_text: In response to (i), the latest Further Education (FE) Sector Activity in Northern Ireland 2020-21 to 2024-25 report shows a continued growth in learner numbers and enrolments; an overall balance of male and female learners; consistent retention, achievement and success rates; and increases in qualifications awarded. These trends underscore the FE sector’s important contribution to addressing skills needs, supporting economic growth, and strengthening the pipeline into priority sectors. In particular for (a) and (b), with 96 per cent male enrolment in construction, planning and the built environment, and 90 per cent in engineering and manufacturing technologies, this highlights an ongoing imbalance of males and females across certain curriculum areas.In recognition of the challenge of encouraging women into these careers, I am committed to ensuring that everyone in our society has access to skills pathways that lead to good jobs, and that women and girls are supported and encouraged to pursue careers in high-value sectors.In response to (ii), I can advise that FE colleges include a range of targeted initiatives designed to broaden engagement, improve visibility of female role models, and create more inclusive pathways into technical and vocational careers. Examples include the Your Face Fits campaign which highlights diverse role models in STEM; the delivery of Women in STEM engagement events across the North; work with local social inclusion clusters and WOMEN’STEC to offer tasters in construction and in engineering to girls; a Female Engineering Academy for vehicle electricians; and Women into Construction Level 1 courses. In addition, two partnership projects involving FE colleges and supported via the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund are focused on women in STEM, including the #NotJustForBoys Pathways+ project. This aims to address the significant under-representation of women in industries such as construction, engineering and mechanics through a holistic, cross-sectoral approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any engagement his Department have had with the GB Government on their immigration white paper, particularly in relation to our need for seasonal agricultural workers.
answer_text: Since the publication of the Immigration White Paper, I have met with the then Minister for Migration and Citizenship Seema Malhotra MP and exchanged letters with her successor Mike Tapp MP to emphasise the impact of the reforms on NI agri-food. Minister Tapp affirmed that using the domestic workforce to fill vacancies within the sector remains a top priority. He also encouraged DAERA to work closely with Defra and the Labour Market Evidence Group. 		Many of the UK Government’s Immigration White Paper proposals have now been implemented, including an increase to the skill level of the Skilled Worker Visa, from RQF 3 (A-level equivalent) to RQF level 6 (degree-level). This has resulted in most agri-food occupations, which are classed as medium-skilled, being restricted from accessing the Skilled Worker Visa. Reforms are also being introduced to increase salary thresholds and the English Language requirements for Skilled Worker visa holders. 		The Migration Advisory Committee were commissioned by the then Home Secretary to undertake a review of the Temporary Shortage List in July 2025. The MAC reported on Stage 1 of the review in October 2025, which confirmed that agri-food occupations are also excluded from accessing the Temporary Shortage List. I strongly oppose the MAC’s and Home Office’s rationale that Food is not crucial to the delivery of critical infrastructure, and I emphasised this to Professor Brian Bell, Chair of the MAC, when I met him in September.		In September 2025, I wrote to the Defra Secretary of State Emma Reynolds MP, highlighting concerns about the impact of UK immigration changes on Northern Ireland’s agri-food businesses, which rely heavily on seasonal and skilled labour. I stressed the operational challenges these changes create and called for urgent attention to ensure labour availability for sectors such as fishing and food processing. I also raised these issues when I met the Defra Minister of State Dame Eagle DBE MP, last month.		Whilst the UK Immigration White Paper did not directly bring effect to changes to the Seasonal Worker’s Visa, I have continued to communicate the issues being faced by the NI mushroom sector when engaging with UK Government Ministers. A Statement of Changes to the Immigration Rules on 14th October 2025 contained an update to the time requirement between visas for seasonal workers. DAERA officials are liaising with NI mushroom stakeholders, as well as Defra and Home Office officials to clarify the implementation of the rule changes.  		I remain committed to raising these issues with the UK Government and the Labour Market Evidence Group to put forward the case for the affected sectors and occupations while working with Defra to explore existing efforts on domestic recruitment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the budget; and (ii) any support, provided through the New Foundations programme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the disposal of St Lucia barracks, Omagh.
answer_text: The disposal process for the St Lucia site is underway and currently at Stage 1: Public Sector Circulation. At this stage, only public sector bodies are eligible to submit an Expression of Interest (EOI). The deadline for submissions is 12 December 2025. If no EOIs are received by that date, the process will progress to Internal Market Stage 2: Community Asset Transfer Circulation.  At that point, details of the asset will be circulated to Development Trusts Northern Ireland, inviting EOIs from community and voluntary sector organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department has taken to create safeguards for non-land-based farmers who cannot avail of the Farm Sustainability Payment, in particular the mushroom industry.
answer_text: For those farm businesses, including those within the mushroom sector, that cannot avail of FSP there are other schemes within my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme that are relevant. The Horticulture Pilot Scheme, which has recently launched includes a bespoke range of support measures, tailored for the specific needs of this sector. It will provide focused knowledge transfer, innovation, supply chain and capital support to the production horticulture industry through three schemes: Sustainable Sector Growth Groups, Innovation Drivers and Support, and a Growers’ Training and Support Pilot Scheme.The Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme will provide financial support to help primary producers with a Category 1 Farm Business ID, including mushroom growers, to improve business efficiency and environmental performance. It is planned that the scheme will be available from early 2026.Supply Chain schemes are being developed for NI agriculture and horticulture sectors, including the mushroom industry. The aim of these schemes is to encourage and facilitate improved collaboration between producers and growers within the supply chain, encouraging them to innovate, problem-solve and value-add beyond what they can achieve in isolation. In March 2025, I also confirmed that the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme will continue in Northern Ireland until a suitable successor, co-designed with the horticulture sector is in place. Under the scheme, my Department will provide match-funding for recognised NI Producer Organisations undertaking an approved 3-year operational programme, including those operating in the mushroom industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timeline for his response to the 27 recommendations contained within the Independent Review of Teacher Workload.
answer_text: I am committed to addressing the issue of teacher and school leader workload.  I want to ensure that teachers can focus on what matters most – teaching, learning and supporting pupils in the classroom. That is why I established an independent panel to review this issue.The Independent Review of Teacher Workload report was only published on 1 December 2025.  My officials and I will require some time to consider the recommendations carefully before we can provide a detailed timeline on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to the Dilapidation Bill and the use of Community Asset Transfer as a tool for addressing dilapidation.
answer_text: The use of Community Asset Transfer along with district council powers to vest land under the Local Government Act (NI) 1972 and the Local Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) (NI) Order 2002 exist as useful tools in tackling dilapidation. As such, I have had no direct discussions with the Minister for Communities in relation to the use of Community Asset Transfer as a tool for addressing dilapidation.		Clause 13 of the Bill will give all councils access to powers under the Conveyancing Act 1881 to seek a Possession Order through the courts to enforce a sale in that way.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has taken place regarding the introduction of a rolling application window for the Small Woodland Grant Scheme.
answer_text: My Department officials work closely with stakeholders in consideration of appropriate forestry grant application windows, including for the Small Woodland Grant Scheme. I am aware of the need to provide the best opportunities available for landowners to submit forestry grant applications to allow for necessary environmental checks to be completed and letters of offer to issue for planting in the targeted planting season.My Department is taking forward the development of a Tree Planting Action Plan aimed at highlighting key actions that can be taken to increase the annual area of new woodland creation towards the target of 12% forest cover by 2050. This will consider how best to promote forestry grant schemes, including the timing of application windows.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the timeline for the publication of the tree planting action plan.
answer_text: Forest Service officials, facilitated by the Strategic Investment Board (SIB), are taking forward the development of a Tree Planting Action Plan.  A series of stakeholder engagement events will take place over the coming months, commencing later in December, to inform the co-design of the Action Plan.   It is anticipated that following analysis of stakeholder feedback the timeframe for delivery of the action plan will be spring 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 36335/22-27, to detail (i) the reason why the number of agency staff in the Department for Communities has increased by 89 per cent, from 1971 at the end of March 2023 to 3729 at the end of March 2025; and (ii) any steps he is taking to reduce these figures.
answer_text: The increase in agency worker assignments within the Department for Communities from 2023 to 2025, has primarily been driven by the expansion of service delivery for the Department for Work and Pensions and the need for additional resources, particularly at the Administrative Officer (AO) grade, to support key services for Northern Ireland citizens.  My department currently has a total of 1,250 declared AO vacancies of which the majority have been temporarily backfilled through the deployment of agency workers to maintain service delivery. Recruitment sits with the Department of Finance, and my officials continue to work closely with NICSHR to fill these vacancies through permanent recruitment as supply from new competitions becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the December 2025 Motor Neurone Disease Association report, Delivering Accessible Homes for People with Motor Neurone Disease.
answer_text: I welcome the Delivering Accessible Homes for People with Motor Neurone Disease report produced by the Motor Neurone Disease Association and fully agree with the premise that accessible housing for people with disabilities is a lifeline, not a luxury. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the challenges, across the UK in providing timely and effective housing adaptations for people with MND.Within the Northern Ireland context, I recognise the need for my Department to work closely with our partners in the Department for Communities, who have ultimate responsibility for housing provision. To that end officials within my Department have set up and co-chair the Interdepartmental Accessible Homes Strategic Forum. The Forum ensures a collaborative approach between health and housing in the development of policy and strategy in this area. The Forum has recognised the need to consider fast-tracking adaptation provision, as referenced in the report, whilst at the same time being mindful of the need to treat each case with fairness and equity. Consequently, a pilot is currently underway for a fast-track process for adaptation provision. The pilot forms part of the Disabled Facilities Grant Review, currently being led by the Department for Communities. The pilot will identify those cases which require fast-tracking through the adaptation process and is likely to include individuals with progressive conditions such as Motor Neurone Disease.My Department will commit to considering the wider recommendations within the report at the next Interdepartmental Accessible Homes Strategic Forum in early 2026.My Department is also represented on the Housing Supply Project Board to ensure the needs of people with disabilities are included in the Housing Supply Strategy Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment his Department has made of the number of dilapidated or dangerous properties currently with no identifiable owner.
answer_text: My department does not hold details on the number of dilapidated or dangerous properties that currently have no identifiable owner and so would be unable to make such an assessment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any coordinated work taking place with colleagues in the Republic, to stop the spread of bluetongue.
answer_text: As outlined in the oral statement I gave in the Assembly on Monday 1 December 2025, of which you will have received a copy, I have discussed directly the recent detection of bluetongue in Northern Ireland with my counterpart in the South, Minister Heydon.  		My officials and I will keep in close contact with colleagues in the Republic of Ireland over the coming days and weeks as we respond to this suspect case of Bluetongue.		Furthermore, my officials and their counterparts in the Department of Agriculture, Food and Marine (DAFM) have been in regular contact regarding our collective preparedness on a whole island basis, for a Bluetongue incursion, since the first case of the disease was detected in Great Britain in November 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions her Department has had with the UK Government, regarding the pay-per-mile tax for electric and plug-in hybrid vehicles, which was announced by the Chancellor in the Autumn Budget 2025.
answer_text: Following the budget announcement, Treasury has requested a meeting with the Driver and Vehicle Agency to discuss how electric vehicle excise duty (eVED) will operate here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the assessment process for bids to the Cancer Charities Grant Scheme 2025-26.
answer_text: Applications for the Cancer Charities Grant Scheme will be assessed by a panel consisting of Department of Health officials. Each application will be marked according to a weighted scoring matrix and ranked in descending order of merit based on the overall score achieved.To be eligible for a grant, applicants must achieve at least 50% of the available marks to be considered eligible for a grant. This threshold may be adjusted depending on the number of applications and the funding available. Grants will then be awarded in order of merit until all funds have been allocated.Applicants who successfully pass the assessment stage will be asked to provide additional evidence to support their proposal and will undergo a financial viability check.Guidance notes and the application form including details of the assessment process can be downloaded from the Department’s website: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-charities-grant-scheme-202526.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to the introduction of a derelict sites levy within the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: Whilst such a levy would not be within DAERA’s remit I am aware that the issue has been raised previously by elected representatives with both the Minister of Finance and the Minister for Communities. 		The purpose of such a levy is to generate revenue and promote regeneration – whereas the purpose of the Bill is to address the negative impact of dilapidated properties on local amenity.		As such I have had no discussions with the Minister of Finance in relation to the introduction of a derelict sites levy. 			I fully recognise that removing the scourge of dilapidated buildings will require cross departmental working. The Dilapidation Bill, together with the proposed changes to the rating system, recently announced by Finance Minister O’Dowd, as well as the role the Minister for Communities plays in driving regeneration will all play a part in this process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to the Dilapidation Bill and its role in addressing housing shortages.
answer_text: The primary objective of the Bill is to provide district councils with appropriate powers to improve local environmental quality, whilst specific issues in relation to our housing stock are a matter for DfC. As such I have had no specific discussions with the Minister for Communities in relation to the Dilapidation Bill in terms of its role in addressing housing shortage		My officials continue to liaise closely with Department for Communities (DfC) officials to ensure that policy objectives are aligned. The Bill should make a positive contribution across a range of issues including  improving the housing stock by enabling councils to tackle low level dilapidation early on, and by putting in place processes to require remedial works to both domestic and non-domestic properties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Noise Complaint Statistics 2024/25 report, in relation to (i) the 9 per cent reduction in the number of complaints made; and (ii) why enforcement action is only taken in 1.57 cases per 100 complaints made.
answer_text: The Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 and the Noise Act 1996 provide district councils with the authority to address noise issues from premises, land or dwellings. My department has no role in adjudicating on how district councils implement this legislation and as such I have made no assessment in relation to these Official Statistics.  The Member may wish to contact the relevant district council to discuss any specific enforcement queries relating to noise.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the Travel Survey for Northern Ireland report 2022 and 2023 data; and (ii) to detail how the figures key into her Department's decision making regarding encouraging active and sustainable travel.
answer_text: My Department welcomes the publication of the Travel Survey for Northern Ireland Report as  it provides insights into the various modes of travel used by our citizens, and their reasons for travelling. The latest data will provide a clearer picture of travel trends post-covid and will be used to update the Programme for Government indicators for active and sustainable travel.I recognise the benefits of encouraging more people to consider active and sustainable travel for many of their everyday journeys and am committed to increasing investment in active travel to meet the requirements of the Climate Change Act. More environmentally friendly and healthier modes of transport can also help to mitigate the impacts of congestion and improve streets for the people who live, work, and spend time there.The new draft Transport Strategy draws upon evidence relating to average journey distances and the dominance of the private car from the Survey to present opportunities for modal shift to sustainable modes, particularly in our towns and cities; to public transport by making it an easier choice over the private car; and to active travel for a greater number of shorter journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of agency staff employed in each Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by section, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trust expenditure on agency staff is incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained.  The use of agency staff in HSC, therefore, contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. Work is currently progressing regionally with the aim of reducing agency and locum expenditure across HSC. Significant progress has already been made since the implementation of the new agency frameworks with expenditure of ‘off-framework’ agency for nurses & midwives reducing by £120m against the base year 2022/23.WTE data is provided in the tables below which is reflective of average WTE for agency across the last three financial years.Agency Staff:Trust2022/232023/242024/25S/Eastern580879687Belfast2,3072,1082,213Southern763701714Source: HSC TrustsThe Northern and Western HSC Trusts do not collate information on agency workers numbers/hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of applicants to the Civil Service that have been unsuccessful at interview due to using notes, in each of the last three years; (ii) any burden of proof needed for this decision to be made; (iii) any appeal process available to candidates excluded in this way.
answer_text: Refer to AQW 27945/22-27.  There have been no further large volume competitions at interview stage since June 2025.Decisions were based on panel observations of the candidates' behaviour during the interview. For pre-recorded interviews, all cases where the panel noted their belief that a candidate referred to notes or received proxy help, a NICSHR panel conducted a further review of each of these video recordings to confirm the decision was justified.Interview decisions made by panels for external recruitment competitions cannot be appealed. Any candidate who wishes to make a complaint about any aspect of their application can do so via the published complaint procedure which can be found on the NICS Recruitment website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of Health Survey (NI): First Results 2024/25, in particular (i) worse health outcomes in more deprived areas; (ii) 38 per cent of respondents reporting having concerns about their own mental health in the last year; and (iii) 25 per cent of respondents exhibiting signs of loneliness.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Health Survey First Results 24/25 clearly sets out information on a range of health related statistics and outcomes which will be used to inform policy and practice going forward.(i) Across a number of key statistics, the survey shows that outcomes are worse in our most deprived communities. For example, respondents living in the most deprived areas (60%) were less likely to rate their health as good or very good than those living in the least deprived areas (76%). Those living in the most deprived areas (19%) were almost three times as likely to rate their general health as bad or very bad compared with those living in the least deprived areas (7%).I have made addressing these persistent inequalities a key area of focus, and while this will take time to show impact, this work will hopefully seek to reduce the health inequalities that exist in the longer term. For example, work is being undertaken to reinvigorate Making Life Better, the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address health inequalities. This will focus on addressing the systemic social determinants of health that drive health inequalities, and will seek to enhance collaboration across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to create the conditions that allow people, families, and communities to improve their health. In addition, I introduced Live Better to test a new placed based approach to help address this issue. Live Better was designed to help address health inequalities in Northern Ireland by bringing targeted health support to communities which needed it most. It is currently being evaluated.(ii) It is recognised that the Health Survey NI First Results for 2024/25 highlight that 38 per cent of respondents reported having concerns about their own mental health in the last year, an increase from 32% in 2022/23. There may be a range of explanations for this increase, including for example societal pressures and increasing awareness of mental health issues. From a CAMHS perspective, some mental health difficulties often emerge during adolescence, and the survey’s findings may reflect similar pressures in this age group. Increased adult mental health concerns can also affect family environments, further influencing young people’s wellbeing.The Mental Health Strategy 2021–31 recognises that health is closely linked to the conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work and age, and inequities in power, money and resources – the social determinants of health – and that the mental health and wellbeing of the population in Northern Ireland is therefore not just a health and social care issue, it is societal.The Mental Health Strategy remains the key vehicle for driving systemic reform in Northern Ireland. While its 2021 launch was met with optimism, no additional funding has been allocated to the Department to support its delivery, creating significant deli

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 35224/22-27, to detail (i) why the number of agency staff in the Department of Justice has increased by 45 per cent, from 212 at the end of March 2023 to 308 at the end of March 2025; and (ii) any steps he is taking to reduce those figures.
answer_text: I have agreed to answer this question that was submitted to the Minister of Finance.  Agency staffing levels are monitored regularly to ensure they remain appropriate and proportionate to operational need.  It is not possible to provide a detailed analysis across the business areas within the Department of Justice (DoJ) in relation to the increase in the use of agency staff.  However, agency staff are primarily engaged to fill vacant posts across business areas to ensure service delivery, or to provide additional support for specific projects and provide flexibility where it is not possible to recruit permanent staff.  It has also been necessary to engage agency workers to deliver time-bound work, or where there are anticipated delays in arranging permanent backfill through recruitment competitions. Vacancies within the DoJ, as in other NICS departments, are filled in line with NICS policies and procedures. NICSHR, within the Department of Finance (DoF), works collaboratively with all departments to prioritise recruitment within the financial and staffing resources available across the NICS.  The DoJ continues to engage with NICSHR on progressing recruitment competitions for permanent staff, which would help reduce reliance on agency workers. That includes, for example, participation in a new approach to recruitment for Administrative Officers for vacancies in the North West that is ongoing and being led by the DoF.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 35224/22-27, to detail (i) the reason why the number of agency staff in the Department for Communities has increased by 89 per cent, from 1971 at the end of March 2023 to 3729 at the end of March 2025; and (ii) any steps he is taking to reduce these figures.
answer_text: While my Department is responsible for managing the NICS recruitment agency contracts, determining when to employ agency staff and how many are required is the responsibility of each Department. It would be for the Department for Communities (DfC) to provide the reason why the number of agency staff has increased in DfC from March 2023 to March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 35760/22-27, to detail the number of candidates who said they were disabled who were subsequently (i) shortlisted; and (ii) not shortlisted, for interview.
answer_text: Candidates were not asked to confirm if they had a disability on the expression of interest form.  However, they were asked to indicate if they wished their application to be considered under the Guaranteed Interview Scheme (GIS). 512 candidates indicated this of which 178 have been invited to interview to date.  As is the case with large scale general service recruitment competitions my Department will be publishing a full breakdown including the section 75 groups once the competition concludes. This will be available on the Open Data portal in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 35224/22-27, to detail (i) why the number of agency staff in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs has increased by 33 per cent, from 255 at the end of March 2023 to 338 at the end of March 2025; and (ii) any steps he is taking to reduce those figures.
answer_text: DAERA’s vacancies are, like those in other departments, filled in accordance with NICS policies and procedures and the Department of Finance’s NICSHR teams work with all departments on an ongoing basis to prioritise recruitment across all NICS departments within their allocated financial and staff resources. Temporary roles support essential services and delivery of statutory and departmental priorities and Windsor Framework obligations. A large proportion of the agency costs relates to Windsor Framework responsibilities that rest with the Defra Secretary of State as from 12 April 2024. The department continues to monitor expenditure to ensure transparency and value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of concerns raised by the Northern Ireland Local Government Association and the Society of Local Authority Chief Executives, regarding the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: I am aware that a substantial NILGA / SOLACE joint submission was made to the AERA Committee as part of the Committee’s ‘Call for Evidence’ process. 		This is not unexpected given that we are proposing to bring together many outdated and disparate pieces of legislation into one modern fit-for-purpose regime so that district councils can have confidence in tackling dilapidation.		I acknowledge that, as a major stakeholder, local government will have pertinent questions on how the new system will work in practice, but I have confidence that these will be examined closely by the Committee during the extended Committee Stage.	I and my officials remain committed to working with the Committee to ensure that we end up with the best possible version of a Dilapidation Bill to tackle the scourge of dilapidation in our communities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the reasons why there was a fall in the number of good jobs in the Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries sector, from 44.8 per cent in 2017 to 38.8 percent in 2025; and (ii) to detail any actions she plans to take to reverse this decline.
answer_text: Whilst the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) has primary responsibility for the Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries sector, I fully recognise the value of the sector to the local economy. I note the decline in the Good Jobs rate in the sector over the period 2017-2025, based on the latest NISRA data, which appears to be driven by a decrease in the proportion of jobs with a permanent contract over the period.Through initiatives like my department’s flagship £46 million Agri Food Investment Initiative capital scheme which will drive innovation, productivity and competitive advantage for our Agri-Food processing sector, and my Agri-Tech Sectoral Action Plan, we are working with DAERA and others to create an environment that drives investment, innovation, productivity and better jobs across the agri-food supply chain and across the north.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 35553/22-27, whether they will ask the Secretary of State to extend the deadline for applications to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme, beyond August 2026.
answer_text: The Executive Office continues to monitor the scheme in close liaison with the Victims’ Payments Board to ensure that all eligible individuals have the opportunity to apply within the existing timeframe. Community Groups and targeted initiatives have played a key role in increasing awareness. We will keep the process under review and will engage with the Secretary of State as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 35760/22-27, to detail the number of candidates who said they could not work on Saturdays who were (i) shortlisted; and (ii) not shortlisted, for interview.
answer_text: Candidates weekend work preferences did not form any part of the selection process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 35552/22-27, to detail the number and proportion of (i) successful: and (ii) unsuccessful, applications that took a) under 12 months b) 12-24 months, c) over 24 months,  to determine.
answer_text: As of the 24 November 2025, the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) has recorded 2,509 successful applications, resulting in over £116 million being paid out to eligible applicants, and 1,429 unsuccessful applications. Each application is unique, and the timeline is largely dependent on the receipt of supporting information from external organisations and from applicants. As a result, the duration of the application process can be outside the control of the Board. The Board has processes in place to monitor delays within the various stages of an application and maintains regular engagement with key stakeholders and information providers to ensure these are kept to a minimum. Additionally, when an application receives an unsuccessful determination, the applicant has up to 12 months to submit an appeal. The appeal process can further extend the overall time an application remains with the Board before a final determination is reached. VPB continues to keep processes under review and is actively exploring ways to improve the throughput of cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rationale for outsourcing the Civil Service staff recruitment process.
answer_text: In the early 2000s, the Civil Service initiated a major programme of reform aimed at strengthening its organisational capacity and capability. The focus of this programme was to modernise processes and infrastructure, enhance skills and professional development, and ultimately deliver improved services to the public. This reform programme was formally launched in October 2004.A central element of the reform agenda was the establishment of seven key projects, one of which involved transforming how recruitment and certain HR services were delivered. As part of this transformation, the NICS introduced a new shared service model which included outsourcing certain parts of the recruitment process to the private sector – now referred to as HRConnect. The HRConnect contract commenced in 2007 and continues to operate as the NICS’ recruitment service delivery model today.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the Social Housing Tenancy Fraud Conference arranged by his Department.
answer_text: The Conference is a testament to the priority that I have given to this important issue. 		It is part of a review led by my Department, which will inform proposals to strengthen policy and legislation in this area. The Conference provided a valuable opportunity to bring the sector together to inform of workable solutions that tackle social housing tenancy fraud. 	Attendees at the Social Housing Tenancy Fraud Conference included representatives from Housing Executive, the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations, 12 Housing Associations, the Tenancy Fraud Forum UK, the Northern Ireland Audit Office, the NI Fraud Forum, Advice NI, MACSNI and tenant representatives from the Housing Policy Panel.	We have received positive, unsolicited feedback from participants representing the Housing Policy Panel, the Tenancy Fraud Forum UK, the Housing Executive and several Housing Associations thus far.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the quarterly cancer survival rates, in the last five years, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the specific cancer type.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not publish cancer survival rates. These   statistics are produced by the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR). Data on survival by cancer type is available in NICR’s published reports. Survival rates are reported by 1 year and 5-year periods rather than quarterly, as follow-up time and sample size are required to ensure accuracy and statistical validity. Survival figures are not routinely broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, as survival analysis is based on patient-level data and requires aggregation to maintain confidentiality and reliability.For all cancers overall (excluding non-malignant skin cancers) the official report on ‘All Cancers (ex NMSC) 1993-2022’, provides detailed information on 1 year and 5-year survival by sex, age, stage and by cancer type as well as trends in cancer survival over time. This is available at the link below:https://www.qub.ac.uk/research- centres/nicr/FileStore/OfficialStatistics1993- 2022/July2025release/All%20cancers%20(ex%20NMSC)%20report.pdfIn addition to this overall report there are reports on each cancer type (e.g. bladder, breast etc) detailing 1 year and 5-year survival by sex, age, stage and trends in cancer survival over time. These individual reports can be found at https://www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/CancerInformation/official-statistics/BySite/

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action report, Voluntary and Community sector-led approaches to economic inactivity.
answer_text: NICVA’s report highlights the important work undertaken by the Voluntary and Community Sector (VCS) in supporting those who are furthest from the labour market into pathways to employment, education, and personal well-being.It emphasizes the challenges and real concerns the VCS and beneficiaries, are facing as the North transitions from the Shared Prosperity to the Local Growth Funding.  The Finance Minister is leading negotiations with the British Government on all Local Growth Fund matters, including devolved decision making and administration, I remain fully committed to supporting him in securing complete replacement funding and ensuring that resources are allocated swiftly to provide much-needed certainty for providers and service users impacted.

answer_text: Please note: The information in the tables below was requested from and provided by the Trusts for the purpose of this response. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of why significantly more females, 13.7 per cent , than males, 9.9 percent, are defined as not in education, employment or training; and (ii) to detail the actions she is taking to reduce this gap.
answer_text: (i)The number of young people here who are not in education, employment or training is of concern and I note that the incidence is currently higher, according to the Labour Force Survey, in females than in males.This is a complex issue involving a diverse range of young people aged between 16 and 24 with multiple factors at play.  My officials are considering the matter, including whether the increasing incidence of poor mental health could be a contributing factor.  Addressing the issue will require a holistic approach from across the Executive and I am committed to working with my Executive colleagues to do so.(ii)  Many of my Department’s programmes are aimed at supporting our young people to thrive in training or employment.  These include entry-level programmes such as Skills for Life and Work and traineeships.  Step Up is funded via my Skills Fund and includes the Women’s Returners Access Programme (WRAP) with a focus on unemployed and economically excluded young women aged 18 and above.  Inclusive Futures, also funded through my Skills Fund, empowers underrepresented groups, individuals who are economically inactive, and those facing additional barriers to learning and employment. As part of the project, over 120 places have been approved to support female returners. All courses are accredited and include areas such as Digital Skills and Green Skills.The Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund specifically promotes apprenticeship uptake from three groups who are currently underrepresented across apprenticeship programmes - women, individuals with a disability and individuals from areas of deprivation or disadvantage. The first women’s project is led by Women’sTec, addressing the significant under-representation of women in industries such as construction, engineering and mechanics, particularly within apprenticeships through a holistic, cross-sectoral approach.  The second women’s project is led by Workplus, addressing supply and demand barriers to greater female participation in the software apprenticeship ecosystem here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the costs for (i) her; and (ii) her staff, to take the trade visit to Boston, broken down by (i) travel; (ii) hotel; and (iii) subsistence.
answer_text: I recently visited Boston to promote the North-West as a leading location for investment and international trade.My costs were as follows:travel £1949.95, (ii) hotel £559.87 and (ii) subsistence £89.50.My team’s costs were as follows:travel £3899.92, (ii) hotel £1119.76 and (ii) subsistence £290.78.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 35107/22-27, to detail any actions his Department is taking to encourage childcare provider capacity building, particularly in constituencies with lower subsidy figures.
answer_text: Constituencies with lower amounts of subsidy claims for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS), does not necessarily indicate provider capacity issues or low uptake of the scheme. Eligibility for NICSS is contingent upon eligibility for Tax Free Childcare (TFC). Uptake among eligible families is strong, with around 87% of children with a used TFC account benefitting from the NICSS. Households in receipt of Universal Credit are not eligible for either scheme. Consequently, constituencies with higher levels of deprivation typically record lower NICSS figures. Actions to support childcare provider capacity have been considered as part of the development of the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for public consultation, which has been issued to my Executive colleagues for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of comments made by the National Franchised Dealers Association, at the meeting of the Committee for the Economy on 19 November 2025, which warned that the industry faces a generational challenge from post-Brexit trading rules, which will mean higher costs and reduced choice for consumers.
answer_text: The Windsor Framework established a new set of arrangements for the movement of goods between Britain and the North. The British Government is responsible for the implementation of these arrangements, which it must do in a way that is fair, transparent and does not undermine competitiveness or consumer choice in the North.  Vehicle Type Approval is a reserved matter and the Department for Transport (DfT) in Britain is responsible for the UKNI and GB Type Approval Schemes. My Department maintains regular contact with the British government, to ensure that issues such as these, which arise because of Brexit, are highlighted.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of comments by survivors of sexual abuse in churches in Northern Ireland who state that there is an emerging crisis of confidence in their Department's handling of the matter.
answer_text: We acknowledge the devastating impact of clerical child abuse and express our deepest sympathy and solidarity to all victims and survivors of this terrible abuse. Throughout this process the Junior Ministers have engaged with victims and survivors to hear their stories and their views on what is needed. We are very grateful to all those who have participated in the process and shared their experiences.We have been carefully deliberating on the findings of the research. These are sensitive, complex issues that deserve thorough consideration.On 10th November we met with Lisa Caldwell, the Independent Chair of the Interdepartmental Working Group, to discuss the reports and the IDWG recommendations.  Officials continue to engage with victims and survivors, whose views we will take into account as we move forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applicants who have (i) applied for; and (ii) been successful, for Affordable Warmth Scheme grants, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 35963/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timetable for consultations on the Warm Healthy Homes Scheme.
answer_text: My Officials have completed their review of responses to the Fuel Poverty Strategy Consultation, which closed on 6 March 2025. Insights from this analysis, along with lessons learned from the outgoing Affordable Warmth Scheme and the outworkings from a series of stakeholder workshops, will help inform the proposed design of the new Warm Healthy Homes Fund and related secondary legislation. It is anticipated these proposals will be issued for public consultation by the end of Q4 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why the Environmental Protection (Wet Wipes Containing Plastic) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 won't come into effect until May 2027, later than in Wales, Scotland and England.
answer_text: A co-ordinated UK-wide approach to tackle unnecessary plastic pollution which poses significant harm to the environment has been taken to introduce a ban on the sale and supply of wet wipes containing plastic. Each nation is responsible for introducing its own regulations resulting in different implementation dates.An 18-month transition period follows the introduction of the legislation to allow retailers time to deplete existing stock and adopt plastic-free alternatives many of which are available on the market.The Welsh Government made its  regulations on 18 June 2025 coming into force on 18 December 2026. DAERA laid regulations on 18 November 2025 these will come into force on 18 May 2027. England has also introduced legislation on 18 November 2025, meaning that the regulations will also come into force there in May 2027, aligning with Northern Ireland. Scotland has not yet made its regulations but I understand that these are forthcoming.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timetable for (i) consideration of the Supreme Court judgement on the teaching of religious education in schools; and (ii) the issuing of new guidelines to schools.
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 has significant implications for our schools. I am carefully considering the ruling in its entirety before deciding on any necessary actions that both uphold the law and protect the role of religion in education.I have sought further legal advice to fully understand the scope and impact of the judgment. In the meantime, I have written to schools to acknowledge this development and to confirm that detailed guidance will be issued in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rationale for not increasing the qualifying income level cap by inflation for the Affordable Warmth Scheme since July 2021.
answer_text: The Affordable Warmth Scheme assists low-income owner occupier and private rented households to improve the energy efficiency of their homes by providing heating and insulation measures. The annual income threshold was increased from £20,000 to £23,000 in 2021 after a public consultation on changes to the scheme.  In 2021, the average income level of approved cases for the Affordable Warmth Scheme was £14,646. This has increased to £15,242 in 2025.  The average household income for successful applications is well below the £23,000 income threshold. The Scheme continues to assist low-income households most in need of help. My Department is currently developing the new Warm Healthy Homes Fund to replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme. Key elements including a review of income eligibility thresholds and future adjustments to reflect inflation were considered in the recent Fuel Poverty Strategy consultation and will inform the scheme’s design. It is anticipated that further public consultation on the scheme will be held early next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34252/22-27, to detail the guidelines used by GPs for prescribing antidepressants.
answer_text: GPs in practice comply with relevant legislation and consider guidance and alerts issued by my Department and its Strategic Planning and Performance Group.My Department has endorsed the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines on the treatment and management of depression in adults updated in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how the €1.8 million announced by the Irish Government for the All-Ireland Pollinator Plan Phase 3 will be spent in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following on from two successful All-Ireland Pollinator Plans (AIPP) spanning the last 10 years, the recently announced Shared Island Funding support for the next Plan (2026-2030) will be supplemented by additional funding from National Parks and Wildlife Service, the National Biodiversity Data Centre, the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine and NIEA.  The new, detailed AIPP will be published in Spring 2026. It will enhance monitoring of our pollinator species, transforming the landscape across the island of Ireland for pollinating insects, and will support collaboration across sectors. The additional Shared Island and partnership funding will support growth of the current team co-ordinating the work plan. Two of the nine positions will be based within Northern Ireland, with the remainder in the Republic of Ireland. Regardless of geographical location, the AIPP team and work programme will contribute to safeguarding pollinators in Northern Ireland, developing knowledge of pollinator species and the resources and support needed to ensure the decline in pollinator numbers is reversed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34252/22-27, to detail how the (i) National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guideline NG215 on safer prescribing and withdrawal management for adults; and (ii) NICE guideline NG222 on treatment of depression in adults, are implemented and monitored by his Department in relation to the prescribing of antidepressants, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how €2 million, announced by the Irish Government, to take forward a new Shared Island Greenways Fund, will impact greenways in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials have been working closely with colleagues in the Department of Transport (DoT) and Transport Infrastructure Ireland (TII) in relation to identifying and progressing a coherent network of cross-border greenway schemes.Cross-border greenways present an exciting opportunity to significantly boost sustainable transport, tourism, and regional connectivity throughout Ireland. The collaboration between two jurisdictions, while requiring coordination across distinct statutory consent processes, brings the advantage of shared expertise and resources. Although resourcing and financial support are important considerations, ongoing partnership and joint investment continue to pave the way for successful delivery of these transformative greenway routes.This funding from Shared Island Fund will help support border councils in the ongoing and future development of priority cross-border greenway projects. Officials will continue to work closely with colleagues in DOT, TII and the relevant councils to maximise this funding opportunity to support delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for autism spectrum disorder assessments, in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) have advised that the waiting times for an autism assessment across their children and adult pathways are as follows:Time band (length of time waiting for assessment)No of individuals actively waiting for ASD assessment (as at 31st November 2025)Children and Young PeopleAdults (Physical Disability)Adults(LD services)0-4 Weeks (0-28 days)3960>4 to 8 Weeks (29-56 days)85160>8 to 13 weeks(57-91 days)74131> 13 to 18 weeks (92-126 days)83150> 18 to 26 weeks (127-182 days)95280> 26 to 39 weeks (183-273 days)221334> 39 to 52 weeks (274-364 days)216305> 52 weeks (365+ days)196142621The information provided is sourced directly from the Trust and not official statistics. It is subject to continuing data validation owing to Trust transition to completely digitised records via Encompass rollout.Health and Social Care Trusts follow the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP), to manage autism referrals. IEAP is a regional guidance document issued by the Department of Health, updated in June 2020. In line with IEAP, referrals for autism assessment are defined as routine referrals, and therefore managed in chronological order by referral date.
