question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any criteria set for rejecting candidates who had expressed an interest for the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer, North West posts.
answer_text: Candidates who expressed an interest in the Civil Service Administrative Officer  North West posts were requested to submit, an expression of interest to assess  whether applicants met the minimum eligibility criteria including age, right to work and the pre-agreed preferred experience requirements for the role. This involved a self-assessment, which asked candidates to rate their level of experience against a set of predefined administrative and organisational criteria that are relevant to AO duties. Each criterion carried a predetermined weighting reflecting its importance to the AO role. The candidate’s total score was calculated by combining their responses across all seven criteria and meeting the minimum eligibility criteria. The top scoring candidates, over 900, will be invited to interview. It is also important to note, that candidates who have not been called for interview on 26 or 27 of November, will remain on the waiting list and could be brought forward to interview at a later stage should further vacancies arise over the course of the next 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how the €6.4m announced by the Irish Government for the cross-border pilot regional cooperation programme on tackling Bovine TB will be spent in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The €6.4 million in Shared Island funding will enable the development and delivery of a regionalised pilot project in partnership with the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM) in the Republic of Ireland. 		The money is anticipated to be spent on a range of proposals within the pilot, including wildlife intervention implementation and preparatory work; people measures including on farm biosecurity advice and additional cattle measures.  	Further engagement with DAFM, the TB Partnership Steering Group and farmers in the proposed area is ongoing.  Final decisions on the proposals for inclusion in the pilot are anticipated to be made early in the New Year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34370/22-27, to detail the spend (i) annually; and (ii) at Christmas, for anti-drink driving campaigns, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The expenditure on anti - drink drive campaigns for each of the last five years is as follows: Financial Year Expenditure on anti – drink drive campaigns at ChristmasTotal Annual Spend anti – drink drive campaign2020/21*£52,596.59£81,569.212021/22*£46,440.14£94,893.132022/23£0.00£0.002023/24£0.00£0.002024/25£128,852.16£128,852.16Total£333,518.85£410,943.46*For the financial years 2020/21 and 2021/22, anti-drink driving campaigns were delivered during both the summer and Christmas periods.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34370/22-27, to detail how she plans to reduce the 72 per cent increase in convictions since 2020.
answer_text: The Road safety Strategy to 2030 was published in September 2024. The Road Safety Strategic Forum, chaired by DfI, and including representatives from the emergency services and other departments and organisations, steers and monitors the progress of the Strategy and its accompanying action plan.  My Department is taking forward a number of interventions under the associated Road Safety Action Plan 2024/25 to address issues of road safety, including: -A review of the drink driving legislative framework with a view to exploring options to introduce a lower legal alcohol limit applicable to all drivers;agreeing a contract for the continued delivery of Courses for Drink Driving Offenders; a proven education tool in the rehabilitation of drink driving offenders; andpreparing options in order to consult on proposals for primary legislation, with enabling powers, which will permit specified limits for drugs to be set out in subordinate legislation to address the issue of drug driving.Additionally, a new Action Plan, to support the targets of the Road Safety Strategy to 2030, is currently being developed by the road safety partners, which will include specific actions focused on drink and drug driving, amongst other measures.  On 20 November I launched the Department’s new anti-drug driving campaign aimed at challenging the behaviours of drug drivers and reinforcing the message that there is no place for drugs behind the wheel.While I very much welcome any enforcement actions leading to convictions for those who choose to flout the law, the figure of 72% includes statistics for 2020 which would have been impacted by the Covid pandemic, and is not reflective of the trend of convictions, as the table cited in my response to AQW 34370/22-27 shows in relation to the years 2021-2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what (a) hospitals; and (b) health centres, provide a registered Changing Places facility; and (ii) any actions being taken to increase this.
answer_text: Below are the details of the number and location of Changing Places facilities provided by each Health and Social Care Trust.    Northern Health and Social Care Trust  The NHSCT has two Changing Places facilities with one located at Antrim Hospital and one within the Ballymena Health & Care Centre.     South Eastern Health and Care Trust  The SEHSCT has one Changing Places facility within the Lisburn Primary and Community Care Centre located on the Lagan Valley Hospital Site.  Southern Health and Social Care Trust  The SHSCT has two fully compliant Changing Places facilities with one located at the Banbridge CTCC site and one located at the Blossom Paediatrics building at Craigavon Area Hospital.     Belfast Health and Social Care Trust  The BHSCT does not yet have a Changing Place facility in any of its acute hospital sites.     Western Health and Social Care Trust   No response received.  In light of the severe financial challenges facing the Northern Ireland Health & Social Care (HSC) sector, as well as the competing demands on the capital budget, the department has no plans to centrally fund the provision of Changing Places facilities in any existing hospitals or health centres where currently not provided. HSC Trusts are however provided with general capital funding monies and may consider the provision of these facilities from this allocation based on their own assessment of need and options available.  Since June 2022 amendments to building regulations require that all new build hospitals and primary care buildings and those whole hospital or primary care buildings formed by a material change of use of existing facilities should provide at least one changing places facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the correspondence from the Mayor of Derry and Strabane District Council, asking her to introduce Caoimhé’s Law to criminalise the unauthorised recording and sharing of images from fatal and serious road traffic collisions.
answer_text: While responsibility for any new law and penalties/offences relating to it falls outside my Department’s policy remit, I am keen to voice support for the introduction of Caoimhè’s Law. I have agreed to attend a roundtable meeting, organised by Road Victim Support Northern Ireland-Donegal (RSVNID), that will include representatives from the Department of Justice and other relevant policing and communications bodies. Previously I agreed to share Road Victim Support Northern Ireland-Donegal (RVSNID) campaigns, including “Drive to Arrive” and “Not Your Story to Tell” on the Department’s social media platforms. Both campaigns were highlighted when RVSNID signed up to the Department’s “Share the Road To Zero” campaign in June 2025. I am happy to continue this support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why final year postgraduate medical students at Queens University cannot access his Department's bursary funding; and (ii) whether he has any plans to change the bursary rules.
answer_text: The Education (Student Support) (No.2) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009 preclude students who already hold a primary degree awarded by an institution in Northern Ireland or Great Britain, from obtaining fee loans or maintenance grants for a second primary degree. This approach is based on the principle that the limited financial resources available should be targeted at primary degree level, thereby allowing a greater number of students to experience third level study. My officials are conducting an ongoing review of the bursary funding arrangements for pre-registration health and social care students. The review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I am unable to provide any further detail until the review is finalised and options developed for consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures she is taking to support small and family businesses.
answer_text: My department and Invest NI are fully committed to ensuring that small and family businesses get the support they need to thrive. Through Invest NI, councils, and other economic development partners, we continue to encourage and support small and family businesses to be more ambitious, more innovative and become export oriented.Invest NI has developed a wide range of solutions specifically designed to support small and family businesses. Examples include its Access to Finance programme which provides a suite of debt and equity funds for start-ups, micro businesses and SMEs, whose growth is constrained by a lack of traditional finance. Over £100 million is committed to the current suite of funds and, when combined with targeted private leverage of £150 million, over £250 million will be invested into start-ups and SMEs over the next ten years.  Furthermore, 250 locally owned SMEs are currently being supported to begin their export journey through Invest NI’s Ambition to Grow programme and its new Business Innovation Grant is supporting 128 locally owned SMEs to turn innovative ideas into viable businesses. Invest NI’s recently published Performance Report (2020-21 to 2024-25) sets out the impact it is having. The report announced that it has supported over 10,000 investments in the last 5 years, which contributed towards over £2.5 billion of investment that will lead to the creation of 16,974 jobs. Locally owned businesses accounted for 93% of these investments and 92% were by small and medium sized enterprises.  To further support small and family businesses, I have also established new Local Economic Partnerships across all council areas. Decisions on the projects to be developed will be made by the Partnerships themselves in response to what they have identified to be the local economic needs in their areas. Councils and stakeholders in Local Economic Partnerships are being provided with support and access to funding of £45 million through the Regional Balance Fund to identify and work together to deliver projects aimed at addressing their economic priorities and needs at a local level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any measures he is taking to reduce the tax burden on small and family businesses.
answer_text: The British Government is responsible for the vast majority of taxes faced by our small businesses, and I regularly engage with the business community to understand the challenges they are facing and the measures my department can take to support them. I have pressed the Chancellor to deliver a tax regime which is fair, proportionate and progressive; one that delivers for our economy and the businesses within it. In my meeting along with the deputy First Minister with the Chancellor on 12 August, we discussed the implications of tax changes on businesses here, including Employers’ National Insurance, VAT and Inheritance Tax, and the need to recognise the impact that tax policies designed in London have here. I reiterated these issues in my letter to the Chief Secretary on 23 October, ahead of the British Government’s Budget.I also remain committed to supporting our local businesses with the powers that I currently have. In relation to the rating system, our only major devolved tax lever, I laid out in the Assembly on 18 November the steps I wish to take in relation to small business rate support following the Strategic Review of Rating, and the funding steps required to progress policy change.  A link to the statement is available at: Finance Minister-oral-statement-rating-policy-update-strategic-road-map. My department is also working to progress negotiations with the Treasury on a full Fiscal Framework, inclusive of the devolution of new fiscal powers, to ensure that the Executive has the fiscal levers necessary to deliver for our businesses and citizens in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any projects where Health and Social Care Trusts are using Artificial Intelligence to improve services.
answer_text: Health & Social Care (HSC) Trusts are using Artificial Intelligence in a range of projects to support health and care professionals (HCP) as well as staff working in administrative roles.This includes a number of developmental pilots using AI to simplify the tasks around meeting administration, from scheduling to agenda setting to meeting minutes generation; as well as AI to support HCP in clinical note-taking.AI in Brain Image Stroke Scanning for CT Brain Perfusion is being used to support clinicians in interpreting specific elements of the CT brain scans required for identifying patients for disability saving interventions (thrombolysis and IAT- intra-arterial therapy). This is envisaged to reduce the time to decision-making for both thrombolysis and IAT.AI Bone Image Fracture Scanning uses an AI-enhanced algorithm to support clinicians in reading X-rays within Health and Social Care Trust Emergency Departments (EDs). The project helps to identify acute fractures, effusions and dislocations in patients from the age of two, and can help reduce waiting times for patients in Minor Injuries by reducing reading time and errors.NI Ambulance Trust (NIAS) is using AI to support demand & capacity planning through an Integrated Analysis & Prediction model and within in the Ambulance Computer Aided Dispatch system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to the Domestic Homicide Review Panel report published on 13 November 2025, whether she will implement the recommendations to (i) raise public awareness of the Domestic Violence Disclosure Scheme; and (ii) provide support to women on how to stay safe on dating Apps.
answer_text: I want to begin by acknowledging the importance of the Domestic Homicide Review (DHR) process in helping us learn from these tragic cases and strengthen our collective response to domestic abuse. I am grateful to the independent Chair, the Review Panel members and, most importantly, to the family involved for their contribution, courage and cooperation throughout the review.It is important to note that recommendations arising from DHRs can span across a wide range of organisations, each of which is responsible for implementing the actions relevant to their own functions, and for working collaboratively to deliver cross-cutting recommendations.The Domestic Homicide Review Panel report published on 13 November 2025 included recommendations relating to raising public awareness of the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme (DVADS) and improving public understanding of how to stay safe when meeting people online, including through dating apps.The DVADS is an important safeguarding tool which enables individuals to make informed choices about relationships by allowing them to request information, or by enabling police to proactively disclose information, where there may be a risk of previous abusive behaviour. Since its introduction in Northern Ireland in 2018, my Department and the Police Service of Northern Ireland have taken a number of steps to promote awareness of the scheme. This has included a significant media campaign during 2023 and 2024, including television, radio and press activity; as well as the publication of guidance, regular social media promotion, and ongoing work with partners and the Department’s Press Office to identify opportunities to highlight the scheme to the public. I am also aware that PSNI now routinely links DVADS messaging into all domestic-abuse related media releases as standard, and that has undertaken internal activity to reinforce officer awareness of the scheme. DVADS was also a focus of last year’s 16 Days of Action campaign, during which the number of applications increased, supported by close working with crime colleagues. In addition, the PSNI has provided inputs at conferences aimed at older people and intimate partner relationships. While work continues, I understand that a bespoke campaign specifically focused on online safety for older people remains outstanding.I believe that the DVADS can provide a robust level of protection to the public, operating under clear principles that enable individuals to access potentially life-saving information where concerns exist. However, I recognise the importance of public awareness. I have asked my officials to ensure that DVADS messaging is incorporated into domestic abuse related media releases and my Department will continue to promote the DVADS scheme as part of its wider work to support victims and improve public understanding of the safeguarding tools available.The report also highlighted the need to improve awareness of online safety for those seeking frie

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the budget for the Small Woodland Grant Scheme, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department provides support to landowners for the creation of new native woodlands through the Small Woodland Grant Scheme. Funding for these payments is drawn from the overall Forest Service capital and resource allocations for forestry grants. The amounts drawn from the budget provision in respect of payments to landowners for completed years in 2024, 2023 and 2022 were £632,379, £1,050,397 and £748,590 respectively.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 34368/22-27, to detail (i) the number of unsuccessful applications; and (ii) the average length of time taken to reach a decision on an application.
answer_text: As of 17 November 2025, there have been 1,421 unsuccessful applications to the Scheme. The Victims’ Payments Board has advised that it is difficult to provide the average length of time taken to reach a decision on an application. Each case is unique, and progress depends on the receipt of supporting information from external organisations, which is outside of the Board’s control. The historical nature of the incidents included in applications, with some incidents taking place up to 50 years ago, makes retrieving certain records difficult or impossible. Additionally, around one in three applications involve multiple incidents, each requiring separate assessment against the legislative tests underpinning the Scheme. Every incident must be verified for eligibility and supported by evidence before a determination can be made. Following a recent review of operations, the Board has established a business transformation team to help ensure applications are progressed as efficiently as possible.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 34368/22-27, whether they will review the current deadline for applications to be submitted.
answer_text: Regulation 8 (2) (b) of the Victims' Payments Regulations 2020 provides the Secretary of State with the power to extend the closing date for the Scheme. It will be for the Secretary of State therefore, to determine if the deadline for applications to the Scheme should be extended.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of his Department's November 2025 report Farm Incomes in Northern Ireland for 2023-2024.
answer_text: The ‘Farm Incomes in Northern Ireland 2023/24’ statistical report provides detailed information on the outputs, inputs and incomes of the main farm types and enterprises within Northern Ireland agriculture.  In particular, the report shows that average Farm Business Income across all farm businesses above 0.5 Standard Labour Requirements (SLRs) decreased from £50,450 to £29,260 per farm between 2022/23 and 2023/24.  Furthermore, it shows that each of the individual farm types except for Pig farms and Cattle & Sheep (LFA) farms recorded a decrease in their average Farm Business Income between 2022/23 and 2023/24.  Similarly, it shows that between 2022/23 and 2023/24, average gross margins fell in 7 out of 11 main enterprises. These falls in Farm Business Incomes and gross margins were mainly due to lower output prices in the 2023/24 year.	It should be noted that market conditions in 2025 differ from those in the time period referred to in the publication.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of his Department's November 2025 report The Statistical Review of Northern Ireland Agriculture 2024, in relation to the 2.8 per cent reduction of people working in the agricultural sector; and (ii) to detail any actions his Department is taking to stop this reduction.
answer_text: Table 1 below shows the agricultural labour force in Northern Ireland for the most recent 5 years for which data is available.  This shows that the number of workers has remained largely consistent over this time.  The observed reduction in the total agriculture labour force of 2.8 per cent between 2023 and 2024 is not considered to be an indicator of a recent trend and is not a significant change. Table 1 – Total Agricultural Labour Force, Agricultural Census in Northern Ireland, 2020-2024YearTotal Agricultural Labour Force (number of persons)202051,301202152,195202251,760202352,676202451,213My department undertakes the following actions to provide training, promote careers and deliver support for the agriculture sector: CAFRE, Greenmount Campus has a well-qualified and experienced Agriculture teaching team who deliver a range of nationally awarded and Apprenticeship Agriculture education programmes to approximately 900 students per year. These courses, which range from Level 2 to BSc Honours Degree, are designed to meet the needs of the agriculture industry by developing the knowledge and skills of students and trainees to undertake operational, supervisory and management roles. CAFRE undertakes a range of activities to promote careers in the agriculture industry including an annual “Opportunities for Employment in Agriculture’’ event. The objective of this event is to connect students with employers from the agriculture industry who are looking to recruit placement students and/or graduates, either now or in the future. In 2025 over 40 exhibitors attended which included small, medium, and large agricultural businesses with a wide range of job roles and placement opportunities available.  In addition, Bursary and Scholarship Awards are awarded from Agri-Food companies to students studying on Agriculture degree programmes through CAFRE’s Industry Support Programme.  This programme enables Agriculture businesses to invest in the future workforce through offering tangible support to CAFRE students.The Sustainable Agriculture Programme’s overall objective is to transition to a more sustainable farming sector by seeking to implement policies and strategies that benefit our climate and environment, while, very importantly, supporting our economically and socially significant agri-food sector. The SAP provides options for all farm businesses in Northern Ireland to secure and improve their viability and environmental sustainability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of his Department's November 2025 report Air Pollution in Northern Ireland 2024, in relation to findings at the monitoring stations in Strabane and Newtownstewart.
answer_text: The Air Pollution in Northern Ireland 2024 report demonstrates that air quality monitoring stations at Strabane and Newtownstewart both monitored particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5) in 2024, with Strabane also monitoring sulphur dioxide. Both sites were compliant with the Air Quality Standards Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2010 limit values, target values and corresponding 2007 UK Air Quality Strategy objectives.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any scoliosis services available; and (ii) the number of patients referred to England for operations, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Belfast Trust provides spinal and scoliosis surgery for adults and children in Northern Ireland.  It is provided in Royal Victoria and RBHSC Hospitals, by spinal surgeons within the Trauma and Orthopaedic serviceThe Belfast Trust spinal surgery service is fully commissioned to support a team of 8 surgeons and associated clinical infrastructure, including neurophysiology to deliver elective spinal surgery and trauma care for adults and children.  In recent years there have been challenges in maintaining the full complement of specialist medical staff, impacting on service availability. This has been compounded by difficulties in the recruitment of neurophysiology staff required to undertake spinal cord monitoring, due to national shortages in this service.  My officials have engaged with the Trust on these challenges and are developing a stabilisation plan including support from the wider NHS.  This is to facilitate greater service resilience, increase capacity and reduce waiting times.  The stabilisation plan will allow the Trust to secure additional spinal surgery capacity in 2025/26 and for two further years, facilitating a reduction in waiting times for scoliosis spinal surgery whilst also facilitating the required training and recruitment of spinal cord monitoring staff. Belfast Trust have advised the number of patients referred to England for operations through their contractual arrangements, in each of the last three years is:  Financial Year              Patients 22/231523/24024/252325/2632*                  *32 referrals total in year, with 29 still active with 3 surgeries completed Patients have also been referred for scoliosis surgery in England through SPPG Extra Contractual Referral processes.  Given the overlap in providers delivering scoliosis activity in England under Belfast Trust processes and ECR arrangements, it has not been possible to validate this information within the timescale set for this response. The figures above are therefore likely to be an underreporting of total activity.In addition to the activity being secured through England, Belfast Trust have secured an independent sector contract for intraoperative spinal cord monitoring which is supporting a level of scoliosis activity locally.  This has recently commenced and will increase the overall outturn in scoliosis activity by year end.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any action he is taking to reduce the waiting list for an ADHD assessment for children and young people in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, which was sitting at 2187 for quarter ending March 2025.
answer_text: There are currently 452 children awaiting assessment in the Western Trust area. Whilst that number is high, it is important to note that across Northern Ireland as per the end of September there were 6,409 children and young people waiting for an ADHD assessment in Northern Ireland. But I absolutely accept and agree that the length of some of those waits within the 452 in the Western Trust are wholly unacceptable. There has been a significant increase in the number of children referred for an ADHD assessment across the Region, regrettably ADHD services do not currently have the capacity to meet this need. In common with other neurodiversity services, funding in respect of ADHD in Northern Ireland has failed to keep pace with this emerging area of clinical need. I am committed to addressing this through investment, subject to funding being available. The Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework published in May of this year proposes a revised approach to neurodiversity which is inclusive, focused upon early intervention and aligned to the needs of children and their families. My Department has commissioned a needs assessment in respect of ADHD. To interrogate existing service capacity against rising demand. This will assist in clarifying areas where investment will be necessary. This assessment will help to inform options around the development of a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts. I expect this report to be submitted to my Department this week. In tandem with this exercise, the Department is working with the Health and Social Care Trusts in the development of regionally consistent neurodevelopmental pathways which will afford an opportunity for needs to be assessed and help to be offered at an earlier stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Statistics on Smoking Cessation Services in Northern Ireland, published in November 2025; and (ii) to detail any further action his Department can take to reduce smoking levels.
answer_text: Recently published statistics on smoking cessation services in Northern Ireland show that the number of people availing of these services continues to rise while smoking prevalence has decreased.  In 2024/25, a total of 9,587 people were reported to have set a quit date through smoking cessation services in Northern Ireland, an increase of 391 (4%) on the previous year. Over the same period, 558 pregnant women accessed services, of whom 74% quit successfully after four weeks - higher than the overall Northern Ireland average. This reflects the bespoke and specialist stop smoking service provided to pregnant women in the hospital setting.These positive developments reflect the successful impact of the NI Tobacco Control Strategy and the ongoing efforts across the Public Health Agency (PHA), Community Pharmacy, Health and Social Care Trusts, GPs and the Community and Voluntary sector to increase footfall to Stop Smoking Services across Northern Ireland. Ongoing co-ordinated action led by the Public Health Agency, in partnership with these sectors, ensures that the 13% of our population who continue to smoke have access to the support and advice they need to successfully quit. The Tobacco and Vapes Bill, currently progressing through Parliament, will create a smoke-free generation, so that anyone born on or after 1 January 2009 can never legally be sold tobacco.  The NI Assembly endorsed Northern Ireland’s inclusion in the Bill through a Legislative Consent Motion in February 2025.  This is a landmark step which provides us with an opportunity to protect our children, young people and future generations from the harms of tobacco and nicotine addiction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to address issues with flooding, in the area of Kevlin Road and Kevlin Gardens, Omagh.
answer_text: Departmental officals are not currently aware of any flooding issues in the vicinity of Kelvin Road and Kelvin Gardens. However, a site inspection will be undertaken during the next period of heavy rainfall to review the nature of the reported flooding and if remedial measures are required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Keep Northern Ireland Beautiful Marine Litter Report 2024, which recorded an average of 341 items of litter per 100m of beach in 2024.
answer_text: My department continues to support the Marine Litter Report. The data collected since 2012 has contributed significantly to establishing a comprehensive evidence base, guiding our actions and underpinning long-term environmental protection efforts.Although the number of reported items remains high, there has been clear progress compared to the 2023 figure of 542 items per 100 meters of beach. Marine litter persists as a challenging pollution issue, requiring collective responsibility and action. I am confident that through continued collaboration, enhanced public awareness, and robust policy measures, we can make substantial progress in addressing this problem.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any data his Department holds on the number of children living in fuel poor households
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the NIHE, as they carry out the data collection for the Northern Ireland House Condition Survey. She has advised me as follows: Fuel poverty figures in Northern Ireland are obtained from the House Condition Survey.  The most recent survey results available are from 2016 (until the 2023 House Condition Survey results are published). The number of children in fuel poverty is not included within the 2016 House Condition Survey report.  However, analysis of the 2016 dataset indicates that there were approximately 54,000 children living in fuel poor households in Northern Ireland.  A child in this instance is defined as 15 or younger.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any funding from their Department for the Unlock Safety Initiative.
answer_text: The EVAWG Change Fund invests in community-based action to help end violence against women and girls, this operates in two strands: Local and Regional. Women’s Aid Federation NI is one of eight organisations to receive funding as part of the Regional Change Fund, administered on behalf of TEO, by Belfast City Council. Women’s Aid Federation NI has so far received just under £20,000 for the Unlock Safety Initiative. The Initiative is designed to equip future nurses with an understanding of how to deal with the issue of domestic abuse and violence against women and girls, providing them with sustained practical knowledge to support victims and play an active role in building a safer society. The Initiative is expected to exceed initial expectations by reaching over 600 nursing students through collaboration with the Royal College of Nursing and the School of Nursing & Midwifery, Queen’s University Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any funding allocated by his Department to the Bookstart Baby programme, in each of the last 3 years, broken down by Health Trust area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional estimated (i) cost; and (ii) capacity, implications for introducing Free School Meals to all children.
answer_text: Modelling was previously undertaken as part of my Department’s review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants. This was based on the projected additional recurring costs in 2025/26 compared to the projected cost to provide FSM to the numbers of pupils that were modelled to be entitled at the then income threshold of £15,000. The estimated annual additional resource costs to provide FSM to all full-time pupils in 2025/26 was estimated to be £142m. It is not possible to effectively estimate the very significant capital expenditure that would be required to increase the kitchen and dining hall capacity that would be necessary in the nearly 1,100 schools in Northern Ireland. Such an increase in FSM provision would inevitably have very significant implications for the Education Authority in terms of the sustainability of its service delivery model and its ability to deliver a very significant increase in the number of meals it would be required to produce each day.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action she has taken over the last year to narrow the gap in spending per head on public transport in Northern Ireland, which is significantly lower than in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: The current financial year has been extremely challenging from a budgetary perspective for both Translink and my Department, which has been operating in a constrained financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  The Department did not receive all the funding it requested for Budget 2025-26. Therefore, the resource allocation to Translink did not meet its requirements in full, nevertheless it did receive nearly 24% of the Department’s resource budget.  Translink also received more than 33% of the general capital budgets allocated to the Department.I will continue to communicate the financial pressures on the public transport network to the Executive and advocate for further funding to support its delivery.

answer_text: My Department does not hold information on the number of agency staff on assignment in the Civil Service who have subsequently secured permanent appointments, broken down by Department, for each of the last five years. This data is not collected or maintained centrally by NICS HR.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans he has to review and update Civil Service pension rules.
answer_text: My Department is currently assessing further technical rule changes with reference to the administration of the Civil Service Pension Scheme, which will be progressed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 33722/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 33722/22-27 was answered on 11 December 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 33908/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any further actions her Department plans to take to reduce the skills gap, following the publication of the Northern Ireland Employer Skills Survey 2024.
answer_text: The Employer Skills Survey provides a vital source of intelligence on skills issues faced by employers and covers a range of topics including skills gaps, and we will consider the findings of this publication.The recent launch of the Skills Action Plan has a forward-looking framework that will equip our people with the skills required to meet both the current and future demands of our economy. My approach to identify opportunities for collaborating with industry and education partners is nurturing new ways of working. I will continue to invest in our universities, further education colleges and apprenticeships, to ensure that individuals are equipped with the knowledge and capabilities needed to thrive in an increasingly dynamic and competitive labour market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimated (i) cost; and (ii) number of people affected, by reinstating the automatic passporting of Help with Health Costs, in each of the next three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Over 195,000 current recipients of Universal Credit in Northern Ireland will be eligible for automatic passporting to Help with Health Costs from 1 December 2025. The underlying threshold eligibility criteria for the Help with Health Costs have not changed, rather, some individuals who may have previously accessed support through the Low-Income Scheme will now be automatically passported. As a result, it is not possible to accurately estimate the number of individuals who will benefit from the reinstatement of automatic passporting, or to provide a reliable assessment of the overall impact on cost or service delivery.The Department of Health does not have access to data on Universal Credit recipients so therefore we are not in a position to provide any more detailed breakdown.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prisoners released early by mistake, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: There have been no erroneous releases from any Northern Ireland prison during the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the estimated annual savings of (i) time; and (ii) money, through the issuing of new procurement guidance by his Department, for each of the next three years.
answer_text: The recently revised and republished guidance (DAO 05/22 Grant or Procurement) will remove unnecessary bureaucracy and simplify procedures for those public bodies who administer grants, allowing projects to be delivered faster and more efficiently. My Department does not issue any direct grant funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timescale for the introduction of secondary legislation to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking.
answer_text: Following my recent announcement to introduce further measures to address inconsiderate pavement parking across the North, I have asked my officials to take forward work to develop the necessary secondary legislation to introduce these new measures. It is my aim to introduce these legislative changes in the latter part of 2026, subject to no significant issues arising during the process.

answer_text: The Ending the Harm campaign is wholly funded by the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC). EPPOC is a cross-departmental programme, jointly financed by the Northern Ireland Executive and the UK Government, and I am the lead Minister.  The breakdown of the campaign budget is as follows:YearStaff and admin costsTV, social media and radioOther costsTotal2020-21£34,200Online - £8,250.00Outdoor - £16,607.47TV - £8,556.00£26,257.51£93,870.982021-22£43,701.25Online - £42,208.28Outdoor - £50,895.87Radio - £22,401.98TV - £76,360.00VOD* - £9,200.00£18,722.67	£263,490.052022-23£35,935.00Online - £28,613.85Outdoor - £45,514.02Radio - £15,242.03TV - £57,892.50VOD - £15,066.00£51,057.48£249,320.882023-24£47,335.00Online - £24,576.20Outdoor - £44,180.85Sunday Press - £557.26TV - £61,659.01VOD - £3,952.50£73,431.97£255,692.792024-25£21,237.50Online - £24,139.77Outdoor - £112,718.58TV - £17,205.00VOD - £744.00£48,076.96£224,121.81TOTAL£182,408.75£686,544.17£217,546.59£1,086,496.51*VOD (Video On Demand) refers to streaming services that let users watch content anytime, such as Sky Go, ITVX, and 4OD.A December 2024 Online Omnibus Survey of a representative sample of the Northern Ireland adult (18+) population, showed that 89% of respondents fully supported what the campaign was saying and trying to do; 77% of respondents regarded the campaign as believable; and 66% of thought it was impactful. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Ex-post Evaluation of the Northern Ireland Rural Development Programme 2014-2020.
answer_text: I welcome the Ex-Post Evaluation of the Northern Ireland Rural Development Programme 2014-2020 undertaken by Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency and its conclusions and recommendations.  Total expenditure under the Programme was over €433m including €228.42m of EU funds.The evaluation found that the Northern Ireland Rural Development programme 2014-2020 was successful in improving competitiveness in agriculture, spending over €73m on promoting knowledge transfer, innovation, new technology adaptation and structural change within the sector. A further €236m was spent on enhancing environmental land management that yielded positive results at local level for biodiversity, water, soils and climate. However, the evaluation does outline shortcomings as regards agri-environment schemes and makes a range of recommendations for future schemes including the development of legislation to enhance outcomes from future investment. The evaluation also reported that the programme was successful in strengthening rural communities by spending £91.6m on supporting businesses, creating jobs and building infrastructure that enhanced tourism and provided essential services. Lessons learned across the programme, identified in ex-post evaluation will be carefully considered in the design and development future agriculture, environment and rural community strategic policy frameworks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions she is taking to reverse the 701 decline in employee jobs in the Derry City and Strabane local council area, outlined in the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency's Business Register and Employment Survey Statistics 2023.
answer_text: The Business Register and Employment Survey Statistics 2023 refers to the number of employee jobs as of September 2023.  In February 2024, my predecessor Conor Murphy took up the role of Minister for the Economy and set four priorities of increasing the number of good jobs, raising productivity, decarbonising the economy and improving regional balance. Since that time, my Department has progressed a number of initiatives to boost economic development and job creation across the north, including in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area.These have included:Progressing an Employment Rights Bill;Establishing the Magee Taskforce and supporting its work with £21.5m of Capital funding;Providing £3m of annual funding to secure important routes for City of Derry airport;Providing assistance to FinTru, Seagate, EY, and Alchemy Technologies for job creation and investment through Invest NI support; andCreating Local Economic Partnerships to address local economic challenges, and supporting them with a £45m Regional Growth Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions she is taking to reverse the 2.4 per cent decline in construction industry jobs, as outlined in the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency's Business Register and Employment Survey Statistics 2023.
answer_text: Measures are being taken by the Department for the Economy to increase jobs in the construction sector.My Department works closely with training providers, individuals, and employers to increase employment opportunities within the construction sector by facilitating apprenticeship opportunities. Alongside funding off-the-job training for apprenticeships, my department offers an incentive payment to employers upon the successful completion of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship. In addition, the Construction Industry Training Board (CITB NI), an Arm’s Length Body of my department, provides Employer Apprentice Grants to further support construction sector employers who take on apprentices.CITB NI also promotes careers in construction by partnering with schools and colleges to highlight industry opportunities and hosting a range of Careers Events. It has a comprehensive online website and an information hub dedicated to promoting career opportunities in the construction sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the timescale for the introduction of the broadcasting of sentencing remarks by judges in courts.
answer_text: As I advised in answer to AQW 27681/22-27, primary legislation will be needed to provide the Department with a power to permit the recording and broadcasting of certain court proceedings. Given the current legislative programme, this will have to be progressed in the next Assembly mandate, subject to the agreement of an incoming Minister and a new Executive.Should primary legislation be enacted, secondary legislation would then be required to permit the recording and broadcasting of the handing down of judgments in the Court of Appeal and of judges making sentencing remarks in the Crown Court.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of Healthy Child, Healthy Future: Health Review Statistics for Northern Ireland 2024-25; (ii) to detail why the number of reviews undertaken, both on time and in total, was lower than the previous year; and (iii) to detail any action his Department is taking to reverse those figures.
answer_text: I acknowledge the importance of the child health reviews delivered under the Healthy Child, Healthy Future (HCHF) framework. These reviews play a vital role in the early identification of health and developmental needs, supporting families, and reducing inequalities.The number of health reviews completed in 2024/25, both on time and overall, was slightly lower than in 2023/24 due to a smaller overall cohort of children during this period. I welcome that the 12 month contact rate has improved, rising from 85% in 2023/24 to 93% in 2024/25. The refreshed Healthy Child, Healthy Future framework, launched in September 2025, sets out a strengthened approach to early identification and support for children. Delivery will be phased and prioritised in partnership with HSC Trusts, with a focus on service redesign and workforce planning, ensuring high-quality support continues within available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding allocated to support community pharmacies in West Tyrone, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In general, my Department commissions community pharmacy services on a Northern Ireland-wide basis and contracts those services with individual community pharmacy contractors under the general pharmaceutical services (GPS).  In order to comply with information governance advice, my Department cannot release information on the payments made to individual contractors. Information on the total payments made to community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland in each of the 5 years between and 2019/20 and 2023/24 is shown in Table 1 below.  Data for 2024/25 is not included as figures are still to be finalised.Table 1: Payments made for Community Pharmacy Services in Northern Ireland by Financial YearHSCNI Payments for Community Pharmacy Services (£ millions)*2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24£119£152£152£144£163* All figures rounded to the nearest £100,000,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the (i) Surfers Against Sewage’s April 2025 report Water Quality Report 2025; and (ii) calls for Northern Irish Water to provide real-time sewage alerts.
answer_text: I am aware of this report and recognise the concerns regarding sewage in our water. More data is required to fully assess the impact of wastewater infrastructure on water quality. NIW is rolling out the installation of storm overflow monitors (Event Duration Monitors), during its current Price Control Process (PC21). For these, my department is preparing guidance on the regulatory requirements for the installation, quality assurance, monitoring, and reporting requirements, which it plans to publish in early 2026. My department has launched a dashboard to monitor Bathing Water Quality at all 33 designated sites in Northern Ireland, helping the public choose when and where to swim: https://daerani.maps.arcgis.com/apps/dashboards/d676449ba9794912aa21ac8904e14cb8
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process for deciding the timings of when the community pharmacy out of hours service in each area is open.
answer_text: During 2023/24 officials from my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group, in conjunction with the community pharmacy representative body, Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), completed a review of Community Pharmacy Rota provision across Northern Ireland to ensure that level of provision of community pharmacy services adequately meets the needs of the population. The review process was informed by monitoring activity during Sunday and Public holiday rotas and feedback provided from participating pharmacies via CPNI.  As part of the review process, feedback on the proposed changes was obtained via a consultation process with the Community/Voluntary and Elected Representatives Engagement Forum.Where additional weekday opening is required in specific towns and cities, local rota arrangements are agreed with local pharmacy contractors, to ensure that the public have access to pharmacy services outside of routine contracted hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people claiming pension credits, in each of the past 5 years; and (ii) his Department’s targets to increase uptake of pension credits through raising awareness.
answer_text: The number of people claiming Pension Credit in the past 5 years is detailed in the table belowMay 202163,450May 202261,100May 202360,010May 202459,220May 202560,210This information can also be found at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/system/files/2025-08/bss-pension-credit-may-2025.xlsxWhile my Department does not have a specific target to increase uptake of Pension Credit, it aims to do so through awareness campaigns, with the most recent advertising campaign launched on 27 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the contents of the Memorandum of Understanding signed with the America250 Commission; and (ii) any celebrations planned regarding the Strabane native, John Dunlap, who produced the first printed version of the Declaration of Independence.
answer_text: Contents of the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) signed with the America250 CommissionThe MoU established the basis for a cooperative relationship to encourage bilateral cooperation to promote the Semiquincentennial anniversary both in the United States of America (US) and in Northern Ireland (NI).Both signatories recognised that a nation’s culture can add significant value to their educational, academic and business activities. Historical perspectives provide essential insights into understanding the issues of the future, helping to articulate their distinct national identity and enhancing mutual understanding between our two countries.The MoU recognises opportunities for my Department to contribute to events commemorating the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence. Those opportunities are further underpinned by the importance of the wider story of migration from Ulster to North America over the past 250 years.The MOU outlines a framework for future cooperation between my Department and the America250 Commission. My Officials are separately developing a programme of engagement and activities organised by a number of cultural institutions including the Public Record Office of Northern Ireland, National Museums Northern Ireland, Historic Royal Palaces/Hillsborough Castle, and the Ulster-Scots Agency in Northern Ireland with collaboration from the National Archives and Records Administration, the Library of Congress, and the Smithsonian Institution in the US.The MoU also noted and welcomed the invite extended to me to attend the international event in the US in June 2026; and the invite to members of the America250 Commission to visit the Ulster American Folk Park and other cultural organisations in Northern Ireland. Celebrations planned regarding the Strabane native, John Dunlap, who produced the first printed version of the Declaration of Independence.My Officials are exploring several options to commemorate the key role played by John Dunlap in the founding of the United States.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the quarterly (i) average waiting time; and (ii) longest waiting time, for biopsy results for cancer diagnosis, in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the last review into pathology turnaround performance across Health and Social Care Trust laboratories took place; (ii) what that review recommended; and (iii) were those recommendations implemented.
answer_text: My Department recognises that turnaround times within some pathology discipline are unacceptable. Action is ongoing to understand the causes of delay and to deliver sustainable, system-wide improvement.The most recent strategic review of pathology services was undertaken through the public consultation on proposals to modernise and transform Health and Social Care (HSC) Pathology Services, launched following the publication of Health and Wellbeing 2026: Delivering Together in October 2016. That review identified the need for greater regional collaboration, service reconfiguration, workforce sustainability, digital enablement and strengthened governance to improve performance and resilience across pathology services.The recommendations from that review have been taken forward through a programme of phased implementation. In November 2021 the then Minister of Health published a Policy Statement committing to the transformation of HSC Pathology Services through four key actions: the reconfiguration of services, the establishment of a single regional management structure, the introduction of a single Northern Ireland Pathology Information Management System (NIPIMS), and the delivery of service-led transformation initiatives.Delivery of these commitments is being coordinated through the Northern Ireland Pathology Network, which works across six HSC organisations, including the five Trusts and the Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service. This highly complex and regulated service is currently undergoing significant transformation in line with departmental policy direction.Progress to date includes region-wide service improvement programmes delivered through nine specialty forums, including digital pathology implementation enabling remote and more timely reporting; the regional delivery of a new cervical cytology service; expanded advanced practitioner roles for biomedical and clinical scientists to increase reporting capacity;  prioritisation of investment for the development of an integrated diagnostic service for haematological malignancies in line with the Cancer Strategy; implementation of a new Laboratory Information Management System; and alignment with the new Electronic Care Record programme, Encompass.Further work is underway, including regional reviews of workforce, 24/7 service provision and sample transport, procurement programmes to replace critical equipment, and exploration of regional reporting networks for histopathology. The Blueprint Programme, launched in January 2022, is also progressing proposals to establish a single regional pathology management agency by 2028.While significant progress has been made in implementing the recommendations arising from the earlier review, I acknowledge that further collaborative efforts are required to fully deliver this programme of reform and to ensure timely, high-quality pathology services that are sustainable for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of Animal Justice Project’s report about on antibiotic resistance on Northern Ireland’s pig farms.
answer_text: Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) is a recognised “One Health” global threat. DAERA actions to confront Antimicrobial Resistance by 2040, are included in the UK AMR National Action Plan (2024-2029), and my Department has a developed Implementation Plan which is taking forward a broad range of specific deliverables to achieve positive future outcomes.DAERA undertakes routine monitoring of AMR in pigs at slaughter, as part of UK and EU surveillance programmes for the purpose of establishing trends and allowing participating countries to assess risks.  In 2023 NI completed EU statutory AMR monitoring for pigs for the first time, in five zoonotic and indicator bacteria, providing an initial NI baseline for future reference.The Animal Justice Project has highlighted one of the 2023 EU monitoring metrics, higher levels of Multi Drug Resistance (MDR) which requires considered interpretation. MDR is heavily influenced by the types of Salmonella found. The Salmonella bacteria found in 2023 NI pig monitoring were mainly S. Typhimurium, its monophasic form and S. Derby which are known to carry extremely high levels of MDR to veterinary antibiotics.  While I do not wish to downplay the concerning results in 2023, it is too early to understand the comparative significance of these findings and the survey will be repeated in 2025 and 2027.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to reduce delays in cancer diagnostic results.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges that there are currently delays in reporting turnaround times within some tumour site pathways. Work is underway to understand the underlying causes and implement sustainable improvements.In the interim, immediate mitigation measures are being applied. These include exploring opportunities for mutual support across Trusts and, where appropriate, utilising capacity within the independent sector to improve reporting times and maintain service continuity.In Northern Ireland and across the UK, pathology services are currently facing significant challenges, these include increased demand and complexity, recruitment and retention challenges exacerbated by the aging workforce, economic pressures with ongoing financial constraints.These challenges are unfortunately leading to strain on pathology service capacity. My Department is working with HSC Trust Pathology Services to progress a range of actions to reduce delays in cancer diagnostic reporting. These include:Recruitment campaigns for Biomedical Scientists (BMS) and Pathologists: these have resulted in recent successful appointments, with continued efforts underway despite challenges posed by low applicant response rates.A regional project is underway to explore the establishment of a reporting network designed to reduce delays in cancer diagnostic results.Ensuring clinical triage processes are in place to prioritise turnaround times for cancer cases. Outsourcing of low-complexity cases to reduce backlogs and prioritise cancer casesProcurement of new diagnostic equipment and investment in digital pathology which will enable flexible and remote reporting.Maximisation of skill mix and advanced roles for Biomedical Scientists, which in turn will free up consultant time for more complex work. Training programmes are also underway for Bio Medical Scientists in histology dissection and consultant-level reporting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the suspected case of notifiable avian flu found in commercial poultry premises in Pomeroy, Co Tyrone.
answer_text: A suspect case of notifiable Avian Influenza was reported on Saturday 01 November 2025 at a commercial poultry premises near Pomeroy, County Tyrone. A Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) has been established, effective from 21:00 hrs on Sunday 02 November 2025, following preliminary positive results for Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI). The decision was taken based on a number of factors including the clinical signs and preliminary results provided by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI).  All poultry at the site have been humanely culled as a disease control measure.  The TCZ comprises a 3km TCZ-A and a 10km TCZ-B. The declaration, which details all the measures that must be adhered to within the 3km and 10km zones, can be found on the DAERA website, as follows:  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/disease-control-zone-declarations
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of animal abuse cases (i) reported to his Department; and (ii) prosecuted, for each of the past five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, my Department is responsible for enforcing the provisions that relate to farmed animals only.  For the purposes of this response, cases of animal abuse, has been interpreted as animal welfare concerns reported to my Department by members of the public and other authorities, for investigation. DAERA Veterinary Service inspectors may utilise a range of options when dealing with an animal welfare case in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy. For example, they may provide advice, give a warning, issue the owner with a legally binding Improvement Notice, impose financial penalties including reduction/withholding of Single Farm Payments or animal disease compensation payments or initiate a prosecution.The response below details the information requested with regard to animal welfare cases reported to, and prosecuted by, my Department.  Total figures are detailed, as figures are not recorded on a Constituency basis. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action she has taken to improve the structural integrity of the road network, since the Independent Review into the Funding Requirements for Structural Maintenance of the Northern Ireland road network was published.
answer_text: There has been historic underfunding for the maintenance of the road network for many years, due to over a decade of underfunding and austerity by the Britsih Government.  I am keen to improve our infrastructure with the limited funding available to me as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy. My officials continue to work closely with the Executive to secure long-term funding and, in particular, to highlight the need for multi-year budgets.I have also asked my officials to develop a new approach to road maintenance. We will focus on delivering higher-quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene. It will be data driven and supported by sound engineering judgement and address the problem in a meaningful and sustainable manner.My officials are finalising work on a draft road maintenance strategy which I will then share in the coming weeks.Public finances are limited, and we have a lot to do to make up for years of austerity and underfunding but I am committed to ensuring that people are at the heart of funding decisions, and that we do all that we can to be efficient and effective in the delivery of public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 15932/22-27, for an update on the 6 to12 month targets set within each of the four themes, within the Child Criminal Exploitation Action Plan.
answer_text: The cross-government Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE) action plan has recently been reviewed and revised by the Children and Young Peoples Strategy (CYPS) Child Protection Group (formerly known as the Child Protection Senior Officials Group (CPSOG) subgroup) which oversees implementation.  I have included the revised action plan at Annex A for your information.Action 1.1 now has a revised target date of December 2025.  It is anticipated that the policy document ‘Co-operating to Safeguard Children and Young People in Northern Ireland’, which provides the overarching policy framework for safeguarding children and young people, will be updated by the Department of Health by December 2025.	There are 2 other actions within the plan which had a 6–12 month timescale. 	Action 1.3 has now closed given the successful bid to Home Office for the rollout of a Regional National Referral Mechanism Child Decision Making Pilot for Northern Ireland. The pilot will be in place by January 2026 and it will test whether determining if a child is a victim of modern slavery within existing safeguarding structures is a more appropriate model for making modern slavery decisions for children. 		This approach includes all forms of child exploitation, including Child Sexual Exploitation and Child Criminal Exploitation, and will enable more timely decisions about whether a child is a victim of modern slavery to be made by those involved in their care and ensure the decisions made are closely aligned with the provision of local, needs-based support and any law enforcement response. The pilot will be evaluated over a number of years and will be monitored through the CYPS Child Protection Group Workplan.		Action 2.1 is also complete as this has been achieved with the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland’s Child Sexual Exploitation subgroup reconfigured in February 2024 to become the Child Exploitation Committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to reverse the decline in butterfly numbers.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Priority Species lists are currently under review, with 11 butterflies currently listed. Priority species are selected as requiring conservation action because of their decline, rarity and importance in an all-Ireland and UK context. Three listed priority species, Cryptic Wood White, Large Heath and Marsh Fritillay, have been selected by the Species Recovery Partnership project. The Department are partners in this RSPB-led initiative which will deliver Species Action Plans and conservation programs to move towards a Species Recovery Curve.The Department has been actively engaged with development and implementation of actions in the 2021-2025 All-Ireland Pollinator Plan. The 186 actions focus on sustainably managing private, public and farmland for pollinators. These actions are science-based, supported by research through the Irish Pollinator Research Network, which is committed to building the evidence base for pollinator conservation and management in Ireland. https://pollinators.ie/research/publications/
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's activities to monitor the health of the Mourne Beg and Derg Rivers, following the Meenbog landslide.
answer_text: My department monitors these rivers, monthly for water quality and pollutants and conducts ecological surveys every three years. After the landslide, my department increased ecological invertebrate assessments in 2020/21, which showed no impacts on the Mourne Beg River.As the competent fishery authority, the Loughs Agency has continued to carry out multifaceted surveys on Mourne Beg River, due to the landslide.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of research published in the Lancet in October 2025 which details the side effects of antidepressant drugs on physical health; and (ii) to detail the applicability of that research for the prescription of antidepressants in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) is the UK competent authority for medicines and is responsible for the licensing and monitoring of all medicines on the UK market, ensuring they meet their high standards on safety, efficacy and quality. The MHRA rigorously monitors the safety of antidepressants from a wide range of sources – including from clinical trials, international databases, and the Yellow Card scheme. Should new evidence warrant changes to product information or additional safety advice, the MHRA will take appropriate regulatory action.In addition to this, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) considers the best available evidence when producing guidance for the NHS and wider health and social care system. My Department has a formal link with the NICE whereby NICE guidance is locally reviewed for applicability and, where appropriate, endorsed for implementation under Health and Social Care (HSC) arrangements. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland (NI) has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of healthcare interventions. My Department currently endorses NICE NG222 - Depression in adults: treatment and management (https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng222). This guidance recommends prescribers discussing and agreeing a management plan with patients if initiating antidepressant medication, including a discussion on the avoidance of unwanted side-effects. Prescribers are also recommended to monitor for side-effects in all patients receiving treatments. If there are any changes to NICE NG222, these would apply in NI and any new NICE guidance published would be considered by my Department in line with Circular HSC (SQSD) 13 22 - https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-13-22.pdf. Patients should continue to take their antidepressant medication as prescribed, however, if a patient has concerns about their medication, including side-effects, they should discuss these with their prescriber or healthcare professional.

answer_text: The Ending the Harm campaign is wholly funded by the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC). EPPOC is a cross-departmental programme, jointly financed by the Northern Ireland Executive and the UK Government, and I am the lead Minister.  The total amount invested by EPPOC in the Ending the Harm campaign in each of the last five years is as follows:YearAmount2020-21£93,870.982021-22£263,490.052022-23£249,320.882023-24£255,692.792024-25£224,121.82TOTAL£1,086,496.52I am pleased that the Ending the Harm campaign has the overwhelming support of the public, with a representative survey in December 2024 finding that 89% of respondents fully supported what the campaign was saying and trying to do.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland 2024-25 which shows a 24 per cent increase of children in care over the last five years; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to reduce the number of children in care.
answer_text: Over the last ten years, the number of children in care has consistently increased, with figures published by my Department showing the following numbers of children in care:DateNo. of children in care31 March 20203,38331 March 20213,53031 March 20223.62431 March 20233,80131 March 20243,99931 March 20254,188Analysis of these figures indicate an increase of 23.7% of children in care since March 2020. Research has pointed to an enhanced awareness of child protection issues over recent years as one factor underpinning decisions to protect children who are potentially at risk by bringing them into care. There is also an established link between increasing levels of child poverty and the numbers of children in care. Children from the most deprived areas in Northern Ireland make up the highest proportion of children in care for 12 months or more (43% compared to just 5% of children from the least deprived areas). The report of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services, published in June 2023, highlighted the impact of poverty and social deprivation on families, and was clear about the need for more support for families to help them care for their children. The Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland 2024/25 reflect the pressures within families and complexity of need that have been growing for a number of years. This is one of the reasons why the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services was commissioned in February 2022. It is also why my Department established the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme in April 2023. The Reform Programme was established in the knowledge that change is necessary to halt the trend of increasing numbers of children in care, on the child protection register and in need of support from statutory services. The answer is partly in addressing wider family dynamics, including exposure to poverty. We know that there is a strong correlation between deprivation and the need for Health and Social Care Trusts to intervene in family life. We also know that in order to reduce the number of children in care we need to invest in early intervention and prevention services to support families to look after their children. With that in mind my officials have submitted a Transformation bid for funding for a Family Support Framework.
