question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the outcomes of the 496 National Referral Mechanism referrals, received by the PSNI Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Unit, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The PSNI does not routinely share information with the Department on the outcomes of any investigations arising from individual referrals.  Outcomes of referrals to the PSNI are a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.  You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of restorative justice practitioners, broken down by local council area, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold a record of restorative justice practitioners across Northern Ireland.  The Department currently provides funding to two accredited restorative justice organisations, Community Restorative Justice Ireland and Northern Ireland Alternatives. The locations covered by these organisations can be found on the DoJ website: Register of community based restorative justice schemes | Department of JusticeOn 17 October 2025, the Department launched a new accreditation process, which will see organisations and individuals accredited to undertake restorative work on behalf of the criminal justice system.  An updated register of accredited organisations and practitioners will be available on the Department of Justice website once the accreditation process has been completed, early in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the impact the Skills Action Plan will have on skills in West Tyrone.
answer_text: This plan will help create opportunities for all our people, including those living in West Tyrone, by putting skills front and centre of what we do.The interventions set out in the Skills Action Plan are designed to complement the existing strengths of our education system throughout the region and the outcomes delivered through our schools, colleges, universities, apprenticeship programmes and a range of established employment/pre-employment interventions. These outcomes include upskilling and reskilling courses; for those already in employment or seeking employment, with many having online aspects which are  accessible throughout the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 32406-22-27, whether he plans to repeat the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme in the next financial year.
answer_text: I am encouraged by the uptake within the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, the first phase of the Farming with Nature Package, which provides support for farmers to carry out environmental actions that create habitats and new green infrastructure and protect water quality. It demonstrates the demand that exists within the farming community to do more for nature.   It is planned to build on this first phase with an expanded Farming with Nature Transition scheme, opening for applications again in 2026.My officials are also working at pace to develop up future strands of the Farming with Nature (FwN) package such as focused support for Designated Areas and Priority Habitats.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the Pesticide Action Network's July 2025 report Pesticide-Free Towns; and (ii) to detail any (a) support; and (b) advice, he is giving local councils to reduce the use of pesticides.
answer_text: (i) I am aware of the Pesticide-Free Towns report published by PAN UK in July 2025 and the Northern Ireland specific summary published in October 2025.  I welcome the progress made by councils across Northern Ireland in reducing the use of pesticides in public spaces.I recognise the increasing public support for pesticide-free spaces. Under the UK National Action Plan on Pesticides 2025 (Pesticides NAP), a UK wide commitment has been made to improve data collection within the amenity sector. This will support a more comprehensive understanding of pesticide usage in public spaces and assist in identifying and adopting sustainable alternatives.(ii) The Department supports local councils in their efforts to reduce pesticide use by promoting best practice and providing appropriate guidance. The DAERA website includes resources on Integrated Pest Management and the UK Pesticides National Action Plan. The DAERA website also signposts users to additional relevant resources. These include the recently published Parks of London Integrated Weed Management Guidance, which outlines a range of approaches to prevent, tolerate, or suppress weed growth in amenity and public realm settings.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work he is undertaking to address the rural urban divide in (i) sexual health; and (ii) HIV, awareness.
answer_text: The Sexual Health Action Plan co-ordinates the ongoing direction for sexual health improvement in Northern Ireland and includes key actions for raising awareness of sexual health and HIV.Amongst the Delivery Groups tasked with taking forward the work of the Action Plan, an Overarching Communications Group, led by the Public Health Agency, collaborates with all Health and Social Care Trusts and the community and voluntary sector to deliver coordinated messaging around key sexual health campaigns, including:Sexual Health Week (February)Pride (Summer)World Contraception Day (September)World AIDS Day (December)These campaigns aim to raise awareness of sexual health and HIV across diverse communities, ensuring consistent and inclusive messaging.In addition, Northern Ireland has officially joined the Fast Track Cities initiative, a global movement aimed at eliminating the spread of HIV through increased testing, public awareness and education. Belfast City Council, Derry City & Strabane District and most recently Lisburn and Castlereagh Councils have signed the Paris Declaration officially committing to become Fast Track Cities. These councils have agreed to ‘Build and accelerate an appropriate response reflecting local needs.’ Although significant efforts are being made to extend the reach of sexual health and HIV awareness across Northern Ireland, stakeholder engagement during the development of the Sexual Health Action Plan highlighted several challenges faced by individuals in rural areas. These include limited proximity to services, inadequate transport links, and barriers to accessing online services and information. These issues were acknowledged as requiring attention in the longer-term improvement of sexual health services. The Action Plan outlines areas for scoping work, which, when completed, will help inform the identification of information and service gaps affecting rural communities going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Simon Community's October 2025 report Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle.
answer_text: The Department welcomes the work done by The Simon Community in Their report ‘Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle’ report (October 2025) and notes the call for a greater focus and investment in homelessness prevention.  I have already begun to address the issue of investment in homelessness prevention by providing a ringfenced allocation of £2.5m in 2025/26 to the Housing Executive for Strategic Homlessness Prevention activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the progress of Marine Licencing.
answer_text: Marine Licensing Exempted activities are set out in the Marine Licensing (Exempted Activities) Order (Northern Ireland) 2011.  This Order was amended in 2022 through the Marine Licensing (Exempted Activities) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2022.  Applicants must meet the relevant qualifying criteria and conditions of the Order, and, dependent on the nature of the exempted activity, may or may not be required to notify the licensing authority prior to any activity taking place.  To date in 2025, the Marine Licensing branch has processed 12 notifiable exemption applications relating from scientific research and emergency repairs to infrastructure, such as the sewerage network and sea defences.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the progress of Offshore Renewable Energy Installation transmission consent.
answer_text: My Department is progressing the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) that outlines the necessary policy and processes to enable offshore renewable generation in the north’s marine area from 2030. 	My officials are working on confirming connection arrangements for Offshore Renewable Energy (ORE) as outlined in the 2025 Energy Strategy Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the advice she received from officials before she issued her Ministerial Direction in relation to the payment of a forward-looking licence fee to A5 landowners.
answer_text: The MLA will be aware the Ministerial Direction will be subject to scrutiny at the Public Accounts committee.Before issuing the Ministerial Direction regarding the payment of a forward-looking licence fee to A5 landowners, I received detailed advice from officials.In this advice officials highlighted options to avoid significant costs associated with undoing advance works to land that was vested and then returned to the original landowner pending the outcome of an appeal.It set out the context and rationale for the licence fee arrangement, outlined their legal basis and the key Value for Money principles outlined in Managing Public Money NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 12404/22-27 to detail his Department's rapid response contingency plan for Asian hornets, given the discovery of the first Asian hornet in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The first Asian hornet record in Dundonald, Belfast led to the discovery by NIEA officials of a nest which has been successfully treated and removed.My Department published an Asian hornet contingency plan which can be found on the DAERA website at -https://invasivespeciesni.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Northern-Ireland-AH-RR-Contingency-Plan-minus-contacts-details.pdf  The plan was scheduled for review and that review will be informed by NIEA experience at Dundonald.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the Woodland Trust’s June 2025 report State of the UK's Woods and Trees - a Summary for Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail how he plans to meet targets for tree planting.
answer_text: I welcome the Woodland Trust’s report, State of the UK’s Woods and Trees: Northern Ireland. The report is an important and timely assessment of Northern Ireland’s woodlands.		The report provides a comprehensive assessment of the state of native woods and trees in Northern Ireland, highlighting their ecological value, current condition, threats, and opportunities for restoration and expansion. 		It is widely recognised that the rate of tree planting and woodland creation needs to increase substantially to meet afforestation policy objectives and deliver tree planting targets in future years in line with Climate Action Plans.At the recent Woodland Trust’s launch event I announced the commissioning of a Northern Ireland Tree Planting Action Plan that will be facilitated by the Strategic Investment Board. The Tree Planting Action Plan will be co-designed in collaboration with stakeholders over the coming months. The Plan will highlight key actions needed to take forward the objective of the 12% forest cover by 2050. This will inform the forestry contribution to net zero targets within Climate Action Plans.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action she is taking to support Easilink in its provision of community transport services, in West Tyrone.
answer_text: I recognise the value of community transport services to those who use them in all areas of the north, including West Tyrone.  I have increased the level of funding available to support rural and urban community transport services in 2025/26 by over 26%.  With respect to Easilink, and all other community transport providers, I have provided funding to fully match their requirements for 2025/26 as contained in their application for grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on progress on Offshore Renewable Energy Generation Consent.
answer_text: My Department is progressing the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) that outlines the necessary policy and processes to enable offshore renewable generation in the north’s marine area from 2030. My officials are finalising the environmental evidence base, informed by a consultation conducted earlier this year, to identify potential suitable areas for offshore renewable energy development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 31854/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for on update on her Department's work to develop the rail network, in West Tyrone.
answer_text: The new line from Portadown to Derry via Dungannon, Omagh and Strabane has been classified as a long-term intervention in the All-Island Strategic Rail Review, i.e., to be delivered between 2040 and 2050, however, this is an indicative roadmap and further work will be needed to prioritise the interventions and determine the sequencing and phasing of individual projects.  The Department of Transport has secured support from the European Investment Bank (EIB) Advisory Services to help progress implementation of the Rail Review’s recommendations. With the assistance of EIB consultants, officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail, and the National Transport Authority to consider the Review’s recommendations. This work has included the preparation of a Project Prioritisation Strategy, which will be published later this year.  Officials are also working closely with Translink who have undertaken several feasibility studies linked to the Rail Review, including an initial study on the new line from Portadown to Derry.  I am currently considering these studies and I intend to publish a summary report of them later in the Autumn.Given the scale of the interventions and the magnitude of transformation involved, delivering the Review’s recommendations will be challenging and the Department will need to explore all avenues of funding. This will include seeking support from Executive colleagues to fund interventions, given their importance in terms of economic activity, connectivity, regional balance and the environment, as well as availing of Irish or British Government investment initiatives such as the Irish Government’s Shared Ireland Fund.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions taken under the Reaching Rural Strategy and Action Plan, to support new housing in West Tyrone.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Under the Reaching Rural Strategy and Action Plan 2021–2025, the Housing Executive has undertaken a range of actions to support new housing in West Tyrone, aimed at sustaining and revitalising rural communities through improved access to affordable and appropriate housing.Key actions include:Site Identification Studies (SIS):                              To address areas with identified social housing need but limited development opportunities, SIS have been conducted in several rural locations within West Tyrone, including Sion Mills, Artigarvan, Ballymagorry, and Trillick.Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP):Between 2020/21 and 2024/25, 36 social housing units were completed in rural West Tyrone, including Magheramason (1), Douglas Bridge (6), Sion Mills (27), and Dromore (2).The current SHDP (2025–2028) includes 34 programmed units in Castlederg (26), Dromore (7), and Newtownstewart (1).Additional schemes may be added in-year through competitive procurement or acquisition of existing properties.Rural Housing Need Testing:A series of rural housing need tests have been carried out to evidence local demand and inform future development:2021/22: Beragh2022/23: Mountfield (with outreach to neighbouring Common Landlord Areas)2024/25: The Rock2025/26: CladyCurrent Testing: Spamount and ErganaghLand Assessment and Feasibility Work:The Housing Executive has identified land with potential for development (subject to feasibility and planning considerations) in the following settlements: Newtownstewart, Erganagh, Clady, Magheramason, Strabane Town, Plumbridge, Glebe, and Donemana. Many of these sites are currently maintained as open space and would require exception planning arguments demonstrating substantial community benefit.Partnership Working:The Housing Executive continues to work closely with local councils, Housing Associations, and other stakeholders through the Local Development Planning process to facilitate new housing delivery and improve access to land in rural areas.These actions reflect the Housing Executive’s commitment to addressing rural housing need and ensuring that investment in new homes is guided by locally evidenced demand.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of correspondence dated 9 October 2025 from the Mayor of Derry City and Strabane District Council raising concerns about the lack of commencement of Part III of the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997.
answer_text: I have received the letter dated 9 October 2025 from the Derry City and Strabane District Council Mayor.I have already been considering the issues raised in that letter and I can confirm that my officials are currently bringing forward a tender process aimed at commissioning a report to establish the likely cost and resource implications of commencing Part III of the 1997 Order.  The resulting report will help inform future decisions. I would also highlight that all the key recommendations pertaining to the Northern Ireland Environment Agency's (NIEA) Environmental Crime Unit, as outlined in the Mills Report, were implemented, reflecting our commitment to continuous improvement and accountability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32637/22-27, to detail (i) the timescale for the introduction of; and (ii) the level of, Minimum Unit Pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: As I have made clear, I believe that Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol could be a key mechanism to reduce alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area. However, such a policy requires the support of the NI Executive, and I continue to await formal confirmation of the position of all my Executive colleagues. Should Executive support not be forthcoming, I will be required to make a statement to that effect to the Assembly under the Licensing & Registrations of Clubs (Amendment) Act (NI), legislation to the Assembly by 6 April 2026.I have not yet made any decision on the level at which an MUP would be set in Northern Ireland, and I would be keen to work with the Health Committee and the Northern Ireland Assembly to set a minimum unit price for alcohol, subject to Executive agreement to the policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any changes he is proposing, in response to the Consultation on The Gender Pay Gap Information Regulations.
answer_text: My Department’s response to the public consultation on The Gender Pay Gap Information Regulations was published on 7 October 2025 and the agreed policy proposals, including any proposed changes, are set out within that response.  My Department will be using the Department for Economy’s Employment Rights Bill to introduce the necessary changes to Section 19 of the Employment Act (NI) 2016 (Gender Pay Gap reporting) and those policy proposals will be subject to the normal Assembly scrutiny processes when that Bill is introduced to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he is ensuring regional balance in the JobStart programme.
answer_text: I want people from right across Northern Ireland to benefit from the JobStart scheme. JobStart is available to everyone who is claiming working age benefits. I would encourage anyone looking for employment to speak with a Work Coach in their local Jobs and Benefits Office (JBO) where they will be provided with advice and guidance to access the JobStart scheme, as well as a range of other support that my department can offer.In addition to this, local JBO Employer Advisors provide support and assistance for employers by offering a range of services. They are actively promoting JobStart in all constituencies and local council areas, applying a consistent, proactive approach by encouraging employers right across Northern Ireland to identify suitable JobStart opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how schools outside RAISE areas can access funding for holiday revision schemes.
answer_text: The Holiday Revision Support Programme is one of seven RAISE cross-cutting initiatives that I have announced to date.  For school-based initiatives, such as the Holiday Revision Support Programme, schools across Northern Ireland can apply, including those not currently serving RAISE localities. The Department is currently running a Halloween Revision Scheme which is accessible to any post primary school or Education Other Than At School (EOTAS) setting to provide additional support for Year 12 pupils preparing to take modular examinations in November in GCSE mathematics, English or science.  Participation in the scheme is entirely voluntary and final expressions of interest have been sought by 15 October.  Schools which provide a scheme will be reimbursed for costs incurred up to an agreed maximum amount which is linked to the number of Year 12 pupils enrolled and ranges from £1,000 to £2,000.The Department’s guidance advises schools that all pupils taking modular examinations may attend however, where pupils are being selected for participation, the schemes should be designed to ensure students who need additional help, particularly those from lower-income backgrounds, receive extra tuition and resources.  It is not permitted to ask parents to contribute to the costs of running a scheme, which is designed to ensure pupils receive free additional support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on Stroke Action Plan; and (ii) to outline his preferred model for hyperacute stoke care.
answer_text: My Department’s Stroke Action Plan sets out six priorities for stroke reform in Northern Ireland (NI). While funding and resource pressures have constrained the pace of implementation, important progress has been made in each priority area. Regarding priority one which focuses on prevention, progress includes the development of a regional model for a 7-day Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) or mini stroke assessment service and the development of action plans for implementation within each Trust. In line with priority two, an audit of thrombolysis has been undertaken which has informed the development of recommendations which will help promote the achievement of a target rate of 16% for NI. The focus of priority three is on the thrombectomy service and progress includes the roll out of Artificial Intelligence software to all eight stroke receiving sites to assist in clinical decision making. In addition, it is anticipated that work on a Strategic Outline Case which will set out the resources required for a 24/7 thrombectomy service will be completed early in 2026.Priority four focuses on long term support and rehabilitation. Progress includes the development of regional models for community stroke services, and the management of spasticity.Under priority six, a stroke workforce review is now underway, with the establishment of a Workforce Review Steering Group in June 2025.  The initial focus of the review is on an assessment of the hyperacute and acute workforce which will inform decision making on future services. It is anticipated that this phase of the workforce review will conclude in the coming weeks. Priority five in the Action Plan focuses on the introduction of Hyperacute Stroke Care. A Hyperacute Stroke Project Board (HSPB) has been established to oversee a three-phase process to identify the optimum configuration for NI. Phase one of the process which used updated modelling to review measures to drive patient outcomes is now complete. The second phase of the process, which will involve an assessment of the sustainability of current services, informed by an assessment of the workforce, is currently underway.  It is anticipated that the HSPB will meet shortly after phase one of the workforce review is completed. The HSPB’s assessment of sustainability will be a key step in identifying a preferred model including whether acute services should continue to be provided at all current sites.  If the HSPB concludes that this is not sustainable, phase three of the process will undertake further detailed analysis to inform a final recommendation on the future number and location of hyperacute and acute stroke units.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people with high blood pressure, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Hypertension is one of the diseases for which prevalence data was previously collected by General Practices as an integral part of the Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF), which has now been replaced by a Clinical Care Domain within the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework (NICAF), a key feature of the 2024/25 GMS Contract.  The NICAF still requires General Practices to maintain disease registers, allowing prevalence data to continue to be collected.Disease prevalence data, including an interactive dashboard, is published at GP practice, LCG and GP Federation level on the Department’s website at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/prevalence-statistics Table 1: The number of patients on the Hypertension register, by Assembly Area, 2020/21 to 2024/25Assembly Area (AA2008)3 Number of patients on the Hypertension register1,22020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25BELFAST EAST15,152 15,055 15,351 15,615 15,712 BELFAST NORTH19,502 19,578 19,759 20,043 20,339 BELFAST SOUTH14,063 14,101 14,368 14,567 14,831 BELFAST WEST16,789 16,792 16,981 17,252 17,485 EAST ANTRIM12,143 12,292 12,414 12,637 12,899 EAST LONDONDERRY15,951 16,016 16,287 16,588 17,019 FERMANAGH AND SOUTH TYRONE17,922 18,185 18,794 19,240 19,785 FOYLE14,930 15,131 15,501 16,006 16,450 LAGAN VALLEY13,688 13,844 14,352 14,656 14,983 MID ULSTER13,065 13,162 13,662 14,095 14,362 NEWRY AND ARMAGH18,076 18,316 18,862 19,469 19,991 NORTH ANTRIM17,972 18,196 18,761 19,173 19,431 NORTH DOWN12,982 13,115 13,464 13,854 14,192 SOUTH ANTRIM11,754 11,902 12,122 12,541 12,945 SOUTH DOWN16,067 16,134 16,289 16,551 16,935 STRANGFORD13,401 13,576 13,772 14,141 14,622 UPPER BANN19,109 19,174 19,693 20,460 20,793 WEST TYRONE15,535 15,748 16,171 16,615 17,122 NORTHERN IRELAND278,101 280,317 286,603 293,503 299,896 Data Source:  The General Practice Intelligence Platform/QOF (GPIP/QOF) as at National Prevalence Day (31st March).  GPIP/QOF became effective from 1st July 2022.  The data source prior to 2022/23 was the Payment Calculation and Analysis System (PCAS) as at National Prevalence Day.  Given that QOF has now ceased in Northern Ireland, the registers are now referred to simply as GPIP Disease Registers, based on the system in which they are collected.The Hypertension register consists of patients with established hypertension.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the methodology her Department used to calculate closure payments for the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme participants.
answer_text: The basis for closure payments, how these will be calculated and worked examples have been included under Section 4, Part C of the Public Consultation on the Closure of the Northern Ireland Non Domestic RHI Scheme which can be accessed through the following link:Closure of the Northern Ireland Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme | Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department has undertaken to progress the Stoke Association's call for a 24-7 thrombectomy service.
answer_text: The Stroke Association’s call for a 24/7 thrombectomy service is in line with Priority Three in my Department’s Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan.Funding and capacity constraints have meant that it has not been possible to move to a 24/7 service in line with the timescale set out in the Action Plan. However, work is ongoing to identify the resources that would be required to support a 24/7 service.  A costed model has been completed which estimates that just over £5 million recurrent funding is required to expand to a 24/7 service. My officials are currently developing a Strategic Outline Case to refine this estimate further. I expect this work to be completed early next year. I must be clear, however, that following approval of the final budget for 2025/26, my Department was left with a funding gap of over £600 million. In this context, I am currently unable to commit to a timeline for a 24/7 service. In the interim, I am committed to ensuring that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. To that end, work undertaken to date includes a regional protocol to optimise access to thrombectomy for patients who present out of hours; and the roll out of Artificial Intelligence software to assist clinical decision making in the identification of patients with large vessel occlusions. It is also important to note that the thrombectomy rate in Northern Ireland in 2024/25 was 5.2%, this is higher than the regional rates in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department will ensure that closure payments of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme, reflect actual boiler output, rather than assumed figures.
answer_text: The proposed annual closure payments will be based on historic heat output for each installation. Historic heat output refers to the years 2017–2019 which has been identified as the most balanced and representative timeframe for estimating continued installation usage under the closing arrangements. As is the case under the existing scheme regulations and guidance, in order to receive an annual payment, participants will be required to comply with a number of ongoing obligations. Further details can be found in our Public Consultation which is open to 24 November 2025 and I encourage all interested parties to share their views to the proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the targets her Department has set, to reduce delay in the criminal justice system.
answer_text: My Department is working closely with justice partners to reduce avoidable delay across the criminal justice system. This is being progressed through the cross-justice Speeding Up Justice Programme, which is overseen by the Criminal Justice Board and focuses on delivering system-wide reform. The programme comprises five projects - Committal Reform, Early Engagement, Out of Court Disposals, the Remit of the Magistrates’ Court, and Digital - aimed at improving the overall pace, efficiency, and responsiveness of the system. Tackling delay, however, is complex and challenging, and reforms take time to embed and for their impact to be seen. In addition, persistent funding issues have been detrimental to the pace at which change can be brought about. In real terms, my Department’s budget is £225m below where it would be if it had only kept pace with inflation during the last number of years, and less than 0.5% of my spend in 2025-26 is considered discretionary. This budgetary situation makes the job of justice system transformation incredibly difficult. Recent funding of £20.5 million from the Public Sector Transformation Board has provided some mitigation, and this is helping support essential work to progress on efforts to reduce avoidable delay. This has provided the justice system with an opportunity to progress at pace with a range of initiatives, including proposals to expand the use of out of court disposals and a range of initiatives to promote early engagement. In line with Public Sector Transformation Board funding, a range of targets have been set for this year; these are action-based targets at present and focus on the development and implementation of new initiatives. This includes the completion of a consultation exercise regarding proposals to expand the use of out of court disposals, commencing a pilot of a new approach for case management in courts, and completing an evaluation of a file review process between PSNI and PPS and commencing a pilot to expand its application.  Monitoring and evaluation are a key part of the overall programme plan and will help to clearly identify the impact of the individual initiatives as they progress.In addition, as part of this programme, the Criminal Justice Board has given careful consideration to the development of formal targets for reducing delay. As part of this, a range of options have been explored to determine how system wide targets could be approached and derived. Whilst defining what measures we should monitor is straightforward, agreeing meaningful targets is challenging given the complexity of the system. This includes aspects such as the seriousness of the offending behaviours, available options for resolving cases, multiple court tiers, specialist approach to certain offences adopted by PPS and PSNI, forensic needs and the need for external specialist support, for example, medical reports. At present, the Criminal Justice Board monitors case processing times (based on median days), this

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of reported comments by the Executive Director of the Royal College of Nursing in Northern Ireland who said that the Executive had reneged on its commitment to maintaining pay parity for nurses.
answer_text: As you will be aware, I have issued a ministerial direction to implement the pay recommendations for 2025-26 for HSC staff. This reflects my recognition of their dedication and the need for fair and timely renumeration.The ministerial direction has been referred to the Executive for consideration and decision, which is still pending.  I have raised the matter repeatedly and continue to press for urgent resolution. I understand and share the Royal College of Nursing’s (RCN) frustration.  As I stated in the Assembly during Oral question time on the 6th October, if we do not act soon, there will be strike action. This will seriously impact patient care, waiting lists and staff morale.I remain committed to working with Executive colleagues to resolve the issue and deliver the pay award our staff deserve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the actions taken by this Department regarding the avian flu outbreak near Omagh; and (ii) what support and advice is being given to neighbouring farms to minimise the risk of spread.
answer_text: My Department carried out a veterinary investigation on Friday 03 October 2025 at a commercial poultry premises outside Omagh, County Tyrone, after a number of mortalities.  Samples were taken for testing at the Agrifood and Biosciences Institutes (AFBI).Following the preliminary results from AFBI, which indicated the presence of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI), the Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) took the decision, based on a number of factors, including the preliminary results, clinical signs and the epidemiological picture, to establish two Temporary Control Zones (TCZs) centred on the infected premises.  A 3km TCZ A and a 10km TCZ B were introduced on Sunday 05 October 2025 effective from 09:00 hours.  A full depopulation of the site was carried out on Tuesday 07 October 2025.Following an outbreak of avian influenza and implementation of disease control zones, all flock keepers within these zones are contacted by my Department with information on the restrictions that must be adhered to in order to mitigate the risk of onward spread of disease.  Further information including comprehensive biosecurity guidance is available on the DAERA website at  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/avian-influenza-ai
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Cancer Waiting Time Statistics: April to June 2025, particularly where the cancer treatment targets are not being met; and (ii) to detail what further action is he taking to meet these targets.
answer_text: (i) The Department recognises that cancer waiting time performance remains significantly below target and acknowledges that this is unacceptable.  Key figures from the April to June 2025 published statistics include:32.5% of patients urgently referred with suspected cancer began first definitive treatment within 62 days (target: 95%).87.9% of diagnosed cancer patients began treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat (target: 98%).6.6% of patients were seen within 14 days of urgent referral for breast cancer (target: 100%). (excluding Southern and Western Trusts where validated data was not available at time of publication).The 31-day performance remains relatively stable and much closer to target than both the 62-day and 14-day breast targets, indicating that once diagnosed, patients are generally treated promptly. The key challenge lies in timely diagnosis, which is critical to meeting the 62-day target.Diagnosis by day 28 is typically required to meet the 62-day target. This is directly dependent on timely access to first outpatient appointments, imaging and other diagnostic tests, all of which continue to face demand that exceeds available capacity.  Poor performance is driven by several interrelated factors, including workforce vacancies that limit the system’s responsiveness, reliance on non-recurrent funding, and the urgent need for sustained investment in core services and diagnostic equipment.Cancer performance is therefore inextricably linked to the Department’s Elective Care Framework and associated implementation plans.(ii) The Elective Care Framework sets out firm, time-bound proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards, and how we will invest in and transform services to meet the population’s future needs. The Department continues to use a multi-strand approach to tackle lengthy waits. This includes investing in and building core capacity to ensure sustainable service delivery. The approach involves working with clinicians and managers to stabilise and reform services, streamline pathways, and expand workforce skill mix to maximise patient care. The system is striving to improve productivity and efficiency by optimising available resources. Alongside this, and where appropriate, we continue to work with the independent sector to tackle waiting lists, particularly for red flag and time-critical patients.  Details of planned investment by specialty, associated targets, and the expected impact on red flag/cancer waiting times where applicable, can be reviewed within the Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan published at the following link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/elective-care-framework-restart-recovery-and-redesign  It is acknowledged that the 14-day breast waiting time performance is particularly poor. Breast services have faced sustained pressure due to rising demand and workforce challenges, leading to unacceptable waiting t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the latest official statistics on domestic abuse cases, particularly the reasons for the 8.3 per cent reduction in offences prosecuted.
answer_text: The latest official statistics on Prosecutions, Courts and Case Processing Time for Domestic Abuse Related Cases were published by my Department on 25 September 2025.These show that during 2024/25, 2,501 domestic abuse related cases were dealt with at court, representing an 8.3% decrease compared with 2023/24. The conviction rate for those cases was 56.3%, a slight increase on the previous year.While these figures provide important insight into trends in how the justice system is dealing with domestic abuse cases, it would not be appropriate for me to speculate on the reasons for the reduction in prosecutions. Decisions on whether or not to prosecute in any individual case are a matter for the Public Prosecution Service (PPS), which operates independently of my Department.It is important that the independence of the PPS is fully respected. However, my Department will continue to work closely with criminal justice partners, including the PPS and the Police Service of Northern Ireland, to ensure that victims of domestic abuse are supported to engage with the justice process and that cases are handled as effectively and sensitively as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the monthly Brucellosis compensation rates, in each of the last five years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the discussions she has had with (i) the GB Government; and (ii) the EU, regarding the introduction of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism.
answer_text: The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism is outside of my official responsibilities as Minister for the Economy. DAERA is the lead NICS Department and has therefore been leading engagement with the British Government on CBAM.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her plans to amend sentencing guidelines for those convicted of causing death by careless driving when under the influence of drink or drugs.
answer_text: AQO 2517 22-27 - ANSWER My planned Sentencing Bill will deliver on a number of recommendations arising from my Department’s public consultation on a review of sentencing policy.  This will include increasing maximum penalties for offences of causing death by driving – including causing death by careless driving while under the influence of drink or drugs – from 14 years to 20 years.  In addition, I intend to increase the maximum sentence, where an offender was previously convicted of any of these offences, to a life sentence; and to increase the minimum period of mandatory disqualification for these offences from 2 to 4 years, and, for repeat offenders, from 3 to 6 years.  I intend to introduce the Sentencing Bill before the end of 2025, subject to Executive approval.  The creation and promulgation of sentencing guidelines is a matter for the independent judiciary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of legal aid cases granted; and (ii) the average legal aid grant issued, for each of the past five years.
answer_text: In respect of (i), the number of legal aid cases granted in each of the past five financial years (2020/21 to 2024/25) is detailed in the Legal Services Agency’s most recent Official Statistics publication - Statistical Bulletin 2/2025, released on 30 September 2025.  This bulletin can be accessed via the Department’s website at the following link: https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/legal-aid-northern-ireland-annual-statistics-march-2025 In terms of (ii), applications for legal aid in Northern Ireland are assessed on a case-by-case basis, in accordance with statutory means and merit tests.  As a result, and due to the nature of the data recorded, it is not possible to attribute grants of legal aid to specific individuals in a way that would allow calculation of an average grant per person.In terms of expenditure, due to the broad scope of legal aid encompassing a wide range of advice and proceedings across both criminal and civil matters, an overall average cost per grant is not calculated.  This is to avoid potentially unwarranted conclusions that could arise from aggregating diverse case types under a single average figure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the action her Department is taking to tackle dog fighting networks.
answer_text: Dog fighting is a despicable, barbaric and wanton act of animal cruelty which has no place in our communities. Those involved show a complete disregard for both the animals they abuse and for the law.I strongly condemn all forms of animal cruelty, including dog fighting. This is a vile offence that not only causes immense suffering to animals but is also often linked to wider criminal activity.From a legislative viewpoint, dog fighting in Northern Ireland is addressed under two main pieces of legislation. The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 imposes restrictions on dogs being of a type to be bred for fighting and includes associated offences. The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 also explicitly lists animal fighting as a criminal offence. DAERA is the lead department for this legislative framework. Where there is evidence that points towards this type of offending, police are committed to working alongside partner agencies such as local councils and DAERA to identify, investigate and bring those responsible before the courts.  Where there is any suggestion that such activity is linked to organised crime gangs, the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) is available to support the ongoing efforts of law enforcement partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the more stringent verification of capital threshold measures, introduced as part of the financial eligibility test, associated with applications for legal aid.
answer_text: ANSWER:The Civil Legal Services (Financial) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 prescribe the financial eligibility criteria for civil legal services. While there has been no change to the regulations, the Legal Services Agency identified a body of applications for individuals whose capital is in excess of the prescribed threshold for advice and assistance. These applicants do not meet the financial eligibility test and as such the applications have been refused. As part of the Enabling Access to Justice Delivery Plan, I will shortly consult on a new, simpler, fairer approach to financial eligibility testing that will improve transparency and access to justice for applicants.  Further details will be provided to the Assembly through the Committee for Justice in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the University of Sheffield April 2023 report Model-based appraisal of Minimum Unit Pricing and Promotion Restrictions for alcohol in Northern Ireland, which estimates that a minimum unit pricing for alcohol of 50 pence would lead to a reduction of 1,327 alcohol related admissions to hospital per year.
answer_text: It is widely recognised that excessive alcohol use has serious consequences not only for the individual but also for their family, community, and indeed for wider society. Research has shown that the full social cost to the NI economy of alcohol-related harm could be as high as £900m per year, with up to £250m directly borne by the health sector alone and a further £383m borne by the Justice sector. The 2023 modelling report by the University of Sheffield suggests that the introduction of a MUP policy in Northern Ireland would be see a reduction of alcohol-related health harms (deaths and hospital admissions), alcohol-related crimes, and lower rates of absenteeism/presenteeism in workplaces. It considers a range of levels of Minimum Unit Pricing, with varying levels of benefits. I was also recently presented with a copy of the University of Sheffield’s September 2025 Report entitled ‘Estimating the Burden of Alcohol on the Health of Northern Ireland’. The British Heart Foundation and the Non-Communicable Disease (NCD) Alliance for Northern Ireland commissioned this report which estimated that the burden of alcohol on the health of Northern Ireland is at least 676 deaths per year and 7,426 hospital admissions (with £19 million associated costs).Recent work by NISRA also highlighted the unacceptable inequalities relating to alcohol related deaths by linking these deaths to census data. This highlighted the disproportionate number of deaths across a number of socio-economic indicators including those with no qualifications, those who are economically inactive, and those in social housing. Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol is not a panacea for all alcohol related harm. However, the evidence available through evaluations to date, and the modelling reports referenced above, supports my assessment that it could be a key mechanism to reduce such harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area. However, such a policy requires the support of the NI Executive, and I continue to await formal confirmation of the position of all my Executive colleagues. Should Executive support not be forthcoming, I will be required to make a statement to that effect to the Assembly under the Licensing & Registrations of Clubs (Amendment) Act (NI), legislation to the Assembly by 6 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department will monitor and support farmers to comply with the new Nitrates Action Programme.
answer_text: To help farm businesses meet the requirements of the updated Nutrients Action Programme (NAP), DAERA will deliver a programme of support through guidance documents, information events and knowledge transfer. The CAFRE online nutrient calculators, which will be reviewed and updated, help farmers comply with NAP regulations and assist with best practice nutrient management on their farm, making specific use of their Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) soil analysis.  Training in Nutrient Management Planning for farmers is provided through the SNHS.In addition, DAERA has a range of programmes that will provide further assistance to help manage nutrients and contribute to improved water quality.  These programmes include the Small Business Research Initiatives on the Sustainable Use of Livestock Slurry and the Sustainable Utilisation of Layer Manure.  Consideration of further capital support to help farmers invest in Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment (LESSE) is part of the ongoing development work for the new Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme. The scheme will help farm businesses improve their environmental performance, reduce ammonia emissions, carbon emissions and nutrient losses.Capital funding and independent advisory support is provided through the Sustainable Catchment Programme, which aims to protect watercourses from diffuse and point source pollution that is impacting on water quality, habitat quality and aquatic species. In addition, my Department launched the first phase of the Farming with Nature Package, the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, in June 2025, which aims to increase the extent of habitats on agricultural land by, for example, protecting watercourses with riparian buffer strips.  Monitoring and compliance with the updated NAP, once agreed, will form part of the Farm Sustainability Standards which will replace the current Cross Compliance regime.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the action her Department is taking to reduce the average planning application process times, from the current 19.8 weeks to meet the 15 week target.
answer_text: My Department has been working collectively over the last number of years with local government, who are responsible for the determination of over 99% of planning applications, and other key stakeholders to deliver a Planning Improvement Programme with the aim of enhancing and making a tangible difference to the planning system in the North.		To deliver this improvement my Department has made recent changes to the Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015, to introduce statutory validation checklists for councils, designed to improve planning performance with better quality applications entering the system, resulting in shorter processing times, more efficient consultee responses and quicker planning decisions. The Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 is currently being reviewed to consider the classes and thresholds for local, major, and regionally significant development.  This follows new legislation introduced to improve Pre-Application Community Consultation and Pre-Determination Hearings. Both of these areas have a key focus around improvements to the planning system across the North.My officials are working with planning authorities to help identify challenges, blockages and delays in the operational aspects of the planning process. There has been constructive and ongoing engagement with the 12 local planning authorities, including observational visits to Planning Committee meetings to help inform opportunities for support, shared learning and best practice, with the aim of improving performance. My Department is also continuing to work with statutory consultees through the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum and performance in this regard is improving with 77% of responses to statutory consultations responded to on time in 2024/2025.  There has also been significant improvement in the average processing time for major applications in 2024/25, which saw the lowest processing times since the transfer of planning powers in 2015. This trend is continuing with major application processing times meeting the 30-week statutory target in the first quarter of 2025/26.Through the planning improvement agenda, I am committed to working closely with local councils and other stakeholders within the shared responsibility of the two-tier system,  to deliver improvements and ensure we have a modern, effective planning system that delivers positive outcomes for all citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of fines or penalties issued for slurry or digestate pollution incidents in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: NIEA consider formal enforcement action, including prosecution under the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 (1999 Order), for water pollution incidents having a high or medium severity environmental impact, where a polluter has been identified.Seventeen fines have been issued under the 1999 Order for slurry or digestate pollution. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator, and Enforcement Statistics 2025-26, Quarter One, April to June 2025; (ii) to outline the reasons why one in seven customers failed to attend a booked theory test; and (ii) to outline the reasons why there was a 35.7 per cent increase in vehicles failing to attend a booked vehicle test this quarter.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) does not routinely collect data on why candidates fail to attend their scheduled theory or vehicle test appointments. Gathering such information would require contacting each person individually, which is not part of their current process.However, in July 2023, the DVA conducted a sample survey of customers who failed to attend a vehicle test. Those who responded advised the main reasons for non-attendance were the vehicle not being ready for test, forgetting about the test, illness, and having other commitments on the day of the test. More recently, customers who have appealed missed appointments have mostly cited illness, family emergencies, bereavement, or that the vehicle was not ready for inspection.While the DVA does not have specific insight into the increase in non-attendance for vehicle tests during the period April to June 2025, steps have been taken to help address this issue. In July 2023, the DVA updated its reminder system for vehicle tests, changing SMS text and email notifications from an ‘opt-in’ to an ‘opt-out’ model. Customers now automatically receive a reminder two days before their scheduled appointment, unless they choose to opt out.The DVA has also recently revised its cancellation policy. For private cars and light goods vehicles, the notice period to change or cancel a vehicle test appointment has been reduced from one clear working day to four hours. For all other vehicle types, one clear working day’s notice is still required. The theory test service in the north is delivered on behalf of the DVA by a third party provider under contract with the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency.  The requirement to provide three working days’ notice to reschedule a theory test appointment remains unchanged.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the budget for the new Initial Teacher Education Bursary Scheme, broken down by subject area.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to AQW 31884/22-27.  The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 3 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the actions her Department is taking to reduce water usage.
answer_text: Water efficiency measures are an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water published its Water Resource and Supply Resilience Plan (WR & SRP) in April 2025.  The WR&SRP is a 50-year plan setting out how NI Water intends to sustainably maintain the balance between supply and demand for water for all its customers over the long-term. The plan has highlighted that water resource availability in the North remains strong, even accounting for the latest UKCP18 predicted climate change impacts. Much of this resilience is attributable to the availability of robust sources of water from our environment.  NI Water also has implemented a comprehensive and proactive approach to promoting water conservation and efficiency, particularly focusing on children and young people through educational programs. Tailored programmes have been developed to achieve changing behaviour and attitudes in line with NI Water’s key water efficiency messages and assist in connecting with those hard to reach within communities. This activity is supported by an established public relations campaign which highlights simple water saving messages in the home, garden and in businesses, particularly farming. In addition to the measures undertaken by NI Water, I recently introduced the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill, which will provide NI Water with a range of additional powers to implement more widespread hosepipe restrictions.Furthermore, Sustainable Water - A Long-Term Water Strategy for Northern Ireland (2015-2040), also aims to improve water efficiency in homes and businesses, through education and public awareness campaigns. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com .  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the grant amounts awarded for the 736 successful applications for the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, broken down by constituency.

answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested broken down by constituency. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Northern Ireland Peatland Strategy to 2040 contains any restrictions on cutting turf.
answer_text: The Peatland Strategy has no specific restrictions on cutting turf, however, there is an action to undertake public consultation on restricting retail sales of peat and peat products.  The focus of an initial consultation will be on the use of peat in the horticulture sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the latest Work Quality in Northern Ireland statistics, with particular reference to the low numbers of workers receiving training or belonging to a trade union.
answer_text: In August NISRA published additional indicators in line with the Carnegie Framework adding to the evidence base on work quality.The results show that 21% of employees aged 18 and over had taken part in education or training connected with their job or a job that they might be able to do in the future, in the last 13 weeks, and that over a third of employees (34%) reported being a member of a trade union. The additional work quality indicators also show that nearly all employees had not had an accident at work and were not underemployed, almost 9 out of 10 employees did not have a zero-hours contract and a fifth of employees had worked overtime.I am committed through my Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill to taking steps to encourage trade union membership here, but would note that at 34%, our level is already the highest in these islands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the effects of liquid digestate from anaerobic digestate to the eutrophication of Lough Neagh and other waterways.
answer_text: Applying anaerobic digestate and other nutrient-rich material can worsen eutrophication in Lough Neagh and other waterways, if not used in compliance with regulations. The 2019 update to the Nutrient Action Programme Regulations limits use to crop needs and sets rules for storage and application methods, to reduce pollution risk.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Officials from the Department of Education have held several meetings with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, and other key stakeholders, to discuss traffic management at St Conor’s Primary School and Nursery Unit, Omagh. The relevant meetings are summarised in the table below.
