question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on discussions with the Minister for Education, regarding the Independent Review of Education recommendation for a single Department covering education, including post-16 provision.
answer_text: I continue to work with the Minster of Education across a number of cross-cutting areas and we have put in place structures to support greater collaboration.  My focus is on the two Departments collaborating to facilitate more strategic and coherent provision across the education system, including post-16 provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the amount of liquid digestate discharged by anaerobic digestate plants, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency; and (ii) what monitoring takes place of this discharge and its effects.
answer_text: The Department would not hold all the information requested as only those anaerobic digestate (AD) plants which hold a waste management authorisation are required to provide data to the Department on digestate. For those plants for which the Department does hold information (not retained at constituency level) the amount produced in each of the last five years in tonnes is: 2020 – 107,6572021 – 137,0772022 – 165,4912023 – 251,2712024 – 252,312	Land application of AD must comply with the Nutrient Action Programme Regulations, and depending on feedstock may also require compliance with waste management regulations. Farm businesses must spread AD based on crop need and prepare and maintain an up-to-date fertilisation plan each year, made available for inspection.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action being taken to reduce inequalities in areas, like West Tyrone, outside of the 18 RAISE Strategic Area Plans.
answer_text: In addition to the 18 Strategic Area Plans, I have also announced a series of Cross-Cutting Initiatives that will span across RAISE areas and beyond including West Tyrone. These initiatives will bring additional opportunities to tackle key common issues. To date I have announced seven cross cutting initiatives and work on these is being progressed by my officials. For school-based initiatives, schools across Northern Ireland will be able to apply. One of the initiatives is the Reading with AI Research Project which will assess the effectiveness of evidence-based AI literacy tools on improving reading outcomes. As of 3 October 2025, there are 145 schools that will be participating in the project, of these 13 are in the West Tyrone consistency area.There is a range of other funding in place to tackle educational disadvantage. In 2025/26, £75 million of Targeting Social Need funding has been allocated to schools across Northern Ireland in recognition of the challenges and costs involved in supporting children and young people from disadvantaged backgrounds, as well as those at risk of educational underachievement. The Extended Schools Programme aims to improve levels of educational achievement for disadvantaged children and young people by providing the additional support they might need to help them reach their full potential. In the 2025/26 financial year, over £450,000 has been provided to 33 schools in West Tyrone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of vacant (i) doctor posts; and (ii) nursing posts, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of HSC active vacancies is published by my Department on a quarterly basis.  The latest information available, including an interactive dashboard, relates to the position at 30 June 2025 and can be found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-health-and-social-care-hsc-active-recruitment-statistics-june-2025At 30 June 2025, there were 419 active HSC medical & dental vacancies, including posts for 172 consultants, 80 specialty doctors/specialists, 163 other Trust doctors and 4 community dentists.  At 30 June 2025, there were 1,180 active registered nurse vacancies.  This information, broken down by Trust, is provided in the table below.Selected HSC Active vacancies at 30 June 2025Belfast HSCTNorthern HSCTSouth Eastern HSCTSouthern HSCTWestern HSCTOther HSC OrgsConsultant51191044417Specialty Doctor /Specialist277419212Other Doctor850728430Community Dentist000130Registered Nurse4481982121771405Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoHIt is worth acknowledging the significant progress which has been made in recent years to stabilise and grow the HSC workforce.  Since my Department published the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy in 2018, there has been a 17.7% increase in whole time equivalent staff directly employed across the HSC.  That amounts to 10,062 additional whole time equivalent staff across the HSC, including 1,019 (+24.3%) more medical and dental staff, 2,938 (+19.4%) more registered nurses and midwives and 2,026 (+24.9%) more professional and technical staff. These increases in staffing numbers have been supported by the strategic approach to workforce development outlined in the Workforce Strategy including strategic workforce planning, increased commissioning of pre- and post-registration training, better attraction and recruitment of staff, reducing agency dependency and developing initiatives to improve staff retention.  While we have made important strides in stabilising and growing our workforce, the positive impact of additional staffing is not felt uniformly across the HSC with some key workforce challenges in particular services and locations.  My Department is committed to addressing these challenges through continued implementation of the Workforce Strategy and will shortly publish the Strategy’s third action plan which will outline priorities identified for progression over the next three years.  Continued implementation of the Workforce Strategy will require sustained and increased funding to ensure we provide the investment we know is required to meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on all capital works planned for GP practices in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to supporting developments in primary care infrastructure and continues to engage with all GP Practices in the West Tyrone area to scope options for additional accommodation requirements for core General Medical Services (GMS) alongside any potential increase in capacity for GP Training consultation rooms and accommodation to provide Physiotherapy, Mental Health and Social Work services as part of the multi-disciplinary teams (MDT) roll out.A number of projects continue to be planned in the West Tyrone constituency area to create additional capacity within GP practices.  These include:Carrickmore Health Centre; engagement has commenced with the Carrickmore Practice and Western HSC Trust to establish a project to consider the potential redevelopment of the Health Centre. I await clarity on the upcoming proposed capital budget, which along with my Department’s Regional Capital Pan, will identify when funding will be available to allow this project to move forward. The project will be subject to the usual business case approvals (to include any potential revenue costs as well as capital costs). Strabane Health and Care Centre – an outline business case has been approved for a new purpose-built hub in Strabane, as part of the Derry & Strabane City Deal and works continue on developing the Project with all key partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on discussions with the Minister for Communities regarding the regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: ANSWEREarlier this year a scoping study was completed on shared property management and a report was submitted to me for my consideration. As part of that study, my officials liaised with counterparts in the Department for Communities.I have examined the report and accept that reform is desirable in this area. I have asked officials to source a small team with suitable expertise to take forward further work. I expect resource to be available and a team to be in place for the start of the new financial year.I further expect that considerable progress can now be made during this mandate and that legislative reform can take place early in the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on discussions with the Minister of Finance regarding the regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: No further discussion has taken place since the response to your question AQW  27343/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he will take to ensure that the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme benefits patients who cannot afford to pay up front.
answer_text: The Waiting list Scheme is a reimbursement only scheme, and one of many initiatives I have in place to tackle the overall waits within Northern Ireland. Discussions regarding alternative mechanisms to pay for treatment had been tabled, however, in the context of deliverability for patients and under requirement and readiness to go live by June 2025, a reimbursement scheme similar to that of previous Republic of Ireland reimbursement Scheme and the EU Cross Border Directive was agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 29378/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the sickness absence rate in Ambulance Service staff, in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: Firstly, I want to acknowledge the vital role our ambulance staff play in delivering urgent and emergency care across Northern Ireland. Their dedication, often under significant pressure, is deeply appreciated.Table 1 illustrates that over the last three years, sickness absence rates within the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) have consistently posed a significant operational challenge, with notable peaks during the winter months, mirroring broader seasonal pressures across the health and social care system.  Encouragingly, recent months have shown early signs of improvement, which I welcome.Table 1:  NIAS Sickness Absence Rates September 2022 – August 2025Sep-22Oct-22Nov-22Dec-22Jan-23Feb-23Mar-23Apr-23May-23Jun-23Jul-23Aug-2311.11%12.30%11.75%14.57%14.95%13.80%14.63%14.25%14.13%14.36%14.34%16.04%Sep-23Oct-23Nov-23Dec-23Jan-24Feb-24Mar-24Apr-24May-24Jun-24Jul-24Aug-2414.41%14.91%16.01%15.56%13.60%12.08%10.70%10.24%9.07%11.00%11.71%11.55%Sep-24Oct-24Nov-24Dec-24Jan-25Feb-25Mar-25Apr-25May-25Jun-25Jul-25Aug-2511.28%10.05%8.63%10.05%10.09%8.67%8.48%8.53%9.17%9.30%9.21%10.57%In response to ongoing workforce challenges, NIAS has prioritised staff well-being and absence management through the establishment of a Maximising Absence Project Board and a supporting Corporate Delivery Plan. This includes enhanced training for managers, new human resources roles to embed a business partner model, and improved workforce data reporting and occupational health processes.The Trust has also implemented manager dashboards, strengthened internal communications, and continues to deliver its Health and Wellbeing Strategy, with a strong focus on mental health and trauma support. Staff have access to peer support, occupational health psychology, and confidential counselling via Inspire (Lena), alongside rapid self-referral access to physiotherapy to address musculoskeletal issues.Oversight of absence management remains a corporate priority, led by the Chief Executive and Director of Human Resources and Organisational Development, and monitored through the Trust’s corporate scorecard and the People, Culture and Organisation Development Committee.While challenges remain, I am encouraged by the progress being made and the commitment shown by NIAS leadership to support staff well-being and reduce avoidable absence. I will continue to monitor this closely and ensure that the necessary support is in place to maintain a resilient and responsive ambulance service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of the Public Health Bill.
answer_text: A public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland took place last year. The Department received over 8,000 submissions in response with some concerns raised about how individuals’ human rights might be affected by the introduction of this legislation. Analysis of this consultation was particularly complex given the volume, diversity and format of consultation responses received. A summary report has now been prepared and will be published on the Department’s website in the coming weeks.Taking into account the obvious level of public interest and the wide variety of issues highlighted in the public consultation response,  as well as the timescales required to address these adequately, I have taken the decision to instruct my officials to reengage with key stakeholders to refine and agree any policy proposals that will ensure any future legislation is fit for purpose. As such I believe it is unlikely that any Public Health Bill will be introduced, or even drafted, in the next twelve months, but given the importance of the issue and the obvious strength of feeling in relation to it I would much rather the engagement be correct rather than hurried.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of money paid out for medical negligence claims in each of the past five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to you of 9 April 2025 (AQW 25017/22-27). The table below provides updated details of the amount paid out for medical negligence claims to include 2024/25 year based on the costs incurred to the Department’s DEL resource budget.Health & Social Care Trusts Medical Negligence Expenditure 2024-25 - £’0002024-25 2023-24  2022-23  2021-22  2020-21 Belfast Trust19,98611,70410,34410,5576,947Northern Trust14,8527,3158,8484,2196,181South Eastern Trust 18,6366,05010,3357,1035,667Southern Trust7,31610,6568,4855,3494,782Western Trust 8,4575,7917,5494,3657,875NIAS3321381122Grand Total69,57941,51745,59931,60431,474My Department continues to be mindful of the need to manage public money carefully, as we navigate the challenging financial environment we are facing. Clinical negligence payments relate to an area which is largely driven by legal process and where financial impacts are hard to predict.There are several factors which have contributed to the increase in the value of settled claims in recent years.The reactivation of medical negligence claims following the Covid pandemic. The introduction of the amended Discount Rate in March 2021 which has led to an increase in the value of claims.The increase to the Guidelines for the Assessment of General Damages in Personal Injury Cases in Northern Ireland, Sixth Edition (The Green Book) which saw increases of in and around 25% to most injury brackets and in some cases 100% – 150% increases.Progress of claims relating to Neurology negligence. Settlement of a number of longstanding and complex high value claims involving Periodical Payments Orders and the cumulative effect of yearly payments.Rising costs of ongoing care in high value claims.Cases are also reaching resolution quicker and increasing numbers of claims settled in each year.It should also be noted that a single high value claim can have a significant effect on any given year.  Specifically, there were 12 very high value claims resolved in 2024/25 each at a value over £1M in damages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the recommendations of the Oral Health Improvement Plans.
answer_text: My Department published the Report on the public consultation of the Children’s and Older Person’s Oral Health Improvement plans in Plans in July 2024 and the Chief Dental officer provided a prioritised list based on the recommendations that would have the most significant impact on population oral health in Northern Ireland.In May I was pleased to approve funding for the expansion of the Happy Smile oral health programme for young children so that it will be expanded to include children in Primary years 1 to 3, who attend a school in the 20% most deprived wards in each Health and Social Care Trust.  I have also continued to identify funding to continue the Enhanced Child Dental Examination Scheme, which provides dentists with a payment for new child registrations for children aged up to and including 10 years of age, to examine a child, provide topical fluoride varnish and individualised oral health and dietary advice.  These interventions are in line with the high prioritised recommendations. Regarding recommendations related to older persons, many of these are linked directly with the terms of service of the General Dental Services contract.  I have committed to a pathway to reform this contract, which will take cognisance of the recommendations made in the Older Persons Plan. My Department has begun work to review the Minimum Standards for Nursing Homes and Residential Homes and this provides an opportunity to include oral health related standards for dependent older people as recommended in the respective plan.While I would like to implement more of these recommendations, I am limited in what can be done within the financial challenges under which my Department is required to deliver.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current staff vacancy rate in health and social care of 7.1 per cent; and (ii) what action is being taken to reduce the vacancy rates in (a) social services of 9.7 per cent; and (b) nursing and midwifery support of 11.6 per cent.
answer_text: (i) Significant progress has been made in stabilising and growing the HSC workforce in recent years with an additional 10,136 (+17.8%) whole time equivalent staff employed directly by the HSC now than in 2018.Compared to 2018 an additional 2,951 (+19.5%) whole time equivalent registered nurses and midwives, an additional 981 (+23.4%) whole time equivalent medical and dental staff and an additional 809 (+21.5%) whole time equivalent social workers are now employed directly by the HSC.  While this growth is welcome, we need to continue to train significant numbers of new healthcare staff each year to ensure a supply of staff to fill the vacancies resulting from ongoing turnover of staff due to retirements, career progression and staff leaving post to pursue opportunities elsewhere(ii) (a) Since ceasing recruitment agency usage in June 2023 there has been a huge effort to attract as many newly qualified social workers as possible into Trust vacancies. The following table gives a breakdown of vacancies filled since June 2023;BHSCT NHSCT SHSCT SEHSCT WHSCT TOTAL2023 HSC SW posts filled614732 40 29 2092024 HSC SW posts filled584943 37512382025 HSC SWposts filled on 30/9/257849373641241             TOTAL   197  145112113121   688My Department has also been steadily increasing investment in additional social work student places since 2019. The table below provides an account of how investment in training places has increased from a baseline of 260 in 2018;UUQUBOUTotal20181481120260201914811215275202014811215275202114811225285202214811240300202314811245305202416112751339202516112756     344(b) My Department’s investment in education and training over recent years has been a key factor in both growing the HSC workforce and reducing vacancy rates.The data shows that our strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 16.4% (+9,316) increase in whole time equivalent staff in post across the HSC in Northern Ireland between March 2018 (56,803) and September 2024 (66,119).   This includes a 24.0% (+1,007 wte) increase in medical and dental staff, a 16.6% (+2,510 wte) increase in registered nursing and midwifery staff and a 22.1% (+1,798 wte) increase in professional and technical staff in post. It is also worth noting that Registered nursing and midwifery vacancies (posts under active recruitment) have fallen from 2,378 (a vacancy rate of 11.3%) in September 2022 to 1,143 in September 2024 (a vacancy rate of 5.5%).  Overall HSC staff vacancies have decreased from 8,048 (9.5%) to 5,381 (6.4%) over this same period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics April–June 2025.
answer_text: My Department published the Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Times Statistics for April to June on 28 August 2025. The report showed that during the quarter, there was a 2.1% increase in ED attendances when compared to last year; however, in June 2025 there was a small decrease in the numbers attending a Type 1 EDs and an increase in the number of attendees at Type 3 EDs, which includes our Urgent Care Centres. While the increase in Type 3 ED activity will have been impacted by the re-designation of Type 2 to Type 3 EDs in April 2025, this is a trend I want to see continue. With almost 85% of attendees at Type 3 EDs being treated and discharged or admitted within four hours, it is important that we continue to see an increase in the number of patients being diverted to Type 3 EDs, first time. Additionally, in June 2025, there were almost 16,000 calls made to local Phone First services, with over 43% of these calls not being referred to an ED for treatment.  Of course, I acknowledge that waiting times in our Emergency Departments are lengthy and growing across Northern Ireland, particularly in Type 1 EDs, and this is disappointing. This situation has arisen, however, due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland waiting time statistics; and (ii) what action is being taken to reduce waiting times.
answer_text: Waiting times for elective services in Northern Ireland are among the worst in the UK. They are unacceptable and have significantly increased in recent years.  This is due to the scale of the gap between funded health service capacity and patient demand and the impact of the wider financial position. Bringing the waiting lists in Northern Ireland to an acceptable level demands a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial investment through multi-year budgets, workforce development and system wide reform.  The updated Elective Care Framework (ECF) published last year sets out a roadmap for reducing our unacceptable waiting times over the next five years. Implementing the actions in the Framework will close the demand capacity gap and tackle the excessive waits, The plan, if fully implemented and funded, would address the waiting list backlog here.Much good work is underway to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, for example through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and Mega Clinics.  In addition, my Department is working with Trusts to increase productivity and efficiency through the use of a wide range of best practice service improvements. This ensures we are maximising the capacity available to us.The budget for 2025/26 remains under extreme constraint, however, the Executive has ringfenced up to £215m in this year’s Health budget for waiting list activities – in line with the finalised Programme for Government. Only £50m of the ringfenced £215m total has involved additional monies for Health. Sustainable, recurrent funding will be essential to our efforts to tackling waiting lists over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's plans to improve the fabric of Artigarven Primary School and Community Nursery, Strabane, through (i) a new school; and (ii) refurbishment and improvements to the existing building.
answer_text: Artigarvan Primary School has not been announced under the Department’s Major Capital Works Programme and therefore there are currently no plans for a new build school. Improvement works to the school’s electrical and heating systems, and upgrades to automatic door operators, have taken place within the last twelve months.  Work to the front roof facia of the school have also been approved and will be progressed by the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the discussions her Department has had with the GB Government, regarding the introduction of the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on engagement with the British Government on all matters related to Local Growth Funding for the north of Ireland.In July 2025 I wrote to the Rt Hon Hilary Benn MP seeking continuation of financing assistance for the local Council led Go Succeed business start-up support service which was supported under the Shared Prosperity Fund.Minister Benn’s response indicated that funding for Go Succeed will be considered as part of ongoing conversations on the new funding and how it will operate from April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline why her announcement of the intention to appeal the High Court judgment on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme was made to the media, before bringing it to the Assembly or the Committee for Infrastructure.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of keeping elected Members informed on significant matters. In this instance, I made a public statement regarding my intention to appeal the High Court ruling on the A5 scheme to ensure timely and transparent communication with the public, given the level of interest in the project. I then engaged with my Executive colleagues to gather their views, before I made my decision.The Department’s Appeal has now been served, and it would be inappropriate for me to comment further on the detail of the Department’s case given court proceedings are ongoing. This does not diminish my commitment to engaging with the Assembly and the Infrastructure Committee, and I will continue to do so through the appropriate channels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any action taken by her Department to reduce rural crime in West Tyrone.
answer_text: A partnership approach is central to addressing crime, including agricultural crime, in all regions. The Department of Justice chairs the Rural Crime Partnership (RCP) with members including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), the Ulster Farmers’ Union, NFU Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Club of Ulster, the Federation of Small Businesses, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). The RCP shares information on emerging and/or re-occurring crimes, to support a coordinated response and preventative approach to addressing rural crime. RCP members regularly engage with the public via social media to raise awareness of rural crime issues and to encourage rural communities to report such crimes. My Department recently participated in Rural Crime Week NI, a local media campaign led by the PSNI, which provided an opportunity to raise awareness of the diverse issues faced in our rural communities and help provide information and advice to people who may need or want support. At a local level, PCSPs, which are funded jointly by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, play a key role in addressing crime, including agricultural crime, and building confidence locally, through engagement and consultation with communities, including rural areas. PCSPs play a pivotal role in supporting communities in identifying solutions to issues of local concern.  They also deliver a range of initiatives to raise awareness and support rural communities and businesses to protect their property and goods.  These initiatives include trailer/equipment marking events and such actions have helped to continue the downward trend in recorded rural and agricultural crime. During Rural Crime Week PCSP trailer marking events were held in Omagh and Fintona where 35 trailers were marked. The next trailer marking event will take place on Saturday 18th October at Taylor's, Fyfin from 10am to 4pm.There are also existing robust mechanisms in place for law enforcement agencies to work collaboratively through the Joint Agency Task Force (JAFT).  Tackling rural crime remains one of the six key priorities of the JATF. The JATF is a highly effective model for tackling cross-border organised crime, with strong practical and strategic collaboration between law enforcement agencies, including PSNI and An Garda Síochána. Notwithstanding the above, the operational detection and investigation of agricultural crime, including how resources are deployed, is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NI Policing Board, and you may therefore wish to also direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of the number of rural crimes, for each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the location of incidents reported to police.  Information on incidents reported to the police and the location at which they occurred is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's total staff costs attributed to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, broken down by (i) grade; and (ii) function.
answer_text: Work on the critical A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme has been ongoing for nearly 20 years. Whilst a dedicated A5 Project Team is in place, it has varied in size over the years and it is also reliant on other areas of my department for expertise, such as lands and finance teams to help with inputs to the development of this much needed infrastructure project.  My Department does not utilise a time management system where staff costs can be accurately attributed to particular functions within the project, and due to the significant period of time that has elapsed, the volume of staff that have worked on the project, and the reliance on expert areas for input, any attempt to provide this information would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department producing data with a limited degree of exactitude, and the exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.My Department does hold information on the staff costs attributed to the dedicated A5 Project team from 2015/16 to 2024/25: 24/2523/2422/2321/2220/2119/2018/1917/1816/1715/16£'millionsStaff Cost0.7 0.4 0.2 0.2 0.2 0.2 0.4 0.3 0.3 0.2 This information does not include staff costs that would be attributed to other areas of expertise within the department, such as lands and finance teams, which would also have completed essential work in relation to the A5 schemeThe A5 Project Team are working diligently on the development of a robust appeal and the work to get this life saving road built must be the priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on plans to introduce legislation controlling the sale and supply of puppies and kittens within the 2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: The consultation on proposed new rules closed at the end of August and over 480 responses were received. Officials are currently analysing the feedback and preparing a consultation response for my consideration. Upon completion, I will outline my final policy position and detail the next steps toward introducing legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Farm Performance Indicators 2023-24.
answer_text: The ‘Northern Ireland Farm Performance Indicators 2023-24’ report provides information on a range of physical and financial performances for individual farm enterprises, against which producers can assess the performance of their own enterprises. The report shows that, between 2022/23 and 2023/24, average gross margins fell in 11 out of 15 main enterprises. For example, gross margins for dairy cows fell from £1,680 to £940 per cow, and all crop enterprises recorded lower margins. The decrease in average gross margins for most of the enterprises was mainly due to a fall in output prices in 2023/24. The results further demonstrate a wide range in farm performance levels. For dairy cows, the difference in gross margin per cow between enterprises classified as 'Excellent' and 'Poor' equates to £694 per cow.It should be noted that market conditions in 2025 differ from those in the time period referred to in the publication.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps her Department has taken to ensure there is no repeat of the legal errors that resulted in the quashing of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: As you are aware I have appealed the recent judgment.  Not only is the appeal considered necessary to get the road built and save lives, it will also seek to resolve important points of law that will affect all parties concerned.   Given the ongoing appeal process I cannot respond in more detail at this stage.However, I must point out that my Department worked extremely closely over recent years with very experienced Senior and Junior Counsel, and in particular during the extensive development of my Department’s detailed response to the PAC recommendations. In fact, in the most recent case, the Senior Counsel who advised by predecessor is now a High Court Judge. Their advice was followed diligently at all times by my officials. The barristers also worked hand in hand with my officials in preparing the voluminous and complex documentation which was laid in front of both my predecessor and the Executive for consideration in taking the Decision to Proceed with the scheme in October 2024.While my immediate priority remains the preparation of a robust appeal, the ultimate aim is to ensure the delivery of this flagship project, and I am determined to do all that I can to ensure that happens so that no more lives are lost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the concerns raised by the Economic Inactivity Coalition regarding the Local Growth Fund, which is due to replace the UK Shared Prosperity Fund from March 2026.
answer_text: The ‘Executive Priorities for Post-2026 Levelling-up/ Local Growth Funding Replacements’ position paper established underlying principles for discussions with the British Government; principles which resonate directly with recommendations outlined by the Economic Inactivity Coalition in recent correspondence to me. Whilst the Chancellor’s Spring statement largely ignored these recommendations, they continue to underpin the Finance Minister’s negotiating position with the British Government in relation to successor SPF funding/ new Local Growth Funding. I remain committed to working in tandem with him and Executive colleagues in delivering against these principles to ensure adequate funding is secured and that decisions on Local Growth funding are devolved to the Executive. It is imperative that the timing for development & implementation of new funding is prioritised in order to prevent a further funding cliff-edge for the Voluntary and Community Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Law Centre NI's response to the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: The Anti-Poverty Strategy Public Consultation closed on 19 Sept. My officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received throughout the public consultation process. While the Department for Communities is leading on the Anti-Poverty Strategy, it remains an Executive Strategy, and I will share the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues. The Executive will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the rationale for his Department applying to the court to formally intervene in the A5 Western Transport Corridor appeal.
answer_text: Throughout my time as AERA Minister, I have been very clear on the importance I place on climate action. The journey to net zero is the defining challenge of our generation. We have made much progress since I took up office, and I am determined that this will continue by working together across government departments and wider society.The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 was a central feature of the judgment made in the High Court on 23 June 2025. I have decided that it is appropriate that my Department applies to the Court to formally intervene in the Appeal. The focus will be to clarify the interpretation of the legislation so that it can be fully implemented as intended.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail an update on his consideration of the issues raised by the Committee of Experts regarding his Department’s obligations under the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages in respect of (i) the Irish language; and (ii) Ulster Scots.
answer_text: I acknowledge the recommendations in this report in regard to Irish-Medium Education (IME). Earlier this year, I approved the development of a dedicated Irish-Medium Education Strategy.This strategy is being co-designed with stakeholders and will seek to address many of the long-standing issues identified in this report and others, including teacher recruitment and retention and long-term planning for demand for IME.The Department recognises the importance of developing and implementing a strategic approach to the recruitment and initial teacher education (ITE) of teachers within the IME sector, including those equipped to support children with special educational needs (SEN). The Department has taken proactive steps to address workforce planning challenges in the IME sector. The recently announced ITE Bursary Scheme, due to commence in September 2026, is designed to attract high-calibre candidates into teaching areas where recruitment pressures are most acute. This includes targeted support for the Irish-medium Post-Primary Enhancement Programme, with eight dedicated bursary places. Where demand exceeds supply, the Department will consider the specific needs of the IME sector when finalising ITE intakes. Furthermore, the Department continues to engage with stakeholders across the Irish-medium sector to support the development of additional post-primary ITE pathways. This collaborative approach ensures that the evolving needs of the sector, including the provision of SEN support, are reflected in future planning.To address the recruitment of suitably qualified SEN support staff, the Education Authority (EA) has launched a pilot recruitment scheme for classroom assistants. This initiative, currently operating in selected areas, aims to streamline recruitment processes and improve access to a pool of trained support staff, thereby enhancing the capacity of Irish-medium schools to meet the needs of all learners.My Department continues to work with the EA to identify solutions to the specific challenges faced by the sector. A working group involving departmental officials, the EA and Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta will consider deliverable solutions to any identified gaps in provision for children with SEN in the IME sector. My Department is also taking part in a Cross-Departmental Working Group, chaired by the Department for Communities, which is developing both Irish Language and Ulster Scots strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total costs incurred to date by her Department, in defending legal actions relating to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: My Department has made £582,000 of payments for legal costs in respect of legal services received in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme since its inception in 2007. We have not yet been invoiced for all external legal costs for the 2024/25 and 2025/26 financial years which would include the most recent A5 Western Transport Corridor judicial review proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the resignation of the board member from Invest NI in June 2025, including whether this discriminates against the North West region.
answer_text: Every part of this region should share in the benefits of prosperity. That is why I have made regional balance one of my key objectives. In line with this objective Invest NI has set out a new regional plan.  Progress has been made for example with the investment at Magee and a number of significant investments in the North West. However it will take time to overturn the long legacy of underinvestment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the expected timeline for the A5 Western Transport Corridor appeal hearing in December 2025, including (i) the anticipated duration of the hearing; (ii) the date by which judgment is expected; and (ii) the implications for project delivery.
answer_text: The appeal is listed for hearing on 9, 10 and 11 December.  At this stage there is no indication from the Court as to the date by which the judgement will be given. The implications for project delivery are dependent on the outcome of the appeal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of Parentkind's National Parents Survey: For Northern Ireland, released in September 2025.
answer_text: I welcome these findings and I am pleased that parents in Northern Ireland have been included in this survey. I am grateful to ParentKind support for their work in providing this insight into the issues that are important to parents and their children.The report findings will be shared with relevant policy officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of scramble bike incidents reported to the PSNI in the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the numbers of incidents reported to police in relation to scramble bikes.  Information on incidents reported to the police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to address flooding issues in the Drumbarley Road area, Drumquin, West Tyrone.
answer_text: Operations staff within my Department’s Rivers Directorate have recently inspected the designated Kilmore Drain at Drumbarley Road, Drumquin with maintenance works scheduled to be completed before the end of December 2025. The effectiveness of the road culvert which crosses the Drumbarley Road will also be reviewed once the adjacent watercourse maintenance work is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Health Inequalities Annual Report 2025; and (ii) what action his Department is taking to address figures showing alcohol-related admissions in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area are more than double the Health and Social Care Trust and local council area average.
answer_text: Tackling health inequalities remains a key focus for me as Health Minister. I therefore believe the Health Inequalities Annual Report 2025, which is a robust and valuable tool for understanding health inequalities in Northern Ireland, should be a challenge to us all to make life better for everyone in Northern Ireland.Making Life Better (MLB) is the overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled to lead healthy lives.MLB is underpinned by a range of public health strategies and policies which focus on specific issues, including substance use.As highlighted in the Health Inequalities Annual Report 2025 Alcohol, smoking and drugs related indicators continue to present some of the largest health inequalities in Northern Ireland.Across the North West area, the PHA works with a range of partners on health and wellbeing interventions/programmes, including Derry City and Strabane District Council, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, other NICS Departments, HSC Trusts, Primary Care, Local Government, Voluntary and Community Sector, local businesses and many other partners to promote activities that will encourage and enable healthier lifestyles that contribute to better health outcomes.My Department continues to lead on the implementation of the cross-departmental strategy to prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use in Northern Ireland. The strategy includes measures to reduce the availability and accessibility of alcohol and other substances and requires a whole system approach. I am committed to working across the NI Executive, with colleagues across the UK and in Ireland to tackle these issues and the underlying societal causes.It is vital that people understand the harm that alcohol can cause. Therefore, my Department and the Public Health Agency promote the UK Chief Medical Officers’ low risk guidelines, which state that to drink at low risk, men and women should drink no more than 14 units of alcohol a week. We also promote specific awareness-raising events such as Dry January and Alcohol Awareness Week.Sadly, figures confirm that alcohol related harms continue to be felt by some parts of our community more than others, with the percentage of alcohol-specific deaths in our most deprived areas (37.6%) almost four times that of the least deprived areas (9.8%).It is because of the unacceptable levels of harm and health inequalities caused by alcohol that I have indicated my intention, subject to Executive and Assembly agreement, to bring forward legislation to introduce Minimum Unit Pricing (MUP) of Alcohol in Northern Ireland.In addition, we are working with Cooperation and Working Together (CAWT) on the rollout of the Community Alcohol Detox+ (CAD+) Project to provide services to people experiencing alcohol dependence syndrome, and their families / carers. This is funded v

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the impact to the Northern Ireland economy, as a result of the introduction of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism by the EU in January 2026 and the GB Government in January 2027.
answer_text: The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism does not fall within my official responsibilities as Minister for the Economy; however, my officials will continue to engage with DAERA to monitor developments on this reserved matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has changed its legal representation in advance of the A5 Western Transport Corridor appeal.
answer_text: The legal team remains consistent, with the exception of Senior Counsel who has been appointed as a High Court Judge. A new Senior Counsel was appointed following the court hearing in April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support provided to parents whose children have been unsuccessful in securing Fair Play Grant Scheme funding.
answer_text: The Department of Health, through the Childcare Partnership team, facilitates an annual grant scheme to ensure children with additional needs are included in registered group-based early years services, known as Fair Play. Following the call for applications all eligible registered group-based early year’s providers are invited to attend information sessions provided by the Childcare Partnership team each year. This provides step by step guidance on how best to complete an application.The scheme is oversubscribed, and resources available do not meet all applications, therefore, the application process is competitive. Funds are awarded to applicants in rank order. The Fair Play Gant Scheme applications are assessed under three criteria: evidence of need; early years intervention; outcomes for the child / children.All unsuccessful applicants are offered feedback on how the application was scored, why they were unsuccessful and advice on how to improve this in the future to support the needs of children and families. The Childcare Partnership also provide access to an extensive free training programme for early years practitioners with includes a range of courses aimed at supporting the inclusion of children with additional needs.Staff training needs referenced in the Fair Play application forms are utilised to support the commissioning of relevant training courses which will support the delivery of high quality inclusive early years provision. This is available to both successful and unsuccessful applicants. Applicants are also signposted to the full range of family support services available on the Family Support NI Website and CCP Member organisations as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the spend on the Fair Play Grant Scheme; and (ii) the number of unsuccessful applications, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department of Health, through the Childcare Partnership team, facilitates an annual grant scheme to ensure children with additional needs are included in registered group-based early years services, known as Fair Play. The funding provides a contribution towards additional staffing capacity. Resources available do not meet all the requests in full, therefore, the application process is competitive. The Fair Play Gant Scheme applications are assessed under three criteria: evidence of need; early years intervention; outcomes for the child / children. Funds are awarded to applicants in rank order.The spend on the Fair Play Grant Scheme and the number of unsuccessful applications, in each of the last five years, broken down by Trust area is detailed in the table below. It is important to note that the value of the Fair Play investment is calculated by academic year September - June. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the letter sent to him and the Education Authority by the National Association of Head Teachers Northern Ireland, which refers to the last-minute scramble for provision of school places for children with special educational needs and urges that this should not be repeated in future.
answer_text: I welcome engagement between the EA and the National Association of Headteachers (NAHT) to address issues in meeting the need for placements for children with statements of SEN which is increasing year on year. I have been clear that placements for children with SEN is an issue that requires the education system to work together collaboratively to resolve.The Education Authority has further advised:The Education Authority welcomes the NAHT’s proposals for “constructive, collaborative and positive engagement” on SEN provision for 2026-27. In particular, the EA welcomes the message in the NAHT open letter that more must be done collectively to bring an end to the annual cycle of “perpetual crisis” over places for children with SEN. EA has been clear that this is an absolute priority. As we have emphasised, this can only be achieved through securing a large-scale expansion of the number of mainstream schools offering specialist provision. This will require EA and school leaders working together proactively and collaboratively – and we look forward to NAHT engaging with us on this basis. It is only by such an approach that we can ensure schools are properly supported and, most importantly, that all children in our community receive the best possible education. Ongoing engagement with schools in areas of highest need for SEN provision is a year-round priority for EA annually, our work on SEN provision for 2026-27 is well underway with circa 60 projects identified for progression. EA plan to meet with NAHT representatives and, indeed, the wider NITC trade unions, at the earliest opportunity to discuss additional mechanisms for engagement with school leaders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people that have been redirected and treated at Altnagelvin Area Hospital; and (ii) the average waiting time for ambulances, in each month since the withdrawal of emergency general surgery from the South West Acute Hospital in December 2022.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the Farming for the Generations pilot scheme; and (ii) whether a wider scheme will be rolled out.
answer_text: The Farming for the Generations pilot scheme is currently being delivered and is due to conclude in March 2026. I am encouraged by the engagement of farming families in the pilot, with delivery and evaluation ongoing.  My officials are working with a wide range of industry stakeholders to codesign a wider scheme which will take onboard the findings from the pilot.  It is my intention that a full programme will be rolled out from November 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Ladies with Letters campaign for a statutory public inquiry into Southern Health and Social Care Trust cervical smear scandal.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's funding for the Move More, Live Better initiative.
answer_text: The total funding provided by my Department for the Move More, Live Better campaign was £40,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 17177/22-27, for an update on his Department’s commissioned Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute research project investigating potential resistance across the native ash tree populations to tackle ash dieback.
answer_text: The DAERA commissioned AFBI research project regarding ash tree microbiomes is a five-year project. Research is ongoing and is scheduled to be completed by the end of September 2025. The project aims to assist the Department in identifying possible microbial or scientific interventions that may be used to treat infected ash trees or identify strains of ash trees which could be resistant to the disease. Following the completion of the research, AFBI will provide a scientific report to Department officials, which is expected in the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of Mr Justice McAlinden's comment that the Executive is seen as a laughing stock over Irish language signs at Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: I note the comments of Mr Justice McAlinden and agree this issue could be worked out through dialogue, something I have attempted to do on more than one occasion. I remain willing to do so and believe this is the best way to avoid legal action and the unnecessary use of public funds.  My position has always been that my decision to provide bilingual signage is entirely consistent with my responsibility as Minister for Infrastructure.Grand Central Station is an all-island transport hub and visibility of the Irish language is important in delivering an inclusive and welcoming environment for all our people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of Mr Justice McAlinden's comment that the Executive is seen as a laughing stock over Irish language signs at Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: I am considering the Judge’s comments.  It would not be appropriate to comment further as the matter is the subject of ongoing litigation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29655/22-27, to detail (i) the number of people who attended the conference; and (ii) in what capacity.
answer_text: I was accompanied by Dr Suzanne Kingon, Acting Deputy Secretary, Department of Education and my Special Adviser Richard Bullick.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the companies supported by Ambition to Grow, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: A total of 250 businesses have been supported through the Ambition to Grow programme, an initiative designed to encourage businesses, at an early stage in their exporting journey, to apply to become Invest NI clients and receive a standardised offer of grant support. These businesses are broken down by constituency as follows:ConstituencyNumber of businessesBelfast East23Belfast North 18Belfast South and Mid Down 26Belfast West10East Antrim15East Londonderry6Fermanagh and South Tyrone18Foyle5Lagan Valley12Mid Ulster28Newry and Armagh13North Antrim14North Down7South Antrim13South Down12Strangford11Upper Bann6West Tyrone13Invest NI uses several marketing, and engagement approaches to encourage greater uptake of ATG from areas with lower economic output. This includes Invest NI’s ‘Always on’ paid social media campaign being weighted 60:40 in favour of areas outside of the Belfast Metropolitan Area (BMA), regional radio promotion, such as Q Radio North-West and Q Radio Causeway, and paid-partnership ezines with organisations, such as Derry Chamber of Commerce, to market ATG to their business networks.  Invest NI also uses regional outreach more intensely in areas that are experiencing lower demand. It has run ATG specific drop-in clinics from each of its regional offices, and last year it promoted ATG via its regional Lunch & Learn events which were attended by 284 people.  This year Invest NI is running Business Breakfasts, from its region offices, and Regional Business Clinics, in partnership with local councils, colleges, InterTrade, providing a platform to promote ATG. In August 2025, Invest NI issued a press release highlighting the positive impact ATG has had on businesses across the North. This was followed up with press releases outlining the positive impact of ATG on businesses in the Western, Southern, North West and North East regions. These releases showcased businesses outside the BMA, providing testimonials on the effectiveness of the programme, with the aim of encouraging more business from these areas to apply to ATG.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) funding; and (ii) service improvements being planned to address the backlog in (a) child; and (b) adult ADHD assessments.
answer_text: My Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail whether there has been any change on his previous timeline of Autumn 2025 to bring proposals to the Executive for the his preferred way forward for the introduction of an independent environment protection agency.
answer_text: The final report from the independent panel, outlining its proposals to strengthen environmental governance in Northern Ireland, is expected over the coming weeks. I plan to bring forward my proposed way forward to the Executive in autumn 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on legislation for the replacement body for the General Teaching Council For Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 27345/22-27, given the strong operational performance of GTCNI over recent years, I have determined that the best option for providing our teachers with a fit-for-purpose professional body is to retain GTCNI, addressing the issues identified in the 2021 Board Effectiveness Review, and other known legislative gaps. On 29 May 2025, the Executive gave its approval for the Department to develop legislation on this basis and a Bill is already at an advanced stage of drafting. My officials will be sharing the details of the Bill with the Education Committee, stakeholders and the wider teaching profession in the coming weeks. Following this, and subject to receiving final Executive approval, I expect to introduce the Bill to the Assembly later this autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the number of responses that were received during the consultation on the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of shared care agreements for adult ADHD currently in operation, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.  The decision to enter into a shared care agreement, or not, is therefore a clinical decision for individual GPs to make and as such, is not monitored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21821/22-27, for an update on his Department's commission of research and analysis of demand for ADHD services for both children and adults to ensure regional consistency across Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Department of Health commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the timescale for publishing the revised Nutrients Action Programme.
answer_text: The next steps in the development of the revised Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) are now underway. An introductory meeting of the NAP Stakeholder Task and Finish Group has taken place.  Over the coming months, the Group will consider the key findings from the recent NAP consultation and propose evidence-based measures that are workable at farm level within realistic timeframes as well as meeting legislative requirements. My Chief Scientific Advisor’s Office will organise a subgroup for science and research to assist the main Task and Finish Group's work.The main Group will propose measures and actions for my consideration for inclusion in a finalised NAP for 2026-29. This will be subject to a further eight-week public consultation in after which it will be brought to the Executive for approval
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 28587/22-27, for an update on action by NI Environment Agency to address issues around foul odours in Artigarvan, West Tyrone.
answer_text: NIEA has issued five enforcement notices to the company from June 2024 to date, with one relating to odour, two relating to breaches of the emissions limits in their permit covering discharges from their effluent treatment plant, one relating to breaches of the emissions limits in their permit covering discharges of surface water and one relating to odour, effluent and surface water discharges. NIEA have also issued one suspension notice in relation to an issue with regards to a broken effluent pipe which has since been repaired and the suspension notice has subsequently been lifted.These notices have led to the company carrying out significant improvement works at the site, with more work currently ongoing. This work has included improvements to their effluent treatment plant, drainage on the site and containment of potential odour sources. NIEA officials are in regular contact with LacPatrick Dairies about this ongoing work and continue to take necessary and proportionate enforcement action in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy where appropriate. The majority of the improvement works have been completed to date including the upgrade of Clarifier 2 on site. This work was completed on the 2nd September 2025. The upgrade to the clarifier appears to have resulted in a significant improvement in odour in the Artigarvan Area. NIEA officials also regularly engage with local stakeholders about their concerns, including residents and elected representatives.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policy in place for (i) Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) GPs regarding accepting ADHD diagnoses made privately by General Medical Council-registered specialists.
answer_text: (i) HSC Trusts accept private assessments compliant with the National Institute of Clinical Guidelines (NICE).  Private assessments are reviewed using NICE guidelines and the Trust assessment processes, as a benchmark to the acceptability of a private assessment/diagnosis. It should be noted that this is not a comment on the quality or content of the assessment.  It merely reflects that the process of the assessment is in keeping with NICE guidelines and with the Trust assessment procedures.  All private providers are clinically responsible for their own work and diagnostic outcomes.Private assessment/diagnosis will not advance the person’s position on a waiting list and referrals will be accepted for future treatment or support in line with their chronological date of referral, in line with the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP). The private health provider, or GP, should advise the service user, that they will remain in the private health care system for treatment, medication and interventions until they reach the top of the relevant HSC Trust waiting list. When they reach the top of waiting list the private diagnosis will be checked against NICE guidelines. If the diagnosis was completed in line with NICE the diagnosis will be accepted, if not the HSC Trust will complete an assessment in line with NICE(ii) As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care, and management of their individual patients.Individuals choosing to procure healthcare privately are effectively stepping outside of the health and social care (HSC) system.  In these circumstances, there is no obligation on GPs to provide pre and/or post treatment support.  Therefore, it remains important for individuals wishing to avail of private healthcare to ensure that all pre and post treatment support costs are included in the overall package of care procured from the private healthcare provider.  In instances where any ongoing treatment and medication is recommended, but is not provided, by the private provider as part of the private service, then individuals should be prepared to meet any associated costs.Where a GP is made aware of an individual’s plans to procure private healthcare in a private capacity, it is recommended that: the individual is encouraged to ensure all pre and post treatment costs are included in the overall package,the individual is informed that they may have to pay for any ongoing treatment or medications which are recommended.GPs remain responsible for clinical decisions regarding the care of individual patients and as such they should exercise their clinical judgement prior to providing any pre and/or post treatment support for individuals availing of private healthcare.In specific circumstances, where a GP is being asked to provide an HSC prescription to a patient based on the recommendation of a private practitioner, there may be additional factors to consider. The

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of procedures that have taken place at Omagh Hospital, in each of the past 12 months.
answer_text: In the last 12 months there have been 6,741 Theatre procedures recorded as carried out in Omagh Hospital from 1st September 2024 to 31st August 2025.It should be noted that the Western Heath and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) is currently in the “Activity Stabilisation” Phase of the Encompass system roll out, post-go-live on 8th May 2025.  During this phase, all Trusts undertake extensive data validation with service teams.  The WHSCT Theatre data on the Epic System is currently still under review and is therefore subject to change post validation and thus subsequently the figure above may change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of occasions she has issued a ministerial directive, since taking office; and (ii) the issues these directives address.
answer_text: I have not issued any Ministerial Directions since taking office. Full details on all Ministerial Directions are published on the Department of Finance website at the link below: Ministerial Directions | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, to detail the number of departmental staff currently assigned to the development of the replacement for the Environmental Farming Scheme Higher Level, given the Office for Environmental Protection’s April 2025 report Review of implementation of laws for terrestrial and freshwater protected sites in Northern Ireland, which recommends that the restoration of protected sites be a central consideration in the new Farming with Nature Transition Scheme.
answer_text: A team comprised of staff from numerous Divisions across my Department is in place to develop the Farming with Nature Package (FwN) as part of my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme.  The aim is to incentivise enabling actions to support the scaling up of nature friendly farming, delivering environmental public goods including improvements in water quality, alongside sustainable food production. Following the first phase, the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, that opened on 23 June, this team is currently developing future phases of the FwN package for 2026 that will include a scheme to replace EFS Higher focusing on support for Designated Areas, Priority Habitats and Landscapes Projects and a scheme that facilitates collaboration between farmers on a landscape scale to achieve environmental outcomes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Table 1 shows how many entitlements have been confiscated from farm businesses in each of the last five years (2020 to 2024) broken down by constituency.	Table 1.
