question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an appropriate assessment under Regulation 63 of the Conservation of Habitats and Species Regulations 2017 was carried out prior to the withdrawal of the Environmental Farming Scheme Higher Level, given the Office for Environmental Protection’s April 2025 report Review of implementation of laws for terrestrial and freshwater protected sites in Northern Ireland that highlights Environmental Farming Scheme Higher Level agreements cover a larger area of protected sites than any other tool for securing appropriate management
answer_text: An appropriate assessment was not carried out as it was not considered a requirement under these Regulations or The Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995.  Current EFS agreements (Wider or Higher) will continue to deliver environmental benefits on agricultural land until Tranche 8 ends in December 2029.I am planning to introduce the Farming with Nature Package (FwN) in phases. The first phase, the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, opened on 23 June and will provide support for farmers to carry out environmental actions that create habitats and new green infrastructure, and protect water quality. Other elements of the FwN Package, focusing on support for Designated Areas, Priority Habitats and Landscapes Projects are currently being progressed. It is planned that existing EFS agreement holders will be eligible for future phases of the FwN Package in 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of occasions he has issued a ministerial directive, since taking office; and (ii) the issues these directives address.
answer_text: Since taking up office I have not issued any Ministerial Directions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a start date for the Strabane Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: Derry City and Strabane District Council has responsibility for delivery of the Strabane Town Centre Public Realm project.  Council officials have advised that they anticipate a contractor to be appointed early in 2026 with construction works commencing by Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of occasions she has issued a ministerial directive, since taking office; and (ii) the issues these directives address.
answer_text: (i) Since taking office, I have issued one Ministerial Direction.(ii) The Ministerial Direction was in relation to payment of a forward-looking licence fee to A5 landowners, in order to seek to avoid significant costs of carrying out “undo” and “redo” works on land previously vested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department is fulfilling its obligations under Article 6 (1) of the Habitats Directive to maintain or restore habitats and species to favourable conservation status, given the withdrawal of the Environmental Farming Scheme Higher Level and the absence of a replacement mechanism to deliver targeted management across designated sites.
answer_text: As Minister I have been clear that roll out of the new Farming with Nature Scheme must be a priority within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, supporting the scaling up of nature friendly farming and delivering environmental public goods, alongside food production. It is my ambition that agri-environment support should become an enterprise on farms, as a key source of income for farmers.My officials are working at pace to develop further phases of the Farming with Nature (FwN) package to realise my ambition to scale up nature friendly farming across Northern Ireland to protect and restore our natural environment.In terms of replacement for EFS Higher, other elements of the FwN Package are being progressed, such as focused support for Designated Areas and Priority Habitats. These are currently being developed with the aim of opening for application in 2026.It is planned that existing EFS agreement holders will be eligible for future phases of the Farming with Nature Package. Prioritisation of those with EFS Agreements due to expire at the end of this year is being considered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the Farming with Nature Higher Level Scheme will be introduced, given the withdrawal of the previous Environmental Farming Scheme Higher Level has removed a key mechanism to meet statutory duties under the Habitats Directive to achieve favourable condition on designated sites.
answer_text: As Minister I have been clear that roll out of the new Farming with Nature Scheme must be a priority within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, supporting the scaling up of nature friendly farming and delivering environmental public goods, alongside food production. It is my ambition that agri-environment support should become an enterprise on farms, as a key source of income for farmers.My officials are working at pace to develop further phases of the Farming with Nature (FwN) package to realise my ambition to scale up nature friendly farming across Northern Ireland to protect and restore our natural environment.I am encouraged by the uptake within the new Farming with Nature Transition Scheme (FwNT) providing support for farmers to carry out environmental actions that create habitats and new green infrastructure and protect water quality.In terms of replacement for EFS Higher, other elements of the FwN Package are being progressed, such as focused support for Designated Areas and Priority Habitats. These are currently being developed with the aim of opening for application in 2026.  Prioritisation of those with EFS Agreements due to expire at the end of this year is being consideredAdvisory support will continue to be an essential feature of existing EFS Group projects and future Farming with Nature Schemes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the September 2025 Pivotal report, 18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive - a review, on her areas of responsibility.
answer_text: The September 2025 Pivotal report on a review of 18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive highlights some of the significant achievements of my department since the return of the ExecutivePublication of the Good Jobs Bill. Sub-Regional Economic Plan.  Magee Expansion. Launch of the 10 Year Tourism Vision & Action Plan. Derry and Belfast  City Deals.£350k SME Productivity Booster Scheme.3-year £12m Skills Fund announced. Launch of a new £21m Energy Efficiency Capital Grant scheme for local businessesPublication of a Green Skills Action Plan.Publication of the Energy Strategy Action Plan.Further to the aforementioned achievements contained within the Pivotal report, my Department’s achievements also include, but are not limited to, the following: Invest NI Three-year Business Strategy. Establishment of 11 new Local Economic Partnerships.Publication of seven sectoral action plans for Agri-Tech, Life and Health Sciences, Advanced Manufacturing, Materials and Engineering, Fintech and Financial Services, Software including Cyber, Screen Industries, and Low Carbon including Green Hydrogen. £5m Founder Labs Programme.4 year £46m capital Agri-Food Investment Initiative.£4m Business Innovation Grant Scheme.10 year £11m Cluster Acceleration Programme.£6.5m of additional core funding to Tourism Ireland and agreement with South’s Tourism Minister on all-island sustainable tourism.Additional £1 million to support students facing financial hardship.£6m Multiply programme for numeracy skills.Launch of joint Careers Action Plan - Careers Action Plan 2025-2028 | Department for the Economy.Multi-year Pay Deal for Further Education lecturers.Additional £2.5m for a sustainable energy programme for low-income households.Invest to Save for Net Zero - £28m invested in almost 60 projects across the central government estate to decarbonise.While acknowledging there remains much to do in the time ahead, I remain committed and focused on delivering the priorities of my Department and the wider Executive during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the September 2025 Pivotal report, 18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive - a review, on his areas of responsibility.
answer_text: I have noted the report by Pivotal, and the conclusion reflects my views which I have already made public.  To deliver a more effective and efficient health service we need a sustained and continued focus on long-term planning as well as shorter term improvements to health service provision to meet the ever increasing demand for our services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what action her Department is taking to reduce the statistically significant increase of 12000 young people not in education, employment or training, over the last year.
answer_text: I am concerned to see the increase in these figures, and I have asked my officials to consider.  These statistics apply to a diverse group of young people. The issue is complex, with multiple factors at play, including potentially the loss of ESF, and will involve input from a range of Departments to identify and address barriers that these young people face.I am committed to working with my executive colleagues, agencies and other stakeholders including the business community to address these barriers, indeed we are already working hand in hand with the Department of Education and Department of Health on a number of cross-cutting matters.In terms of my Department, there are a range of targeted measures in place including the Apprenticeship Action plan, Level 2 Traineeships and the PEACEPLUS Youth Programme. We  also deliver a range of skills programmes, including some with elements that specifically target those who are NEET such as Step Up.Our Further Education colleges offer flexible delivery to accommodate diverse learner needs and financial support is also available for eligible students and officials are currently finalising a consultation on widening participation in higher education in the North of Ireland.In addition, in June 2025, following a comprehensive review, I outlined my proposals for a programme of reforms for school leavers with Special Educational Needs. Subject to appropriate investment, the changes will enhance the pathways and supports for school leavers who want to access the Department for the Economy’s education, skills and training provision.  Ultimately this is a complex and cross-cutting issue which will require input from across the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what action her Department is taking to reduce the 12 per cent increase in children killed or seriously injured in road traffic collisions in 2024.
answer_text: Road Safety is a priority for me, particularly for our most vulnerable road users. My Department continues to work hard on a number of measures to reduce the number of children killed or seriously injured on our roads.  My Department has already implemented part-time speed limits at 233 schools. In June I announced a further 40 schools to benefit from this safety intervention.  I also announced that I am putting measures in place to reinforce existing policy around the proper use of signs and lights on buses carrying children to and from school.  I want to explore wider road safety measures such as speed limit reductions and school bus safety; officials have provided further information on these matters, and I am considering the next steps. My Department continues to deliver targeted and relevant road safety campaigns and educational programmes, including rear bus and on-street advertising, which reminds drivers to watch out for children crossing the road in front of and behind stationary buses, the Road Safety Teaching Aid Calendar and Child Pedestrian Training.  I am working closely with other Departments and agencies, such as Translink and the Education Authority to explore opportunities to work together on road safety issues. Furthermore, through the Road Safety Strategic Forum, my officials continue to collaborate with ‘Blue Light’ organisations and other departments to progress the actions of the Road Safety Strategy, with one of the targets being a 60% reduction in the number of children killed or seriously injured by 2030.  While many of these interventions are to support children, we all need to have honest conversations about our personal responsibility on the road, and the things we can all do to keep ourselves and others safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the September 2025 Pivotal report, 18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive - a review, on her areas of responsibility.
answer_text: I welcome the recent Pivotal report and its recognition of my Department’s progress in the delivery of infrastructure. The report highlights successes such as the recent launch of the draft 2035 Transport Strategy and how DVA has delivered a record number of MOT tests, cutting waiting times from 100 days to around 30. The Report also notes the progress of our Planning Improvement Programme with significant improvement in the average processing times for major planning applications, which has seen its lowest annual processing time since the transfer of planning powers in 2015 and a decrease of 6.9 weeks compared with 2023/24.Since the Assembly was restored in February 2024, my Department has also delivered several significant infrastructure projects, including the opening of the £340 million Belfast Grand Central Station and the completion of the £33 million Belfast Tidal Flood Alleviation Scheme.Modernising our ageing wastewater infrastructure remains a top priority for me and the report recognises the need for sustained investment in it. I have outlined my three-pronged approach to this including: working with Executive colleagues to boost investment, exploring developer contributions, and introducing the Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill. Since Autumn 2024 through ring-fenced allocations by my predecessor and I, NI Water has been able to unlock wastewater capacity to enable future wastewater connections for over 5,000 new homes. This is already above the 4,300 that NI Water had originally planned to connect, if fully funded, by the end of this Price Control Period in 2028, and it is only 2025.The report also highlights the delay in starting construction on the A5 following the court judgement at the end of June. It is deeply regrettable that we received this judgment, especially given the continued loss of life on this road. This project is supported by both the Executive and the Irish Government, and I confirmed in August that the Department will appeal the decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the September 2025 Pivotal report, 18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive - a review, on his areas of responsibility.
answer_text: The September 2025 Pivotal report highlights some of the significant achievements of the Executive since its return, including a number attributed to my department. These, along with the other achievements of my department, include:Agreeing three Budgets;Delivering a balanced budget in 2024-25 despite significant funding challenges; Achieving the income generation target of £113 million set out in the Restoration Package through a range of targeted measures;Delivery of our Budget Sustainability Plan, an important step in developing fiscally sustainable budgets and policy making; The creation of the Public Sector Transformation Board to deliver the £235 million Transformation Fund, from which £129 million has already been allocated, and An agreement with the Treasury on our relative funding methodology, which has delivered some £1.3 billion in additional funding to the Executive since the signing of the Interim Fisal Framework.The report also acknowledges that there remains much to do in the time ahead.  My Executive colleagues and I remain committed and focused on delivering on the Executive’s priorities during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the September 2025 Pivotal report, 18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive - a review, on her areas of responsibility.
answer_text: I welcome this latest, September 2025 Pivotal tracker report ‘18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive: A review’ I have noted that it highlights some of the significant achievements of the Executive since its return whilst also noting that challenges remain. In relation to my Department, I note the report’s findings for justice and its recognition of our ability to deliver upon our priorities over the period to date.  In this respect, I am very much focussed on continuing to work collectively with Executive colleagues to ensure that we are continuing to make the case to identify the resources necessary to deliver on our Programme for Government priorities across this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the September 2025 Pivotal report, 18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive - a review, on his areas of responsibility.
answer_text: The September 2025 Pivotal report on a review of 18 Months of the Northern Ireland Executive highlights some of the significant achievements of my Department. At the same time it acknowledges there remains much to do in the time ahead. I remain committed and focused on delivering in my areas of responsibility during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of (i) the Transforming Business Rates proposals being worked on in England and Wales; and (ii) their applicability for business rates in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Transforming Business Rates proposals have no applicability locally as business rates are a devolved matter. The proposals in England and Wales are predicated on the specific structure, funding model and legislation for business rates systems for those two jurisdictions which operate on a Uniform Business Rate model. This means that all local authorities apply the same business rate level. Scotland also applies a Uniform Business Rate model. Our local system, by contrast, levies two taxes, a district rate (to fund councils) and a regional rate (to fund central government), through the one business rates system. This means we have different business rate poundages in each of the 11 district council areas. This is because each council sets poundages to meet their specific expenditure needs.  Throughout summer recess, as part of the Strategic Review of Rating for Small Business Rate Relief, my Department has been working with business representatives to consider alternative policy options available within our local system to meet our own needs. That process will inform my assessment and any related proposals put to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 29471/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 29471/22-27 was answered on the 7 July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale for not asking for a stay on the vesting orders for the A5 Western Transport Corridor upgrade; and (ii) whether this was a policy decision by her or if the deadline missed.
answer_text: The A5WTC High Court the judgment was delivered on Monday 23 June 2025. The Judge ruled that the Orders including the Vesting Order (VO) would  be quashed. The Court Order was filed on 27 June 2025, and ownership of vested lands reverted to the original landowners with effect from that date.Even if a stay of the quashing of the Vesting Order were granted, it would not return the affected land to the Department’s ownership. No deadline has been missed in relation to seeking a stay on the vesting orders. Landowners have now received an update about the next steps in relation to the A5 now that an appeal has been submitted. The Department is offering to enter into a licence with landowners to hold the land, in its current state. This would allow the Department to retain the benefit of advanced works and undertake necessary surveys, with appropriate compensation provided to the landowners.My priority remains the safety and wellbeing of all road users, and I remain committed to delivering the A5WTC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer November 2024 recruitment process, to detail (i) the number of applicants excluded due to technical difficulties; (ii) the number of applicants allowed to resubmit applications due to technical difficulties; (iii) the number of applicants excluded due to using notes; and (iv) the number of applicants who successfully complained about the process.
answer_text: 510 candidates were unsuccessful at interview as the panel did not have a complete set of responses to the interview questions to assess, which may have been as a result of technical issues.26 complaints have been received relating to technical issues at interview stage of these 26, 1 candidate was afforded another opportunity to complete one interview question.189 applicants unsuccessful at interview stage due to ‘using notes/proxy help.’See response to ii) above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 29381/22-27, to detail any actions she will take to ensure there is sufficient certainty, in the absence of a climate action plan, to successfully appeal the judgement.
answer_text: Following the ruling on 23 June 2025, we are working intensively with our legal team to assess the grounds for appeal and explore a range of options to address the judgement. In parallel, officials at various levels are actively engaging with DAERA colleagues regarding the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 and the draft Carbon Action Plan to ensure alignment and clarity in our approach. These coordinated efforts aim to provide a robust basis for any legal proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has the power to appeal the High Court ruling on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme unilaterally.
answer_text: On 11 July 2025, I wrote to Executive colleagues to provide an update and context on the judgement.  In this letter I clearly set out as Minister for Infrastructure, the decision to appeal rests with me. However, given the project’s Executive flagship status and the unanimous support to proceed in October 2024, I have invited their views on a potential appeal before making my final decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timescale she anticipates for getting Executive approval to appeal the recent ruling on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: Since the judgement, my officials and I have been working intensively to determine the best path forward and I am committed to working collaboratively with my Executive colleagues to find an effective solution that gets the road built and saves lives. Any appeal must be made within 6 weeks of the verdict coming into effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any guidance his Department issues to local councils to address excessive algae in ponds or lakes under their control, such as at Ecclesville Demense Park, Fintona.
answer_text: My Department has led in the development of an Inter-Agency Blue Green Algae Monitoring Protocol which was published in May 2024. This provides advice and guidance in relation to blue green algae. Although this was developed primarily as part of the Lough Neagh Action Plan, it can be used anywhere in Northern Ireland where blue green algae develop.  The Inter Agency Protocol is published on the DAERA website www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/inter-agency-blue-green-algae-monitoring-protocol
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Police Ombudsman's report into the murder of Marian Beattie.
answer_text: The office of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland is independent of the DoJ and was established to provide impartial oversight of police conduct.  As such, it would not be appropriate for me to offer an assessment of the Ombudsman's report into the tragic murder of Marian.  However, I have read the report and note its findings.  As always, my thoughts are with the Beattie family and I sincerely hope they can get the answers they deserve.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 29282/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 29282/22-27 on 3 July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) financial support; and (ii) other support provided to the NI Transplant Games Team.
answer_text: My Department and Sport NI have not provided financial support to the Northern Ireland Transplant Games Team.

answer_text: The Strategic Asset Management Unit (SAMU) in Land & Property Services (LPS) is responsible for the management, oversight, and provision of expert advice in relation to the disposal of surplus public sector assets, in accordance with the D1 disposal process. All transactions associated with this process are systematically recorded and monitored through the OSCAR software application. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the process of restoring affected lands, followed by repeating preparatory construction works, would result in significant carbon emissions that may not be lawfully accommodated within the current framework and obligations set out by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The emissions generated due to the reinstatement, and subsequent repeating, of some advance works are not likely to be significant in the context of the overall carbon budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schools selected for part-time 20mph speed-limit zones, broken down by (i) constituency; and (ii) tranche.
answer_text: I was pleased to announce the next phase of part-time 20mph speed limit schemes for schools across the North. A full list of schools included in the third tranche is available on my department’s website 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3 | Department for Infrastructure.Information on the schools included in the previous two tranches is available at the following links:20mph speed limit schemes for Schools Tranche 1 and 20mph speed limit schemes for Schools - Tranche 2Prior to implementation of Tranche 1, my Department previously piloted the introduction of part-time 20mph speed limits at 17 schools. A list of these schools can be found in the report https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/part-time-20mph-speed-limits-at-schools-review-nov2024.pdfMy Department does not collate this information by parliamentary constituency, however this information is available on the Education Authority’s website https://www.eani.org.uk/find-a-school

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the estimated total cost associated with reversing or restoring lands affected by the preparatory works for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme; and (ii)  whether funding remains in place to immediately recommence necessary works, once all legal and legislative barriers are resolved.
answer_text: The Department are in discussions with landowner agents on the extent of restoration works required.  As a number of options are available to landowners it is too early in the process to establish an estimate for the costs associated with these works. The project remains a priority for me and I determined to build it. Additionally, this critically important infrastructure  is an Executive flagship project. The 2025-26 budget is still in place and is currently being reviewed, however future year budgets have not yet been agreed. Annual budget allocations to the scheme will be subject to Executive and Assembly approval as part of the statutory budget process. Executive agreed Flagship projects are usually considered to be the top funding priority and therefore are addressed at the outset from the budget available to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Federation of Small Businesses in Northern Ireland report, Banking on Small Business.
answer_text: I welcome the insights provided by the FSBNI report, which provides a comprehensive overview of the business banking landscape for our small businesses in the North. While my Department has a clear interest in this area, regulation of the banking sector in the North is a Reserved Matter and the Minister of Finance has the lead role in the North in terms of engaging with the local banks and other financial sector stakeholders. I am however committed to building an economy where small and medium businesses can scale, grow and transform. To that end, my Department, through Invest NI, has established a comprehensive Access to Finance portfolio, comprising debt and equity funds to support start-ups, and SMEs whose growth is constrained by limited access to traditional finance. I will therefore continue to keep this important aspect of the business trading environment under review and work, where appropriate, with relevant stakeholders, Executive colleagues and business representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the costs for him and his staff to attend the Core Knowledge Foundation’s National Conference in Florida, broken down by (i) travel; (ii) hotel; and (iii) subsistence.
answer_text: A breakdown of the available costs incurred is supplied below; Travel and subsistenceHotel£  10,484.51£  5,161.05

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will review the latest regulations contained in the Northern Ireland Plant Health Label Scheme to remove unnecessary paperwork for businesses.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of plants from Great Britain to Northern Ireland, including the Northern Ireland Plant Health Label Scheme, is under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amounts of (i) counterfeit; and (ii) illicit drugs, seized for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland record information in relation to drugs seized by them.  Issues around the recording and analysis of data recorded by police are a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the amounts of counterfeit and illicit drugs seized, as raised in the question, to the PSNI. Alternatively, the statistics are routinely published online by enforcement agencies (e.g. PSNI, Border Force, OCTF).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their discussions with UK Government Ministers regarding (i) the timescale; and (ii) terms of reference, for the £1million Connect Fund.
answer_text: Our statement to the Assembly on 30 June set out the matters discussed at the meeting of the East-West Council on 12 June, in relation to the Connect Fund. The £1 million fund is already in operation and is administered by the Northern Ireland Office (NIO). The Connect Fund was the subject of prior discussion between NIO, the Department of Finance and the Department for Communities to promote its complementarity with Executive funding programmes. The terms of reference and timescale for the scheme, which will initially run over two years, were determined by the NIO.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will introduce emergency legislation to amend the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, to ensure the protection and prioritisation of human life.
answer_text: The recent judgement in the A5 case is detailed and complex, addressing key issues including our shared responsibilities under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022. My Department is carefully considering these issues and all of the options available to progress the A5WTC including the potential for legislative change.  Since the judgement, my officials and I have been working intensively to determine the best path forward and I am committed to working collaboratively with my Executive colleagues to find an effective solution that gets the road built and saves lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the allocation of funding to schools through the Teacher Professional Learning Fund in years two and three will be adjusted to take into account changes in teacher count.
answer_text: The Teacher Professional Learning Fund allocations to schools will be adjusted in years two and three to take into account changes in teacher headcount. The allocation figures, published on 23 June 2025, are intended to guide planning and should be viewed as indicative. The figures are based on teacher headcount data collected by my Department in November 2024 and published in June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the proposals contained in the Third Cycle River Basin Management Plan as they effect West Tyrone.
answer_text: The 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan covers the period from 1 January 2022 to 31 December 2027 and contains national and local measures. Measures affecting the West Tyrone constituency, which my Department has been involved in, include: Strategic Environment FundThe Strategic Environment Fund provides a grant to the Strule Tributaries and Rivers Trust Ltd for a four-year project to restore habitat in small upland glens.Water Quality Improvement Strand of the Environment FundTo date nine projects have been funded through the annual Water Quality Improvement Strand. This is a grant competition for not-for-profit organisations and councils, funding projects to deliver water quality improvements, help connect people with the water environment and bring about behaviour change. The table below outlines the organisations that have been awarded funding under the fund.yearOrganisation fundedProject title23/24Omagh Anglers AssociationOwenreagh River Restoration Scheme (ORRS)24/25Dennett Valley Health NetworkDennett Community Watershed Awareness/Projection Project24/25Mourne River Fishery GroupDerg and Mourne Conservation Project (DMCP)24/25Ulster Farmers UnionFarm Quality Officer Pilot Project24/25Omagh Anglers AssociationLeft bank Owenreagh River Restoration Scheme LORRS24/25The Conservation Volunteers TCVRiver habitat enhancement by tree planting25/26Omagh Anglers AssociationBallynahatty River Restoration (BRR)25/26Dennett Valley Health NetworkDennett Community Water-Quality Protectors25/26Mourne River Fishery GroupDerg & Mourne Conservation Project Phase 2Sustainable Catchment Programme (SCP)The Sustainable Catchment Programme (SCP) began operating in the Owenkillew river catchment area in 2024. Approximately £114k has been spent implementing on-farm measures which aim to improve water quality in the catchment area. Part of cross-border PEACEPLUS funding (Investment Area 5.3)The Protecting Shared Waters Project (€7,593,610.07) led by Northern Ireland Water will be informed by scientific research and practical experience and contribute to improved water quality and benefit the drinking water supply population in the Strule (North-West) and Fane (Neagh-Bann) catchments.Conservation Management Plans (CMP)Nine of the 45 Conservation Management Plans published by NIEA fall within the West Tyrone constituency. Conservation Management Plans have been developed and published for the Owenkillew River, River Foyle and Tributaries, and the River Faughan and Tributaries, Moneygal Bog, Fairy Water Bogs, Tully Bog, Deroran Bog, Cranny Bogs, and Tonnagh Beg Bog. The CMPs for a further 2 SACs are currently under development for Upper Ballinderry River and Black Bog. Published CMPs are available at this link https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/f2f05c09be774be99e27c0c9b74a7fd2 Sustainable Forest ManagementWithin the period of the 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan, to date, 249 hectares of the public forest estate have been harvested
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why her Department did not adopt Recommendation 3 of the Planning Appeals Commission Public Local Inquiry into the A5 Western Transport Corridor Road Scheme report that recommended that, any decision to proceed with any part of the scheme, should be time limited; and (ii) whether her Department will now adopt this recommendation.
answer_text: The Departments response to PAC’s Recommendation 3 report is dealt with in the Departmental Statement 2024 [5.2.6 page 138]. This is available on https://www.a5wtc.com/pages/departmental-statements.  The key reasons include the following:The size and nature of the A5WTC project are of an entirely different magnitude to any other development (public or private) which has ever taken place inthe north.The project is promoted by a public authority, which is funded exclusively by and is subject to the constraints and uncertainties of the public expenditure system. The Department felt this could provide an impediment to the ability of the Department to implement the scheme, in the event of unforeseen delays associated with legal challenges and the possibility of competing spending priorities emerging in the interim period. This condition may also have undesired impacts upon outstanding commercial negotiations with the designated contractors.The High Court Judgement, was very detailed and complex, so it is important that we take time to carefully consider it in full, including any implications for the scheme and the Department's next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the budget required to deliver the Careers Action Plan 2025-28.
answer_text: Officials from my Department and the Department of Education collaborated to produce the joint Careers Action Plan. This was approved by the Executive and formally published on 18th June 2025. The main aim of the Plan is to establish a universally accessible, high quality service that supports people across all stages of life. The Plan seeks to ensure that all providers of careers, including the Advisers of the Careers Service and teachers in schools, work more closely, share resources and knowledge, embrace new technologies, drive professionalism and collaborate to benefit all of our people. In this regard, it is not currently anticipated that any significant additional funding will be sought to take forward the actions for which my Department has responsibility for implementing. The Plan is primarily about re-positioning existing resources.  However, additional funding will be required to take forward the development and delivery of the bespoke Careers Portal. It is currently estimated that this will be in the region of £8-10m over a five year period. A pre-market engagement exercise is currently underway to inform the procurement process and refine the cost estimate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) applications; and (ii) grants awarded, as part of the Cladding Safety Scheme, in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Whilst my department funds the Northern Ireland Cladding Safety Scheme (NICSS) under an agreement with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG), the NICSS applications are processed and delivered by Homes England. I have sought the requested information from Homes England who have advised me as follows:“There are seven live applications, of which three are yet to be submitted. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail an update on the traffic management scheme at St Conor’s Primary School, Omagh.
answer_text: The Department is committed to supporting St Conor’s Primary School and to continuing to work with stakeholders, including the Council and the Department for Infrastructure to address the traffic management concerns at St Conor’s Primary School. However, the need for capital investment across the education estate far exceeds the funding available and my priority remains the delivery of projects in order to keep schools open and safe and provide education access to the most vulnerable.  Therefore, proposals to address the traffic management concerns will need to be carefully considered to ensure they provide a value for money, affordable solution to the issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what he steps he taking to increase the adult registration rate with a dentist from 35 per cent, in the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area.
answer_text: General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are reducing their Health Service (HS) commitment and switching to private care and independent insurance plans. As a result, patient’s registrations are lapsing and many of those patients have not been able to either re-register with the same dentist or register with another dentist.This is an issue across Northern Ireland; however, I am also aware that some areas of the country have historically lower registration and treatment rates – particularly in the West, including the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area. This is at least partly due to geography; areas with a low population density will have a lower density of practices.It is important to remember that GDPs are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of HS care they provide. Nor does the Department have a role in deciding the location of new practices as this is a business decision made by practice owners.For many months now, the Department has been attempting to address the trend in registrations and access with targeted investments to increase both the attractiveness of HS dental provision, and access for patients.For 2024/25 a £9.2m investment package supported access to General Dental Services, including:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.To assist with the increase to National Insurance contributions, £2.5million has been made available for General Dental Services.In May I announced a further £7m investment for 2025/26 to continue supporting the industry until reform can be progressed. The investment will fund continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canals, alongside a new £1.6m allocation to provide additional support to dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care for their patients.While I am pleased to have been able to announce this investment, it has been made in the face of a £600m deficit for my Department, which has severely limited my ability to implement the kind of transformative change we would all like to see. Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.The first step on this path is to determine the real cost of providing dental services so that realistic options for reform of the service can be developed. To achieve that I have approved the commissioning of a cost-of-service review to be completed in 2025/26.As for the particular issues in Fermanagh and Omagh, the challenges in Dental Services, and other HSC services, in rural areas like Fermanagh are not limite

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the consultation her Department undertook with the (i) Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs; and (ii) the Department for Infrastructure, regarding the climate impact of major projects, such as the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: In 2023, my department collaborated with DAERA, DfI and all departments, on the 'Consultation on Northern Ireland’s 2030 & 2040 Emissions Reduction Targets & First Three Carbon Budgets & Seeking views on Climate Change Committee (CCC) Advice Report: The path to a Net Zero Northern Ireland'. This consultation gave stakeholders the opportunity to respond to sector-specific advice provided by the CCC setting out the North's pathway to net zero. My department is currently collaborating with DAERA, DfI and all departments, on the 'Public Consultation on Northern Ireland's draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027'. The Climate Action Plan identifies sector-specific and cross-cutting policies and proposals to reduce emissions to ensure that the first carbon budget is achieved.My Department has also engaged with DfI on the greenhouse gas emissions associated with the construction of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme. The detail of this engagement is set out in the published High Court decision, delivered 23 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 28771/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 28771/22-27 was answered on 26 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline her Department's rationale for proceeding with the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme without ensuring that the required (i) sectoral plans; and (ii) climate action plans, as mandated by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, were in place.
answer_text: The A5WTC is an Executive flagship project and would cut across the responsibilities of other Ministers. For this reason, the Department referred the matter to the Executive Committee in the summer of 2024 seeking Executive consideration and agreement before a final decision was taken on whether to proceed with the project, either in full, or in part.In advance of the then DfI Minister’s Decision in October 2024 to proceed with the project, and in recognition of the absence of a CAP, the Executive and the Attorney General were provided with a suite of documents and information for their consideration, which included hundreds of pages of scientific and environmental evidence and mitigations. This information included assessments of the greenhouse gas emissions produced during the construction and operation of the A5WTC (the first for any project in the north). This work was carried out on a cross-departmental basis with both DAERA and DFE.The following information was provided to the Executive which set out the specific rationale for dealing with the lack of a Climate Action Plan:“Paragraph 5.3.19 (Departmental Statement) considers the PAC recommendation with regards to the expected trajectory of net Northern Ireland GHG emissions through 2030 to 2050 in the absence of the scheme, and the impact on that trajectory of the greenhouse gas emissions expected to arise from the construction and operation of the part of the scheme being authorised. In the absence of an approved CAP and more detailed knowledge of the carbon impact of future infrastructure projects, it is not currently possible to show the trajectory with and without the Proposed Scheme. However, whilst the scheme will result in an increase in emissions, the A5WTC is a priority investment for the Northern Ireland Executive, and therefore the emissions generated from the construction and operation of the scheme will need to be factored into the Executive’s plans to meet the emissions reduction targets specified in sections 1, 3 and 4 of the Act and as required by section 24 of the Act to ensure that any carbon budget set under the Act is not exceeded.”This rationale was accepted by the Executive. On this basis, the Minister was assured that in the absence of a CAP, the Executive was aware that there would need to be carbon trade-offs, to facilitate the building of this Executive flagship project and reach net zero by 2050.The judgement provided on 23 June 2025 was extremely detailed, and the Department won 4 of the 7 grounds of challenge. In essence though, based on an initial analysis, the judge did not accept that the position outlined above demonstrated sufficient certainty so as to discharge the duties under s.52 of the Act.You can view the whole statement online -A5 Western Transport Corridor – Departmental Statement | Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what his Department is doing to reverse the 20 per cent fall in the number of patients registered with a dentist.
answer_text: General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are reducing their Health Service (HS) commitment and switching to private care and independent insurance plans. As a result, patient’s registrations are lapsing and many of those patients have not been able to either re-register with the same dentist or register with another dentist.This is an issue across Northern Ireland; however, I am also aware that some areas of the country have historically lower registration and treatment rates – particularly in the West, including the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area. This is at least partly due to geography; areas with a low population density will have a lower density of practices.It is important to remember that GDPs are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of HS care they provide. Nor does the Department have a role in deciding the location of new practices as this is a business decision made by practice owners.For many months now, the Department has been attempting to address the trend in registrations and access with targeted investments to increase both the attractiveness of HS dental provision, and access for patients.For 2024/25 a £9.2m investment package supported access to General Dental Services, including:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.To assist with the increase to National Insurance contributions, £2.5million has been made available for General Dental Services.In May I announced a further £7m investment for 2025/26 to continue supporting the industry until reform can be progressed. The investment will fund continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canals, alongside a new £1.6m allocation to provide additional support to dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care for their patients.While I am pleased to have been able to announce this investment, it has been made in the face of a £600m deficit for my Department, which has severely limited my ability to implement the kind of transformative change we would all like to see. Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.The first step on this path is to determine the real cost of providing dental services so that realistic options for reform of the service can be developed. To achieve that I have approved the commissioning of a cost-of-service review to be completed in 2025/26.As for the particular issues in Fermanagh and Omagh, the challenges in Dental Services, and other HSC services, in rural areas like Fermanagh are not limite

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of why children per head of population in Northern Ireland have more fillings than children in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: The most common reason for dental fillings is for the treatment of  dental decay, so comparison of rates of dental decay is a more reliable measure of the state of child oral health.  The most recently published data for Northern Ireland was the 2021-22 Child Dental Health Survey of 5-year-old children. This showed that 31% of children surveyed aged 5 had tooth decay into dentine. A comparable survey in England in 2024 demonstrated a prevalence of 22.4% for 5-year-old children.In terms of the prevalence of visually obvious tooth decay among 5-year-old children in other areas of the UK, the current published data is summarised in Table 1: Area of the UK% of 5 year olds with visible dental decayReference PeriodEngland 22.4 %2023-24 survey data collected from 5-year-old childrenScotland26.5%National Dental Inspection Programme (NDIP) 2020Wales32.4%2022-23 data collected from 5-year-old childrenTable 1. Current data on dental decay amongst 5-year-olds in UK.The data across the UK is difficult to compare as it does not all relate to the same time period, and there will be wide variation in both prevalence and severity of experience of dental decay by geographical area, level of deprivation and ethnic group within each area of the UK. Current data demonstrates that rates of decay amongst 5-year-olds is highest in Wales, followed by Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland is recognised as the most deprived part of the United Kingdom.  Dental decay is closely related to levels of deprivation or disadvantage, with highest levels of decay demonstrated amongst most deprived areas of Northern Ireland. In addition frequency of sugar intake has a marked impact on the decay experience. Other factors include exposure to fluoride through drinking water and toothpaste.  Children in England, for example, tend to use toothpastes with a higher fluoride content and brush their teeth more frequently.  Approximately 10% of children in England drink fluoridated water daily, compared to a lack of fluoridated water in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department sought formal advice from the UK Climate Change Committee, before approving the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: As stated in the Statement by The Department on the Report on the Public Local Inquiries into the A5 Western Transport Corridor Road Scheme, published in October 2024 (Section 5.2.2 of Annex A), the Department’s carbon management tool for the transport sector is used to inform the Department’s decisions and priorities around carbon emissions and its climate responsibilities. To validate this tool and its methodology, analysts from the Climate Change Committee and Department for Transport were consulted. Both groups acknowledged the high level of local detail that has been built into the model and requested that outputs and learnings were shared with them for future carbon budgets and NI advice. A5 Western Transport Corridor – Departmental Statement | Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the role of his Department in advising the Department for Infrastructure on whether the A5 Western Transport Corridor would be compatible with carbon budgets and climate targets.
answer_text: Following the Planning Appeals Commission recommendations for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, officials from my Department engaged with officials in DfI to advise and update them on the latest position in relation to the setting of the first three carbon budgets, the setting of the emissions reduction targets for 2030 and 2040 and the development of Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan. The purpose of this engagement was to assist DfI in the preparation of their Departmental Statement in response to the recommendations. It did not include advising DfI on whether or not the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme was compatible with carbon budgets and emissions reduction targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what assessment her Department carried out to demonstrate that the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme would not prevent Northern Ireland’s statutory greenhouse gas emission targets from being met.
answer_text: Through the development of a Transport Emissions Model the Department can demonstrate how the sector will almost fully decarbonise by 2050.  The Department is therefore confident that it would still be possible to achieve its transport sector obligations and that the transport emissions from the scheme would not be incompatible with achieving the objectives of the Act. Considering the emissions from the scheme in the context of the carbon budgets, the Department is confident that, given the priority afforded to the Scheme, it would not prevent the emissions targets specified in Sections 1, 3, 4 and 24 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 from being met.Further detail on this can be found in the Departmental Statement in paragraph 5.3.16.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department (i) sought; or (ii) received, agreement from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on the greenhouse gas trajectory calculations for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, prior to making the decision to proceed.
answer_text: I can confirm that the additional predicted greenhouse gas emissions relating to the scheme were considered by my Department, built into the assumptions in relation to the transport sector emissions and shared with both DAERA and Department for Economy (DfE) as part of the engagement undertaken by the Department.   In addition, this information formed part of the documentation provided to the Executive when the decision was made to proceed with this part of the scheme.I have met with Minister Muir since the judgement, and we are continuing to work together to see how we move forward to deliver the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department has, at all times, followed the legal advice provided in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme; and (ii) to provide an overview of any occasions when her Department proceeded with action in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme that was not in line with advice provided.
answer_text: My Department has, at all times, followed the legal advice provided by Senior Counsel in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to relieve traffic congestion in Omagh town.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the traffic congestion which occurs in Omagh town particularly on the A5 Great Northern Road during peak periods. I remain fully committed to the proposed A5 Western Transport Corridor dual carriageway which, will greatly improve traffic progression around Omagh through the removal of strategic traffic from the town. There has been significant carriageway improvement works carried out in Omagh in recent years. In 2019, Strathroy Link Road was completed and opened to traffic at a cost of £7m. This included for 0.9km of new carriageway, a new shared use cycleway /footway and over 400m of road widening. In 2023, a 0.8km carriageway realignment and widening scheme was completed on the Glencam Road at a cost of £0.8m including the provision of a shared use cycleway/footway. Last year a road widening scheme at the A5 Great Northern Road/ Railway Terrace Junction was completed providing an extension to the left turn lane for traffic travelling from Strabane direction wishing to turn left onto the A32. There were also works completed on the A32 Dromore Road, where the left turn slip road for traffic travelling onto the A5 Great Northern Road was extended. Both these schemes have improved traffic progression by increasing capacity through the junction.In 2023, a traffic signal upgrade was also completed in association with a resurfacing scheme at Drumragh Avenue/Bridge Street/Sedan Avenue Junction in Omagh at a cost of £0.5m.Construction is due to commence next month on an £3.4m carriageway improvement scheme on the B4 Mountjoy Road/Gortin Road. The scheme includes road widening, to allow for two lanes of traffic in each direction and provision of shared use footway and cycleway and will address the anticipated increase in pedestrian, public transport and vehicle traffic and movement in relation to the operation of Strule Shared Education Campus.  In addition to improving the road network for vehicles, my officials are also considering active travel schemes on Old Mountfield Road, Farmhill Road and Dublin Road which could be introduced to encourage people to walk, wheel or cycle, to reduce congestion in our towns and cities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of animal abuse cases (i) reported to his Department; and (ii) prosecuted, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below details the information requested with regard to animal welfare cases reported to and prosecuted by my Department.Under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, my Department is responsible for enforcing the provisions that relate to farmed animals only.  For the purposes of this response, cases of animal abuse, has been interpreted as animal welfare concerns reported to my Department by members of the public and other authorities, for investigation. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on discussions with (i) Derry City and Strabane District Council; (ii) Fermanagh and Omagh District Council; and (iii) Mid Ulster District Council, on Greenway developments.
answer_text: Recent discussions with Derry City and Strabane District Council have focussed on the Strabane-Sion Mills Greenway, which is at preliminary design stage, and my Department looks forward to seeing these plans being progressed.Officials are actively engaged with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council on the Sligo-Enniskillen Greenway. The public have been consulted on the emerging preferred corridor, which was published in late April 2025 and, when feedback has been considered, it is anticipated that the preferred corridor will be published in the autumn. Ongoing discussions are taking place between officials and the council on a number of elements of the project.With respect to Mid Ulster District Council, my department recently issued Letters of Offer to support active travel enhancements in Dungannon Park, and officials will continue to engage with the council in regards their future greenway priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 27042/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of vacant (i) doctor; and (ii) nursing, posts broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of HSC active vacancies is published by my Department on a quarterly basis.  The latest information available, including a new interactive dashboard, relates to the position at 31 March 2025 and can be found at the link below.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-health-and-social-care-hsc-active-recruitment-statistics-march-2025At 31 March 2025, there were 319 active HSC medical & dental vacancies, including posts for 143 consultants, 82 specialty doctors/specialists, 87 other Trust doctors and 7 community dentists.  At 31 March 2025, there were 1,178 active registered nurse vacancies.  This information, broken down by Trust, is provided in the table below.The total HSC active vacancies for medical & dental posts reduced from a total of 385 at March 2023 to 319 at March 2025 and the total HSC active vacancies for registered nurses reduced from a total of 1,727 at March 2023 to 1,178 at March 2025.Our strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 17.7% (+10,063) increase in whole time equivalent (wte) staff in post across the HSC in Northern Ireland between March 2018 (56,802) and March 2025 (66,865).   This includes a 24.2% (+1,018 wte) increase in medical and dental staff, a 19.4% (+2,938 wte) increase in registered nursing and midwifery staff and a 24.9% (+2,026 wte) increase in professional and technical staff in post.Selected HSC Active Vacancies at 31 March 2025Belfast HSCTNorthern HSCTSouth Eastern HSCTSouthern HSCTWestern HSCTOther HSC orgConsultant3115555325Specialty Doctor/Specialist278224192Other Doctor55011219Community Dentist00313Registered Nurse4001722491721778Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH

answer_text: The HSC sickness absence rate is measured as the percentage of scheduled hours lost due to sickness absence or industrial injury.  The reported rates for the NI Ambulance Service (NIAS) HSC Trust in each month of 2024/25 are detailed in the table below.Percentage of Scheduled Hours Lost due to Sickness Absence/Industrial Injury in NIAS in 2024/25 by monthMonthSickness Absence Rate (%)Apr-2410.24%May-249.07%Jun-2411.00%Jul-2411.71%Aug-2411.55%Sep-2411.28%Oct-2410.05%Nov-248.63%Dec-2410.05%Jan-2510.09%Feb-258.67%Mar-258.48%Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoHThe NIAS final sickness absence rate for 2024/25 as a whole was 10.30%. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the appropriateness of Fujitsu’s continued eligibility to bid for, or deliver, publicly funded contracts in Northern Ireland, such as the Trader Support Service whilst the Post Office Horizon Inquiry remains ongoing.
answer_text: Public contracts are awarded and managed in strict adherence with public procurement policy and legislation and as such must be considered on a case by case basis. Contracts currently in place will have been awarded and will therefore be managed in accordance with the Public Contract Regulations 2015.Since 24 February 2025 the process and procedures for awarding and managing public contracts is legislated under the Procurement Act 2023 (as amended) and the Procurement Regulations 2024. The Trader Support Service contract was awarded by HMRC and therefore was not awarded by a local public body.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 26947/22-27, to detail the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme 2024-25 awards in West Tyrone, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: In 2024/2025, 112 rural businesses in the West Tyrone constituency were offered grants totalling £399,630.43 through the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme. These awards issued through Letters of Offer on 10 January 2025.The breakdown of these awards in the West Tyrone constituency by local Council area is as follows:Council AreaNumber of grants awardedValue of grants awardedDerry City and Strabane District Council (West Tyrone Constituency)37£122,321.26Fermanagh and Omagh District Council (West Tyrone Constituency)75£277,309.17Total112£399,630.43
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans she has to reduce the (i) gender; and (ii) ethnicity pension gap.
answer_text: One of the most effective tools we have to address these gaps is automatic enrolment into workplace pensions.  Since its introduction, automatic enrolment has significantly increased pension participation across the workforce, benefiting groups who were historically less likely to save for retirement—such as women, ethnic minorities, and those in lower-paid or part-time roles.  My Department actively engages with the UK Government to explore enhancements to the scheme.In addition, my Department recently conducted a 12-week public consultation on The Gender Pay Gap Information Regulations, which closed in February 2025and is currently considering the responses to this consultation to inform the most effective way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 27129/22-27, to detail a timetable for publication of the Civil Service Pay and Reward Strategy.
answer_text: The Civil Service People Strategy, a five-year plan launched on 8 April, features a two-year delivery plan that will direct the development and implementation of the Pay and Reward Strategy. This includes preparations for a two-year pay settlement and the establishment of a clear employee value proposition. The Pay and Reward Strategy is anticipated to be completed as a year-one delivery action, with publication expected by March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department’s work to reduce economic inactivity.
answer_text: Addressing high rates of economic inactivity in Northern Ireland is a shared responsibility across the Executive, with barriers such as ill health, caring responsibilities, difficulty accessing affordable childcare, and low skills or qualifications preventing a journey towards work.   My Department continues to deliver a range of employability supports to help people move closer, into and progress in employment; with £28.9 million allocated during 2024/25 alongside support delivered through our frontline Jobs and Benefits Offices and in partnership with a range of stakeholders. However, if we are to address economic inactivity we must significantly invest to increase capacity of existing provision and implement new provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 27268/22-27, what action his being taken to ensure better geographical balance in the allocation of Invest NI's Access to Finance funds.
answer_text: Invest NI’s Access to Finance initiative provides a suite of funds which are designed to helped SMEs and micro businesses across the North. Indeed I recently announced a £3.5m investment into two Coleraine based SMEs, the first investments by Co-Fund III. As part of the launch of the latest Access to Finance Funds, Invest NI held Business Breakfast events in each of the regions, where each of the Fund Managers were present to provide information and have discussions with businesses. The Fund Managers are also actively engaged with Councils and Local Enterprise Agencies to help raise awareness and increase the level of interest, demand and opportunities in each of the regions. Investments made by the funds are demand-driven, meaning there needs to be a pipeline of entrepreneurs and businesses willing and ready to take on investment. The Fund Managers will continue to be visible across all regions to attempt to bolster demand.
