question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Attendance Matters Strategy.
answer_text: Officials have taken some time to ensure the draft “Attendance Matters” strategy reflects the current position.  I will consider the draft “Attendance Matters” strategy in the coming weeks and intend to consult on this thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on discussions with the Westminster Government regarding the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme.
answer_text: UK Immigration Policy is an excepted matter and therefore I do not have the remit to deliver changes to the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme. As part of his speech at the NFU Conference on 25th February 2025, the Defra Secretary of State announced the UK Government’s extension of the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme until 2030 and noted that annual quota reviews will ensure the right balance is struck between supporting farms while gradually reducing visa numbers as alternative solutions are developed.I remain extremely concerned about inadequate labour availability for Northern Ireland agri-food businesses. Key horticulture and fishing sectors are already experiencing real impacts on their productive capacity, with inadequate labour having been identified as a fundamental factor. I have recently communicated these issues in detail with the Defra Secretary of State, the Home Secretary and the Minister for Migration and Citizenship. In my letter, I provided feedback on the UK Government’s Immigration White Paper following its publication on 12th May 2025 and reiterated immigration policy proposals which would provide solutions to these challenges, including those put forward by the NI Fishermen’s Federation (NIFF). With regards to the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme, I commented on the limitations of the scheme in relation to the length of the visa. I await a response from Defra and the Home Office. My Department is working with the Department of the Economy in relation to making further representations to the Home Secretary on issues arising from the White Paper.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the main changes between the Third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme and its predecessor.
answer_text: The Third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3) was developed in response to the Third UK Climate Change Risk Assessment (CCRA3) which was laid in Parliament in January 2022 and which considered 61 areas of risks and opportunities arising from climate change. This is an increased number of priority risks from the 2017 Risk Assessment which informed the development of the Second Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP2).  As such NICCAP3 addresses a wider scope of risk areas than NICCAP2. The overall level of urgency rating assigned to risks has also increased significantly from CCRA2 to CCRA3 which has required NICCAP3 to go further than its predecessor.  NICCAP2 was structured to align to 5 high-level key priority areas whereas NICCAP3 is aligned to the Climate Change Committee’s (CCC) adaptation monitoring framework published in March 2023. This monitoring framework sets out 13 thematic areas for climate impacts, which will be used by the CCC to assess progress in future independent assessments.The approach to align the development of NICCAP3 to the CCC’s adaptation monitoring framework has ensured that the NICCAP3 covers a broader sectoral scope than that of its predecessor and consequently has brought in a wider range of actions from within both Government and a wide range of key stakeholders. This is evident with NICCAP3 containing over three times the number of actions than were included in the NICCAP2 at publication.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 28172/22-27, to detail the targets savings for the amount lost to the welfare budget due to (i) customer fraud; (ii) customer error; and (iii) official error.
answer_text: Tackling fraud and error is a key priority for me and my Department has a robust risk based counter fraud and error strategy in place. 		Although there is no specific monetary savings target, the latest published figures show that fraud and error work carried out by my Department’s Benefit Security Division generated savings of £55.1million in 2023/24. This highlights the value of continued investment in counter fraud and error activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the discrepancy in costs between Northern Ireland and England in providing Givinostat for young muscular dystrophy patients.
answer_text: The difference in estimated costs relates not to the drug itself, which is provided free of charge through the manufacturer’s Early Access Programme (EAP), but rather to the additional staffing infrastructure that would be required to safely administer, manage and monitor patients were the Belfast Trust to provide this new medicine through the EAP. The Trust has provided an initial estimation of the additional costs which are prohibitive in the current financial context, and which I understand are higher than those of other UK centres. This may be attributable to differences between existing infrastructure and staff baselines across centres and economies of scale.  I have asked the Trust to consider whether the initial estimates could be refined based on approaches that have been adopted in other centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 27203/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timetable for the delivery of the Making Best Practice, Common Practice Programme research schemes.
answer_text: Making Best Practice, Common Practice provides funding and support for three core strands of activity: research informed conferences hosted by schools; Monthly Newsletters on Science of Learning for teachers; and quarterly publications on education research tailored for classroom practitioners. Following the recent completion of an Expression of Interest (EoI) process, 52 Research Informed Conferences will be held across the 2025/26 academic year, organised by schools, either individually or collaboratively, encompassing a broad range of themes. Details can be found on the Department’s website here. This has been a very popular element of the programme and a further call for proposals will issue for the 2026/27 academic year.The first two editions of the Science of Learning newsletters were published on 30 April 2025, a further two editions were published on 28 May 2025. These will resume in the new academic year.The first edition of the Quarterly Publication on Education Research will issue at the start of the new academic year, with subsequent editions to follow on a quarterly basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Yellow Card Centre Northern Ireland Annual Report 2023-24.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Yellow Card Centre Northern Ireland’s first Annual Report 2023-24.Traditionally, Yellow Card reporting in Northern Ireland has been significantly lower than in other regions of the UK. It is promising to see that throughout the period of 2023-24, particularly following the launch of the Northern Ireland Yellow Card Centre, that Yellow Card reporting demonstrated an upward trend across Northern Ireland. The 2023-24 figure of 797 reports marks a 32% rise compared to non-COVID-19 Yellow Card reports from the calendar year 2022.In Northern Ireland the number of reports has historically been below 40 reports per 100,000 population, dropping to less than 20 reports per 100,000 population in 2021. For 2023/2024 the current level is 41.7 reports per 100,000 population, indicating a small and encouraging increase in reporting levels.Patient safety is a top priority. The Yellow Card scheme plays a crucial role in our ongoing safety monitoring of medicines and medical devices, helping to identify potential issues early to protect public health. The first full operational year of the Yellow Card Centre Northern Ireland will be in 2024-25. The centre has a strategy including further engagement and awareness raising in Northern Ireland therefore I look forward to the publication of the 2024-25 report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to  AQW 28262/22-27, to detail any plans he has to extend the number of pupils eligible for free school meals, as has been done in England.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 28407/22-27, which is published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 20th June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) his engagements on his June 2025 visit to Brussels; and (ii) the issues discussed.
answer_text: Engagements during my visit to Brussels included meeting MEPs, officials and farming representatives to discuss the important role DAERA has played in supporting the outcomes of the recent EU/UK summit and the implementation of the Windsor Framework. I also met senior representatives from the UK Mission to the EU and the Permanent Representation of Ireland, the British Agriculture Bureau and the Irish Farmers’ Association.  Issues discussed included ensuring the continued supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland, the Veterinary Agreement between Switzerland and the EU, the EU-UK TCA Review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why patients with Type 1 diabetes cannot access insulin pump therapy with hybrid closed loop ability, unlike patents in Scotland, England and Wales.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.I have recently supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan which outlines an appropriate timescale for rollout.This plan should provide equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) why the business services and finance sector decreased by 4.5 per cent, according to the June 2025 Northern Ireland Economic Output Statistics; and (ii) what actions she intends to take to reverse this trend.
answer_text: The Business Services and Financial sector is a success story in the north, driven by a combination of foreign direct investment and indigenous growth, supported by Invest NI and others. My Fintech and Financial Services Sector Plan sets out a series of actions to support the growth of the sector in line with my economic priorities. My officials monitor the economic data closely. Quarterly fluctuations in output data are not unusual and do not represent a trend.  For example, the sector recorded growth in output of 6.4% in the preceding (third) quarter of 2024.  On a rolling four-quarter basis, which presents a fuller picture, output in the Business Services and Financial sector increased by 2.4%, which compares favourably with growth of 1.2% over the same period in Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the timeline for consideration and response to the report on the joint study research by Ulster University and the International Organisation for Migration, Identifying child criminal exploitation in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department co-commissioned this research project to inform policy development and enhance our response to Child Criminal Exploitation. I acknowledge the findings from the research project and my Department is actively considering the recommendations along with other Departments and stakeholders. Any resulting actions for my Department will be taken forward through the implementation of the 2-year Child Criminal Exploitation Action Plan and the 3-year Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to diabetes, to detail (i) how many people are affected; and (ii) the cost to the health service in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Diabetes affects nearly 115,000 people in Northern Ireland, with the number continuing to grow.  However, the exact cost to the health service in relation to diabetes in not known, given the complexity of the lifelong condition.When launched in 2016, the Diabetes Strategic Framework stated an average of 10 people were newly diagnosed with diabetes every day.  It was estimated that in the United Kingdom, 10% of the Health and Social Care budget was spent on the treatment of diabetes and its complications. This was equivalent to about £1 million daily in the context of Northern Ireland and was projected to rise to 17% of the total budget by 2035.Diabetes UK estimated the annual diabetes care cost in Northern Ireland is circa £291millon. This estimation is broken down through diagnosis, management and complications costing arising from living with diabetes, with projected costs raising to £524.1 million in Northern Ireland in 2035.However, many of the complications are preventable through early intervention and improved management of the lifelong condition.In 2024, an updated cost of illness analysis, commissioned by Diabetes UK and carried out by York Health Economics Consortium at the University of York, estimated that type 1, 2 and gestational diabetes together cost the UK almost £14 billion in 2021/22.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the recent security alert in the Mount Carmel area in Strabane, to outline any guidance issued to developers in relation to having more than one road to access and egress an estate.

answer_text: Since 2022-23, the Department for Communities (DfC) has delivered childminding academies through the Labour Market Partnerships (LMP). In 2024-25, my Department allocated £303k additional funding to DfC from the Early Learning and Childcare ring-fenced budget to expand the number of childminding academies to 10 of the 11 local LMP areas. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the General Ophthalmic Services Statistics for Northern Ireland 2024-25, to detail why there was a 2 per cent decrease in health service eye tests carried out during the last year.
answer_text: General Ophthalmic Services (GOS) in Northern Ireland is demand-led and therefore susceptible to a number of internal and external forces, pressures and drivers.The 2 per cent decrease in GOS eye test activity in 2024/25 was in line with expectations; at the beginning of 2024/25 my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) anticipated that GOS contractors would deliver an annual total of 459,746 sight tests. The performance metrics laid out in SPPG’s Service Delivery Plan by year-end record delivered sight test activity at 459,055, or 99.8% of that expected.Although this is 2% down on 2023/24 figures, activity is demand-led with factors influencing and driving activity. GOS eligibility, practice capacity for service provision and the personal choices which the public make in relation to accessing eye care will impact on GOS activity levels each month.In addition, in Northern Ireland we have in place an acute eye pathway, which allows patients to access unscheduled eye care within a GOS practice. This Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service (NI PEARS) frees capacity in Trust ophthalmology clinics, eye casualty, ED and in general practice, managing the presenting patient in primary care optometry and closer to home. In 2024/25 there were 51,517 NI PEARS assessment and treatments carried out, around 4,000 regionally per month. This marks a planned shift in service delivery and a move to commissioned services which make appropriate use of community optometrists’ skills, expertise, equipment and capacity. This planned approach therefore offers patients choice: to meet visual and sight needs via GOS, or meet acute eye presentation requirements (sore, red, watering eyes etc.) via the NI PEARS commissioned service.Both services offer safe, effective, accessible and cost-productive eyecare solutions, managing patients closer to home.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Northern Ireland greenhouse gas statistics, in particular that the reductions in emissions are lower than Wales, Scotland and England.
answer_text: Northern Ireland has a very different sectoral emissions profile to that of the rest of the UK, with each sector requiring a range of bespoke interventions to reduce their emissions profile further. For example, Northern Ireland has a higher percentage of emissions from agriculture accounting for 30.8% of its emissions, compared to a total of 12.1% in the UK. As most of agricultural emissions are methane which are relatively more challenging to reduce, this will have a knock-on impact on our overall total.Related to this are the proportionately high emissions associated with the Land Use, Land-Use Change, and Forestry (LULUCF) sector which when combined with agriculture accounts for 42.6% of Northern Ireland’s emissions. The equivalent proportion for agriculture and LULUCF as a share of total UK emissions is 12.4%. That is why it is so important that my Department continues its work to reduce emissions in these sectors through our Sustainable Agriculture Programme and our plans for woodland creation and peatland restoration.Sectors such as Buildings and Product Uses, Industry, and Waste, which make up 24.2% of our emissions profile, show a broadly similar downward trajectory as the UK.  In addition, as we further adopt less intensive forms of fuel combustion and uptake of renewable energy our emissions profile will diminish further.  My Department is consulting on the draft Climate Action Plan which sets out the policies and proposals necessary to meet the first carbon budget and set a pathway to Net Zero. This consultation closes on the 8th October 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what action the NI Environment Agency are taking to address issues around foul odours in Artigarvan, West Tyrone.
answer_text: Lacpatrick Dairies (NI) Ltd , Artigarvan is permitted under the Pollution Prevention and Control (Industrial Emissions) Regulations (NI) 2013 (PPC regulations).NIEA has issued three enforcement notices to the company in the last year with one relating to odour, one relating to breaches of the emissions limits in their permit covering discharges from their effluent treatment plant and one relating to breaches of the emissions limits in their permit covering discharges of surface water.These notices have led to the company carrying out significant improvement works at the site, with more work currently ongoing. This work has included improvements to their effluent treatment plant, drainage on the site and containment of potential odour sources. NIEA officials are in regular contact with LacPatrick Dairies about this ongoing work and continue to take necessary and proportionate enforcement action in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy where appropriate. The majority of the improvement works have been completed to date with additional measures being identified to be completed by the end of July 2025. NIEA also regularly engages with local stakeholders about their concerns, including residents and elected representatives.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to the Northern Ireland Inter-Departmental Business Register Statistics 2025, to detail the geographical spread of businesses, broken down by local council area, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Data on the geographical spread of businesses, broken down by local council area, for each of the last five years is contained in Worksheet 3.1 of the IDBR Northern Ireland Businesses 2025 Tables, which can be found at the following link: Northern Ireland Business: Activity, Size, Location, and Ownership 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department plans to introduce further Micro Food Business Investment Schemes.
answer_text: The Micro Food Business Investment Scheme was a Rural Policy Framework pilot designed to support small-scale food and drink manufacturing businesses in Northern Ireland that process primary agricultural produce.  My Department provided funding to seven businesses under this pilot scheme worth approximately £149,000. Evaluation of the scheme is in progress with lessons learned being used to inform the development of rural policy. A co-design process with a wide range of stakeholders is underway, and it is my intention that draft proposals for a future rural policy will be consulted on later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the quarterly economic inactivity rate for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The latest data on the quarterly economic inactivity rate (Aged 16 – 64), for each of the last five years can be found in Section 4, Figure 9 of the NISRA Labour Market Report. The report can be viewed at the following link: June 2025 Labour Market Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether an assessment has been made of the number of pensioners who would be entitled to receive a Winter Fuel Payment, broken down by constituency, in light of the announcement by the UK government that the eligibility criteria will be extended in England and Wales.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the Statement I made in the Assembly on 10 June 2025, Update on Winter Fuel Payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency Labour Market Statistics for June 2025.
answer_text: I am pleased to note that the latest NISRA Labour Market release shows that payrolled employees, earnings and the number of hours worked in the local economy have increased over the year, and that the claimant count fell below 4% for the first time since June 2024.  There were no statistically significant changes to the employment, unemployment or inactivity rates, and the 12-month totals for confirmed and proposed redundancies notified to my department remain in line with pre-pandemic trends. More information can be found in the Labour Market Report section of the NISRA website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social homes units built for each of the past 5 years, broken down by (i) constituency; and (ii) number of bedrooms.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 28519/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the budget of the North South Language Body, broken down by contribution from each government,  for each of the past 5 years
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider a self-driving taxi pilot scheme, following the recently announced pilot scheme by the UK Government's Transport Secretary.
answer_text: The proposed self-driving taxi pilot scheme is based within the legislative framework in GB for the use of fully autonomous vehicles without a safety driver.  There is no such framework here. My department will monitor the progress of the pilot scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) the discussions he had with the Finance Minister regarding his bid for £230 million to build social housing; and (ii) any explanation he was given for why this bid was rejected.
answer_text: I submitted a bid of £230m to the Department of Finance in November 2024.  The 2025/26 Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) allocation is £168.5m. This is significantly short of the funding required to deliver the target of 2,000 New Build Social Homes. A further bid of £61.5m was submitted at June monitoring to maximise the SHDP budget and deliver the additional homes committed to in the Programme for Government, however I was only allocated £9m with a first call of £2m from the next round.To truly tackle the housing crisis we face in Northern Ireland, we must have appropriate and sustained investment in the SHDP. You may wish to seek further information from the Finance Minister as to why the full bid was not met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail why the Communities Minister's bid for £230m of funding for social housing was only allocated £63million.
answer_text: The Capital DEL budget that the Executive had at its disposal for allocation as part of the Budget 2025-26 process was insufficient to meet all bids placed on it by departments. The Budget for 2025-26 was agreed by the Executive on 3 April 2025.  As part of this budget, the Department for Communities (DfC) was allocated £270 million Capital DEL. This included an allocation of £100 million from the available RRI borrowing which has been earmarked by the Executive for social housing. It is the responsibility of the Minister for Communities to determine how the remainder of the budget should be allocated within his department. It is my understanding that DfC will allocate £168 million to housing in 2025-26; approximately £105 million for pre-commitments to housebuilding from the 2024-25 financial year and £63 million to facilitate the start of new builds in the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of comments from the charity Barnardo's that the Anti-Poverty Strategy is not fit for purpose and will not make any difference to the lives of the children, families and communities that our organisation support every day.
answer_text: The draft Anti-Poverty Strategy sets out the NI Executive’s commitment to a long-term, joined-up approach to addressing the root-causes of poverty, as well as its impacts, in a way that is affordable and deliverable. The draft Strategy provides, for the first time, an integrated framework across Government, allowing us to target actions and funding in a co-ordinated way.	I would encourage Barnardo’s, alongside other stakeholders, to respond to the current public consultation with their suggestions and recommendations of areas where they feel the draft Strategy could be strengthened.I have asked my officials to allow for a 14-week consultation period, and we will listen carefully to the views of all those who respond. The contents of the final Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive Approval.

answer_text: AQW 26191/22-27 was answered on 3 July 2025

answer_text: Unfortunately, my Department continues to face significant capital pressures and there continues to be no funding available to progress youth capital projects in this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patient education and self-management programmes for people with long term conditions in each of the last three years, broken down by (i) medical category; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A long-term condition is an illness that cannot be cured such as diabetes, arthritis, high blood pressure, epilepsy, asthma and some mental health conditions. Long Term Conditions are usually controlled with medicines or other treatments.Other treatments can include patient education and supported self-management. This gives patients the knowledge, skills and confidence to manage their own health and care by putting in place interventions such as peer support, self-management education and health coaching.Details of the number of patient education and self-management programmes for people with long term conditions in each of the last three years, broken down by (i) medical category; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust are published in an annual report available at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/patient-education-programmes The report for 2024/25 is not yet available but its publication expected in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether the review of Small Business Rate Relief Scheme and Non-Domestic Vacant Rating will be completed in the 2022-27 Assembly mandate
answer_text: The reviews will be completed in early autumn 2025 to allow recommendations to be brought to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27894 22-27, whether she is minded to delay the disposal process regarding St Lucia's barracks, as suggested by Fermanagh and Omagh Council.
answer_text: The disposal exercise for the St Lucia site has commenced. My officials have engaged with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council to ensure that the Council is fully aware of the process. In line with the usual practice for disposal of surplus public sector lands and property, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, along with any other public bodies with an interest in acquiring the site, will be able to register an interest in the site prior to any public sale.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) cost and (ii) logistical implications of expanding free school meals to parents receiving Universal Credit from September 2026, as is being proposed by the British government for pupils in England.
answer_text: The announcement by the Department for Education in England that it will be expanding access to free school meals to all children in families in receipt of Universal Credit from September 2026 is a major policy change in England as at present families in receipt of Universal Credit would only be eligible for free school meals if they had a net household income of less than £7,400 per year. This is significantly lower than the £15,000 income threshold that currently applies in Northern Ireland. If a similar approach were to be implemented in Northern Ireland, it would be expected to result in over 40,000 extra pupils gaining entitlement to free school meals compared to the current academic year. Taking into account the potential loss of income from pupils who would previously have paid for a meal, initial estimates are that it could cost up to an additional £22m each year. There would also be likely one-off capital costs to facilitate the expansion of capacity in canteen and dining halls that may be necessary depending on the circumstances in each school.  The logistical implications would be dependent on the individual circumstances in each school, for example their current staffing levels as well as current kitchen and dining hall capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what action he is taking to reverse the 18 per cent reduction in GP practices in the Western Health and Social Care Trust since 2014.
answer_text: Since 2014, there have been a number of mergers of GP practice which has resulted in a reduction in the overall number of practices of 10. It is important to note that in all cases, the merged practices have maintained the patient lists of the legacy practices, meaning that patients across the Western area have retained access to a GP in their local area. In addition to the mergers above, since 2022 there have been 6 contract handbacks in the area.  A contractor was found for 1 of those Practices and the remaining 5 are currently being managed by the Western Trust. There are now a total of 47 GP practices in the Western area.In terms of short-term measures in response to emerging difficulties, the General Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team (GPICRT) is able to support practices at risk to develop and implement effective practice recovery plans to ensure that they can deliver their contractual obligations to their registered patients. Whilst this has been important in providing stability, I recognise that more sustainable options are required in the longer term.  To that end, my Department is working with the Western Health and Social Care Trust to support them to move away from the locum-reliant model that has been in place in Trust-run practices and implement a more sustainable salaried GP model.  We are also working with GP Federations to explore how they can play a role in the longer-term sustainability of GMS.I am also acutely aware of the challenges in attracting and retaining GPs, particularly in rural areas. Therefore, my Department has also continued its focus on building the workforce and has funded a number of initiatives to recruit and retain GPs including the International Induction programme, the Attract Recruit Retain scheme, the GP Retainer scheme and the GP Mentor scheme.GP Fellowship schemes are also supporting recently qualified GPs to transition from the training environment to independent practice, with opportunities for personal and professional development. There is also a South-West Skills Mix service provided in the South-West GP Federation areas of Omagh & Fermanagh. The aim of this service is to increase the capacity of GP Practices to meet the demand for patient appointments, assisting struggling practices and to assist in patient choice.My Department has increased the annual number of GP training places from 65 in 2015/16 to the current all-time high level of 121 and has worked with key stakeholders, to increase the exposure of undergraduates to General Practice; encourage more GP trainees; and ensure that there are sufficient GP training practices to meet demand and support GPs in training.  In terms of the wider primary care workforce, in 2024/25 we continued to boost its capacity by allocating £25.5 million for the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) Programme, £20.5million for the General Practice Pharmacy Programme, and £3million for Advanced Nurse Practitioners working in primary care.A key f

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action she will take to reverse the (i) 13 per cent decline in overall number of overnight trips to Northern Ireland; and (ii) 10 per cent decline in spend expenditure associated with these overnight trips, contained in the latest Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency's annual tourism figures.
answer_text: Tourism is one of our key successes and a regional economic driver across the North. The latest NISRA figures show £1.1 billion of tourism expenditure for 2024. Whilst the headline figures are disappointing in comparison to 2023, overall, there is a mixed picture with some positive news in terms of increased external visitor numbers and spend and increases in hotel and other guest accommodation occupancy rates. The main decreases relate to overnight trips and spend from residents across Ireland. 2024 saw a ‘resettling’ of tourism markets as international travel fully resumed post pandemic. The statistics suggest that this resulted in greater numbers of people from across Ireland choosing to holiday abroad last year. In contrast, and very positively, overnight trips here from visitors outside Britain and Ireland increased by 12% with an increase of 25% in overnight trips for holiday purposes.Tourism NI's consumer sentiment and industry data indicate a more stable outlook for 2025 as we look forward to the return of The Open in July and a range of events and festivals, as well as the forthcoming Fleadh Cheoil in Belfast 2026, all of which will drive visitor numbers and revenue.My Department launched a Tourism Vision and Action Plan in January that set out our ambition for growth in our tourism sector over the next decade including a Strategic Goal that the tourism expenditure from our overnight visitors will exceed £2 billion by 2035. The Action Plan identifies 17 activities which are critical to achieving this ambition in a way that promotes regional balance, raises productivity, creates good jobs, and lowers carbon emissions.In addition to job creation and the generation of substantial export earnings in the form of visitor spend, the outworking of the Vision and Action Plan has the capacity to cultivate and showcase local history, culture and our natural and built heritage. All of this will make the North not only a great place to visit but also a great place to live. Tourism NI will continue to promote the North as a tourism destination to drive demand, focussing on its best prospect audiences, and promoting the convenience and value for money of a short break here to counter cost of living concerns. Lucrative niche markets such as luxury, incentive travel, golf tourism, screen tourism, peace tourism and business events will be maximised to attract high spending visitors.  Tourism NI will build on the continued success of its Embrace A Giant Spirit brand, as well as work with Fáilte Ireland to identify all island opportunities for collaboration.Working in partnership with Tourism Ireland, Tourism NI will develop and deliver a programme of sales and marketing activity to support the local tourism industry to increase programming from international operators.Tourism NI will develop new capital investment and development programmes to support new and enhanced tourism experiences and support the successful delivery of the major new tourism investme

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the additional measures he plans to introduce to reduce losses due to (i) customer fraud; (ii) customer error; and (iii) official error in the welfare system.
answer_text: My Department’s Benefit Security Division takes the lead in counter fraud and error activity. There is an extensive programme of work in place, including case reviews to address customer error and criminal investigations in cases of potential customer fraud.   My Department is committed to reducing official error and has robust checking regimes in place to ensure benefit payments are accurate and cases are corrected at the earliest opportunity, if appropriate.   I have repeatedly bid for additional resources to support greater investment in fraud and error activities, and I continue to make the case to Executive colleagues for this funding. In my statement to the Assembly on 11 February 2025, I announced the establishment of a Task and Finish Group to critically examine every intervention taken regarding benefit fraud and error. The findings of this Group will inform any additional measures which may be put in place to address customer fraud, customer error and official error. My Department has reintroduced the publicising of benefit fraud sentencing outcomes from 1 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timetable to consider and act on the recommendations contained in the independent Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.
answer_text: I am currently considering the findings of the Review and will provide a formal response to each of the recommendations in the coming weeks.  I  have announced my intention to establish a Curriculum Taskforce that will lead the design and development of the new curriculum. I intend that the Taskforce will be operational in Autumn 2025.  In line with my commitments within TransformED, I wish to have a new statutory curriculum framework in place by autumn 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to introduce further 20mph speed limit schemes outside schools.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  A review of the recent initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools.  The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.  I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the next financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies.  Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools is included within my budget plans for the next financial year and I intend to make an announcement on the next tranche in the coming weeks.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department monitors the number of defibrillators that comply with the NI Ambulance Service's Advice and Best Practice Guidance for their use.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) leads on the implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice for defibrillators. NIAS has partnered with the British Heart Foundation to develop The Circuit (https://www.thecircuit.uk), a national online defibrillator network that connects every ambulance service in the UK. NIAS can readily provide information on Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) registered on The Circuit to 999 callers who require access. Currently, there are 3,910 community defibrillators registered.Further details on defibrillator locations can be found at https://www.defibfinder.uk or by contacting the NIAS Community Resuscitation Team at resus.admin@nias.hscni.net or via telephone at 028 9040 5215.All PADs should be registered on The Circuit, and each must have an assigned guardian. Both NIAS and The Circuit recommend registering multiple guardians to reduce the risk of missed emails or oversight. The registration process involves inputting the hours of public availability, providing the AED’s precise location—including address details—and dropping a pin on the provided map.The responsibility for maintenance, upkeep and checks of PADs then rests with the registered guardians of the defibrillator. Regular, automatic emails are sent to each guardian from The Circuit to check their defibrillator. The checks involve ensuring the defibrillator is serviceable, pads are in date, battery is in date and the emergency ready kit (razor, scissors, towel & face shield) is stocked. If a defibrillator is deployed by the ambulance service, the guardian will receive an automated email asking them to perform checks and log into their Circuit account and return the defibrillator to emergency ready. The Circuit actively monitors guardian compliance, tracking the time taken to return defibrillators to emergency readiness after deployment and adherence to routine maintenance checks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27342/22-27, to detail (i) when; and (ii) by whom, a decision will be made on whether the St Lucia site, Omagh, will be sold in one lot or two lots.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the impact of the new regulations regarding business-to-business parcel movements under the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: New arrangements for business-to-business (B2B) parcel movements were introduced by the British Government on 1 May 2025 as part of the implementation of the Windsor Framework. These changes mean that B2B parcel movements are now subject to the same requirements as freight shipments moving from Britain to the North. It is currently too early to assess fully the impact of these changes. However, I remain vigilant regarding the potential implications for businesses, particularly in relation to their ability to access supplies delivered to the North. My Department continues to engage closely with HMRC to monitor the implementation and impact of the new regulations. We also continue to emphasise the importance of maintaining the smooth and uninterrupted flow of goods moving East to West. I encourage all affected businesses to avail of the free support offered through the British Government’s Trader Support Service. In addition, further guidance and information is available via Invest NI’s (nibusinessinfo.co.uk) website and InterTradeIreland’s Trade Hub.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the cost effectiveness of Project Stratum.
answer_text: The business case for Project Stratum evaluated the costs and benefits of the Project, and it was subject to a value for money review by BDUK. These assessments indicated that the Project offered value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what steps her Department is taking to increase the number of women enrolling for Higher Level Apprenticeships in Higher Education.
answer_text: Apprenticeships are demand-led and rely on employers creating workplace opportunities.  As such, Higher Level Apprenticeship (HLA) uptake by women reflects wider employment trends. The most recent published statistics show that 39% of participants on the HLA programme in Higher Education Institutions are female.  The corresponding figure for female HLA participation rates at Further Education colleges is 30%. My Department promotes and markets the benefits of apprenticeships to all.  A suite of advertising campaigns run biannually to raise awareness of apprenticeships for both prospective apprentices and employers and encourages the uptake of these programmes. Nevertheless, my Department recently developed an Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund to specifically promote apprenticeship uptake from three groups who are currently under-represented across apprenticeship programmes, i.e. women, individuals with a disability and individuals from areas of deprivation or disadvantage.  Two of the six funded programmes support increased female participation in apprenticeships: the #NotJustForBoys Pathways+ programme and the Software for All programme.  These programmes are particularly welcome in that they also encourage female participation in STEM based occupations, reflecting the Department’s ambition to encourage women into STEM subjects and occupations, as set out in the Women in STEM Action Plan which was published in 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 27206/22-27, whether he will consider additional mitigations to increase support to families in constituencies where the value of subsidy claims is significantly lower than average.
answer_text: For the period 1 September 2024 to 31 March 2025, the distribution of Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) expenditure across constituencies broadly reflects the distribution of households eligible and registered for Tax-Free Childcare (TFC) with children below primary school age. The Department recognises that the value of subsidy claims may vary across areas due to a range of factors including patterns of childcare use and access to other childcare support schemes such as Universal Credit.  While there are no immediate plans to introduce additional measures, we continue to keep this under review.  My officials continue to liaise with His Majesty Revenue and Customs (HMRC) to promote uptake of TFC and are engaging with childcare providers to encourage registration for both the TFC and NICSS to help support families with the affordability of childcare.

On this occasion no consultation with the Irish Government was required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment for the reasons behind the 28 per cent reduction in people being stopped and searched in 2024-25 compared to the previous year.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the use of stop and search powers by the police in Northern Ireland.  The use of stop and search by police officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether reforms to the broadcasting of certain court proceedings will be carried out in the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Should there be support for the proposals in the consultation paper I published on 20 May, primary legislation would be needed to provide the Department with a power to permit the recording and broadcasting of certain court proceedings. Given the current legislative programme, this would have to be progressed in the next Assembly mandate, subject to an incoming Minister and a new Executive agreeing to legislate on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Children and Adolescent Mental Health Service Waiting Time Statistics for Northern Ireland March 2025.
answer_text: Due to the ongoing roll-out of Encompass there is limited data available on waiting times across the region. My Department is working closely with HSC Trusts to quality assure information recording and ensure robust reporting is available.  CAMHS waiting list information is published quarterly at:https://datavis.nisra.gov.uk/health/quarterly-camhs-ni-march-2025.html Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services continue to be challenged to meet routine 9-week access targets, however services generally meet access targets with respect to Emergency and Urgent waiting times (the most vulnerable and high-risk children and young people). CAMHS staff offer specialist assessments and manage increased severity of illness and clinical risk. There is greater complexity and acuity of presentations of children and young people within CAMHS that require longer treatment plans for young people, resulting in less capacity within services.Levels of deprivation, poverty and debt contributing to poor emotional wellbeing increasing vulnerability to mental health issues. CAMHS Clinicians aim is to deliver timely, responsive, compassionate care to the children, young people and their families in receipt of CAMHS care. Ongoing recruitment challenges to specialist child mental health staffing positions remains an area of focus for my Department. Workforce stabilisation aligned with the mental health workforce review is a key development priority throughout 2024/25 in keeping with the NI Mental Health Strategy. Funding will be required to secure ongoing progression of this priority. The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of cases when a defendant pleaded guilty and (i) received a reduced sentence; and (ii) did not receive a reduced sentence, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: When determining the length of sentence, a judge may take into consideration a range of factors including a guilty plea.  Although the judge is required to state in open court what factors have impacted on the sentence, only the final sentence is recorded.  The reduction in the sentence and the factors that led to the reduction are not recorded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the actions contained in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, particularly those encouraging the benefits of non-pharmaceutical supports.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 sets out a ten-year vision to improve mental health outcomes in Northern Ireland through 35 actions across three themes: promoting mental wellbeing and resilience, providing the right support at the right time, and new ways of working. Several actions emphasise non-pharmaceutical supports to promote mental health and wellbeing, focusing on prevention, early intervention, and community-based approaches. Despite the extremely challenging financial context, good progress has been made in taking forward a number of these actions, including:Development of a three-year Early Intervention and Prevention Action Plan, with progress made on implementation of actions; Roll-out of the emotional health and wellbeing teams in schools, with initial cohorts of schools identified and engaged; Work to scope and develop a digital mental health service action plan;  Commissioning of a review of the scope, scale and capacity of the community and voluntary (C&V) sector to ensure they are fully integrated in the delivery of mental health services, building upon the previous report on the C&V’s collective voice; andConsideration of psychological therapy hubs in primary care.In addition, Health and Social Care Trusts are providing non-pharmaceutical interventions such as cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and other evidence-based talking therapies. Further detail on progress against each action in the Annual Delivery Plans is available on the Department’s website at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/mental-health-strategy-2021-2031.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the National Farmers' Union's alternative proposal regarding Inheritance Tax to introduce a clawback which allows 100 per cent relief on qualifying assets, but charges Inheritance Tax on those assets if sold by the beneficiary within seven years.
answer_text: The Finance Minister and I met with representatives of the Ulster Farmers’ Union and Northern Ireland Agricultural Producers’ Association on 30th January 2025 to discuss their concerns around inheritance tax changes and the impact of these changes on our local farming families.  At this meeting, we discussed potential mitigating measures that could be put forward to UK Government.  Following the meeting, I wrote to the Defra Secretary of State, Steve Reed MP on 11th February 2025 to reiterate my concerns regarding the potential impact of the inheritance tax policy change on the farming community in Northern Ireland  and to call on him to ask the Treasury to consider the mitigating measure that inheritance tax liability be deferred and then cancelled where agricultural land is inherited if it continues in agricultural use and is not disposed of within a seven year period. I wrote to him again on 12th March 2025 to request a meeting to discuss this matter but unfortunately, I have not received a response to either letter. I believe this proposal is reasonable as this is a common approach used by other European countries in this policy area. This mitigating measure would help end any exploitation of the inheritance tax reliefs while at the same time mitigate against the adverse impacts on family farming.  I will be writing again to the Defra Secretary of State asking that the UK Government reflect upon this issue and its impact and confirm, if they do not plan to reconsider their decision, what provisions they have considered or will put in place in regard to supporting and safeguarding our most vulnerable farming families.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Heritage, Culture and Creativity Programme.
answer_text: I launched the Heritage, Culture & Creativity Programme in July 2024 to deliver four new policies for public libraries, museums, the arts and the historic environment.  Significant progress has been made since I announced the programme, with a comprehensive round of engagement across local government and executive departments having been completed.The focus has now moved onto policy drafting and planning for public consultation. On current timings, it is anticipated that, allowing for the full public consultation processes, all the policies in this first phase of the Programme will be in place by spring 2026.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the cost implications of the proposals contained in the Animal Welfare Pathway 2025-27; and (ii) which of the proposals in the Pathway will be implemented in the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Each individual proposal contained within the Pathway will have financial impacts. These impacts will be assessed in full, as policies are developed. It is my intention that I will deliver the reforms within the lifetime of this Assembly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of recent comments by the Chairman of the Police Federation for Northern Ireland that policing is at the back of the queue when it comes to financial allocations.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 15 - AQO 2078/22-27			   ANSWER  I do not recognise the description of policing being at the back of the queue when it comes to financial allocations.  Of my Department’s £1,415.3m 2025-26 opening Budget, PSNI has been allocated £910.6m – this represents 64% of the total budget.  In June Monitoring, of the £5m indicative allocation to my Department towards the Safer Communities Programme for Government priority, PSNI will receive £4.75m - 95% of the additional funding available. PSNI will also receive a £3.3m general allocation along with £4m HM Treasury funding for NI Protocol. This will bring the PSNI non-ringfenced resource budget to £922.8m.  Following receipt of the Police Remuneration Review Body’s 2024 report, the PSNI was initially unable to confirm affordability.  However, on receipt of additional funding from the Executive and my Department in November 2024, the Chief Constable was then able to confirm that the PRRB recommendations were affordable. Thereafter, my officials worked closely alongside those from PSNI and the Department of Finance to ensure that the respective pay remit documentation was submitted, considered and approved within 14 working days.   Following these approvals, I am happy to report the 2024-25 pay award was made to officers up to the rank of chief superintendent in their December 2024 salary.  I have consistently pressed for additional resources for the PSNI to enhance public safety, as well as for the entire justice sector.  These efforts remain a central focus in my discussions with my Executive colleagues and I will continue to advocate for this vital funding as strongly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on discussions between his Department and the Northern Ireland Museums Council regarding the establishment of a Harry Ferguson Museum.
answer_text: I met with Northern Ireland Museums Council (NIMC) Director, Councillor Gowan, and representatives from the Ferguson Celebration Committee at the Ferguson homestead on 4 March 2025. Following this meeting, NIMC issued information to the Celebration Committee on Accreditation, Collections Development and NIMC membership, and have offered to comment on any feasibility studies or proposals produced in order to establish a new museum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what discussions have taken place between her Department and the Irish Government in regards to reinstating daily flights from Derry Airport to Dublin Airport, over the last six months.
answer_text: In the New Decade New Approach Agreement, the Irish Government committed to taking forward a review of the potential for Government support for the air route from Dublin to Derry.This review was led by the Department of Transport in Dublin in cooperation with the Department for Transport in London which is responsible for Public Service Obligation policy.  At a recent meeting with the Secretary of State for Transport, I asked for an update on progress and have written to the Minister for Transport in Dublin emphasising the significant economic and social opportunities of reopening the Dublin-Derry route for the North West. I also asked when the government’s response to their review into the route will be made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 26634/22-27, for her assessment of the comments made by the Chairman of the Police Federation that policing is at the back of the queue when it comes to budget allocations.
answer_text: I do not recognise the description of policing being at the back of the queue when it comes to financial allocations.Of my Department’s £1,415.3m 2025-26 opening Budget, PSNI has been allocated £910.6m – this represents 64% of the total budget.In June Monitoring, of the £5m indicative allocation to my Department towards the Safer Communities Programme for Government priority, PSNI will receive £4.75m - 95% of the additional funding available. PSNI will also receive a £3.3m general allocation along with £4m HM Treasury funding for NI Protocol. This will bring the PSNI non-ringfenced resource budget to £922.8m.Following receipt of the Police Remuneration Review Body’s 2024 report, the PSNI was initially unable to confirm affordability.  However, on receipt of additional funding from the Executive and my Department in November 2024, the Chief Constable was then able to confirm that the PRRB recommendations were affordable. Thereafter, my officials worked closely alongside those from PSNI and the Department of Finance to ensure that the respective pay remit documentation was submitted, considered and approved within 14 working days. Following these approvals, I am happy to report the 2024-25 pay award was made to officers up to the rank of chief superintendent in their December 2024 salary.I have consistently pressed for additional resources for the PSNI to enhance public safety, as well as for the entire justice sector.  These efforts remain a central focus in my discussions with my Executive colleagues and I will continue to advocate for this vital funding as strongly as possible. The Programme for Government identifies safer communities as a key priority and the Executive can help deliver on that by ensuring the PSNI receives the investment it needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of staff in (i) schools; (ii) the Education Authority; and (iii) his Department, who have availed of repeated full-pay sick leave by returning to work briefly, for each of the past 5 years.
answer_text: The figures requested by the Member are not available for the reasons provided below.For support staff in schools, staff in the Education Authority and staff in the Department of Education, occupational sick pay is calculated on the first day of absence from work and continues to be recalculated throughout the absence on a rolling period.  Periods of absence occurring at intervals are treated as cumulative, which means that no reset occurs if the employee returns to work briefly. It is therefore not possible to avail of full-pay sick leave by returning to work briefly.For teachers, the calculation of occupational sick pay differs from the method set out above as entitlement to full sick pay does involve a ‘reset’ after a return to work.  However, the information requested is not readily available as it would require manual checking of a vast number of individual teacher payroll records which would be extremely resource intensive to collate and could compromise normal payroll operations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to reverse the 8.8 per cent increase in vacancies under active recruitment in the past year.
answer_text: The most recent data published by my Department report that as of 31 March 2025 there were a total of 5,851 vacancies actively being recruited across Health and Social Care (HSC) in Northern Ireland, equating to an overall vacancy rate of 6.9%.This represented an 8.8% increase (+471) compared to the 5,380 active vacancies recorded at 31 March 2024, but is 20.8% (-1,541) lower than the 7,392 vacancies actively being recruited across the HSC at 31 March 2023.It is worth acknowledging the significant progress made in recent years to stabilise and grow the HSC workforce.  Since my Department published the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy in 2018, there has been a 17.7% increase in whole time equivalent staff directly employed across the HSC.  That amounts to 10,062 additional whole time equivalent staff across the HSC, including 1,019 (+24.3%) more medical and dental staff, 2,938 (+19.4%) more registered nurses and midwives and 2,026 (+24.9%) more professional and technical staff.These increases in staffing numbers have been supported by the strategic approach to workforce development outlined in the Workforce Strategy including strategic workforce planning, increased commissioning of pre- and post-registration training, better attraction and recruitment of staff, reducing agency dependency and developing initiatives to improve staff retention.  While we have made important strides in stabilising and growing our workforce, the positive impact of additional staffing is not felt uniformly across the HSC.  Key workforce challenges remain in particular services and locations.  My Department is committed to addressing these challenges through continued implementation of the Workforce Strategy and will shortly publish the Strategy’s third action plan which will outline priorities identified for progression over the next three years.  Continued implementation of the Workforce Strategy will require sustained, increased funding to ensure we provide the investment we know is required to meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the implications of the new UK-EU agri-food deal on Northern Ireland agri-businesses.
answer_text: Since the UK left the EU there is no doubt that there has been a significant impact on the UK economy and Northern Ireland due to uncertainty and confusion created by myriad of new operating requirements following EU Exit and associated negotiations. A study by the Centre for Economic Performance has estimated that the cost of non-tariff agri-food barriers between the UK and EU, from December 2019 to March 2023 has cost UK households around £6.95 billion. The EU has traditionally been the UK’s largest agri-food trading partner but since Brexit UK exports to the EU are down 21% and imports of EU products to the UK are down 7% (2018-2024). Many agri-food businesses have scaled back or stopped trading altogether due to increased costs, red tape and delays at borders. Clearly getting a resolution to these issues are in the interests of everyone. I believe that this new Common Framework commitment between the UK and EU will help to go towards resolving a specific range of issues but regret UK Government decision to exclude joining a customs union or wider EU single market thereby refusing the opportunity to garner the benefits of wider co-operation and alignment.The UK Government and the European Commission have committed to align regulations in respect of Sanitary and Phytosanitary (SPS) goods in GB with rules in the EU, which should mean the removal of almost all certification and controls on movements of SPS goods between GB and the EU. This in turn should make it much easier to move agri-food goods and plants from GB to NI. On 22nd May 2025, the UK Government published their assessment of the long run impact of a SPS Agreement with the EU which concluded a positive impact of up to 0.14% on GDP.  This is equivalent of up to £5.1bn when compared to projected UK GDP levels in 2040.Unfortunately, veterinary medicines are not in scope of the agreement I will continue to engage with the UK Government and EU to urge a resolution to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines on a long term basis.Whilst the continued reciprocal access to waters to fish brings some certainty for our fishing sector I am disappointed both by the lack of prior engagement and that issues have not been addressed such as access for Northern Ireland fishing vessels to the Irish 6 to 12 nautical miles region.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) permanent; (ii) temporary; and (iii) agency, staff working in his Department at the end of March 2025.
answer_text: (i) As at 1 January 2025, the number of permanent staff that work in the Department for Communities was 7,864 (99.7%).(ii) As at 1 January 2025, the number of temporary staff that work in the Department for Communities was 23 (0.3%).(iii) As at month ending March 2025, the number of Agency staff that work in the Department for Communities was 3,729 (32.2 %).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 16620/22-27, for an update on her Department’s plans to increase the number of women leading companies in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: According to the Gender Index 2025 report, 17.5% of all companies in the North were female-led in 2024. As in the previous report, this was a lower proportion than in Wales (18.5%), Scotland (18.0%) and Britain (19.1%). The North did however have the highest performance of newly incorporated companies which were female-led at 21.8% - this was higher than the British figure of 20.5%. Comparable figures for the South aren't currently available.The number of female-led incorporations increased by approximately 30% in 2024, with the North being one of the few monitored regions where the share of female-led incorporations increased since 2023.  Among ‘fast growth’ companies in the North 14.2% were female-led, accounting for the second highest share among monitored regions, surpassed only by the North East of England (16.1%). London is set to experience the largest growth in female entrepreneurship, with female-led companies projected to account for 30.1% of firms by 2030. In contrast, the North and Scotland are projected to remain below the national average.The Global Entrepreneurship Monitor report indicates that in 2023, the rate of female Total Early-Stage Entrepreneurial Activity (TEA) was 8.2%, up from 7.3% in 2022 and continuing an upward trend seen in recent years. The gap between males and females remained unchanged, as the male TEA rate also increased, rising from 10.2% in 2022 to 11.3% in 2023. The trend of male TEA rates being higher than female rates was also recorded in England, Scotland and Wales.It is fair to say that while there is more to do, overall the North is on an upward trajectory in terms of female-led businesses and I believe that is attributable, in no small part, to the ongoing work of my Department and its business partners.  We have a suite of programmes and sponsorship/access to finance in place to encourage businesses and women entrepreneurs in the North including:The Women Returners Programme supported through the Skills Fund has provided skills and training for over 140 female participants in 2024/25. The programme delivered through local colleges in partnership with community-based organisations and employers, includes training in  sector specific skills, as well as transversal skills including leadership, project management and business improvement techniques. BRAVE Women in STEM 2024/25 delivered through Ulster University has funded 40 places for females employed within the STEM sector, encouraging diverse perspectives that will benefit decision-making and innovation. This programme has a specific focus on investing in female leadership. Go Succeed is a Council led business start-up service, directly funded by the British Government, through Shared Prosperity Funding. Go Succeed is a key component of the entrepreneurial ecosystem in the North which has a target of 50% for women engaging in its service.  To help achieve this target, Go Succeed host workshops specifically targeted at women.The Ambition to Grow Support

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18753/22-27, for an update on the gambling industry Code of Practice.
answer_text: My officials are currently finalising Northern Ireland’s first Gambling Code of Practice following the conclusion of the consultation on 24 December 2024. I intend to issue the Code shortly after the conclusion of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16097/22-27, for an update on the body camera scheme for hospital staff, being piloted by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Trust is currently engaging with staff, trade unions and relevant organisations to develop the policy and the planning for a pilot to use body worn cameras, with the aim of going out to public consultation by the autumn.The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is planning to pilot the body worn camera scheme in the Royal Victoria and Mater adult Emergency Departments after the public consultation is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the timescale for her delivery of the Sentencing Bill.
answer_text: I received Executive approval to the proposed content of my Sentencing Bill on the 19 December 2024.  It remains my intention that the Bill will be introduced in Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on when the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment competition, launched in August 2024 with interviews in November 2024, will be completed and results compiled.
answer_text: A merit list has now been compiled and results have been issued to all candidates on 21st May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance pursuant to AQW 19667/22-27, to detail: (i) whether the scoping report requested by his predecessor was delivered to him by Easter; and (ii) when he will decide on the next actions regarding the regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: ANSWERA detailed scoping report, prepared by the Civil Law Reform team in my Department, was delivered to me in April 2025. I am currently considering that report and the next steps in relation to this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update of discussions between his Department and the Department of Finance regarding the regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: My officials engaged with Department of Finance colleagues on two separate occasions to feed into this work. It is my understanding that a report has recently been submitted to the Finance Minister on this subject.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on legislation for the replacement body for the General Teaching Council For Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 24638/22-27, I advised you that I hoped to be in a position to write to the Executive in the near future seeking approval to legislate.I can now advise that I wrote to Executive Ministers on 2 May 2025 enclosing a Draft Executive Paper setting out policy proposals to reform the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland.  It is hoped that the paper will be considered at an upcoming Executive meeting and, subject to securing legislative consent, my officials will begin work with the Office of the Legislative Counsel to prepare a Bill for introduction to the Assembly later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 24039/22-27, to detail (i) whether the site will be advertised as one or two lots; and (ii) the timetable for disposal.
answer_text: The disposal of the St Lucia site will follow the standard practice for disposal of surplus public sector lands and property. I can confirm that the site will be advertised as one lot covering the entire site or two lots, one lot the Historic Core and the second lot, the remainder of the site The disposal process has commenced and my officials are working to complete this process within six to nine months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the timetable to introduce a Public Health Bill.
answer_text: The Department received approximately 8,200 submissions in response to the public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland.  A draft consultation summary report is being prepared which I expect to publish on the Department’s website in the months ahead. A further update will be provided when the final summary report has been published.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail: (i) any discussions between his Department and Omagh High School regarding the design and contents of their school as part of the Strule Shared Campus; and (ii) the grounds on which requests for adjustments by the school were turned down.
answer_text: The design consultation for the Strule Programme has taken place over more than a decade. Initial design engagement with all six Strule schools occurred during RIBA Stage 3 (Developed Design) from November 2015 to 2017, with further detailed reviews of the Department’s Building Handbook and Building Regulations completed prior to the procurement of the now onsite contracting team.While RIBA Stage 4 (Technical Design) does not typically involve further consultation, following contract award the Department did hold further extensive meetings with all six schools to ensure their input was heard and reflected where possible. The design requests raised by Omagh High School were carefully considered by the Strule Programme Team and its specialist advisors during this engagement, which took place between September and November 2024.Every effort has been made to respond constructively to school-specific feedback and the Department has accommodated requests where feasible, however there were a range of requests that no new school build within the estate would typically receive. Equity across the school estate is a critical principle, and all designs must align with the Department of Education’s School Design Handbook standards.
