answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with 2023-24, ending 31st March 2024, being the most recent year available. For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the timetable for introducing legislation which will entitle carers to up to five days of unpaid leave in a rolling 12 month period; and (ii) the timetable for deciding whether to provide paid leave for these carers.
answer_text: On 28th April, I announced my intention to the Assembly, and wrote to my Executive colleagues, with my proposals to introduce legislation which will enable eligible carers to 5 days of unpaid leave within a rolling 12-month period. This will be part of the wider Employment Rights Bill. Subject to Executive agreement, I aim to introduce the Bill to the Assembly in January 2026. Subordinate Regulations will be required to operationalise these rights. Timing will be dependent on when the wider Bill completes all legislative stages and receives Royal Assent.I have also made clear my preference that a paid carer’s leave entitlement should also be introduced. The timeline for this will depend on the Executive agreeing to fund the costs, or similar rights being introduced in Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of; and (ii) change of numbers of full-time equivalent health and social care staff in each of the last five years, broken down by (a) staff group; and (b) Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the whole-time equivalent (WTE) of staff employed in Health & Social Care (HSC) organisations is published by my Department on a quarterly basis.  The latest information relates to the position at 31 March 2025 and can be found at the link below.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-health-and-social-care-hsc-workforce-statistics-march-2025At 31 March 2025, 66,865.1 WTE HSC staff were employed by HSC Organisations, an increase of 1.3% (881 WTE) since 31 March 2024.  Additional information on HSC staff employed by staff group and organisation can be found at the link above.HSC Staff Employed (WTE) 31 March 2021 - 2025At 31st March:WTE2021 63,247.8 2022 64,359.3 2023 64,688.4 2024 65,984.2 2025 66,865.1 Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoHExcludes domiciliary care workers, bank staff and staff on career break.Our strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 17.7% (+10,063) increase in whole time equivalent (wte) staff in post across the HSC in Northern Ireland between March 2018 (56,802) and March 2025 (66,865).   This includes a 24.2% (+1,018 wte) increase in medical and dental staff, a 19.4% (+2,938 wte) increase in registered nursing and midwifery staff and a 24.9% (+2,026 wte) increase in professional and technical staff in post.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the proposed expansions for (i) Sure Start; (iI) the Pathway Fund; (iii) Toybox; and (iv) the Bookstart Baby Programme, as announced on 15 May 2025.
answer_text: The proposed expansions are detailed below:Sure StartSure Start services provided by the existing 38 Sure Start projects will be made  available  to  additional  children  and  families  who  live  beyond  the current Sure Start geographical boundaries and who require Sure Start support.  Rather than incorporating whole Super Output Areas, a targeted outreach model will be adopted ensuring that children with demonstrable need can be identified and referred into the programme, regardless of where they live.The Pathway FundExpansion of the Pathway Fund will include an additional funding stream specifically for Childminders and Approved Home Childcarers (AHCs). Childminders or AHCs have applied for funding (up to £500) to provide support which will enhance the quality and/ or sustainability of provision to children aged 0-4. In addition, the maximum award available to Pathway settings will increase to £40,000 (from £30,000) for Stream A and £20,000 (from £15,000) for Stream B. ToyboxThe Toybox project which provides early learning support to Traveller and Roma children and families, will be expanded to also support Newcomer children (0-4 years) and their families.The Bookstart Baby ProgrammeIn 2024-25 BookTrust received funding to enable the re-introduction of the Babystart programme in Northern Ireland for a 7-month period.  The funding has been increased in 2025-26 to cover the full year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26190/22-27, to detail the reduction in Did Not Attend rates since encompass was rolled out, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: South Eastern Trust:In the 12 months before South Eastern Trust Go-Live (November 22 to October 23) the average DNA rate for both new and review appointments was 10.2%. In the last 12 months (June 2024 – May 2025) the DNA rate was 9.2%. This is based on the Regional Quarterly Outpatient reporting which is Consultant-Led outpatient services only excluding virtual and ICATS.Belfast TrustThe following information refers to Consultant & ICATS onlyIn the 12 months before Belfast Trust Go-Live the DNA rate for new appointments was 7.3%, this reduced to 6.6% in the 12 months following go live.In the same period, the pre-Go Live rate for review appointments was 7.6%, which reduced to 7.4% in the 12 months following go live.Northern TrustAs Northern Trust is still in a phase of stabilisation, comparable information is not yet available.Southern & Western TrustsThe Southern & Western Trusts went live with encompass on 8th May 2025, and are not yet in a position to provide this information.The Trusts are currently implementing the standard Epic / regionally followed post-implementation Activity Stabilisation processes, to ensure the system is being used correctly and activity levels are stabilising.As part of Activity Stabilisation processes the Trusts are currently identifying workflow and recording issues within each service and until this work is completed any data from the system will not be fully validated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the spending on the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme since its introduction, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The table below provides a provisional breakdown, by constituency, of the subsidy claims paid from 1 September 2024 to 18 April 2025.Value of subsidy claims paid by the child’s home constituency from 1 September 2024 to 18 April 2025Home constituency of childrenValue of subsidy claims*Belfast East £573,471 Belfast North £241,744 Belfast South and Mid Down £783,175 Belfast West £162,010 East Antrim £336,343 East Londonderry £326,157 Fermanagh and South Tyrone £436,070 Foyle £259,928 Lagan Valley £757,764 Mid Ulster £490,943 Newry and Armagh £449,354 North Antrim £321,809 North Down £449,408 South Antrim £529,117 South Down £510,564 Strangford £432,599 Upper Bann £551,873 West Tyrone £325,925 Not Known** £59,285 Total £7,997,539 *Breakdown is based on the 2008 parliamentary constituencies, as per recent NISRA guidance, as this is the basis on which current members of the Northern Ireland Assembly were elected.  Some figures may differ from previously published data which was based on 2024 constituencies.**Not known relates to invalid postcodes provided by parents upon registration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26190/22-27, to detail the estimated efficiency savings envisaged with the roll-out of encompass, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As per the Business Case, both qualitative and quantitative benefits will be achieved through the introduction of encompass across NI.  Total possible estimated cash releasing benefits are £29.6million over 14 years, and non cash releasing benefits value £514 million, including increased theatre utilisation, reduction in rejected lab samples, reduced error in charts and medication administration, reduced delayed doses of medication, reduction in outpatient DNAs (Did not attend), reduction in VTEs (Venous thromboembolism) and pressure ulcers, improved outpatient utilisation, improved patient experience. There is also potential for income generation via increases in academic grants for research, and increase in recurring benefit of health sector development.There is an indicative recommendation in an externally commissioned report by Deloitte in February 2023 that further benefits may be accrued as follows: income benefit £47.6million, societal benefits £64.5 million.Efficiency savings were calculated collectively across all Trusts within the Business Case therefore this figure cannot be provided by HSC Trust at this point.  As the realisation of benefits progresses, certain elements may be more easily attributed to specific organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the timetable for the consultation on the Anti-Poverty Strategy; and (ii) what action his Department will be taking during the consultation process to tackle poverty.
answer_text: Following on from the Executive’s approval of the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy on 15 May, I have asked my officials to arrange the launch of the public consultation as soon as possible. Ahead of consultation, I will also engage with key stakeholders.During the consultation, my Department will continue to support disadvantaged people across a wide range of areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Northern Ireland Civil Service HR Pay and Reward Strategy to address issues of competitive salaries in the private sector.
answer_text: ANSWERIn accordance with the Civil Service People Strategy 2025-2030, a Pay and Reward Strategy is being developed to create a comprehensive total reward system for Civil Service employees. To address disparities with private sector salaries, the Civil Service has implemented a Recruitment and Retention Allowance policy. This policy permits key professions and areas experiencing recruitment and retention difficulties to propose higher pay rates through a business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the targets being set as part of the new regional breast cancer assessment to address disparities in waiting times for red flag breast cancer cases.
answer_text: The introduction of the single regional waiting list seeks to address inequity in waiting times across the region. The 14-day target for red flag breast cancer referrals remains unchanged.My Department is currently undertaking a wider review of breast services to address demand/capacity gaps across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 25016/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 25016/22-27 on Monday 7 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what further action his Department plans to take to reduce the cost of childcare where it is highest, such as in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area.
answer_text: On 15 May, I announced that eligibility for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will be extended to include school age children from 1 September 2025. This will provide support for a much wider group of working parents and represent an estimated 60% increase in the number of children eligible for the subsidy (24,000 compared to 15,000 at present). This significant expansion will help address the cumulative childcare costs faced by families with multiple children aged 0-11 across all Council areas.Further actions to support affordability will be included within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, which I plan to bring to the Executive in autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the May 2025 Labour Market Statistics that show the economic inactivity rate increased by 1.8 per cent to 27.2 per cent; and (ii) what steps her Department will take reduce this figure.
answer_text: The latest labour market report from NISRA shows that the economic inactivity rate in the North, for January-March 2025, was estimated at 27.2%.  This was an increase of 0.6pps over the quarter, and 1.8pps over the year – it is important to note however, that neither of these changes were statistically significant, meaning that they were within the expected sampling variability, and therefore not likely to reflect real change.  Economic inactivity is an important policy area, led by the Minister for Communities, and has proven a persistent and long-standing issue for the economy in the North. I am committed to working with the Minister for Communities and other Executive colleagues, including the Minister for Health, to help address barriers to employment. My Department plays a key role through the provision of skills programmes which aim to make skills and education more accessible, including for those experiencing additional barriers, such as ill health or disability. The Skills Fund provides a range of interventions which are available to those who are economically inactive - this includes the Step Up programme, the Women Returners Access Programme (WRAP) and the Breaking Barriers programme, all of which are aimed at aiding unemployed or economically inactive people to stay in, or enter, the workforce. Step Up offers the Disability Access Route to Education and Employment (DARE) which is open to all those aged 16 and over, who have a disability, and are unemployed or economically inactive. WRAP supports unemployed or economically inactive women aged 18 years plus, who wish to build confidence, refresh skills, and gain qualifications and work experience in areas of economic demand, while the Breaking Barriers programme provides training, mentoring and work experience to increase opportunities for individuals with additional obstacles to learning and employment. Another key area where my Department aims to improve access to the labour market is in relation to Good Jobs, and I recently announced to the Assembly my intention to introduce a comprehensive Employment Rights Bill, subject to Executive agreement to legislate, within the remaining mandate.  By providing for a right to up to 5 days of annual leave for those with caring responsibilities, and enhanced rights to make statutory flexible working requests from the first day of employment, the Bill will empower people suffering from or caring for those who suffer from ill health or disability, to enter or remain in the workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action he plans to take to mitigate the impact of the UK Autumn statement, as laid out in the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre 'Cost of Doing Business Impact Report'.
answer_text: ANSWERI have shared a copy of the Cost of Doing Business report with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, and impressed upon him when we met on 24th April, the need to take urgent action in the June Spending Review to address the impacts of the National Insurance Contributions’ changes.I have also shared a copy of the research with my Executive colleagues so they can consider the implications of this work for their departments and the areas they are responsible for.In addition, I held a roundtable meeting with business body representatives and NICVA on the 6th May and encouraged them to use the report to lobby the British Government to take account of the impact of its decisions on the individual sectors they represent.My department, through Land & Property Services, already offers a wide range of rate support schemes to support business ratepayers providing some quarter of a billion of rate support, with policy reviews into the Small Business Rate Relief and Non Domestic Vacant Rating now being taken forward as part of my department’s Strategic Rates Review. I will continue to make the case for better funding and will work together with my Executive colleagues to support our employers, workers, and communities.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the budget for; and (ii) the numbers participating in, the Step Up programme, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Step Up commenced in June 2023 following a £15m funding allocation through the British Government.  This funding package ended at 31 March 2025. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the timetable for implementing the recommendations of the Review of Neurology Services; and (ii) whether he is going to take any action ahead of the consultation process to start increasing the number of neurology beds, doctors and support staff as recommended in the report.
answer_text: The final report of the Regional Review of Neurology Services identifies four priorities for improvement, each of which is supported by a number of specific recommendations. Each recommendation is identified as a short, medium or long term action in the Action Plan set out in the final report.  The full implementation of the report’s recommendations will require significant additional funding, with over £65m required within the first five years of implementation. If this level of funding can be identified, it is estimated that the majority of the workforce growth recommended in the report and an interim increase in dedicated Neurology beds in the Neurosciences Centre could be secured within the first five years of implementation. Given the current financial environment it is likely that the pace of implementation will be constrained by the budgetary pressures faced by my Department.A public consultation on the final report is now underway. In the interim, I can assure you that my officials continue to work closely with Health and Social Care Trusts to optimise current resources and improve collaboration to enhance efficiency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) an update on discussions between him, his Department and the International Ministerial Advisory Panel; and (ii) when he will receive their first report.
answer_text: The education experts on the Ministerial Advisory Panel have been appointed to provide ongoing guidance, expertise and advice around the development and implementation of relevant education policies.To date my officials have engaged with the panel and received detailed written feedback on the TransformED strategy  and the development of interim three-year proposals on statutory Key Stage Assessment.  They have also provided detailed feedback to the Strategic Review of Curriculum.  I was also pleased to welcome members of the panel to speak the TransformED conference on 8 May to over 500 school leaders on a wide variety of education topics.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 26005/22-27, to detail why there are still no face to face events listed as being held in Northern Ireland as part of the UK Government's Pathways to Work Green Paper public consultations.
answer_text: The details of the Northern Ireland consultation event on the Pathways to Work Green Paper will be published as soon as the arrangements have been confirmed with the Department for Work and Pensions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the targets set for the reduction of the use of agency staff as a result of the introduction of the new frameworks announced for (i) nurses, midwives and healthcare support workers; and (ii) multi-disciplinary, agency staff.
answer_text: Significant progress has already been made since the implementation of the new agency frameworks with expenditure of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use falling from £134m in 22/23 to £19m in 24/25 across Nursing & Midwifery and Social Workers. For these two staff groups, the continued progress being made has yielded an overall reduction in agency expenditure in 2024/25 compared to the base year (2022/23), of £34m. No specific targets have been set in regard to the reduction of the use of agency staff, however, HSC Trusts will be looking at the best use of their available resources.Alongside this work our strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 17.7% (+10,063) increase in whole time equivalent (wte) staff in post across the HSC in Northern Ireland between March 2018 (56,802) and March 2025 (66,865).   This includes a 24.2% (+1,018 wte) increase in medical and dental staff, a 19.4% (+2,938 wte) increase in registered nursing and midwifery staff and a 24.9% (+2,026 wte) increase in professional and technical staff in post.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of NHS Resolution in England and Wales as a way of reducing the cost of healthcare legal action.
answer_text: NHS Resolution does not operate in Northern Ireland, and it would not be appropriate for me to assess its impact on reducing the cost of healthcare legal action.  However, I can confirm that there are well established processes in place in Northern Ireland to manage Clinical Negligence and other legal claims in a fair, timely and cost-effective manner.Mediation is one of a number of alternative dispute resolution mechanisms endorsed by the Directorate of Legal Services (DLS), who are responsible for the handling of Clinical Negligence claims on behalf of the Health Trusts in Northern Ireland, to help ensure the speedy and cost-effective handling of claims. The High Court requires legal practitioners to consider mediation as a form of resolution for Clinical Negligence claims and therefore mediation is built into the well-established claim management processes.In addition, costs penalties may apply for any party who unreasonably refuses to mediate.  In Northern Ireland the Law Society maintains a register of approved and accredited mediators from which the DLS can call off in order to engage mediation as part of the claims handling process.In the current financial environment, every effort is being made to bear down on costs to find savings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the implications that the welfare reform proposals in the British Government's Green Paper Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working will have on the Northern Ireland welfare mitigations package.
answer_text: Legislation was passed in the Assembly on 18 March 2025 to extend those welfare mitigation schemes that were due to end on 31 March 2025 to 31 March 2028.Work is ongoing to assess the implications of the UK Government’s Green Paper for people in Northern Ireland, however it is not expected there will be any impact on the Welfare Supplementary Payment schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 20735/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimated number of procedures that will be reimbursed with the £10 million allocated to the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs. An initial £10m will be invested in the Scheme for this financial year, allowing people to claim back the costs of overdue treatments arranged outside Northern Ireland. It is estimated that approximately 1,400 patients could benefit from this investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any positive outcomes of her meeting with BT on the 30 April 2025.
answer_text: Following the announcement made by BT on 30 April 2025 I, along with officials from Invest NI, immediately met with BT senior management to discuss the matter. I stressed that this announcement was completely counter to my drive for regional balance and offered the full support of my Department and Invest NI to retain the jobs in Derry. However, BT decided to proceed with their consultation.Officials from Invest NI and my Department will work with the company’s management over the coming weeks and months. For those staff members that do not wish to leave the business under the enhanced Voluntary Leavers Scheme now on offer by BT, my officials will engage with Department for Communities Jobs and Benefits, Derry City and Strabane District Council, Enterprise Northern Ireland, Local Enterprise Agency and other local businesses and stakeholders, to provide advice and identify opportunities for alternative employment, self-employment and/or re-training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the main changes; and (ii) any additional costs, contained in the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026 – 2029.
answer_text: The main changes include additional measures to reduce the phosphorus surplus within the agricultural sector; extending the mandatory use of Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment (LESSE) and standard values for dairy cow nutrient excretion based on annual milk yield. The Nutrients Action Programme consultation proposes 12 new additional measures or controls and 16 changes to existing measures, which are firmly based on science and evidence.The projected additional capital costs mainly arise from the mandatory use of LESSE and separation equipment to remove excess phosphorus at Anaerobic Digestor (AD) plants. These are estimated to be in the region of £33m for all 3 tiers of the phased rollout of LESSE up to 2030 and £7m for separation equipment at AD plants.Costs to the Department associated with inspections / compliance assessment, establishment of a Fertiliser and Feed Database and update and development of other NAP related IT systems are estimated at £3.6m per year.The actions that farmers may take in response to the Farm Phosphorus Balance measure and the revised Nitrogen excretion rates for dairy cows will be highly variable and will depend on individual farm circumstances. This makes the associated costs at overall level difficult to quantify.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to 25578/22-27, to detail what actions his Department is taking to encourage applications to the Northern Ireland Regional Food Programme from under represented local council areas.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Regional Food Programme (NIRFP) is open to any not-for-profit organisation wishing to raise the profile of NI quality regional food, and, by doing so, increasing its consumption in NI, and the rest of Ireland and the UK.  NIRFP is a highly competitive funding programme that is frequently oversubscribed.  The delivery of the Programme is based around a call for applications, through which applicants must demonstrate how their proposed projects will contribute towards the overall objectives of the NIRFP. My officials will directly contact a comprehensive list of relevant stakeholders to announce the opening of the application window.  The opening of the NIRFP is also promoted through local and regional press. All local councils receive notification of the opening of the NIRFP and it is their judgement as to whether they wish to apply for funding to support any relevant events they are planning to deliver during the year.  Options to review the NIRFP are also being taken forward as part of the NI Food Strategy Framework Action Plan. This will seek to ensure the NIRPF is aligned with the Framework’s ambitions going forward.  During this review, my officials have consulted widely with key stakeholders, including local council officials.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to regulate the supply of weight-loss injections.
answer_text: The regulatory regime for human medicines is set out in the Human Medicines Regulations 2012 ("the HMRs"). The HMRs provide a comprehensive scheme for regulating human medicines that covers, amongst other areas, their licensing, manufacture, import, labelling, distribution, advertising and sale and supply.All authorisations for medicinal products for human use and licences for manufacturers and wholesalers of such products across the UK are issued by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA).  MHRA will consider the safety and quality standards of medicines.My Department works to ensure continuing compliance with existing laws and guidance. Where evidence or information suggests unlawful practice is occurring or a breach of the HMRs is identified, my Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) will consider a range of enforcement options proportionate to the circumstances to achieve compliance, including for example, through the issuing of warning or advice, referral to professional bodies, or prosecution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any extra funding his department will allocate to reverse the decline in the condition of listed buildings.
answer_text: I have more than doubled the funding available to the Repair Stream of the Historic Environment Fund (HEF) this year from £240k to £500k. This is focused on support for roof and window repairs and is capped at relatively small amounts so that the maximum number of buildings can be assisted.My Department is also currently looking at a pilot project regarding potential Financial Transaction Capital loan funding for the purpose of regenerating buildings within Londonderry’s Historic City Conservation Area.  I hope to be in a position to provide more information on this in the coming weeks.  In the longer term, the recently published research on the condition of listed buildings and soon to be published research on the reasons for market failure in this area, will inform the development of decisions for support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Department of Finance has approved her business case to recover PSNI officer numbers to 7,000 over the next three years.
answer_text: The PSNI Workforce Recovery outline business case was recently approved by the Department of Finance solely in respect of value for money.  However, affordability remains a significant challenge because without additional resource, it will not be possible to implement the recovery plan.   I am firmly committed to addressing the issue of affordability and will raise it with both the Minister of Finance and the Executive, recognising that this is not just my responsibility but a critical collective challenge for the entire Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail any discussions between his Department and the Department of Health in the last 12 months in relation to minimum unit pricing for alcohol; and (ii) for his assessment of the introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to introduce minimum unit pricing for alcohol.

To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail any discussions between her Department and the Department of Health in the last 12 months in relation to minimum unit pricing for alcohol; and (ii) for her assessment of the introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: There have been no discussions between my Department and the Department of Health in relation to this issue.I have considered the proposals from the Minister for Health and am satisfied that, given the likely level of minimum pricing proposed, they should not have an undue adverse impact on either the hospitality or drinks manufacturing sectors here.  Nor would I expect them to have an adverse impact on consumers generally which would outweigh the public health benefits.  I am therefore supportive in principle of the proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail any discussions between his Department and the Department of Health in the last 12 months in relation to minimum unit pricing for alcohol; and (ii) for his assessment of the introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: The Health Minister has been clear in his view that Minimum Unit Pricing (MUP) for Alcohol could be a key mechanism to reduce alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area.  While the assessment of the introduction of MUP in Northern Ireland and any impacts as a result have been undertaken by the Department of Health which is the lead Department in this area, I and my officials continue to engage with the Health Minister and his team to provide what information and support we can to assist with the development of this policy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail any discussions between his Department and the Department of Health in the last 12 months in relation to minimum unit pricing for alcohol; and (ii) for his assessment of the introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: I have had no discussions between the Department of Education and the Department of Health within the last 12 months in relation to minimum unit pricing for alcohol.  Furthermore, the Department holds no position on introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 25822 /22-27, for a timetable for when the Anti-Poverty strategy will be discussed at the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on discussions with further education Trade Unions in relation to pay.

The Further Education college employers have communicated this pay offer to their lecturers and to Trade Union officials as part of on-going negotiations. I hope this pay offer will be accepted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the terms of reference of the Tariff Working Group; and (ii) whether the minutes of the Tariff Working Group will be made public.
answer_text: I established the Tariff Working Group to assess the implications of US tariffs and any countermeasures across the main US-NI tradable sectors. Details of the Working Group including its Terms of Reference are available on my department’s website. As part of its discussions, the Group considers information that is commercially sensitive and shared in confidence by industry representatives – it would not therefore be appropriate to publish minutes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools that (i) spent; and (ii) did not spend their full Outdoor Learning Project Capital allocation, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: It is not yet possible to detail the number of schools that have availed of their full allocation as orders are still being receipted and invoices forwarded for coding to the project budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail any support provided to jurors that are exposed to disturbing or graphic evidence; and (ii) for her assessment of whether that support is sufficient.
answer_text: (i) Jury service is undertaken by members of the community as an essential part of the justice process. I understand that some cases may cause upset and entail exposure to a wide range of evidence, some of which may be challenging. The availability of post-verdict counselling is explained to all potential jurors at the start of the juror selection process, alongside information on what to expect during the process of a trial. Where a Crown Court Judge is of the view that jurors are likely to have become distressed by evidence they hear or see in the course of a trial, the trial judge may, after the verdict, direct that counselling be made available to jurors. The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) has a contract with a service provider to provide members of a jury with up to six free counselling sessions.  This service, which can be delivered face to face, by telephone or video sessions, allows jurors the opportunity to talk through their experience of jury service with experienced counsellors.  Jurors who require additional counselling will be signposted to appropriate services, such as those provided by the NHS.  (ii).  NICTS first put in place a Jurors Counselling Service in 2001 and has continued to review service provision.  It was announced in 2024 that England & Wales were piloting a Jurors Counselling Service across 15 Crown Courts.  My Department will consider what, if any lessons there are for Northern Ireland when the evaluation of the England & Wales pilot is published.

answer_text: The information requested is not held by the Department.  Officials have requested input from CCMS and the Education Authority who have provided the following responses:Question TabledCCMS Response(i) the average time for cases to be resolved; Medical8 weeksPrecautionaryUp to 3 years(ii) the number of medical suspensions and precautionary suspensions that were later dismissed;Medical1Precautionary1(iii) the longest time between suspension and resolution.MedicalCurrently 3 yearsPrecautionaryQuestion TabledEA Response(i) the average time for cases to be resolved; Disciplinary cases for employees who have been placed on precautionary suspension - average time to be resolved as follows:- 2021-2022 – 2.7 years (excluding Labour Relations Agency (LRA) appeal stage 1.8 years)* 2022-2023 – 3.8 years (excluding LRA appeal stage 3.5 years)* 2023-2024 – 1.3 years *Disciplinary Procedures provide for potential dismissal provide for independent appeal to the Labour Relations Agency (LRA). Figures and timescales for the period 2021-present relate to a period of time following Covid. Employee Relations (EA) staff were redeployed during Covid to critical services and ER cases were paused. The Labour Relations Agency, courts and Tribunals were closed and with gradual restarting of services at the LRA it created a significant backlog with cases being prioritised.(ii) the number of those who were precautionary suspended that were later dismissed;7 Teachers/Principals were dismissed in total. 1 teacher left before final disciplinary outcome. 4 cases included in the figures are cases that remain ongoing. 2021-2022 – 2 dismissals (1 post primary and 1 primary) 2022-2023 – 1 dismissal (primary) 2023-2024 – 5 dismissals (3 post primary and 2 primary) (iii) the longest time between suspension and resolution.The longest period of time for a case between suspension and resolution was 10 years and 1 month. This unique case was due to exceptional circumstances relating to serious allegations involving external criminal investigations and subsequent criminal proceedings which coincided with Covid. This resulted in a number of court proceedings being postponed during this time. Please note;Figures do not include teachers from voluntary grammar schools.Figures are pursuant to AQW 25749/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will improve the availability and sustainability of NHS dentists.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timetable for assessment and the next steps following the consultation on changing the frequency of MOT testing from one year to two years.
answer_text: The Biennial MOT Testing Consultation closed on 16th April 2025 with over 1,300 responses received.Officials are currently analysing the high volume of feedback from the public, stakeholders and businesses. In due course a synopsis of feedback will be made public.  I will then consider the way forward for MOT testing here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for an update on Land and Property Services issuing an incorrect link on rates bills to 295,000 ratepayers; and (ii) to detail any action he is going to take to minimise the risk of this happening again.
answer_text: ANSWERThe incorrect URL resulted from an oversight before rate bills were sent to print. All rate bill templates have been reviewed to ensure the incorrect URL is no longer present.The Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) confirmed that the incident does not meet the data breach reporting obligations but recommended continued monitoring for any future impact. The National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) found no evidence of the site breaching its Registrar’s Domain Name Abuse terms and conditions.A learning review is underway to identify opportunities to reduce the risk of similar occurrences in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25017/22-27, whether he will review the current processes to assess whether this amount can be reduced.

To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the Economic and Social Research Institute April 2025 report entitled Economic Overview of Ireland and Northern Ireland, that states that early school leaving rates are three times higher in NI than in Ireland; and (ii) to detail any action his Department is taking to address this disparity.
answer_text: I would urge caution in selecting highlights from a research paper which is endeavouring to cover a range of different issues across three different areas – the United Kingdom, Ireland and Northern Ireland. As reflected in the paper, the contexts, challenges and funding are quite different and, as the report itself notes, comparisons will not always be on a like-for like basis. The figures referred to relate to those aged 18 to 24.I am happy to draw on effective practice wherever it exists.  As you know, I have launched the TransformED strategy and believe that this will make a fundamental difference to the education and learning experiences of our young people.  With Executive agreement, that work will include bringing forward in the current mandate, legislation to provide for all learners to participate in education, apprenticeship or training until age 18. Through this legislative change, I want to encourage and inspire all young people to aim high, to see remaining in education as a lifelong journey, to achieve and to succeed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Economic and Social Research Institute April 2025 report entitled Economic Overview of Ireland and Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the report and believe we all stand to benefit from developing the All-Ireland economy.  		The report provides high level comparisons on areas that are key focuses within my Department’s Business Plan, including productivity and increasing the standard of living through creating more ‘Good Jobs’. 		I also note the report’s recognition that in the north several crucial areas such as trade and migration are largely reserved matters. This can mean differences in how data is captured and can lead to limitations on the Executive’s ability to help grow our economy. 		My officials and I will continue to consider the report in-depth as we shape the policies, programmes and interventions that will deliver my economic vision for the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Economic and Social Research Institute April 2025 report entitled Economic Overview of Ireland and Northern Ireland, that states long-term waiting lists are higher in NI than the Republic; and (ii) to detail any action his Department is taking to address this disparity.
answer_text: It is clearly recognised that our waiting lists are entirely unacceptable and amongst the worst in Europe.  The plan to tackle our elective care waiting lists is detailed in the Elective Care Framework that was updated and published again in May last year.Much good work is already underway to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, for example, through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and Mega Clinics which have contributed to small but tangible improvements to the waiting list position.As outlined in my statement to the Assembly on 6 May 2025, I have detailed how the £215 ringfenced allocation for Health in this year’s budget will be invested across three areas for waiting list activities; to expand core capacity to reduce the red flag/time critical capacity gap (£85m), increase core routine capacity (£80m) and non-recurrent funding (£50m) to start tackling the backlog of patients waiting.The package of measures which will be taken forward to tackle waiting lists include targeting long waiting patients, expansion of elective services in Primary Care, investment in Mega Clinics and a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme.Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and it will not be resolved in a year or two. Investment at this level or higher will need to be sustained for up to five years to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Economic and Social Research Institute April 2025 report entitled Economic Overview of Ireland and Northern Ireland, that states life expectancy is 82.4 years in the Republic compared to 80.4 years in NI, with the gap increasing over recent years; and (ii) to detail any action his Department is taking to address this disparity.
answer_text: Given my focus on health inequalities, I welcome any evidence and information that helps support us in tackling this important issue.  However, it should be noted that official life expectancy estimates for Northern Ireland (NI) are produced by statisticians within the Department of Health and the UK Office for National Statistics (ONS) whereas estimates for Republic of Ireland (ROI) are typically published by Eurostat and there may therefore be differences in methodology and quality assurance practices.Improving life expectancy and healthy life expectancy, along with reducing the health inequalities in these areas, remain a particular challenge, which my Department actively seeks to address through all its activity.Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address health inequalities. It was developed based on the evidence that health and wellbeing, and health inequalities, are shaped by many factors including age, family, community, workplace, beliefs and traditions, economics, and physical and social environments. Many of these are outside the control of the Department of Health.MLB is underpinned by a range of public health strategies and policies which focus on specific issues, such as substance use, suicide prevention, tobacco use and obesity prevention. These policies also have a focus on health inequalities and are implemented on the ground by the Public Health Agency (PHA).Reducing inequalities in health is a significant challenge. Through MLB, and across a range of key issues, my Department and the PHA continue to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally. The work undertaken under MLB also aligns with the Integrated Care System for NI.I have also introduced a targeted, place-based approach called Live Better which has been designed to help address health inequalities by bringing targeted health support to communities which need it most and my officials are working collaboratively with officials for ROI on aspects of this approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of encompass, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Following Implementation in South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in November 2023 and in Belfast Health & Social Care Trust in June 2024, both Trusts have completed periods of stabilisation and are currently looking at optimising the system.  Activity data being produced in encompass represents their new business as usual Service Delivery Plan baseline and the system is well embedded with measurable benefits already being realised. These include improved outpatient clinic utilisation, saved clinical time as charts can be viewed instantly, more timely interventions, increased safety in the administration of medications and better utilisation of resources and appointments with a reduction in ‘Did Not Attend’ rates.Alongside these quantitative benefits, there are also an array of qualitative measures, where staff highlight the huge benefits of a fully integrated platform for delivering joined up health and social care, including safety, efficiencies and other upsides.Northern Trust was the most recent Trust to go live in November 2024 and continue in their stabilisation phase, with many services reporting a return to pre Go-Live activity. Post Live Assessments have been carried out with favourable outcomes being reported and action plans in place to resolve any remaining issues.The last two Trusts, Southern & Western are scheduled to go live jointly on 8th May 2025. Both Trusts have completed Go-Live Readiness Assessments (GLRA).  Through each implementation, the experience in the system and the processes involved in its implementation are strengthened and significant learning is carried forward into each subsequent Go-Live to support planning and to minimise impact on patients and clients.This has materialised in three hugely successful implementations to date and HSCNI is in a strong position for the joint Go-Live.  Plans are also underway for managing their stabilisation periods follow the Go-Live events.Work also continues across each of the live Trusts in relation to MyCare (Patient Portal) access with over 120,000 users live.  Following implementation in the last 2 Trusts, further engagement will be carried out to support an increase in MyCare use across Northern Ireland, supporting service users on their care journey.  This aspect of the programme will be under continual development for the life of the encompass programme (2033) and will ensure that patient engagement and communication is central to care delivered within the HSCNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on when the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment competition, launched in November 2024, will be completed and results compiled.
answer_text: ANSWERThe merit list is anticipated by 16th May 2025, candidate results will then issue the following week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26002/22-27, to detail any action her Department is taking to reduce the number of staff vacancies in DFI Rivers, based in Omagh.
answer_text: As of 17 April 2025, Rivers Directorate area office in Omagh had a headcount of 35 people with 15 vacancies recorded. Responsibility for all recruitment within the NICS rests with NICS HR. There continues to be significant gaps at Professional & Technical (P&T) Specialist grades as well as Industrial Grades. Rivers Directorate currently has 7 P&T and Industrial competitions registered with NICS HR which are at various stages of progression. My officials in Rivers Directorate have been working closely with my officials in People Development & Workforce Planning Directorate (PD&WPD) to prioritise recruitment competitions for the most business-critical grades. Despite recent improvements, my Department has difficulty recruiting enough staff to offset attrition rates combined with historical underfunding. Also, our efforts to recruit and retain professionals are hindered by external challenges, primarily pay rates. Competing with the private sector's competitive salaries is difficult due to the professional qualifications of many team members. NICS HR is developing a pay strategy to address this issue.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQO 1639/22-27, for a timescale for the setting up of an Artificial Intelligence unit in the Executive Office.
answer_text: We have established the AI and Digital Unit.  The work of this unit will be informed and guided by the work being taken forward by the delivering on the commitment made in the Programme for Government (PfG), the Chief Scientific and Technology Adviser (CSTA) established and chaired a Task-and-Finish (T&F) Group to help inform and shape the Office of AI and Digital. The T&F group comprised representatives from government departments, industry and academia. Contributions thus far have been invaluable in identifying key challenges and opportunities associated with adoption of AI across the public sector.  With stage 1 of the work complete we  formally launched the Office of AI and Digital.  This event took place on Wednesday 11 June at the AI Collaboration Centre in Belfast.  The Office of AI and Digital, alongside an AI Advisory Panel and the Government AI Network will develop:A draft an AI strategy and action plan to 2030.A detailed Business Plan for an AI Hub for delivery.A risk framework for AI projects. An AI for Citizens plan for engagementThe completion of stage 2 is scheduled for late Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25528/22-27, to detail the timeline for the review of guidance issued to adoption agencies.
answer_text: In November 2023, my Department issued ‘Access to Birth Records and Adoption Agency Case Records for Adults Impact by Adoption – Practice Guidance’.  My Department also provided training for all Adoption Agencies to support implementation of the Guidance, monitored implementation of the guidance for 12 months, and committed to reviewing the Guidance after one year.  It is anticipated that the review will further strengthen practice around the recording and sharing of information relating to adoptions.As part of the work to conclude the monitoring of the Practice Guidance, and to inform the review of it, a learning event for adoption agencies’ practitioners was held in November 2024.  This identified a number of issues for consideration by my Department within the review.  Other issues have also been identified through direct feedback from those impacted by adoption.  The review is currently in the early stages, and will require consideration of a number of complex issues. It is currently hoped that the review will be completed by autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the effectiveness of the JobStart 50+ pilot scheme.
answer_text: Funding for the JobStart 50+ pilot scheme allowed 180 participants to join the scheme, with 160 of these completing their opportunity. I am pleased to advise that 83 participants (52%) moved into sustained employment either with their JobStart employer or with an alternative employer following their participation on the scheme.  An independent final evaluation of the JobStart 50+ pilot scheme is expected by end of July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the reasons behind the 29.2 per cent reduction in sales to the Rest of World, as contained in the NI Food and Drinks Processing Report 2022.
answer_text: The £91.8 million (29.2%) reduction in sales to the Rest of World between 2021 and 2022 was offset by increased sales of £739.4 million to other markets.  Total sales to all markets increased 11.0% between 2021 and 2022. Total food and drink processing sector sales, and individual subsector sales, to different markets tend to fluctuate annually due to changing market conditions.  Sales to the Rest of the World in 2022 constituted 3.4% of total sales of the NI food and drink processing sector and is similar to the typical percentage of sales going to this market over the last 20 years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 25018/22-27, whether she plans to increase the opening of Strabane Courthouse to beyond two days a week to help reduce waiting times for court cases.
answer_text: Strabane Courthouse was designated as a Hearing Centre in 2009 which means that the building will only open on days when the courts sit. There are currently no plans to change the designated status of Strabane Courthouse or increase the opening times, based on current business demands and utilisation. The allocation of court business and listing of cases is a judicial matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the reasons behind the 255 fall in full-time equivalent staff employed in the beef and sheepmeat sub sector, as contained in the NI Food and Drinks Processing Report 2022.
answer_text: The beef and sheepmeat subsector is the largest of the NI Food and Drinks Processing subsectors, generating 26.3% of the sectors total sales, 19.6% of its total value added to the economy and 21.6% of its total employment.Changing market conditions cause all three of these key measures of size to fluctuate annually, both across the wider sector and within individual subsectors.The reduction in employment by 255 full time employee equivalents in the Beef and Sheepmeat subsector between 2021 and 2022 should be seen in the context that employment in this sector has increased over the last 20 years from 2,810 full time employee equivalents in 2002 to 5,507 in 2022.  Therefore, it is too early to say whether the fall in 2022 is a yearly fluctuation or the beginning of a trend.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department budget will allow him to expand the JobStart 50+ scheme following the pilot phase.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. I am in the process of agreeing my Department’s 2025-26 Budget allocations. In allocating my Budget, one of my priorities is employment support, to help people overcome barriers faced in moving into, retaining and progressing in employment.  My Budget allocation plans will be announced in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of vacancies in her Department for staff based in the West Tyrone constituency, broken down by (i) section; and (ii) town.
answer_text: The responsibility for managing vacancy information system lies with NICS HR, under the Department of Finance (DoF). Information has been sought from the DoF regarding this matter who have advised that vacancy data is recorded by town, rather than constituency. As of the end of March 2025, there are 25 vacancies in my Department for staff in towns based in the West Tyrone constituency. answer_text: The number of businesses that have filed for bankruptcy; been subject to a creditors petition for bankruptcy and the number that have entered into a voluntary arrangement with their creditors in the West Tyrone constituency in the last five years is detailed in the table below. 2020-212021-222022-232023-242024-2025 Sole TraderWinding-Up OrdersSole TraderWinding-Up OrdersSole TraderWinding-Up OrdersSole TraderWinding-Up OrdersSole TraderWinding-Up OrdersBusiness Own Petitions0000000000Creditors Petitions000112104157Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs)4071493546Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVA’s) are administered by private sector insolvency practitioners. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any events being held in Northern Ireland as part of the UK Government's Pathways to Work Green Paper public consultations.
answer_text: The Department for Work and Pensions is arranging a series of virtual and in person consultation events to support the UK Government’s consultation on its Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working Green Paper. I have asked my officials to engage with counterparts in the Department for Work and Pensions with a view to delivering specific Northern Ireland consultation events. The dates and timing for such Northern Ireland specific events have yet to be confirmed.

The breakdown by Department and town is shown in the table below.NICS Vacancies at Month End March 2025TownDAERADEDfCDfEDfIDoFDoHDOJPPSTEOGrand TotalAntrim30 25 1  2  58Armagh8 9 4  2  23Ballinamallard    10     10Ballykelly106 113       219Ballymena14 122 66 8  156Ballymoney1 11 1     13Ballynahinch  8       8Banbridge  7    2  9Bangor17712 13 3  97Belfast359 15361303354466323141683209Brussels     11Bushmills  3       3Carrickfergus  10    21  31Coleraine10 11 42  3  66Cookstown17 5 46     68Craigavon  2 2516 2  45Crumlin1         1Cullybackey3         3Derry-Londonderry2933414 428 1513 536Downpatrick39 10 3  1  53Dungannon15 13 1  7  36Enniskillen41 8 11     60Fivemiletown    2     2Greyabbey  4       4Irvinestown1 2       3Kilkeel  9       9Larne75 6 1     82Limavady  12 1     13Lisburn139 28 411 20  229Lurgan2 6       8Magherafelt2 1421     19Moira  7       7Newcastle  9       9Newry9 88 2  16 106Newtownabbey5 16 8     29Newtownards6 10 3  1  20Omagh7 4 2413 4  52Portadown  8 1     9Portballintrae  1       1Portrush1         1Richhill1         1Seaforde    1     1Strabane2 8 1     11Strangford    4     4Washington DC            11Location to be confirmed2       158 160Grand Total92611025511335805136348160695486Source:NICSHRWhen a department identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25017/22-27, to detail why medical negligence claims have increased by over a third in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department continues to be mindful of the need to manage public money carefully, as we navigate the challenging financial environment we are facing. Clinical negligence payments relate to an area which is largely driven by legal process and where financial impacts are hard to predictIn March 2022, during the period in question, DOJ introduced a new personal injury discount rate in Northern Ireland through the Damages (Return on Investment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.   This personal injury discount rate is used to calculate the multipliers in special damages awards in personal injury cases, meaning that payments which included a future pecuniary loss claim, particularly in circumstances where the plaintiff has sustained serious personal injuries or injuries at birth, had the value of their future loss increased.The new discount rate was set at -1.5%, changed from the previous rate of -1.75% which was in place from May 2021. Prior to that the rate was +2.5%.  This resulted in significant additional costs in relation to HSC Trust related clinical negligence cases.  Due to the impact this had, additional funding of £10.2m was provided to cover the impact of the change to the Personal Injury Discount Rate in 2022-23.In addition, there was an effect of ‘catch-up’ from the delay to the number of cases that were fully processed and settled during Covid, resulting in an increasing number of settled claims each year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20736/22-27, whether there are sufficient funds in his capital budget for 2025-26 to fund a new Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: The ability to take forward the redevelopment of the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital is dependent on the capital budget allocation that the Department of Health (DoH) receives in each year that investment is profiled for the project, as well as the scale of the inescapable and other capital pressures in the Health & Social Care sector for those years.  There is also a need to consider the ongoing resource cost implications of the project. The capital allocation in the final NI Executive Budget settlement for DoH in 2025/26 was £391 million with those for the medium term only expected to be confirmed as part of the next NI Executive Budget process.  Although there is significant uncertainty in respect of the outcome, the expectation is that the funding available will not be sufficient to meet the considerable demands on the DoH capital budget.   In response, the DoH capital investment plan is currently being reviewed to provide an assessment of the projects that might be taken forward in the absence of a significant increase in the level of funding available.  The redevelopment of the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital has been included as part of this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the (i) timeline; and (ii) budget to complete the Strule Shared Education Campus.
answer_text: On the current programme timetable full campus opening is planned for September 2028.The estimated total costs of the campus (including costs to date) are £375 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 25465/22-27, to detail any action his Department is taking to encourage applications to the Back in Business scheme, particularly in local council areas where applications are low.
answer_text: Land & Property Services have published information about Back In Business online and have promoted the scheme across a number of social media platforms to encourage ratepayers to apply for this support. In addition to this, Land & Property Services intend to analyse newly occupied properties that may be eligible for the scheme and contact the businesses to encourage take up.  My Department continues to engage with business owners who are already in receipt of support from the scheme to highlight the opportunity to others who may be eligible. I recently met with a business owner in Newry who positively endorsed the scheme by outlining how it has helped establish the business as well as giving opportunity for growth.Officials in my Department have been engaging with Council Officials the Department for Communities to facilitate communication of the Back in Business scheme alongside wider council and departmental schemes operating in a similar area.I would urge anyone planning to start a business or expanding their existing business to consider setting up in an empty unit and avail of the rate support on offer through Back in Business.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is planning to open further female-only temporary crisis accommodation in locations outside Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive, through the Supporting People Programme, has commissioned a new service called the West Area High Support Service. This service will provide 40 units of dispersed accommodation, with support, for homeless individuals with high and complex support needs in Londonderry. The service is mixed gender. However, as the accommodation units are dispersed via individual properties, this will enable female only accommodation within the scope of this provision.  Aimed at meeting the needs of individuals experiencing homelessness, this service will provide a level of support based on the assessment of need. This will include support tailored for women, enabling them to move into their own home in the community and live independently with appropriate support.  The service is currently being implemented in partnership with First Housing Aid and Support Services.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of staff in his Department that have been employed on a temporary contract for over three years.
answer_text: As of 1 January 2025, there were 86 members of staff on a temporary fixed term contract for over three years.  In addition, as of 27 March 2025 383 agency workers have been assigned to DfC for over three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Anti-Poverty Strategy, following its circulation to Executive colleagues on 31 March 2025.
answer_text: The paper I circulated on 31 March seeks agreement on the content of the draft Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and I have asked colleagues for their comments prior to discussion at the Executive. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement. The Strategy will be subject to public consultation prior to publication of the final draft.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of admissions for pre-schools and primary schools deemed to be of Socially Disadvantaged Circumstances in West Tyrone, broken down by school.
answer_text: A copy of the table containing the information requested has been placed in the Assembly Library.

answer_text: The average (median) gap between rent and the Local Housing Allowance for households in the private rented sector, broken down by constituency, is shown below. Note that due to retrospection of UCFS data UC figures are subject to change.	Median monthly gap between rent and the Local Housing Allowance for Housing Benefit claimants in the private rented sector with rents that exceeded the LHAConstituencyMedian Gap Belfast East70Belfast North80Belfast South and Mid Down146Belfast West225East Antrim63East Londonderry64Fermanagh and South Tyrone77Foyle83Lagan Valley129Mid Ulster71Newry and Armagh80North Antrim60North Down113South Antrim75South Down65Strangford78Upper Bann74West Tyrone55Northern Ireland76Data source: NIHE Housing Benefit Data - August 2024	In producing the geographical analysis, records were attributed to an LHA area and parliamentary constituency based on their postcode. Not all records can be correctly allocated using this method and some cannot be allocated at all. All figures are rounded to the nearest pound.Note that the rate of LHA used to calculate Housing Benefit/Universal Credit housing costs award depends on the area where the customer chooses to live, who lives with them and the number of bedrooms they need.  This may have an impact on the median monthly difference as some customers may be under-occupying their property and will therefore have a larger shortfall between their eligible rent used to calculate their Housing Benefit/Universal Credit housing costs award and the contractual rent charged by their landlord.The median figures above are an average across all household size requirements (number of bedrooms needed). The median difference will be affected by both the household size requirements and the Broad Rental Market Area (BRMA) rates within each parliamentary constituency. This should also be considered when drawing comparisons between UC and HB due to the differences in their caseload characteristics.
