question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) a breakdown of the funding for the Derry-Heathrow route between her Department and the Department for Transport; and (ii) any guarantees on ticket pricing which have been negotiated with LoganAir.
answer_text: The breakdown of the funding is as follows:Department for the Economy – £2,313,581.50Department for Transport – £2,313,581.50This will allow for the funding of the operation of a Public Service Obligation (PSO) air service between City of Derry Airport and London for 24 months (1 April 2025 to 31 March 2027).  My Department’s role, in partnership with the Department for Transport, is to fund and maintain an air link between Derry and London which is vital to the socio-economic prospects of the north-west region. Derry City & Strabane District Council, as owners of the airport, led the procurement process and manage contractual arrangements with Loganair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the consultation responses on antisocial behaviour in relation to the use of antisocial behaviour orders.
answer_text: The Anti-Social Behaviour Order (ASBO) is a key tool of last resort at the disposal of relevant authorities, it is the final stage of a graduated response to address anti-social behaviour (ASB) where all other methods have failed. Following a multi-agency review of ASB legislation, my department sought views, via public consultation, on potential amendments to the order to ensure it can be as relevant and effective as possible in addressing persistent ASB in 2025 and beyond.  The overall response from the public to the potential amendments was positive however the Department is conscious of the concerns raised by some stakeholders including those representing the interests of young people, tenants and vulnerable individuals. With a view to addressing those concerns, a working group was set up to consider the outworking of the consultation, which included a diversity of voices and interests, including from the community and voluntary sector alongside those of statutory partners. The considerations of the working group, together with further engagement with a variety of stakeholders, will feed into the final policy decision on the future of the ASBO. It is important to note that any legislative change will not occur until the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the schemes being funded through the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund will improve inclusion in West Tyrone.
answer_text: Six projects have been funded under the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund to develop and test innovative approaches to promoting inclusion.  The lessons learned through these projects will inform improvements to wider apprenticeship provision. The ‘Bridging Futures’ Inclusion Challenge Fund project led by North-West Regional College, which serves West Tyrone aims to increase apprenticeship access, participation and retention for students eligible for Free School Meals and those from socio-economically disadvantaged areas.  The project will operate to September 2026 and will address barriers such as skill gaps, financial constraints, low confidence levels and underachievement. Other region wide Challenge Fund projects including ‘#NotJustforBoys’ led by Women’s Tec and ‘Software for All’ led by WorkPlus may also promote inclusion across West Tyrone.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the consultation responses on antisocial behaviour in relation to drinking in public.
answer_text: The responses to the joint Department of Justice (‘DoJ’) and Department for Communities public consultation, on a review of anti-social behaviour legislation, was published by the DoJ on 4 April 2025.The responses suggest that the legislative framework to tackle drinking in public needs to be updated to ensure it is fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support available to cross-border small and medium-sized enterprises that are having trouble accessing business bank accounts in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Regulation of the banking sector is a Reserved Matter. I, in partnership with the Department of Finance, meet with the sector to advocate for business, emphasising the importance of supporting development and growth in the current economic climate. I am aware that the Department of Finance has raised the matter of cross-border SMEs struggling to access bank accounts in the North with UK Finance, the banking representative body, and is currently awaiting a formal response. Support and guidance are available from Invest NI and InterTradeIreland to support businesses in exploring alternative finance and banking options.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of outstanding cases of those people that have contacted her Department to seek to have their convictions quashed under the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Act 2024.
answer_text: There are currently no outstanding cases of people that have contacted my Department to seek to have their convictions quashed under the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Act 2024.  All individuals that have contacted my Department have received a response.I would encourage anyone who believes they have a relevant conviction and has not received a letter to date to make contact with the Department so their convictions can be reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22650/22-27, to detail the number of schools (i) that spent their full Outdoor Learning Project Capital allocation; and (ii) that did not spend their full Outdoor Learning Project Capital allocation, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: It is not yet possible to detail the number of schools that have availed of their full allocation as orders are still being receipted and invoices forwarded for coding to the project budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to review the guidance for getting an accessible parking bay outside a home.
answer_text: There is comprehensive guidance on the provision of an outside a home on NI Direct: Accessible parking bays | nidirect, and there are currently no plans to revise this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the second Access to Finance report published by Enterprise NI and the British Business Bank that suggests one in five Northern Ireland small and medium-sized enterprises experience barriers in accessing finance.
answer_text: The NI SME Access to Finance Report provides a comprehensive analysis of the SME finance landscape in the North of Ireland. It contains key insights that will help inform economic policy and facilitate change. The report highlights that one in five SMEs experience barriers to accessing finance, with respondents identifying ability to obtain/repay finance (41%) and a lack of awareness or availability of options/support (27%) as key issues. The ability to repay finance will be driven mainly by macro-economic conditions such as demand, supply, inflation and interest rates, the majority of which is uncontrollable. It is not surprising that this barrier has increased since the last survey, as businesses are facing multiple issues that are impacting on profit and sustainability, such as high inflation, high interest rates, National Insurance increases and general uncertainty. Businesses in the North that experienced barriers to finance were more likely to mention lack of awareness or availability of finance options and support (27%), than their counterparts in Scotland (17%) and in Wales (13%). This suggests that further efforts are required to raise awareness and access to funding sources among businesses. My Department continues to work with various stakeholders to deliver initiatives and programmes aimed at promoting external finance for business growth. Additionally, my Department, through Invest NI, has put in place a suite of debt and equity funds for ambitious start-ups, micro businesses and SMEs across the North whose growth is constrained by a lack of traditional finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 24905/22-27, to detail any measures being taken by her Department from the High Street Task Force recommendations in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department’s regional planning policy contained in the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) requires a town centre first approach for the location of new retail development and other main town centre uses.  Councils are required to take account of this in the preparation of their Local Development Plans.  Following the Department’s direction in December 2024, it is expected that Derry City and Strabane District Council will adopt their draft Plan Strategy with modifications in the coming weeks. This will provide the retail policy provision reflective of the regional position, for that district council including the constituency area of West Tyrone.I can advise that in October last year my Department responded to correspondence from the First Minister and deputy First Minister in relation to the proposed next steps on implementation of the report of the High Streets Task Force. A senior DfI official has been nominated to sit on any cross departmental group of the relevant departments, along with local councils, to explore the opportunities for progressing the recommendations contained in the Task Force Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 24905/22-27, to detail any measures being taken by his Department from the High Street Task Force recommendations in West Tyrone.
answer_text: Recommendation 5 of the High Street Task Force refers to prioritising the delivery of place-making by developing and implementing an investment programme for blue-green infrastructure, cleaner and greener public realm and sustainable transport.  My Department is taking forward an Outline Business Case for a cross-departmental regeneration programme, in partnership with the Department for Infrastructure and the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. The vision is to implement a strategic and integrated approach to tackling the challenges facing our village, town and city centres. The proposals will be consulted upon in 2025 and the overall Programme will require Executive approval.  It is envisaged that the proposed Programme would benefit all council areas. In addition to this, my Department is also investing significantly in high streets in West Tyrone, as can be seen by my Department’s commitment to the Strabane Town Centre Public Realm Scheme and the Canal Basin Regeneration (City Deal) Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the 2030 Energy Strategy targets of (i) a 25 per cent energy use saving for business and industry; and (ii) an 80 per cent use of renewable electricity sources, will being met.
answer_text: My department continue to facilitate work to ensure the 2030 targets of the Energy Strategy are achieved, including:An Energy and Resource Efficiency programme of support for businesses, launched in May 2024; the publication of a scheme design due in Q3 2025 for future low carbon heat and energy efficiency support in residential buildings. The Final Design for the Renewable Electricity Support Scheme, on the pathway to launching the first auction in 2026. We must build on our previous success in renewables development and the Executive needs to take a collaborative, whole system approach to facilitate delivery of the 80% by 2030 target. Whilst my Department has legislative responsibility for the renewable electricity consumption target, collaboration across departments and with wider stakeholders, including industry, will be essential to the successful delivery of this target.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when will a Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland be appointed.
answer_text: We are pleased to report that the competition to appoint a Commissioner for Public Appointments for NI (CPANI) launched on 10 March. The competition is continuing to progress as planned, with interviews scheduled to take place in forthcoming weeks. A Commissioner will be appointed as soon as possible, following the conclusion of the appointment process.Public appointments continue to be made in this intervening period and the CPANI Code of Practice continues to apply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 24905/22-27, to detail any measures being taken by her Department from the High Street Task Force recommendations in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the High Street Task Force Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Ongoing work within my Department to progress this recommendation includes the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter.  This work will have a positive impact across the North, including West Tyrone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 24905/22-27, to detail any measures being taken by his Department from the High Street Task Force recommendations in West Tyrone.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the Member to the answers to AQW 17411/22-27 and AQW 24999/22-27 which outline my Department’s response to the recommendations.  Regarding West Tyrone, the constituency has benefitted, and continues to benefit, from the measures taken by my Department as referred to within those answers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the guidelines issued to adoption agencies to obtain and keep a record of information about an adopted child's health; and (ii) what information should be passed on to the adopter in relation to hereditary diseases.
answer_text: All adoption agencies are required to operate within the relevant legislation and guidance, most notably the Adoption (Northern Ireland) Order 1987, the Adoption Agency Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1989 and the Adoption Regional Policy and Procedures (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/adoption-regional-policy-and-procedures) .When considering adoption for a child, an adoption agency is required by law to obtain and keep a record of information about the child’s health. As far as it is practicable, a birth family health history should also be obtained, including details of any serious physical or mental illness and any inherited and congenital disease.  Written information on the child’s health history and current state of health is shared with the adopters.  Medical information, including the birth family health history, is required to be placed in the child’s case record and preserved for at least 75 years.  Any medical information held in adoption records may be limited to information which was known at the time of adoption.  To assist those who have been adopted to access their adoption record, including any information it contains relating to the health records of birth parents, my Department issued ‘Access to Birth Records and Adoption Agency Case Records for Adults Impacted by Adoption to Health and Social Care Trusts and Adoption Agencies – Practice Guidance’ (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/access-birth-records-and-adoption-agency-records-adults-impacted-adoption-practice-guidance) on 29 November 2023, for immediate implementation.  This guidance is currently being reviewed, and it is anticipated that more emphasis will be given to encouraging birth families to provide as much information as possible at the time of the adoption.  This will be reflected in the health information provided to adopters.When a child is placed for adoption, he or she is issued with a new Health and Care number. Processes are in place to advise relevant health care settings of the child’s new name and health and care number, and to arrange for the transfer of relevant medical records. Through the ongoing development and implementation of a digital health and care record system, the Department is working to ensure that a continuous record of care both pre- and post-adoption is maintained, while at the same time ensuring that all necessary steps are taken to respect the privacy of the adopted person and their family.The Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 introduces a new legal framework for disclosure of information. When implemented, it will provide for a new and more consistent approach to access to information held in adoption agency records and by the Registrar General about adoptions which take place after the Act comes into operation. 	Work is ongoing to implement the provisions in the Act on a phased basis, and new regulations will be subject to consultation. In the interim, the current Adoption Agencies Regulations (North

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on discussions with trade unions in relation to pay and conditions for further education lecturers.
answer_text: Whilst recognising that Further Education colleges are employers in their own right, I recently met with trade union representatives to listen to their concerns in respect of lecturers’ workloads, outstanding pay agreements and impending action short of strike.  I have also met with representatives of the Further Education college employers’ forum. I understand that Further Education colleges’ engagement is ongoing with trade unions on lecturers’ pay and conditions and a workload committee has been re-established to specifically address workload issues. I have urged both parties to continue to work collaboratively at pace to achieve agreement regarding pay and conditions for Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of His Majesty’s Inspectorate of Constabulary and Fire and Rescue Services report, The Police Service of Northern Ireland: An inspection of police effectiveness and efficiency, which states that the PSNI must improve how it manages risks posed by violent and sexual offenders.
answer_text: While the inspection report identifies operational challenges for the PSNI and the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB) to take forward, it also underscores the impact of reduced police officer numbers on communities.  Furthermore, it highlights the critical need to address the decline in numbers and warns of future risks if the situation is not addressed.Increasing police numbers is a key priority that the Chief Constable and I share.  The Chief Constable and I are fully committed to recovering workforce levels to 7,000 officers and 2,572 staff within the next three years.The Workforce Recovery business case is presently under consideration by the Department of Finance.  However, affordability remains a significant concern.  I have continuously engaged with the Finance Minister and my Executive colleagues to advocate for a better financial settlement for my Department, to ensure the PSNI receives sufficient funding to stabilise and recover staffing numbers back to a sustainable level and will continue to do so.By keeping communities safe we enable them to have the confidence they need to live productively and engage fully in society.  That is clearly laid out in the new programme for government, and we will continue to advocate strongly for additional resources for the PSNI to enhance public safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to provide details on the pilot project by the Housing Executive and EnergyCloud NI to heat hot water for free; and (ii) to detail the timetable for the assessment of this pilot project.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) EnergyCloud NI is a collaboration across the energy generation, transmission, network distribution and supply sector. The Housing Executive engage with EnergyCloud NI as part of our role as the Homes Energy Conservation Authority for Northern Ireland. The purpose of EnergyCloud NI is to utilise wind energy which would otherwise be unused to provide free hot water to social housing families. To date, all parties are collaborating to develop a fully automated system to exploit the use of the EnergyCloud NI concept and provide free hot water in future years.  NIHE agreed in April 2021 to include this workstream within the pilot ‘Rural Leading Energy Transition (RULET)’ project in partnership with Ulster University under SPIRE2 Interreg Project to investigate low carbon heating solutions within a retrofit context. This small pilot of up to 20 dwellings in Omagh is being rolled out to assess the customer experience to the EnergyCloud NI opportunity of free hot water. (ii) The EnergyCloud NI team will evaluate the outputs over an 18-month period to determine the potential for future scalability.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Samaritan's March 2025 report Lost Futures: Poverty, Inequality, and Suicidality in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I fully agree with the findings within the report. The report adds further to feedback received during stakeholder engagements for the review of the Protect Life 2 Action Plan.Protect Life 2 has long recognised suicide as a major inequality and the disparity between suicide rates in the most deprived areas and the least deprived areas is stark. For this reason the Strategy focuses investment on those areas where rates are highest and the greatest difference can be made. The new Protect Life 2 Action Plan and Implementation Plan will underline the importance of a cross-departmental approach to bring down the rates of suicide and this cohesive approach was agreed at the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention on 3 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 24463/22-27, to detail any steps her Department is taking to encourage more schools to offer the Cycling Proficiency Scheme.
answer_text: All primary and special schools across the North are invited to participate in the Cycling Proficiency Scheme (CPS) each year. I have ensured all the necessary resources have been put in place to enable participating schools to deliver the scheme efficiently and effectively.The annual letter to launch the scheme was issued to schools and registered instructors and, to further promote the scheme this year, accompanied by a press release and accompanying promotional video on my department’s social media channels. I’m pleased to say that over 250 schools have indicated their intention to take part in CPS this year, with over 5000 pupils expected to participate. This would represent a significant increase on last year, when almost 3900 young people took part. Educational resources are being distributed to schools and training is well underway for all new instructors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of visits to Movanagher Fish Farm by (i) schools; and (ii) members of the public in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Over the past five years, Movanagher Fish Farm has hosted no school visits and only one visit by members of the public in 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update in relation to invasive zebra mussels in (i) Lough Neagh; and (ii) Movanagher Fish Farm.
answer_text: Zebra mussels have spread widely through Lough Neagh since 2003 as there are currently no effective controls that will not cause serious harm to the wider aquatic ecosystem. It is anticipated that their numbers will stabilise as the Lough ecosystem rebalances. Results from recent sampling at Movanagher Fish Farm confirmed no live Zebra Mussel veligers present. The low water temperatures, typically below that for reproduction of Zebra Mussels, has allowed for a limited stocking of some Public Angling Estate waters. However, it is difficult to ascertain if all pipework is clear of Zebra Mussels on the actual fish farm, but is unlikely, as the Lower Bann that feeds the fish farm does have Zebra Mussels present.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: AQW 23451 22-27 was answered on 2 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on discussions with teachers' unions in relation to the 2024-25 pay award
answer_text: I welcomed a decision by the Northern Ireland Teachers' Council (NITC) on 4 April 2025 to accept a 5.5% pay offer for teachers.The pay settlement was agreed by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) and will bring to an end the ongoing industrial action.Intensive work has taken place to find the funding from within the 2024/25 education budget to enable us to offer a 5.5% pay increase. I am pleased that the five trade unions which make up the NITC have now formally accepted this on behalf of their members.The pay settlement will see the starting salary for graduate teachers increasing to £31,650. This is an increase of £1,650 over the year and it follows on from the teachers’ pay settlement in April 2024 which saw the starting salary for teachers raised by almost 25%. New teachers are now being paid £7,513 more than the salary paid prior to the Executive returning last year.The pay award reflects the value that I place on those working in education. It firmly demonstrates my support for teachers and recognises the vital contribution they make to children’s life chances.It is anticipated that the pay increase will be applied to most teachers in May and a full-time teacher on the starting salary will receive £1,237.50 in back pay (before statutory deductions) for the period from 1 September 2024 to 31 May 2025.A full-time teacher on Upper Pay Scale Point 3 will receive £1,913.25 in back pay (before statutory deductions) for the period from 1 September 2024 to 31 May 2025.The agreement comes after a series of recent meetings between myself and the Northern Ireland Teachers' Council. Management Side will now move to implement the offer with the award backdated to 1 September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to encourage local councils, not meeting the 15-week local planning application statutory target, to improve.
answer_text: Local government are responsible for the determination of over 99% of all local and major planning applications and my Department has been working closely with them and other key stakeholders through the Planning Improvement Programme to improve performance in the system.The programme has already delivered positive changes including legislation to enable council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists to improve the quality of planning applications entering the system, and in turn reduce the potential for delay through the application process. My Department is also conducting a review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 to enable the planning application process to be further streamlined. Ongoing work with statutory consultees has already resulted in performance improvements and this engagement will continue.My officials are also currently engaging with individual council planning authorities and other key stakeholders in the planning system with a specific focus on identifying challenges in current development management (planning application process) practice and procedures as well as individual performance related matters, to inform further opportunities for efficiencies and improvements to the system.  I want to see a good performing planning system in the North and am committed to working with all stakeholders to bring that about.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the total cost of himself and officials attending the International Summit of the Teaching Profession in Iceland.
answer_text: The total cost of attending the International Summit of the Teaching Profession in Iceland was £8,098.12.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the cost implications of raising allowances for councillors by 5 per cent.
answer_text: From 1 April 2025, the additional cost to councils of the 5% increase in the basic allowance (BA) from £17,456 to £18,329 (£873), if the maximum was applied to all councillors, would be £403,326.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to encourage local councils, not meeting the 70 per cent in 39-weeks planning enforcement statutory target, to improve.
answer_text: The North’s local planning authorities are autonomous bodies and performance against the statutory target is a matter for individual councils. However, I recognise that effective enforcement is the backbone of a good planning system to ensure that the credibility and integrity of the process is not undermined by inappropriate development.  In line with this my officials will be engaging with local government during the next phase of the Planning Improvement Programme to take a focussed look at enforcement resourcing and prioritisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review the school bus road safety laws.
answer_text: I am committed to considering any initiatives that can improve road safety, which is a priority for me as Minister.  With regard to school buses, I have asked my officials to look at all the options that may be available to improve safety for school children and to report back to me as a matter of urgency.I have also asked my officials to reinforce the messaging to help keep all road users safe, especially the vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the voluntary and community organisations that will receive the £1500 support payment, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer this question, I have arranged for the information to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of slaughterhouses in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the number of slaughterhouses that do not have CCTV, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Currently, there are 20 slaughterhouses licensed by my Department in Northern Ireland. The number of slaughterhouses without CCTV, broken down by constituencies is as follows:ConstituencySlaughterhouses without CCTVNorth Antrim1Upper Bann1South Antrim1The operations within every licensed slaughterhouse are supervised by an Official DAERA Veterinarian who, amongst other duties, ensures that animal welfare standards at the time of slaughter meet legal requirements. I want to emphasise that the current proposal to mandate the installation of CCTV in all slaughterhouses is about achieving parity with the rest of the United Kingdom, rather than being driven by any specific concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 15932/22-27, whether all targets set within the Child Criminal Exploitation Action Plan for the first six months have been met.
answer_text: The cross departmental multi-agency Children and Young People’s Strategy Child Protection Group (previously known as the Child Protection Senior Officials sub-group) is responsible for action 1.1 which is the only target set within the first six months. While this action remains ongoing, there has been significant progress: a Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE) definition and accompanying narrative has been agreed and included feedback from children and young people; and the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland Child Exploitation Committee is finalising specific CCE guidance and service pathways. Co-operating to Safeguard Children and Young People in Northern Ireland (CTSCYP), which provides the overarching policy framework for safeguarding children and young people, will be updated by the Department of Health upon completion of CCE guidance and final agreement on service pathways by the Child Exploitation Committee. Following this, other Departments and Agencies will update their own policies and guidance.  Timings will depend on development of guidance materials and service pathways, but CTSCYP should be updated by September 2025 at the latest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 13234/22-27, for an update on her Department's Share the Road to Zero campaign.
answer_text: My department continues to work in partnership with business, community and sporting organisations to promote the Share the Road to Zero campaign. Since June 18, 2024, this has included Drivetech and the North West Road Safety Partnership. Belfast Giants and Ulster Rugby have also joined the list of organisations which have taken part in social media videos as part of their pledge to Share the Road to Zero. Most recently, the IFA has come on board, with Antrim-born forward Emily Wilson backing the campaign on behalf of the senior women's football team and Liverpool star Conor Bradley, who pledged his in September 2024.I am looking forward to further high-profile partnerships in the near future that will be highlighted through my department’s continued social media campaign.Share the Road to Zero currently has 31,462 subscribers to its weekly road safety advice newsletter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on whether his Department has any plans to introduce a statutory fuel poverty target.
answer_text: Improved accountability is an important aspect of the draft Fuel Poverty Strategy for Northern Ireland.  The public consultation closed on 6th March and in this I proposed introducing a basket of indicators to improve the understanding of fuel poverty and help measure progress towards outcomes, with an annual report outlining progress.  I also proposed establishing a Fuel Poverty Advisory Group with partners from statutory agencies and the Voluntary and Community Sector to guide, advise and challenge actions and progress over the lifetime of the strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Autism Strategy 2023-2028 Delivery Plan 2023-2025.
answer_text: The Autism Strategy 2023-2028 was published, along with a two-year 2023-2025 Delivery Plan on 13 December 2023 and work on implementation is underway across government departments and public bodies. My Department recently published the year one progress report on the first year of the 2023 to 2025 delivery plan.  The report can be accessed at:  Autism Monitoring Report 23 24 - January 2025 final versionWork is due to commence shortly on the year two progress report and when completed will be made available on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on discussions to increase the admission and enrolment numbers at Dean Maguirc College, Carrickmore.
answer_text: The Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS), as Planning and Managing Authority for Catholic Maintained Schools, has indicated that it will seek to increase the admissions and enrolment numbers at Dean Maguirc College, via the Development Proposal (DP) process under Article 14 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986. As a statutory process, CCMS will work with the school in this regard. The DP process can be separated into four main phases:1. identification of need in the Area Planning process;2. pre-publication consultation;3. publication of a DP; and4. statutory objection period leading to a decision by the Minister.I should point out that the Department’s role in the DP process commences when a proposal is published.On 16 January 2025, the Education Authority (EA) published its second Operational Plan (OP2) 2024-2026.  Dean Maguirc College features in the Operational Plan under a Short-Term Work Steam i.e. “The Council for Catholic Maintained Schools is reviewing the approved admission and enrolment numbers for Dean Maguirc College. The Council for Catholic Maintained Schools will engage with the Board of Governors regarding provision for the area.”While this work is ongoing, the school remains able to apply for Temporary Variations to enable it to increase its intake whenever there is oversubscription in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Derry and Strabane City Deal.
answer_text: The Derry City and Strabane District Council City Deal was signed on 18 September 2024. Council is finalising the required Deal documentation and working on legally binding Contracts for Funding for the most advanced projects, which will allow funding to flow. Integrated Consultancy Teams are being procured for some projects, and the Derry North Atlantic Museum, the furthest advanced project, is at tender award stage. Work around subsidy control, social value, inclusivity and benefits realisation is also ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the greenways and active travel projects funded by her Department in West Tyrone in the last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not monitor greenway and active travel projects on a constituency basis. The West Tyrone constituency falls partly within 3 council areas; Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, Derry City and Strabane District Council and Mid Ulster District Council.The following link provides a breakdown of the direct departmental spend recorded against active travel, including greenways, from 2021/22 to 2023/24:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/DFI%202024-0175%20-%20Attachment.PDFThere has been no change to the provisional outturn figure listed for 2023/24. The figures for 2024/25 have not yet been finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15542/22-27, whether he has received the necessary approvals to allow him to support a Private Members Bill on banning hunting.
answer_text: The consultation on John Blair’s Members Bill about banning hunting, ended in January 2025. When the Bill is formally introduced into the Assembly, I will seek Executive’s colleagues’ agreement to support the Bill prior to its Second Stage.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for an update on the future of Strabane Courthouse; and (ii) to detail any capital improvements to Strabane Courthouse in the last three years.
answer_text: Strabane Courthouse is designated as a Hearing Centre and is opened for two days per week, primarily for the delivery of Magistrates’ Court business. The publication of its Estate Strategy in December 2023 was an important milestone for NICTS, as it provides a long-term framework for investment over the next decade and beyond. To implement the Strategy, NICTS is using data and evidence to baseline the existing estate against the agreed Strategic Outcomes and to develop prioritised investment delivery plans in the context of the finite resources available. The future use of Strabane Courthouse will form part of this process.In the last three years, capital improvements to the building have involved upgrades to the environmental controls and Wifi throughout the building, and the installation of automatic meter reading systems and video conferencing technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the main differences between (i) UK Pesticides National Action Plan 2025;  and (ii) the 2013 plan it replaces.
answer_text: he 2013 UK Pesticides National Action Plan reflected the policy approach taken by the four Governments in response to the first consultation on implementation of the EC Directive on the Sustainable Use of Pesticides and the measures in the subsequent Plant Protection Products (Sustainable Use) Regulations 2012.The 2025 Plan goes further than its predecessor, reflecting the current priorities and revised ambitions of all four UK governments, to reducing pesticide use through promoting and supporting behavioural change.The main difference between the two plans, is the introduction of the first domestic UK target to reduce the potential harm from pesticides by 10% by 2030 - measured across 20 indicators.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to review or amend the regulations and fees in relation to Houses in Multiple Occupation.
answer_text: The Houses in Multiple Occupation fees are kept under regular review in order to keep the licensing scheme cost neutral for councils. My Department is currently reviewing the license fee along with the information provided on the running costs of the scheme and will seek to amend the Houses in Multiple Occupation (Fees) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2019 if this is appropriate. There are no plans to review or amend any other regulations pertaining to Houses in Multiple Occupation at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on when the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer November 2024 recruitment competition will be completed and results compiled.
answer_text: We are currently at the final stages of compiling the merit list and are planning to contact candidates with results during April 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any measures contained in the High Street Task Force recommendations that have been considered for high streets in the West Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: A number of recommendations are being taken forward by Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance and Infrastructure. Further detail can be sought from those departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to narrow the gender gap in science, technology, engineering and mathematics jobs.
answer_text: My department is focused on ensuring that everyone in our society has access to skills pathways to access Good Jobs.  This includes supporting females into STEM -based subjects and employment. Following recommendations from the initial Women in STEM Matrix Report, my department established a Steering Group to develop an Action Plan which was published in 2022.  Work continues to deliver on this Action Plan but more is needed.  As of June 2024, data suggested that females made up 22.3% of Science, Research, Engineering and Technology Professionals, with female enrolments on narrow STEM courses at Further and Higher Education institutions also showing lower than acceptable levels.  There are a range of interventions in place to help address this including development of a careers bulletin to showcase STEM study and career pathways for younger girls and support provided for cyber and engineering programmes.   We also have the “Your Face Fits” campaign which was developed by the Advanced Manufacturing and Engineering Sectoral Partnership and our FE colleges are actively promoting females into narrow STEM programmes through the ‘Get Engineering Fair’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Action for Children’s new report, Paying the Price of Child Poverty, particularly in relation to the finding that removing the two-child limit and benefit cap would be the single most effective action to reduce child poverty.
answer_text: My officials are reviewing the conclusions of Action for Children’s report, including those around the two-child limit and benefit cap. 		The Benefit Cap is already fully mitigated in Northern Ireland. At its meeting on 5 December, the Executive agreed to extend the Benefit Cap mitigation scheme to March 2028.  On 18 March 2025 the legislation to provide for this extension was passed in the Assembly.		Work is also ongoing work to bring forward an effective, sustainable and deliverable Anti-Poverty Strategy.	I will continue to consider all possible actions available to tackle poverty and more specifically child poverty. However, these will be dependent on available budget and Executive agreement on providing additional funding.	The final content of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive approval and public consultation prior to its final publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action she is taking to boost retail output, which remains 1.1 per cent below its pre-pandemic level.
answer_text: I acknowledge that retail output has not yet fully recovered to pre-pandemic levels although the latest data reflects progress towards this recovery. Encouragingly, since reaching a low point in Q3 2022, local retail output has grown by around 9%, having increased in 6 out of the 10 most recent quarters for which there is available data. However, I recognise that the local retail sector continues to face significant challenges and will have serious concerns surrounding the tax and spending changes implemented in early April as announced in the British Government’s Autumn Statement.  I am committed to engaging with local retailers to understand the issues they are facing. On 13 January 2025, my predecessor met with representatives of 23 local business groups including Retail NI, Hospitality Ulster, Food NI along with several local chambers of commerce. I will again be meeting with Retail NI in the coming weeks.My Department and Invest NI provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets. Invest NI also has a portfolio of grants, loans and investment funds, many of which are specifically targeted at early stage and small businesses. In addition, my Department’s Regional Economic Plan is focussed on identifying key economic opportunities and priority actions for each part of the North, supported by a £45m Regional Development Fund.When Finance Minister, I reinstated the Back in Business scheme to help to incentivise the use of long-term vacant retail space by helping new ventures across our towns and cities in the vital first years in business – this is already bearing fruit by way of new businesses appearing on our high streets. It is important to note that the small business rate relief scheme for 2024/25 was extended to provide support to some 30,000 small businesses throughout our towns and cities. Towards the end of my time as Finance Minister, I commissioned a Cost of Doing Business research project which is currently being finalised by the Department of Finance. The study will cover a broad range of factors including the impacts of the recent Budget announcements around additional employer National Insurance Contributions and increases to the national minimum and living wages, as well as other contributing factors such as insurance, property and energy costs and the disparities in tax regimes, such as VAT, between the North and the South.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to encourage schools to harness the power of Artificial Intelligence to support learning.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Education Information Solutions (EdIS) Programme/C2K team are currently running two separate “Proof of Concept” research and trialling projects in relation to teacher use of the generative AI products: Microsoft Copilot for Microsoft 365 and Google Gemini.There are approximately 200 teachers from 150 schools involved in these two projects and participating schools include nursery, primary, post-primary and special schools, as well as EA managed Education otherwise than at school (EOTAS) provisions.  Ulster University has been engaged to undertake an independent evaluation of the “Proof of Concept” work and the report will publish on 7 April 2025.  That report will focus on the impact of Microsoft Copilot for M365 and how it has impacted on schools.A business case supporting the increased rollout of Generative AI to schools, through EdIS/C2K, will be submitted to my Department in May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action her Department is taking to reduce the economic inactive rate, which has increased by 36.5 per cent since the start of the pandemic and increased by 3 per cent since February 2025.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 21887/22-27 which I answered on 13 February 2025.

answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has completed its initial review of driver licensing fees.  Further consideration is being given to a number of statutory provisions and policy decisions currently in place, which is anticipated will take a few months to complete.The DVA will consult as required on any proposed changes to the existing fees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his Department’s work developing policy proposals for the regulation of property management companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on legislation for the replacement body for the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 19665/22-27, I have now considered the options paper along with the associated business case and have advised officials of my preferred option.Officials are currently taking further advice from relevant stakeholders before confirming a final list of policy changes to be delivered in the Bill and I hope to be in a position to write to the Executive in the near future seeking approval to legislate on this basis.The Department still remains committed to passing the required legislative changes within the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's policy to have a defibrillator on every school site.
answer_text: A procurement exercise to supply the defibrillator units required for schools has commenced with the publication of a tender in February 2025.  The contract for the supply of the units is expected to be awarded in April/May 2025.  A programme for installation of the units will commence thereafter. To date 891 schools have responded to a survey issued by the Department to ascertain which schools currently have a defibrillator.  This has determined that 420 schools currently have a defibrillator and 471 schools do not.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 22839/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 22839 22-27 was answered on 1 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 21573/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the setting of a minimum unit price for alcohol for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have set out that I believe that Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol could be a key mechanism to reduce alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area. Clearly this is an issue that requires an Executive-wide approach, and I have requested the definitive positions of all Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 22936/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 22936 22-27 was answered on 25 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 23614/22-27, to detail (i) the reasons for a significant rise in the number of children in foster care; and (ii) any action his Department is taking to reduce these numbers.
answer_text: Re - the reasons for a significant rise in the number of children in foster care:There has been a very significant increase in the looked after population in Northern Ireland.  This is related to children spending longer periods in care as well as new admissions.  The increase in the looked after children population is related to many different components relating to the identification and assessment of significant harm.  Societal norms have evolved to include increase in deprivation, exposure to social media, increase substance use to name but a few alongside an increased professional understanding and knowledge of the impact of trauma.  The number of children being discharged from care is relatively stable but the number becoming looked after has included a particular increase in the last year (2023/24). The arrival of separated and unaccompanied asylum children into the looked after system has inevitably added to an already struggling system.  At the end of March 2024 128 of the 3,999 looked after children were separated unaccompanied asylum-seeking children.Further data and information is available on DOH website:Children's social care statistics for Northern Ireland 2023/24 | Department of HealthRe: any action his Department is taking to reduce these numbers:The Department of Health are currently implementing the Health and Wellbeing 2026, Children and Young People Strategy 2020-2030 and A Life Deserved – Caring for Children and Young People in Northern Ireland 2021.  Both these major strategies continue to inform the Department of Health’s planning priorities to improve services for children and families in Northern Ireland.Following the Independent Review of Social Care Services in Northern Ireland commissioned by the Department of Health, Professor Ray Jones’ Report made several recommendations which are being taken forward by The Children’s Strategic Reform Board under the leadership of Department of Health.  This Reform Programme has been set up to address key issues for children services by working creatively and collaboratively with HSC Trusts and other stakeholders to assess the issues, underlying causes, and develop robust and creative strategies for action.  There are nine workstreams with one dedicated to focus on the early help models available at the front door in order to prevent further admissions into care alongside other initiatives designed to improve outcomes for those children and young people already in care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on discussions with NIE Networks in relation to compensation for those affected by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Following disruption and damage caused by Storm Éowyn on 24th of January, a working group involving the Department for the Economy, NIE Networks, and the Utility Regulator, was established to consider compensation payments for electricity customers most affected by power outages. The working group was established at the request of the Executive to consider options where compensation payments would be recovered through customer electricity bills. The working group has now updated the Executive of its findings that there is currently no existing route to make payments to customers. NIE Networks has applied a severe weather exemption provided for in legislation under which customers can apply for compensation if they have been off electricity supply. The working group concluded that any other options involving recovery of payments from network charges would require legal or regulatory modifications which cannot be applied retrospectively for those customers impacted by Storm Éowyn.

answer_text: AQW 23541/22-27 was answered on 28 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of cases impacted by the current Criminal Bar Association industrial action.
answer_text: Due to the fluidity of proceedings on any given day it is difficult to assess the full impact since outset of the CBA action in November; however, based on management information our assessment is 164 trials, 328 plea and sentencing hearings, 539 victims and 2811 witnesses were affected by the withdrawal between 6 January and 28 February. While significant, those numbers do not tell the real story which is the impact on the individuals involved. We should be clear that  every case and every victim and witness affected is one too many and many of these who have been impacted are extremely vulnerable. The delays created by the withdrawal mean there is a real tangible risk of harm and of those individuals not getting the justice they deserve. I welcome the reduction in the scope of the CBA action and hope that, through ongoing engagement through the working group I have established, they will will return to full service and continue to work with me and my officials to address any remaining concerns they may have.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Ulster Bank survey that shows staffing levels in Northern Ireland's private sector fell in February 2025 for the first time in four years.
answer_text: The latest employment indicators from the Ulster Bank PMI survey are disappointing, with respondents reporting lower staffing levels in February. This was linked to a softening in demand, as well as firms’ attempts to limit their overheads before the impending British Government increase in Employers’ National Insurance Contributions comes into effect. The local labour market remains relatively robust however, with most key indicators having improved over the past year and some remaining at, or near, record performance levels – this is exemplified by the North’s unemployment rate, estimated to have reached a record low of 1.6% in the final quarter of 2024. I and my officials continue to keep the situation under close review and engage with local business and employee representatives on the relevant issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the £750,000 in funding to support the Ulster American Folk Park The Journey Ahead project will be spent.
answer_text: The funding will support the development of National Museums NI’s outline business case and procure an integrated consultant team (ICT) to assess options for the Ulster American Folk Park’s Journey Ahead project. The ICT will provide essential construction-related professional services, led by an architect or project-management consultancy, to guide the project’s development. Their specialised expertise is crucial for the successful planning and delivery of this transformation project which will see investment of £20 million through the Mid South West Regional Growth Deal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Department of Finance has approved her business case to recover PSNI officer numbers to 7,000 over the next three years.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on how he is addressing increasing challenges arising from avian influenza outbreaks.
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement to the Assembly on 24 February 2025 and my Oral Statement to the Assembly on 11 March 2025 provide extensive detail on how I and my officials have been addressing the challenges arising from the Avian Influenza outbreaks.  The Statements include information on the actions taken such as the implementation of disease control zones, communications plans, engagement with colleagues in other jurisdictions, colloboration with stakeholders and the next steps to be taken.Feedback from industry stakeholders on my Department’s response to the outbreak has been very positive. My officials, including the Chief Veterinary Officier (CVO) and his team, will remain in close contact with colleagues across the other jurisdictions and are regularly keeping industry stakeholders updated on our response to these incursions. We will continue to reinforce the importance of adhering to high standards of biosecurity on all poultry premises and the need for vigilance to protect our poultry flock from any further incursions of this disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 23347/22-27, to detail the timetable for the review of postgraduate tuition fee loans levels.
answer_text: The timetable has not been finalised but it is intended to inform the Academic Year 27/28.  I expect to receive further advice from officials on timing in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with the US President in relation to the effects of tariffs on the economy.
answer_text: Whilst there have been no discussions directly with the US President in relation to tariffs, the deputy First Minister raised the potential impact of any tariffs at several meetings during her visit to Washington DC, including the UK Ambassador to the US and senior State Department officials.The FM and dFM discussed the matter with the US Ambassador to the UK, Warren Stephens.Any moves that create barriers to trade with this significant market are unwelcome. Businesses need certainty in their trading arrangements.We continue to engage with all stakeholders to ensure the trade interests of NI’s economy are addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the subsidy provided as part of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to AQW 23948/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 14 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions she has had with the Home Office, since the new UK Government was elected, in relation to tackling illegal drugs.
answer_text: As Minister of Justice, my role focusses on the criminal justice response including actions aimed at reducing the level of drug related harm and offending in Northern Ireland.The policy and legal frameworks relating to the misuse of drugs, including the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 and the Psychoactive Substances Act 2016, is a reserved matter and therefore the responsibility of the UK Government. As such, it is important that the Health Minister and I are in regular contact with our Home Office counterparts.Whilst the new UK Government has only been in place for a short time, the Health Minister and I recently attended a meeting with Ministers from all other regions of the UK in respect of drug related issues.  During that meeting there was a discussion on our key priorities in relation to tackling illegal drugs. Subsequently, the Minister of Health and I wrote jointly to the Minister for Policing, Fire and Crime Prevention in the Home Office to convey our concerns at the harms that illegal drugs in general can cause. Given that the harms caused by substance use extend widely to families and communities, it was encouraging to hear the joint ambition from all four nations to tackle these issues. These shared priorities are something that we are likely to discuss again at future UK Ministerial meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the work her Department is undertaking to tackle homelessness.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) have responsibility for assessing and meeting housing needs, and working with communities to develop programmes to meet those needs.  However, accommodation challenges for those leaving the criminal justice system requires cross-executive collaboration and a multi-agency approach. It is my Department’s aim that everyone in custody should have accommodation available on release. The Protocol for the Management of the Accommodation and Related Support Needs of People in Custody in Northern Ireland was published in December 2023.  This sets out a collaborative approach to addressing accommodation needs from NIHE, Probation Board NI and the Northern Ireland Prison Service for those entering and leaving custody.My Department contributes to the DfC led Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan (IDHAP). This plan was developed to compliment the NIHE Homelessness Strategy, and sets out a cross-departmental collaboration to improve the lives and life chances of those who are at most risk of homelessness.  We have contributed to years one to five of this plan in support of this work.  One of my Department’s actions in the Plan is to deliver early intervention support for those exiting custody. In recognition of the particular needs of people leaving prison custody the Northern Ireland Prison Service has put in place a number of initiatives to mitigate against the risk of homelessness for those leaving prison.  These are:an assessment of prisoner needs upon committal, which includes their housing arrangements, and enables staff to put suitable interventions in place, such as support from the Housing Rights advocacy service;a pilot of NIHE Housing Advisors working within Maghaberry Prison.  This provides early intervention on housing needs and allows decisions on accommodation to take cognisance of other resettlement factors including health and employability. An evaluation of the pilot will be completed over the coming months, and subject to the outcome, it is hoped to introduce Housing Advisors into Magilligan and Hydebank Wood;the used of ‘Approved Premises’ to enable appropriate support and supervision arrangements to be put in place for high-risk offenders who are subject to PPANI requirements; andwork with Belfast City Council led Complex Lives multi-disciplinary team (including Extern, NIHE and Belfast Trust) to identify how better collaboration could support female prisoners who are known to the programme that come into contact with the prison system and most at risk of homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council's suggestions made in their assessment of the 2025-26 Draft Budget, such as reviewing pay parity and increasing household contributions.
answer_text: The consultation on the draft budget closed on the 13th of March 2025. All responses will be given due consideration including the latest report from the Fiscal Council.

answer_text: An estimated 180k households are expected to receive the payment.An estimated 250k pensioners are expected to receive a payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the impact that the United States of America's proposals for tariffs on imports of steel and aluminum on both GB and EU will have on the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: A 25% tariff on all steel and aluminium imports to the US took effect on 12 March 2025. Tariffs are a barrier to trade and are bad for business. I will continue to monitor developments and work with all partners to highlight the need to protect our economy, and to advocate on behalf of trade with and investment in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the future of the St. Lucia site in Omagh.
answer_text: My Department intends to commence disposal of the St Lucia site later this year. It is intended for a joint exercise with Ministry of Defence to allow for the site to be advertised in one or two lots. The disposal will follow the standard practice for disposal of surplus public sector lands and property.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on all capital works planned for GP practices in West Tyrone.
answer_text: A number of projects have recently been completed, are under way or are planned in the West Tyrone constituency area to create additional capacity within GP practices.  These include:Capital works completed to accommodate core GP services and additional staff required for the rollout of Multi-disciplinary teams at Omagh Primary Care Complex, Western Rural Health Care (Newtownstewart, Ederney and Castlederg sites), Drumquin Healthcare and Dunamanagh Medical Practice.Carrickmore Health Centre – Minor capital works were competed in Carrickmore Health Centre in 2021 and 2025.    In addition to these works, SPPG Primary Care Infrastructure Development team (PCID) have been working with a range of stakeholders, to include DoH colleagues, Trusts and NI General Practitioners Committee (NIGPC), to agree a prioritisation approach to the development of an investment priority plan for HSC Trust owned spoke premises.  This prioritisation plan for a number of Trust projects identified as needing prioritised investment, including Carrickmore Health Centre, has just been completed. The PCID team have already commenced engagement with Carrickmore Practice, and Western HSC Trust to establish a project for redevelopment of Carrickmore Health Centre. The project will be subject to the usual business case approvals (to include any potential revenue costs as well as capital costs). Timings will be dependent on the outcome of this process and approval of funding via the Department’s overarching future capital plan. Strabane Health and Care Centre – an outline business case approved for a new purpose-built hub in Strabane, as part of the Derry & Strabane City Deal.The SPPG are currently working with all other West Tyrone Practices regarding options for additional accommodation requirements for core General Medical Services (GMS) alongside an increase in capacity for GP Training consultation rooms and space for Physiotherapy and Mental Health and Social Work services as part of the multi-disciplinary teams (MDT) roll out.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on a planned park and ride facility in Strabane.
answer_text: My Department has identified the potential for further Park and Ride facilities in the vicinity of the existing Bus Station in Strabane on Bradley Way.Whilst I am supportive of expanding our Park and Ride schemes where needed, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and available funding must be prioritised accordingly.  Whilst my officials continue to consider options for Park and Ride sites, including Strabane, it is likely that future development on Park and Ride projects will be limited without appropriate funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the capital works planned for schools in West Tyrone.
answer_text: The current position of the Major Capital Programme projects is as detailed below:St Catherine’s Primary School, Strabane, is in the concept design stage (RIBA Stage 2).Drumragh Integrated College is in the technical design and pre-tender stage (RIBA Stage 4).  Dean Maguirc College, Carrickmore, was paused in 2023 and remains so. The current position of the School Enhancement Programme projects is as detailed below: St Mary’s Primary School, Cloughcor, is at the developed design stage (RIBA Stage 3).  The proposed project is for an extension to the main school building, the refurbishment of the existing main school building and site works to provide hard play areas, traffic management and car parking facilities. Land and Property Services are negotiating a second plot of land to facilitate the project.Strabane Primary School, is at Business Case development stage, following completion of the Technical Feasibility Report.  The proposed project is for the refurbishment of the existing building and a two- storey extension. In addition, construction began on site for the Strule Shared Education Campus in early February 2025. Full opening of the Campus is planned for September 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the (i) Being Open Framework; and (ii) Duty of Candour, including a time frame for introduction.
answer_text: In terms of the Being Open Framework, I launched a public consultation on 10 December 2024.  The consultation will run for 14 weeks until 21 March 2025.Since the start of the consultation period my Department has to date facilitated eight engagement sessions.  These vital sessions have presented the opportunity for attendees to receive a detailed overview of the framework, offer their feedback and ask questions.  Three further engagement sessions have been arranged before the consultation closes.A detailed analysis of all responses received will be undertaken when the consultation closes help inform the final Being Open Framework. It is anticipated that implementation of the new Framework will commence in September 2025.As part of the Health and Social Care Three Year plan, I have committed to advance proposals for an organisational duty of candour, in the first instance, as well as considering proposals for an individual duty of candour in the coming months.  These considerations will take account of the UK-wide work on the Hillsborough Law and the work arising from the Infected Blood Inquiry.The Prime Minister has committed to introducing the new Hillsborough Law before the 35th Anniversary of the Hillsborough disaster in April 2025.  Given the wide scope of this legislation (potentially impacting on all public sector staff in Northern Ireland) the Department of Finance is leading on the Northern Ireland implications.  My Department is represented in a cross-Departmental Working Group to progress arising issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GP practices that have written to (i) his Department; and (ii) the Health and Social Care Trusts, to express concern about delivering current GP contracts in each of the last three years.
answer_text: I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs.The following outlines the GP practices who have contacted my Department to advise they wish to hand back their GMS contract:Year Contract Handbacks2024/2025           5 (to date2023/2024112022/202313** Z00661 is counted as 22/23 Practice - their notice was served December 2021Despite the pressures on General Practice and the number of practices that have handed back their contracts, with the support of GP Federations and Trusts in taking on contracts, my Department has been able to ensure that in recent years, only one of these contract hand-backs has resulted in practice closure. In terms of short-term measures in response to emerging difficulties, the General Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team is able to support practices which are at risk to develop and implement effective practice recovery plans to ensure that they can deliver their contractual obligations to their registered patients.Whilst this has been important in providing stability, we recognise that more sustainable options are required in the longer term.To that end, my Department is working with the Western Trust to support them to move away from the locum-reliant model that has been in place in Trust-run practices, and implement a more sustainable salaried GP model. We are also working with GP Federations to explore how they can play a role in the longer-term sustainability of GMS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Royal Courts of Justice judgement in relation to the failure to adopt an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: My Department has noted the findings of the Court and officials are considering these in detail. The Anti-Poverty Strategy remains a key priority for me, and I have presented a draft strategy to the Executive.It should be noted that the case brought against me as Minister for Communities was dismissed and the Court recognised my “intention to produce an anti-poverty strategy in the near future for consideration and, if appropriate, adoption by the Executive Committee.”It would be inappropriate to comment further on this matter until after the judge has ruled on potential relief in this case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will agree to the request from Derry City and Strabane District Council to extend the deadline for the Northern Ireland Football Fund beyond 14 March 2025.
answer_text: The Department has no plans to extend the deadline for applications beyond 14 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of agency staff in his Department in the last three years; and (ii) any action he is taking to reduce this number.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 8620/22-27 which detailed the number of agency workers in my department per year in the period 2019 -2023. The number of agency workers at the end of March 2024 was 239.Not every agency worker post is required to be filled by a substantive member of staff. For example, agency staff employed relating to special exercises, a temporary promotion chain or to cover an absence caused by maternity leave would not ordinarily result in the post being filled substantively. If a post is required to be filled substantively NICS vacancy management procedures are adhered to. These procedures include the appointment of staff from NICS HR merit lists following recruitment competitions; internal transfers of staff; secondments; or NICS wide trawls. Each of these options is considered on a case-by-case basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the Woodland Trust BIO-WELL study; and (ii) what his Department is doing to support native woodlands.
answer_text: I welcome the findings of the recent study supported by the Woodland Trust, which demonstrates the role that the biodiversity of our woodlands and forests have in providing health and wellbeing benefits to people. This science and evidence base underpins these benefits that people gain from visiting my Department’s forests and woodlands. Increasing woodland cover in NI is a key priority for my Department and also the protection and enhancement of high conservation value, including native woodland. Recognising the importance of native woodland habitat, the Forests for Our Future Programme has, since 2020, included the Small Woodland Grant Scheme which is designed to specifically support new native woodland. This scheme, along with the Forest Expansion Scheme has supported planting of approximately 3.4 million native trees to create, almost 1,900 hectares of new native woodland. Within the more general presence of native tree species throughout the forest estate Forest Service manages 4,600 hectares of areas specifically categorised as native woodland. The sustainable forest management of our woodlands incorporates long term plans that include doubling the area of native woodland over the long term as forests are restructured following harvesting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
