question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the target for 1900 shared ownership homes by the end of March 2027 contained in the Programme for Government 2024-2027 is in addition to the commitment to 5,850 new build social homes also mentioned in the document.
answer_text: Yes, shared ownership is intermediate housing targeted primarily at First Time Buyers. It is delivered by the Northern Ireland Co-Ownership Housing Association supported by Financial Transactions Capital loan funding provided by my Department. These homes are therefore not included in the social housing target.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the disability employment gap in each of the last three years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: ANSWEREstimates of the disability employment gap are sourced from the Labour Force Survey.  The disability employment gap is the difference in percentage points (pps) between the employment rate for respondents who identified as having a disability and the employment rate for respondents who did not identify as having a disability.  As an example, the employment rates for disabled and non-disabled individuals in Belfast were 40% and 79.6% respectively in 2023, giving rise to a disability employment gap of 39.6 percentage points (pps).The table below provides details of the disability employment gap broken down by local government district for the most recent three years for which data is available, 2021, 2022 and 2023.Table 1: Disability gap by local government district, 2021 – 2023 2021(pps)2022(pps)2023(pps)Antrim and Newtownabbey46.531.131.7Ards and North Down37.045.737.1Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon49.744.440.5Belfast40.940.039.6Causeway Coast and Glens33.348.946.6Derry City and Strabane42.649.945.1Fermanagh and Omagh46.051.337.6Lisburn and Castlereagh34.423.540.8Mid and East Antrim41.238.133.0Mid Ulster54.937.750.8Newry Mourne and Down39.952.846.3Total42.342.441.3Source: NI Labour Force Survey

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Water Classification Statistics 2024 Report; and (ii) to detail any work he is undertaking to increase the number of transitional or coastal water bodies achieving high ecological status from the current zero level.
answer_text: Whilst I welcome the publication of the updated Water Classification Statistics report 2024, it is disappointing to note that the statistics show an overall picture of stagnation in the status of our water bodies. Whilst there were some improvements in ecological status, there were also deteriorations. It is clear that Northern Ireland will not achieve its objective of achieving good ecological status for surface water bodies by 2027.The Programme of Measures outlined in the draft 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan aim to prevent deterioration, protect, enhance and restore water bodies, (including river, lake and transitional and coastal waterbodies), to achieve good ecological status and good surface water chemical status through an integrated catchment approach.  This includes a range of education, regulation and policy initiatives.Increasing nutrient concentrations remains an issue in estuaries, sea loughs and coastal waters predominantly from agricultural and human wastewater sources. Other contributing factors in not achieving high status are related to human development and activities such as shoreline reinforcement and fishing activities.As the presence of invasive alien species is the main cause for transitional and coastal water bodies not achieving high ecological status, my Department has published ‘An Invasive Alien Species Strategy for Northern Ireland’. This includes biosecurity measures, identification of pathways to introductions, and the identification of new invasive species threats. However, once invasive alien species are established, these are extremely difficult to eradicate. It is unlikely that this trend can be reversed to increase the number of transitional and coastal water bodies reaching high ecological status in the near future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the guidance issued on the provision of cannabis-based medicines to treat illnesses such as epilepsy is fit for purpose.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) produces Clinical Guidelines which are evidence-based recommendations systematically developed using input from experts, people using services, carers and the public. They assess the cost and clinical effectiveness of different treatments and ways of managing a specific condition to ensure they improve quality of care while representing good value for money.My Department established formal links with NICE on 1 July 2006 whereby guidance published by the Institute from that date would be locally reviewed for applicability to Northern Ireland and, where appropriate, endorsed for implementation in Health and Social Care (HSC). This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions.NICE Clinical Guideline NG144 which covers prescribing of cannabis-based medicinal products for people with intractable nausea and vomiting, chronic pain, spasticity and severe treatment-resistant epilepsy was published by NICE on 11 November 2019 and was subsequently endorsed for implementation within the HSC by my Department on 14 January 2020. The Departmental review is not a reassessment of the clinical and cost evidence used by NICE in forming its advice. NICE guidance is proofed by my Department only to check for legal, policy and financial consequences related to its implementation in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority report Review of the Pathways Associated with the Temporary Suspension of Emergency General Surgery at South West Acute Hospital; and (ii) to detail the progress on implementing the ten recommendations in the report.
answer_text: My Department commissioned RQIA to carry out a review of the effectiveness of clinical pathways associated with the temporary suspension of emergency general surgery at South West Acute Hospital (SWAH), with a view to identifying any aspects for improvement. A detailed joint response to the Review by my Department and Western Trust, including work to progress the recommendations, is available athttps://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-01/doh-rqia-review-response-290125.PDF The report made two recommendations to be taken forward with urgency. I can advise that direct admissions to ward at Altnagelvin continues in line with the progress noted in the response to the report.  Similarly, the Western Trust continues to have daily engagement with NIAS on the effectiveness of the pathways and has maintained a significant focus on its ambulance turnaround times.  My Department will continue to monitor the Trust’s progress on implementation of the recommendations.

answer_text: AQW 23244/22-27 was answered on 3 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her work with the Irish Government to develop the rail networks.
answer_text: The Department continues to work with our colleagues in the Department of Transport, who have secured support from the European Investment Bank Advisory Services (EIB), to help progress implementation of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review recommendations. With the assistance of EIB consultants, my officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail and NTA to consider the recommendations and identify those rail interventions which could be well advanced or delivered over the next decade to 2035. The outcomes from the ongoing EIB work will help inform which projects to prioritise.Already, rail services on the island are being developed towards the Report’s vision. This includes an increase to an hourly train service between Dublin and Belfast, while procurement has also started on a new, faster, more sustainable “Enterprise” train fleet for the Dublin-Belfast route.The Irish Government has allocated Shared Island funding which has supported the introduction of the new hourly service in October 2024, which coincided with the opening of Belfast Grand Central Station, moving this key service closer to the city centre and delivering more connectivity with other bus, coach and train services across the North of Ireland and beyond.Officials have also engaged extensively with colleagues in the Department of Transport and SEUPB to help support the thematic areas being developed for the Peace Plus programme and €165m of Peace Plus funding will be allocated to the Enterprise Fleet Replacement project. The investment will improve the experience of people travelling by train and encourage others to use these services. With more frequency and capacity, it will make it even easier for people to better connect and make greener transport choices which will bring economic and social benefits for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the recommendation of the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority report Review of the Pathways Associated with the Temporary Suspension of Emergency General Surgery at South West Acute Hospital (SWAH) for an additional £2 million of recurrent funding for ambulances to transfer patients between SWAH and Altnagelvin Area Hospital will be implemented.
answer_text: A recommendation of the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) Review of Pathways Resulting from the Temporary Suspension of Emergency General Surgery at South West Acute Hospital (SWAH) identified ‘There is a need for the Department of Health to consider the provision of a more sustainable and resilient ambulance capacity to strengthen the model supporting emergency surgical services within the Trust area, and address concerns about the impact on NIAS services more widely in the area’.The recommendation does not state that recurrent additional funding is required to achieve this.  My Department is currently undertaking a regional capacity and demand exercise with Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS).The piece of work will factor in service reconfigurations across NI including the temporary arrangements for the transfer of patients from SWAH to Altnagelvin Hospital and the impact on NIAS resources.The transfer of patients from SWAH to Altnegelvin is a provisional arrangement following the suspension of emergency general surgery at SWAH.  My Department are working closely with NIAS through the usual monitoring arrangements to identify additional resource requirements on a non-recurrent basis to meet service pressures.This outcome of the capacity and demand exercise will help determine the NIAS model going forward and will inform future discussions with NIAS on investments required which will be subject to funding being available.Discussion on NIAS future modelling will also be subject to the outcome of a consultation on a permanent closure of Emergency General Surgery in SWAH and consideration to changes to service configurations in SWAH.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the Domestic Homicide Review - Marcella; and (ii) to detail any progress her Department and agencies have made on the key recommendations contained in the report.
answer_text: First and foremost, I want to acknowledge the victim, whose life was tragically lost. My thoughts remain with their family and friends, who continue to endure the profound impact of this devastating event.I also want to recognise the work of the Domestic Homicide Review (DHR) panel led by the independent chair for the thorough approach it took throughout this review. This work is essential in identifying learning that can help to enhance the protection for other individuals who are at risk from domestic abuse.The review identified ten overarching recommendations. Three of these recommendations have also been made in other DHRs; whilst these reviews were published previously, they relate to deaths which were broadly contemporaneous with the death of Marcella (pseudonym), and therefore it is unsurprising that some of the learning and recommendations in this review reflects those made in earlier reports. One of the overarching recommendations falls to my department, and work is already underway to complete this; several recommendations sit with PSNI, PPS and PBNI to implement, and they have provided an update on their progress. Progress includes:RecommendationsUpdate	To mitigate human error and ensure that perpetrators of domestic violence and abuse are appropriately referred to PPANI in a timely manner, an electronic prompt to be added to police information systems alerting officers to review previous qualifying convictions and any ‘current significant concerns.’	In the interim, to mitigate associated potential delays arising from the technical development and roll-out of such an information system process, PSNI will support officers and staff with circulation of relevant internal instructional documentation. 		PSNI note communication across organisations about the requirement for PPANI referrals to be made and the parameters for this.  Consideration for a technical alert or solution to this as a mandatory systems check to be completed	Multi-agency Working Group to develop and share safe and constructive points for relevant frontline staff to consider that will reduce the likelihood of DV perpetrators manipulating staff and situations. These principles should be tailored towards each organisation’s needs and incorporated into guidance and training.	Work has progressed, led by the Probation Board for NI, to develop a training package with a working title of ‘Grooming and Manipulation of Professionals’.  This training will be made available to other agencies to deliver within their own organisation.	Investigative and prosecution standards, as outlined in the PPS & PSNI Service Level Agreement on Domestic Violence:	Are subject to on-going	          monitoring to ensure	          compliance, 	Places a greater focus on victim risk in evidence led prosecutions, including where a victim is unable to engage or has withdrawn their support.	An updated Service Level Agreement on the Investigation, Management and Prosecution of Domestic Abuse Cases came in

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the latest first consultant-led outpatient appointment waiting time figures; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to reduce these waiting lists.
answer_text: Waiting times for outpatients continue to be unacceptable.  The provision of efficient and effective outpatient services is crucial to the timely assessment and diagnosis of patients referred to secondary care services. A wide range of work is on-going to ensure that patients referred to outpatient services are seen in a timely manner.  This includes a focus on the efficiency of current service models, modernisation of outpatient services, with an emphasis on enhanced triage and patient-initiated follow-up (PIFU). These workstreams will enhance outpatient triage through streamlining patient pathways to ensure patients are directed to the most appropriate service first time, while ensuring only patients for whom it is clinically appropriate received an outpatient review appointment. Taken collectively, this will reduce unnecessary new and review outpatient appointments and help reduce the waiting times for outpatient assessment and follow up. Bringing the waiting lists in Northern Ireland to an acceptable level demands a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial investment through multi-year budgets, workforce development and system wide reform.  The NI Executive’s Programme for Government (PfG) includes a reference to investing up to £215m a year on the waiting list backlog, in line with the required investment levels detailed in the revised Elective Care Framework, published last year. However, the draft 2025/26 NI budget does not contain any additional funding for waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions she is taking to increase satisfaction in public transport, following recent figures from the Continuous Household Survey that shows satisfaction levels down 3 per cent.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink is currently working through significant investment programmes aimed at enhancing public transport in the North. As well as step changes in infrastructure with developments like the construction of Belfast Grand Central Station, technology investments see the introduction of contactless payment and Account Based Ticketing through Translink Future Ticketing Strategy. Furthermore Translink is rolling out state of the art fleet with the introduction of Zero Emission bus fleet and the expansion of rail carriages, as well as developing the Enterprise replacement programme. There is a significant range of other ongoing and upcoming developments being delivered through a Continuous Improvement approach.In addition to the recent perception survey, ongoing independent monitoring of customer satisfaction takes place with passengers on bus and rail services as part of my Department’s  Public Service Agreement with Translink. The monitoring is carried out by the CARD Group, and their market researchers interview passengers across all services either on board, at a stop, or at a station. Over the course of the year over 6,000 bus, Glider and rail passengers are interviewed. The results are published twice a year and used to pinpoint any areas of Translink services which may need improvement.Passengers are asked to rate their satisfaction with a wide range of service attributes including punctuality and reliability, value for money as well as overall satisfaction with their most recent journey.The results for the period Monday 1st April 2024 – Sunday 29th September 2024, show that overall satisfaction score with most recent journey on Translink services was 88% and the results for overall satisfaction for individual bus and rail services along with key service attributes are available on the Translink website.Overall Customer Satisfaction Results With Most Recent Journey on Translink ServicesOverall Satisfaction88%Duration of Journey 94%Frequently 85%Staff Helpfulness93%Accessibility of Vehicle96%Cleanliness93%Comfort of Seating 94%Punctuality87%Safety of Driving 98%Safety on Board 97%Value for Money 84%Information on Board93%Recent historic perception scoring is as follows:Spring / Summer 24/25 https://www.translink.co.uk/customer-satisfaction-results Overall Satisfaction with Most Recent Journey – 88%Autumn / Winter 23/24https://www.translink.co.uk/customer-satisfaction-results-23-24 Overall Satisfaction with Most Recent Journey – 90%Spring / Summer 23/24https://www.translink.co.uk/customer-satisfaction-results-2023 Overall Satisfaction with Most Recent Journey – 89% For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use o

answer_text: (i)  None(ii) None

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Young People’s Attitudes to Refugees and Asylum Seekers in Northern Ireland 2024 report.
answer_text: It is important to understand public attitudes to refugees and asylum seekers, and how these are changing. We therefore welcome the publication of this report. Its findings will help to inform the delivery of a Refugee Integration Strategy and associated Delivery Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action he is taking to increase the low participation from the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area in the Continuous Household Survey.
answer_text: In order to maximise response in Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, and across the North, NISRA has recruited over 100 interviewers in the last 12 months. NISRA has also updated a range of training materials for new interviewers and enhanced the refresher training for existing interviewers for all surveys. NISRA routinely monitors the progress of all surveys. Participation in the Continuous Household Survey in Fermanagh and Omagh is higher than average.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any joint work she and her counterpart in the Republic of Ireland are undertaking to tackle gang violence.
answer_text: I held an introductory virtual meeting on 4 February 2025 with Minister O’Callaghan following his appointment and we are due to meet in person in the coming days.  During the call we committed to continue building on the existing partnership arrangements of a collaborative cross border approach to tackling crime, including gang-based violence.  This work is underpinned by the Cross Border Policing Strategy 2025-27 which can be found here: Cross Border Policing Strategy 2025 - 2027.  One of the key priority areas in the Strategy is tackling crime and preventing harm.We also receive regular briefings on the work of the Joint Agency Task Force which enables collaboration between law enforcement agencies and is key to successfully disrupting and preventing organised criminality.  It continues to be a highly effective model for tackling cross-border organised crime, with strong practical and strategic collaboration between law enforcement agencies delivering real results.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine view that our Emergency Departments are facing a worsening crisis; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to address this.
answer_text: There is no doubt that Emergency Departments are currently under significant pressure, a situation that is reflective of the current pressures across the entire Health and Social Care system.The pressures are related not just to the numbers of patients attending ED, but also the acuity of patient conditions. A central factor is capacity in social care, which impacts on hospital discharge rates and consequently on patient flow, which then manifests in EDs.When I published the Department’s winter preparedness plan on 6 November last year, I was clear that we expected a very difficult winter and that the plan would mitigate pressures as far as possible, but it would not eliminate them.Work has already begun to prepare for next winter and I have commenced a series of workshops with the aim of identifying  lasting solutions both in the short-term and long-term to address pressures across the system. I personally attended the first of these workshops on 4 March and was encouraged by the engagement and enthusiasm from HSC leaders across the system. Consequently, I expect to see better outcomes next winter.In the longer-term, my vision involves diverting patients to the most appropriate care pathways first time, to provide patients with the best care possible and to avoid unnecessary Emergency Department attendance. The work to date has seen the introduction of Urgent Care Centres and local Phone First services in every Trust, along with the introduction of rapid access clinics and other additional pathways. While work will continue to improve and refine these services, it is important to acknowledge that it will take time. Unfortunately, there is no quick fix and addressing the current situation requires sustained long-term effort, additional recurrent funding and support across the political spectrum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is supporting female participation in sport.
answer_text: ‘Active Living’, the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland, was launched by my Department in March 2022.  A key theme of the strategy is promoting participation, inclusion and community engagement in sport and physical activity with a particular focus on currently under-represented groups, which includes women and girls. It highlights the need for a continued focus and prioritisation of female participation across all aspects of sport and physical activity to ensure that the positive participation trends of recent years continue to be realised and the participation gap between men and women is closed.Encouraging the participation of women and girls in sport is also a key priority for Sport NI, an Arm’s-Length Body of my Department, and this issue has been included in the 2024-25 Business Plan under their Women in Sport ‘Engagement and Delivery’ Plan.As this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the action his Department is taking to address the increase in (i) Japanese Knotweed; and (ii) Giant Hogweed.
answer_text: Both Japanese knotweed and Giant Hogweed have been established in Northern Ireland for many decades.(i) Japanese knotweed is not listed in the Order, so it is not illegal to have it on land, nor is there any statutory mechanism in Northern Ireland to require a landowner to control it. The Department carries out focused work to address it; prioritising designated and high value biodiversity sites, via conditioning its removal through planning applications, invasives management in NIEA country parks, and through the Environmental Farming Scheme. Invasive species control is also conditioned on all funding streams, such as the Environment and Challenge Funds, and Water Quality Improvement Grants. Department officials frequently provide advice to homeowners about Japanese knotweed. (ii) Following public consultation in 2019, the Department designated giant hogweed as a Widely Spread Species (WSS), which now obligates all landowners to initiate management measures if it is reported to be present on their land. Where giant hogweed is confirmed present, Department officials will contact the landowner and require that management measures be put in place to stop spread and ultimately facilitate its removal. There are currently over 400 ongoing landowner giant hogweed cases being monitored by the NIEA Invasive Non-Native Species Team.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has any plans to increase financial support for postgraduate students.
answer_text: Following a review in 2022 the postgraduate tuition fee loan was increased from £5,500 to £6,500 for an eligible postgraduate course.  The review recommended considering the position after three years. My Department is committed to undertaking a review of higher education funding which, among other issues, will consider the merit and practicability of adjustments to the current package of student support, including support for postgraduate students.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the budget for the Ending the Harm public awareness campaign for 2024-25; and (ii) the proposed budget for 2025-26.
answer_text: The budget for the Ending the Harm public awareness campaign for 2024-25 is £185,000 and the proposed budget for 2025-26 is £100,000.The current campaign, launched in October 2024, based on a ‘statement’ creative initially developed as part of a previous Ending the Harm campaign, cost less than £5,000 to develop.  It gives a name to the many ways that paramilitaries exploit vulnerable people; and it helps to expose and challenge the true nature of paramilitary influence so that it cannot hide in plain sight. It has appeared in communities across Northern Ireland and is also present on bus advertising and social media, providing further visibility in geographical areas where there may be a lack of advertising space. You can view the posters on the EPPOC website: endingtheharm.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 22843/22-27, for a timetable of the procurement process for Project Gigabit.
answer_text: The procurement for Project Gigabit commenced on 11 November 2024, with an invitation to interested suppliers to complete a selection questionnaire to demonstrate their capacity and capability to deliver such a contract.  Those suppliers who meet the published criteria of the selection questionnaire will be invited to submit a response to the requirements of the Invitation to Tender for the project. The procurement will seek a supplier to address an initial scope of approximately 11,000 premises. My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit in the coming months, subject to compliant bids being made, bids meeting the published criteria, and all necessary approvals being agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 21464/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 21464 was answered on 11 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a timetable for his response to the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing System in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The report of the independent review of the licensing system including the surrender principle was published on 21 February 2025. I have asked my officials to consider carefully the report of the independent review of the licensing system, including the surrender principle, and to provide me with advice on each of the 26 recommendations.	In accordance with section 23 of the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) (NI) Act 2021, my intention is to publish a plan setting out how I will respond to the report by 20 August 2025, and I will make an oral statement about my plan to the Assembly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the suspected case of avian influenza at a site near Pomeroy.
answer_text: A suspect case of notifiable Avian Influenza was reported on Saturday 22 February 2025 at a commercial poultry premises near Pomeroy, County Tyrone. A Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) was established, from Sunday 23 February, following preliminary positive results for Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI). The decision was taken based on a number of factors including the clinical signs and preliminary results provided by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI).  All poultry at the site have been humanely culled as a disease control measure.  The TCZ comprises a 3km TCZ-A and a 10km TCZ-B.  On 28 February 2025, following the receipt of official laboratory confirmation, the Chief Veterinary Officer for Northern Ireland has confirmed that HPAI H5N1 is present at the affected premises in Pomeroy.  As a result, the TCZ has been replaced by a 3km Protection Zone (PZ) and a 10km Surveillance Zone (Zone).The declaration which details all the measures that must be adhered to within the 3km and 10km zones can be found on the DAERA website, as follows:  Disease Control Zone Declarations | Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of comments made by the independent autism reviewer at the joint meeting of the Committee for Health and the Committee for Education, when she stated his plans for special educational needs reform are quite disappointing and that schools are not ready for it.
answer_text: Senior Officials have regular scheduled meetings with the independent autism reviewer.  Officials recently met with the independent autism reviewer to discuss her views on specific areas of the delivery plan.  As part of the SEN Reform Agenda Delivery Plan, I outlined the need to maintain engagement and open channels of communication.  Practitioner and expert advisory input will also be features of the implementation of the Delivery Plan.  Delivery of my five-year plan will require phased implementation in partnership with children and young people, parents, schools, early years setting, the community and voluntary sector, youth organisations sectoral, local government, Executive Departments and their delivery partners. This approach will support the collaborative commitment necessary to bring about sustainable change for children, their families and schools.

answer_text: SPPG commissions GP Out of Hours (OOHs) services across Northern Ireland from five GP OOHs Providers.  Western Urgent Care (WUC) provide GP OOHs services for Western areas respectively. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the effectiveness of the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016; and (iI) whether he has plans to review the legislation.
answer_text: The Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 (the Act) came into operation seven years ago to ensure that public authorities listed in the Schedule to the Act have due regard to rural needs when developing, adopting, implementing or revising policies, strategies and plans and in the design and delivery of public services.Section 3(2) of the Act places an obligation on my department to publish an annual report containing a summary of each Rural Needs Impact Assessment completed by the public authorities listed in the Schedule to the Act. While the completion of a Rural Needs Impact Assessment does not require public authorities to undertake a certain course of action or demonstrate that they have met their due regard to rural needs, it requires them to consciously consider the needs of rural communities when considering developing, adopting, implementing, revising a policy, strategy or plan and designing or delivering a public service.  The overall number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments completed has increased since commencement of the Act from 157 to 502 which demonstrates a significant degree of commitment by public authorities to give due regard to the impact of their policies on rural communities.I recognise that there is still scope for more to be done to help ensure that the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 delivers better outcomes for rural communities and I am keen that the Act is more widely reviewed now that it has been in operation for seven years. I have asked my officials to consider this in the development of new evidence-based rural policy proposals, which will be subject to public consultation this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimated savings to budgets following the new initiative on medical and dental agency costs.
answer_text: Whilst a full assessment of the amount that can be delivered from this has yet to be made, a reduction of some £20 million could be achieved if we could reduce spending to 2022/23 levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of kidney transplants in each of the last ten years; and (ii) what his Department is doing to encourage kidney donations.
answer_text: Information on the number of kidney transplants carried out are published by NHS Blood and Transplant and are available on its website atwww.organdonation.nhs.uk/helping-you-to-decide/about-organ-donation/statistics-about-organ-donation/ With regards to encouraging kidney donation, my Department has a statutory duty to promote organ transplantation under the Health (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.  In line with this duty, my Department oversees a comprehensive rolling programme of outreach and engagement activity to promote knowledge and informed discussion about organ donation, which is aimed at driving registrations on the NHS Organ Donor Register (ODR) and sustaining and increasing rates of consent to both living and deceased organ donation

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the legacy assets released to the Dairy Council NI from the Residual Milk Marketing Board.
answer_text: Since the dissolution of the Residual Milk Marketing Board, the Department has held the assets transferred, amounting to £76,882.60.  In line with the relevant regulations, my Department held a targeted, 8 week consultation with dairy industry stakeholders from 13 August 2024 until 08 October 2024 to seek views on how best to dispose of the assets. Additional information on the consultation can be found on DAERA’s consultation webpage.   The Milk Marketing Board (Residuary Functions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995 as amended by the Milk Marketing Board (Residuary Functions) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2002 regulations stipulated that the legacy assets must be transferred to a body corporate or other organisation connected with milk production in Northern Ireland, or beneficial to relevant producers or to persons engaged in the production of milk in Northern Ireland.My Department considered all consultation responses and concluded to release the legacy funds in full to the Dairy Council for Northern Ireland as their proposal most fulfilled the legislative requirement of benefiting those in milk production in NI, focussing on promoting the importance of dairy providing good nutrition within schools.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has to upgrade Omagh Courthouse.
answer_text: There are no specific plans at this time for an upgrade of Omagh Courthouse. In addition to ongoing statutory and reactive maintenance, in recent years minor works projects have been progressed to improve consultation facilities and the custodial heating system. The NICTS Estate Strategy, published in December 2023, provides a long-term framework for investment over the next decade and beyond. To implement the Strategy, NICTS is using data and evidence to baseline the existing estate against the agreed Strategic Outcomes, assess the adequacy of its buildings, and in the context of the finite resources available, develop prioritised investment plans that will be subject to public consultation, as appropriate.Future upgrades to Omagh Courthouse will be considered as part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 21572/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to reduce the current 11.9 per cent vacancy rate within the Nursing and Midwifery Support Staff Group.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of investing in the training and career pathways of the nursing and midwifery support roles for the recruitment and retention of this important element of the HSC.In 2023, the Chief Nursing Officer launched 'Shaping Our Future’, a five-year vision for nursing and midwifery. This initiative aims to maximise the potential of the entire nursing and midwifery workforce, including healthcare support workers, in a safe, inclusive culture. Key priorities under this five-year plan are: workforce and workload planning; pre- and post- registration education and training; and the development of a quality assurance framework for Nursing & Midwifery.The Chief Nursing Officer has also commissioned the Northern Ireland Practice Education Council (NIPEC) to develop a career pathway for Nursing and Midwifery within Northern Ireland, which will include nursing and midwifery health care support worker roles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on discussions with the Association of British Insurers in relation to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: ANSWERI had a meeting with the Association of British Insurers (ABI) to discuss the insurance industry response to Storm Éowyn on Thursday 13 February. I pressed the ABI on the importance of insurance companies adequately supporting consumers and businesses in the wake of the unprecedented impact of the storm. In particular, I stressed how important it was that the insurance industry helps those impacted in appropriately assessing damages and then providing the funding needed for vital repairs. I am also fully aware of the significant financial pressure insurance costs can put on consumers. Therefore, I urged the ABI to ensure that consumers are not further penalised for the Storm through increased premiums.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how their Department will take advantage of the opportunities presented by Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: As a region, we are already taking advantage of the benefits of AI. In many respects we are at the forefront of capability in this area, as evidenced in the Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre (AICC). Funded by DfE across Queen’s and Ulster Universities, the training and development offered by the centre helps to address the AI skills gap within our thriving SME sector. In addition, we have a range of Regional City Deals that support companies with cutting-edge projects based on advanced AI. For example, Studio Ulster and Momentum 1.0 are both using the latest AI advances to support our industries, ensuring we are genuinely world-leading in areas such as virtual media production and cybersecurity. We are working to establish an AI Unit within The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 21573/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why there is an increase in children (a) waiting for a Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service assessment; and (b) waiting over nine weeks in the Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any action he is taking to reduce these figures.
answer_text: (i) (a & b)The Western’s Trusts Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service continues to be challenged, like all other CAMHS regionally. Whilst the Service meets access targets with respect to Emergency and Urgent waiting times (the most vulnerable and high-risk children and young people) it is challenged to meet routine 9-week access targets. Western Trust CAMHS is cognisant that CAMHS staff continue to offer specialist assessments and manage increased severity of illness and clinical risk. There is greater complexity and acuity of presentations of children and young people within the Service thus requiring longer treatment plans for young people. This longer treatment plans results in less throughput.This is undoubtedly is a reflection of the challenges young people and their families are facing daily. Levels of deprivation, poverty and debt contributing to poor emotional wellbeing increasing vulnerability to mental health issues. CAMHS Clinicians aim is to deliver timely, responsive, compassionate care to the children, young people and their families in receipt of CAMHS care. Western Trust CAMHS acknowledges that ongoing recruitment challenges to specialist child mental health staffing positions remains an area of focus for the Service. The Service is currently operating at a significantly reduced staffing capacity level with the secondary impact of increased waits for young people to access the Service. Recruitment challenges are amplified by the close proximity to the Republic of Ireland. (ii)To mitigate the challenges Western Trust CAMHS has adopted creative and responsive ways of working in an attempt to meet these demands. Service performance initiatives have included rolling recruitment drives, realignment of staffing positions to attract a more diverse skilled workforce, CAMHS staff supporting additional new patient clinics to support waiting lists initiatives whilst collaborative quality improvement projects with the wider network are currently progressing in an effort to support timely appropriate access. My Department provided an additional £55,445 non-recurrent funding in December 2024 to support additional capacity within a pre-existing CAMHS/ Community provider to offer therapeutic intervention to children who may be stepped down to receive therapeutic intervention at a step 2 level. Workforce stabilisation aligned with the mental health workforce review is a key development priority throughout 2024/25 in keeping with the NI Mental Health Strategy. Funding will be required to secure ongoing progression of this priority. Western Trust CAMHS continues to work in partnership with the Department of Health regarding waiting times/access to service and ensuring positive outcomes for young people in receipt of the Service.Emotional Health & Wellbeing Framework funding allocation in 2022/2023 saw the establishment of the CAMHS Emotional Health & Wellbeing Team in School project (EWTS).  The DOH/DOE jointly published the Childre

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the British Association of Social Workers NI and the Social Workers Union research into the financial circumstances of social work students.
answer_text: My Department has received a report of the research conducted by the British Association of Social Workers NI and the Social Workers’ Union into the financial circumstances of social work students. I am very aware of the difficulties which can arise as a result of financial hardship which social work students can experience while undertaking professional training courses. Regrettably, given the extremely challenging budgetary situation, and the need to protect and maintain front-line services, my Department is currently unable to provide more assistance to students on professional training courses than it does at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the reasons behind the 9 per cent rise in house prices in 2024.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for setting affordable (social and intermediate) housing policy, as well as funding and oversight of affordable housing delivery. Consequently, assessing the varied and complex reasons for price increases within the wider Northern Ireland housing market sits outside its remit.With affordability remaining an issue for many aspiring homeowners across Northern Ireland, my Department does provide significant investment to assist working people and families on lower incomes, particularly first-time buyers, into affordable home ownership, through continued funding for our delivery partner for shared ownership housing, Co-Ownership.I recognise that housing supply to meet demand remains an issue and housing officials continue to explore ways to increase supply across all tenures. My Housing Supply Strategy launched in December 2024, will be critical to addressing this.https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/housing-supply-strategy-2024-2039

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of social workers employed by his Department in each of the last ten years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the bursary support for social work students in (i) Northern Ireland; and (ii) neighbouring jurisdictions.
answer_text: The NI Social Work Student Incentive Scheme is administered by the Department of Health. The Scheme is intended to incentivise applications to social work training courses and to offset some of the expense involved in undertaking a full-time degree programme. Eligible students are entitled to apply for a payment of £4,000 per annum towards general living expenses and £500 per annum towards travel costs associated with mandatory practice placements. I am aware that each of our neighbouring jurisdictions in Great Britain and the Republic of Ireland operate different bursary schemes for students undertaking professional social work training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to increase the (i) Social Work Student Incentive Scheme; and (ii) travel allowance in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: As a result of the extremely challenging budgetary situation for my Department and the need to protect and maintain essential front-line services, there are no plans to increase the quantum of the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme at this time. In recent months my Department has been able to make a modest additional payment to social work students who have been assessed as having to travel an excessive distance to/from their mandatory practice learning placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department is supporting farmers with mental health issues in West Tyrone, both financially and practically.
answer_text: Mental health support in Northern Ireland is available to anyone who needs it. It is recognised that the farming community is particularly susceptible to poor health and wellbeing, partly driven by the wide demands impacting on farmers across a range of social and economic factors. They often work long and anti-social hours which can lead to social isolation and often have difficulty accessing traditional health care services. The Public Health Agency (PHA) has supported the organisation Rural Support in delivering a range of services to help promote positive mental health among isolated rural dwellers, farmers and farm families across the region. These include:Conducting information sessions to promote positive mental health, encourage farmers and rural dwellers to seek help and link with other support services;Training for rural support staff and volunteers – to support, strengthen and build skills in mentors working on the rural support programme through coaching and skills development; andMentors providing one-to-one support to individuals experiencing financial distress and where appropriate signposting to other services. The team of mentors work closely with the Health and Social Care Trusts, Healthy Living Centres, Farm Family Health Checks and Rural GPs.  Rural Support’s Life Beyond programme provides support to farming families who have lost someone. It recognises that as well as the grief and trauma of the loss, there is also an added stress on the farm business, its day-to-day management and long-term plans.  The PHA also works in partnership with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to develop and co-fund the Farm Families Health Checks (FFHC) Programme. The overall aim of the FFHC Programme is to improve the health and social wellbeing of rural farmers and farm families in Northern Ireland by increasing local access to health screening services, by providing health-related advice and information and to signpost to existing services for further advice and support.

answer_text: There are currently four schools in the West Tyrone parliamentary constituency area offering Social Communication specialist provision classes.The Education Authority (EA) is actively engaging with schools across Northern Ireland to expand specialist provision, including Social Communication provision, for September 2025 and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 20442/22-27, to detail whether his Department will give the Housing Executive additional support to reduce these adaptation delays.
answer_text: As the Housing Executive advised in my response to AQW 20442/22-27, issues impacting the speed of delivery for major adaptations across all areas include obtaining build over agreements, statutory approvals, contractor capacity, procurement issues i.e. nil returns, changes to briefs, tenant refusals, design constraints, technical resources, complexity of requirements etc and availability of decant properties.As advised in AQW 18589/22-27, in 2024/25 I bid in the October Monitoring Round for capital funding for priority improvement works to adapt Housing Executive properties for tenants living with a disability.  The implications of this bid not being funded meant that the Housing Executive has had to use its Stock Investment Reserve.My Department has been advised of its draft 2025-26 budget allocation. This budget is currently subject to consultation. Therefore, until a final budget has been confirmed, I am unable to advise the Housing Executive of its budget allocation for 2025-26. My officials have commenced work on options to live within a further year’s constrained Budget and the impacts of this. An Equality Impact Assessment on the Department’s 2025-26 Budget Allocation will be drafted and published for consultation to inform Final 2025-26 Budget allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action she and her Department have taken to mitigate the impacts of air passenger duty on tourism.
answer_text: In January my Department launched the Tourism Vision and Action Plan outlining 17 actions to drive sustainable, regionally balanced economic growth in the sector. It provides a roadmap for the growth of tourism over the next ten years and sets a Strategic Goal to increase the annual expenditure from overnight trips to £2billion.Early actions in the year ahead include incorporating local regions into Failte Ireland’s regional brands; development of Destination Stewardship Plans; and restoring appropriate funding to Tourism Ireland to increase tourism revenue from overseas. My Department works closely with Invest NI and Tourism Ireland to promote the North as a market to airlines and through the provision of co-operative marketing assistance to new and existing routes. An Aviation Policy is also being developed by my officials working closely with all three local airports, which aims to identify monetary and non-monetary policy levers which will help us enhance our air connectivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the pollutant incident on the Glenmornan River, Artigarvan.
answer_text: Loughs Agency staff discovered dead fish in the Glenmornan River, Artigarvan on the morning of Friday 14 February 2025 whilst carrying out planned survey work.An investigation was rapidly initiated with Loughs Agency taking the lead assisted by NIEA Water Quality Inspectors. Loughs Agency has confirmed that over 4000 fish, including salmonid species of all year classes, have been killed. It is deeply frustrating and regrettable that this type of incident continues to occur.The investigation has progressed and is currently being taken forward jointly by Loughs Agency and NIEA. Potential sources of pollution have been identified and statutory samples have been collected. As the investigation is ongoing, I cannot provide any further details at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the availability of cross-border prescription drugs; and (ii) ongoing issues in relation to their supply following Brexit.
answer_text: Medicines are authorised by the appropriate competent authority for the market in which they are intended to be supplied. From 1 January 2025, under Windsor Framework arrangements for medicines, all medicines in Northern Ireland (NI), including prescription only medicines, are now authorised by the UK medicines regulator, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and can be packaged on a UK-wide basis. These arrangements addressed the pharmaceutical industry’s main concerns in relation to the long-term supply of medicines into NI. Medicines released onto the NI market by a qualified person (QP) must now contain ‘UK only’ on their outer packaging to prevent the movement of these medicines into the Single Market. My focus is on the supply of medicines into NI to meet NI patients’ needs. Whilst the Windsor Framework Agreement changed how innovative and novel medicines are licensed in NI, it did not change any legislation on the manufacture or supply of medicines into NI from the European Union, including the cross-border movement of prescription medicines, or dual market access for NI manufacturers and wholesalers. In terms of UK/ NI authorised medicines being supplied from NI and accepted for use on the Irish market, this is a matter for the Irish Government. However, I am aware that the Irish medicines regulator, the Health Products Regulatory Authority (HPRA) has updated its guidance and provided clarity to healthcare professionals and industry that it will accept the movement of NI authorised medicines from NI under certain circumstances, including on an unlicensed basis to meet patients’ needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail funding for Air Ambulance NI in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Air Ambulance NI (AANI) is a company limited by guarantee and registered charity and, as such, receives financial support from the general public to provide an air-ambulance service. Importantly, the Helicopter Emergency Medical Service is a separate entity operating in partnership between AANI and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service.While my Department does not ordinarily allocate funding to AANI, £1m was provided to AANI in February 2021, at the height of the COVID-19 crisis, to help the service navigate the difficult landscape at that time for organisations relying on donations from the general public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current cross-border pollution incidents which include involvement by the Loughs Agency.
answer_text: The Loughs Agency is not currently involved in any cross-border pollution incidents.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why there is a short timescale for schools to spend their Outdoor Learning Project Capital allocations.
answer_text: All schools have been asked to submit their outdoor learning orders promptly.  This is both a practical and effective approach to ensure the available budget is fully utilised and to benefit pupil learning before the end of the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 18750/22-27, to detail any action his Department is taking to increase the number of schools availing of the School Milk Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: The School Milk Subsidy Scheme, which is delivered on a day-to-day basis by the Education Authority, aims to encourage the consumption of dairy products by making them available to school children at a reduced cost. It is, however, a matter for individual schools whether they want to participate in the scheme, and for parents or guardians of children in participating schools to decide whether they wish their child to participate.  Participation in the scheme by both schools and pupils has been declining for some time.  In order to understand the rationale behind falling uptake, my department recently surveyed primary and special schools and parents/guardians, to understand their attitudes towards the scheme.  These surveys closed on 10 January 2025 and responses are being considered.  I hope to be able to make a decision about my next steps in the coming months.  Finally, I was recently delighted to announce the release of Residual Milk Marketing Board legacy funds to the Dairy Council for Northern Ireland to promote the nutritional benefits of dairy products, including milk, in schools.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19062/22-27, for an update on the timescale for the introduction of legislation to ban the commercial sale of puppies and kittens under eight weeks old.
answer_text: I remain committed to regulating the sale of puppies and kittens in Northern Ireland. My officials have recently met with councils to obtain their feedback on the proposal, and it is my intention to launch a public consultation shortly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's plans to expand water sampling times at beaches to cover outside the official bathing season.
answer_text: My department recognises that there has been a marked increase in open water swimming with a considerable number of people accessing bathing waters all year.  Responses to the latest Bathing Water Review indicated overwhelming public support for an extension to the season. Given the significant policy change, it is important that all implications are considered.  This will take time, resources and further public consultation.  In this context, my department has begun the process of sampling outside of the bathing season to inform future policy direction. This data is published on the department’s website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on discussions with the Westminster Government to extend the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme.
answer_text: I remain very concerned about the impact that UK Immigration Policy changes have had on the ability of NI agri-food businesses to secure appropriate labour. Stakeholders continue to communicate the serious operational and financial impacts that inadequate labour supply is having, both at a business and sector level. The Migration Advisory Committee (MAC) undertook a self-commissioned review of the Seasonal Workers Visa last year, under the previous UK Government, which was published on 16th July 2024. DAERA provided a response as part of the MAC’s call for Evidence for the review. In DAERA’s response, it was highlighted that without the visa scheme, NI businesses would be in very difficult positions due to low levels of interest among domestic workers for these seasonal roles and automation not currently being a viable solution in their sectors. The main issue that was raised in the DAERA response was the permitted length of stay under the visa which is limited to 6 months. 		The MAC did not include a recommendation that the length of the visa should be extended, nor did they suggest the scope of the visa should be widened to include other industries. 	At the time of writing, the UK Government’s response to the MAC’s review remains outstanding therefore the longer-term future of the scheme is still unknown. 		Defra are in the process of developing an Agri-food Workforce Strategy, which is currently in the early scoping stages of development. DAERA will be involved in this process and will share further information on it, including opportunities for engagement with stakeholders, when it becomes available.		The Prime Minister has announced that the UK Government are planning to introduce an Immigration White Paper imminently, setting out details of a plan to reduce immigration. I await details of the White Paper and will consider the impact on the NI agri-food industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any cross-departmental work with the Department for Infrastructure on strategic flood management.
answer_text: I recognise that Northern Ireland’s fishing sector has faced several challenges in recent years, one of which has been to secure labour.  I wrote to the Defra Secretary of State in October, setting out the issues that are being faced by the fishing industry and the wider agri-food sector, and suggested modest changes to UK immigration policy to provide a resolution to these issues. The Defra Secretary of State rejected my recommendations and, with regards to the fishing sector, Minister Reed indicated that Defra would work with the seafood industry to understand concerns about labour shortages and ways to increase the attractiveness of seafood jobs to UK workers.At a meeting of the Inter-Ministerial Group for Environment Food and Rural Affairs on 18th November 2024, I reiterated to Minister Daniel Zeichner MP the labour shortage pressures that are faced by the fisheries and mushroom sectors.Challenges around access to labour are being faced by sectors across the economy and as such it has been agreed that my officials will work in collaboration with Department for Economy officials to develop an immigration policy position that will be agreed by the Executive and then sent to the Home Secretary. I met with representatives from the fishing industry on 13th February who have provided a paper outlining the fishing industry’s key asks. This paper will inform the development of the Executive papers and industry representatives have agreed that it can be used for this purpose.  As I have previously indicated, I am content to attend any possible meeting with the Home Office alongside the First Minister and deputy First Minister plus the Economy Minister. I will also continue to press access to labour issues concerning the agri-food sector with the UK Government.In addition to this, I am committed to working with the fishing industry to develop alternative workforce solutions such as apprenticeship schemes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 20735/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18072/22-27, and following confirmation of the extra budget for the Social Housing Development Programme, whether the October target of 1400 additional new social housing starts will be met.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive have advised that the current budget will allow for the 1400 new social housing starts target to be exceeded in this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21743/22-27, for an update on discussions with Trade Unions in relation to the teachers' pay dispute.
answer_text: Discussions are ongoing with NITC in an attempt to resolve the current pay dispute and the industrial action.Despite reaching a formal agreement with Management Side on the pay offer NITC is now requesting that further negotiations take place in an attempt to find a solution that is acceptable to teachers and school leaders.INTO, UTU and NEU are now engaging with their members to find a definitive understanding of the workload issue in schools. Management Side is continuing to engage with NITC in an attempt to resolve this industrial dispute, however, until the unions can explain what it is their members are requesting it is difficult to make progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his meeting with the National Pharmacy Association.
answer_text: I have accepted an invitation from the Chair of the National Pharmacy Association to meet with NPA. This meeting has been rescheduled to take place on 27 March 2025.
