question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her Department’s actions to equip workers with the skills needed to deliver the green energy transition.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Policy Exchange’s paper entitled The £2 Billion+ Cost of Legacy in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The First Minister and deputy First Minister have not jointly considered this paper.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the implications of her capital bid for £6 million for Translink Climate Action in the October Monitoring Round only receiving £0.5 million funding.
answer_text: As a result of additional funding not being allocated through the year for Climate Action, investment in the next phase of the Zero Emission Bus Programme was deferred as well as a number of track drainage and earthworks projects on the rail network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the implications of her £8.2 million bid for Railway Capital in the October Monitoring Round not being funded.
answer_text: My Department did not receive an allocation for railway capital in October Monitoring Round. As a result of the bid not being funded, some projects to renew or refurbish railway assets have been deferred or delayed to future financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the implications of his capital bid for £26.7 million for Education ICT Infrastructure in the October Monitoring Round only receiving £7.9 million funding.
answer_text: My Department submitted a capital bid for £26.7m for Education ICT infrastructure in the October monitoring round to cover pressures identified in the delivery of much needed ICT infrastructure, ICT operations and cyber security.  No capital funding was received in the October monitoring round.This bid was reduced from the October position to £7.9m in the January monitoring round to reflect what could be delivered in this financial year. My Department was allocated a £12.5m general capital allocation in the January monitoring round. My Department continues to prioritise spend across the many competing priorities for capital investment, to meet the most pressing needs, whilst remaining within budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the implications of his capital bid for £10.6 million for SEN Minor Works in the October Monitoring Round only receiving £3.8 million funding.
answer_text: My Department submitted a capital bid for £10.6m for Special Educational Needs Minor Works in the October monitoring round. No capital funding was received in the October monitoring round.This bid was reduced from the October position to £3.8m in the January monitoring round on the basis of need and deliverability.My Department was allocated a £12.5m general capital allocation in the January monitoring round. My Department continues to prioritise spend across the many competing priorities for capital investment, to meet the most pressing needs, whilst remaining within budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the implications of his capital bid for tackling rural poverty in the October Monitoring Round not being funded.
answer_text: At opening 2024-25 budget stage there was only sufficient Capital DEL available to the Executive to allow it to agree to fund inescapable commitments in DAERA and other departments at that time.  As the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme and the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme, which are part of the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) programme, were due to launch during 2024-25, I decided to progress this programme at the start of the year with the intention of securing additional Capital DEL at in-year Monitoring Rounds to help fund it.		I was successful in securing a £2.0 million allocation to this important programme at the October Monitoring Round.  In relation to the residual £2.4 million that remained unfunded at that time, this has subsequently been funded from within the wider DAERA Capital DEL budget as part of my Department’s work on the January Monitoring Round.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the implications of his capital bid for £53.3 million towards Investment in New Build and Housing for All in the October Monitoring Round not being funded.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 18593/22-27 which was answered on 17th December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the implications of his capital bids for climate change and green growth in the October Monitoring Round not being funded.
answer_text: At opening 2024-25 budget stage there was only sufficient Capital DEL available to the Executive to allow it to agree to fund inescapable commitments in DAERA and other departments at that time.  		Given the importance of the climate change and green growth programmes within my Department, I decided to progress with a number of these programmes with the intention of securing additional Capital DEL at in-year Monitoring Rounds to help fund them. 	Although it was not possible to secure additional funding for these bids as part of the October Monitoring Round, sufficient funding has been identified from within the wider DAERA Capital DEL budget to allow these projects to progress this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the implications of her capital bid for £30 million for NI Water in the October Monitoring Round not being fully funded.
answer_text: NI Water was allocated £19.5m in the October Monitoring Round, which represented nearly 50% of my Department’s capital budget awarded at that time.  This funding was specifically allocated to progress work to enable additional sewerage capacity for housing connections. As a result of this decision, NI Water is now taking forward projects which will lead to connections for some 2,300 properties across Antrim, Derry and Tyrone. NI Water has plans to undertake further investment on water and wastewater assets when additional funding is available. I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to make the case for extra investment in water infrastructure to improvements in this essential infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the work her Department is undertaking to improve the rural road network in and around Mountfield, West Tyrone.
answer_text: Unfortunately, as a result of budgetary constraints, my Department has had to introduce a limited service maintenance policy over the past number of years meaning that only the highest priority road defects are repaired. This underfunding has resulted in a deteriorating road network. The condition of the roads in the Mountfield area are reflective of the historical underinvestment in our road infrastructure and, unfortunately, the condition of these roads are similar to many rural roads in the area. With regards to roads maintenance, my Department is heavily reliant on in-year funding to carry out road repairs and react to deteriorating roads over the winter period. Unfortunately, due to other budgetary constraints no additional monies were able to be allocated to road maintenance activities from the recent January monitoring round.While there is no resurfacing possible at present, officials are aware of the junction of C0612 Lenagh Road and U1253 Inishclan Road which has been identified as a resurfacing priority due to heavy plant accessing the local quarry. This section is currently in a proposed programme to be considered when funding becomes available.In the meantime my Department will continue to complete their inspection of roads in the Mountfield area in line with our limited service policy and action any defects that meet the threshold for repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of convictions for water pollution offences in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The range of enforcement options available to NIEA includes not only prosecution but also formal warning letters, enforcement notices, cost recovery and the application of financial penalties for those in breach of Cross Compliance requirements. NIEA seeks to pursue appropriate enforcement action where a water pollution incident is of Medium or High Severity environmental impact and a polluter has been identified from investigation.The number of convictions for water pollution offences investigated by NIEA, based on the Court outcome date, over the last five years are outlined in Table 1.Table 1 Number of Convictions for Water Pollution Offences in Each of the Last Five YearsYearNumber of Convictions for Water Pollution20207202114202218202324202412	Three Adult Cautions were also accepted by alleged polluters in respect of 3 separate water pollution incidents. Whilst an Adult Caution is not a criminal conviction, the alleged polluter has to admit the offence and accept a caution. The Loughs Agency have powers under The Foyle Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1952 and The Foyle Fisheries Act 1952 in relation to water pollution investigations and would also have records in relation to convictions for water pollution offences within the same timeframe.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any support her Department is providing to encourage motorists in Northern Ireland to switch to electric vehicles.
answer_text: Switching fuels to low or zero emission alternatives is a key tenet of our approach to transport decarbonisation.  I believe that the North’s participation in the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes (VETS), from January 2025, is an important and positive step to encourage those that depend on vehicles for transport to switch away from fossil fuels.  Supporting this transition by further accelerating the number of zero emission vehicles entering the new car market will also build stock to enter the second-hand market, which provides additional avenues to make the switch to electric vehicles.The introduction of VETS also gives confidence to the private sector led charging infrastructure providers that the number of Electric Vehicles (EVs) in the North will continue to increase. Charge Point Operators’ investments in EV infrastructure and the subsequent expansion of the charging network will play a key part in providing the services that EV owners will require to seamlessly move away from petrol and diesel.My officials are also undertaking a range of actions and wider policy initiatives to support the transition to net zero vehicles and help meet targets set out in the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022. These include progressing work on cross pavement electric vehicle charging solutions.My officials also continue to engage with their counterparts from both the British Government’s Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV), and the South’s Department of Transport, on grants and funding available for EV Infrastructure in the north. With 1,000 workplace charge points now installed here through OZEV’s Workplace Charging Scheme, and 48 Sports Clubs availing of Shared Island Initiative funding for EV charge points, these provide additional options for charging to EV owners and further compliment the growing publicly accessible commercial charge point network.My Department uses social media pages, its internet presence, and NI Direct to share information with the public on electric vehicles. My officials are also working closely with the Consumer Council and other key stakeholders, involved with the EV Infrastructure Task Force, to make sure useful information is available to the public in relation to EV ownership. My officials continue to work with their counterparts from Britain’s Department for Transport and other Devolved Governments on the development of a communications strategy to combat misinformation and disinformation about EVs and promote a more positive narrative about the transition more generally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 21575/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 21575 /22-27 was answered on 17 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21742/22-27, to detail the longest length of time before a pension was issued in each of these years.
answer_text: 2021*20222023*2024*2025**Longest length of time before a pension was issued in calendar monthsNineNineNineSevenData not yet available* Retrospective award of Incapacity Benefits.**submission of a retirement application is due three months before retirement date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Ulster University's Economic Policy Centre report which highlights that there will be a shortage of 5,440 workers a year, which, if not addressed, could hold back economic growth.
answer_text: The Skills Barometer highlights the long-term challenge posed by our ageing population. The number of young people coming into the labour market is insufficient to meet the rising demand for jobs. The shortfall is such that an undersupply of people is anticipated across all skill levels.Brexit has further limited our ability to recruit from the EU, emphasising the importance of assisting individuals facing barriers to employment or training. Upskilling those who are out of work remains a vital solution.Ensuring that labour supply meets demand is crucial for driving economic growth and underscores the need to attract more individuals into the workforce. My Good Jobs agenda addresses this by encouraging employers to pay the Real Living Wage. This is also the primary aim of my Employment Bill, which I intend to bring forward shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20640/22-27, to detail the reasons these 47 candidates were allowed to resubmit their applications.
answer_text: The 47 candidates reported a technical issue before the interview deadline. They were reviewed and investigated on a case-by-case basis and the candidates were afforded another opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the guidance issued by his Department to schools in relation to entering children for exams; and (ii) the process for parents to appeal against a decision by a school not to enter their children for exams.
answer_text: (i) DE Circular 1999/06 sets out the legislative position of Article 136 of the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989, in relation to the obligation on schools to enter pupils for public examinations for which they have been prepared.There are certain circumstances in which the Board of Governors does not have to enter a particular pupil for such examinations. These are: if the Board of Governors decide that there are educational reasons why the pupil should not be entered for the examination and the pupil’s parent agrees; if the pupil’s parent requests in writing that he or she should not be entered; if the pupil has been entered for another public examination in another corresponding syllabus for which they were prepared by the same course of study. (ii) If parents have any concerns about the process that has been followed I would suggest that they follow the school’s complaints procedure and raise the matter with the school’s Board of Governors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the proportion of pupils not being entered into GCSE exams due to (i) poor attendance; and (ii) other reasons in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Department does not collect information on the number or proportion of pupils that are not entered into GCSE examinations each year by schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20823/22-27, for an update on when the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer November 2024 process will be completed and results compiled.
answer_text: Assessments of the interviews for this competition are still ongoing and candidate outcomes will not be known until all interviews have been assessed and results compiled. This is scheduled to be March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how she will spend the additional £8 million allocated to her Department following Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether 1921-1973 constituency maps held by PRONI can be made available to the public via NIDirect.
answer_text: PRONI holds maps detailing constituency boundaries dating from 1921 - 1973. However, the coverage is incomplete.The maps can be consulted onsite at PRONI, and copies of the maps can also be made by anyone carrying out research. However, there are no current plans to digitise the existing constituency boundary maps and make them available online via NI Direct.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the timescale for the introduction of the Public Health Bill.
answer_text: As advised in response to AQW 19569/22-27, the Department received approximately 8,200 submissions in response to an extended public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland. These responses are currently being analysed and will inform a consultation summary report, which will be published on the Department’s website. This report will assist officials to refine policy proposals over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the health and safety equipment bought though the motorsports sector grant will be stored and managed.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport Northern Ireland will award approximately £187,000 to 2&4 Wheel Motorsport Ltd. for the provision of safety equipment.  2&4 Wheel Motorsport Ltd. will manage the investment on behalf of their four affiliated governing bodies – Motorcycle Union of Ireland (MCUI), Association of Northern Ireland Car Clubs (ANICC), North of Ireland Karting Association (NIKA), and Motorcycle Racing Association (MRA).  Sport NI will support 2&4 Wheel Motorsport Ltd. to procure all equipment in line with NI public procurement policy.  2&4 Wheel Motorsport Ltd. will distribute equipment as per immediate investment priorities identified by each governing body.  The investment will be used to fund safety barriers/bales for MCUI and ANICC, chestnut fencing for MRA, and karting barriers for NIKA.   Through our award, Sport NI will ensure terms and conditions are in place which specify how equipment is to be stored and shared (if appropriate) amongst affiliated clubs and organisations.  Sport NI will also ensure appropriate tenure is in place (including access/rental agreements) for facilities where equipment will be stored.  Most of the equipment funded by Sport NI will be stored at Kirkistown Circuit, Bishopscourt Circuit, and dedicated storage facilities in Coleraine.  The chestnut fencing will be installed at several moto-x circuits throughout Northern Ireland”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

answer_text: For the period 21 December 2022 to 5 February 2025 inclusive, at total of 1088 patients were treated across the two Rapid Diagnosis Centre (RDC) sites.Of these 1088 patients, 507 were treated at the South Tyrone Hospital RDC and 581 patients were treated at the Whiteabbey Hospital RDC.The number of patients treated at the two Rapid Diagnosis Centres broken down by year is as follows:South Tyrone Hospital RDCWhiteabbey  Hospital RDC2022112023183156202427435920254965Total507581The constituency where each patient resided is not recorded during the referral or treatment process and therefore this data is not currently held.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the research carried out by Ulster University Economic Policy Centre and Dublin City University which shows that people here are much more likely to be out of work due to sickness or disability compared to people in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the All-Island Economic Intelligence produced by Ulster University Economic Policy Centre and Dublin City University and believe it will prove to be a very useful tool for making labour market comparisons across the island.I note the research shows that sickness accounts for around half of those aged 20-64 who are out of the labour market in the North, the so called economically inactive. I also note that this proportion has been increasing over time and I aim to address this. The research highlights the need for further upskilling and reskilling in the North. Although the Department for Communities has lead policy responsibility for employability and for reducing the number of people outside the labour market, my Department will continue to make an important contribution through our skills and education remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the targets contained within the Protect Life 2 - Suicide Prevention Strategy in light of NISRA's Suicide Statistics 2023.
answer_text: The previous reduction target was superseded by statistical work to reclassify suicide deaths which led to a reduction in the number of deaths. A new baseline will be set as part of the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan which is currently being finalised. The existing 10% reduction target will then apply to that revised baseline. I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions. A Protect Life 2 Progress Report for 2023-2024 has also been published and is available at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/protect-life-2-progress-report-update-2023-2024.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the funding required to complete the early actions contained on page 7 of the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda - Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: As set out in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda, costings will be finessed in detailed implementation plans and in collaboration with key delivery partners.An analysis of the current SEN financial model has identified opportunities to restructure existing resource towards more effective interventions and support, creating return on investment as well as opportunities for better outcomes for children and young people with SEN.  There are also opportunities to draw in additional investment. A bid has been made to the Interim NICS Transformation Board which, if successful, will enable us to commence the phased implementation of this long-term inclusive SEN Reform Agenda.  We will also seek opportunities within education and with other identified partners in Health and Economy, the voluntary and community and private sectors to jointly fund actions facilitating not only value for money, but a collaborative and child-centred approach to investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of NISRA’s Suicide Statistics 2023, which shows an 8 per cent increase on the previous year.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the devastating impact of each and every suicide, not only in the lost life but the impact on loved ones and communities. I believe every suicide is preventable up to the point of completion, so while we may set realistic targets, in reality the only acceptable number is zero. The NISRA statistics caution against inferences being drawn on one year changes. The 3 year rolling average of suicide deaths has remained relatively stable since 2017. It should also be noted that suicide statistics relate to year of registration by the coroner and not the number of deaths within that calendar year.The new Protect Life 2 Action Plan and accompanying implementation plan has recently been circulated to stakeholders for comment and the Department is currently considering comments received. This will enable further refinement. The new Action Plan will contain a wide range of actions to reduce the rate of suicide in Northern Ireland. The Lifeline 24/7 helpline remains available for anyone in distress or despair on 0808 808 8000. The Self harm Intervention Programme has recently been recommissioned and is being provided on a regional basis.I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why her Department failed to submit affidavit evidence by 17 January 2025 in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor court challenge, meaning the hearing scheduled for the first week in March 2025 may be delayed.
answer_text: At the review hearing on 3 December 2024, the parties agreed a very ambitious and condensed case management timetable to facilitate expedition of this complex and important legal challenge with a hearing scheduled for 3 March 2025.  The evidence which the Department considered to be necessary for the purposes of the case was extremely voluminous including, historical, technical, scientific and legal analysis.  To ensure the Department’s evidence before the Court comprehensively addresses all elements of this legal challenge so that the most robust defence is advanced, the Department required a short extension of time to finalise its affidavit evidence.The hearing has now been relisted for 18 March 2025 days. In agreeing the revised timetable, the Judge acknowledged that the original timetable was ambitious, noting  that the case involved complex issues, and commended the parties on their efforts to progress the case and also for their detailed revised case management timetable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16016/22-27, for an update on his Department's commission of research and analysis of demand for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder services to ensure regional consistency across Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Department of Health has recently commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  This work has now commenced, with an estimated completion date of end June 2025.The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there was a service level agreement as part of Project Stratum in relation to the reconnection of customers following a severe weather event.
answer_text: The contract for Project Stratum was awarded to Fibrus Networks Limited for the deployment of broadband services on a wholsale access basis. The minimum service requirements that Fibrus Networks must adhere to in providing wholesale services to Retail Service Providers (RSPs) are set out in the Project Stratum contract. Requirements include that the contractor provides RSPs with 99.5% network availability, and that problems are resolved within two working days, following the relevant NICC (National Interoperability Consultative Committee) standard.	Where these requirements are not met, an RSP is entitled to compensation under a scheme that must follow Good Industry Practice and any requirements from the regulator. Fibrus Networks’ compensation scheme is available on its website at https://hyperfastni.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/Wholesale-Access-Service-Level-Agreement-v2.0.pdf. Customers who have experienced an outage in their broadband service should contact their Retail Service Provider. Unresolved complaints can go through an Ofcom-approved alternative dispute resolution service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why his Department will not be meeting its legally binding active travel targets; and (ii) any plans he has to address this situation.
answer_text: My Department is committed to increasing investment in active travel in order to meet the requirements of the Climate Change Act and have been developing plans to support the increased investment since the introduction of the Act.This work has included the development of an Active Travel Delivery Plan. When complete, this plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan (2022) and the Strategic Plan for Greenways (2016) to provide a firm basis for future prioritisation of active travel infrastructure investment and delivery. Public consultation on the Active Travel Delivery Plan is ongoing, and I would encourage the public to engage with the consultation before the closing date of 28 February 2025. My Department has also taken a number of important steps to increase capacity to support the future delivery of active travel projects, including filling key posts within the Active Travel team, and increasing supply chain capacity to facilitate an increased delivery pipeline. Ramping up an ambitious programme of investment of this nature will take years and careful planning is essential to ensure that the investment is well spent. My Department is working on the basis of increasing overall active travel investment to meet our Climate Change Act obligations by 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16016/22-27, whether there is a mechanism for Health and Social Care Trusts who do not take adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder referrals to (i) commission the service from another Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) refer a patient to another  Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is no commissioned ADHD service for adults, therefore there is no process for referrals from one Heath and Social Care Trust to another Health and Social Care Trust. Regarding provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), assessment and diagnosis has grown across Trusts.All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, it will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the timetable for her delivery plan for justice reform.
answer_text: The Enabling Access to Justice Programme Delivery Plan I published on 30 January sets out my priorities for the remainder of the mandate. The Plan is subject to consultation and sequencing may be subject to change. I also expect the programme will be refined over time as new evidence emerges from consultations and concept testing initiatives, but I have set out a deliberately ambitious and wide-ranging  plan because I am committed to progressing at pace during the remainder of the Assembly to deliver better outcomes and make better use of resources. That of course will not be the end of the journey. Widescale reform will not be achieved in the short-term, but I believe the programme I have initiated will help set the framework for delivery of a fairer, more accessible, proportionate, responsive and cost-effective system which places the citizen at its heart.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her meeting with the GB Safeguarding Minister in relation to safeguarding and addressing violence against women and girls.
answer_text: I recently met with the UK Government’s Minister for Safeguarding and Violence Against Women and Girls, Jess Phillips MP, and used the occasion to update her on key developments in relation to safeguarding and Justice reform in Northern Ireland.I took the opportunity to highlight the launch of the Power to Change campaign, which I had attended earlier that day, as well as my priorities since taking office. We discussed the progress that has been made in Northern Ireland on a range of issues including Sexual Offences Legal Advisors (SOLAs), Domestic Homicide Reviews (DHRs), the introduction of Remote Evidence Centres, domestic abuse and stalking legislation, and offences such as upskirting and downblousing. I also reiterated my strong belief in the need to move upstream to focus on prevention and early intervention before individuals fall into the justice system, noting that this is the responsibility of all Executive Departments.A key theme of our discussion was the importance of cross-jurisdictional information sharing and the exchange of best practice to ensure a consistent and effective approach to tackling serious issues such as domestic abuse. I also observed that there is much that my Department hopes to learn from the ongoing pilot in England to test Domestic Abuse Prevention Notices and Orders. We agreed on the need to maintain regular contact on these matters.Finally, we briefly discussed the legislative consent process an the need for early engagement where we may wish to be included in provisions for which legislation is being developed in England and Wales. This meeting reinforced the shared commitment across jurisdictions to tackling these issues, and I look forward to continued engagement with Minister Philips to drive meaningful change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on discussions with Trade Unions in relation to the teachers' pay dispute
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm that following detailed formal negotiations Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (“TNC”) made a formal written offer to the Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council (“NITC”) on Friday 31 January 2025.Management Side’s formal Teachers’ Pay 2024-25 offer includes;In addition to the incremental progression within salary scales which is payable by contractual entitlement, teacher salary scale points will be revalorised as follows:A 5.5% cost of living award for all teachers will be paid from 1 September 2024.Teaching and Special Needs Allowances will be increased by 5.5% paid from 1 September 2024.The pay award and allowance increases will cost £48.5m in 2024/25 with £83m annual recurring cost thereafter.In addition to a formal pay offer, Management Side has outlined measures that they are committed to progressing in conjunction with the NITC to address workload. In so doing we wish to bring the current pay dispute to an end and ensure our children and young people experience a sustained period of stability free from industrial action, including action short of strike.To underpin the delivery of a long-term, sustained period of stability NITC has been invited to:Agree that industrial action should only be taken as a last resort in any dispute, and therefore, alongside Management Side, commit to act in line with the Code of Practice: Industrial Action Ballots and Notice to Employers at all times.Commit to a period free from Industrial action and the threat of industrial action to allow for this agreement to be fully implemented.Work jointly with Management Side to implement the 2024/25 pay agreement and the delivery of the agreed programme of work, within the context of the Jordanstown Agreement.NITC will now require time to take the appropriate steps to ascertain their members’ views on the offer. I and Management Side of TNC hope that the teachers will approve the offer so that an agreement can be ratified and implemented as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the funding levels for the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.
answer_text: The Sustainable Agriculture Programme is funded from both Capital DEL and Resource DEL funding. In 2024/25 Capital DEL has been allocated by the Executive to DAERA for this programme and Resource DEL funding is currently from the ‘HMT Earmarked’ budget for agriculture, agri-environment and rural development. In light of the change in treatment from 2025/26 as a result of the Chancellor’s Budget on 30 October 2024, I formally asked the Minister of Finance that the £332.5 million Resource DEL that has been baselined in the block grant is treated as ‘Executive Earmarked’ funding for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development purposes. On 19 December 2024 the Executive agreed to my request to earmark this funding to my Department as part of the wider agreement on the Draft Budget for 2025/26 that is undergoing consultation. This is the main source of funding for the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.The Executive also agreed a £12.3 million Capital DEL Earmarked allocation for a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture as part of its Draft Budget. I shall be making the case for further allocations to this fund in future public expenditure exercises. In addition, I propose to allocate £13.9 million from my Department’s Draft Budget Capital DEL allocation for 2025/26 for this programme. This work will be progressed through the Sustainable Agriculture Programme and the £26.2 million Capital DEL total is subject to the outcome of the Executive’s agreed Final Budget for 2025/26.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to set out the timetable for the consideration of the Policing Board independent review.
answer_text: The report was received by my Department on 22 January 2025 and was published on the 30 January 2025.  I will now take the time to consider the report’s findings and afford the Policing Board and the Chief Constable time to consider the recommendations relevant to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail progress on delivering the 40 recommendations of the Chief Veterinary Officer’s review of bovine TB.
answer_text: The Chief Veterinary Officer’s (CVO) review of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland was published in November 2024 and can be viewed at:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/chief-veterinary-officer-review-bovine-tuberculosis-northern-ireland-november-2024 At that time, I agreed with the CVO’s recommendation to immediately establish a new TB Partnership Steering Group (TBPSG), with its first task being to assist in the development of a new bTB delivery plan.  The first meeting of the TBPSG took place on Wednesday 29 January 2025.I have also written to Ministerial colleagues across the UK and Ireland to seek their support for the CVO’s proposal to enhance CVO North-South and East-West co-operation on bTB.I have requested advice on the content of the new bTB delivery plan by the end of March 2025.  It is my intention that this plan, which will draw upon the CVO’s proposals alongside actions within the 2022 bTB Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland, will clearly set out our priorities to be taken forward in the time ahead to tackle the unacceptable levels of bTB in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 20917/22-27, whether their Department has taken a position in relation to the Secretary of State’s decision.
answer_text: The decision made by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland does not require The Executive Office to take a position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when the National Joint Council pay award 2024-25 will be paid.
answer_text: My Department has now approved the Housing Executive’s pay remits for 2024-25 and I understand the Housing Executive is moving to implement these with a likely date for pay of March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of research published by Ulster University, entitled a Profile of Classroom Assistants in Northern Ireland, that includes some respondents to the survey that indicates that classroom assistants feel that they are poorly paid and treated as dogsbodies in schools.
answer_text: I welcome the contribution of the research carried out by Ulster University to the wider Special Educational Needs reform programme. In particular, I value the opportunity provided to classroom assistants to share their views and experiences of the education system.In my opening remarks at the launch of the report on 28 January, I highlighted the integral role played by classroom assistants in supporting teachers and ensuring that every child has access to the best possible learning experience. I also emphasised the need to critically evaluate our current practices and reconsider the traditional approach of one-to-one classroom assistant support.  Evidence suggests that collaborative approaches, where classroom assistants work with small groups or in partnership with teachers, can be more effective in promoting engagement and learning.  Some of our schools are already seeing the benefits of these approaches to children and we must make these opportunities more widely available as we continue to be guided by best practice and evidence on what works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of mothers giving birth in the maternity ward at the South West Acute Hospital that have required emergency surgery or transfer to other hospitals after giving birth since December 2022, broken down by month.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they had no mothers who after giving birth in the maternity ward at the South West Acute Hospital required transfer out to other hospitals for emergency surgery, since Dec 2022.It is however noted that there are mothers that are transferred to other hospitals after birth but this is because their baby is transferred to a neonatal unit and they are transferred to be close to their baby.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Heritage Skills Apprenticeships scheme will be an annual recurring scheme.
answer_text: The Heritage Skills Apprenticeships scheme is not an annual recurring scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the legal implications of not allowing his officials access to ground that has been vested as part of the A5 construction project.
answer_text: On 25 November 2024, the Vesting Orders from north of Sion Mills to Ballygawley became operative and lands transferred into the ownership of my Department. As such, my Department has full access rights. My officials, nevertheless, wish to work collaboratively with all former landowners and will listen carefully to any concerns they may have and accommodate all reasonable requests as far as practicable.  Each affected landowner should receive communication from the relevant construction contractor and are advised to speak with them in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will review his Department's processes to respond to incidents such as Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: My Department formally activated its Major Emergency Response Plan (MERP) in response to the ‘Red’ weather warning issued by the Met Office at 09:42 a.m. on Thursday 23 January in relation to Storm Éowyn.The policy dictates that any activation of the MERP necessitates a review into the response in order to identify learning that could be helpful in future civil contingency events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the mutual aid agreement between NIE Networks and English distribution companies; and (ii) whether that agreement and other contingencies will be reviewed following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Mutual aid to NIE Networks comes via the NEWSAC agreement (North East South West Area Consortium) which is an enabling protocol for the transfer of resources between electricity network operators in the Britain, North and South of Ireland, and the Isle of Man (including National Grid). The members of the consortium are all private companies, and the people involved are their employees.The NEWSAC assistance agreement is exceptionally helpful in providing support to customers off power and there is a long history of assistance via the agreement. The consortium responds to the requests made from its members in an emergency and allocates the resources that are available to be shared according to the needs of the members dealing with the emergency.  During Storm Éowyn, NIE Networks requested and obtained significant resource support via the NEWSAC process, including operational teams, mobile generators and helicopters. NIE Networks has confirmed that mutual aid support will augment local operatives by around ten per cent.	NIE Networks has a systematic process for preparing for major weather events, for managing them, and for reviewing them after the event occurs. My Department will liaise with NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator after it has conducted its post major incident review for Storm Éowyn and will respond to any learnings or requests arising from that review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will review her Department's processes to respond to incidents such as Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The Department regularly reviews its response processes, including the Major Emergency Response Plan, and Business Continuity Plans, in order to ensure they remain effective, current, and fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) funding allocated; and (ii) money spent on the (a) Housing Renovation Grant; (ii) House Replacement Grant; and (iii) Home Repair Assistance Grant in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided the following information:“Please see the tables below which detail the funding allocation and actual expenditure of each grant in each of the last five years:Funding Allocated (Budget)Discretionary Grant 2020/21*2021/22*2022/23*2023/24*2024/25** £K£K£K£K£KHousing Renovation Grant1,3341,5001,491934327House Replacement Grant10214014514922Home Repair Assistant Grant26030023213265Total Budget1,6961,9401,8681,215414* closing budget allocation** current budget allocationActual Expenditure (including accruals)Discretionary Grant 2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25 YTD*** £K£K£K£K£KHousing Renovation Grant1,5321,4191,124575202House Replacement Grant52112123(1)Home Repair Assistant Grant268242178549Total Expenditure1,8521,7731,314632210***Year to date (YTD) expenditure from April 2024 to December 2024.Please note, from 2022/23 financial year to date, budget was allocated by DfC for prior year carried forward financial commitments (i.e. to cover approvals from previous years). No new approvals were permitted in these financial years and funding could not be redirected to new approvals.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on when (i) Strabane Primary School; and (ii) Our Lady of Lourdes Primary School, Greencastle, will reopen.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that both Strabane Primary School and Our Lady of Lourdes Primary School, Greencastle, reopened on Tuesday 28 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the Education Authority processes in place to deal with incidents such as Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has in place its Major Emergency Plan, Business Continuity Plans and Severe Weather Protocol for incidents such as Storm Éowyn. These are developed to provide guidance and consistency to EA senior management and services in both business continuity and emergency response during periods of severe weather.The EA moved swiftly to activate its Major Emergency Plan once the Met Office Red Warning was received on 23 January, with a Tactical Co-ordination Group (TCG), comprising of key service area leads including maintenance, catering, ICT, transport, education and communications, activated immediately. The TCG continued to meet multiple times per day during and after Storm Éowyn to ensure an effective, co-ordinated and pupil-centric response.These processes proved effective in minimising, as far as possible, disruption to education service provision and in restoring normal operations as soon as possible. The EA actively reviews its emergency arrangements, Business Continuity Plans and procedures. As part of the Major Emergency Plan activation, a full internal debrief will be performed and the EA will participate in multi-agency debriefs. Therefore, I have no plans to initiate a review of EA processes to deal with future incidents such as Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to introduce the right for journalists to report from family courts, similar to England and Wales.
answer_text: I am of the view that permitting media reporting of some proceedings concerning children would be a helpful initiative to enhance public understanding of, and confidence in, the important work of the family courts.  However, I am also mindful such cases involve the most sensitive and personal matters, so there would need to be safeguards put in place to protect the privacy of the children and families involved.My officials have already undertaken scoping work, including looking at the judiciary-led Reporting Pilot in England and Wales.  Further to that work, I intend to discuss this matter with the Lady Chief Justice shortly, in the context of a wider discussion regarding what we can both do to enhance the openness and transparency of our justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will review the NIE Networks' processes in place to deal with incidents such as Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: NIE Networks advises that it has a robust Emergency Plan. This process is reviewed annually and after major incidents.  NIE Networks shares its learnings with relevant stakeholders. Lessons learned from internal reviews of the NIE Networks response to Storm Isha (January 2024) and Storm Darragh (December 2024) have also informed the NIE Networks Storm Éowyn emergency response. My Department participated in the major task force that was put in place during and after Storm Éowyn, coordinating with the significant work done by NIE Networks and its dedicated staff, to restore power to the most number of customers, and the most vulnerable, as quickly as possible.My Department will liaise with the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks after NIE Networks has conducted its post major incident review for Storm Éowyn and will respond to any learnings or requests coming from that review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will make all Ordnance Survey Northern Ireland (OSNI) admin boundary maps public again for free via NIDirect, including (i) post-1973 maps held by OSNI; and (ii) 1921-1973 maps held by PRONI.
answer_text: The post-1973 administrative boundaries referred to (i.e., Local Government District boundaries, Wards Boundaries, and Parliamentary Constituencies boundaries), and held by Ordnance Survey are already available under Open Government License (OGL) via the Open Data NI portal, Spatial NI, and/or through direct contact with OSNI.The 1921-1973 maps held by PRONI are not within OSNI’s remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to review and reform the Education and Training Inspectorate.
answer_text: I have no plans to review and reform the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the Education and Training Inspectorate budget in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) is a unitary inspectorate which provides independent inspection services and policy advice on all aspects of education and training provision for the Department of Education, Department for the Economy  and other commissioning departments. There is no separate resource budget allocated for ETI since it is included within the Department’s overall resource budget allocations.  However, actual expenditure incurred by ETI is identified and reported on an annual basis.  Resource expenditure in respect of the last five years is as follows: Financial yearResource Expenditure £m2019-20£5,6862020-21£5,6132021-22£5,4222022-23£5,2002023-24£5,461

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 20739/22-27, for his assessment of the statement from the Planning Appeals Commissioner criticising his planning department for failing to follow its own legislative procedures.
answer_text: Although disappointed at the Planning Appeals Commission decision to suspend the Public Local Inquiry, the Department acknowledge the concerns of the public and Fermanagh and Omagh District Council. The Department accepts that there was no alternative but to suspend the Inquiry until further consultation takes place. The Department acknowledges that the principle of public participation should be fully upheld. Before any resumption, to ensure the inquiry proceeds efficiently and to avoid any further potential delays, as a referring authority my department will be asking that the PAC to include a list of sub-topics or issues in the timetable.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when a Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland will be appointed.
answer_text: We are pleased to report that the competition to appoint a Commissioner for Public Appointments for NI (CPANI) launched on 10 March.  The competition is continuing to progress as planned, with interviews scheduled to take place in forthcoming weeks.  A Commissioner will be appointed as soon as possible, following the conclusion of the appointment process. Public appointments continue to be made in the intervening period and the CPANI Code of Practice continues to apply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the level of funding per capita of the rural population by Rural Community Transport Partnership, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold annual figures on rural population levels within Rural Community Transport Partnership areas. Funding for the Dial-a-Lift transport service is not awarded on the basis of population in any specific area.  The Dial-a-Lift scheme is only open to those who meet the membership criteria for their respective Rural Community Transport Partnership.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the amount spent on school crossing patrols; and (ii) the number of school crossing patrols, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority has provided the table below detailing the amount spent on School Crossing Patrols (SCP) and the number of SCPs in each of the last five years.Financial Year19/2020/2121/2222/2323/24Annual Cost1£2.862m£2.822m£2.757m£2.759m£2.955mAverage number of SCPs2-4457449431388365These figures include annual pay awards which have varied from 2.3% in 2021/22 to 12.28% in 2023/24. The number of SCPs in any given year varies and therefore the EA has provided the average for each year. SCPs are automatically replaced within a 2 year period, , eg. if someone vacates the post, however if the period is longer a new assessment must be completed to consider any relevant changes within that time. Assessments  are based on the criteria as set out in the Road Safety GB’s ‘School Crossing Patrol Service Guidelines.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the funding for the Critical Incident Response Team in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There is no earmarked resource funding for the Education Authority (EA) Critical Incident Response Team (CIRT).  There has been no funding allocated from the Department in each of the last five years.Funding was provided in 2018/19 and 2019/20 to support development and training of the CIRT.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how his Department is supporting tourism initiatives in the (a) Omagh area; and (b) Strabane area; and (ii) the amount of money spent in both areas in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) how his Department is supporting tourism initiatives in the: 		Omagh area and b) Strabane area	My Department is committed to achieving greater regional economic balance and tourism is vital in delivering this.  The Omagh and Strabane areas form part of a broader visitor destination which incorporates the Sperrins and which span the Fermanagh and Omagh (FODC), and Derry City and Strabane District Council (DCSDC) areas. Tourism NI collaborates with FODC, DCSDC and other partners as part of a shared approach to developing tourism in the area. Tourism NI is represented on the Sperrins Partnership Board and several sub-groups focused on developing aspects of the tourism offer.  In 2023, Tourism NI part-funded a Visitor Experience Development Plan for the Fermanagh and Omagh area which sets out the area’s tourism priorities over the next decade.  Most recently, Tourism NI has provided advice and input to the development of the emerging Sperrins AONB Management Plan.Tourism NI is also leading or supporting a range of initiatives to develop the area’s tourism potential including:Engaging with Outdoor Tourism businesses in the area, in conjunction with Ireland’s Association for Adventure Tourism, to develop and market their offer.  Supporting businesses to attend international events and trade shows and delivering Tourism NI led events such as Meet the Buyer, thereby connecting operators with international buyers.  Working with local businesses such as Far and Wild to improve recruitment and retention in the sector as part of the Make It Here campaign. Actively promoting the Sperrins Sculpture Trail, including sculptures located in the Omagh area.Supporting proposals to redevelop the Ulster American Folk Park in Omagh in order to maximise its visitor appeal as part of the Mid South West Growth Deal. Initiatives to expand the offer to visitors and help business to grow are complemented by Tourism NI’s suite of marketing, communications and PR activities. For instance, as part of Tourism NI’s seasonal marketing campaigns, there are weeks of coverage dedicated to Fermanagh and Omagh, and Derry City and Strabane council areas. The area benefits too from Tourism NI’s close partnership with Tourism Ireland encompassing the hosting of media and influencers from key international markets, and a wider programme of marketing and PR activity including social media, email marketing, and influencer marketing.  	(ii) the amount of money spent in both areas in each of the last five years.The table below summarises Tourism NI spend over the last five financial years for those activities where spend is able to be allocated to an individual Council area. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the 1840 redundancies in 2024, broken down by (i) company; and (ii) constituency.
answer_text: The number of redundancies broken down by constituency is routinely published on the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency’s (NISRA) website and can be accessed in Table 3 at the following link: https://www.nisra.gov.uk/system/files/statistics/lmr-redundancy-tables-january-2025.xlsxNISRA provide assurance to employers providing redundancy information that their individual returns will be processed in confidence. As such it is not possible to provide a breakdown of redundancies by company.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency Labour Market Statistics for January 2025, which suggests a 0.3 per cent drop in employee numbers in December 2024.
answer_text: In December 2024 the number of payrolled employees in the North was 803,000. 		While this has grown at a stronger rate post-pandemic than in England, Scotland and Wales, the December figure does represent a 0.3% decline from the previous month’s count. 	It is too early to definitively conclude what factors may have driven this, though recent local business intelligence has indicated that the increases in employment costs arising from the Autumn Statement could have a negative impact on some firms’ staffing and hiring intentions. 	I and my Officials will continue to keep key economic indicators and business trading conditions under review and liaise with local business stakeholders in the context of my Economic Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of research by Confused.com and Esure which states that 20mph speed limit zones reduce car Insurance costs; and (ii) whether his Department will fund more 20mph speed limit zones.
answer_text: Officials from my Department are aware of the statements made by the car insurance industry, and if the reduced claim frequency in areas with 20mph speed limits indicates a lower level of collisions then this is to be welcomed. During my time in office, I would like to see what can be done to improve our attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but also to make places cleaner and greener. As part of my consideration of the draft of the recently published review of the part-time 20mph speed limits at schools I asked my officials to prepare a paper on 20mph speed limits in general. This will focus on schools, residential areas and places where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.The review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools is available on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools  The review report recommends that the Department should provide a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work updating our policy document is underway.  Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools will be included within budget planning considerations for the next financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19837/22-27, to detail the number of patients that picked up a pseudomonas infection in a hospital in the last three years, broken down by (i) hospital; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Avian Influenza Prevention Zone.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department is currently implementing a number of measures to ensure the poultry sector in Northern Ireland (NI) is prepared and protected against the potential of any highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) incursion here.My officials have been undertaking a comprehensive communications campaign across DAERA social media channels and other external media to highlight the three key messages of reporting dead wild birds, registering kept birds and the utmost importance of biosecurity. The key messages have been disseminated to stakeholders, local councils and other public bodies including NICS Departments.  Furthermore, detailed biosecurity guidance and a checklist has been provided on the DAERA website. In addition, as of 12.00 noon on Saturday 18 January 2025, I took the decision to introduce an Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) in Northern Ireland following confirmation of HPAI H5N1 in three wild birds. The AIPZ places a legal requirement on all bird keepers in Northern Ireland to follow strict biosecurity measures.  This applies if you keep pet birds, commercial flocks or just a few birds in a backyard or hobby flock.  A copy of the AIPZ declaration which details the requirements that must be adhered to can be found on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/disease-control-zone-declarations My officials continue to work closely with their counterparts across the UK and the Republic of Ireland to monitor the situation to determine next steps as appropriate. The last case of avian influenza in kept birds in NI was confirmed during October 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
