question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the amount of funding his Department allocated to reinstate the GCSE Motor Vehicle and Road User Studies; and (ii) the number of years the funding is in place.
answer_text: I have allocated £25k capital and £11k resource to reinstate the GCSE Motor Vehicle and Road User Studies this financial year. The funding is for this financial year only as budgets for future years have not been agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the action his Department is taking to increase the number of schools offering the GCSE Motor Vehicle and Road User Studies.
answer_text: It is a matter for each school to decide which qualifications to deliver to meet the needs, interests and aspirations of their pupils. I recognise however that it is important for our young people to be educated on the dangers that they can face as road users, particularly as they become new drivers. The “learning for life and work” area of learning requires that pupils in all post-primary schools should have opportunities to develop preventative strategies in relation to accidents in the home, school and on the road.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Secretary of State's decision in relation to Schedule 6B of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, as outlined in his correspondence to the Speaker on 20 January 2024.
answer_text: The rationale for the Secretary of State’s decision is detailed in his correspondence to the Speaker of the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 19329/22-27, whether the platform provider reported a power outage to their system for a period of time during the pre-recorded interview window.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 19329/22-27, the interview platform provider confirmed that there was no power outage to their system during the pre-recorded interview windows.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is taking to mitigate risk following the foot and mouth outbreak in Germany.
answer_text: The emergence of Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD) in Germany is of significant concern, not only for our livestock owners but also for the entire agricultural sector across the UK, Republic of Ireland and the EU.On notification of the outbreak in Germany my officials immediately carried out a tracing exercise to identify if any susceptible species, germplasm or products of animal origin had been imported to Northern Ireland from Germany within an established risk window.Furthermore, portal controls in line with the requirements laid down in legislation are implemented at points of entry to Northern Ireland.  These controls include identity and physical checks on consignments and the screening of passenger luggage and parcels arriving through airports.  In addition, DAERA implement a system of certification verification to ensure that animals and products arriving here have been certified in line with the required animal and public health attestations.My Department has well established robust disease control contingency plans in place to manage the risk of disease incursions and protect our farmers and food security. The FMD response plan outlines clear procedures for responding to suspect cases, including rapid diagnostic testing, protocols to restrict animal movements and the establishment of disease control zones to mitigate for onward spread of the disease. This plan was tested extensively during a UK national exercise in 2018. Communications reminding livestock keepers of the necessity of adhering to high levels of biosecurity are being regularly disseminated. In addition, veterinary practitioners and livestock keepers have been urged to remain alert to any unusual symptoms and report these immediately to a veterinarian or directly to DAERA.Officials are maintaining close and frequent communication with their counterparts across these islands to facilitate a comprehensive response. Finally, I updated my Ministerial colleagues at the Executive meeting on 16 January 2025 on the developments in Germany and informed all MLAs via a Written Ministerial Statement published 17 January 2025. This statement is available at https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/globalassets/documents/official-reports/written-ministerial-statements/2022---2027/bv169_daera-17012025.pdf and provides further information on the actions taken to date and future steps to be taken.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the recruitment processes for staff undertaken by the Health and Social Care NI; and (ii) how this process differs from that of the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: Recruitment to posts within HSC is undertaken through a partnership approach between hiring HSC organisations, as the employer, and the Recruitment Shared Services Centre, based in the Business Services Organisation, who manage a centralised comprehensive transactional recruitment service to all HSC organisations.  While this model has traditionally focused on individual competitions for specific posts, the HSC has recently moved to more centralised recruitment for some professions such as regional recruitments for nursing and midwifery and social work staff.The Department of Finance has policy responsibility for recruitment to the Northern Ireland Civil Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is taking to mitigate risk following a case of Avian Influenza near Dungannon.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department is currently implementing a number of measures to ensure the poultry sector in Northern Ireland (NI) is prepared and protected against the potential of any highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) incursion here.My officials have been undertaking a comprehensive communications campaign across DAERA social media channels and other external media to highlight the three key messages of reporting dead wild birds, registering kept birds and the utmost importance of biosecurity. The key messages have been disseminated to stakeholders, local councils and other public bodies including NICS Departments.  Furthermore, detailed biosecurity guidance and a checklist has been provided on the DAERA website. In addition, as of 12.00 noon on Saturday 18 January 2025, I took the decision to introduce an Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) in Northern Ireland following confirmation on Wednesday 15 January 2025 of HPAI H5N1 in a sampled collected from a wild goose found near Black Lough, Dungannon.  Furthermore, on Friday 17 January 2025 two further wild birds have tested positive for HPAI H5N1.  One was a buzzard found near Moira and the other a whooper swan in Portballintrae. The AIPZ places a legal requirement on all bird keepers in Northern Ireland to follow strict biosecurity measures.  This applies if you keep pet birds, commercial flocks or just a few birds in a backyard or hobby flock.  A copy of the AIPZ declaration which details the requirements that must be adhered to can be found on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/disease-control-zone-declarations My officials continue to work closely with their counterparts across the UK and the Republic of Ireland to monitor the situation to determine next steps as appropriate. The last case of avian influenza in kept birds in NI was confirmed during October 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 19329/22-27, whether those figures include the recruitment processes in November 2024.
answer_text: The figures provided in response to AQW 19329/22-27 refer to the Department for Communities DWP AO competition (IRC298527). They do not include figures from the NICS Administrative Officer 2024 competition (IRC303169) for interviews which commenced in November 2024. Assessments of the interviews for this competition are still ongoing and candidate outcomes will not be known until all interviews have been assessed and results compiled.  This is likely to be February/March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why the legislative procedures in relation to consultations with the Irish Government on the goldmine planning application in West Tyrone were not followed.
answer_text: Whilst my Department did carry out transboundary consultation specifically with Donegal County Council, officials are now carrying out further transboundary consultation with the Irish Government as the EEA State, which will allow the public in the south the opportunity to have their say and express their views on the project prior to the resumption of the public local inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Royal College of Nursing report on corridor care, published on the 16 January 2025; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to reduce corridor care.
answer_text: I welcome the ‘On the Frontline of the UK’s Corridor Care Crisis’ report, published on 16 January 2025 by the Royal College of Nursing (RCN).  The insights gained from across the United Kingdom highlight the profound challenges facing our health and social care system. I acknowledge the issues which this report has highlighted and am conscious of the increased demand for care.  I recognise that there can be no quick solution to the issues which have been raised.  My Department will work collectively with the RCN and other stakeholders across the health and social care system to proactively seek solutions to address those issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Western Health and Social Care Trust has incorporated a dementia-friendly design into its submitted proposal for the redevelopment of the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: My ability to take forward the redevelopment of the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital is dependent on the capital budget allocation my Department receives. The proposed capital allocation for my Department in 2025/26 is £391 million and does not provide the funding required to progress new capital investment, unless the final budget settlement provides an increased allocation.  The Trust will be invited to submit a business case for this project once budget affordability is confirmed. When the necessary expenditure approvals have been secured, an Integrated Consultancy Team will be appointed to lead on the design of the new build. During the Design Stage, the Trust will consider the needs of all users of the new facility, including the needs of people with dementia.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18147/22-27, and following his visit and statement on Altnagelvin Emergency Department, when he will identify funds within his capital budget for a new Emergency Department.
answer_text: My ability to take forward the redevelopment of the Emergency Department is dependent on the capital budget allocation my Department receives. This project is currently unaffordable based on the level of funding indicated for my Department in the Draft Capital Budget for 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 13961/22-27, to detail the proportion of patients with Alzheimer's disease that have their assessment within the nine-week target, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Assembly Commission whether it plans to implement the recommendations contained in the letter to MLAs from the Clerk/Chief Executive on 14 January 2025.
answer_text: In her letter to all Members on 14 January 2025, the Clerk/Chief Executive outlined that she was considering the recommendations of the review and that she intended to introduce a number of new administrative measures to implement them. The Clerk/Chief Executive also set out that where any changes are required to the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Determination), she would be recommending these to the Assembly Commission.  The Clerk/Chief Executive has informed the Assembly Commission that she will be bringing a paper to a future Assembly Commission meeting once she has completed consideration of the recommendations.  The Assembly Commission will then consider the recommendations of the Clerk/Chief Executive in relation to any actions it is required to take, particularly changes to the Determination.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 19329/22-27, how many of the 446 candidates were allowed to resubmit their applications.
answer_text: Of the 446 participants, 47 were afforded an opportunity to resubmit their applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Economic and Social Research Institute and the National Institute of Economic and Social Research report on Modelling Northern Ireland within the context of the all-island economy, which suggests GDP growth in Northern Ireland is to average 1.2 per cent per year in the medium term, compared to 2.2 per cent for the all-island economy over the same time period.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of this report and its potential to improve our understanding of the economic linkages between the North and South. The short-term performance of the local economy is projected to be positive, surpassing the British economy during the same period before returning to more modest growth over the medium term. The report remarks on the link between that relatively robust short-term growth and comparatively strong cross-border trade, highlighting the importance of the all-island economy to our local businesses and economic prospects. As it delivers the Economic Vision, my Department is focused on growing trade on an all-island basis and with other trading partners. By focusing on productivity, good jobs, regional balance and decarbonisation my aim is to bolster long term growth in a manner that is sustainable and benefits all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 19329/22-27 and AQW 14034/22-27, and given that 873 candidates have been affected in the last two years, for her assessment of the current processes in relation to the pre-recorded interview stage for applicants.
answer_text: For clarification, the total number of participants unsuccessful in a pre-recorded interview process due to end user / candidate technical issues (sound or/and video) in the last two years (January 2023 - January 2025) was 446 not 873.  Figures provided in AQW 13348/22-27 relate to phase 1 interviews (427). A second phase of interviews were carried out of which 19 candidates received this outcome. 446 is the cumulative total of phase 1 and phase 2.  The pre-recorded interview format utilises modern technology and allows candidates to complete their interview at a time of their choice during the interview period. It also speeds up the interview process and reduces costs in terms of interview rooms and paper packs for panel members. A large volume of candidates can be interviewed in a much shorter time whereas doing this in person significantly increases time and resources taken to complete interviews. The platform used has a high level of accessibility to allow for reasonable adjustments to be enabled where possible, including JAWS, NVDA, voiceover software and Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG 2.1). This made the interview process more accessible to a wider range of people with disabilities. The use of pre-recorded video interviews is best practice across other public sector organisations and NICSHR continuously examines how improvements can be made to the recruitment process to enhance participant experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why there are no schemes funded in the West Tyrone constituency though the Education Authority Local Project fund to support rural youth work.
answer_text: The Education Authority Youth Service (EAYS) has advised that the EA does not plan, deliver or report on youth work by constituency area but on a local council area basis. The West Tyrone constituency area therefore takes into account Mid Ulster, Fermanagh and Omagh as well as Derry and Strabane Council areas.  The delivery plans for these council areas and associated programmes and initiatives can be viewed on the EA Youth Funding website. The EAYS has also advised that having reviewed and considered the needs of children and young people, and the level of youth work delivered through EA Local Youth Development Plans, EAYS has deemed that local project funding is not required for the 2025/26 period and that adequate youth provision is currently in place via the provision of significant statutory youth services alongside the following funding streams and Programmes:1.EA Generic Non-Targeted;2.EA Local Area Based; 3.TEO Planned Intervention and TBUC Camps;4.DE Horizons – through ‘A Fair Start’;5.DE REACH – Emotional Health and Wellbeing. At a regional level this provision is also supplemented by EAYS supporting regional organisations to develop and support rural youth work including Young Farmers, Bytes, Faith Based organisations, Boys and Girls Clubs, Fóram na nÓg and Youth Action.  Each of these regional organisations have a specific goal to support and develop rural youth work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support that will be provided to the six council areas that did not get schemes funded through the Education Authority Local Project fund to support rural youth work, including Fermanagh and Omagh District Council.
answer_text: Support for the six council areas where Local Project Funding has not been advertised comes in the form of existing provision as well as other provision that will be available moving forward such as PeacePlus.  This will significantly fund key themes of work for children and young people including emotional health and wellbeing and rural needs.  Rural youth work remains as a key priority for the Education Authority (EA) and it has recently published a model for rural youth work that will be implemented, funded and supported through future regional and local plans.To further support youth organisations across the 6 identified council areas, the following awards were released under the funding scheme:6 x Regional Project Funding Opportunities, 3 of which are for international work under the ‘Go Global’ Programme with a further 3 opportunities under the Go Lead programme’ that provides leadership development for children and young people and an opportunity to exchange with other youth providers across the 4 UK nations and Ireland; andRegional Development funding to support the recruitment and retention of volunteers in the uniformed, faith based and Irish medium groups.Support for youth organisations funded under Regional Strategic Funding has continued this year.  This includes funding awards held by Young Farmers, Uniformed Headquarter Organisations and Foram na Og who have many member organisations based in rural communities. These regional groups offer significant support across themes such as governance, workforce development, curriculum support, safeguarding and child protection, youth work outcomes, and representation and collaboration.Additional support is also available through the T:BUC Camp Programme which offers grants of between £3,750 to £22,500, the Planned Intervention Programme which offers grants of up to £6,000 and the DE ‘A Fair Start’ Programme which offers grants and opportunities to collaborate with other providers to deliver the Horizons programme.The Education Authority is currently in the process of developing and publishing the Regional Assessment of Needs Document for 2026 with a full public consultation planned for launch in February 2026. This will help identify and prioritise existing, new and emerging needs that the Youth Service will consider for the 2026-2029 funding cycle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the latest Ulster Bank Northern Ireland Growth Tracker report which shows that business activity has fallen for first time in just over a year.
answer_text: The decline in business activity in the most recent Ulster Bank Northern Ireland Growth Tracker was primarily reported in the retail and construction sectors and offset increases in the manufacturing and services sectors. This has been largely attributed to waning customer demand and a second consecutive monthly fall in new orders. On a more positive note, it is encouraging to see that business optimism improved slightly in December and that the North was the only monitored region to experience increased staffing levels – thus extending our sequence of jobs growth to 24 months. 	My officials and I continue to monitor relevant economic intelligence and engage with local business stakeholders in the context of my economic plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) ambulance response times in West Tyrone; and (ii) the number of call outs that have missed his Department's response time targets, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Table 1, shows the average response time for calls in the West Tyrone constituency, over the last five years. Table 1YearAverage Response Time (HH: MM: SS)202000:34:45202100:38:21202200:32:44202300:42:12202400:44:13Table 2, shows the number of calls over the last five years in the West Tyrone constituency, broken down by call category, that have met / have not met, the response time target. For ease of reference, response time targets are included at the bottom of Table 2. Table 2YearCategory of callsResponse Time Target MetResponse Time Target MetYesNoYesNo2020Cat118222145%55%Cat22228182055%45%Cat3296917594%6%Cat4129910293%7%2021Cat123628845%55%Cat22337248848%52%Cat3221217693%7%Cat4108315987%13%2022Cat126733145%55%Cat21823215546%54%Cat3213618992%8%Cat481792%8%2023Cat128337343%57%Cat21511246638%62%Cat3165033583%17%Cat417965%35%2024Cat126440040%60%Cat21246247533%67%Cat3171732484%16%Cat42167%33%Response times targets: Category 1: Mean Standard - 8 minutesCategory 2: Mean Standard - 18 minutesCategory 3: 90th Centile Standard - 120 minutesCategory 4: 90th Centile Standard - 180 minutesNIAS continues to prioritise patient care and operational efficiency, however, it must be recognised that ambulance response times have increased over recent years and remains a significant challenge for the Trust.For context, the target to respond to Category 1 patients is within 8 minutes and in December 2024, these patients received an average response within 13 minutes.A key enabler for NIAS to maintain response time targets, is the timely completion of patient handovers at hospitals with a target of 15 minutes from arrival (plus 15 mins to clear after handover) to support patient safety and ambulance availability. In December 2024 94% of all Handovers exceeded 15 minutes.NIAS is working closely with the Department of Health to increase our available resources and we continue to engage with all HSC Trusts around improving patient handover times at Emergency Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of suicides in West Tyrone in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  Deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision).  Data on the numbers of deaths from suicide from 2013 to 2022, broken down by parliamentary constituency (including West Tyrone) can be found in table 9 of the publication Suicide Statistics 2022 Tables. Overall totals of suicide deaths registered in 2023 are available in table 6.1 of the Registrar General Annual Report 2023.  The Suicide statistics 2023 detailed report and tables (including breakdown by parliamentary constituency) are scheduled to be published on Wednesday 5 February 2025.There are some situations where the registration of the death can be delayed, specifically where the death has been accidental, unexpected, or suspicious, and must be referred to the Coroners Service, only to be registered after completion of the investigation. It should be noted that suicides registered during a particular time will therefore not be reflective of suicides that occurred in that period.  NISRA recommends that users of suicide statistics refer to the subset of ‘self-inflicted injury’ (ICD code X60-X84 &Y87.0) as the most accurate count in relation to longer term trends.In 2019 NISRA and the Coroners Service commenced a review of suicide statistics following the identification of a classification issue in published statistics which concluded in 2022. This marked a major revision to this statistical series with a break in the time series being introduced from 2015. Comparisons with data prior to 2015 should therefore be made with caution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department is promoting pork exports from (i) West Tyrone; and (ii) the rest of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department’s role is to facilitate meat exports (including pork) from the whole of Northern Ireland, and to support organisations which lead on trade promotion such as Invest NI and the Department for Business and Trade.The inspection and enforcement activities my Department carries out in slaughterhouses, cutting plants and cold stores guarantees regulatory compliance throughout the supply chain and safeguards high standards in animal health and welfare. This vital work underpins our ability to trade by providing the necessary assurances to our trading partners.The Department’s Trade Programme oversees a robust system of controls to guarantee that product leaving Northern Ireland has the export health certification that is required to facilitate its entry into other countries. DAERA officials promote market access by hosting third country inward trade missions to demonstrate the high standard that are in place here. My officials work in close liaison with Defra, the UK lead Department for trade policy and implementation, to ensure that all opportunities to promote the export of pork, and other products of animal origin, are fully capitalised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 20088/22-27, to detail the action he will take in relation to the owners of the 4 per cent of XL Bully dogs that have not registered.
answer_text: Councils have responsibility for enforcing the legislation and will take appropriate action in relation to any XL Bully dog which has not been exempted.  These dogs can be seized and the council can choose whether to prosecute the owner or to apply for a destruction order for the dog to be euthanised.  Regardless of the approach taken, the judge will consider whether the dog is suitable for exemption before making a final decision.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support he is providing Health and Social Care Trusts to increase the percentage of patients treated for gynaecological cancers within 31 days from the current level of 77.7 per cent to reach the target of 98 per cent.
answer_text: NI waiting time targets state that at least 98% of patients diagnosed with cancer should receive their first definitive treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat. In the quarter ending September 2024, 139 patients started their first definitive for gynaecological cancer.  The percentage of gynaecological patients starting treatment within 31 days in all five Trusts was 77.7%, similar to the same quarter last year.Increasing demand, access to theatres and workforce challenges are impacting on the 31 day standard across a range of specialties.  In regards to gynaecology specifically, many of the Trusts are experiencing recruitment challenges along with maternity and sickness leave in relatively small gynaecology teams.  Trusts are committed to filling medical and other vacancies wherever possible including using international recruitment drives and developing skill mix in line with available evidence.The Department continues to work with all Trusts to reduce the current waiting time for cancer treatments.  In 2024/25 non-recurrent funding has been allocated to Trusts to support additional waiting list initiative (WLI) work, using both inhouse and independent sector capacity. This included funding for gynaecology WLI in all Trusts.  In addition, the Department continues to facilitate the equalisation and reduction of waiting times through the inter-Trust transfer of patients where appropriate.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group of DOH meet quarterly with trust colleagues to review performance against 14-day breast, 31 and 62 targets/pathways to maximise on efficiencies within current service provision.  Trusts also provide Cancer Performance Optimisation Plans.  These plans are intended to reduce variation in practice, reduce variation in waiting times, maximising workforce capacity and skill mix, thereby optimising capacity and productivity within core service provision.    SPPG also closely monitor theatre utilisation performance ensuring that theatre improvement plans are developed where required.  The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trusts, Public Health Agency (PHA) and General Practitioner (GP) representation, to oversee ongoing service improvement recommendations and implementation at a local and regional level – this includes overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report for Northern Ireland”.  This report was published in January 2024 and provided a review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland along with 20 recommendations to improve gynaecology services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the additional cost; and (ii) the additional number of people covered, in extending the Influenza vaccination programme to everyone aged 50 to 64 years of age.
answer_text: There will be no additional funding associated with the extension of the influenza vaccination programme to 50–64-year-olds.  Due to lower than anticipated uptake in the routine cohorts, vaccine stock, which has already been purchased, is available.  In addition, the funding allocations already made for GPs and community pharmacies administration costs are also expected to cover the additional costs associated with this new cohort.It is estimated that around 305,000 additional people will now be eligible to receive an influenza vaccine.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18919/22-27, (i) why his Department does not maintain a list of roads added to the gritting schedule each year; and (ii) whether he plans to review this policy.
answer_text: At the start of each winter season a review is undertaken and additional roads added to the salted network if they meet the current policy. Updates to the gritting schedule are managed at a local level, with changes made to the publicly available map for the gritted network.  Given the availability of this information on a live basis, a list of roads added to the schedule each year has not been held.My officials are currently considering the delivery of a centralised winter service route optimisation solution for the next winter service season.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why the number of patients seen by a breast cancer specialist following an urgent referral within 14 days has fallen from 52.9 per cent this quarter a year ago to 30 per cent this quarter.
answer_text: Breast cancer waiting times are unacceptable and have been for some time. While the number of patients seen within 14 days of referral has decreased, the total number of patients assessed (3,449) in the quarter has increased by 3.8% (126) from the same period last year. It is not possible to deliver an efficient service in the current format. In the current model, each Trust relies on a small team to deliver their breast services and therefore an absence of any kind can have a severe adverse impact on a Trust’s ability meet this target. The introduction of Encompass in South Eastern and Belfast HSC Trusts during the quarter also further increased pressures on breast services. In September, I commissioned a review of breast cancer services, and this work is currently considering how regional breast cancer services can be shaped to deliver timely and appropriate care for patients. The first phase of this work, the regionalisation of patients through a single regional waiting list for breast assessments, will go live in the coming months, and I expect plans for the future of breast services to be presented in the middle of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why no Health and Social Care Trust is meeting its target of 98 per cent of patients to be treated for cancer within 31 days; and (ii) how he plans to help Health and Social Care Trusts meet these targets.
answer_text: NI waiting time targets state that at least 98% of patients diagnosed with cancer should receive their first definitive treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat. In the quarter ending September 2024, 2,842 patients started their first definitive treatment, an increase of 2.5% (69) from the same quarter last year.  The percentage of patients starting treatment within 31 days in all five Trusts was 87.9%, the same as the quarter ending September last year.   Increasing demand, access to theatres and workforce challenges are impacting on the 31 day standard across a range of specialties.  Recruitment challenges are often due to UK shortages.  Trusts are committed to filling medical and other vacancies wherever possible including using international recruitment drives and developing skill mix in line with available evidence.  The Department continues to work with all Trusts to reduce the current waiting time for cancer treatments.  In 2024/25 non-recurrent funding has been allocated to Trusts to support additional waiting list initiative work, using both inhouse and independent sector capacity.  In addition, the Department continues to facilitate the equalisation and reduction of waiting times through the inter-Trust transfer of patients where appropriate. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group of DOH meet quarterly with trust colleagues to review performance against 14-day breast, 31 and 62 targets/pathways to maximise on efficiencies within current service provision.  Trusts also provide Cancer Performance Optimisation Plans.  These plans are intended to reduce variation in practice, reduce variation in waiting times, maximising workforce capacity and skill mix, thereby optimising capacity and productivity within core service provision.    SPPG also closely monitor theatre utilisation performance ensuring that theatre improvement plans are developed where required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of why, according to figures published by her Department, there was a reduction in the percentage of custodial disposals for domestic abuse cases from 37.1 per cent in 2022-23 to 30.6 per cent in 2023-24.
answer_text: Sentencing in individual cases is a matter for the independent judiciary.  Judges arrive at their sentencing decisions having taken into consideration all the unique circumstances of each case, including any aggravating and mitigating factors, as well as any sentencing guidance that is available for the particular offence charged.  They can only impose a custodial sentence where they consider the case merits it.Additionally, the fact that the legislation was new in 2022/23 may have impacted the volume, mix and type of cases that came before the courts over the two years. Further years’ data will be required before analysis of trends for disposals in these cases can be robustly established.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19330/22-27, to detail the timescale for coming forward with a plan to bring Lough Neagh into community ownership.
answer_text: Whilst I am personally in favour of community ownership of Lough Neagh and have engaged with Earl Shaftesbury twice since taking up office, the immediate focus of my officials’ work, in the context of constrained resources, is on necessary interventions in respect of the blue green algae and water quality issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18921/22-27, for an update on the current reserves of grit held by his Department.
answer_text: My Department has used 42,580 tonnes of salt so far this winter, with 24,550 tonnes of salt remaining in our depots and another 20,000 tonnes of contingency salt available in strategic reserve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of exemption certificates that have been issued to owners of XL Bully dogs.

Complete final data on the number of dogs which have been granted an Exemption Certificate will not be known until all applications have been processed and so the final number of certificates approved may vary slightly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of why, according to figures published by her Department, there was a 25 per cent increase in the average time for dealing with domestic abuse cases between 2022-23 and 2023-24.
answer_text: The Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 (the Act) created a new domestic abuse offence which criminalises a course of abusive behaviour. The Act also introduced a statutory aggravator (Section 15) which can be applied to any offence, other than the new DA offence.  Recording of this type of offence came into operation on 21 February 2022.The Department of Justice issued a statistics bulletin on 7 January 2025 in relation to offences under this legislation.  The bulletin included figures on the average (median) time taken for a case to complete at court, from the date an offence was reported.  The median times reported for all domestic abuse related cases under the legislation that were dealt with at courts were 108 days in 2022/23 and 139 days in 2023/24.  This difference in these years is most likely due to fact that 2022/23 was the first year that this legislation was in operation and was a period of implementation. This also means that the data presented is solely comprised of cases where both the offence and resolution of the case occurred within the same year.  For 2023/24, however, there is the addition of cases that take longer having commenced in the previous year but resulted within the 2023/24 counting period.  This would be a normal pattern for cases within the system. Further years’ data will be required before robust analysis of trends in case processing time for these cases can be established.It should also be noted that the median time taken for cases involving an offence under the 2021 legislation was less in both 2022/23 and 2023/24 (108 days, and 139 days) than the overall median time for cases dealt with at courts (206 days in 2022/23 and 190 days in 2023/24).Tackling delay across the criminal justice system is a key priority for the department and criminal justice partners.  Together with criminal justice partners, a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay has been prioritised, which will be of benefit to all those who come into contact with the system.   This programme of work does not specifically target individual offence types, and instead is based around five key workstreams, taking forward existing work on committal reform, existing and emerging work in the digital arena and exploring other areas for efficiencies, such as early engagement, court remits and out of court disposals.  It seeks to reform the way in which cases are handled, getting the most serious cases to court more quickly and dealing with all cases in the most effective and proportionate manner. Taken together, this is a comprehensive package of measures to help reduce demand on the system and enable cases to be dealt with more quickly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to review and rebrand the Universal Credit Contingency Fund.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her discussions with the Criminal Bar Association to resolve the dispute in relation to legal aid payments.
answer_text: My Department has been engaging with professions on an ongoing basis since the outset of the access to justice reform programme. I also met the representatives of the professional bodies in advance of my statement to the Assembly in December which highlighted my intention to implement an uplift in fees as soon as possible. Officials met again with representatives of the Bar and CBA when the latest withdrawal of services was announced at the end of December. I am now reflecting on issues raised in the course of that discussion and will of course make every effort to address concerns, where they are within my gift, and where it is appropriate to do so.  While I fully support the need to ensure fair proportionate remuneration for work undertaken and intend to issue a consultation on proposed fees changes at the end of January as planned,  I must follow proper process which takes time. I will, however, continue to engage with the Criminal Bar Association in interim in the interests assuaging their concerns and ending the withdrawal of services and the consequent disproportionate impact on victims and witnesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the report by the Save our Acute Services that 100 potential beds at the South West Acute Hospital are not being used.
answer_text: I can confirm the following bed capacity in South West Acute Hospital.Upon opening in 2012, the hospital had a maximum ‘future-proofed’ capacity of 281 in-patient beds (including paediatrics).  The total number of commissioned in-patient beds was 211. There were 35 day-case bed spaces included at the opening of SWAH in 2012 (including maternity bed spaces). These are not classified as in-patient beds.  As at 2024/25, the current planned bed complement for SWAH is for 198 in-patient beds made up of:•	Adult Wards: 		172•	Critical Care Wards:	 6•	Paediatric Wards:		20(Only patients who are clinically appropriate can be admitted to Paediatric and Critical Care wards).2 of the 5 theatres are not commissioned but one is periodically used by Musgrave House to deliver HSC waiting list initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) bed capacity when the hospital was built; and (ii) current bed capacity, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: (i)	The Department of Health does not hold historical data on the number of beds in each hospital when it was built. (ii)	The Information and Analysis Directorate of the Department of Health provide information on average available beds in each hospital (as an indicator of current bed capacity), broken by quarter, this can be found at hs-inpatient-hts-tables-23-24.xlsx. The data dates back to 2014/15 for comparison purposes. On 9th November 2023, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) launched ‘encompass’ - a new electronic patient record system.  Its rollout across the other Trusts will follow on a phased basis throughout 2024/25.  Bed data on or after 9th November 2023 in South Eastern HSCT are still being developed following the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records.  For this reason, South Eastern HSCT data has only been included until 8th November 2023 in the file.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of patients that died after picking up a Pseudomonas infection in a hospital in the last three years, broken down by (i) hospital; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.The information requested is not available as there is no data gathered via the death registration process to identify the source of an infection. There were 56 deaths registered from 2021 to 2023 where ‘Pseudomonas’ was mentioned (i.e. where pseudomonas contributed to the death but may or may not have been the underlying cause of death) on the death certificate; all but four of these deaths occurred in a hospital setting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Department’s work on recommendation 22 of the Independent Review of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to bring forward Recommendation 18 of the Independent Review of Education to the Executive in order to establish a single Department for the entire education journey.
answer_text: In my statement to the Assembly on 21 October 2024 I set out my response to the Independent Review of Education. Alongside my statement I also provided my Department’s response to the report’s recommendations.In relation to the recommendation regarding the establishment of a single Department for the entire education journey, this is primarily a matter for the Executive.I have recently circulated a draft paper with Executive colleagues, raising awareness of planned education actions, subject to budget, and highlighting cross-cutting/significant recommendations and matters which primarily rest with the Executive and/or other Departments.Regardless of the approach taken, work will continue with other departments to ensure that departmental and organisational boundaries do not impede positive outcomes for our children and young people, for example through ongoing work with the Department for the Economy on the 14-19 strategy and with the Department of Health on Early Years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15789/22-27, for an update on the timetable to introduce a total ban on single-use vapes.

These regulations will come into operation in Northern Ireland on 1 June 2025.This replicates actions taken by UK, Scottish and Welsh Governments and ensures a UK-wide ban will be effective from that date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 15218/22-27, for an update on legislation for the replacement body for the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials have prepared an options paper on this matter, however, a decision on the way forward can only be made if it is supported by a costed and approved business case.  This case has also been prepared and is currently being scrutinised by the Department’s Economists.  I have been advised that I should receive the options paper and business case shortly, allowing me to take a fully informed decision on the way forward; and thereafter to seek Executive approval to make the necessary legislative changes.The Department still remains committed to passing the required legislative changes within the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish the results of his Department’s consultation on the School Uniform Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 15712/22-27, for an update on her Department’s work developing policy proposals for the regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: I have previously indicated that management companies must act in accordance with existing companies legislation, which falls outside the remit of my Department. However, I recognise that shared property management also attracts my Department’s interest in substantive land law, and I have been keen to make progress in examining this issue.I have engaged with officials to ensure the appropriate expertise in this complex policy area is secured. Resource has been identified and a study of the issues relating to the operation of property management companies and agents will shortly commence. I will consider the outcome of that work, which I have asked to be completed by Easter, before determining the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the timeline to introduce a Public Health Bill.
answer_text: Following an extended public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland, the Department received over 8,200 responses and is considering all responses.  This process is ongoing at present, and a consultation summary report will be published on the Department’s website.  This report will assist officials to refine next steps over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Pension Credit applications received by his Department each month for the last three years.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the loopholes in the payments criteria for the mitigations package that he will be closing.
answer_text: I refer Member to my answer to AQW 19578/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the projected funding requirements for the complete mitigations package for (i) 2026-27; and (ii) 2027-28.
answer_text: The forecast requirement for the welfare mitigations package is £48.2 million for 2026-27 and £49.3 million for 2027-28.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 15017/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 18270/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 18270/22-27 was answered on 7 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the proposed resurfacing schemes in the West Tyrone constituency in the (i) 2024-25 financial year; and (ii) 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years. This has had a significant impact on our ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. Whilst many roads in the West Tyrone constituency would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of interventionThe 2024/25 resurfacing programme for the West Tyrone constituency was outlined in the Department’s Annual Report Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and Derry City and Strabane District Council can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/fermanagh-and-omagh-council-report-2024-network-maintenancehttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/derry-city-and-strabane-council-report-2024The programme of work planned for 2025/26 will be published in the Annual Report to the relevant Councils which will be produced once budgets for that year are finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the implications for preschools of moving from part-time to full-time provision.
answer_text: Approximately 40% of pre-school settings currently deliver full-time pre-school provision and I intend to approve at least a further 100 to begin offering this session length from September 2025.Settings delivering a full-time session length operate for a minimum of 4.5 hours per day, rather than a minimum of 2.5 hours per day in part-time provision.  This provides additional time for children to benefit from the rich play-based learning environment of the pre-school setting, provides additional time for pre-school staff to interact with and support young learners, and provides access to a funded school meal for eligible children.  The longer session length also supports parents in their childcare arrangements, by providing a longer period of funded provision for their children each day.All pre-school settings, regardless of session length, deliver to the same pre-school curricular guidelines and are inspected to the same educational standards by the Education and Training Inspectorate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19144/22-27, to detail the level of increase in applications since the Chancellor’s announcement.
answer_text: Since the Chancellor of the Exchequer’s announcement of the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment eligibility on 29 July 2024, Pension Credit application received by my Department, up to and including 02 December 2024, have increased by 72% when compared against the same period in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the preschools moving from part-time to full-time provision in the West Tyrone constituency in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: As result of work being undertaken in the current financial year, five pre-school settings in the West Tyrone constituency have been approved to move from part-time to full-time provision from September 2025.  One further setting is under consideration.  Details are provided below.I have approved (i) Cooley Primary School, Sixmilecross, (ii) First Steps Community Playgroup, Castlederg, (iii) St Mary's Primary School, Killyclogher, (iv) St Oliver Plunkett Primary School, Beragh and (v) The Magic Roundabout Playgroup, Altamuskin to transition to deliver the standardised pre-school education session length from September 2025.  Details of all settings approved thus far to transition have been published on the Department of Education website at Minister’s Decisions on Pre-School Settings Requesting Transition from September 2025 | Department of EducationIn addition, Omagh Integrated PS NU has been included in Tranche Three of settings that have requested to transition from September 2025.  Consultation on this tranche of settings will close on 13th December and I expect to make decisions on these settings shortly thereafter.It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to the standardised session length. The speed of transition will largely be dependent on the resources available from the Executive at each stage, as well as the circumstances of individual pre-school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 13110/22-27, for an update of her Department’s plans to protect shop workers at their place of work during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: Further to my response to AQW 13110/22-27, it remains my intention to bring a new offence of assaulting a person providing services to the public, performing a public duty or providing a public service forward via my proposed Sentencing Bill. This new offence will apply where a shopworker is assaulted in the course of their work, and will carry a higher maximum penalty than that currently available for the offence of common assault.  In addition, I will make the fact that the victim of a more serious assault is such a worker an aggravating factor so that this will be reflected in their sentence where a person is convicted of such an offence.I intend to introduce this Sentencing Bill before the end of 2025, although how quickly this Bill is able to progress will be a matter for the Northern Ireland Assembly.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any discussions they had with the Secretary of State ahead of his statement to the House of Commons on 4 December 2024 in relation to the Legacy Act; and (ii) their assessment of his proposals.
answer_text: We have met with the Secretary of State on numerous occasions, and we have raised this issue with him. We have also discussed this with the Prime Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the December 2024 carbon intensity indicators that shows of the 42 indicators, recent trends suggest that ten are negative and seven show no change; and (ii) to detail any action he intends to take to change these trends.
answer_text: The data presented in the December 2024 carbon intensity publication show that the majority of the long-term and the short-term trends, for the 10 key intensity indicators, are positive and are to be welcomed.  The explanations for those proxy indicators, that report a negative trend or no change, are wide ranging and should be considered in the overall trends apparent within these key indicators.Last month, the first three carbon budgets for the period 2023 to 2037 and the interim 2030 and 2040 emissions targets were set. The Climate Change (Carbon Budgets 2023-2027) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 came into operation on 12 December 2024. Officials are now in the process of finalising the draft Climate Action Plan document for consultation and I look forward to bringing it to the Executive as soon as possible. The Climate Action Plan represents the Executive’s approach to reducing emissions in line with our carbon budgets and a Net Zero trajectory and will be published for a 16-week consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will make a decision on whether to expand water sampling times at beaches to cover outside the official bathing season.
answer_text: My Department recognises that there has been a marked increase in interest in open water swimming and a considerable number of people are accessing bathing waters all year.  Results from the latest Bathing Water Review indicated overwhelming public support for an extension to the season.  Given the significant policy change, it is important that all implications are considered.  In this context, my Department has begun the process of sampling outside of the season to inform future policy direction.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on discussions with the Earl of Shaftesbury in relation to the ownership of Lough Neagh.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on discussions between his Department and the Western Health and Social Care Trust to find long-term solutions for GP services in (i) Fintona; and (ii) Trillick and Dromore.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us.  However, GP practices are under significant pressures at present as they deal with immediate demands and the consequences of wider service pressures.  My department continues to work with all GP Practices across Northern Ireland to maintain the provision of GMS services to the resident population.The Western Trust are committed to testing a new model of service delivery to the patients in Fintona and Dromore/Trillick area.  They have recently appointed 5 Salaried GPs and a second recruitment drive for additional Salaried GPs is currently underway.  The Western Trust and SPPG have established a Strategic Planning Group to review the management arrangements for those GP Practices whom the Trust are currently managing and are planning to meet early in the New Year.In the meantime, the current Western Trust management arrangement should provide stability for the Practice and patients should continue to contact the practice as normal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the review of the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation Framework.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework has been in place for 8 years and my Department has commissioned an independent review which is examining how effectively it has been implemented. The review will assess the effectiveness of the Framework as an initiative for DAERA to work in partnership with other public authorities in tackling poverty and social isolation in rural areas. It will also examine the extent to which the objectives and intended outcomes have been achieved and how the measures delivered have contributed to addressing the priority areas for intervention.  Additionally, the review will examine how the implementation, monitoring and evaluation arrangements set out in the Framework have been implemented and how effective the programme of measures delivered under the TRPSI Framework has been in targeting the target groups. The review is engaging with a range of stakeholders, including public authorities and rural community organisations.  The review is on schedule to report back in early 2025. The findings and recommendations emanating from the review will be considered, alongside the evaluations of other pilots and initiatives, available research and approaches across other jurisdictions. These will all inform the development of an evidence-led rural policy which reflects the changed rural landscape.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18273/22-27, when the Driver and Vehicle Agency's review of driver licensing fees will be completed.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency anticipates the review of driver licensing fees will be completed in early 2025. If changes to the existing fees are required, the Department will consult on proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the targets set and timeline for delivery of the Heritage, Culture and Creativity Strategy.
answer_text: I established the Heritage, Culture & Creativity Programme to deliver four new policies in respect of my Department’s responsibilities for Public Libraries, Museums, the Arts and the Historic Environment.  The Programme is intended to give each of the constituent policies the status they merit; provide each of the communities of interest their own focus; and allow each exercise to proceed on its own timetable.  Significant progress has been made since I announced the Programme in July 2024.  A comprehensive round of engagement across local government and Executive Departments is in its final stages and my officials have begun the work of developing options that will form the basis of public consultation.  On current planning it is anticipated that the first of the policies will be ready for public consultation in early 2025 with the remainder following in the Spring.  The process to develop the final policy positions will include a period to take account of responses to the consultation process.  Current planning anticipates publication of the finalised policies in the summer of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 13911/22-27, for an update on the Duty of Candour and Being Open Framework, including a time frame for introduction.
answer_text: I launched a public consultation on the Being Open Framework on 10 December 2024 which will run for 14 weeks and close on 21 March 2025. The Being Open Framework seeks to put in place the necessary support and systems required to enable and nurture a truly open culture and help to prevent patient harm.I have also committed, as part of the Health and Social Care Three Year plan, to advance proposals for an organisational duty of candour as well as considering proposals for an individual duty of candour in the coming months.Development of the proposals around duty of candour will take account of the findings in the Being Open public consultation as well as the outworkings in relation to the Infected Blood Inquiry recommendations, the proposed Hillsborough Law and the ongoing reviews of duty of candour in the other UK jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 16545/22-27, to detail the number of payments made in the successful determinations, broken down by (i) under £30,000; (ii) £30,000-£40,000; (iii) £40,000-£50,000; (iv) £50,000-£60,000; and (v) over £60,000.
answer_text: As of week commencing 2 December 2024, there have been 1,489 successful determinations, resulting in almost £70 million paid to victims since the Scheme opened for applications.  If the Victims Payment Board determines that an applicant is entitled to a payment, payments will be made monthly or as a lump sum. The level of award is dependent on the percentage disablement following the medical assessment stage. Applicants can request for a payment to be made as a lump sum instead of monthly payments if they are over 60, or terminally ill. Applicants may also be entitled to a payment backdated to 23 December 2014.The Board has advised that it is not possible to break down the payments made monthly in the manner requested.  However, the Board has provided the following averages for successful determinations:Payment TypeAverage Payment Backdated£42,633.73Lump Sum£43,044.81Monthly£414.41The Board has also advised that 64% of successful applicants to date have had the option to take a lump sum, with 81% of these opting to take the lump sum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he is taking to reduce the number of (i) Staff Officer; and (ii) Administrative Officer vacancies in his Department.
answer_text: Recruitment sits with the Department of Finance.  My officials work closely with NICSHR colleagues to progress the filling of all vacancies. Several NICS wide recruitment processes have been launched by NISHR within the last year, including at Staff Officer and Administration Officer grades. A new AO General Service competition launched 19 August 2024 with circa 5,000 candidates invited to interview. A merit list is anticipated in February 2025, with permanent supply available from March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Pension Credit applications received by his Department since the UK Budget announced the end of the universal winter fuel allowance for pensioners.
answer_text: Since the Chancellor of the Exchequer announced the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment eligibility on 29 July 2024, my Department has received 4528 Pension Credit applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions he and his Department has had with the Department of Health in relation to midwifery apprenticeships.
answer_text: My officials have engaged in recent months with a range of public sector employers to inform our work to promote apprenticeship uptake within the public sector. This has included the Department of Health and the wider Health and Social Care Sector to discuss a range of pathways including midwifery. In line with the Skills Fund that I launched, my Department will continue to work with stakeholders across the public sector, including the Department of Health to facilitate development of apprenticeship frameworks. Without significant additional funding available this engagement will involve promoting the benefits of apprenticeships, supporting the development of new pathways and identifying policy and support needs. The apprenticeship training in the public sector is funded by each employing organisation or Department.

answer_text: Following the allocation of 2022-23 departmental budgets by the then Secretary of State, in the absence of Ministers difficult decisions were taken by officials to manage within the budget allocated.  Given the significant pressures the Department was managing, these decisions included stopping discretionary spending on Road Safety Advertising in 2022-23.

I would expect to be in a position to launch a full public consultation early in the new year.

answer_text: The issue has been raised with both the Secretary of State and the Prime Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide details of the Farming for the Generations Pilot Scheme.
answer_text: My Department’s new Farm Support and Development Programme contains a Farming for the Generations Scheme which is currently being rolled out as a pilot. The pilot scheme aims to raise awareness of the need for succession planning on farms by supporting farm families through a three phased approach: planning for succession, developing the successor, and supporting the lead generation within the farm family. It will also link farmers without a family successor to new entrants to provide access to land and other resources.Rural Support is delivering this pilot scheme on behalf of DAERA. A roadshow entitled ‘Passing the Torch - Planning for the Generations to Come’ was launched last month. This will deliver 5 in-person and 2 online awareness events across Northern Ireland between now and the 10 December. These workshops will offer a welcoming environment for families to discuss concerns and aspirations, with guidance from trained facilitators. The workshops are free to attend and will provide valuable insights and strategies to help ensure the continued success of their farms across generations. Evaluation of the pilot scheme will input into a future Farming for the Generations scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQO 1193/22-27, to detail the next steps he agreed with the Ulster Farmers’ Union on 26 November 2024 in relation to the introduction of an inheritance tax on farms worth over £1 million.
answer_text: I had a very constructive meeting with the Ulster Farmers’ Union and we agreed to explore further analysis which could be done on the impact of the changes announced in the Budget to inheritance tax on Northern Ireland farms.  It was also agreed that we would continue to make representations to the UK Government urging a rethink of the policy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road repairs that took place in the West Tyrone constituency during the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: Road surface defect data dating back to 2008/2009 is regularly updated and publicly available on the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects.  The data is updated each evening and can be downloaded in various formats.  The information includes: spatial location details, road section name, local district council, surface defect type (e.g. potholes, cracking, depressions, rutting), date recorded, defect status, target response time, and actual completion date.   It is important to note that there can be a time lag between when each surface defect record is repaired and when this is updated on the maintenance management system.  Repair work is also only carried out subject to a defect meeting our current intervention levels set out in the ‘Limited Service’ policy, (see Director of Engineering memorandum ‘Road maintenance standards for safety – limited service': https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/network-maintenance).You will also find my Department’s official statistics on the condition of the road network published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2022-23.More specifically, the total number of road repairs undertaken in the West Tyrone constituency in 2023-24 financial year is 9,104
