answer_text: AQW 17719/22-27 was answered on 2nd December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last review took place of the roads that are gritted; and (ii) any additional roads that were added to the gritting schedule following that review.
answer_text: My Department salts the main through routes that carry more than 1,500 vehicles per day and, in exceptional circumstances, roads with difficult topography and carrying between 1,000 and 1,500 vehicles per day.  The application of this policy ensures that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, at an annual cost of approximately £7 million.  Any extension of the schedule to cover 90% or 100% of traffic volumes would increase the cost to approximately £14 million or £28 million per annum, respectively.At the start of each winter season a review is undertaken and additional roads added to the salted network if they meet the current policy.My department does not maintain a list of roads added to the schedule each year, however I can advise that a recent addition has included the A6 Dungiven to Derry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current reserves of grit held by his Department; and (ii) how long these reserves will last given the current usage.
answer_text: My Department has used 20,870 tonnes of salt so far this winter, with 35,210 tonnes of salt remaining in our depots and another 20,000 tonnes of contingency salt available in strategic reserve.Over the last 5 years an average of 49,200 tonnes of salt has been used each winter, and as a total of 55,210 tonnes remains available in reserve, this should be sufficient to cover the upcoming winter.   The salt supply contract allows for additional deliveries of a minimum 15,000 tonnes of salt a month if severe or prolonged snow and ice are encountered later this season.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to list the roads on the gritting schedule for the West Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: The salted network is 7,000km long, covering 28% of the road network and facilitating approximately 80% of traffic.  Details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via the following link; DFI Roads Salted Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the next review of the roads that are gritted will take place; and (ii) whether he plans to expand the proportion of roads that are gritted.
answer_text: My Department salts the main through routes carrying more than 1,500 vehicles per day and, in exceptional circumstances, roads with difficult topography and carrying between 1,000 and 1,500 vehicles per day. The application of this policy ensures that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, at an annual cost of approximately £7 million. Any extension of the schedule to cover 90% or 100% of traffic volumes would increase the cost to approximately £14 million or £28 million per annum, respectively.At the start of each winter season a review is undertaken and additional roads added to the salted network if they meet the current policy.In light of the significant resources already deployed by my Department to deliver winter service provision, there are no plans to expand the salting policy at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the campaign in rural areas to safeguard vernacular heritage.
answer_text: There is no formal campaign, but joint principles resulting from a 2021 conference are published on the department’s website  at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/traditional-or-vernacular-buildings. This page also sets out the department’s contribution of developing a vernacular record; its listing programme; developing guidance; commenting on planning applications and policy; and supporting communities to research, repair and celebrate their heritage.  It also refers to our MOU with Department of Housing, Local Government and Heritage in Ireland, to share and promote knowledge on the built vernacular. As a result, we have jointly published information on caring for this heritage and on sources of information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reasons why folic acid will to be added to non-wholemeal flour by law from 2026.
answer_text: Folic acid deficiency is a leading cause of Neural Tube Defects (NTDs), which can cause a large number of serious and debilitating conditions to babies in the womb, including spina bifida.   The introduction of The Bread and Flour (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 aims to reduce the incidence of NTDs affected pregnancies by increasing dietary intake of folic acid for women who could become pregnant. This approach has been adopted in over 80 countries world-wide and the benefits are well documented. The fortification of non-wholemeal wheat flour is expected to prevent around 200 cases of debilitating brain and spine defects in babies in the United Kingdom per year. This change will see a reduction in preventable serious health issues for newborns and help to support the health of the population overall.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that have had their application for a tenancy succession rejected by the Housing Executive in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive does not hold or collate the information in the format requested.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to introduce dedicated women's health hubs, similar to those being rolled out in England.
answer_text: While there are a number of different approaches to ‘women’s health hubs’ across the UK, the concept aims to provide a range of services with rapid, local access within a single visit, provide assessment and treatment where possible and reduce referrals into elective waiting lists. In Northern Ireland, Health and Social Care Trusts have established ambulatory care models with a focus on gynaecology and early pregnancy services, which can reduce presentations at Emergency Departments and referrals to gynae waiting lists. Through the above regional planning structures, it is envisaged that further work to develop appropriate service models and enhance existing services across NI Trusts will be progressed through 2025, subject to available staff capacity and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work he and his Department have been undertaking to improve mobile coverage on (i) the A5; (ii) the A6; and (iii) other major roads.
answer_text: As you are aware, Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter with the Department for Science, Innovation & Technology (DSIT) holding the policy responsibility on improvements to mobile connectivity. The Department for the Economy (DfE) maintains regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters ensuring that all local issues are given appropriate consideration for any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of mobile connectivity here.Mobile infrastructure is currently provided on a wholly commercial basis by Mobile Network Operators (MNOs), however, my officials are involved in a number of initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity here. These include the Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce which aims to identify the main issues that impact on the roll-out of mobile technologies and provide solutions to remove barriers that prevent the fast and efficient deployment of mobile connectivity here. In addition, my department has been working with the British Government led Shared Rural Network (SRN) which is a project developed with the four main MNOs. SRN aims to deliver 95% 4G coverage and enable rural businesses and communities to thrive. More information about the SRN can be found at srn.org.uk and MNOs are willing to provide you with more specific information on request.My officials will continue to support relevant stakeholders with a view to improving mobile coverage here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the results of the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme.
answer_text: Currently, around 50% of the results for the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) are available; it will be 2026 before full results for the Scheme are received and a full assessment can be made. However, at this stage some trends of note are emerging for example in relation to soil pH and phosphorus.pH, one of the most important soil parameters as it controls the efficiency of nutrient uptake and use by crops, shows an overall average value of pH 5.83, but this masks a fairly large range between farm types.  For example, the average for arable farms is pH 6.2 compared to an average of pH 5.8 for Beef Rearing farms. There is also a lower general pH average in the West (Zone 2) compared to the East (Zone 1). Available phosphorus (P) is also a very important parameter, both as an essential crop nutrient and because of the danger excess levels in soil pose when lost to the aquatic environment. The average level in the 300,000 fields measured is 26.2 mg/l (Olsen Index 3), just slightly above the target level recommended for agricultural soils. Although soil P levels are generally higher in all intensively used agricultural fields in both Zones, there is a significantly higher average in Zone 1 (28.2 mg/l Olsen Index 3) compared to Zone 2 (24.3 mg/l Olsen Index 2+). Research in SNHS has shown a direct relationship between the proportion of river catchments with above optimum soil P and concentrations of P in-stream.    	The SNHS is providing a wealth of individualised information to participating farmer (both through the results & the training CAFRE provide on their use and interpretation). It is also generating a significant research platform to allow further research on soil nutrient use and distribution as well as future policy development.  When the full SNHS results are available in 2026, a detailed assessment will be carried out and learnings disseminated broadly to relevant stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the schools that already have a defibrillator on site, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This information is still being gathered so I am unable to provide you with accurate details at this time. A recent survey has identified that 297 out of 633 schools that responded currently have a defibrillator on site. To help understand the full extent of those schools who require a defibrillator, a follow up letter was issued on 26 November to ask those schools that failed to respond to the survey to contact the Department.  This will provide officials with more accurate details to identify those schools requiring provision of a defibrillator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the gambling industry Code of Practice; and (ii) to detail the timescale for its production.
answer_text: A consultation on a proposed initial Gambling Code of Practice was launched on 1 October and will close on 24 December.  Subject to consideration of the outcome of the consultation, it is expected the code will issue as soon as practicably possible in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the Housing Executive's comments in relation to the unprecedented and record-breaking levels of demand for emergency housing in recent days, to detail any further support he and his Department can provide the Housing Executive.
answer_text: I am committed to the prevention of homelessness and to ensure that everyone has a safe and warm place to stay.Discussions have been ongoing with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, and my Department has been provided with assurances on the steps taken to support those who found themselves homeless recently. My Department received an additional non-ring-fenced Resource allocation of £11.3m as part of October Monitoring and I am currently working through the process to determine allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of schools that have availed of the School Milk Subsidy Scheme in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below sets out the detail requested.YearNumber of Primary (including Special) schools participating in the scheme2019/204422020/212462021/222842022/232992023/24295
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why he bid for £9.7 million of capital funding for the backlog of safety critical street lighting in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being fully funded.
answer_text: I can inform the member that my bid for £9.7m capital funding in October monitoring was to address the replacement of a number of street lighting installations that have been deemed to be near the end of their serviceable lives. This programme helps ensure that my Department’s street lighting network is renewed as required, in order to remain serviceable and in line with current standards.The implications of this not being funded, will delay the replacement of these schemes until such time as the necessary funding is made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why he bid for £25 million of capital funding for Structural Maintenance NI in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being fully funded.
answer_text: The existing Structural Maintenance baseline funding is inadequate and has been for many years. It has been independently established that £194m per annum (at today’s prices) is needed to adequately maintain the structural integrity of the country's road network. The main purpose of the bid for £25m for structural maintenance was to make a vital contribution to improving road conditions for all road users and also help to address the significant underfunding of recent years.My Department did not receive all of the funding that was bid for but I have allocated £12.5m of capital funding for Structural Maintenance that will be targeted to improving road conditions on the entire road network and help address the significant backlog of work. It will also have the benefit of securing jobs in the construction industry.  The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and my Department will continue to seek additional funding through the monitoring round process to ensure continued investment in the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why he bid for £30 million of capital funding for NI Water in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being fully funded.
answer_text: NI Water advised that they could programme an additional £30m of capital expenditure in the remainder of this financial year.   Following the October Monitoring round, I have allocated NI Water a total of £32m of additional funding in total, of which, £19.5m is capital funding for targeted action to release wastewater constraints. NI Water must prioritise investment in line with the available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why he bid for £10 million of capital funding for the Belfast Transport Hub in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being fully funded.
answer_text: A bid for £10 million of capital funding for the Belfast Transport Hub was made in the October Monitoring Round to deal with a pressure on the budget allocated to this project in the current financial year.My officials will continue to work with Translink  to ensure that any capital pressure is managed within its capital allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) why he bid for £10.6 million of capital funding for Special Educational Needs Minor Works in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being funded.
answer_text: The total capital funding requirement for the Department of Education in 2024/25 was £463m.  The initial capital allocation I received was £254m, resulting in a considerable shortfall when compared to identified need. My Department’s bid for £10.6m capital funding for Special Educational Needs Minor Works in October monitoring was to cover pressures identified to provide suitable education facilities, or adaptions to education facilities to enable access to the curriculum for children and young people with a statement of Special Educational Needs.Not receiving the funding will reduce the planned delivery and implementation of Special Educational Needs Minor Works.  My Department will continue to prioritise spend across the many competing priorities for capital investment, to meet the most pressing needs, whilst remaining within budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) why he bid for £30 million of capital funding for Statutory Minor Works in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being funded.
answer_text: The total capital funding requirement for the Department of Education in 2024/25 was £463m.  The initial capital allocation I received was £254m, resulting in a considerable shortfall when compared to identified need. My Department’s bid for £30.0m capital funding for Statutory Minor Works in October monitoring was based on an assessment of the current level of need alongside the capacity to deliver.Not receiving the funding will reduce the planned delivery and implementation of the Minor Works programme which addresses the most urgent works required to keep schools open and children safe.My Department will continue to prioritise spend across the many competing priorities for capital investment, to meet the most pressing needs, whilst remaining within budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) why he bid for £12.5 million of capital funding for Major and School Enhancement Project works in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being funded.
answer_text: The total capital funding requirement for the Department of Education in 2024/25 was £463m.  The initial capital allocation I received was £254m, resulting in a considerable shortfall when compared to identified need. My Department’s bid for £12.5m capital funding for Major and School Enhancement projects in October monitoring was to cover pressures identified in the provision of new build, refurbishment or extension projects at schools in which the condition of their building has rendered these works critical in ensuring that every child is educated in a high-quality learning environment.Not receiving the required funding will delay the planned delivery and progression of several Major Capital Investment and School Enhancement projects in the current financial year. My Department will continue to prioritise spend across the many competing priorities for capital investment, to meet the most pressing needs, whilst remaining within budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) why he bid for £4.4 million for Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being funded.
answer_text: At opening 2024-25 budget stage there was only sufficient Capital DEL available to the Executive to allow it to agree to fund inescapable commitments in DAERA and other departments at that time.  As the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme and the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme, which are part of the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) programme, were due to launch during 2024-25, I decided to progress this programme at the start of the year with the intention of securing additional Capital DEL at in-year Monitoring Rounds to help fund it.		While there was insufficient Capital DEL available to the Executive to fund my bid in the June Monitoring Round, I was successful in securing a £2.0 million  allocation to this important programme at the October Monitoring Round.  In relation to the residual £2.4 million it is likely that this will be able to be funded from within the wider DAERA Capital DEL budget and this is due to be confirmed as part of my Department’s work on the January Monitoring Round.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why he bid for £53.3 million of capital funding for Investment in New Build and Housing for all in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being fully funded.
answer_text: The bid of £53.3 million would have met the budget bid that the Northern Ireland Housing Executive made for the delivery of new social housing in 2024/25.As a consequence of the Housing Executive bid not being fully funded, the ambition to deliver 2,000 new social housing starts will not be achieved. The Housing Executive are currently considering what can be delivered with additional funds following October monitoring round.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why he bid for £12.7 million of capital funding for Retention of House and land sales in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being funded.
answer_text: This bid was for priority improvement works to adapt Housing Executive properties for tenants living with a disability. Whilst receipts from the Housing Executive’s house and land sales are declared to the Department initially, they have historically been returned to the Housing Executive’s Landlord Services, subject to budget availability, to help fund expenditure on disabled adaptations and other investment programmes.However, currently in 2024-25, the budget allocation does not allow the retention of capital receipts by the Housing Executive Landlord, and these will instead be retained by the Department for wider Departmental prioritisation. The implications of this bid not being funded means that the Housing Executive will have to use its Stock Investment Reserve. This position will be reviewed again in the forthcoming January 2025 Monitoring Round.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why he bid for £30.5 million of capital funding for the Pandemic Preparedness Stockpile in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being funded.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions he has had with the UK Government in relation to securing a financial contribution to the revised costings for Casement Park.
answer_text: I wrote to the UK Government on 22 February, 22 March and 4 June 2024 in relation to their financial contribution to Casement Park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Article 161 Agreements in West Tyrone in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and been advised that there have been 37 Article 161 Agreements in total in the West Tyrone constituency over the last five years, with the figure for each year shown below:YearNo. of Article 161 Agreements authorised in West Tyrone constituency20207202112202282023920241For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) why he bid for £26.7 million of capital funding for Education ICT Infrastructure in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) the implications of this not being funded.
answer_text: The total capital funding requirement for the Department of Education in 2024/25 was £463m.  The initial capital allocation I received was £254m, resulting in a considerable shortfall when compared to identified need. My Department’s bid for £26.7m in October monitoring was specifically for Education ICT infrastructure to cover pressures identified in the delivery of much needed ICT infrastructure, ICT operations and cyber security.Not receiving the funding will delay the planned delivery and implementation of ICT infrastructure projects to support business critical services in schools.  My Department will continue to prioritise spend across the many competing priorities for capital investment, to meet the most pressing needs, whilst remaining within budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 18072/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 18072/22-27 was answered on 4 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional costs that will be incurred by the delay in delivering the IT services to schools due to the cancellation of the Fujitsu contract.
answer_text: The procurement process was undertaken in accordance with the Public Contract Regulations (2015), and the Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy as required by legislation.   The process was robust as evidenced by the various Government Gateway Assurance Reviews undertaken at key stages of the process and supported by external commercial and legal expertise.   The Education Authority is required to retender the Strategic Partner element of the contract and as such will incur a cost to do so.  The procurement costs will be significantly reduced as much of the work undertaken in the previous procurement will be reused to support the procurement of a new Strategic Partner.   I was fully briefed by DE Officials and the Chief Executive of the Education Authority on 13 November 2024 as to the seriousness of this issue. At that meeting assurances around a continuation of service provision were received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how robust was the procurement process for the IT contract with Fujitsu.
answer_text: The procurement process was undertaken in accordance with the Public Contract Regulations (2015), and the Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy as required by legislation.   The process was robust as evidenced by the various Government Gateway Assurance Reviews undertaken at key stages of the process and supported by external commercial and legal expertise.   The Education Authority is required to retender the Strategic Partner element of the contract and as such will incur a cost to do so.  The procurement costs will be significantly reduced as much of the work undertaken in the previous procurement will be reused to support the procurement of a new Strategic Partner.   I was fully briefed by DE Officials and the Chief Executive of the Education Authority on 13 November 2024 as to the seriousness of this issue. At that meeting assurances around a continuation of service provision were received.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to offer grants for domestic residences to install solar panels.
answer_text: As you may be aware, the Windsor framework enables the extension to the North of the VAT relief for energy-saving materials that was previously available only in Britain. That means that people here will also be able to apply zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials, such as solar panels.Homeowners may also wish to seek independent advice on solar panels and other energy efficiency matters from organisations such as NI Energy Advice, the Energy Saving Trust or Citizen’s Advice. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a timetable for public realm work in Strabane.
answer_text: Derry City and Strabane District Council has responsibility for delivery of the Strabane Town Centre Public Realm project.  The Council has provided an updated timetable for the works and anticipates a contractor will be appointed by Autumn 2025 with works expected to last for a period of 14-18 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any discussions he and his Department had with the Education Authority in relation to the decision not to proceed with the IT contact with Fujitsu.
answer_text: The procurement process was undertaken in accordance with the Public Contract Regulations (2015), and the Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy as required by legislation.   The process was robust as evidenced by the various Government Gateway Assurance Reviews undertaken at key stages of the process and supported by external commercial and legal expertise.   The Education Authority is required to retender the Strategic Partner element of the contract and as such will incur a cost to do so.  The procurement costs will be significantly reduced as much of the work undertaken in the previous procurement will be reused to support the procurement of a new Strategic Partner.   I was fully briefed by DE Officials and the Chief Executive of the Education Authority on 13 November 2024 as to the seriousness of this issue. At that meeting assurances around a continuation of service provision were received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the evidence given by his predecessor at the Covid-19 Inquiry; and (ii) to detail any changes in structure that have been put in place to manage future crises.
answer_text: The evidence given by my predecessor at the Covid-19 Inquiry is a matter for Mr Swann MP as it is his personal reflection.My department remains the Lead Government Department for pandemic preparedness and response and has put in place new structures to support our future planning. This includes a dedicated Pandemic Resilience Branch within the department and new structures to build response resilience across the Health and Social Care system. My officials are also working closely with The Executive Office to develop a cross-government programme of work to coordinate planning and resilience across all departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists 2024 report that states that the waiting list for gynaecology treatments has increased by 95 per cent since March 2020.
answer_text: Waiting lists for gynaecology services are much longer than we would wish and there has been many pressures across the health system, including within the gynaecology service.At present three of the five Trusts are in the process of transitioning to a major new IT system, called Epic, and this makes it difficult to get an accurate position on waiting lists. It also makes a direct comparison to the waiting lists in March 2020 very difficult.However, I do acknowledge that if we compared waiting lists to March 2020 there would be a significant increase on patients waiting.Staff across the Health and Social Care system, who are responsible for, and manage, gynaecology services are working hard to try and implement ways to help to address this issue.The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust, Public Health Agency (PHA) and General Practitioner (GP) representation, to oversee ongoing service improvement recommendations and implementation at a local and regional level – this includes overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report”.  This report aims at maximising capacity while addressing the outpatient and surgical waiting lists through introducing a number of improvement techniques to the service regionally and through pathway redesign between primary and secondary care.As part of this work, SPPG has just started to establish specific Task and Finish Groups to consider services such as Menopause, Endometriosis, Uro-gynae, Fertility and Recurrent Pregnancy Loss.  They will also be establishing a Regional Engagement Forum in the coming months to garner expert user feedback from stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to review the availability of cardiac rehabilitation to ensure a consistency of approach in the services provided across the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The SPPG has established a Cardiac Rehabilitation Strategic Leadership Group to devise a standardised cardiac rehabilitation pathway in order to ensure equitable access for service users from the five Health and Social Care Trusts. This work is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement he and his Department has had with the Trussell Trust; and (ii) any actions he is taking to reverse the 93 per cent rise in the use of foodbanks in the last five years.
answer_text: During the past five years, my officials have engaged with the Trussell Trust at a regional level as well as with individual members of its network.  This includes engagement on the rollout of the Social Supermarkets programme funded by my Department, the aim of which is to address the root causes of food insecurity alongside supporting households facing food insecurity.  As a representative on the Anti-Poverty Co-design Group, the Trussell Trust contributed towards the development of the Anti-Poverty Strategy. Alongside other stakeholders, Trussell Trust was engaged in informing the aims and objectives of the ‘Integrated Advice Partnership Fund’ which I launched earlier this month.   Most recently, I accepted an invitation from the Trussell Trust and met with the organisation at a local foodbank during a visit to Larne.  Whilst my Department has no power to set different rates for Northern Ireland in the annual benefits Up-rating Order, it has its own unique initiatives to help reverse the use of foodbanks such as the Discretionary Support Service. Providing a person meets the eligibility criteria, Discretionary Support provides help towards short-term living expenses such as food costs or essential household items, to people experiencing financial difficulties. The Trussell Trust had a representative on the Independent Panel who undertook a review of Discretionary Support in 2021.More information about the financial support available through the Discretionary Support scheme can be found on the NIDirect website www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-supportMy Department provides annual funding of £6.6m to the independent advice and debt sector who play a critical role in addressing poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action he and his Department are taking to reduce waiting times for gynaecology treatments.
answer_text: Waiting lists for gynaecology services are much longer than we would wish. However, given the publication of the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology and the recent media attention on this service, gynaecology will need to be a health service priority going forward. The GIRFT Report for Gynaecology report sets out recommendations designed to maximise capacity, reduce waiting times, improve patient care and outcomes. The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has established a Regional Gynaecology Improvement Forum, with Trust and Public Health representation. This forum will oversee ongoing service improvement, and the implementation and development of gynaecology service pathways at primary, intermediate and secondary care level for local and regional populations, as well as overseeing the implementation of recommendations from the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report. As part of this work, Task and Finish groups are being established to take forward improvement work, and pathway development for a range of areas including:Uro-gynae with mesh;Heavy menstrual loss;Recurrent pregnancy lossMenopause; and EndometriosisTrusts have also been asked to proceed with establishing their own local implementation groups, to include key stakeholders/representatives of staff working in gynaecology roles (clinicians, theatre staff, senior managers and Allied Health Professionals), and to develop an appropriate action plan for taking forward the recommendations which are relevant to their individual Trust.It is recognised that some of the recommendations will require additional investment which is not currently available, and this will inevitably impact on delivery.While this work is underway patients are being managed based on clinical urgency and therefore waiting times can vary depending on each patient’s clinical condition, but considerable work is underway to try to address pressures in this service, despite the current financial constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Driver and Vehicle Agency's review of driver licensing fees.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency’s review of driver licensing fees is ongoing, and the Department will consult as required on any proposed changes to the existing fees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he and his Department has had with Social Enterprise NI in relation to increased costs contained in the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: The Budget will affect local businesses in a variety of ways, though the nature of that will depend upon the characteristics of each individual business, including its size, which sector it operates in and how it chooses to manage new arrangements.There are welcome benefits for workers as a result of the increases in the National Living Wage and National Minimum Wage. However, rather than offsetting this increase in pay with a decrease in taxes, the Budget increases employer National Insurance contributions. At a time of high costs on businesses this will put many businesses under more strain.The full impacts of the Autumn Budget on individual sectors, including the social enterprise sector, will take time to become clear.My officials and I will continue to engage with local social enterprise business stakeholders and consider the full detail of the announcements, as well as the possible implications for the North’s economy, in the context of my economic plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he and his Department has had with Post Office UK in relation to potential branch closures and job losses in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Under the Northern Ireland Act, postal services are a reserved matter and come under the responsibility of the Department for Business and Trade. The Post Office do not consult with my Department on future planning.There are statutory consultation processes which are engaged in cases of larger scale redundancies. It is my expectation that all employers would comply with their responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he and his Department has had with the Housing Executive in relation to Barnett consequentials announced in the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated and it will be a decision for the Executive to decide how the amounts confirmed for 2024/25 by the Finance Minister in her oral statement in the Assembly will be allocated.DfC has had no specific conversations with the Housing Executive in relation to the Barnett consequentials. The Department is in regular contact with the Housing Executive in relation to all its financial and budgeting requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Empty Homes strategy.
answer_text: The Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan was operational from 2013 to 2018. A review of the Strategy was completed and published on my Department’s website in 2021. The Review can be accessed at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-empty-homes-strategy-and-action-plan-2013-%E2%80%93-2018I have no plans to review the strategy a second time.While the Review recognised that the Strategy had limited success in addressing housing stress, as a result of this work we are now much better informed about the complexities of this issue and the reasons why so many properties appear to be vacant.		A broad programme of work is planned to deliver transformative change across housing, including the delivery of the Housing Supply Strategy. The draft Strategy includes several long-term commitments and actions which will include consideration of actions around housing led regeneration and the reuse of existing buildings to generate additional supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the 839 developments that remain unadopted, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not retain details of unadopted developments by constituency.  However, my Department does hold details of developments with 80% occupancy but which remain unadopted and these can broken down by Council Area. The list is further categorised as follows.Priority 1 – 80% occupancy greater than 5 years oldPriority 2 – 80% occupancy between 3-5 years oldPriority 3 – 80% occupancy less than 3 years oldThis list has been deposited in the Assembly Library.I would also like to offer a clarification.  Officials have identified a typographical error in the information provided for the recent Motion on The High Number of Unadopted Housing Developments and Roads.  The backlog figure quoted should have been 893 and not 839.  A letter correcting this error has been sent to the Speaker.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his discussions with the British Government on the introduction of an inheritance tax on farms worth over £1 million.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of the National Pharmacy Association recommendation that pharmacies withdraw services.
answer_text: At this time the only information my Department has received from the National Pharmacy Association (NPA) in relation to pharmacies withdrawing services are the summary results of the survey for NI. I have, therefore, agreed to meet with NPA representatives to hear their concerns. In relation to the specific services referred to in the survey: medicines delivery, prescription collection and provision of free blisters (outside of DDA requirements), these are non-commissioned services and withdrawal would be a commercial decision for an individual pharmacy contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions that have taken place with the British Government in relation to the reform of medicine funding arrangements for pharmacies.
answer_text: No discussions have taken place with the British Government in relation to the reform of medicine funding arrangements for pharmacies because health is a devolved matter. The annual review of commissioned community pharmacy services in 2023/24 shows that the level of government funding for community pharmacies in NI compares favourably to other parts of the UK. It also shows that funding for community pharmacies in NI has increased in recent years. The opening investment total for community pharmacy services was £104m in April 2018. By April 2023 it was £128m and in April 2024 it stood at £147m, an increase of £43m or 41% in six years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on potential legal action in relation to the A5.
answer_text: I can confirm that legal proceedings have been issued against my Department in relation to the authorisation of the new road between Strabane and Ballygawley. This is currently going through due legal process. My officials and I are currently working with our legal team and will robustly defend against this challenge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support and help that is offered to those suffering from postnatal depression.
answer_text: The last two years have seen the roll-out of the specialist perinatal mental health teams across the five Trusts. These teams provide a comprehensive service to women with a wide range of mental health problems in both the antenatal and postnatal periods, including illnesses such as postnatal depression and postnatal psychosis.As well as these specialist services there are a wide range of services in maternity, primary care and the community and voluntary sector to help women with postnatal depression.In addition, my officials are also progressing the provision of a Mother and Baby Unit.  In early 2023 my Department commissioned an independent review to make recommendations and provide advice on a suitable model and location for a Mother and Baby Unit, which would best meet the needs of the population.   The Department received the final report from the review team in mid-September 2023.  It provided recommendations on the preferred service model for the unit, and the location of the Mother and Baby Unit, with the Belfast City Hospital site identified as the most suitable location. At that point, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust was invited to prepare a business case for the provision of the proposed regional unit.The business case for Mother and Baby Unit has been submitted to my Department for scrutiny. It should be noted that progression of this project will remain subject to the necessary funding for the unit being identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14656/22-27, for an update on discussions with the Westminster Government to extend the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme.
answer_text: I outlined the key issues facing the NI agri-food industry as a result of UK Immigration policy changes in a letter to the Defra Secretary of State on 10th October 2024.  		In particular, I suggested that the introduction of a two-year occupation-specific bespoke visa route for the mushroom harvesting sector would be more suitable than the Seasonal Workers Visa and this would help ensure that the sector in NI could continue to compete with their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland. I received a response from the Defra Secretary of State on 29th October 2024, in which he rejected this suggestion and pointed to the Seasonal Worker visa continuing to offer businesses in NI with a bespoke visa for the horticulture and poultry sectors’ labour demands. I am disappointed by the response and continue to raise these issues with the UK Government to see if solutions can be found. 		On 18th November 2024, I outlined the pressures faced by the mushroom and fisheries sectors with regards to labour shortages with Daniel Zeichner MP, Minister for Food Security and Rural Affairs at a meeting of the Inter-Ministerial Group for Environment Food and Rural Affairs. I will continue to make representations on these matters to help Defra communicate these issues to the Home Office. Defra are developing an Agri-Food Workforce Strategy and I will ensure my officials are involved in its development.I welcome the UK Government’s announcement on 21st October 2024 that the quota allocation for the seasonal worker scheme for 2025 will be 45,000 visas, which is adequate to meet demand.  However, the longer-term future of the seasonal agricultural workers scheme is still to be announced as the Government has yet to formally respond to the Migration Advisory Committee’s seasonal worker visa review findings and recommendations, published on 16th July 2024.It remains vital that labour solutions can be found for NI sectors currently reliant on migrant labour, until such time that automation is more advanced and accessible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of a new Emergency Department build for Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust has a submitted proposal for Phase 5.2 of capital works at Altnagelvin Hospital, including the redevelopment of the Emergency Department, which is included in the future capital plan.The Trust will be invited to submit a business case for this proposal once budget affordability is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the criteria that will be set by his Department to decide which projects proceed to Phase 2 of the Small Business Research Initiative.
answer_text: My Department has not yet prescribed specific selection criteria yet for Phase 2 of the Lough Neagh Blue Green Algae Small Business Research Initiative. Selection of those to proceed to Phase 2, including the number selected, will be based upon appropriate and objective selection criteria once duly developed and affordability with respect to the available budget.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will bring forward legislation on assisted dying during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: Assisting or encouraging someone to commit suicide or killing someone on compassionate grounds (euthanasia) remains a criminal offence. The Department of Health has no plans to bring forward legislation on assisted dying.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 16309/22-27, to detail the benefits of the decision by Ofgem to approve a new electricity link between Scotland and Northern Ireland.
answer_text: On 12th November 2024 Ofgem reassessed their earlier decision and approved the LirIC electricity interconnector between Scotland and the north. Interconnectors have the potential to offer benefits such as: improved security of supply, reduced wholesale electricity costs and the ability to export surplus renewable electricity.The LirIC interconnector requires approval from both Ofgem and the Utility Regulator. The Utility Regulator is currently working on the LirIC licence application, with a decision expected by the end of this year.  Whilst my Department is working closely with the Utility Regulator on interconnection, it has no role in the licence approval process and must remain impartial.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the unsuccessful departmental capital bids in the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: Departments submitted capital bids totalling £346.1 million against £68 million of available funding. Details of the 2024-2025 October Monitoring Round Capital Bids and capital allocations are available on the Department of Finance website at -	https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/oral-statement-public-expenditure-2024-25-october-monitoring-11-november-2024

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how they plan to spend the £7million allocated to their Department through the October Monitoring Round in relation to victims’ payments.
answer_text: As of the week, commencing 11th November 2024, the Victims’ Payments Board has received 10,282 applications since the Scheme opened on 31 August 2021.  More than 2,200 determinations have been made by the Board and 1,440 applications have been determined as eligible, resulting in payments to eligible applicants totalling over £66 million to date. 	The Victims Payments Scheme is demand led and there are a range of uncertainties associated with the Scheme which make forecasting the overall costs extremely challenging. Payments to eligible applicants in the current financial year are estimated at just under £50m and the additional funding allocated in the October Monitoring Round will be used for this purpose. Budgetary requirements for the Scheme are kept under constant review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to sign off on 1,400 new social build homes in the next four months before the end of 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Following confirmation of the extra budget for the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) in the October monitoring round, it will be for the Housing Executive, as the commissioning body for the SHDP, to assess how the additional budget may best be used to meet identified housing need before any final starts figure for 2024/25 can be confirmed. I am advised that work on this is underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he plans to spend the £2 million allocated through the October Monitoring Round for Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation.
answer_text: The Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Programme is a key priority for my Department.  In relation to the £2.0m Capital DEL that the Executive allocated to this programme in the October Monitoring Round, this will be allocated to the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme, the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme and the Village Catalyst Programme to meet planned spend.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he plans to spend the general allocation of £11.3 million allocated through the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: My Department submitted non-ring-fenced pressures of £24.1m in October Monitoring. The £11.3m general allocation received will be used to offset this pressure. Work is ongoing to consider specific allocations against the pressures faced and to ensure my Department can live within its 2024-25 Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he plans to spend the £22.8 million allocated through the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The £22.8 million allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round will be used towards fully funding my department’s Winter Service, with an additional £1m targeted intervention for gully emptying. In addition, allocations have been provided towards the maintenance of our roads. Funding has also been allocated to NI Water and Translink. NI Water has been allocated £11.5m, which is half of the total resource allocation received and Translink has been provided with £6.5m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he plans to spend the £39.6 million allocated through the October Monitoring Round towards capital pressures.
answer_text: The £39.6 million allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round has provided additional funding towards the structural maintenance of our roads and the street lighting network. NI Water is also receiving an extra £19.5m in capital funding for targeted action to release wastewater constraints, which is almost half of the total capital allocation received. Translink will also receive an additional capital allocation of £5m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he plans to spend the £350 million allocated through the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The Department of Health reported, during October Monitoring, a total short fall of £450m.  This included £130m to fund the continuation of existing services and £320m for non-contractual pay to match the English pay offers.The Department received £350m from October Monitoring leaving a £100m shortfall.  This leaves the Department in a position where it cannot achieve both financial breakeven and fully meet pay parity.Options are now being considered on how to move forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he plans to spend the £17 million allocated through the October Monitoring Round to partially mitigate the impact of the cut in winter fuel payments for pensioners.
answer_text: I refer the member to my statement on 19th November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered adopting Northern Ireland-wide standards for staff training, similar to Scotland, Wales and England.
answer_text: The various bodies responsible for the delivery of post-graduate education and training for healthcare professions, operate on Northern Ireland wide basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the budget for staff training in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this level of information. This information is the responsibility of the HSC Trusts as the employers of our HSC workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 16769/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 16931/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 16931/22-27 was answered on 8 November 2024

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered creating a bespoke agency to co-ordinate NHS staff training in order to improve consistency and quality, similar to Scotland, Wales and England.
answer_text: I fully recognise the benefits of healthcare professions working together and creating training opportunities on a multi-disciplinary basis. My Department is convening discussions among the leaders of our post-graduate training bodies to explore how such opportunities can be better realised in practice. Any review of the post-graduate education and training landscape would require careful scoping as well as a firm commitment to resourcing what would be a potentially significant project involving the full range of stakeholders across the professions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the revival of the Portadown to Derry rail line, connecting Omagh and Strabane, as contained in the All-Ireland Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The new line from Portadown to Derry via Dungannon, Omagh and Strabane has been classified as a long-term intervention in the All-Island Strategic Rail Review, i.e., to be delivered between 2040 and 2050, however, this is an indicative roadmap and further work will be needed to prioritise the interventions and determine the sequencing and phasing of individual projects.  Translink have a number of feasibility studies underway, linked to recommendations in the Rail Review, which include a preliminary feasibility study on the new Portadown to Derry line. This study is due to report by spring/summer next year.While I am keen to deliver what I can within the remainder of my mandate, I will need to consider which projects to prioritise as funding opportunities become available. The outcomes from the ongoing feasibility studies will help inform this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any work undertaken to reduce the minimum age of 60 for bowel cancer screening.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the latest Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services waiting list figures, which state that 299 patients in the Western Health and Social Care Trust have been waiting for an assessment for over nine weeks, compared to 155 patients in March 2023.
answer_text: The Western Trust CAMHS continues to be challenged in meeting access targets with respect to 9 week routine (new patient) appointments. I can confirm, that as of 14th November 2024 there are no young people requiring an emergency or urgent assessment waiting longer than the identified time frame of 24 hours for emergency and 5 days for urgent assessment. Monthly variations in the number of young people waiting for routine assessment are determined by fluctuating demand and capacity variables within the service. For instance, demand for WHSCT CAMHS services was higher in the first 2 quarters of 2024/25 financial year compared to the same period in 2023/24. 637 referrals were accepted between April-Sept 2024 compared to 571 in 2023/24. This increase of 66 was 11.5% up on previous year period.Across WHSCT CAMHS, workforce stabilisation and recruitment is the most challenging issue. CAMHS currently is operating at a 34% staffing deficit, with only 66% of actual staff in post. The service is engaged in concurrent recruitment drives in their effort to backfill into these vacant positions. The service continues to face challenges recruiting appropriately skilled and experienced staff into specialist roles. WT CAMHS continue to work in partnership with Senior Trust Managers, Trust Quality Improvement and Performance colleagues with regular monitoring meetings with DOH regarding waiting times / access to the service and ensuring positive outcomes for young people in receipt of the service. Alongside vacancies, WHSCT CAMHS is experiencing an increase in Clinical acuity levels and risk laden co-morbid presentations. This requires intensive working through multi-disciplinary and multi-agency involvement/partnership in safety planning to mitigate risks. This further reduces capacity within the service as there is a need for co - working of cases. Episodes of care are generally longer and more intensive for this cohort of young people which impacts on patient flow.My Department recognises the challenges across Mental Health Services and developed the Mental Health Strategy in response. Action 32 recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. My Department is committed to create a workforce that will meet the needs of the population and which is fit for the future. I am committed to working with Executive colleagues to secure all necessary resources to meet the actions outlined in the Mental Health Strategy. The Mental Health Strategy also acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to deve
