question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to review the policy for prostate cancer testing.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee which advises the four UK countries, last considered the evidence for screening healthy men with no symptoms for prostate cancer in 2020 and concluded that screening was not recommended.  This is because of the poor ability of the prostate specific antigen (PSA) test to predict cancer, which could lead to unnecessary tests that carry risks of potentially life-changing harm. Additionally, some prostate cancers may not produce elevated PSA levels, leading to false-negative results that provide false reassurance. The UK NSC has commissioned a prostate cancer screening evidence review and cost effectiveness model, and it is expected to review this work during 2025. The UK NSC process will include a public consultation. The UK Health Departments, through the National Institute of Health and Care Research (NIHR), have invested in a significant programme of research to address the evidence gaps in this important area. A clinical trial (called the TRANSFORM trial), which will compare the most promising tests and provide evidence about the best way to screen for prostate cancer, is due to start in early 2025.My department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes. Anyone with symptoms which they believe may be related to their prostate gland should contact their GP as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Simon Community submission to the consultation on the draft Programme for Government, which states that it is concerned with the lack of detail, outcomes, milestones and measurable targets within the draft.
answer_text: My Department will work closely with colleagues in the Executive office to consider all responses to the public consultation on the draft Programme for Government relevant to our work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timescale for the roll-out of the Integrated Advice Partnership Fund.
answer_text: Following my announcement of the Integrated Advice Partnership Fund on 5 November, the Department issued a Memorandum of Understanding to all 11 district councils.  Councils will progress proposals through their internal governance processes and funds will be released on return of signed Memorandums of Understanding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Renters’ Voice No-Fault Eviction survey findings that states that 62 per cent of respondents said they had delayed reporting a repair to their landlord or letting agent for fear of being evicted.
answer_text: I am concerned that any tenant would delay reporting the need for a repair because of fear of eviction. An eviction can be a very stressful situation for a household, and I would advise anyone in this situation to contact their local Council Environmental Health Team or the Housing Rights tenant helpline for advice.    There is already a legislative requirement for the landlord to keep their rental property in repair, including any installation for water, gas or electricity and the heating system. It is also already an offence for a landlord to harass a tenant with the aim of forcing them to leave their home. This includes withdrawing or withholding services which the tenant reasonably requires to live in the property and would likely cover all those installations I have listed. There are penalties in place for any landlord who does not fulfil these obligations, and local councils have the necessary powers to enforce these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Farm Incomes in Northern Ireland 2022-2023 report which shows a fall in income for five out of seven farm types.
answer_text: The ‘Farm Incomes in Northern Ireland 2022-2023’ report provides information on the financial position for the main farm types and the performance of individual farm enterprises.  The report shows that while average Farm Business Income measured across all farm businesses above 0.5 Standard Labour Requirements (SLRs) increased from £43,252 to £51,043 per farm between 2021/22 and 2022/23 there were decreases in average Farm Business Income for most of the individual farm types mainly due to rising costs.  The increase in the average Farm Business Income measured across all farm businesses is mainly attributable to the rise in average income of Dairy farms which was due to the substantially higher milk prices during 2022/23.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of the Pathway Fund by constituency, for each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Statistical Review of Northern Ireland Agriculture 2023.
answer_text: The ‘Statistical Review of Northern Ireland Agriculture 2023’ is a compendium report covering a wide range of statistics across farming, the environment and rural issues.  In particular, the report shows that there was a substantial fall in farm incomes for 2023; agricultural production levels have been growing in recent years and remain high; and that very significant environmental problems remain to be addressed e.g. river water quality and greenhouse gas emissions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Climate Commissioner.
answer_text: We refer the Member to the answer given to AQO 989/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5791/22-27, for an update on EOTAS provision for the Strabane area.
answer_text: Education Otherwise Than at School (EOTAS) and Behaviour Support arrangements are in place within the Strabane area for both primary and post primary aged pupils with social, behavioural, emotional and wellbeing needs.Post Primary EOTAS provision in the Strabane area operates out of Knockavoe Resource Centre, located in the grounds of Knockavoe Special School, and offers up to 12 full time placements for young people in Key Stage 4. Presently, Post Primary EOTAS provision in Strabane is operating at full capacity for this academic year. Fir Trees EOTAS provision for primary school aged children in Strabane and the wider area operated from the same site and was temporarily closed in September 2023. Arrangements for the delivery of two sessions per week in Strabane Youth Resource Centre continue to be available for those primary aged pupils directly affected by the temporary closure of Fir Trees EOTAS, and those requiring a bespoke social engagement programme. The Education Authority (EA) have advised that work remains ongoing to identify replacement accommodation for Fir Trees EOTAS.   EA officers have identified two accommodation options outside of Strabane as potential temporary replacement accommodation. A cost analysis and a building survey are being completed and the EA advise that, while not in the town of Strabane, these options could be suitable to address the critical need for replacement accommodation on a temporary basis for Fir Trees EOTAS. The development of a wider strategic EOTAS area plan is also progressing, which will include the identification of a suitable site for permanent Primary EOTAS in the Strabane area.As soon as temporary accommodation has been secured for Fir Trees EOTAS, details will be communicated to local primary schools and the parents of those pupils currently being supported by Primary Behaviour Support and Provisions

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 14838/22-27.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to promote organ transplantation under the Health (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.  In line with this duty, my Department oversees a comprehensive rolling programme of outreach and engagement activity to promote knowledge and informed discussion about organ donation, which is aimed at driving registrations on the NHS Organ Donor Register (ODR), and sustaining and increasing rates of consent to both living and deceased organ donation. The programme is delivered by the Public Health Agency (PHA) through a partnership approach with Health and Social Care Trusts, NHS Blood and Transplant, local government, and charity partners and spans the community, voluntary and education sectors. It includes local media campaigns which are delivered across a range of platforms and tailored for maximum impact in line with an intelligence-led communications plan, and uses health intelligence data to measure and report on the impact of promotional activities. The appointment of an Organ Donation Promotion Manager in July 2020 allowed specific focus to be applied to the development and delivery of this programme, however the statutory duty did not formally come into effect until April 2022. A detailed report of this activity from 2022/23 is currently being finalised for submission to the Assembly in the coming months. This will provide details and impacts of all outreach and engagement activity, partnerships, information campaigns, events such as the Balmoral Show and Belfast Marathon, and focused initiatives including Organ Donation Week.In summary, during 2022/23 organ donation rates in Northern Ireland were comparably high for deceased donation, at 30 donors per million of population (pmp). It is important to note that in any year, only a small number of people (around 1%) will die in circumstances where organ donation is clinically possible, so all activity is geared towards increasing opt-in registrations on the ODR and increasing the consent rate in these cases. The consent rate for donation after death in Northern Ireland in 2022/23 was 65% which is comparable to other regions, although the overall UK consent rate fell from 66% to 62% in that period. As at 31 March 2024, 55% of the population (1,048,273 people) in Northern Ireland had registered as an organ donor on the NHS Organ Donor Register – well above the UK average of 42%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14836/22-27, (i) what criteria is set for a referral to the lung transplant waiting list; and (ii) what input do patients have into that process.
answer_text: NHS Blood and Transport (NHSBT) oversees the allocation of all organs in the UK based on compatibility/matching of organ to potential recipients, and is responsible for the development and setting criteria for the selection of adult and paediatric patients onto the UK national transplant list and, where necessary, criteria for de-selection.  The criteria apply to all patients receiving organs from deceased donors.  All eight highly specialised NHS designated units in England use the same selection criteria.(i)The broad selection criteria are that the paitent must:Be on maximal medical therapy Have a potential survival benefit from transplantation or a potential significant improvement in their quality of life as a result of transplantationHave projected post-operative survival >5 years with a quality of life acceptable to the patientThere are also disease specific criteria which will be considered. These can be found in the document, NHSBT POL231/7 – Lung Candidate Selection Criteria on the NHSBT website.(ii)Belfast trust stated that it is essential for the patient's family/carers to be involved in the assessment process. These initial assessment procedures often follow outpatient consultation and are undertaken over several days.  A number of factors are used to inform decisions around the appropriateness of transplantation in each patient context and these must all be made explicit to the patients and their relatives.Selection cannot be an exact science, and any patient who is dissatisfied with the decision made in his/her/their case is entitled to an opinion from a second transplant centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria set by his Department in relation to non-departmental public bodies spending money on legal disputes.
answer_text: The Department and its Non-Departmental Public Bodies (NDPBs) apply the principles contained in Managing Public Money Northern Ireland (MPMNI) relating to the authority for expenditure, regularity, propriety and value for money in all expenditure decisions, including those related to legal disputes. In line with MPMNI, legal advice underpins decisions relating to legal disputes and the value for money assessment for these decisions.  Although MPMNI advises that Department of Finance (DoF) consent is required for all expenditure or resource commitments, in practice DoF delegates to departments authority to spend within predefined limits, using a framework of delegations set out in DAO (DoF) 08/21 – Departmental delegations / requirements for DoF approval.  A further level of delegation is provided by my Department to its NDPBs. These limits are set out within the individual Management Statement and Financial Memorandum (MSFM) for each body. Each MSFM is approved by DoF and published on the relevant NDPB’s website. Any proposed payment exceeding an NDPB’s delegated authority is submitted to the Department for approval.  Approval requests for payments which exceed the Department’s delegated authority are in turn submitted to DoF for approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how many people visited a library as part of Book Week NI, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Libraries NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“During Book Week NI 2024, 21 October - 26 October, the total footfall in 98 libraries was 77,662.  Libraries NI does not have the footfall data for Book Week NI  2022 or 2023. With the rollout and implementation of new IT systems, the footfall data for Book Week 2022 and 2023 cannot be validated.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Libraries NI directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17001/22-27, given that the minimum unit pricing for alcohol in Scotland is 65p per unit, what price does he plan to set in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The modelling taken forward by the University of Sheffield considered a range of possible levels for minimum unit pricing, from 40 pence up to 80 pence per unit. While several different social and health outcomes are discussed in the modelling, in general terms it shows that a fall in alcohol consumption would be smaller for lower minimum unit pricing (MUP) thresholds and larger for higher thresholds. I have not yet decided on the level at which MUP should be set. Subject to Executive agreement to the principle of legislation in this area, I intend to reflect further on the available evidence and to take on board the views of the Committee for Health before setting the level. Any future regulations in this respect will be subject to Assembly scrutiny and agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the total funding spent on the Derry to London public service obligation route for each of the last seven years; and (ii) whether there are any guarantees regarding pricing on that route.
answer_text: The table below details the total funding spent on the City of Derry Airport (CoDA) to London Public Service Obligation (PSO) Route for each of the last seven years. In each year this funding has been split with the British Department for Transport;Total Funding2023/24£2,141,0012022/23£2,227,8582021/22£2,081,5232020/21£2,0395892019/20£2,071,3222018/19£1,880,1832018/17£1,775,778Derry City and Strabane District Council (DCSDC) are responsible for the contract with Loganair which operates the PSO. This contract ensures that an adequate minimum level of service is maintained and includes the payment of a subsidy to the airline to cover the operating losses on the route when accounting for the costs and revenue generated by the service, including a reasonable profit.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on engagements they have had with the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland in relation to repealing and replacing the Legacy Act.
answer_text: We have met with the Secretary of State on numerous occasions, and we have raised this issue with him.  We have also discussed this with the Prime Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of ash dieback in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Since the first finding of ash dieback in Northern Ireland (NI) in 2012, surveys have since indicated that the disease has been found to be present in most parts of NI. Monitoring surveys for the disease has confirmed its presence within the majority of individual 10km grid squares across NI.  Ash dieback becoming established in NI has mirrored its similar progression across European countries. This led to an EU legislative amendment to the Plant Health Regulations in 2019, which removed ash dieback as a quarantine pest. On that basis, affected trees are no longer required to be removed as a disease control measure, however it remains important for landowners to exercise their public safety responsibilities regarding the management of affected trees and woodlands. There is significant research ongoing across the UK and Europe, including a DAERA commissioned AFBI research project to investigate potential resistance across the native ash tree populations and tree breeding for enhanced resistance. It is expected that ash trees will continue to be affected by the disease in the future, however there is some evidence that more tolerant and resistant trees will be identified.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current level of ammonia emissions in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The National Atmospheric Emissions Inventory - Air pollution Inventories for England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland: 2005-2022, was published on 24 October 2024.  The report presents air pollutant emissions inventories for eight priority pollutants including ammonia (NH3). The report presents the most recent data on total ammonia emissions for Northern Ireland, broken down into different sectors including agriculture. The report shows that total ammonia emissions in Northern Ireland in 2022 were 31.82 kilotonnes of which 30.86 kilotonnes were from agriculture. The report is available at https://naei.energysecurity.gov.uk/reports/air-pollutant-inventories-england-scotland-wales-and-northern-ireland-2005-2022 Further information on the levels of ammonia measured at 28 sites across Northern Ireland, which are part of the United Kingdom Eutrophying & Acidifying Network (UKEAP) National Atmospheric Monitoring Network, is available at https://uk-air.defra.gov.uk/networks/network-info?view=nh3 and includes data up to March 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on enforcement action taken against dredging at the River Foyle.
answer_text: My Department’s Marine Licensing branch does not have any active dredge licences or applications for the Foyle catchment. There are currently no active enforcement cases or investigations underway in the Lough Foyle catchment from a marine licence perspective.  NIEA has also not been made aware of any dredging activity in the River Foyle system, nor has there been any consent sought for dredging in the non-tidal stretches.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of current salmon migration along rivers in Tyrone.
answer_text: There are a number of primary salmon rivers that have sections located in county Tyrone. These include the River Mourne and tributaries, the Ballinderry River and Blackwater River. Atlantic salmon are monitored at Sion Mills Weir on the River Mourne where salmon migrating to the River Mourne and tributaries are counted. Not all fish go through the fish counter so the count there is only a proportion of the overall run. Counts for the period January to September 2024 are above the 5-year average for the same time period. The DAERA assessment of salmon migrating to the Ballinderry River and Blackwater River is carried out by AFBI, and indicates that they are higher than those recorded in 2023. The data has not yet been finalised for these rivers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of research from Scotland in relation to the impact of the introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: As part of the introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol (MUP) in Scotland, a 5-year evaluation into the impact of MUP was commissioned to inform future decisions by the Scottish Government. This evaluation sets out strong evidence that MUP reduced deaths directly caused by alcohol consumption in Scotland compared to what would have happened in the absence of MUP. The overall reduction was driven by reductions in deaths due to chronic causes, such as alcoholic liver disease. Evidence similarly shows a reduction in wholly alcohol attributable hospital admissions due to chronic causes. There is also strong and consistent evidence of a reduction in alcohol consumption following MUP implementation. Total alcohol sales reduced by 3% driven entirely by a reduction in sales through the off-trade (supermarkets and other shops). Those households that purchased the most alcohol prior to MUP also reduced their purchasing the most after implementation. Further information on the evaluation in Scotland is available at: https://publichealthscotland.scot/media/20366/evaluating-the-impact-of-minimum-unit-pricing-for-alcohol-in-scotland-final-report.pdf. The NI modelling shows similar estimated outcomes: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/consultations/health/doh-mup-revised-modelling.PDF and indicates that MUP could have significant positive benefits for reducing the harm alcohol can cause, reducing the impact on our health service with fewer alcohol-related hospital admissions, and reducing the number of alcohol-related deaths. MUP can have further positive impacts on wider society, with the model suggesting Northern Ireland could also experience fewer alcohol-attributable crimes each year. Having considered the options and the evidence, I believe that MUP has the potential to have a real and lasting positive impact on the level of alcohol misuse in Northern Ireland. This is a cross-cutting issue, and I intend to discuss the potential introduction of MUP in Northern Ireland with my Executive colleagues in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of people who have died from alcohol-specific causes in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office. Table 1 below shows the numbers of alcohol-specific deaths by parliamentary constituency, 2018 to 2022. Please note that the data are based on the year the death was registered rather than the year it occurred.  Events such as alcohol-specific deaths may have to be referred to the coroner, only to be registered after completion of the investigation. Therefore, the number of alcohol specific deaths registered in a particular year may differ to the total which occurred in the same period.Table 1: Number of alcohol-specific deaths Registered in Northern Ireland by Parliamentary Constituency, 2018-2022Parliamentary Constituency20182019202020212022Belfast East1917142025Belfast North3028353329Belfast South1222221328Belfast West2724332331East Antrim1518122114East Londonderry1417201611Fermanagh and South Tyrone99181514Foyle2327253429Lagan Valley1616141114Mid Ulster121591416Newry and Armagh1625232823North Antrim1013211114North Down151617128South Antrim1614182515South Down612181116Strangford101971116Upper Bann1724253125West Tyrone1720202128Total284336351350356Alcohol deaths are defined using the National Statistics definition which includes conditions known to be exclusively caused by alcohol.  Deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision) using codes F10, G31.2, G62.1, I42.6, K29.2, K70, K73, K74, K86.0, X45, X65 and Y15.Final figures for 2023 are not yet available but headline alcohol-specific death totals will be published as part of the Registrar General Annual Report, currently scheduled for late November 2024. Table 1c of the Registrar General Quarterly Tables for quarter 2 2024 provides provisional headline figures for alcohol-specific deaths registered in 2023 and in quarters 1 and 2 of 2024.

answer_text: The Department has published an online map viewer which can be accessed using the following link: Reservoir Flood Map. This viewer provides the locations of potential Controlled Reservoirs in the North and it will be updated as reservoir managers register their controlled reservoirs following commencement of the relevant Section of the Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015.The viewer does not name Service Reservoirs and does not currently show all Service Reservoirs. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support that will be provided for localities not included in the RAISE programme, such as West Tyrone.
answer_text: My department continues to provide significant support to schools in disadvantaged areas.  This includes more than £70m of Targeting Social Need (TSN) funding to schools each year, as well as £8.1m for 458 schools eligible for the Extended Schools Programme. In 2024/25, schools in the West Tyrone area have been allocated over £6 million pounds of TSN funding and £892,622 in Extended Schools funding. There are, of course, a wide range of other support services available.  For instance, Sure Start services are available in both Strabane and Castlederg. The Sure Start programme supports can support parents from pregnancy until their child starts school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland 2023/24.
answer_text: The recently published Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland 2023/24 reflect the pressures within families and complexity of need that have been growing for a number of years. This is one of the reasons why the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services was commissioned in February 2022.  It is also why my Department established the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme in April 2023. The Reform Programme was established in the knowledge that change is necessary to halt the trend of increasing numbers of children in care, on the child protection register and in need of support from statutory services. The answer is partly in addressing wider family dynamics, including exposure to poverty. We know that there is a strong correlation between deprivation and the need for Health and Social Care Trusts to intervene in family life. Around 43% of children in care come from the 20% most deprived areas in Northern Ireland – compared to 5% from the least deprived. Also, previous research identified children in the 10% most deprived neighbourhoods in Northern Ireland were nearly 6 times more likely to be on the child protection register.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment on whether there is correlation between the lack of road maintenance and the increase in vehicle damage claims.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The constraints on public funding mean that my Department continues to operate a limited road maintenance service, resulting in reduced levels of pothole repair, grass cutting and spend across other areas such as road markings and safety barriers.The number of roads related public liability claims, in particular vehicle damage claims, can increase due to external factors beyond my Department’s control such as severe weather conditions, an increased volume of vehicles using the road network and greater public awareness of the deterioration of the road network and the right to submit a claim. There is no doubt, however, that there is a correlation between the historical under-investment in the maintenance of the road network and the increasing claim numbers.The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the comment made by the Chief Executive of Invest NI at the meeting of the Committee for the Economy on 16 October 2024, that dual market access had not attracted direct foreign investment.
answer_text: EU Exit and its outworkings created an extended period of uncertainty and instability for businesses and investors across these islands and beyond. The Windsor Framework, agreed last year, provides a welcome degree of stability by confirming our unique dual market access position whereby traders in the North of Ireland have access to the EU and British markets for goods without border checks, tariffs or customs declarations.   Combined, these markets have well over 500 million people and produce around £17.5 trillion in GDP (2023).Investment decisions take time, typically years, and require a degree of certainty which, thankfully, we now enjoy to a much greater extent. There is also a job of work to raise awareness and understanding of our trading position, both at home and further afield. That is why I have put dual market access at the front and centre of our international value proposition, working alongside Invest NI to take that message directly to buyers and potential investors. It is also important to point out that a key benefit of dual market access is the potential to grow domestic manufacturing exports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how changes to the administrative earnings threshold for Universal Credit has impacted on the number of claimants.
answer_text: There has been no change in the number of Universal Credit (UC) claimants as a result of the changes made to the Administrative Earnings Threshold (AET).The AET changes increased my Department’s opportunity to provide individually tailored work coach support to a greater number of claimants to help them develop skills and relevant experience in their current work, to increases their prospects of securing more sustainable work and increase their earnings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment as to why the number of redundancies are up 30 per cent in the last 12 months, as reported in the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency Labour Market Report October 2024; and (ii) how his Department plans to reduce these figures.
answer_text: The most recent data indicate that the number of confirmed redundancies in the twelve months to September 2024 was approximately 13% lower than the recent peak seen in July 2024, and broadly in line with the long-term level of confirmed redundancies seen in the pre-pandemic period. The work of my department is wholly focussed on the creation of sustainable good quality work through the delivery of my economic priorities.  We will achieve this by taking a strategic approach that concentrates on those areas that have the biggest potential to grow, particularly by increasing exports.  We will drive up productivity to create more highly skilled jobs across all our regions, and we will ensure that our people have access to effective training to take up these new opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the comments made by the Mineral Products Association at the Committee for Infrastructure meeting on 16 October 2024, that the roads network in Northern Ireland is facing its biggest funding crisis in over 25 years.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The road network is the north’s largest and most valuable strategic infrastructure asset but it has not been given priority or appropriate resources for many years and road maintenance funding is consistently below the required levels. It has been independently established (Barton 2018) that c£143m (£194m at today’s prices) is needed to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. Both the Barton Report and the Northern Ireland Audit Office Value for Money report on ‘Structural Maintenance of the Road Network’ (March 2019) emphasised the need for certainty around longer term funding of road maintenance budgets and highlighted the planning and delivery challenges across the my department and the wider construction industry.Structural maintenance expenditure over the past decade has totalled almost £1bn; however, this amounts to less than two-thirds of the expenditure required and has led to a road maintenance backlog of more than £1.2bn. This lack of investment leads to an asset that deteriorates quicker and requires more costly and complex future interventions. Put simply, it is better value to maintain the asset well and in a timely fashion. While my officials have worked very hard to maintain the road network, they have nonetheless been forced to concentrate on only the highest priority road repairs.  As a result, we have all seen a continued deterioration in our infrastructure with large numbers of potholes and other defects developing across the road network, especially after periods of particularly cold or wet weather.The current allocation will allow my Department to deliver a programme of capital works aimed at improving the integrity and condition of the road network. However, the current programme will be largely committed by end of October 2024, and so my Department is seeking additional funding through the October monitoring round to ensure a continued pipeline of work to contractors, avoiding degeneration of capacity within the contracting industry which would reduce ability to deliver future schemes. If successful, the additional funding will make a valid contribution to secure jobs in the construction industry as well as providing critical investment in the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the medication trials taking place in Northern Ireland for the treatment of Parkinson's disease; and (ii) the process to be considered for these trials.
answer_text: (i) The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Regional Movement Disorders Service, which sits under Neurology at Belfast Trust, has successfully competed to become the regional recruiting centre for Northern Ireland for this platform study.  Platform studies are a new approach to clinical trials, which allow several types of medicines to be studied through the one research protocol. This is an accelerated clinical trial platform methodology focusing on potential novel therapies for Parkinson's disease.  The Trust’s Regional Movement Disorders Service will deliver this study in partnership with the Medical Research Council Clinical Trials Unit at University College Hospitals London and the University of PlymouthFurthermore, Belfast Trust has been successful in obtaining competitive funding for a trial-funded Research Nurse/Practitioner who, when appointed, will assist with recruitment and delivery of this trial which is called EJS-ACT PD trial.The Northern Ireland Clinical Research Network hosted at Belfast Trust has a neurodegenerative research portfolio demonstrating one current open study.  This is called the COBALT study (COmBining memantine and cholinesterase inhibitors in Lewy Body Dementia treatment Trial). Study opened to recruitment in March 2024. (ii) In order to access trials or determine suitability, patients should contact the clinician responsible for delivering their care for advice on criteria and process for accessing medication trials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department plans to ban smartphones for children at school, as suggested by the Parentkind National Parent Survey for Northern Ireland 2024.
answer_text: In September, I issued guidance for schools on the personal use of mobile phones (and other similar devices) during the school day.  The guidance recommends that pupils should not use their mobile phones during the school day.  It also outlines that personal mobile phones should not, in normal circumstances, be brought into primary schools. The guidance sets out a range of potential approaches to restricting the use of mobile phones in schools, as well as the key practical issues that should be considered and which are likely to form part of a school’s policy approach on mobile phone use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to consulting parents as standard ahead of major policy announcements, as suggested by the Parentkind National Parent Survey for Northern Ireland 2024.
answer_text: I am committed to seeking the views of parents whenever possible in relation to the revision of existing policies or the introduction of new policies. My department routinely consults with the public, using tools such as the Citizen Space website and also through engagement with external organisations, such as Parentkind who may be invited to carry out targeted consultation on behalf of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to set up an independent advisory body on how to reduce school costs, as suggested by the Parentkind National Parent Survey for Northern Ireland 2024.
answer_text: I welcome the Parentkind research and I share their concern about the effects of poverty and the cost-of-living crisis on children and families. Whilst I currently have no plans to set up an independent advisory body as suggested in the Parentkind report, combatting disadvantage is a key priority for my Department. There is currently much work ongoing and I will continue to invest significantly in this area. My Department will also contribute fully to the cross-departmental development of the Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy.   My Department’s consultation on school uniform policy closed recently and received a high number of responses. My officials are currently analysing all responses and then I intend to bring legislation forward to support actions to address affordability of uniform requirements for parents. My Department is also undertaking a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants and a consultation on this is due to launch in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the medications that are available in the UK for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease that are not available in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There are a number of well-established medications for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. NICE guidance on the management of Parkinson’s disease in adults (NG71), which includes recommendations on the pharmacological treatment of motor and non-motor symptoms, has been endorsed by my Department as applicable in Northern Ireland. More recently, NICE have published NICE TA934 (foslevodopa–foscarbidopa for treating advanced Parkinson’s with motor symptoms) which has been endorsed by my Department as applicable for use in Northern Ireland. It is my Department’s assumption that any medications routinely used for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease available in other parts of the UK are also available for use in Northern Ireland. If you wish to enquire about the availability of a specific medication, my Department will endeavour to provide you with further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 16099/22-27, to detail any discussions he and his Department have had with (i) Derry City and Strabane District Council; and (ii) the Department for Infrastructure in relation to the future funding for the City of Derry Airport.
answer_text: Discussions have been taking place between my Department and the Department of Infrastructure and Derry City and Strabane District Council in relation to the provision of funding to the Council for the future sustainability of City of Derry Airport (CoDA) from 1 April 2025. In relation to (i) a series of discussions have taken place with Council officials to enable the Department to carry out a commercial appraisal of the Council’s Business Case and to ensure Council compliance with the Subsidy Control Act 2022; (ii) discussions have taken place between departmental officials to clarify the Council’s funding requirements from 1 April 2025 which explored the legislative basis for transfer of airport policy and funding to my department. These discussions are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action he and his Department is taking to address the issues raised by the October 2024 Office for Environmental Protection report on the drivers and pressures affecting nature in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My department is already progressing a wide range of measures to address the issues of land use and nutrient pollution from agriculture, which were identified as pressures causing biodiversity loss.  The Environmental Improvement Plan and the Lough Neagh Action Plan provide details of many of these measures.  These include a review of the Nutrient Action Programme, delivery of the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, publication of an Ammonia Strategy and delivery of the Farming with Nature Package. I recognise that more needs to be done quickly and I acknowledge that my department must continue to provide both the right policies and financial support to place us on the correct path to reverse environmental degradation. A number of the issues raised in the report fall outside the remit of my department including nutrient pollution from sewage treatment and unsustainable extraction and use of raw materials. As with climate change, another issue reflected in the report, it is for everyone to play their part in restoring and protecting nature in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) bus; (ii) rail; and (iii) plane routes that are funded through his Department's public service obligation in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to Translink, under the Public Service Obligation, to support all Metro (including glider) and Ulsterbus routes as well as the rail routes operated by NIR. There are no plane routes funded through my Department’s Public Service Obligation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a list of the groups and organisations who have requested a meeting with him but he has declined to meet since taking up office.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action he and his Department is taking to address the issue of Northern Ireland being the lowest-earning region in the UK.
answer_text: The work of my department is wholly focussed on the creation of sustainable good quality work through the delivery of my economic priorities.  I am taking a strategic approach that concentrates on those areas that have the biggest potential to grow, seeking to drive up productivity to create more highly skilled and highly paid jobs across all our regions, and ensuring that our people have access to effective training to take up these new opportunities.  		Earnings data from HMRC PAYE indicate that employees here had a median monthly pay of £2,241 in September 2024, below the average of £2,397.  The data also indicates however that our employees fared favourably over the past year in terms of growth, with monthly earnings increasing by £139 (6.6%) over the year to September 2024, while the British increase was £120 (5.3%). 	Despite that, we remain the lowest earning region and there is a lot of work to be done to address this.  I am therefore supporting the creation of more good jobs by making sure that everyone in employment has a right to decent terms and conditions of employment through a new and wide-ranging Employment Rights Bill and will work with the Labour Relations Agency to support a Good Work Charter that encourages best practice across all employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the guidelines and risk assessments undertaken before allowing a property to be used for temporary accommodation placements by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive and been advised that:“In the acquisition of Single Lets the Housing Executive will inspect the property and ensure that the following criteria is met: Proof of ownership such as solicitor’s letter or copy of deedsElectric meter – meter card typeNICEIC Cert for wired smoke alarmsGas Safe or Oil Boiler test certificateCarbon monoxide alarmsProof of Public Liability InsuranceConfirmation that provider is registered on the Landlord Registration schemeConfirmation that property is registered with Land & Property Services (LPS)Provision of fire blanket or extinguisher in the kitchenIf any of the above criteria is not met, and/or the inspection at the point of acquisition is unsatisfactory, the Housing Executive will not use the property.For placements in non-standard accommodation (B&B/Hotels) the Housing Executive is acquiring the use of accommodation in the same manner as other customer groups whereby the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation in order to operate as a short-term accommodation. The Housing Executive is not responsible for such inspections. However, the NIHE have conducted an exercise on two occasions (Nov 23 and July/Aug 24), which involved a visit to ensure non-standard accommodation was being used by those placed in such accommodation, staff will also have raised in any concerns in respect of the quality of accommodation should there have been any concerns.The Housing Executive’s long-term plans for temporary accommodation are guided by the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation. As part of this action plan the Housing Executive intends to commission work with customers and providers to agree a temporary accommodation standards framework for Northern Ireland. This should include physical standards, suitability standards and service standards for all types of temporary accommodation.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people placed in emergency temporary accommodation that were placed (i) within 20 miles; and (ii) over 20 miles from their previous residence, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive and been advised that:“The Housing Executive does not hold this information.  Placements into emergency temporary accommodation are made on the basis of availability at the time of need. While the Housing Executive will record the number of placements made by an office and offer assistance, when required, to avail of the placement, the actual distance from the applicant’s last recorded address is not calculated or recorded on Housing Executive systems.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the emergency temporary accommodation available in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive and been advised that:	“The Housing Executive does not maintain a list of non-standard premises (Hotel/B&B) available over the past 5 years, such accommodation is only availed of for emergency placements and usage varies depending on location, requirement and availability. 		Information is held in relation to where the Housing Executive has placed people for emergency accommodation using a free text field on our Housing Management System and therefore, there is no readily identifiable option to extract placements in non-standard by individual premises. 		Provision of the requested data in respect of this would involve a review of all non-standard placements for the last 5 years to identify the premises referred to in the free text fields.”	For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 13641/22-27, for an update on his Department's plans to introduce new regulations in relation to electrical safety standard check in all rented homes by the end of 2024.
answer_text: The Electrical Safety Standards for Private Tenancies Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 remain on track to be made before the end of this year.	In accordance with the draft affirmative legislative procedure, the Regulations were laid before the Assembly on 7 October 2024, and the associated Motion tabled for debate. 	Subject to the approval of the Assembly, the Regulations will become operational for any new tenancies granted on or after 1 April 2025, and for existing tenancies by 1 December 2025, to allow sufficient time for compliance. 		In respect of the social rented sector, through the Regulatory Framework, Registered Housing Associations already comply with an obligation to ensure “electrical systems comply with current regulations (18th edition) and are subject to periodic inspection every 5 years”.  The Housing Executive currently complete electrical checks and are working to transition to a 5-year cycle by the 2027/28 financial year.		My officials are currently reviewing the Decent Homes Standard to enhance quality standards for social homes, which may include health and safety standards for electrical safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement he and his Department has had with the Women's Enterprise Policy Group over the last three years.
answer_text: I and my Department have had no direct engagement with the Womens Enterprise Policy Group over the last three years. However, there has been contact with Roseann Kelly (Chief Executive of Women in Business NI), who is a member of the Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the criteria for awarding a tenant housing points for intimidation.
answer_text: Under the current Housing Selection Scheme an Applicant will be entitled to Intimidation points (200) if any of the following criteria apply in respect of the application: The Applicant’s home has been destroyed or seriously damaged (by explosion, fire or other means) as a result of a terrorist, racial or sectarian attack, or because of an attack motivated by hostility because of an individual’s disability or sexual orientation, or as a result of an attack by a person who falls within the scope of the Housing Executive’s statutory powers to address neighbourhood nuisance or other similar forms of anti-social behaviour. OrThe Applicant cannot reasonably be expected to live, or to resume living in his/ her home, because, if he or she were to do so, there would, in the opinion of the Designated Officer, be a serious and imminent risk that the Applicant, or one or more of the Applicant’s household, would be killed or seriously injured as a result of terrorist, racial or sectarian attack, or an attack which is motivated by hostility because of an individual’s disability or sexual orientation, or as a result of an attack by a person who falls within the scope of the Housing Executive’s statutory powers to address neighbourhood nuisance or other similar forms of anti-social behaviour.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline (i) why there are fewer companies led by women in Northern Ireland than in England, Wales and Scotland; and (ii) what he doing to address this trend.
answer_text: According to the Gender Index 2024 report, 17.8% of companies in the North were female-led in 2023. This was a lower proportion than in Wales (19.7%), Scotland (19.4%) and all of the 9 English regions (20.3% overall).The North also exhibited a lower share of newly incorporated companies which were female-led, at 19.3%. However, this represents an increase of 15.4 percentage points (pps) from 2022, while the average across Britain and north decreased by 4.4pps to 21.2%.The number of female-led incorporations increased by 71.5% in 2023, compared to 38.8% for male-led. Among ‘fast growth’ companies in the North, 13.0% were female-led. This was higher than the average across Britain and the north of 12.4% and ranked joint-fourth with the South-West of England. The Global Entrepreneurship Monitor report indicates that in 2023, the rate of female Total Early-Stage Entrepreneurial Activity (TEA) was 8.2%, up from 7.3% in 2022 and continuing an upward trend seen in recent years.  The gap between males and females remained unchanged, as the male TEA rate also increased, rising from 10.2% in 2022 to 11.3% in 2023. The trend of male TEA rates being higher than female rates was also recorded in England, Scotland and Wales. Overall, the North is on an upward trajectory in terms of female-led businesses.  We have a suite of programmes and sponsorship/access to finance in place to encourage businesses and women entrepreneurs including:Hello Possible (formerly known as Entrepreneurship for All), which seeks to create a diverse culture of innovative entrepreneurship activity with under-represented people, improving ambition and confidence;The Role Models Initiative, which champions diversity and inclusivity in innovative entrepreneurship by showcasing exemplary innovative founders that have taken the road less travelled, or have overcome systemic barriers to entrepreneurship – including gender, class, sexuality, disability, race, education, location or otherwise; The Ambition to Grow Supporting Women (ATGSW) programme, which was launched in October 2023. Developed in partnership with Women in Business NI (WBNI), ATGSW primarily targets women-led businesses that are not engaged with Invest NI and seeks to assist them in creating new employment and targeting new customers beyond the North.Through the initial call, 19 women-led businesses were supported and a total of £564,960 grant assistance was offered.  This support is projected to result in the creation of 38 new jobs which will generate total annual salaries of £1.1m, with annual sales outside the north projected to increase by £5.4m across the supported businesses.In addition to receiving financial support, the successful applicants attended a series of six leadership development workshops during which they created and presented a detailed growth plan for their business.  The workshops also focussed on increasing peer learning activities between participants and creating networks that will last beyond the dur

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what (i) funding; and (ii) support his Department has given to Men's Sheds, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Over the past 5 years the Public Health Agency (PHA) has funded Groundwork NI to provide a coordination and support service to Men’s Sheds. This includes providing training, facilitating network meetings and visits to other Sheds, facilitating a Whatsapp group and producing newsletters to share information across Sheds.In 2023-24, the PHA provided additional in-year funding to Groundwork to facilitate a Small Awards Scheme for Sheds, whereby they could apply for up to £200 per shed. The Small Awards were used by the Sheds for: Refreshments for group meetings; Workshop training; Health & Safety / Equipment maintenance; Group activities; support with rent, building insurance and utility bills.In 2022-23, the PHA provided additional in-year funding to Groundwork on 2 occasions, one for a range of additional projects and services, and one to enable them to facilitate a partnership between two Men’s Sheds and Ulster Architectural Heritage Society to identify buildings which used to be Belfast parks, and to recreate models of those buildings.Funding to Groundwork to support Men’s Sheds over the past 5 years has been as follows:Core budget (£)Additional In-year funding (£)2024-2556,4635,0002023-2453,47816,9812022-2351,65010,229; 1,5002021-2249,692-2020-2148,718-The PHA has also, on occasion, provided in-year funding to some of the above organisations to enable them to support their local Men’s Shed:In 2020-21 The PHA gave Forward South £2,208 to buy tools for Belvoir Men’s Shed.In 2023-24 the PHA gave Top of the Rock Healthy Living Centre £3,053 to buy tools for their local Men’s Shed. In 2024-25 the PHA has provided additional funding to support Men’s Shed in the Fermanagh area, through the Elevate Mentor and Grants programme. The aim of this project is to provide a social space for men to engage in meaningful activities, reducing isolation and improving mental well-being.The total amount of direct funding provided from PHA to support Men’s Sheds over the last 5 years has been £293,711.The PHA also funds indirect support through:a full-time worker in Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, whose remit includes support for Men’s Sheds in disadvantaged areas of south and east Belfast; andfunded posts within a range of community development network organisations who provide support to Men’s Sheds in their locality. Organisations who have been funded by PHA and have provided this community level support over the last 5 years have included:Ballynafeigh Community Development AssociationEast Belfast Community Development AssociationResurgam Healthy Living Centre (Lisburn)Forward South (south Belfast)Top of the Rock Healthy Living Centre (Upper Springfield)Lower Ormeau Residents Action Group

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the UK Government proposals in relation to (a) zero hours contracts; (b) flexible working; and (c) changes to statutory sick pay regulations; and (ii) whether these proposals will impact on the work his Department is undertaking in the same area.
answer_text: The British Government published its Employment Rights Bill on 10th October 2024. Employment rights are a devolved matter. I have already announced my intention to bring forward an Employment Rights Bill for the north of Ireland and recently consulted on this issue. The consultation sought views on a range of actions, including those to address zero hours contracts and promote flexible working. Whilst I wish to consider the proposals within the British Employment Rights Bill, the work of my Department will also be informed by the outcome of the recently closed Good Jobs consultation.Statutory sick pay is a matter for the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of (i) successful; and (ii) unsuccessful applications for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Board, which makes decisions on any entitlement to payment under the Scheme, has advised that its system does not currently allow for a breakdown of successful and unsuccessful applications by constituency.As of week commencing 07 October 2024, there have been 1,363 successful determinations resulting in over £60 million paid to victims since the Scheme opened for applications.  728 determinations have been unsuccessful.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Climate Commissioner, expected in May 2024.
answer_text: Establishing a Climate Commissioner here is a key requirement of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, which falls to this Department. When established, it will be an important element to support our transition to Net Zero.Prior to February 2024, officials took forward significant development work on the options for the Commissioner, including how they would complement existing advisory bodies, and began drafting the required regulations. We have agreed that the necessary procedures be undertaken to have these regulations laid and made in this Assembly as soon as possible. This will be an important first step in establishing the Commissioner. Our officials are now undertaking the work required to have the regulations laid and made. The Committee for the Executive Office will consider them in due course.The appointment of the Climate Commissioner will follow once the Assembly procedures are completed and the regulations are in place.Although there is no date stated in the Act as to when a Climate Commissioner should be appointed, once the regulations have been approved we will move as quickly as possible to see that this appointment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 15115/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans his Department has to increase curlew numbers across Northern Ireland once the EU-funded Curlew LIFE project ends.
answer_text: My Department plans to enhance Curlew numbers across Northern Ireland by:encouraging farmers and landowners to enter land within the Department’s Environmental Farming Scheme. EFS Group schemes targeting curlew are in place in the Antrim Hills, Garron Plateau, and (lower) Bann Valley,continuing to support existing multi-year (2023-2028) and in-year Environment Fund projects which aim to increase Curlew numbers, including part funding for the Curlew LIFE project, protecting Curlew habitats within existing and potentially further designated sites, and providing support to future funding proposals which aim to increase Curlew numbers.Additionally, the Department’s new Farming with Nature package will focus on reversing the trend in biodiversity decline through creating and restoring habitats and supporting species recovery, including through landscape-scale projects involving collaboration between farmers and fostering innovation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of (i) Staff Officer; and (ii) Administrative Officer posts currently vacant, broken down by department.
answer_text: Details of the total number of vacant posts in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a department identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. As at the end of September 2024, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 397 General Service Staff Officer vacancies and 1045 General Service Administrative Officer vacancies. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of recent data from PropertyPal that states that rents have risen by 10 per cent in the last year; and (ii) how he plans to address this issue.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the increases in rents in the private rented sector, as a result of increasing demand and housing supply challenges across all tenures. The long-term solution to these affordability challenges is increasing housing supply. I am currently finalising a Housing Supply Strategy to provide the framework for collective action across the Executive to achieve this goal.	I am also continuing to explore innovative ways to deliver new affordable homes, including via shared ownership and my new intermediate rent product.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the schools where a hot meal is currently not being provided; and (ii) the action his Department and the Education Authority is taking to resolve staff shortages in these schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that as at 11 October 2024, due to staff shortages a hot meal is currently not able to be provided in Downshire Primary School and Loanends Primary School. A hot meal is also currently not able to be provided until the completion of maintenance repairs in St Therese of Lisieux Primary School and New Lodge Nursery School.EA Catering is experiencing similar recruitment issues that are being seen across the wider catering and hospitality sectors. In order to address staff shortages, the EA is continuing to try to recruit staff to fill catering vacancies.  Various promotional methods are being deployed include using social media, school platforms, roadshow recruitment days and the use of the EA website. The EA also deploys a small mobile catering team. The recent agreement reached over the pay and grading review has enhanced the hourly rates within the EA Catering Service, which is hoped will encourage people to apply for catering positions in the EA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his discussions with Professor Raphael Bengoa and others about restarting Health and Wellbeing 2026: Delivering Together.
answer_text: One of my first initiatives on becoming Minister was to invite Professor Rafael Bengoa to return to Northern Ireland to help us reboot the health and social care reform agenda. I held an initial meeting with Professor Bengoa in June of this year which led to the conference ‘Health and Social Care Reform – Accelerating Change’ which was held on 9th October. It is important to note that the purpose of re-engaging Professor Bengoa in the conversation was to accelerate the reform agenda, recognising that good progress has been made in a range of areas and identifying where action is required to drive the necessary change. The event was very well attended by over 160 people, made up of a wide range of stakeholders across Northern Ireland including the health and social care sector, service users, political representatives, and local and central government. The event highlighted the motivation to work together in continuing to improve the quality of care we provide to our citizens. I met with Professor Bengoa again on 15th October to take stock of the outcomes of the conference and to discuss the next steps. On 9th October I also met, alongside Professor Bengoa, with my Executive colleagues to discuss health and social care reform. In particular, the discussion centred on the need for a cross-government approach to improving population health and tackling health inequalities. We can only hope to achieve this, and deliver meaningful reform, if we adopt a whole government approach with support right across the Executive, the wider public sector, our communities and our clinicians. My ambition is better outcomes for all our population, and I am energised by the focus and determination of those across our system to realise that vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to reduce school non-attendance in constituencies with above average non-attendance.
answer_text: Since schools returned to normal operation in 2021/22 my department has seen very concerning levels of absence from school. My officials have been working to provide tools and resources to manage non-attendance across all schools.The introduction of Absence Tiers reporting, in September 2022 provided an alternative way of monitoring, reporting and assessing the level of absence within a school in a more nuanced way than simply reporting a percentage present figure or percentage absent figure. It also assists schools in conveying this key message to parents.  My department monitors school level attendance on a monthly basis and liaises with schools and EWS throughout the year.In June 2024, I launched a suite of resources, called Attendance HERO, designed by Knockmore Primary School pupils, a school that has seen a 3 percentage point increase in attendance during the pilot period and progress is continuing.In August 2024, my department published “Attendance Matters - Practical guidance to improve attendance together”. The purpose of this guidance is to provide additional support to manage and improve pupil attendance at school highlighting examples of strategies that have been identified through the Education Authority’s Attendance Pilot Programme (2022-2023) as having been effective in improving pupil attendance at school.My department is developing a new “Attendance Matters” strategy and will be consulting on that document in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 15017/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the discussions he and his Department have had with Ofgem in relation to the proposed new electricity link between Scotland and Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator is responsible for licensing the interconnector here and Ofgem is responsible for licensing the interconnector in Britain. The Utility Regulator is currently working on Transmission Investments’ license application for the proposed interconnector between Scotland and here.Whilst my Department is working closely with the Utility Regulator on interconnection, it has no role in the license approval process and must remain impartial. As such, my Department has had no discussions with Ofgem on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he and his Department have had with the People’s Postcode Lottery.
answer_text: Department officials have met with representatives from the People's Postcode Lottery, at their request, on two occasions: 3rd August 2022 and 5th February 2024. At both meetings representatives discussed the potential operation of the People’s Postcode Lottery in Northern Ireland.  Following the February 2024 meeting, the People's Postcode Lottery requested a meeting with me on the same subject and subsequently invited me to speak at their Stormont Reception on 13th June.  I did not attend either, however, a Departmental official did attend the People’s Postcode Lottery reception as an observer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the grants awarded as part of local peatland restoration projects.
answer_text: The Shared Island Peatland Challenge Fund has recently awarded approximately £2.7 million of funding to five not for profit organisations in Northern Ireland to deliver valuable restoration work to our peatlands.  Details of the grant awards are set out in the table below.Applicant NameProject TitleProject OutlineFunding Awarded for 2024 - 2027Ulster WildlifeBuilding capacity for peatland restoration Restoration of 2 Peatlands priority habitats - complete the restoration of Glenullin bog that will allow the entire site to move fully into favourable management. Removing conifers and peatland restoration of afforested habitats at Kinnagillian forest, which lies adjacent to Black bog and Haughey’s bog.
