department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Air Pollution in Northern Ireland 2023 Report in relation to West Tyrone.
answer_text: There are two air quality monitoring sites in West Tyrone, the data from which informed the Air Pollution in Northern Ireland 2023 report.  Both sites were compliant with the Air Quality Standards Regulations limit values, target values and corresponding Air Quality Strategy objectives for the pollutants measured in 2023. Against the more strict, non-legislative World Health Organisation guidelines both sites were compliant for PM10 and sulphur dioxide but non complaint for PM2.5.No further assessment, other than what is contained in the report, has been undertaken by DAERA in relation to West Tyrone.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of abdominal aortic aneurysm screening tests in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of men screened, by Trust, over a five-year period is set out in Table 1 below: Table 1NUMBER OF MEN SCREENEDCOHORT17/1818/1919/2020/2121/22BELFAST15681603129314411602NORTHERN20182016157421681973SOUTH EASTERN15271654138618371581SOUTHERN14551474136015921494WESTERN12981391109914781341NORTHERN IRELAND78668138671285167991These figures have been taken from the Director of Public Health Core Tables - Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm screening uptake rates.  Uptake is the proportion of men offered screening who are tested.  It is not possible for the programme to report on the total number of tests completed across Northern Ireland, as this may include more than one scan for a screening participant.  Published data for 22/23 and 23/24 has been delayed due to the pause to the programme and the focus on the recovery of the programme.  The programme has fully recovered since February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister of Finance regarding the regulation of Property Management Companies; and (ii) for his assessment of an independent body to deal with complaints about Property Management Companies.
answer_text: (i)	This matter falls outside the remit of my Department and is the responsibility of the Minister of Finance.  At a meeting on 2 October 2024 the Finance Minister noted her desire to resume work on the issue of managing agents when resourced to do so.  I agreed that my officials would liaise with colleagues in her Department to inform their work.  (ii) 	As this work falls outside the scope of my Department, I have made no assessment of an independent body to deal with complaints about Property Management companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the overall budget available in relation to the Olympic and Paralympic medalists being invited to nominate local grassroots sports clubs and projects to receive an investment boost.
answer_text: I welcome the success of our Northern Ireland athletes on the Olympic and Paralympic stage in Paris this year and I was delighted to be able to mark these successes at my Department and Sport NI’s celebration event hosted in the SSE Arena on Thursday 3 October. This was not only an opportunity to pay tribute to our athletes, but also to the coaches and support staff who have encouraged and supported them to become who they are today.At the celebration event, I announced an Olympic Medallists’ Fund of £100,000, which will be paid to grassroots clubs and projects in Northern Ireland nominated by our six medallists.The breakdown of the awards to be disbursed are as follows:£20,000 for each gold medal£10,000 for each silver medal£5,000 for each bronze medal

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the actions his Department is taking to meet the target time to process (i) regular; and (ii) major planning applications.
answer_text: Local councils are responsible for processing over 99% of planning applications and my Department monitors performance through key statutory planning targets for local and major applications through quarterly and annual planning performance statistical publications. Planning is a collective issue, and all stakeholders must play their part in delivering the necessary changes and improvements. I am committed to ensuring that the planning system operates in an efficient and effective way. The Planning Improvement Programme is an example of my Department working closely with local government and partners, to improve current processes and the performance and delivery of the system.  It has provided an opportunity to create a more structured framework around which key issues can be addressed in a collaborative working partnership with my Department, local government and stakeholders. The wide-ranging improvement programme has already delivered important changes, including fee uplifts; consultations on policy and procedures and the development of legislation, all of which will continue to improve the overall efficiency and effectiveness of our planning system.  I want to build on the positive work that has been taken forward to date and have asked my officials to arrange a series of meetings with Councils with a specific focus on improving performance; identifying challenges; recognising success and promoting key learning across the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department plans to introduce grants for the installation of green energy alternatives in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My department works in partnership with Invest NI to deliver a 5-year Energy & Resource Efficiency (ERE) Programme for Businesses which I committed £40m to upon its launch in May this year. The programme includes grant support for energy efficiency measures along with low carbon and renewable technologies, technical consultancy support, a resource matching service and a resource efficiency capital grant. My department also works in partnership with the Strategic Investment Board in the delivery of the ‘Energy Invest to Save’ fund. This targeted funding offers 100% support for public bodies to deliver energy capital projects. Since 2021, my Department has invested £73m in energy and carbon saving projects across the Government Estate with over £27m committed in the current year. My department is currently consulting on the future of low carbon heat support in residential buildings and is preparing a consultation on evidence-based options for domestic energy efficiency support. The outcome of these consultations will help to inform the design, rules, and guidance of future support.  In the interim, my department is working with partners such as the Utility Regulator to further scale up existing energy efficiency programmes and I have committed £2.5m in the current year to NISEP to improve insulation in low-income households.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his Department's bid for Sustainable Utilisation of Livestock Slurry phase 2 was successful; and (ii) to detail the timetable for the bid process.
answer_text: Following the successful conclusion of Phase 1 of the SBRI on the Sustainable Utilisation of Livestock Slurry (SULS) in August 2023, a business case for SULS Phase 2 with an overall funding pot of £12m was approved by DoF on 28 June 2024. The project received an opening allocation of £1.5 million in FY 24/25. Bids for the 2025/26 to 2027/28 requirements will be submitted in future Budget exercises in line with Departmental processes.  The SULS Phase 2 competition was launched in July and a 6-week application window ran from 5 July – 16 August 2024. The competition was open to all six lead companies from SULS Phase 1. The assessment panel evaluated the applications and recommended the successful suppliers. This recommendation was agreed by the Project Board on 9 September 2024. The SULS Phase 2 project team are currently agreeing contracts with the successful suppliers. The names of the successful companies will be made public once contracts have been signed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the specific date that work on the A5 Western Transport Corridor will commence.
answer_text: I can advise that works on the Strabane to Omagh section are programmed to commence next year, with the Omagh to Ballygawley section to commence one year later.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the recommendation in the Planning Appeals Commission Inquiry report on the A5 Western Transport Corridor Road Scheme in relation to the Ballygawley to Aughnacloy section.
answer_text: At the North South Ministerial Council (NSMC) Transport Sectoral Meeting of 27 June 2024, it was agreed that the A5 dualling and cross-border linked road projects, including the N2 Clontibret to Border Road Scheme, are crucial to the development of the region, improving road safety and stimulating economic growth. It noted the continued progression of these projects, with my officials working collaboratively with those in the South to deliver on them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the six Sustainable Utilisation of Livestock Slurry phase 1 schemes.
answer_text: Since the SBRI on the Sustainable Utilisation of Livestock Slurry (SULS) Phase 1 completed in August 2023, DAERA has been working on developing a business case for a Phase 2 which aims to support the establishment of demonstration plants to process livestock slurry.  The business case received DoF approval in June 2024 and SULS Phase 2 opened for applications on 5 July 2024. The focus of SULS Phase 2 is to turn the proof of concepts from SULS Phase 1 into reality, with a target of 1,000 tonnes of excess phosphorus from livestock slurry being exported annually from NI by the end of the 3-year project. SULS Phase 2 funding is primarily focused on capital equipment to extract and valorise nutrients from livestock slurry and anaerobic digestate. The Phase 2 competition was open to all six successful Phase 1 lead suppliers. The DAERA SULS project team are currently agreeing contracts with the successful suppliers who will be awarded £4m each over the course of the 3-year project. The names of the successful companies will be made public when contracts are signed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department plans to take over the cost of running the City of Derry Airport from ratepayers, ahead of the setting of next year’s rates bill.
answer_text: In my statement to the Assembly on 1 October launching the Sub-Regional Economic Plan, I confirmed that my Department is in the process of taking responsibility for the funding provided by Derry City and Strabane District Council to the City of Derry Airport. My Department aims to assume responsibility of the funding from 1 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to fund body cameras for staff at hospitals.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust is currently planning for the implementation of body worn cameras as a pilot initially.  As part of this planning phase, the Trust will engage with staff, trade unions and some lay representatives to draft a policy which will then be subject to a public consultation.  This will take place in 2025 and it is envisaged the pilot will commence in May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14659//22-27, whether his Department will undertake further investigation into why the number of patients in receipt of antidepressants increased by over 33,500 in the last five years.
answer_text: No investigation into the rise in anti-depressant use is currently planned as this would require access to information on the reason for prescribing. As noted in the General Pharmaceutical Services for Northern Ireland Annual Statistics 2023/24, it is not possible to infer the medical conditions for which patients are being treated on the basis of the medications dispensed to them. Many medications, including anti-depressants, are used to treat multiple conditions.My Department and I remain committed to a range of non-pharmaceutical interventions to promote, develop and maintain good mental health. As outlined in the response to AQW 14659/22-27, a number of actions within the Mental Health Strategy will, once implemented, expand the availability of non-pharmaceutical interventions for mental health conditions, including depression. These include talking and psychological therapies, social prescribing and attendance at Recovery College courses as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the targets for job creation in West Tyrone contained in the Sub-Regional Economic Plan.
answer_text: My Sub-Regional Economic Plan provides for the formation of Local Economic Partnerships with funding of £45m. It will be for each Partnership to identify and agree on actions that support delivery of their economic priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15544/22-27, for his assessment of the 32 charge points per 100,000 population, given the comparative figures of 103 for Scotland, 97 for England and 88 for Wales.
answer_text: My Department published an Electric Vehicle (EV) Infrastructure Action Plan in November 2022 outlining measures to encourage the development of EV charging infrastructure in the North. The Plan included an action to introduce payment for charging across the Electricity Supply Board’s existing network of EV charging stations here. This action, completed in April 2023, has catalysed the EV charging infrastructure market here with more than 20 Charge Point Operators now operating commercially, and the charging network has grown by over 60% during this time with over 600, publicly accessible EV charge points here.I welcome the most recent quarterly growth figures showing a 13.4% rise in public charge points in the North, compared with an average of 8.3% across England, Scotland, and Wales. I am also encouraged that the number of EV charge points per 100,000 population here has risen by 58% since April 2023.Through the South’s ‘Shared Island’ fund, we will soon have additional charging facilities at nearly 50 sports clubs across the North. A local council consortium, led by Derry and Strabane District Council, has made a successful application to The Office for Zero Emission Vehicles’ On-Street Residential Charge Point Scheme, for which my department has provided match funding, which will also add more than 100 further charge points, bringing new access to public charging infrastructure into the heart of our communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the strategy to provide support for patients who have been diagnosed with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
answer_text: Trusts have provided the following responses regarding provision for patients who have stopped their medicationBHSCTFor paediatric patients who have a confirmed diagnosis of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and have stopped their medication and request to restart medication, BHSCT require a referral from their GP to the community paediatric triage service. Upon accepting the referral, the patient will be invited to complete Quantitate Behavioural QB test to determine their current level of impairment regarding attention, activity and impulsivity. The results of the QB test will be discussed at a face to face appointment with a consultant paediatrician where suitability for restarting medication will be discussed and facilitated. Within adult mental health services if a person on the waiting list has had their medication stopped, on a case by case basis GP will be asked to provide additional clinical information that would inform clinical prioritisation for assessment. NHSCTIf a child already known to the paediatric ADHD service has stopped their medication and requires a reassessment, they will be offered a further outpatient appointment within paediatrics.  If a child has moved to adult services in the intervening period then paediatrics can provide detailed information to adult services is respect of their ADHD diagnosis if requested.Adult ADHD services are not funded within Northern Ireland. In January 2024, NHSCT commenced a small pilot to begin to address adult assessments and intervention in ADHD. Referrals to Adult Mental Health Services (AMHS) are still accepted for young people transferring into AMHS  from Child and Adolescent MH Services who have a diagnosis of ADHD and are on active medication treatment.SEHSCTWithin Children’s Service if a child or young person has stopped medications and have been discharged from the service; they can be referred back in from Primary Care and triaged through Children’s Health Single Point of Entry (SPOE). The patient does not need to sit on the new patient waiting lists, as they would require a review appointment only. For those that have passed the age of 18 years; they would be referred to adult services. For adult patients who have stopped their ADHD medication and are subsequently discharged from the service, the patient would need to be re-referred by their GP in the usual way.   The patient would then go on a waiting list to be reassessed. As these patients already have an ADHD diagnosis, this is a different waiting list than that for patients who are referred for a new diagnostic assessment. Waiting times in this case is shorter, compared to the waiting time for a diagnostic assessment.SHSCTIf a patient with an established diagnosis of ADHD and a recently active treatment plan (within 1-year) is discharged by a consultant psychiatrist due to disengagement, the service can consider accepting a re-referral and re-prescribing medication as appropriate. If a patient has stopped their

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: My department is concluding the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and intend to publish a summary report on its findings in autumn 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 15865/22-27, to detail the discussions he and his Department have had with the Education Authority about the use of extended induction.
answer_text: The legislative duty for the placement of children with special educational needs (SEN) rests with the Education Authority (EA) under the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996.  Therefore, the placement of children and young people with a statement of SEN is an operational matter for the EA. The EA, through the SEN Placements Taskforce, has kept my officials informed of potential delays with on-site school start dates due to the need for continuation of building works into Term 1 in new specialist education provision established for the 2024/25 academic year and on the contingency arrangements for the pupils impacted by these works which included a range of measures including phased induction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15542/22-27, what are the necessary approvals he needs to get to support a Private Members Bill.
answer_text: Following the introduction of a Members Bill to the Assembly, current guidance requires the Minister with lead responsibility for the subject matter of a Bill to seek Executive agreement to the position which is to be taken at Second Stage. In addition, there are a number of considerations to be taken into account before a position to be taken is proposed to the Executive. Prior to the Second Stage, the Department leading on a Members’ Bill needs to take Office of Legislative Counsel’s view on whether the Bill is technically defective and on what the provisions of the Bill actually do. This is necessary because a Members’ Bill may not achieve the legal results that its Sponsor intended and may have unintended effects and defects. Depending on advice regarding the provisions of the Bill, what would be feasible to do by way of amendment at Consideration Stage may also affect the Department’s decision on what position to take at Second Stage. Further information on dealing with Members’ Bills can be found in “Handbook for Members’ Bills for the 2022 to 2027 Mandate.”
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the additional estimated cost and capacity implications for introducing Free School Meals across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to progress a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants. I plan to launch a consultation on the options considered by this review in the coming weeks. The options include the provision of universal FSM to some/all year groups.  The consultation documents are currently being finalised and they will outline the modelling undertaken as part of the review, including the number of additional pupils who would be able to access a free school meal and the estimated associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) motorists; and (ii) motorcyclists in West Tyrone that have had their licences suspended for motoring offences relating to driving under the influence of drink or drugs, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is notified by the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunal Service (NICTS) of drivers who have been convicted for driving offences and any disqualification to be imposed or penalty points to be endorsed on the person’s driving licence or record.  The table below details the total number of motorists who have been disqualified by the court following conviction in the West Tyrone area.  The data has been extracted from the driver licensing database and I would advise that the figures for the West Tyrone constituency relate to the address that the DVA currently holds on the driver record and therefore, may not have been the address of the driver at the time of the offence or conviction.  I would also advise that the DVA cannot split the figures to return the vehicle the driver was detected driving at the date of offence, as a driver may hold entitlement to drive a number of categories of vehicle. For example, a driver who applies for provisional entitlement to drive vehicles in the B (car) category will be automatically awarded provisional entitlement in the A (motorcycle) category. They may subsequently take driving tests to drive cars, motorcycles, and larger vehicles such as lorries and/or buses, and all of these entitlements are returned in any search of the database.  In addition, when a driver is disqualified from driving, the individual driving record is updated and their driving entitlements are revoked. As a consequence, there are a number of drivers who remain disqualified or who have not returned to driving following disqualification, and the DVA is unable to easily report on the entitlements they held at the time of the conviction.   Table 1 - Drivers disqualified from driving due to a drink or drug driving offence in West Tyrone constituency - 1st January 2019 – 30th September 2024YearTotal20199520201062021116202214820231192024 (to Sept)92Total676Source: DVA Driver Licence DivisionTo note – address in the West Tyrone constituency relates to the current address held by the DVA and may not be the address at the date of offence or conviction

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of liquor licences in place in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Although my Department is responsible for the policy and legislation regarding the retail sale and supply of alcoholic drinks, the courts are responsible for issuing liquor licences.  		Each District Court provides the Department with the total number of licences each year by category of licence, however this is broken down by District Court area and not local council area.  		Each District Court does however provide the Department, on an annual basis, with the number of liquor licences for public houses by Local Government District.  The Department holds information on this for 2021, 2022 and 2023 and has published the data as required under Section 24 of the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.  Please see table below.		Number of liquor licences for public houses by Local Government District. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the SMART targets she has set for each of the four themes within the Child Criminal Exploitation Action Plan for the next 12 months.
answer_text: The Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE) action plan is a 2-year cross-Departmental, multi-agency plan to improve the response to criminal exploitation of children and young people in Northern Ireland.   The Child Protection Senior Officials Group (CPSOG), supported by its subgroup, is responsible for monitoring progress. CPSOG provides cross-Departmental strategic direction on existing and emerging child protection issues and is chaired by the Department of Health. A more detailed copy of the action plan including timescales and responsibilities is at Annex A for your information. The Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland is responsible for co-ordinating the action plan. Annex ARECOMMENDATION TIMESCALE LEAD Theme 1: Increasing Awareness and Building Confidence and Capability 1.1	Agree a definition and accompanying narrative for Child Criminal Exploitation, in the context of a continuum of child exploitation and abuse, and ensure relevant strategies, policies, procedures and guidance are updated accordingly. A child and young person friendly version of the definition and associated materials will also be developed. Within 6 monthsCPSOG Sub-group (subject to approval by CPSOG)1.2	Develop and implement a trauma-informed multi-agency CCE awareness-raising and training programme for a range of front-line practitioners and their managers. 12 – 24 monthsSBNI1.3	In line with the draft three-year Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking (MSHT) Strategy, increase awareness of the offences as they relate to children in the Human Trafficking and Exploitation (Criminal Justice and Support for Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 and in the case of a child, whether it is in the child’s best interests to make a referral into National Referral Mechanism (NRM).  6 – 12 months DoJ1.4	Agree a CCE data development agenda under the auspices of CPSOG, to ensure all relevant agencies and partners collect, collate and share information to help create and sustain a shared understanding of the nature and scale of child criminal exploitation in Northern Ireland. In line with the data development agenda, identify areas for research and evaluation. 12 – 18 monthsCPSOG Sub-group (subject to approval by CPSOG)1.5	Develop and implement a public information/awareness raising programme to support children and young people, and parents and carers to recognise the signs and indictors of CCE and how to access support/ report concerns.  A gender conscious approach will be adopted as appropriate. Consideration will also be given to how the programme can effectively challenge community norms and narratives which facilitate and sustain the enduring presence of violence in communities.12 to 24 months SBNI1.6	The findings/recommendations of future reviews/inspections relating to child exploitation are considered in full under CPSOG governance arrangements and inform the implementation of the Task and Finish Group recommendations as appropriate.  OngoingCPSOG Sub-group (subject to approval by

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he ensures that the special educational needs of statemented children on extended induction, as set out in their statement, are being met.
answer_text: Neither the department nor the Education Authority (EA) hold this information.  It is the responsibility of each school to manage the special educational needs (SEN) as detailed on a child’s statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the research from the University of Stirling that indicates younger and lower income workers are more likely to opt out of pension schemes; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to address this issue.
answer_text: Figures from the 2023 NI Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings show that 15.5% of employees aged 18-21 had a workplace pension, whereas 75.6% of employees aged 22 to 29 had a workplace pension. The Pensions (Extension of Automatic Enrolment) Bill aims to increase pension participation in younger age groups and by low-income workers.  Once enacted, it will provide my Department with regulation making powers to reduce the lower age limit at which otherwise eligible workers must be automatically enrolled into a pension scheme by their employers.  The policy intent is to reduce the lower age limit from 22 to 18.  The Bill will also confer regulation making powers to reduce or abolish the lower limit of the qualifying earnings band (currently £6,240) so contributions are calculated on more of a person’s earnings. This will be particularly beneficial to lower earners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children that have been on extended induction on 1 September in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of complaints his Department has received in relation to air pollution or noxious odours in West Tyrone in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The department has received 38 complaints regarding odour or air pollution (dust) over the period 1st October 2021 to 30th September 2024. For the stated period the number of complaints received for each year were:2021 – 32022 – 62023 – 172024 – 12
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14940/22-27, (i) to detail the estimated slippage on the timetable to introduce a total ban on single-use vapes; and (ii) whether he will consider stand-alone legislation.
answer_text: My officials and I are continuing to engage with UK and Devolved Governments to agree and align on a revised UK wide coming into force date for the ban on single-use vapes, with the aim of keeping any slippage to a minimum. Alignment remains the preferred approach of Ministers, government officials and industry. It reduces differences in trading conditions, avoids complex internal market implications. and ensures that enforcement of the ban across the UK will be more effective.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he is taking to address the issues raised by the Committee of Experts in relation to his Department's obligations under the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages regarding the protection and promotion of Ulster Scots.
answer_text: The Committee of Experts recommended that authorities should adopt a language strategy to promote Ulster Scots in education, media and other areas of public life.Section 28D of the NI Act states that it is for an Executive to adopt an Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy. My Department is leading on this work on behalf of the Executive. Through Co-Design and working alongside Co-Design Groups, a draft strategy action plan has been proposed and a Cross Departmental Working Group is considering the proposed action plan. I am considering next steps in terms of the development of the strategy and associated action plan and will engage with Executive Colleagues to ask them to consider, and if content, agree on those next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has had discussions with his Executive colleagues regarding the need for legislation to regulate property management companies.
answer_text: At a meeting on 2 October 2024, the Finance of Minister noted her desire to resume work on the issue of property managing agents when resourced to do so.  I agreed that my officials would liaise with colleagues in her Department to inform their work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he is taking to address the issues raised by the Committee of Experts in relation to his Department's obligations under the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages regarding the protection and promotion of the Irish language.
answer_text: I am considering next steps in terms of the development of the Irish Language strategy and associated action plan and will engage with Executive Colleagues to agree on those next steps.My Department’s Central Translation Hub provides translation services for the 9 Executive Departments, Arm’s Length Bodies, Local Government and Public Bodies. These translation services are available to over 150 public sector clients in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he and his Department have had with Translink to help reduce traffic congestion around Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: The Department has been working closely with Translink to introduce a number of temporary mitigation measures which aim to reduce the impact of the roadworks on bus operations during the construction of Belfast Grand Central Station.These new temporary bus lanes came into operation on Monday 1 July 2024 and are operating successfully to provide a viable alternative to private car use.Translink has significantly enhanced their services on a number of metro corridors and on the Goldliner services to the north and south of the province.  Bus services to the Park & Ride sites have also been increased and these measures aim to ensure public transport remains a viable option for anyone wishing to travel to or from the city centre. In addition, the Department’s Traffic Information and Control Centre continues to monitor city centre traffic and adjusts traffic signals timings to optimise traffic progression.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the financial support his Department provides to Helping Hands schemes (i) in West Tyrone; and (ii) in Northern Ireland as a whole.
answer_text: My Department currently does not provide any financial support to Helping Hands Schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the financial support his Department provides to Helping Hands schemes (i) in West Tyrone; and (ii) in Northern Ireland as a whole.
answer_text: My department provides funding to the Women’s Aid Federation of Northern Ireland (WAFNI) for the delivery of the Helping Hands programme across Northern Ireland. The Department does not hold information on the amount of funding provided to regional Women’s Aid organisations, such as those in West Tyrone.  Prior to the 2023/24 financial year, WAFNI received an annual grant of £60k.  In the 2023/24 financial year, this was reduced to £50k due to cuts across the education budget to manage financial pressures. Funding for 2024/25 has been set at £50k, subject to business case approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) whether she plans to bring forward regulation of property management companies; and (ii) any discussions she has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: Management companies must act in accordance with existing relevant companies legislation, which falls outside the remit of my Department.However, the issue of how shared property is managed also has an interface with substantive land law and is one that I am keen to see some progress in developing policy proposals. I am currently exploring with officials how to secure the appropriate expertise in what is a complex area. Further progress will depend on that and competing legislative priorities over the remainder of this mandate.I raised this matter with the Minister for Communities on 1st October and my desire for our officials to continuing to engage on this area of work.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the financial support their Department provides to Helping Hands schemes (i) in West Tyrone; and (ii) in Northern Ireland as a whole.
answer_text: The Executive Office has not allocated any funding in 2024/25 to Helping Hands schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the BBC Spotlight programme entitled I Am Not Okay, what urgent improvements he plans to support parents trying to access respite care.
answer_text: I fully recognise the current situation is unsustainable and am determined to see urgent improvements in this area.There has been an increase in children & young people reported to have disability and there has been an increasing demand for access to short breaks & respite services.Some children and young people with a disability have also required longer-term residential placements, and this has led to occasions when overnight short breaks units have had children and young people placed there for longer than 90 days. This has meant that these units at times cannot maintain a short breaks service.A Children with Disability Urgent Summit was convened by my officials on 26.09.2024 to consider potential options in respect of improving the regional access to Short Break provision. This Summit was attended by senior representatives from each of the five HSC Trusts, and senior representatives from RQIA. It is important that solutions are found to improve family support, short breaks, residential care and behavioural support. The Children With Disability Framework is currently being finalised, by the Department of Health in response to the increasing challenges faced by families of Children With Disability and the corresponding difficulties experienced by Trusts in meeting rising demands and increased complexity of need. It is recognised that a review and reform of how services are structured, delivered and resourced, both from a regional perspective and locally in each of the Trusts is required. This includes the provision of Short Breaks.  A Children with Disability Workstream has also been established as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Board.  This workstream is specifically considering the regional position in respect of Children with Disability, which includes a focus on short breaks / respite. The Children with Disability Workstream will seek to deliver reform within Children’s Social Care services to address a range of known service challenges, particularly in relation to an ever-increasing volume and complexity of case work. The outcomes of this work will inform future commissioning.The DoH will continue to work with each of the HSC Trusts in terms of closely monitoring regional service provision for children with disability. This will assist with informing of regional and local pressures – inclusive of future planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7871/22-27, for an update on the estimated cost needed to maintain the structural integrity of the road network.
answer_text: In AQW 7871/22-27 I highlighted the high importance my Department places on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day Essential Maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, and also a properly resourced programme of Structural Maintenance to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, funding allocations to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years.It has been independently established, through the Barton Report, that some £194 million (at 23/24 prices) is needed to maintain the structural integrity of the road network in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the discussions his Department has had with Terex in relation to job losses and cuts in working hours.
answer_text: Terex is one of our largest manufacturing companies with which Invest NI has a long term strategic relationship, helping to secure internationally mobile projects within the group for the north of Ireland and supporting the business with investment in innovation and R&D.  		Earlier this year the company announced redundancies resulting from reduced global demand for its products, which have affected the whole materials handling sector.  In response to this global market adjustment, Terex has had to take the necessary steps to safeguard jobs for the longer term.  		Invest NI continues to work closely with Terex senior management to explore projects that will deliver good jobs, promote regional balance, and raise productivity in an environmentally sustainable manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action his Department is taking to reduce the number of deaths on rural roads which, according to the latest Northern Ireland Road Safety Strategy figures, increased by 57 per cent in 2023.
answer_text: As you will be aware I recently launched the Road Safety Strategy to 2030 and the supporting 2024/25 Action Plan.  The Action Plan contains 10 Strategic Interventions and 35 in-year Actions.  The Strategic Interventions are identified as those actions that will have the biggest impact on Road Safety, longer term, and include a review of drink driving and drug driving legislation.  The in-year actions are led by my Department and Road Safety Partners including the PSNI, Fire and Rescue Service, Ambulance Service, DAERA and DoJ.  While the Road Safety Strategy aims to improve road safety for everyone, there are specific actions that target rural road safety.  These include targeted road safety campaigns such as ‘Share the Road to Zero’, the Department's community road safety initiative, regularly issued messages on social media about the dangers of rural roads, with rules from the Highway Code NI and safety advice. My Department continues to deliver a programme of priority targeted road safety interventions through the Local Transport & Safety Measures programme, progressing improvements across the road network.  One of the best ways to ensure the safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050 is by changing road user behaviour. If all of us who use the roads do not change our attitudes our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.  We all have our part to play in helping reduce road deaths.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s efforts to increase the vaccination rate up to 95 per cent, as recommended by the World Health Organisation.
answer_text: There are many factors that may have contributed to declining vaccination uptake rates and while work to improve uptake rates is ongoing, it may take time before we see the results. The Public Health Agency (PHA) has established a multi-disciplinary working group to identify and implement how to improve vaccination uptake rates and my Department is represented on this group.The PHA have developed an action plan to support the work of the group across five separate themes of data, access to services, communications, operational management, and training and education. Actions taken forward include:additional training for vaccinators in the use of the Child Health System (CHS) to call eligible children appropriately;the creation of additional public resources for cohorts where uptake is known to be low, including the provision of materials in different languages and for populations where literacy may be lower;liaison with the Education Authority to promote awareness of the importance vaccination within educational settings;providing training and resources for Sure Start leads to promote vaccinations in pre-school settings;providing outreach clinics to specific communities where uptake is known to be lower;developing communication resources for stakeholders;supporting the Living Well campaign through approximately 500 local pharmacies promoting childhood vaccination, specifically MMR; andre-introducing incomplete vaccination status training for healthcare professionals.In response to increasing measles cases across the UK and Europe, as well as the declining uptake in the MMR vaccine, the PHA implemented an MMR catch-up campaign during February and March 2024, by offering vaccination to those up to 25 years of age with less than the required two doses of vaccine. Those aged 26 years and over could request vaccination via their GP.  An initial review of the number of people vaccinated within the campaign shows that 3,189 people were reached who may not otherwise have accessed services.  The PHA is a member of the “UK forum to facilitate discussion on declining coverage and inequalities”, which is chaired by the UK Health Security Agency. This group brings together relevant stakeholders from across the UK nations and is exploring the likely contributing factors to declining coverage across the routine immunisation programmes, to identify common issues, discuss potential actions to address the issues, and facilitate the sharing of experience and learning across the UK nations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 8846/22-27, whether he will bring forward legislation to ban hunting with dogs during the current Assembly mandate.
answer_text: My Department has no immediate plans to introduce legislation to ban hunting with dogs during the current mandate.  I am minded to support, subject to the necessary approvals, John Blair’s plans to introduce a Members’ Bill into the Assembly later in the year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 8746/22-27, for an update on the review of the personal injury discount rate.
answer_text: In accordance with the Damages Act 1996, the Government Actuary carried out a review of the personal injury discount rate for Northern Ireland, which commenced on 1 July. Further to this she determined that the rate should be set at +0.5%. The report of the review was laid before the Assembly and published on 26 September and as provided for under the 1996 Act, the new rate came into effect the following day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of farm subsidies post-Brexit for Northern Ireland farmers.
answer_text: Funding for farmers and land managers in Northern Ireland has been maintained by the UK Government in cash terms since 2020 and will continue until the end of the 2024-25 financial year.I have written to the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs on a number of occasions expressing my concerns about the future for the earmarked agricultural budget, with the current funding package coming to a conclusion at the end of March next year. I have stressed to him that any future settlement should, as an absolute minimum, maintain the current budget allocation uplifted for inflation to allow delivery of my Department’s policy objectives and ambitions. My Department is engaging with the Department of Finance on raising this with HMT as part of the broader budgetary processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of electric vehicle charge points in the West Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold records of the number of publicly available electric vehicle (EV) charge points in the North. The British Government publishes periodic updates by both Local Council area, and Parliamentary constituency, which you may find helpful. These can be found in the link below. https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/electric-vehicle-public-charging-infrastructure-statistics-july-2024Latest statistics show that there are 625 publicly accessible EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 60.5% in the total number of publicly accessible EV charge points since April 2023. The number of rapid charge points, which provide significantly faster opportunity to charge, has grown by over 300% in that same period. Relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of further charge points within your constituency. Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’, which my Department has match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under this scheme in 2021-22, on behalf of nine Local Councils to provide 127 charge points across those Council areas. Derry City and Strabane District Council, and Fermanagh and Omagh District Council are best placed to advise on the number of additional charge points this will provide in the West Tyrone constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the action his Department is taking to reverse the 71 per cent drop in butterfly numbers in Northern Ireland as identified by the 2024 Big Butterfly Count.
answer_text: This year has seen a significant decline in butterfly numbers, likely exacerbated by poor weather but also reflecting a long term and continuous decline, largely due to habitat loss and changing land management.My Department has been actively engaged with the development and implementation of actions in the 2021-2025 All-Ireland Pollinator Plan. NIEA officials work closely with partner organisations to develop pollinator actions into project work plans. Conservation action is also supported and funded through the Environment Fund and Environmental Farming Scheme to support habitat restoration and creation, including those that support butterfly species and other pollinators.  NIEA is engaged through the UK Pollinator Monitoring Scheme to gather data to identify population trends, impacts and threats for Northern Ireland. Additionally, Northern Ireland Priority Species lists, including butterflies, are currently being updated. The lists are used to guide local government and public bodies to meet their biodiversity duties through policies, strategies and decision making, and to support conservation actions by land-owners and land managers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to educate children and young people about the benefits of contributing to a pension scheme.
answer_text: Financial capability, including money management and savings, is already embedded as a statutory and integral element of the curriculum.  Opportunities for the teaching and learning of financial capability occur across the curriculum and through young people’s everyday experiences inside and outside school.  The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides a range of support and guidance to schools and has worked closely with other organisations to promote financial capability and to help produce resources for teachers to use.  An example of this is the ‘Your Money Matters’ textbook and teachers’ guide that was produced by Young Enterprise Northern Ireland and co-funded by Martin Lewis OBE and the Money and Pensions Service.  This resource, which was made available to every post-primary school, was designed for use with young people aged 14 to 16 and covers topics including spending and saving, borrowing, debt, insurance, student finance and future planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how his Department is protecting the red grouse under EU Law; and (ii) any engagement his Department has had with the Irish Government in relation to this.
answer_text: The Red grouse receives protection under the Game Act (Northern Ireland) 1928, which permits it to be hunted between 12 August and 30 November inclusive. It also has protection under the terms of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 (article 2 (4) refers). This is within scope of Annex II (a) of the EU Birds Directive.While always a localised species, numbers have declined across Northern Ireland with upland habitat degradation being perceived to be the main driver behind this decline. The Department’s focus is on restoring these habitats, especially blanket bog, thereby protecting the species that depend on them.My Department has had no recent engagement with the Irish Government on red grouse.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the action his Department is taking to manage and mitigate the consequences of an outbreak of bluetongue; and (ii) any engagement his Department has had with the Irish Government in relation to this.
answer_text: My Department is taking extensive action my Department to mitigate against the consequences of an outbreak of Bluetongue virus as communicated in my letter of 18 September 2024 to all MLAs, a copy of which is also being placed in the Assembly Library.  That action includes close and continuing engagement  and collaborative working with Irish government colleagues at both ministerial and official level to monitor the evolving disease situation and extensive stakeholder engagement to emphasise the importance of vigilance, prompt reporting and promoting the responsible sourcing of animals and germplasm.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to help reverse the trend of people who are economically inactive due to mental health issues.
answer_text: I recognise the need to address the issue of economic inactivity on mental health grounds. A 2022 research study, published by the Mental Health Foundation (MHF) and the London School of Economics (LSE), estimated that mental health problems cost the NI economy around £3.4billion per annum, much of this due to loss of productivity. The study makes clear that preventative measures are important in combatting mental health issues that lead to economic inactivity.The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) 2021-2031 is the key strategic framework for promoting and developing good mental health for all in Northern Ireland. In alignment with the MHF/LSE research findings, Actions 1 and 2 of the MHS relate to early intervention and prevention (EI&P). These two actions include provision for access and signposting to the available sources of EI&P help and assistance available to those with the potential to develop, or showing early signs of, mental ill health. An EI&P Plan was developed as part of Actions 1 and 2. It has an initial focus on the effective cross government and sector working, and on the development of the structures required to ensure long-term societal change. These structures are in the process of being put in place.Additionally, Action 3 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to increasing the supports available to individuals, families and communities to address the social factors that impact on their mental health, which should also help to address economic inactivity on mental health grounds in the longer term. While full implementation of all MHS actions, including Action 3, will be subject to future budget availability, good progress across a range of other MHS actions has been made, which will help ensure that everyone, including those who are economically inactive due to their mental health, can access the right support when they need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on all capital works planned for GP practices in West Tyrone.
answer_text: A number of projects have recently been completed, are under way or are planned in the West Tyrone constituency area to create additional capacity within GP practices.  These include:Omagh Primary Care Complex - capital works to support GP practices has been completed.Western Rural Health Care (Newtownstewart) – capital works completed in March 2024 for a multi-disciplinary suite to accommodate physiotherapist, counsellor, social worker and associated clinical administration space. Strabane Health and Care Centre – outline business case approved for a new purpose-built hub in Strabane, as part of the Derry & Strabane City Deal.Carrickmore Health Centre – SPPG, Western HSC Trust and GP practice partners and management are currently exploring options to meet short-term practice needs.  In addition, SPPG Primary Care Infrastructure Development team (PCID) have been working with a range of stakeholders, to include DoH colleagues, Trusts and NI General Practitioners Committee (NIGPC), to agree a prioritisation approach to the development of an investment priority plan for HSC Trust owned spoke premises.  This prioritisation plan for a number of Trust projects identified as needing prioritised investment, including Carrickmore Health Centre, has just been completed. The PCID team will now commence engagement with Carrickmore Practice, DoH Health Estates and Western HSC Trust to agree next steps. The project will be subject to the usual business case approvals (to include any potential revenue costs as well as capital costs). Timings will be dependent on the outcome of this process and approval of funding via the Department’s overarching future capital plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans he has to reduce the (i) gender; and (ii) ethnicity pension gap for staff in his Department.
answer_text: Research indicates that the way that people are auto-enrolled into pension schemes in the private sector can contribute to a gender or ethnicity pension gap. Under current auto-enrolment legislation private sector arrangements often impose a lower earnings threshold as part of their criteria for membership, which can mean that lower earners who are female or from an ethnic minority, may not be eligible for scheme membership.This cannot occur in the NICS where all new employees are automatically enrolled into the pension scheme regardless of the auto-enrolment earnings thresholds and have access to a high quality defined benefit public service pension which is protected against inflation, regardless of their gender or ethnic background.Pay can still influence pension outcomes for civil servants. However, in line with the Equality Commission NI Code of Practice on Equal Pay, the Civil Service carries out annual equal pay audits and any gaps identified are investigated and, where appropriate, actions are taken to address any gaps. The gender pay gap reported in 2023 in favour of males remains for 2024 but has decreased from 4.1% last year to 2% this year. Different working patterns (for example taking time off work for caring responsibilities or working part time) can also contribute to pension gaps. The NICS pension scheme incorporates a number of provisions to reduce these effects, - including making all paid maternity, paternity and adoption leave pensionable. Periods of unpaid leave (including career breaks) also count as qualifying service in the scheme, and upon return to paid employment, the member has the option to consider ways of increasing the value of their pension benefits - including for example by making additional voluntary pension contributions and purchasing added pension amounts, either by lump sum or deduction from salary. My Department continues to monitor research and developments in this area to ensure NICS pension rules are reviewed and updated as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6476/22-27, for an update on when the traffic management scheme at St Conor’s Primary School, Omagh, can recommence.
answer_text: The position remains unchanged regarding this scheme. The need for capital investment across the education estate far exceeds the funding available and my priority remains the delivery of projects in order to keep schools open and provide education access to the most vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's activities in relation to the Meenbog landslide.
answer_text: My officials worked closely with Loughs Agency, which led the criminal investigation of this incident, resulting in a successful prosecution on 7 June 2022 in Letterkenny District Court.		DAERA provided advice and funding to Loughs Agency to enable the necessary monitoring and instream and riparian works on the Mourne Beg and Derg Rivers to be carried out, with the measures targeted to ensure that the river is at optimum quality condition to improve the chances of successful spawning and survival salmonids.  Loughs Agency continues to monitor the health of the Mourne Beg River and is currently carrying out a number of field surveys to further assess the impact of the landslide in terms of the conservation status. Loughs Agency has also designed a number of future schemes that will ensure optimal conditions that support each stage of the salmonid life cycle and further improve the habitat on the Mourne Beg River.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her plans to amend sentencing policy for those convicted of causing death by careless driving when under the influence of drink or drugs.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No. 11 - AQO 880/22-27ANSWERThe Sentencing Bill I plan to bring forward will deliver on a number of recommendations arising from my Department’s public consultation on a review of sentencing policy.  This will include increasing maximum penalties for offences of causing death by driving - including causing death by careless driving while under the influence of drink or drugs - from 14 years to 20 years. In addition, I intend to increase the maximum sentence, where an offender was previously convicted of any of these offences, to a life sentence; and to increase the minimum period of mandatory disqualification for these offences from 2 to 4 years, and, for repeat offenders, from 3 to 6 years. On present planning assumptions we anticipate this Bill being introduced later in 2025.  This will be dependent upon the progress of earlier Assembly business.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his timescale for progressing the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
answer_text: As my decision on this flagship project is considered to be one of a cross-cutting nature, I have been in consultation with Executive colleagues and issued papers to them in June, July and again last week (20 September).    Executive agreement would enable me to make my formal decision and announcement on the project. I hope to be in a position to make that announcement very soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 15019/22/27, to detail his Department's actions to encourage applications to the Northern Ireland Childcare Support Scheme in constituencies with 5 per cent or less of the overall applications.
answer_text: There is no evidence at this stage of significant geographical variation in the uptake of the Northern Ireland Childcare Support Scheme amongst those eligible.  However, officials will continue to monitor this as more data on the scheme becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the Pension (Extension of Automatic Enrolment) Bill, to detail any plans his Department has to improve the take-up amongst groups of workers such as those (i) on zero hours contracts; (ii) with multiple jobs; and (iii) working in the hospitality industry.
answer_text: The proposals in the Pensions (Extension of Automatic Enrolment) Bill are intended to help lower earners build resilience for retirement; support individuals in multiple part-time jobs; and simplify automatic enrolment for employers.  Communication is a key part of the implementation of the proposals. Subject to the Assembly passing the Bill, substantive work on a communications plan, in tandem with DWP, will take place alongside development of the required subordinate legislation. This will ensure that relevant, effective communication is carried out so that employees are aware of the changes and the benefits of saving for their future by contributing to an occupational pension.  It will also provide employers with time to prepare their systems.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the future of Strabane Courthouse.
answer_text: Strabane Courthouse is currently designated as Hearing Centre and is opened for two days per week for the delivery of Magistrates’ Court business.In December 2023, the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) published its Estate Strategy, Making the Place for Justice.https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-courts-and-tribunals-service-estate-strategy-2023-2030The Strategy is structured around 4 Improvement Aims and 12 Strategic Outcomes, that if realised, will result in a courts and tribunals estate that is capable of providing modernised and improved facilities for all citizens of Northern Ireland.To implement its Estate Strategy, NICTS will use data and evidence to baseline the existing estate against the agreed Strategic Outcomes, and develop prioritised investment delivery plans in the context of the resources available.	Decisions on the future use of Strabane Courthouse will form part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 6749/22-27, to detail the number of animal abuse cases (i) reported to his Department; and (ii) prosecuted, in 2023.
answer_text: Under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, my Department is responsible for enforcing the provisions that relate to farmed animals only.  For the purposes of this response, cases of animal abuse, has been interpreted as animal welfare concerns reported to my Department by members of the public and other authorities, for investigation. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to raise the minimum pension contribution rate to encourage people to save more towards their retirement.
answer_text: My Department has direct responsibility for the NICS pension scheme.  This scheme, like the other public service pension schemes, is a ‘defined benefit’ scheme. This means the level of benefit payable to members is set in the scheme rules, and not directly determined by the amount of contributions paid by employees and employers. The pension paid on retirement is determined by the scheme accrual rate, in combination with the member’s level of earnings each year and their length of service. This is in contrast to a ‘defined contribution’ scheme where the benefits payable on retirement are normally subject to the market performance of investments funded directly by the combined member and employer contributions made.Contributions rates for the NICS scheme, and the other public service schemes, are set following regular scheme valuations which are used to calculate the appropriate employer and employee share of the cost of pensions being paid to retired scheme members. The current employer contribution rate for the NICS Pension Scheme is 34.25% of pensionable pay and the current employee contribution rates range from 4.6% to 8.05% dependant on the member’s salary.These rates are based on the outcome of the last completed valuation, which is published on the Department’s website. The next valuation is underway and will set the appropriate rates in the NICS scheme from 1 April 2027. Therefore, raising the minimum pension contribution rate for those in a ‘defined benefit’ public service scheme, such as the NICS scheme, is unlikely to encourage people to save more towards their retirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 5945/22-27, whether her plans to amend sentencing policy for those convicted of causing death by careless driving when under the influence of drink or drugs will still occur during the 2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: The Sentencing Bill I am planning to bring forward will deliver on a number of the recommendations arising from my Department’s public consultation on a review of sentencing policy.  This will include increasing maximum penalties for those convicted of offences causing death by driving.The Sentencing Bill is expected to be a large and complex Bill, given the variety of matters to be included. On present planning assumptions I anticipate this Bill being introduced later in 2025.  This will be dependent upon the progress of earlier Assembly business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5793/22-27, to detail any discussions that have taken place with the Minister for the Economy in relation to the Independent Review of Education recommendation for a Single Education Department, including post-16 education.
answer_text: During the recent internal consultation process following the publication of the Independent Review of Education final report, departmental officials from DE and DfE met to discuss cross-cutting recommendations, including post-16 education.  I recently received advice from officials on the Independent Review and I will provide a response on this in the coming weeks. Thereafter, I will liaise with the Minister for the Economy in due course. The recommendation for a Single Education Department may be a matter for wider Executive consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on legislation for the replacement body for the General Teaching Council For Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Since the decision to dissolve and replace the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland was announced, the Department has conducted a number of consultation and focussed engagement exercises with the teaching profession and has sought to engage constructively with the Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council to build consensus on the preferred way forward.Officials are currently finalising a costed options paper which will allow me to take an informed decision on how the essential functions of GTCNI will continue to be delivered. Once that decision has been taken, I will seek the required legislative approval from my Executive colleagues, allowing the detailed drafting of the necessary Bill to be progressed. The Department remains committed to passing the required legislative changes within the current Assembly mandate.

Engagements have been across a number of areas including skills development, supply chain opportunities, trade and sector development.  Invest NI officials met the H&W Interim CEO at the Belfast yard earlier this month.  Discussions covered the trading status of the company and the review currently being undertaken. My officials and Invest NI have also been discussing skills support with the company. Invest NI continues to engage with both the company and the Department for Business and Trade (DBT) on the evolving situation. And to advocate for the best possible outcome for the North of Ireland, where possible.

The 12 week consultation was recently extended by 2 weeks until 14 October 2024.  Following the consultation period, my officials will analyse the responses and put forward policy recommendations that will underpin the Bill. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on all greenway developments on which his Department is engaging with Derry City and Strabane District Council.
answer_text: I was delighted to attend a celebration event earlier this month to mark the completion of the Northwest Greenway Network.  Derry City & Strabane District Council and their partners have successfully delivered this significant project that includes greenway, walking and cycling links between Strabane and Castlefinn, and between Maydown and Quigley's Point. I’m pleased that my Department was able to contribute funding and support to the establishment of that network.Officials continue to liaise with all councils to better understand their 5-year greenway programme and have recently engaged with Derry City and Strabane District Council to discuss their greenway priorities for this financial year in line with their programme. answer_text: The current budget allocation for the Casement Park project, approved by the Executive in 2011, is £62.5 million. The UCGAA contribution remains at £15 million, while the Irish government announced in January 2024 its intention to contribute €50 million (circa £43 million).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any response they have had from the new Secretary of State in relation to their request to extend the deadline to apply for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: We wrote to the previous Secretary of State in April and May 2024 in support of an extension to the deadline to allow eligible applicants to avail of backdated payments, highlighting the potential impact that not extending the backdating deadline would have on victims and survivors. We were pleased that the new Secretary of State announced on 1 August 2024 that the backdating deadline would be extended to align with the Scheme closing date of 30 August 2026.  This will allow victims and survivors the time to complete and submit applications to the Victims’ Payments Board.We note that the Secretary of State’s review of the Scheme determined that an extension to the date for which new applications can be made is not necessary at this time. We will keep this under review along with the victims’ and survivors’ groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the data breach which occurred on 1 August 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action he is taking to increase the public transport spending per head in Northern Ireland, which is significantly lower than in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: It is difficult to make comparisons between expenditure in the North with that of the rest of England, Scotland and Wales due to the differing levels of deregulation and geographical spread in the regions.  The rail network is also much more extensive in England , Scotland and Wales.  . The All-Island Strategic Rail Review, which was published in July seeks to address the limitations of the existing network by proposing additional track capacity, electrification, increased speeds, higher service frequencies and new routes. I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to explore all avenues of maximising funding for the public transport .
