question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why the number of children and young people in care continuously for 12 months or longer is 46 per cent higher than ten years ago.
answer_text: The number of children continuously in care for 12 months or longer has increased from 2,071 at the end of September 2013 to 3,024 at the end of September 2023, an increase of 46%. The total number of children in care has also risen, from 2,807 at the end of March 2013 to 3,801 at the end of March 2023, an increase of 35%. This increase can be attributed to a number of factors. More children are being admitted to care than are being discharged. In 2013, 995 children were admitted to care and 850 children were discharged. This means that for every one child discharged from care, 1.17 children were admitted. That gap has widened. In 2023, 980 children were admitted to care and 748 children were discharged, meaning that for every one child discharged, 1.31 children were admitted.Children are also spending longer in care. In 2013, 35% of children discharged from care had been in care for less than three months. By 2023, only 14% of those discharged had been in care for less than three months. In 2023, 25% of children discharged from care had been in care for five years or more—up from 18% in 2013.Research has pointed to an enhanced awareness of child protection issues over recent years as one factor underpinning decisions to protect children who are potentially at risk by bringing them into care. There is also an established link between increasing levels of child poverty and the numbers of children in care. Children from the most deprived areas in Northern Ireland make up the highest proportion of children in care for 12 months or more (43%), compared to just 5% of children from the least deprived areas. Children in care can have a range of additional and often complex needs, and one fifth of the children in care for 12 months or longer at 30 September 2023 had a disability. Practitioners describe growing complexity of need among children and young people over the last number of years, with families often struggling to meet those needs at home.	The report of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services, published in June 2023, highlighted the impact of poverty and social deprivation on families, and was clear about the need for more support for families, to help them care for their children. My Department has established a programme of reform which is leading on the implementation of a number of the Review’s recommendations, including improving support for families and services for children with disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to increase the number of people donating organs.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to promote organ transplantation under the Health (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.  In line with this duty, my Department oversees a comprehensive rolling programme of outreach and engagement activity to promote knowledge and informed discussion about organ donation, which is aimed at driving registrations on the NHS Organ Donor Register (ODR), and sustaining and increasing rates of consent to both living and deceased organ donation. The programme is delivered by the Public Health Agency (PHA) through a partnership approach with Health and Social Care Trusts, NHS Blood and Transplant, local government, and charity partners and spans the community, voluntary and education sectors. It includes local media campaigns which are delivered across a range of platforms and tailored for maximum impact in line with an intelligence-led communications plan, and uses health intelligence data to measure and report on the impact of promotional activities. The appointment of an Organ Donation Promotion Manager in July 2020 allowed specific focus to be applied to the development and delivery of this programme, however the statutory duty did not formally come into effect until April 2022. A detailed report of this activity from 2022/23 is currently being finalised for submission to the Assembly in the coming months. This will provide details and impacts of all outreach and engagement activity, partnerships, information campaigns, events such as the Balmoral Show and Belfast Marathon, and focused initiatives including Organ Donation Week.In summary, during 2022/23 organ donation rates in Northern Ireland were comparably high for deceased donation, at 30 donors per million of population (pmp). It is important to note that in any year, only a small number of people (around 1%) will die in circumstances where organ donation is clinically possible, so all activity is geared towards increasing opt-in registrations on the ODR and increasing the consent rate in these cases. The consent rate for donation after death in Northern Ireland in 2022/23 was 65% which is comparable to other regions, although the overall UK consent rate fell from 66% to 62% in that period. As at 31 March 2024, 55% of the population (1,048,273 people) in Northern Ireland had registered as an organ donor on the NHS Organ Donor Register – well above the UK average of 42%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the decision to cease Education Authority assistance with school enrolments to children and young people living in asylum contingency accommodation.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has not ceased assistance to families in contingency accommodation seeking school enrolment support. The EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) team continue to play a leading role in the education sector’s response to children arriving through Asylum and Refugee processes and are an invaluable service to families living in Contingency Accommodation (CA).  Significant progress has been made in recent months to reduce the length of time spent in contingency hotel accommodation and current trends now show families receive an offer of temporary community-based accommodation within a short period of time.  Given the reduced timeframe for housing, IES will delay the formal school enrolment process to avoid the significant disruption caused by the circumstance where a child is placed in a school, only to move accommodation within a very short period of time, hence requiring a new placement.  The needs of every child will, of course, be considered on an individual basis and where it is clear that some families will have longer periods in temporary hotel accommodation, IES will work with those families to secure a suitable school placement as quickly as possible.Families can, of course, approach schools directly to register children, should they wish and IES will advise families accordingly. Where a family requests immediate placement, they will be provided with guidance on how to approach schools and signposted to support from school admissions colleagues in the EA.The EA will review this temporary change in operational practices at the mid-term point around the end of October and any revised service delivery arrangements will be agreed between my Department and EA at that point.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to expand and update the school facilities at St Mary's Primary School, Cloughcor.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing an unavoidable minor works project to replace a mobile at St Mary’s Primary School. A School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project is also progressing for the school. The proposed SEP project involves the construction of a new extension to the main school building, the refurbishment of the existing building and site works to provide hard play areas, traffic management and car parking facilities. The school’s mobile units will be removed as part of the SEP. The SEP project is currently at developed design stage (RIBA Stage 3).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5790/22-27, for an update on Waiting List Initiatives taken by his Department.
answer_text: Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities. It is, however, a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.    The funding available for this year falls far short of what is required to address our lengthy waiting lists.  The £76m allocated by my Department for waiting lists will only enable us to stand still in terms of cancer and time critical patients.  My Department estimates that, in addition to the £76m, up to £135m is required per year for 5 years for long waiting patients along with a further recurrent requirement of £80m to build sustainable elective care capacity. However, against this difficult financial backdrop, much good work is underway to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, for example through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and initiatives like mega clinics.  In addition, my Department is working with Trusts to increase productivity and efficiency through the use of a wide range of service improvements.  As a result, there has been small but tangible improvements in the waiting list position.  In March 2024 there was a 14.3% reduction in the number of patients waiting for inpatient/daycase treatment compared to March 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to relieve traffic congestion in Omagh town.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the traffic congestion which occurs in Omagh town and particularly on the A5 Great Northern Road during peak periods. The planned A5 Western Transport Corridor dual carriageway will remove strategic traffic from the Great Northern Road and greatly improve traffic progression around Omagh. In the interim, my officials are continuing to identify improvements to the road network around the town to improve traffic progression and reduce congestion. Recently, work was completed on a road widening scheme at the A5 Great Northern Road/ Railway Terrace Junction. This scheme provided an extension to the left turn lane improving traffic progression by increased capacity through the junction. Design work is ongoing to improve traffic light sequencing at this location. Work has also been completed on the A32 Dromore Road on approach to Omagh. The left turn slip road has been extended for vehicles wishing to turn onto Railway Terrace/A5 and the footway has been widened providing improved pedestrian facilities at this location. Both these schemes were completed ahead of a proposed resurfacing scheme on the A5 Great Northern Road/A32 Dromore Road programmed for later this year. A traffic signal upgrade was also completed in association with a resurfacing scheme at Drumragh Avenue/Bridge Street/Sedan Avenue Junction in Omagh.In addition to improving the road network for vehicles, it is important to explore other modes of transport to reduce congestion on our roads and to grasp opportunities to improve facilities for walking, cycling and public transport. As part of last year’s active travel programme, further shared use cycleways/footways were completed on Dublin Road, Cookstown Road and on the Glencam Road in conjunction with a road widening and realignment scheme. Active Travel works are planned on A505 Cookstown Road, Farmhill Road and Gortin Road in conjunction with a road widening scheme as part of the proposed Strule Campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number; and (ii) the type of procedures that have taken place at Omagh Hospital since its designation as the second regional Day Procedure Centre, broken down by month.
answer_text: The number of procedures that have taken place at Omagh Hospital from April 2023 to March 2024, broken down by month are set out in Table 1 below: Month of ProcedureNumber of procedures*Apr-231021May-231251Jun-231291Jul-231198Aug-231159Sep-231188Oct-231285Nov-231374Dec-231216Jan-241355Feb-241410Mar-241143Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health NI.                                                                                                                               Only admissions to Acute POC have been included.  Figures do not denote individuals and therefore an individual may have more than one procedure within the time period.                                    *Activity levels reflect coded activity and will be subject to changeThe procedures undertaken include laparoscopic cholecystectomies, varicose veins, transurethral resection of the prostate (TURPs), hydroceles, removal of stones and endoscopies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the latest report from the Trussell Trust, which estimates that 30,000 claiming Universal Credit in Northern Ireland have needed to use a food bank in the last 12 months.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering real, positive and lasting change for individuals and families. This includes making sure that every pound spent will have a positive impact on the lives of those who need it most.I have noted with concern the Trussell Trust’s estimate that up to 30,000 Universal Credit claimants in Northern Ireland have needed to use a foodbank in the last 12 months.The rates of social security benefits including Universal Credit are set by the UK Government each year and maintained in parity in Northern Ireland. Any deviation from parity, including higher rates of benefits, would result in increased costs to the Block Grant.My Department does offer additional support for those in need of short-term financial help through the Discretionary Support Scheme, subject to meeting eligibility criteria. Further information is available at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-supportThe Department also continues to fund Social Supermarkets via local Councils to assist those facing food insecurity.Help is also available from my Department’s Make The Call service which will carry out a needs assessment based on a person’s individual circumstances and look at what benefits, supports and services that may be available to them. This service is free and confidential. Further information is available at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-service

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on discussions with the Westminster government to extend the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme.
answer_text: On 9th July 2024, I wrote to the newly appointed Defra Secretary of State, Steve Reed MP, bringing to his attention a range of pressing issues for my department, including labour shortages and the importance of immigration policy to our agri-food industry. On 30th July 2024, the UK Government published a Ministerial Statement in respect of legal migration, confirming that changes to immigration policy and its associated visa routes introduced by the previous Government, would remain in place. The UK Government are yet to make a public announcement specifically in relation to the Seasonal Agricultural Workers route or in respect of the Migration Advisory Committee’s (MAC’s) seasonal worker visa review, published on 16th July 2024. The MAC did not include a recommendation that the length of the visa should be extended, nor did they suggest the scope of the visa should be widened to include other industries. The UK Government is currently considering the MAC’s recommendations and will announce a detailed response in due course.It remains vital that labour solutions can be found for NI sectors currently reliant on migrant labour, until such time that automation is more advanced and accessible. Whilst immigration policy is not a devolved matter, I remain committed to working with local agrifood businesses and the UK Government to find solutions for those occupations that are experiencing shortages.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14139/22-27, for his assessment of why the number of patients in receipt of antidepressants increased by over 33,500 in the last five years.
answer_text: Further investigation would be required to understand the reasons why use of anti-depressants has increased over the last five years.  The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) 2021-2031 fully recognises the benefits of non-pharmaceutical supports, for example talking therapies and social prescribing, for individuals struggling with their mental health, including those impacted by depression.Talking therapies are available in each Health and Social Care Trust, as well as through various community and voluntary sector (C&V) organisations. Actions 5 and 19 of the MHS respectively commit to expanding hubs for talking therapies and to further embedding psychological therapies across all steps of care. Additionally, Action 4 of the Strategy commits to the provision of social wellbeing supports, such as social prescribing, as interventions for those struggling with their mental health. Social prescribing is a way to link medical care to non-clinical, locally delivered support services, and is typically available through the C&V sector.Action 16 recognises the importance of Recovery Colleges, already well established in each Health and Social Care Trust. These are facilities where recovery-focused mental health courses are jointly delivered by individuals with lived experience of mental distress and practitioners in the field. They are free and open to all.Subject to availability of future funding, delivery of all these actions will ensure that a full range of non-pharmaceutical options is available to promote good mental health and aid recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A5 upgrade, in light of his previous statement that he hoped to receive Executive approval to proceed with the project in mid-August 2024.
answer_text: As this is a cross-cutting issue, I briefed my Executive colleagues in July on progress and issued my initial response to the Planning Appeals Commission report. I hope to submit a further paper shortly for their agreement on progression of the A5 project. I hope to be in a position to make an announcement on the way forward soon after that.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the sickness absence rate in Ambulance Service staff in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: The sickness absence rate for the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) for each of the latest twelve months available data is outlined below.Month NIAS Sickness Absence Rate #Aug-2316.04%Sep-2314.41%Oct-2314.91%Nov-2316.01%Dec-2315.56%Jan-2413.60%Feb-2412.08%Mar-2410.70%Apr-2410.24%May-249.07%Jun-2411.00%Jul-2411.71%# Source: NI Ambulance Service.  The sickness absence rate is defined as the percentage of scheduled hours lost to sickness absence & industrial injury.NIAS has experienced high levels of sickness absence in the post Covid period. The highest levels of absence have pertained to mental health and musculoskeletal (MSK) reasons. To address this issue, NIAS has implemented a range of actions that have yielded improvement over the course of the 12-month period.  These include investment in the establishment of a new Health and Wellbeing Team including full time dedicated Paramedic Peer Support Officers with a dedicated focus on mental health; a pro-active approach to management of work-related stress including assessment of specific stress factors and related working with occupational health colleagues; dedicated commissioning of a full-time physiotherapist through occupational health to support those with MSK issues; and a wide range of health and wellbeing initiatives for staff to support physical and mental health.NIAS is committed to continuing this improvement and to continuing to invest in the wellbeing of its workforce. As such, progress continues to be closely monitored by the Trust at the most senior level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to upgrade (i) Strabane bus centre; and (ii) Omagh bus centre.
answer_text: Translink are currently carrying out a feasibility study regarding potential improvements to the Omagh Bus Station and this report is due to completed later this year. There are no current plans to further upgrade Strabane Bus centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the NI Chamber and BDO NI Quarterly Economic Survey Summary Q2 2024 report regarding the creation of good jobs.
answer_text: It is encouraging to see the focus on Good Jobs in the most recent quarterly survey. The results have provided insight from responding members. This survey, along with other research and studies, contributes to the wider evidence base which will inform future policies on Good Jobs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of the 100 new battery electric buses that will be based in (i) Belfast; (ii) Derry; (iii) Limavady; (iv) Strabane; (v) Coleraine; and (vi) Portadown.
answer_text: The 100 new BEV's will be assigned as follows:  Derry-Londonderry – 10, Coleraine – 12, Craigavon – 18 and Belfast – 60.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 14218/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 14218/22-27 was answered on 24 September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why applicants to the Northern Ireland Civil Service EO1 recruitment competition undertaken in May 2024 have not been informed of any outcomes of the competition.
answer_text: Results from the latest phase of interviews for the NICS general service EO1 competition were issued to candidates on 20th June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the financial support provided to those competing in the (i) Paris 2024 Olympic Games; and (ii) Paris 2024 Paralympic Games.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's top priorities for discussion with ministers of the incoming UK government.
answer_text: The historic underfunding of our infrastructure and utilities means that our services need a sustained multi-year funding commitment and associated plan to improve. As such the immediate priority for discussion with ministers of the incoming UK government for my Department is the need to stabilise our infrastructure across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department's top priorities for discussion with ministers of the incoming UK government.
answer_text: My priorities for discussion with the incoming British government remain the same those set out in my Economic vision speech presented to the Assembly in February 2024:	To increase the proportion of working-age people in good jobs;	Promote Regional Balance;	Raise productivity and; 	Reduce Carbon emmissions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail her Department's main priorities for discussion with the new Chancellor of the Exchequer.
answer_text: I had an introductory meeting with the new Chief Secretary to the Treasury, Darren Jones, on 9 July 2024 and, together with the First and deputy First Ministers, met with the new Chancellor, Rachel Reeves, on 17 July 2024.Both meetings focussed on resetting the relationships to allow a collaborative partnership between the British Government and the Executive, the pressures on the Executive’s finances and the need for proper funding of public services, including public sector pay.I also raised the issue of funding for Casement Park, in particular the need for urgent confirmation of the British Government’s contribution in the context of the deadline for Euro 2028.I intend to have further meetings with both the Chief Secretary to the Treasury and the Chancellor in the coming weeks and months for further discussions on the Executive’s finances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's top priorities for discussion with ministers of the incoming UK government.
answer_text: I have sought to engage early with the new Government, in particular with my counterparts, on a range of issues. I look forward to working collaboratively with them on matters which reflect a priority for me and my department.I have already met with the Secretary of State of Department of Work and Pensions and have revised the two child limit for Universal Credit, the Winter Fuel Payment and Disability and Work.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their top priorities for discussion with the new Prime Minister and his government.
answer_text: We discussed a range of issues at our meeting with the Prime Minister on Monday 8 July 2024.  These included fiscal sustainability, transformation, health and growing the economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she will answer AQW 13811/22-27.
answer_text: The response to AQW 13811/22-27 was issued on 5th July.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions he is taking to ensure regional input into the Autism Strategy.
answer_text: The Autism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 places a responsibility on the Department of Health to prepare a cross-departmental autism strategy in collaboration with all government departments which sets out key priorities and actions to enable autistic people and their families to be supported throughout their lives. The Autism Strategy 2023 to 2028 has been developed to reflect the methodology, content and timeframes set out in the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022 legislation. This followed extensive stakeholder engagement with people who have lived experience of autism, organisations that advocate on their behalf, all HSC Trusts, Departments and public bodies and included a public consultation on key priorities in December 2022.The Autism Strategy 2023-2028 was published on 13 December 23 with a two year Delivery Plan for 2023-2025 which will be reviewed on an annual basis.  The Delivery Plan sets out the actions to be achieved against commitments, timeframes, associated outcomes and the key partners involved, all of which are being delivered on a local and regional basis.Work on implementation of the strategy is underway across government departments and public bodies and progress will be assessed through the annual monitoring report, which officials have commenced work on. This will be shared with Executive Colleagues and published on the Department’s website once collated and approved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 13909/22-27, to detail the targets his Department has set in the action plan to grow the Advanced Manufacturing, Materials and Engineering sector.
answer_text: The Advanced Manufacturing, Materials and Engineering Sectoral Action Plan sets out 25 key actions that will be taken forward by my Department to help grow the sector and deliver on my four key economic priorities of good jobs, regional balance, high productivity, and decarbonisation. The action plan sets out which of my key priorities each action will contribute to, the delivery partners involved and the timescales around delivery. Work is ongoing within my Department to develop a suite of metrics for tracking progress against my four priorities.  These metrics will be set out within the 2024/25 DfE Business Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to his Written Ministerial Statement on 3 July 2024 in relation to Heritage, Culture and Creativity, to detail the targets and timeline for delivery of the strategy.
answer_text: The Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme will deliver policies for heritage, arts, museums and public libraries.  Work to develop each policy will progress on a bespoke timetable, to meet the needs of the sector.  Work is in the early stages and, as noted in my statement of 3 July 2024, I will provide further information, including on anticipated timing, in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will answer AQW 13294/22-27.
answer_text: I can confirm that I answered AQW 13294/22-27 on 5th July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 12067/22-27, for an assessment of the scale and timescale for the roll-out of social supermarkets by his Department.
answer_text: My Department has been working with local Councils on a co-design basis to roll-out social supermarket models. Good progress has been made with projects operating in each Council area either as fully fledged social supermarkets or adhering to the high-level principle of providing more than food. These have offered vital support at a time an increasing number of people are seeking support with the increased cost of living.The approach taken in each area differs, reflecting a range of variables including the capacity of the Voluntary and Community Sector, the geographical spread and rurality of the area, and pre-existing levels of partnership working. Models range from new standalone projects serving the whole area to supporting multiple pre-existing charitable food providers across the council area to enhance their support and ensure those using the service are as a minimum referred onwards to additional wraparound services to address the underlying causes of their households food insecurity. The models continue to evolve and develop and in recognition of the level of need, and the challenges faced in providing a service across wide geographical areas, an increased initial budget of £2.75m has been allocated to support the continuation of this work in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the progress that has been made in meeting targets set for his Department in the Autism Strategy delivery plan 2023-25 for the end of March 2024.
answer_text: My officials are currently finalising the Department of Education’s (DE) 2023/24 update to the Department of Health (DoH) ‘Autism Strategy Delivery Plan 2023/25.  The final report will be published by DoH.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the progress that has been made in meeting targets set for his Department in the Autism Strategy delivery plan 2023-25 for the end of March 2024.
answer_text: Work on implementation of the Autism Strategy 2023-2028 and Delivery Plan 2023-2025 is underway across government departments and public bodies. This includes the development of guidance for employers to increase understanding and support for autistic people in employment in collaboration with the Equality Commission NI, delivery of autism capacity building sessions for Northern Ireland Housing Executive, the development of GP training videos to improve equality of access to primary care. Work is also underway with the HSC Leadership Centre to undertake a review to better understand the needs of people with learning disabilities and a co-existing diagnosis of autism and also to take forward phase one of the development of a Regional Autism Information Service.Progress will be assessed through the annual monitoring report which officials have commenced work on. This will be shared with Executive Colleagues and published on the Department’s website once collated and approved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a timescale on the appointment of the Independent Autism Reviewer.
answer_text: I am fully aware of the responsibly to appoint an Independent Autism Reviewer for Northern Ireland and acknowledge the significant role this post will bring for autistic people and their families. I had deferred a decision on the Reviewer until I could consider Health funding pressures in the round, particularly taking account of the outcome of June monitoring, but I will take that decision in the coming weeks.I recognise the importance of ensuring that all people with Autism or those waiting on an assessment for Autism, their families and carers can receive the right access to services.  My Department will continue to work in partnership across government departments, the health and social care sector, public bodies and the community and voluntary sector to make this happen.  Whilst the monitoring and reporting role will be fulfilled by the Independent Autism Reviewer, in adherence to legislative requirement, until this appointment is made the Department of Health will commit to this role.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients receiving antidepressant drugs, broken down by (i) gender; (ii) age profile; and (iii) Health and Social Care Trust area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The HSC Business Services Organisation holds data on prescriptions prescribed in primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist, dispensing doctor or appliance contractor in Northern Ireland. Tables 1, 2 and 3 overleaf present the number of patients in receipt of antidepressants by gender, age band and Local Commissioning Group for each of the last 5 financial years (between April 2019 and March 2024).  This information is publicly available in Annex Tables 3.1 and 3.3 of the Family Practitioner Services General Pharmaceutical Statistics for Northern Ireland 2023/24, which was published on 27th June 2024 and is available on the HSC Business Services Organisation Website (hscni.net).  Footnotes provided under Table 1 apply to all three tables.Table 1 Number of individuals in receipt of antidepressants, by gender and financial year 1,2,3,4,5,6YearMaleFemaleTotal2019/20130,726225,931356,6572020/21129,702227,420357,1222021/22135,471236,663372,1342022/23139,733242,555382,2882023/24143,373246,819390,192Source: Family Practitioner Services Pharmacy Payment System, HSC Business Services Organisation1 All information is for prescriptions prescribed in Primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist, dispensing doctor or appliance contractor. If a patient does not take a prescription to a dispenser and have it provided to them, the information will not be included in the dataset. Data on secondary care prescribing and private prescriptions are not held by the Business Services Organisation.2 Antidepressants are used to treat a range of conditions and BSO dispensing data does not capture the indication for which the drug has been prescribed and subsequently dispensed.3 Information has been provided for medications listed under BNF Chapter 4.3.4 Patient numbers have been estimated based on unique patients whose dispensed prescriptions have been successfully matched against the central GP patient register in the given time period. There is a chance that some patients may have been missed if all of the prescriptions dispensed to them in the given time period were not successfully scanned.  However, because patients receiving these medications will generally have submitted multiple prescriptions in a year, there is a high probability they will have been picked up at least once. There is a risk of some patients who were newly prescribed these medications towards the end of the year being missed if the few prescriptions dispensed to them were not successfully scanned.5 Geographical location is assigned by linking patient address postcode information to NISRA’s Central Postcode Directory. Patients for which it has not been possible to assign a geographical location are recorded as ‘Unknown’.  Patient postcode information is only captured when a prescription form has been successfully scanned. 6 The age, gender and geographic information for individuals receiving such items is taken at the time the first prescription containing those items was processed

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the targets his Department is setting for the US/NI Cultural Working Group.
answer_text: The United States / Northern Ireland Cultural Working Group is an inter-agency partnership that has been set up to identify and promote practical opportunities to explore, preserve and promote the unique cultural heritage that connects Northern Ireland and the United States. The initiative aims to build on the work of a range of partners including the Ulster-Scots Agency, the Public Record Office and the Northern Ireland Bureau in promoting the role of the Ulster-Scots and other communities in shaping America. The Group is giving consideration to planning a programme of cultural activities to promote opportunities at Federal and State level in the United States and in Northern Ireland on the historical links.  The Group is led by my Department and operates within agreed terms of reference.  My Department has not set any specific targets at this stage given that the Group is at an early stage, and the need to encourage collaboration and creativity in developing new opportunities. The work of the Group and the 250th anniversary of American independence in 2026 represents a unique opportunity for Northern Ireland and I welcome the US administration’s support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the average level of debt for students in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Statistics available from the Student Loans Company show that the average student loan debt for those graduating, with student loans issued by my Department, in each of last five years was as follows:Financial YearAverage loan balance (£) 2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24Average loan balance for those graduating23,270                       24,420                         23,790                      24,090                        25,370

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 13699/22-27, for a breakdown of the location of the 665 full time equivalent posts currently vacant in his Department.
answer_text: My Department’s vacancy control measures will reflect 665 full time equivalent (FTE) existing vacancies not being filled in 2024-25.  As at 1 April 2024, these vacancies include – 537 FTE in Work & Health and Operational Delivery Groups, predominantly related to social security benefit and pensions delivery; 72 FTE in Corporate Services Group;34 FTE in Communities, Place and Local Government Group; and 22 FTE in Housing Sustainability Group.It is not currently possible to provide a breakdown of these posts by location.  This information would need to be manually gathered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update, including a timeframe, on flood alleviation schemes in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing feasibility studies in both Omagh and Strabane to determine if economically viable solutions to address river flooding can be identified. These studies are expected to be completed by the end of 2024. A review of the feasibility of potential flood alleviation measures for Sion Mills is also planned to commence later this year and will be completed early in 2025.  In addition, my Department has commenced the design of the Elm Wood Green Flood Alleviation Scheme in Erganagh, Castlederg and it is hoped this phase will be complete by Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) budget; and (ii) staffing costs for setting up and servicing the US/NI Cultural Working Group.
answer_text: The United States / Northern Ireland Cultural Working Group is an inter-agency partnership that aims to further cultural links with the United States.The Group does not have a specific budget allocation as such. It will be a matter for each individual partner organisation to meet the cost of their own contribution to the programme.  The Group is chaired by the Director of the Public Record Office of Northern Ireland. PRONI also provides the Secretariat to the Group with these costs being met from existing resources. PRONI’s involvement builds on key aspects of its existing outreach work in promoting its archives and collections to American audiences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the timescale for (i) the Good Jobs consultation; and (ii) delivery of the Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: The public consultation launched on 1 July and will run until 30 September 2024.I estimate that the Employment Rights Bill will be introduced to the Assembly by January 2026; or earlier if there is scope to do so.  Once introduced, progress of the bill will be determined by Assembly processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department plans to spend the £500,000 extra arts funding announced in the June monitoring round.
answer_text: The additional funding for this important sector will be distributed via my Department’s Arm’s Length Body, the Arts Council of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 13348/22-27 and AQW 13349/22-27, whether she plans to review the current processes.
answer_text: NICSHR are working closely with the interview platform provider to examine where improvements to the guidance for the prerecorded interview stage can be made to enhance the advice to candidates in order to mitigate issues and increase the candidate experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how his proposals to remove zero-hour contracts will impact substitute teachers.
answer_text: My “Good Jobs” Employment Rights consultation seeks views on a range of options for dealing with zero hours contracts. Responses from stakeholders will be crucial in shaping legislation that will provide the best solutions to replace zero hours contracts with contracts that both provide flexibility and protect workers’ rights.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of (i) the number of people currently in good jobs; and (ii) the proposed target increase being set by his Department.
answer_text: I have recently set out that I will be using the Carnegie Framework as the basis for assessing Good Jobs.  This Framework identifies seven dimensions of job quality and my Department will work to progress all seven dimensions with a particular focus on addressing issues around job security, guaranteed hours and low wages. An assessment of progress will be included in the Department’s forthcoming business plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 13113/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 13113/22-27 was answered on 1 July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on building a new youth centre in Strabane.
answer_text: Unfortunately, my Department continues to face significant pressures across all capital programmes and are, therefore, unable to provide a timeline for delivery of the proposed scheme for Strabane Youth Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what proportion of cystic fibrosis patients have access to a specialist social worker.
answer_text: All children and adult cystic fibrosis patients within Northern Ireland have access to support through regional centres for Cystic Fibrosis in Belfast Health and Social Care Trust which have dedicated multidisciplinary teams including specialist social workers. The Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children Cystic Fibrosis (CF) unit is the regional centre for the care of all children with Cystic Fibrosis in N Ireland while the Adult Cystic Fibrosis Unit is based at the Belfast City Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to address recruitment and retention issues in specialist cystic fibrosis teams.
answer_text: Cystic Fibrosis (CF) is an inherited disease affecting, and in due course, damaging the lungs and digestive tract.  Belfast Trust is the regional centre for CF patients with support provided by a dedicated multidisciplinary team including medical, nursing, AHP, pharmacy and social care input.  In 2019/20, the funded recurrent baseline for staffing in the adult unit was £1.03m, and the paediatric unit was £0.77m. This was increased in 2020 by an additional recurrent investment of £0.52m and £0.51m respectively to secure additional staffing to support the introduction of new specialist medicines, taking the recurrent baseline infrastructure funding to £1.55m for the adult unit, and £1.28m for the paediatric unit. My Department continues to engage with Belfast Trust and regularly reviews changes in clinical demand to inform the future service delivery model and staffing requirements for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the proportion of patients with Alzheimer's disease that have their assessment within the nine-week target, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information is not routinely collated specifically for Alzheimer’s, however the Department receives information from the Trusts on their Non Inpatient Mental Health waiting times returns for patients waiting for all Dementia specialist services.  That information is presented in the following table.Trust0-9 weeks            (0-63 days)Total WaitsWait 9 weeks or less as proportion of total waits (%)Belfast22750145.3Northern26749953.5South Eastern21667132.2Southern141141100.0Western21976728.6Region Total1070257941.5Source: Mental Health Outpatient Waiting Times returns from Trusts

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Child Support Enforcement Bill departs in any way from the provisions of Child Support (Enforcement) Act 2023 for England, Wales and Scotland.
answer_text: As part of my department’s commitment to improve the Child Maintenance Service in helping to secure more child maintenance for qualifying children, and by extension improve adherence to the timely payment of child maintenance, I recently introduced the Child Support Enforcement Bill to the Assembly. The Second Stage of the Bill was completed on 25 June 2024.In line with the principle of parity, this Executive Bill mirrors the provisions of the corresponding Child Support (Enforcement) Act 2023, which received Royal Assent on 20 July 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will make representations to the incoming Westminster government to extend the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme.
answer_text: I wrote to the Home Secretary on 8th March 2024 to express my concerns about the effects of UK Immigration policy changes on our wider agri-food industry. While it was welcome that the UK Government confirmed in May 2024, that the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme (SAWS) will be extended until 2029, it was disappointing that no changes regarding increasing the length of the visa to more than 6 months was made. Following the outcome of the General Election, I plan to write to UK Government Ministers, to continue to emphasise the impact of Immigration policy changes on our agri-food industry and the importance of real and timely engagement with the devolved administrations on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Duty of Candour and Being Open framework, including a timeframe for introduction.
answer_text: Being Open FrameworkMy officials are currently developing proposals for a regional Being Open Framework with plans for a consultation exercise in the months ahead. I anticipate the Framework will be in place in late 2024 or early 2025. Duty of CandourI am in favour of every individual working in public service being honest and candid when people suffer unnecessary harm, whether physical, mental or psychological. That is my desired outcome. The question then becomes how to create a regime that minimises the chill factor that deters individuals from being candid and maximises their willingness and responsibility to engage in an honest review of what went wrong. I wish to assess the impact defined sanctions is likely to have on that balance.While this is a priority for me and my Department, I have no set timeframes for final decisions at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he and his Department has had with the Northern Ireland Mushroom Growers Association in the last three years.
answer_text: I and my predecessor have met NIMGA on 3 separate occasions to discuss issues including labour shortages, challenges facing mushroom growers as well as potential opportunities for growth for the mushroom industry. I have also accepted a further invitation to meet with NIMGA and will visit one of its member’s farms in the coming months. My Departmental officials have engaged with NIMGA in a variety of ways including meetings, email correspondence and wider stakeholder events. Areas of particular focus have included Immigration Policy with regards to the Seasonal Agricultural Worker’s Scheme and the impact on labour availability, the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme, the consultation on a proposed Bill to amend legacy EU Common Market Organisation (CMO) schemes and opportunities for training and support via CAFRE and Departmental support schemes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department is taking to reverse the drop in manufacturing output.
answer_text: The Department recognises the challenges facing the Manufacturing sector across the North of Ireland which include the need to innovate and incorporate new technologies, a lack of skilled labour, access to finance and the need to decarbonise. The Department’s recently launched Action Plan to grow the Advanced Manufacturing, Materials and Engineering (AMME) sector outlines proposed actions that will help address these challenges and deliver on my key economic priorities. The proposed actions will support investment by manufacturing companies in skills and R&D, in emerging technologies for future growth and will provide opportunities for the sector to grow and prosper through maximising the benefits of dual market access.  The Action Plan also supports Invest NI’s work with companies across the sector to innovate, develop skills, create good jobs, focus on sustainability, and grow new business. This includes supporting businesses to reduce their carbon emissions through initiatives such as the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant, the Resource Efficiency Capital Grant, and the Resource Matching Service. Since 1 April 2024, Invest NI has facilitated over 50 manufacturing companies to exhibit on North of Ireland stands at international trade shows with a further 19 exhibitions and sector missions planned between now and March 2025.  In addition, Invest NI has hosted eight inward VIP/Influencer visits for manufacturing businesses across all sectors.  The visits consisted of a total of 48 visitors from a number of countries including France, Germany, China, US, Belgium, Nordics, Japan and Australia.The Action Plan also encourages the use of activities and innovations offered by the City and Growth Deal investments which provide access to automation and robotics, cleantech, smart design, digital twinning, industrial decarbonisation, and artificial intelligence.Through the Action Plan, the Department and key delivery partners such as Invest NI, InterTradeIreland and the Makers Alliance network which includes Manufacturing NI will continue to work with the sector to harness its potential.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with the Labour Party in relation to the funding of Casement Park.
answer_text: The First Minister raised the issue with the Secretary of State and the Prime Minister.

Officials have been tasked with examining further how Failte Ireland branding can be extended to include regions in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the required formal processes will be completed to allow the supply of Kaftrio, Symkevi and Orkambi for patients with cystic fibrosis.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. When the final guidance for these treatments is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfIn the interim, Kaftrio, Symkevi and Orkambi are currently available to clinically suitable patients in Northern Ireland under existing Health and Social Care arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the General Dental Services Statistics 2023/24, what action he is taking to encourage the 32 per cent of the population not currently registered with a dentist to do so.
answer_text: General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and as such are not required to register new patients. The Department does not commission Health Service places in practices, most of which offer a mixture of Health Service and private dentistry.Between 2014 and 2021 the percentage of the population registered with a dentist remained stable at around 64%. This increased to 70% by 2023, however 2024 saw a decrease to 68%. Despite registration rates remaining relatively high with over two-thirds of the Northern Ireland population registered with a practising health service dentist, I am aware that some practices are reducing their level of Health Service commitment.To further support practices and patients, my Department has introduced measures to ensure that patients in need can receive treatment, while options for longer term reform are developed.In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years. Practices in Fermanagh and Omagh will benefit from this additional investment.In March 2024, the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Again, practices in Fermanagh and Omagh will benefit from all three of these new funding streams.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the General Dental Services Statistics 2023/24, what action he is taking to encourage clinical examinations to get back to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: Routine clinical examinations are an important part of managing oral health.  Traditionally, patients have been encouraged to attend at regular 6-month intervals, irrespective of their risk of developing dental disease. There is, however, a weak evidence-base underpinning this recommendation. Current guidelines from the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) are that the recommended interval between oral health reviews should be determined specifically for each patient and tailored to meet their needs. This has been the direction of travel for some time in England and Wales, alongside a move toward prevention-based dentistry. The same goals and guidelines apply in Northern Ireland, and this thinking will form a part of the basis in planning for future reform of the General Dental Services contract.The focus of my Department recently has been on improving access to General Dental Service in general, and particularly for high-need patients, and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices while reform is progressed. This has resulted in the £9.2 million additional investment for 2024/25, which was announced on 27 March 2024.

answer_text: General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and as such are not required to register new patients. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to address the shortage of (i) Concerta XL; and (ii) the alternative Delmosart for patients with ADHD.
answer_text: While supply disruptions of some methylphenidate products used for the treatment of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) have now been resolved, there continues to be ongoing disruptions across the UK affecting various preparations and strengths of these medicines due to global manufacturing issues and an increase in demand for these products at national and global level.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK, working closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department, and other operators in the supply chain. In line with actions agreed at UK wide medicines shortages management groups, my Department has taken steps to help mitigate the effects the supply disruptions of ADHD medicines, including methylphenidate products such as Concerta XL and Delmosart tablets, have been having on patients in Northern Ireland.My Department has issued guidance, including the publication of a National Patient Safety Alert on 28 September 2023, to healthcare professionals providing them with advice and actions to take if patients are unable to access their prescribed ADHD medicines. Additionally, a list of currently available and unavailable ADHD products has been published to assist healthcare professionals prescribing these medications on the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service’s (SPS) website and healthcare professionals can also register to access the SPS medicines supply tool for information on shortages and anticipated resupply dates of affected products. These resources are updated regularly by DHSC, and my Department has encouraged healthcare professionals to use these resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on discussions with the Earl of Shaftesbury in relation to the ownership of Lough Neagh.
answer_text: I met the Earl of Shaftesbury on 21 February 2024 when we discussed a range of issues in connection with Lough Neagh, including ownership.  I am currently seeking a further meeting with the Earl to discuss his recent comments on the potential transfer of ownership of Lough Neagh to a charity or community ownership model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to introduce traffic calming measures on Bellspark Road, West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department has received a request to consider the introduction of traffic calming in The Glebe area of the B165 Bellspark Road.An assessment will be carried out in the coming months to determine the need for traffic calming at this location and if so, to determine the relative priority of this work compared to other traffic calming requests in the council area.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 10936/22-27, for an update on the Education Authority Pay and Grading Review.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that following the successful conclusion of negotiations, a proposed agreement has been developed with the support staff unions.  GMB, NIPSA, Unite the Union and UNISON have agreed to consult their membership on the terms of the proposed agreement in the coming weeks.Full details of the proposed agreement can be found at Pay related issues | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)  The Joint Negotiating Committee (JNC) support staff unions are conducting individual consultations using their own processes however they have suggested we may receive a collective response within two to three weeks.  Subject to a positive collective response, and allocation of funding by the Executive from June monitoring, stage 1 of the proposed agreement should be paid by late autumn 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 13296/22-27, for a breakdown of where within his Department these 695 job reductions will occur.
answer_text: The extremely challenging 2024-25 Resource position resulted in my Department facing a £115.8m shortfall against its 2024-25 Resource requirements.  To protect core public service delivery and live within Budget, my Department’s vacancy control measures will reflect 695 full time equivalent (FTE) vacancies not being filled in 2024-25.  As at 1 April 2024, 665 FTE posts were already vacant, with vacancies predominantly related to social security benefit and pensions delivery.  It is expected a further 30 FTE reduction in Baseline headcount across my Department can be delivered through natural attrition to 31 March 2025
