question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer 12477/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education for an update on discussions between his Department and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools in relation to bringing forward a Development Proposal to increase the admission and enrolment numbers at Dean Maguirc College, Carrickmore
answer_text: CCMS, as Planning and Managing Authority for Catholic Maintained Schools, has indicated that it will seek to increase the admissions and enrolment numbers at Dean Maguirc College, via the Development Proposal (DP) process under Article 14 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.  As a statutory process, CCMS will work with the school in this regard.  There have been no further discussions between my Department and CCMS in relation to a potential proposal and I should point out that the Department’s role in the DP process commences when a proposal is published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to publish an annual fuel poverty report.
answer_text: My officials have concluded a series of pre-consultation stakeholder engagement events to inform the development of the Fuel Poverty Strategy with a public consultation planned in the Autumn.  Due to its early stage of development, it has not yet been determined whether there will be an annual fuel poverty report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to have a statutory fuel poverty target.
answer_text: My officials have concluded a series of pre-consultation stakeholder engagement events to inform the development of the Fuel Poverty Strategy with a public consultation planned in the Autumn.  Due to its early stage of development, it has not yet been determined whether a statutory fuel poverty target will be established.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Strabane Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: I am currently considering this project for approval alongside a number of other capital priorities across my Department.  The member will of course understand the wider context, in terms of a difficult capital budget settlement set against a number of competing priorities for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to introduce a common electrical safety standard check to be carried out in all rented homes.
answer_text: My officials are progressing work to develop regulations to require private sector landlords to undertake five-yearly electrical safety checks of the hardwired electrical installation in their rental properties. Subject to Committee clearance and the agreement of the Assembly, I anticipate these regulations will be brought in by the end of 2024 and will include operational lead in periods to allow landlords sufficient time to become compliant.   		In respect of the social rented sector, it is anticipated that the ongoing review of the Decent Homes Standard will consider whether existing electrical safety measures, as laid out in the Housing Association Guide, should be incorporated into the revised standard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to at least equal the reductions in England, Wales and Scotland.
answer_text: Significant work has gone into drafting Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan, which will set out proposals and policies for meeting the first carbon budget and pathway to meet the 2030, 2040 and 2050 emissions reduction targets.My Department leads a cross-departmental Strategic Oversight Group, Sector Leads Group and Evidence and Analysis Group to support the development of the Climate Action Plan and Northern Ireland’s pathway towards its Net Zero target. This collaborative process is designed to deliver a Climate Action Plan that is transformational, evidence-based and informed by stakeholders.Science and innovation plays a key role in informing, monitoring and evaluating policies and strategies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and providing new innovations to address the challenges of climate change. My Department is investing heavily in climate-related science, including for example, recent investments with Science Foundation Ireland and UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) in a new Climate Plus Co-Centre and with UKRI in a significant large Land Use for Net Zero research programme.For the sectors which my Department has responsibility, there are a number of key interventions being developed and/or taken forward, including:In relation to Agriculture, the new Farm Support and Development Programme, the objective of which is to transition to a more sustainable farming sector by seeking to implement policies and strategies that benefit our climate and environment, while supporting our economically and socially significant agriculture sector.In relation to Land Use, Land Use Change and Forestry, my Department is working on developing and delivering policies relating to afforestation, peatland restoration, and land management schemes to reduce emissions and increase carbon sequestration.For Waste management, my Department has already significantly reduced emissions in the sector by 62% since 1990 through a range of initiatives such as separate collection of food waste and increased recycling. I aim to build upon this work by developing measures to divert even more biodegradable waste from landfill and to deliver further increases in both household and business recycling.For the Fisheries sector, my Department is investing in research and development to decarbonise the fishing fleet. The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 requires policies and proposals to be delivered in the fisheries sector to help achieve targets and carbon budgets but this is not specifically required by equivalent legislation in England, Scotland and Wales.  DAERA and the other UK fisheries policy authorities have agreed through the UK Joint Fisheries Statement to work together to support innovative solutions to reduce emissions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to promote early detection and diagnosis of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care Trusts, along with SPPG, will continue to work on finalising the development of a long-term solution in respect of adult neuromuscular services, taking account of operational considerations. This will include information on the provision of Risdiplam for patients. Risdiplam is provided through Belfast Trust at present. This is owing to previous arrangement regarding service delivery with an agreed position whereby Risdiplam prescriptions are covered until 31 March 2025 in the context of service handover.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to relieve continuing pressure on small business owners.
answer_text: I set out my vision for the economy in the Assembly back in February, providing direction for collaborative efforts focused on improving our economic prospects.  My vision aims to create more good jobs in the north while moving us towards meeting our Net Zero goals by boosting productivity, supporting local innovation, and aligning our skills system with employer needs.My Department and Invest NI provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.   Invest NI also has a portfolio of grants, loans and investment funds, many of which are specifically targeted at early stage and small businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the main changes contained in the latest Teacher Education Partnership Handbook.
answer_text: The updated Teacher Education Partnership Handbook, published on 13 June 2024, provides an overview of the key stages of professional learning for student teachers and newly qualified teachers, as well as university tutors, teacher tutors and all those involved in the early years of teacher professional learning.New additions in the handbook (which do not alter the basic nature of the advice provided in the previous version) include a sample teacher tutor calendar, a comprehensive summary of the key stages of professional learning for student teachers and early career teachers and a list of valuable publications and websites. It was also produced with updated terminology and design enhancements for easy navigation.The Handbook has been revised following extensive collaboration and input from our education partners. It also demonstrates my ongoing commitment to supporting our teachers and their professional learning – as outlined in the ‘Learning Leaders’ Strategy – and will contribute to delivering a highly skilled, motivated, and high-functioning education workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of why Northern Ireland has performed less well than England, Wales and Scotland in reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
answer_text: Northern Ireland has a very different sectoral emissions profile to that of the rest of the UK, with each sector requiring a range of bespoke interventions to reduce their emissions profile further. 		For example, Northern Ireland has a higher percentage of emissions from agriculture accounting for 29.1% of its emissions, compared to a total of 11.7% in the UK. As most of agricultural emissions are methane which are relatively more challenging to reduce, this will have a knock-on impact on our overall total.	 	Related to this are the proportionately high emissions associated with the Land Use, Land-Use Change, and Forestry (LULUCF) sector which when combined with agriculture accounts for 39.2% of Northern Ireland’s emissions. The equivalent proportion for agriculture and LULUCF as a share of total UK emissions is 11.9%. That is why it is so important that my Department continues its work to reduce emissions in these sectors through our Farm Support and Development Programme and our plans for woodland creation and peatland restoration.		Sectors such as Buildings and Product Uses, Industry, and Waste, which make up 28.6% of our emissions profile, show a broadly similar downward trajectory as the UK.  In addition, as we further adopt less intensive forms of fuel combustion and uptake of renewable energy our emissions profile will diminish further.	  	My Department continues to make advances in drafting a Climate Action Plan, with high level cross-departmental engagement and co-operation to quantify and identify policies and proposals for a pathway to meet net zero by 2050.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the high-level actions outlined in the Emotional Health and Well-Being Framework for children and young people in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan, point 2.a.
answer_text: The Department of Education has continued to progress the implementation plan associated with the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework launched in February 2021. An update on the projects is provided below.  The Text-a-Nurse service for post primary age pupils commenced in 2020/2021 and was extended to parents, carers and school staff in 2022/2023.  438 young people were supported through this service in 2022/23. The expansion of existing Regional Integrated Support for Education (RISE) to Key Stage 2 (primary – P5-P7) supported small group and whole class support to 223 schools in 2022/23.The Resilience in Education, Assisting Change to Happen (REACH) programme for children and young people aged 6-19 commenced in 2021/2022.  In 2022/23 10,780 pupils took part in the Programme, with 203 schools engaged.The School Nursing pilot provides a school nurse in five post primary schools across the Health and Social Care Trust areas. commenced in 2021/2022.  The service has also expanded to provide support to feeder primary schools.The Attach Programme (TAP) for Children Looked After operated in 340 primary schools in 2022/23.The Being Well Doing Well Project commenced in the 2023/24 academic year.  132 schools have benefited from this whole school programme in its first year of operation.The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools has operated in 46 post primary schools in the 2023/24 academic year.The Wellbeing Hub project is complete with resources available in a central hub on the CCEA website. The Optimising Achievement staff wellbeing project was not funded beyond March 2023 due to financial pressures.The Healthy Happy Minds pilot primary school counselling service, began in 2021/22 with the availability of Covid-19 funding and ceased on 31 March 2023 due to financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on proposals to fund 15 additional Nurture Groups per year to prevent further impacts on children’s emotional and mental health and support them to engage fully in learning, as recommended in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan, point 2.i.
answer_text: All 16 of the Nurture Groups established in 2022 under the Fair Start Action Plan are delivering Nurture with the support of funding from the Department of Education.   Due to funding pressures, the Department was unable to fund any further new Nurture Groups from the Fair Start budget allocation in either 2022/23 or 2023/24.At the time of writing, the Department’s funding allocation for Nurture provision for 2024/25 is still to be confirmed.In 2023/24, the Department invested £4.52 million to support Nurture provision across Northern Ireland. This funding supports two key elements: 62 established Nurture Groups in Primary Schools, with funding of £62,00 per Nurture Group; and the Nurture Advisory and Support Service in the Education Authority.Officials are undertaking work to develop options for the Department’s future, longer-term approach to supporting Nurture provision, with a view to establishing a sustainable model that will ensure we are effectively targeting and supporting those children who are most in need of this type of intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the proposed Construction and Procurement Delivery framework, as recommended in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan.
answer_text: In 2023, RSM UK was commissioned to carry out a detailed scoping study of all funded pre-school education settings, which included an analysis of the training and qualification requirements and support currently available. A copy of the final report was published on 13 June 2024 and is available at:- Final Scoping Study Report | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)My officials will consider the findings of the report in the context of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy in conjunction with colleagues in the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of diagnostic assessment at Key Stage 1 to Key Stage 3, in order to save the education system significant budget each year, as recommended in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan, point 3.ii.
answer_text: My Department has commissioned the Council for the Curriculum Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) to develop diagnostic assessment tools for use in all schools to address Key Action 3.ii of the Fair Start  Final Report and Action Plan.   The Computer Adaptive Testing (CAT) pilot began in 2017/18, initially to develop a prototype for numeracy assessment and was subsequently widened to include literacy and numeracy.In 2023/24 over 38,000 pupil assessments were completed by 220 primary, 77 post-primary and 9 special schools engaged in the pilot. A pilot Irish medium numeracy assessment was completed in February 2024 by 9 schools. Funding has been provided to CCEA to continue the pilot until March 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what progress her Department has made in fundamentally considering assessment at Key Stage 4, as recommended in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan, point 3.iii.
answer_text: Key action 3(iii) of the final report of ‘A Fair Start’ is a medium to long term action that could only be progressed once more normal awarding arrangements had been established post-COVID.  As the exam system is on track to complete the journey back to more normal arrangements this summer, my officials are considering how best to address this particular action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on working with the Department of Health to lead a process of co-design with key partners to develop an adequately resourced and supported training, qualifications and CPD strategy for the Early Years workforce, as recommended in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan, point 1.v.
answer_text: In 2023, RSM UK was commissioned to carry out a detailed scoping study of all funded pre-school education settings, which included an analysis of the training and qualification requirements and support currently available. A copy of the final report was published on 13 June 2024 and is available at:- Final Scoping Study Report | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)My officials will consider the findings of the report in the context of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy in conjunction with colleagues in the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on proposals to make speech and language development an explicit part of the ante-natal and 0-3 space, as recommended in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan, point 1.e.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) has policy responsibility for the Universal Child Health Promotion Programme (CHPP) contacts, for all children in their early years. A key element of this programme is the promotion of early speech and language which is an integral component of Midwifery and Health Visiting education with parents/carers.  This is delivered at key stages as set out in the DoH Healthy Child Healthy Future, Child Health Promotion programme.A refresh of Healthy Child Healthy Future is nearing completion.  This will include a greater emphasis on the first 1000 days, with an even stronger focus on the interaction between mother and baby through talking, songs, music and books.Speech and language development is a central component of the Department of Education’s Sure Start programme which supports children aged 0 to 3 years in disadvantaged areas.  DE requires all 38 Sure Start projects to provide speech and language support as a core element of its service delivery.  Working collaboratively with health professionals, Sure Start complements Speech, Language and Communication services provided by the Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on reviewing staff to child ratios across all pre-school education settings, as recommended in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan, point 1.x.
answer_text: My statement of 23 May on Early Learning and Childcare measures provided details of the package of measures I plan to implement in 2024/25 including the standardisation of pre-school session lengths. I published the final report of the Standardisation of the Pre-School Education Programme Scoping Study and wrote to all MLAs on 13 June about its contents.  All three documents are available on the Department of Education website.In addition to the core objective of providing all children with 22.5 hours per week of pre-school education, the Scoping Study report identifies and costs a range of potential additional actions including changes to pre-school staffing ratios. The scale of what I can do in implementing options contained in the report will be determined by the budget allocated by the Executive. £25m has been earmarked for an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy in the current financial year, with £5m of that assigned for pre-school standardisation. This will allow me to begin the process of transition. I intend to have an additional 2,200 22.5-hour places available for September 2025.   It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to a 22.5-hour weekly session length.  I may widen the parameters of the project should additional resources become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress of standardising the length of a pre-school education day to at least 4.5 hours per day, including access to free school meals for eligible children, as recommended in A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan, point 1.iv.
answer_text: My statement of 23 May on Early Learning and Childcare measures provided details of the package of measures I plan to implement in 2024/25 including the standardisation of pre-school session lengths. I published the final report of the Standardisation of the Pre-School Education Programme Scoping Study and wrote to all MLAs on 13 June about its contents.  All three documents are available on the Department of Education website.In addition to the core objective of providing all children with 22.5 hours per week of pre-school education, the Scoping Study report identifies and costs a range of potential additional actions including changes to pre-school staffing ratios. The scale of what I can do in implementing options contained in the report will be determined by the budget allocated by the Executive. £25m has been earmarked for an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy in the current financial year, with £5m of that assigned for pre-school standardisation. This will allow me to begin the process of transition. I intend to have an additional 2,200 22.5-hour places available for September 2025.   It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to a 22.5-hour weekly session length.  I may widen the parameters of the project should additional resources become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is supporting rural transport initiatives in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department subsidises Translink for the provision of the public transport network and also supports all capital investment across the network.  Ulsterbus provides the majority of the rural network, as well as inter-urban services and rural and smaller town services and it is clear that improved public transport helps connectivity across all of the North.  My Department also provides funding to Easilink Community transport to support the delivery of rural Dial-a-Lift Scheme transport services across the West Tyrone region.  The Scheme improves access to goods and services, keeping people connected to their local communities and often is the only travel option for those who are rurally isolated.  I have made the decision to provide funding to community transport for 2024/25 at the total levels awarded for 2023/24.  This is subject to the planned Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the budget and so this allocation is indicative at this stage.  I do, however, expect to be able to confirm opening budget allocations, once I have taken final decisions following consideration of responses to the Equality Impact Assessment after an initial four-week period.  My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's plans to ease traffic congestion in Omagh town.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the traffic congestion which occurs in Omagh town and particularly on the A5 Great Northern Road during peak periods. In this regard I recognise that the planned A5 Western Transport Corridor dual carriageway will remove strategic traffic from the Great Northern Road and greatly improve traffic progression around Omagh.  In the interim, my officials continue to identify improvements to the road network around Omagh in an effort to improve traffic progression and reduce congestion.Work has recently started on a road widening scheme at the A5 Great Northern Road/ Railway Terrace Junction.  This scheme will provide an extension to the left turn lane from the A5 Great Northern Road onto Railway Link Road coming from the Strabane direction and should improve traffic progression and increase the capacity through the junction.  Revisions to the traffic light sequencing at this junction are also planned to improve progression of traffic travelling straight ahead towards Ballygawley.There are also proposals to extend the left turn lane on the A32 Dromore Road for vehicles wishing to turn onto Railway Terrace and A5. This should improve traffic progression on the A32 Dromore Road and improve access through the junction for vehicles travelling into town. These schemes will be completed in conjunction with a proposed resurfacing scheme on the A5 Great Northern Road and the A32 Dromore Road later this year, subject to funding.Officials are also continuing to work with our signals contractor to investigate potential design changes at other junctions in Omagh.In addition to improving the road network for vehicles, it is important to explore other modes of transport to reduce congestion on our roads and to grasp opportunities to improve facilities for walking, cycling and public transport. As part of last year’s active travel programme, further shared use cycleways/footways were completed on Dublin Road, Cookstown Road and on the Glencam Road in conjunction with a road widening and realignment scheme. Further Active Travel works are planned on A505 Cookstown Road, Farmhill Road and Gortin Road in conjunction with a road widening scheme as part of the proposed Strule Campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any plans to recruit civil servants in West Tyrone.
answer_text: Details of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a department identifies a vacancy it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. As at the end of May 2024, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 3271 vacancies across the Civil Service. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on all capital projects in West Tyrone.
answer_text: I can advise that officials are currently finalising work on the full business case for the Strule Shared Education Campus project.  The current programme estimates contract award in Summer 2024, subject to all necessary approvals being in place.  Assuming successful appointment of a main works contractor and following the pre-construction design period, construction is expected to commence on site in late 2024 with completion currently estimated as early 2028.A new build school for Gaelscoil Uí Dhochartaigh completed in June 2024 under my Major Works Programme.  St Catherine’s Primary School, Strabane, is in the concept design stage (RIBA Stage 2) under the same programme and Drumragh Integrated College, is in the technical design and pre-tender stage (RIBA Stage 4).  Dean Maguirc College, Carrickmore, was paused in 2023 and remains so. Under my School Enhancement Programme, St Mary’s Primary School, Cloughcor, is at developed design stage (RIBA Stage 3) and Strabane Primary School is at business case preparation stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the level of community transport funding per head of the rural population in each of the last five years, broken down by Rural Community Transport Partnership area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the staffing level reductions contained in his budget for 2024-25.
answer_text: In light of the extremely challenging 2024-25 Resource position which has resulted in a £115.8m shortfall against the 2024-25 requirements, and to protect core public service delivery, my Department will not fill a further 95 FTE posts in 2024-25, this is in addition to 600 FTE posts not filled in 2023-24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Organised Crime Task Force Annual Report and Threat Assessment 2022/23.
answer_text: The Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) was established in 2000 and works to reduce the harm caused by organised crime and secure a safe community in Northern Ireland, where we respect the law and each other.  The Task Force brings together police, customs and other law enforcement agencies, along with Government Departments, academia and the business community to combat organised crime.The 2022/23 Annual Report highlights the efforts and successes of OCTF partners who work together to tackle organised crime in Northern Ireland.  They continue to embrace new technologies and adapt to existing, as well as emerging, threats.  I continue to be impressed and encouraged by their dedication, commitment and persistence in thwarting criminals who set out to exploit, threaten and spread misery and fear across communities and particularly those most vulnerable.   Their work is essential in protecting individuals, communities and businesses from organised crime.  The 2022/23 annual report highlights the dangers of organised crime by bringing issues such as drug trafficking, modern slavery/human trafficking, cyber-crime, counterfeiting, fuel laundering, waste crime, tobacco and alcohol smuggling, fraud, money laundering and acquisitive crime to the fore.Beyond this it is also an opportunity to highlight successes that have been achieved by operational partners, with around £4.8 million of cash and assets seized from criminals during the reporting period.  During 2022/23 police seized drugs with an estimated street value of over £9 million and Border Force was involved in seizures of Class B drugs with an estimated street value of £2.4 million.  In addition to disruption activities, operational partners ongoing recovery of criminal assets has enabled the allocation of funding through the Assets Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS), which supports initiatives aimed at preventing crime and reducing the fear of crime across local communities.All of this sends a clear message to both criminals and the public that we will continue to do everything within our power to disrupt these gangs and seize proceeds of crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what further enforcement action the Northern Ireland Environment Agency is taking regarding illegal dumping in response to the Northern Ireland Environmental Statistics Report 2024.
answer_text: I am committed to tackling the illegal dumping of waste including issues highlighted in the Northern Ireland Environmental Statistics Report 2024.Enforcement to tackle illegal waste disposal is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Environment Agency’s Environmental Crime Unit (ECU), which is focused on investigating and pursuing offenders.  The ECU focuses its resources on those cases which present the most significant risk to the environment and public health and utilises powers contained within the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to pursue prosecutions. Such prosecutions are often coupled with confiscation proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 aimed at depriving those convicted of the proceeds generated from their crimes whilst asserting the ‘Polluter Pays Principle’.The ECU continues to adopt a multi-agency approach through operational cooperation with other government agencies.  The Unit is  an active member of the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) and Joint Unit for Waste Crime (JUWC). Both groups bring together law enforcement agencies, environmental regulators, HMRC and the National Crime Agency to tackle environmental criminality including illegal dumping. Additionally,  ECU also works in close partnership with district councils to address fly tipping of waste through an agreed working protocol  with the majority of district councils. 	As part of its response to illegal waste dumping the ECU will seek remedial action initially in consultation with the landowner or person in control of the land and, where applicable, through service of a notice under Article 27 the 1997 Act. 		A failure to adhere to this remediation notice is a criminal offence under Article 27 (5) of this Act and may result, depending on the circumstances, in a criminal prosecution.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what further action his Department is taking regarding climate change in response to the Northern Ireland Environmental Statistics Report 2024.
answer_text: On behalf of the Executive my Department is leading on the development of the draft Climate Action Plan which will set out Northern Ireland’s collective approach to delivering the emissions reductions required to achieve our first carbon budget. All departments have a legal duty to deliver on the targets set in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. The Northern Ireland Environmental Statistics Report 2024 outlined that Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions in 2021 were estimated to be 22.5 Megatonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent, a 23% reduction from 1990 levels. Updated data to include the 2022 year was published on 18 June 2024. Information on GHG levels informs the development of policies and proposals by all Departments to meet emission targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the staff vacancy control measures contained in his budget.
answer_text: The extremely challenging 2024-25 Resource position resulted in my Department facing a £115.8m shortfall against its 2024-25 Resource requirements.  To protect core public service delivery and live within Budget, my Department’s vacancy control measures will reflect 695 full time equivalent (FTE) vacancies, predominantly related to social security benefit and pensions delivery, not being filled in 2024-25.  This position reflects a 16% increase on the 600 FTE critical vacancies not filled in 2023-24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Environmental Statistics Report 2024.
answer_text: I welcome the Northern Ireland Environmental Statistics Report 2024 and I am currently considering the contents as part of the wider environmental policy agenda.  I continue to seek the Executive’s approval of the Environmental Improvement Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what further financial support his Department can provide carers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Social security policy and legislation in Northern Ireland, including the main benefit rates, is generally maintained in parity with that in place under the Department for Work and Pensions in Great Britain.Carer’s Allowance is the main benefit for unpaid carers in the social security system.  The weekly rate of Carer’s Allowance increased by 6.7% to £81.90 from April 2024.  Depending on individual circumstances carers on a low income may be able to access additional financial support through income-related benefits, such as Universal Credit, and their associated carer premiums/elements.  The main rates in these benefits also increased by 6.7% from April 2024.In the annual benefits up-rating exercise the Department has no power to set different rates of benefit from those set by the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions in Great Britain.Recommendations relating to additional support for carers were made in the Independent Advisory Panel’s Report into the future of welfare mitigations that was published in October 2022. Any future mitigation package will need to be agreed by the Executive and assessed in the climate of financial constraint with the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years. In addition, the Department’s Make the Call service can carry out an entitlement check to ensure a person is accessing all the benefits, supports and services available to them. It can be contacted via Freephone number 0800 232 1271 or by emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to introduce legislation in this mandate to protect shop workers at their place of work.
answer_text: Assault of a retail worker can be prosecuted under existing laws designed to protect any member of the public from harm. In light of increasing concerns about assaults on a range of workers who serve the public, I intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a person engaged in providing a public service, performing a public duty, or providing services to the public.  The new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty in the magistrates’ courts than that currently available for the offence of common assault which applies regardless of the victim’s occupation. This would include retail workers.Alongside this new offence I intend to make assault on such a person in more serious cases a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect that fact in their sentencing decisions.I intend to bring this new offence forward via my proposed Sentencing Bill before the end of this mandate.Following devolution of justice matters in 2010, authority for the development and introduction of criminal and civil offences and penalties was devolved to each Northern Ireland Assembly Department. It falls to the Department owning the overarching policy area to consider whether there are gaps in the law or amendments required to existing legislation and, where that is the case, responsibility for any subsequent legislative changes remains the responsibility of that Department. It would, therefore, be open to the Department of Economy to consider whether any specific provision is required in respect of the retail sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has met with the Union of Shop, Distributive and Allied Workers to discuss legislation to protect shop workers at their place of work.
answer_text: I have not met with, nor been invited to speak directly with the Union of Shop, Distributive and Allied Workers (USDAW) to discuss legislation to protect shop workers.  However, in recent months I have engaged with a number of individuals and organisations representing the retail sector who have expressed concerns about attacks on these workers in their place of work.  I also attended the launch of the Retail NI Crime Survey Report 2024 where I engaged with a number of retailers and retailer organisations. I am, therefore, cognisant of the views from the retail sector and their desire for legislative change.  As outlined in my answer to AQW 13110/22-27, I plan to legislate to increase protections for all those who provide a public service, perform a public duty or provide services to the public.I have been appalled by the levels of aggression being reported against workers who engage face-to-face with the public, and am fully committed to working with all partners, including businesses, the community and voluntary sector, PSNI, as well as other Departments and organisations, to ensure that we have a community where we all feel safe to live and work.As part of this commitment, my Department is a member of the Business Crime Partnership, chaired by the PSNI, which brings together representatives from across the retail sector. This valuable partnership is a key mechanism for sharing information and taking a partnership approach to addressing the issues that impact many businesses.Additionally, my Department, alongside the Northern Ireland Policing Board, currently provides approximately £4.5 million in funding annually to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) to support a range of community safety initiatives.  Each PCSP addresses the issue of business crime within their Action Plans and PCSPs raise awareness of business crime via sharing and amplifying partner’s messages and activities in this space.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12543/22-27, for his assessment of the Consumer Council’s statement on 23 May 2024 that the increase in fares were a blow for public transport users.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. While public fares were frozen between 2019 and 2023, Translink continued to deliver services within a constrained budget and an increasing cost base. Consequently, an increase in fares has been required to ensure the future delivery of services. The Concessionary Fares Scheme remains available to provide free and discounted fare travel to some of the most vulnerable in our society.  The value of our public transport services cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12543/22-27, and given budget restraints, why he had previously frozen fares on public transport.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. While public fares were frozen between 2019 and 2023, Translink continued to deliver services within a constrained budget and an increasing cost base. Consequently, an increase in fares has been required to ensure the future delivery of services. The Concessionary Fares Scheme remains available to provide free and discounted fare travel to some of the most vulnerable in our society.  The value of our public transport services cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) number; and (ii) nature of complaints his Department has received in relation to the process used for allocating thatching contracts in the last three years.
answer_text: My department has recorded five official complaints and other correspondence from a single person (and NIPSO responding to this) in respect of this general issue in the last three years. You should note that the Department does not allocate thatching contracts. Based upon applications made to the Historic Environment Fund, it issues letters of offer to owners to support repair work to listed buildings.The complaints have raised issues around: thatching industry standards; work at individual listed buildings; ridge work; listed building consent; use of water reed; the departments’ thatch condition survey; depth specification for work; historic discussions on this; alleged competitive disadvantage; details provided in the department’s FOI responses.

answer_text: The additional 40 social work training places referred to in AQW 12225/22-27 will be allocated as follows; Queens University Belfast : 15 places Ulster University                : 15 places  Open University (NI)         : 10 places

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work his Department is carrying out to support Cystic Fibrosis Awareness Week.
answer_text: Cystic Fibrosis (CF) is an inherited disease affecting, and in due course, damaging the lungs and digestive tract.  Belfast Trust is the regional centre for CF patients with support provided by dedicated multidisciplinary teams including medical, nursing, AHP, pharmacy and social care input.  There are a range of treatments which help control symptoms and prevent or reduce complications.My Department is aware of Cystic Fibrosis Awareness Week, organised by CF Trust, and which is running this year from 17-23rd June, and welcomes the increase in focus on CF within the Northern Ireland community. My Department also acknowledges the work of charitable groups and their contributions to service improvements and welfare of patients, and recognises and values the work of the individuals and groups that participate in fundraising events. In terms of specific actions for Cystic Fibrosis Awareness Week, Belfast Trust as the regional provider of CF Services are to place ‘Raising Awareness’ graphics for Instagram and Facebook on their social media platforms. Additionally. Belfast Trust have developed a promotional video including members of the CF adult and Children’s team, to highlight and to advise of multiple multi-professional innovations occurring within the Belfast CF service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients in the Western Health and Social Care Trust receiving care for cystic fibrosis in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Belfast HSC Trust is central to providing care to those with cystic fibrosis, and provided the following figures on request:DateNo. of Patients09/07/202031628/07/202132530/06/202233213/11/202333029/04/2024331

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will increase the number of funded nursery places in West Tyrone for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group is responsible for managing the Pre-School Education Programme and is tasked with ensuring there is sufficient pre-school provision in all areas to meet the commitment to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target age child whose parents want it. Stage One of the pre-school admissions process concluded on 25 April with 98.33% of children offered a funded pre-school place in a setting of their parents’ preference. Stage Two of the admissions process is currently underway. The EA has confirmed that sufficient funded pre-school places remain in all areas with 141 funded places still available in the West Tyrone parliamentary constituency area at close of Stage One to accommodate 18 children who submitted preferences at Stage One of the admissions process but were not offered a place in a setting of their parents’ preference at that time.  Parents of the children who did not receive an offer of a funded pre-school place at close of Stage One were invited to submit further preferences during Stage Two of the process.  Those parents that remain in the process and engaged in Stage Two will receive notification of the outcome of their applications at the conclusion of Stage Two on 11 June.The EA has advised therefore there are no plans to increase the levels of pre-school provision in the area at this time. The EA PEG will, however, continue to monitor provision in all areas.

I have reissued this updated response as the table has been updated to include two fines in 2017 that were not included in the original reply. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the areas in the West Tyrone constituency that are scheduled to benefit from the LED street lighting replacement scheme in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has commissioned the LED retrofit of over 600 street lighting units across multiple sites within Omagh, Newtownstewart, Erganagh, Castlederg, Killen, Killeter and Strabane which will be completed within the 2024-25 financial year.Future programmes of work to be taken forward by my Department, including planned LED work, will be listed within the annual report presented to local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 10212/22-27, what action his Department is taking to encourage bids from Fermanagh and Omagh District Council.
answer_text: Officials have been working closely with councils over the past year, including Fermanagh and Omagh District Council. The engagement with councils allows officials to better understand their 5-year greenway programme and is helping to develop an appropriate grant and support framework for the effective delivery of greenways going forward. Fermanagh and Omagh District Council have identified a number of potential schemes including the development of the Belcoo to Enniskillen greenway. They have also completed early-stage feasibility works on the Omagh to Ulster American Folk Park & Omagh to Gortin Greenway; Belleek to Castle Caldwell Greenway; and Enniskillen to Clones Greenway.When the Council plans are further developed, officials from my department will be happy to engage on these proposals. Future delivery will be dependent upon availability of funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of funding allocated from the Assets Recovery Community Scheme in West Tyrone since its introduction in 2012.
answer_text: Since it was first launched in 2012 the Asset Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS) has awarded approximately £9 million to projects aimed at tackling crime and the fear of crime.  It funds valuable initiatives, mainly in community settings, that actively help victims, communities or the environment in support of a culture of lawfulness.  While my Department does not hold a breakdown of funding by constituency, since October 2021 there is information available on funding allocated by council area.  It should be noted that many projects would impact on a Northern Ireland-wide basis, rather than within a single geographical area.Further detail on ARCS awards more generally is available athttps://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/asset-recovery-community-schemes

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of large families on the housing waiting list in the West Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me of the following:“The total number of large family households on the waiting list for the West Tyrone constituency at December 2023 were as follows:Dec 23Large Family HouseholdsParliamentary ConstituencyApplicantsWest Tyrone139The large family classification is defined as 1 or 2 persons aged 16 or over, and 3 or more children aged 0-15, or 3 or more persons 16 or over and 2 or more children aged 0-15.The household group is recorded at the point of application and may not reflect the current composition of the household.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the streets and roads that have received approval and funding from his Department for resurfacing works in the West Tyrone constituency for the 2024-25 financial year; and (ii) when these works are expected to be completed.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been finalised. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of vacant (i) teacher; and (ii) classroom assistant positions in West Tyrone.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) is not the employer of teachers, classroom assistants or any school-based staff. Staff vacancies are not reported to DE. My officials asked the employing authorities and sectoral bodies to provide this information, the responses are as follows:School SectorTeacherVacanciesClassroom AssistantVacanciesVoluntary Grammar Schools34*(EA) Controlled Schools 3471 (includes Controlled and Catholic Maintained Schools)(CCMS) Catholic-maintained Schools7 (permanent)7 (temporary)2  (Part-time)Irish Medium Schools03  *  May include vacancies which were unfilled and re-advertised.The management of staffing is delegated to the Board of Governors of each school and EA supports schools with the delivery of recruitment processes to fill their vacancies.Does not include Grant-Maintained Integrated Schools as vacancy information is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the findings of a BBC Spotlight investigation which reports on environmental breaches at Moy Park.
answer_text: The BBC Spotlight investigation reported on breaches of the emission limits on the discharges to sewer from Moy Park’s processing plants which are regulated by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) under the Pollution Prevention and Control (Industrial Emissions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 (PPC). These PPC permits contain emission limits on discharges to the sewer network. Breaches of the permitted emission limits on discharges to sewer at the Moy Park sites detailed in the programme were categorised as low risk as the discharge is further treated at the wastewater treatment works before final discharge to the watercourse. NIEA officials have been working with Moy Park and NI Water to address these breaches and ensure plans are in place to ensure compliance at both the Dungannon and Craigavon sites where the majority of the breaches have occurred.A number of additional wider actions are also being explored including reviewing working protocols between NIEA and NI Water in terms of enforcement of trade discharge consent breaches and how to monitor and respond to cases of lower risk but cumulative trade discharge consent breaches.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

Parents of the children who did not receive an offer of a funded pre-school place at close of Stage One were invited to submit further preferences during Stage Two of the process. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 11735/22-27, and given the increase in school non-attendance, when the new attendance strategy will be published.
answer_text: My officials are currently reviewing and refreshing the existing attendance strategy in order to take account of challenges facing our children and young people to attend school regularly.It is important that the draft strategy reflects the views of children and young people, and the Education Authority’s Youth Service has agreed to undertake this work in Autumn 2024. The draft strategy will be consulted upon thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the schools in the West Tyrone constituency that still have Year 8 places available for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: In the West Tyrone constituency, Castlederg High School, Holy Cross College, Christian Brothers’ Grammar School, Drumragh Integrated College, Loreto Grammar School, Omagh Academy Grammar School, Omagh High School, Sacred Heart College and St John’s Business and Enterprise College still had Year 8 places available for the 2024-25 academic year as at 3 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time from referral to completion of works for occupational therapy services in West Tyrone in the last three years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information centrally so requested it directly from the Western Health and Social Care Trust.  Please find their response below.This request would require a manual trawl and data collection through hundreds of patient’s paper and electronic records and could not be completed within the timescales or without significant resource.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to assist the establishment of new business ventures in the West Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: Creating a vibrant ecosystem of new business ventures across the North is one of my top priorities and aligns with my objective of promoting a regionally balanced economy.  My Department provides a suite of interventions to support entrepreneurs across the North who are in the early stages of business development. Support available through Invest NI and InterTradeIreland includes financial assistance, advice and guidance, mentoring, signposting to wider assistance and support for business plan development. Invest NI also provides funding to several partner organisations that deliver a range of debt and equity funds for high-potential start-ups. Following Local Government reform in 2015, responsibility for local business start-ups transferred to the 11 Councils.  In November 2023, the Councils launched Go Succeed, a service available across the North which offers advice and support to entrepreneurs at all stages of business development. 	   Invest NI provides a comprehensive guide to business support on www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk. Two sections of the website are especially useful for start-up businesses - My New Business and Starting a Business.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of revenue that has been lost through errors made by his Department when recovery is not sought in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The table below shows the value of official error benefit overpayments where recovery was not sought by the Department, for each of the last three years. YearValue £2021/226,694,6352022/236,461,6162023/245,745,846

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why fully-subscribed preschool settings are not being provided with additional places, resulting in parents being referred to preschool settings further away from their home.
answer_text: The number of funded pre-school education places allocated to individual providers is based on the assessed level of need in the area and all non-statutory settings are aware that they are not guaranteed a minimum, or any, allocation of funded places from one year to the next. The pre-school admissions process is also preference based, however, it is not possible to guarantee every child a place in their first preference setting. Permitting settings to increase their pupil allocation number (PAN) to enable them to admit all first preference applicants could have a negative impact on other settings in the same area, potentially undermining the sustainability of these settings, which in turn would have a knock-on effect on the overall pre-school education programme. It is essential therefore that demand and supply of provision is as closely matched as possible, while also taking into account parental preference and the range of relevant statutory duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the actions his Department is taking to support festivals.
answer_text: Tourism NI is responsible for the administration of tourism events funding within my Department.Events funding from Tourism NI operates on an annual open call basis. The key objectives of Tourism NI’s Tourism Events Funding Programme (TEFP) are to:Enhance the visitor experience.Increase visitor numbers and spend.Develop a positive international profile.Generate increased bednights.Elongate the tourism season.Tourism NI is not a core funder of events, therefore applications for financial assistance must evidence how the funding will be utilised to generate additional tourism benefits and maximise on the key objectives of the TEFP. Since 2019, Tourism NI has provided over £1.6m of financial assistance to festivals that contain elements of musical programming, for example the Cathedral Quarter Arts Festival. Tourism NI has also supported funded event and festival organisers with skills development and capacity building through one-to-one mentoring, and masterclasses on topics such as delivering environmentally sustainable events and developing commercial opportunities.As music festivals are largely privately held and funded events there is no single source of data on their economic impact in the North over the past five years. Music festivals are however considerable economic drivers, providing a much-needed boost for our local tourism and hospitality industry and employ thousands of people each year. Recent examples of this are last year's Belsonic festival in Ormeau Park and Emerge Music Festival, held at Boucher playing fields, which between them attracted 200,000 paid attendees, with substantial numbers of customers travelling to the events, and some coming from as far away as Australia, USA and mainland Europe. These two events also generated additional economic activity amounting to £30.8 million and created almost 6,000 paid employment opportunities, according to an independent report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to simplify the rules in relation to the payment of carers allowance.
answer_text: There are no current plans to change the rules in relation to the payment of Carer’s Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when he plans to publish the report following the Public Inquiry on the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the economic benefit of festivals to the economy in each of the last five years.
