answer_text: Due to the volume of information required to answer this question, I have arranged for this information to be provided in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the publication of the Budget 2024-25, for an update on the Strule Shared Educational Campus.
answer_text: The Budget announcement confirmed £20m of capital funding would be available to support the Strule Shared Education Campus in 2024-25.My officials are finalising work on the Full Business Case for the Programme and a potential contractor is in place. On the current programme timeline, subject to all necessary approvals being in place, contract award is planned for Summer 2024.  Assuming successful appointment of a main works contractor and following the pre-construction design period, construction would then begin on site late 2024 with completion currently estimated as late 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to review the Bovine TB Eradication Strategy.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sit within my ministerial responsibilities and are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the publication of the Budget 2024-25, for an update on the Education Authority Pay and Grading Review.
answer_text: The Budget allocations provided for 2024-25 do not include a specific allocation for the Pay and Grading Review for support staff.  However I, together with Executive colleagues, am committed to working collectively to find a resolution for these important workers.The Executive has agreed to seek approval from the HM Treasury to reprofile some of the repurposed funds provided in the Executive restoration financial package for this purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the publication of the Budget 2024-25, for an update on the (i) Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer; and (ii) Casement Park project.
answer_text: The Sub-Regional Programme is a key priority for me and I hope to be in the position to make an announcement shortly. Engagement is ongoing with all funding partners in relation to the redevelopment of Casement Park. The Irish Government has announced its intention to contribute €50 million to the Casement Park project.  I am currently awaiting confirmation from the UK Government on its funding commitment towards the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the publication of the Budget 2024-25, for an update on the Derry City and Strabane City Deal.
answer_text: The Derry and Strabane City Deal will play a key role in supporting regional balance and I want to see delivery of the Deal accelerated.  Officials from my Department are supporting the Council and its partners to progress projects with the aim that the final stage of the Deal will be signed this summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the impact of NI Reval2026 on the commercial property vacancy rates on high streets.
answer_text: It is not possible to assess the impacts of Reval2026 on vacancy rates on high streets, as vacancies are a result of many economic factors. The point of a revaluation is to redistribute the rate burden based on up to date market rents. A revaluation follows the market evidence, so factors that influence rents paid in a locality (such as vacancies) should be evident in the rental information collected.  Land & Property Services (LPS) has just begun gathering Rent & Lease information for Reval2026. The information collected will be used to produce a new Valuation List. This will take effect on 1st April 2026.I encourage all business ratepayers to reply to LPS requests for information in relation to Reval 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's work to ensure that migratory fish are not impeded in terms of reaching their spawning sites in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department’s Inland Fisheries Inspectorate has commenced assessing and evaluating barriers that could potentially prevent fish migration on primary salmon rivers. The Department continues to work with developers and other organisations to mitigate existing barriers and to advise on fish passages where construction may be planned. The Inland Fisheries Inspectorate also carries out a programme of inspections and takes the necessary action to ensure compliance with the Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1966.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish updated guidance in relation to restraint and seclusion in educational settings.
answer_text: Officials have now provided me with a draft report on the outcome of the public consultation on the draft statutory guidance on the use of restrictive practices in educational settings. Subject to my approval, the consultation report will be published shortly and I will also determine the appropriate next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the action he is taking to support farmers impacted by increasing costs of supplies and services.
answer_text: I am aware of the inflationary pressures which have impacted farmers across Northern Ireland and further afield. Data analysed by my Department indicates that while costs have decreased over the last year, they remain elevated when compared to their 2020 levels. This is reflected as a rise in input prices (costs) across the UK of just over 31% from 2020 until February 2024. During the same period, farmgate prices in Northern Ireland have also risen. For example, milk price is 32.5 % higher, beef price is 35.6% higher, sheep prices are 55.7% higher and pig prices are 31.4% higher. Barley prices have also risen during the period, but to a lesser extent, up by 16.8% since 2020. To mitigate against the increase in costs, my Department delivers a number of programmes to support farmers and growers during the current period of high input costs. CAFRE’s Knowledge Transfer provision continues to focus on financial efficiency in addition to environmental sustainability. Farmers who have benchmarked their business with CAFRE can access an Adviser to help identify where improvements can be made to reduce costs. All farmers can access the CAFRE Dairy Margin Over Concentrate package on the CAFRE website, which also has a dedicated page focussed on managing rising costs on Northern Ireland farms.Through the Farm Business Improvement Capital investment scheme (FBIS-C), NI farm businesses have taken the opportunity to invest in new technology and infrastructure that is currently assisting with production and resource efficiency. Under Tranche 4 of Tier 1 of this scheme, 1,743 farmers have benefited from £8.16m grant-aid to purchase items including precision fertiliser and pesticide applicators, precision feeding systems, automated product handling and measurement equipment, storage facilities and weighing systems. My officials also continue to work on the new Farm Support and Development Programme that through a range of projects, will encourage farmers to better manage risk within their own businesses and become more resilient. A Crisis Framework will be developed under the Programme to enable my Department to assess potential industry impacts such as weather or market failure. It will determine the most appropriate timing and form of intervention necessary for a specific crisis. This will involve setting out trigger points (such as scale of impact or market price reductions) to inform the operation of this Framework.I also recognise that rising costs and financial pressures are a concern for many farmers and some who are particularly impacted may benefit from initiatives designed to support improved mental health and wellbeing. My Department has provided financial support to Rural Support since it was established in 2001 and I understand that the organisation has witnessed increased demand for their services, including their Financial Mentoring service during 2023/2024. For this reason, as part of the wider budget discussions to ensure funding is targeted towa
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why ten members of staff in his Department have had temporary promotions for over seven years.
answer_text: My Department has in a small number of cases needed to use extended temporary promotions. Nine of the cases relate to retaining staff with the necessary skills to ensure the continued delivery of some frontline services due to; a lack of supply at the appropriate grade; increasing demand in response to the pandemic; and unprecedented demands associated with the cost-of-living crisis.    The tenth case relates to a staff member who has been successful in four separate, consecutive temporary promotion opportunities over the last 7 years. The duration of a temporary promotion recorded on HRConnect relates to the individual not the specific post.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action he is taking to reduce the number staff in his Department with temporary promotions for over three years.
answer_text: The number and duration of temporary promotions is reviewed regularly, and my officials are engaged with NICS HR to progress recruitment competitions that will fill more posts substantively and to improve and speed up recruitment processes through the NICS People Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the differences in terms and conditions between agency staff and permanent staff in his Department.
answer_text: Whilst agency workers are not employed by NICS, the NICS is governed by the Agency Worker Regulations (AWR) (NI) 2011. The regulations state that agency workers within NICS are entitled to “relevant terms and conditions” meaning terms and conditions relating to pay, the duration of working time, night work, rest periods, rest breaks, and annual leave.NICS agency workers also receive enhancement to the Agency Worker Regulations as the NICS provides parity with Civil Service permanent colleagues in terms of pay, annual leave and paid time off for medical and dental appointments from day one of their assignment.		Employer pension contributions are compulsory, and the Recruitment Agency is legally responsible for these payments. By law the minimum contribution to a pension scheme is 8%, with at least 3% employer contribution.  The Department makes a 3% contribution towards recruitment agency staff’s pension.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what areas his Department is unable to retain parity with the Department for Work and Pensions due to staff shortages; and (ii) what he plans to do to address these issues.
answer_text: My Department is unable to maintain parity with the Department for Work and Pensions’ plans for maximising employment by reducing economic inactivity and supporting the progression of claimants into work, and for tackling fraud and error as set out in the Government's Back to Work plan and successive budget statements, without the allocation of the funding made available to address these issues. 	While the additional workloads created by the Move to Universal Credit exercise and ongoing legislative changes are currently being absorbed on a priority basis by existing work areas within Universal Credit, this situation serves only to further erode our capacity and capability to maintain parity with the Department for Work and Pensions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what statutory duties in his Department are not being fulfilled due to staff shortages.
answer_text: The time taken for recruitment competitions to complete, following the pause in NICS recruitment and resumption in November 2023, has contributed to staff shortages across my department. Not having key staff resources available has in part impacted on the fulfilment of the following statutory duties:statutory duty to protect, conserve and provide access to State Care Monuments (SCMs);statutory requirement to respond within 21 days to planning applications referred to HED by councils; statutory duty to compile a list of buildings of special historical or architectural interest; andstatutory duties to receive, preserve and make available the public record of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action he is taking to reduce the number staff in his Department who have been employed on temporary contracts for over three years.
answer_text: Staff on temporary (fixed term) contracts provide a vital support to the delivery of services and enable an influx of new skills and experience. As at the end of February there were 6 staff in my department who have been on a temporary (fixed term) contract for over 3 years in a workforce of over 10,000 people. Temporary (fixed term) contracts are regularly reviewed to determine ongoing need and the most appropriate approach to retain resources to ensure business requirements and continuity of critical services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the findings of the recent examination of half of NI Water's combined storm overflows which found that 80 per cent were in breach of legal limits.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water and the Environmental Regulator, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency. You should therefore direct your question to either NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com, or  the Northern Ireland Environment Agency on 0300 200 7856 or through nieainfo@daera-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to increase the budget for NI Water to reduce the 20 million tonnes of wastewater spilling into public waterways each year.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.The table below shows the level of funding provided to NI Water for PC21 to date:  YearCapital DEL £mResource DEL £m£000's£000's2021-22222,025148,5622022-23290,123171,6292023-24337,286172,000Total849,434492,191

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what action she is taking to reduce the number staff in her Department who have been employed on temporary contracts for over three years.
answer_text: The increased use of agency workers in recent years is due to a number of factors including a previous embargo on recruiting permanent staff and temporary pressures arising from EU Exit and Covid. Over 100 NICS recruitment competitions are currently underway, including more than 30 specifically for my department and this will reduce reliance on temporary workers within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action he is taking to reduce the number staff in his Department who have been employed on temporary contracts for over three years.
answer_text: NICS has a long standing policy on the use of temporary contracts which allows Departments to temporarily fill vacant posts where there is a clear business need and no immediate alternative supply available.  My department avails of this supply route, when there is no supply of permanent staff at particular grades / disciplines, or when a particular skillset is required for a time bound period.  It also makes clear, however, that, by their nature, these are temporary and therefore short-lived arrangements.  My officials continue to work with NICS HR in the Department of Finance – who provide a centralised HR function for the NI Civil Service – to recruit personnel to fill all affordable vacancies and meet our requirements as quickly as possible.  As always, these appointments will be on the basis of merit, through fair and open competition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what proportion of hours were covered as part of Western Urgent Care’s GP out of hours service in (i) Strabane; and (ii) Omagh, in each month of the last three years.
answer_text: Western Urgent Care (WUC) is commissioned to provide the GP Out of Hours (OOHs) service across the Western area.  The GP OOHs service is available Mon-Fri from 6pm to 8am the next morning, as well as 24 hours at weekends and during public holidays.  All Patients can contact WUC via a single telephone number, there are no telephone numbers available to contact bases directly. Patients who contact the service will be triaged by a telephone or video call by clinical staff who could be located in any one of the service’s bases in Derry/Londonderry, Enniskillen, Limavady, Strabane or Omagh.  In the last three years the GP OOHs service has been available to patients at all specified service opening times.Strabane GP OOHs base is currently open between 8am and 4pm on weekends and bank holidays with one Clinician, a GP or an Advanced Nurse Practitioner (ANP), working in the Strabane base at a time. WUC have advised this is in response to low numbers of clinicians booking shifts in the Strabane base and has been necessary in order to efficiently utilise the available resources.  Omagh GP OOHs base is currently open at all specified service opening times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department’s rationale for temporarily promoting staff outside a formal selection process.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 10604/22-27.  My department follows the NICS Temporary Promotions Policy, and all Temporary Promotions have been managed in accordance with the NICSHR policy guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the proportion of current temporary promotions in his Department that have not gone through a formalised selection procedure.
answer_text: All temporary promotions in my Department are managed in accordance with the NICS Temporary Promotions Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will raise the need for a multi-year budget for his Department during budgetary discussions at the Executive.
answer_text: I agree that after years of single year Budgets and short-term fixes, it is vital that the Executive returns to multi-year budgets to allow for a more strategic, long-term approach to be taken. I have, and will continue to, raise the importance of securing multi-year budgets for Northern Ireland in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will seek standalone outcomes for housing as part of the Programme for Government.
answer_text: The inclusion of a standalone outcome for housing as part of a Programme for Government will be a matter for the Executive to collectively prioritise and consult upon. However, the delivery of good quality, affordable and sustainable homes is a priority for me, and I will highlight this in any forthcoming discussions around a new Programme for Government. Work has commenced on the new Programme for Government and housing officials are engaged with the process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 9169/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 9169/ 22-27 was answered on 07 August 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether drivers from Northern Ireland, who are unable to attain a valid MOT certificate due to the delays in getting an appointment, are legally allowed to drive in Great Britain.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) appreciates that in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.The PSNI has agreed not to penalise drivers whose MOT has expired so long as:their vehicle is in a roadworthy and safe condition and doesn’t have a statutory off-road notification (SORN) declaration.their vehicle is properly insured. they can prove (by showing an MOT appointment notification/ reminder, either in electronic or paper form) that a test appointment has been booked for their vehicle.All other vehicle roadworthiness and insurance offences will be dealt with in the usual way.These arrangements only apply in the North. They do not cover other jurisdictions, where a valid MOT certificate is required.The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to drive in Britain is to:•	book the earliest available appointment; •	keep checking the booking system for an appointment before their MOT expires. If they have been unable to secure a suitable appointment they should complete the short notice appointment request form, and the DVA will do its best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of funding distributed to community groups through the Asset Recovery Community Scheme in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Since it was first launched in 2012 the Asset Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS), has awarded approximately £9 million to projects aimed at tackling crime and the fear of crime.  It funds valuable initiatives, mainly in community settings, that actively help victims, communities or the environment in support of a culture of lawfulness.  Community-based initiatives funded from ARCS over the last five financial years are as follows:   Year				Funding Allocation2019/20 			£1,120,6022020/21			£611,4172021/22			£169,782*2021/22			£440,333**2022/23			£389,4062023/24			£639,260* An additional 6 months (April – Sept 2021) was granted to 2020/21 projects to allow extra time to complete following Covid-19 interruptions. ** 2021/22 Scheme ran for the second half of the year only (Oct 21 – Mar 22).A range of interventions are supported through ARCS, including those working with young people to build skills and confidence; addressing anti-social behaviour; reducing re-offending; supporting victims of modern slavery, domestic abuse and hate crime; raising awareness of fraud and scams and organised crime as well as support for community safety initiatives.Further information on the 2021/22 ARCS can be found in the OCTF Annual Report and Threat Assessment:OCTF Annual Report and Threat Assessment 2021-2022 | Organised Crime Task Force

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department plans to support people in the private rented sector, following the latest Office for National Statistics figures that show rents had risen by 10.1 per cent in the last year.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the increase in rents in the private rented sector. Research carried out by my Department concluded that the best ways of relieving pressure on affordability for renters was by ensuring the benefit system properly takes account of the cost of housing and by increasing housing supply.  It is welcome news that the Local Housing Allowance rates have been unfrozen and increased from this month. Some of these rates have increased by over forty percent, so this action will go some way in helping many renters pay their rent relieving the pressure on their household budgets. You will further be aware that one of my key priorities is to increase the supply of good quality and affordable homes. To this end, I have committed to continue to prioritise the building of new social homes and utilise new funding sources to support the delivery of intermediate housing, including the delivery of a new intermediate rent product.  I am also currently reviewing a draft Housing Supply Strategy, aimed at unlocking supply across all tenures, and considering options on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many meetings took place with parties now in the Executive on a Programme for Government, between May 2022 and January 2024.
answer_text: We have been informed that between May 2022 and 3 February 2024, the Head of the Civil Service and other senior officials met with parties on many occasions. Briefings were offered to parties entitled to form an Executive to provide up to date information on a range of issues, including the Executive’s financial position and provided a forum through which the Northern Ireland Civil Service could be kept appraised of political priorities. These were not decision-making meetings. On 24 occasions Programme for Government formed part of the discussion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what funding models he is considering to enable the Housing Executive to borrow money in order to build more social housing.
answer_text: The NIHE faces a significant investment challenge. I am carefully considering advice and options which will support the long-term sustainability of the NIHE landlord. This includes access to borrowing which would enable increased investment in homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any policies are in place in the rest of the United Kingdom that allows drivers from Northern Ireland, who are unable to attain a valid MOT certificate due to the delays, to be exempt from prosecution for not having a valid test certificate.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) appreciates that in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.The PSNI has agreed not to penalise drivers whose MOT has expired so long as:their vehicle is in a roadworthy and safe condition and doesn’t have a statutory off-road notification (SORN) declaration.their vehicle is properly insured. they can prove (by showing an MOT appointment notification/ reminder, either in electronic or paper form) that a test appointment has been booked for their vehicle.All other vehicle roadworthiness and insurance offences will be dealt with in the usual way.These arrangements only apply in the North. They do not cover other jurisdictions, where a valid MOT certificate is required.The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to drive in Britain is to:•	book the earliest available appointment; •	keep checking the booking system for an appointment before their MOT expires. If they have been unable to secure a suitable appointment they should complete the short notice appointment request form, and the DVA will do its best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the policy report entitled Equal protection from assault in England and Northern Ireland: The health, education and legal case for legislative change to remove the reasonable punishment defence and to prohibit all physical punishment of children; and (ii) whether he will introduce legislation in line with the recommendations contained in the report.
To ask the Minister for Communities how much NI Reval2026 will cost her Department.
answer_text: Department of Finance (DoF), Land & Property Services (LPS) is revaluing all non-domestic properties in Northern Ireland for business rates.  My Department is engaging in the revaluation activity but the financial impact of the revaluation on my Department is not yet known.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will publish the breakdown of his Department’s estimated £56.4 million cost for the removal of the two-child cap.
answer_text: In 2022, the Welfare Mitigations Review - Independent Advisory Panel Report estimated the cost to offset the two-child policy at £52.9 million in 2024-25.  The report is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-mitigations-review-independent-advisory-panel-report Uplifted for recent higher levels of inflation applied to benefit and pension payments, the revised estimate is £56.4 million for 2024-25. My officials are currently taking forward work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Advisory Panel’s Report to help inform decisions on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the feasibility studies, funded by the British government, as part of their connectivity review.
answer_text: Translink is undertaking three feasibility studies financed by the Union Connectivity Development fund on reinstating of the Portadown to Armagh railway line, electrification of the railway line between Belfast and the border and the reopening of the Lisburn to Antrim (Knockmore) line.  The studies are ongoing and are on programme for completion in spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the projects his Department has discussed with the British government as part of their connectivity review.
answer_text: My Department and Translink have been successful in receiving funding through the British Government’s Union Connectivity Development Fund to take forward three studies focused on railway improvements.  They continue to engage with Department for Transport officials to seek further funding from this fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the spend on the greenways strategy, broken down by constituency, for the last five years.
answer_text: Monitoring of greenway spend within the Department is not broken down by constituency.In general terms, Councils are responsible for the development of greenways. The following table provides information in relation to the capital investment recorded or committed directly by my Department for greenway projects over the past five years: Council areas£mBelfast City Council4.2Derry and Strabane City Council(including contributions from DfC and DAERA towards the Strathfoyle Greenway)3.0Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council0.6Newry and Mourne District Council0.8Ards and North Down District Council0.7Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council0.9Mid Ulster District Council 0.5Mid and East Antrim Borough Council 0.7Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council0.3The table above includes provisional amounts for 2023/24 which have not yet been finalised. There are three greenway projects which have been funded under the INTERREG Va Programme, namely the Ulster Canal Greenway, the Carlingford Lough Greenway and the North West Greenway.  The Department’s capital contribution towards the match funding of the three greenways for the last 5 years is set out below: Ulster Canal Greenway: £443kCarlingford Greenway: £461k North West Greenway: £3,548k

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much of the 115 kilometre Craigavon to Derry greenway has been delivered.
answer_text: 22 kilometres of greenway route between Craigavon and Derry has been delivered along the 115 kilometre route corridor.  The 22km relate to sections between Strabane and Derry, including the recently completed Strabane North greenway.Delivery of greenways are generally council led, with my Department funding up to 50% of the capital cost of delivering approved projects.  My officials have been working with councils to identify their greenway priorities over the next 5 years and to develop an appropriate grant and support framework for the effective delivery of greenways going forward. By working together we can provide more people with access to high quality greenways, encouraging more walking, wheeling and cycling and addressing the climate emergency.
