question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many households in the private rented sector are in receipt of Local Housing Allowance, through (i) Universal Credit housing costs, and (ii) housing benefit.
answer_text: In November 2023, there were (i) 36,670 households in the private rented sector in receipt of Local Housing Allowance (LHA) through Universal Credit housing costs, and (ii) 22,740 claimants in the private rented sector in receipt of LHA through housing benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action his Department will take to help those who are unable to find a private sector rent guarantor due to their parents not being homeowners, they have grown up in care or are from overseas.
answer_text: The Department has no responsibility in relation to guarantors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of drug-related deaths, broken down by (i) council area; (ii) age; and (iii) sex, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  Drug related deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision) using codes F11–F16, F18–F19, X40–X44, X60–X64, X85 and Y10–Y14.2022 is the latest annual information available for the breakdowns requested. Once the 2023 Annual Report of the Registrar General is published (Autumn 2024) statistics for alcohol deaths will subsequently be published in winter 2024.The information requested, up to 2022, can be found in NISRA’s annual report Drug-Related and Drug Misuse Deaths 2012-2022 at the following link:https://www.nisra.gov.uk/sites/nisra.gov.uk/files/publications/Drug-related%20deaths%20in%20NI%2C%202012-2022_0.xlsxNumbers of drug-related deaths for the most recent 5 years, broken down by sex can be found in Table 1 of the report, while breakdowns on age and council area can be found in Table 2a and Table 9a respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of alcohol-related deaths, broken down by (i) council area; (ii) age; and (iii) sex, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  Alcohol deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision) using codes F10, G31.2, G62.1, I42.6, K29.2, K70, K73, K74, K86.0, X45, X65 and Y15.2022 is the latest annual information available for the breakdowns requested. Once the 2023 Annual Report of the Registrar General is published (Autumn 2024) statistics for alcohol deaths will subsequently be published in winter 2024.The information requested, up to 2022, can be found in NISRA’s annual report Alcohol-Specific deaths in Northern Ireland, 2022 at the following link:https://www.nisra.gov.uk/sites/nisra.gov.uk/files/publications/Alcohol_Tables_2022%20Final%20updated.xlsxNumbers of alcohol-specific deaths for the most recent 5 years, broken down by sex can be found in Table 1 of the report, while breakdowns on age and council area can be found in Table 2 and Table 6 respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health report Worried and waiting: A review of paediatric waiting times in Northern Ireland 2024, which states that waiting lists had risen by 172 per cent for general outpatient care over 7 years.
answer_text: This report reflects the challenge faced by Health and Social Care of a continuing upward trend in referrals into paediatric services since 2016.  While my Department is working to understand to causes of this growth in referrals, it clearly demonstrates that we must do everything we can to manage demand and improve waiting times for children and young people.  Ongoing resource constraints, compounded by the pandemic, have adversely impacted children’s services and the health and wellbeing of those awaiting assessment and treatment.  My Department is working with the Child Health Partnership to rebuild and reconfigure services in the face of these challenges. I had a very constructive meeting with representatives of the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health prior to the publication of this report to discuss outpatient waiting times and listen to their suggestions of how to manage demand and improve services.  I want to commend staff across the Health and Social Care system who are working hard to see and treat increasing numbers of children from within current resources.Tackling lengthy waiting times is a key priority.  It is, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.  Our waiting lists are unacceptable, and the need to transform services on a sustainable basis is paramount.  These figures show the scale of the waiting list crisis and demonstrate the need for multi-year funding.  However, I am also keen to ensure that steps are taken within current resources to manage demand to ensure that children and young people are seen in a timely manner in the right settings appropriate to their individual needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of recorded suicides, broken down by (i) council area; (ii) age; and (iii) sex, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  There are some situations where the registration of the death can be delayed, specifically where the death has been accidental, unexpected or suspicious, and must be referred to the Coroner, only to be registered after completion of the investigation. Such deaths may not appear in published statistics until at least six months after the death occurred. Suicides registered during a particular time will therefore not be reflective of suicides that actually occurred in that period.Deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision).  Since 2001, the ICD10 codes used for ‘Suicide and self-inflicted injury’ are X60-X84 and Y87.0 and the ICD10 codes used for ‘Undetermined intent’ are Y10-Y34 and Y87.2.2022 is the latest annual information available for the breakdowns requested. Once the 2023 Annual Report of the Registrar General is published (Autumn 2024) statistics for suicides will subsequently be published in winter 2024.The information requested, up to 2022, can be found in NISRA’s annual report at the following link:https://www.nisra.gov.uk/system/files/statistics/Suicide%20Statistics%202022%20Tables.xlsx Numbers of suicides for the most recent 5 years, broken down by age and sex can be found in Table 2 of the report, while breakdowns by council area can be found in Table 6.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to legislate for a mortgage rescue scheme, similar to the one in place in Wales.
answer_text: The Department currently has no specific plans to legislate for a mortgage rescue scheme in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to amend his Department’s definition of Affordable Housing.
answer_text: My Department published a revised definition of affordable housing on 1st April 2021. This definition allows for the introduction of a greater range of intermediate housing products to cater to a wider array of household needs and circumstances.It is sufficiently flexible to allow for the development of a range of products over time, adapting to changing needs as they arise. The definition can be updated to include new intermediate housing products as they are developed. There are no plans to amend it at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 8743/22-27, for his assessment of Sport NI’s annual report and accounts.
answer_text: I note that the Comptroller & Auditor General has certified the Sport NI Annual Report & Accounts for year ending 31 March 2023 and has not qualified her opinion.The Comptroller & Auditor General also included a short report which outlines a number of concerns she has on governance in Sport NI. I too share her concerns but acknowledge her conclusion that significant steps are underway within Sport NI to address the issues identified. My officials will continue to work closely with Sport NI to monitor progress going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will expand the Housing First model across Northern Ireland to help address issues in relation to homelessness.
answer_text: While the Housing Executive has the statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation, my aim is to support the NIHE to improve its response to homelessness and to make homelessness rare, brief and non-recurring.  I want to prioritise prevention to create as much capacity as possible in homelessness services and seek to extend a long-term commitment to developing Housing-Led and Housing First Provision in collaboration with the Department of Health, Department of Justice and other partners, budget depending. My Department Chairs the Inter-Departmental Task and Finish Group to establish the Housing First model for those at risk of homelessness.   In supporting the work of the Task and Finish group the Housing Executive has commissioned independent research on the feasibility study for Housing First for Northern Ireland. It is expected that this research will be published in Quarter 2 of 2024/25 and upon publication the Housing Executive will explore the development of further actions arising from the recommendations. It will take time to achieve these ambitions but my short term focus will continue to be, supporting those currently in crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to legislate to introduce a duty to prevent homelessness and require Executive departments to work together to prevent homelessness.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.It is worth noting that: The Housing Executive already collaborates with a multitude of frontline services as well as several Departments across NICS working towards the prevention of homelessness. Section 6A of The Housing (NI) Order 1988 already places a duty to develop a homelessness strategy, which must include prevention, and compels various statutory bodies to take this into account in the exercise of their functions.  Section 6B clarifies that “homelessness strategy” means a strategy for preventing homelessness.Section 14 of The Housing (NI) Order 1988 already places a duty on certain bodies to co-operate with the Housing Executive in the exercise of Homelessness functions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what percentage of Northern Ireland Civil Service staff earn less than the Real Living Wage, broken down by Executive Department.
answer_text: I can confirm that no Civil Service staff earn less than the Real Living Wage.  With effect from 1 April 2024, all Civil Service staff are earning the latest Living Wage Foundation’s Real Living Wage rate of £12 per hour.  Non-industrial staff who are paid monthly will see this reflected in their April pay which will be paid on 26 April 2024.  Industrial staff who are paid weekly will already have had the rise reflected in their pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the (i) number; and (ii) percentage of applicants failing the theory driving test, for each quarter of the last five years.
answer_text: DVA publish quarterly theory test figures in the National Statistics publication ‘DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator and Enforcement Statistics’. Theory Testing information is contained in Section 3 of each publication.  Theory tests conducted by test category are detailed in Table 3.2 and pass rates by test category are shown in Table 3.4. These tables can be found at Driver & Vehicle Agency Activity Statistics | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).The published tables contain data for each quarter for the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what proportion of staff employed by the Education Authority earn less than the Real Living Wage.
answer_text: During 2023/24, 13.5% of the full-time equivalent staffing complement in the Education Authority were paid below the current Real Living Wage rate of £12.00 per hour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what proportion of staff employed by Health and Social Care Trusts earn less than the Real Living Wage.
answer_text: The latest Real Living Wage, announced on 24th October 2023 by the Living Wage Foundation, is £12.00 per hour for 2023/24.  This equates to an annualised salary of £23,463.  Based on the current pay arrangements (2023/24), Agenda for Change Bands 1&2 (£22,383) and the entry level of Band 3 (£22,816) are below the real Living Wage.  As at 31st December 2023, 17% of HSC Trust staff were paid at those Bands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to introduce a social housing decarbonisation fund, similar to those in Great Britain or the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has developed a Housing Supply Strategy which acknowledges the importance of ensuring we have good quality homes.  One of the proposed long-term commitments of the Strategy is to ensure there is an emphasis on improving the energy efficiency of homes and ensuring a just transition to decarbonised solutions.  My Department has also supported the Housing Executive, in securing and utilising €22.951 million from the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) for their Energy Efficiency in Social Housing Programme with match funding from the Housing Executive.  The programme improved the thermal efficiency of 1,406 non-traditional construction homes within the Housing Executive stock.A further £57.5 million has been made available for the Housing Executive’s thermal improvement and low carbon programmes in the financial year 2024/25.My officials are also working on developing the NIHE revitalisation programme and a potential future retrofit funding model for registered Housing Associations. Given this level of investment in social home decarbonisation alongside the severe financial and budgetary constraints that all Departments are currently facing, I have no plans to introduce a separate Social Housing Decarbonisation Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what further action his Department will take to mitigate the spread of blue-green algae.
answer_text: As the member will be aware, there are no quick fixes to the occurrence, probable reoccurrence and spread of blue-green algae in Lough Neagh and other watercourses across Northern Ireland.However, my Department is taking action on a number of fronts. It is leading a cross-departmental Water Quality Steering Group and good progress has been made with the development of the Lough Neagh Report.The report will include a set of actions to tackle blue-green algae and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland.I intend to bring this report to the Executive very soon and, while I am not in a position to provide more details at this point, I will do so as soon as possible.I am also pleased to confirm that action on the ground has already commenced. For example, my Department, through its College for Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise, has already delivered nutrient management programmes for farmers/land managers at various levels targeted towards the Lough Neagh catchment areas.However, while these actions are vitally important, I cannot resolve this by myself. Improving water quality is a cross-cutting issue and collective responsibility across government, the public and private sector and wider society is required to address the problem.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 8744/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 8744/22-27 was answered on 22 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what his Department will do to halt the decline in the number of hen harriers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Hen Harrier is listed as a Northern Ireland Priority Species, requiring specific conservation action and protection.   NIEA classified two Special Protection Areas (SPAs) under the Birds Directive to protect the Hen Harrier: Antrim Hills SPA and Slieve Beagh-Mullaghfad-Lisnaskea SPA.   A conservation management plan is currently under development for the Antrim HillsillHill.My Department supports and is participating in a Hen Harrier Working Group which was convened in 2023 following the UK Hen Harrier survey, to inform actions to address this decline including assessment of deliberate persecution. Given the range of pressures and threats this species faces, partnership working will be required across conservation bodies and land managers in our uplands. Wider strategies impacting on upland management will need to recognise the needs of this and other vulnerable species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 6473/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health why there was a delay in a pay rise for consultants until this financial year, resulting in many having to pay extra tax due to breaching the upper limit of their pension accrual annual allowance tax free threshold.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive and ministers, my Department could not take decisions which would have been unaffordable and committed funding in excess of the allocated budget. It was simply not possible for officials to make any decision in respect of pay awards.Following the return of the NI Executive, my Department received an additional £550.7m to manage its pressures to the end of the financial year, including pressures arising from pay awards across HSC.  This has allowed progress to be made.One of my first actions as minister was to instruct that officials proceed with pay recommendations for hospital doctors and dentists. Final approvals for the business case and pay remit were received 09 April 2024. The pay circular setting out the revised arrangements was issued by my Department on 10 April.The Business Services Organisation will now require a further period to process the necessary IT changes and complete the calculations required to implement the pay award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the reasons for the decline in the number of hen harriers in Northern Ireland, given numbers have increased in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: Reasons for the decline in the number of Hen Harriers in Northern Ireland include a reduction in the extent, or degradation, of suitable breeding and foraging habitats, climate change (for instance, wet springs or warmer, drier summers), and disturbance or displacement of Hen Harriers during the breeding season from anthropogenic sources, burning of heather moorland and predation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 5629/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 5629/22-27 was answered on 10 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the figures published by the NI Composite Economic Index, which states that the Northern Ireland economy is 5.6 per cent above its pre-pandemic position, whilst the Republic of Ireland's economy is 20.5 per cent above its pre-pandemic position.
answer_text: The NI Statistics and Research Agency, (who publish NICEI) state that these figures should not be considered as precisely like-for-like.  They note that the South’s GDP data (which is the basis of the 20.5% figure) has been volatile over recent years, with the Central Statistics Office (CSO) highlighting the impacts that globalisation and the activities of multinational enterprises have had on Ireland’s GDP figures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to the figures published by the NI Composite Economic Index which states that economic output decreased by 0.2 per cent over the last quarter, what measures he is taking to stop the economy going into recession.
answer_text: It is important to note that, while the latest NI Composite Economic Index showed that economic output in the North decreased by 0.2% over the quarter to December 2023, it has increased by 0.3% over the year. I am now taking a range of steps to address the economic challenges I have identified.  My focus on productivity will support stronger sustainable economic growth but, crucially, it is aimed at growth that is shared across all our regions and that supports better working conditions.I will be publishing actions to deliver on my economic objectives as part of the Department’s business plan for 2024/25.  I will also work in collaboration with business representatives, trade unions and academia in pursuit of these aims.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what funding his Department provides for art therapy in West Tyrone.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency does not specifically invest in Art Therapy in West Tyrone, however it does have three regional contracts in place to promote the use of Art as a means of improving health and wellbeing and reducing inequalities. The contracts cover the whole of Northern Ireland and are focused on two specific population groups, Looked After Children and Older People. The contract details are listed below:Delivery Organisation Key Objectives Value Arts Care- Here and Now Project To improve health and wellbeing and reduce loneliness, social isolation and health inequalities work in partnership with the Age Friendly Officers to co-ordinate and implement arts-based workshop programmes for over 60s £88,250Arts Council of Northern IrelandPHA contributes to a regional Arts and Older People Grants Programme that strengthens the voice of older people and promotes positive mental health and emotional wellbeing through the use of arts£89,000Arts Care -Twilight Programme for Looked after ChildrenDelivery of 5 Arts programmes specifically for Children in Care £34,000Similarly, the Department of Health do not commission Art Therapy services.The Department of health procured and awarded a regional contract to Arts Care for Arts in Health provision in 2015. The current annual value of the contract is £242,000 and Arts Care provides services regionally across programmes of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the long-term plans for Fintona Medical Centre.
answer_text: Fintona Group Practice notified the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health (DoH) of their intention to hand back their contract for the delivery of General Medical Services (GMS) on 31st March 2024.  As of 1st April 2024, Western Health and Social Care Trust are now contract holders for the Fintona Practice.  This arrangement should provide stability for the practice and patients should continue to contact the practice as normal.With regards to the Fintona and wider Fermanagh and Omagh area, DoH/SPPG are currently working with Western Trust colleagues in order to identify and take forward a long-term solution for issues in the area.DoH/SPPG continue to work with all GP Practices across Northern Ireland to maintain the provision of GMS services to the resident population.   Currently, initiatives such as the introduction of General Practice Pharmacists and Advanced Nurse Practitioners are available to many practices across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the publication of his Department’s Poverty and Income Inequality report, what action he is taking to address the increase of children in relative poverty before housing costs.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for all in our communities, including our young people, right across Northern Ireland.		The Poverty and Income Inequality report shows a rise across some indicators relating to the number of children living in poverty in 2022/23. This is a matter of concern and, although my Department is leading on the development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy, in order to deal with this effectively, it will be necessary for the Executive to act together.		The Anti-Poverty Strategy will aim to address the root causes of poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage and inequality and set out the NI Executive’s commitment to a joined up, long term approach to addressing poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the publication of his Department’s Poverty and Income Inequality report, what action he is taking to address the increase of working age adults in absolute poverty after housing costs.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for all in our communities right across Northern Ireland.The Poverty and Income Inequality report shows a rise across some indicators relating to the number of working age adults living in poverty in 2022/23. This is a matter of concern. Although my Department is leading on the development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy, in order to address this effectively, it will be necessary for the Executive to act together. The Anti-Poverty Strategy will aim to address the root causes of poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage and inequality and set out the NI Executive’s commitment to a joined up, long term approach to addressing poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish a response to the evaluation of the Healthy Happy Minds pilot project and the 14 recommendations contained in the report.
answer_text: There are no plans to publish a response to the evaluation of the Healthy Happy Minds pilot.  I will take the time to assess the findings of the evaluation and the recommendations contained therein.  It will also be important to consider other relevant research and feedback from stakeholders as my department develops a longer-term approach to supporting children and young people’s emotional health and wellbeing in education.  Of course, this must be delivered within the budgetary resources available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following the publication of the Legal Aid Annual Statistics by her Department, why the costs of Legal Aid has risen by £6,479,931, equating to seven per cent, whilst the number of people granted Legal Aid has dropped by 4,986, which equates to seven per cent.
answer_text: Statistics covering legal aid grants and authorised expenditure within any given time period are not directly comparable and should be viewed as independent figures.  As legal aid cases can last for several years, payments made in one year can and will reflect work undertaken from legal aid applications granted in previous years.  Similarly, not all legal aid cases granted in any given year will have been completed or resulted in a payment being made during that same time period. In addition, different types of legal aid cases typically incur different costs meaning that the annual mix of applications granted will influence future expenditure.  While the overall number of legal aid grants dropped in 2022/23, this was a direct result of more stringent verification of capital threshold being applied as part of the application process for civil Advice and Assistance (down 51%, 6,203).  This will have had a minimal impact in terms of overall expenditure as civil Advice and Assistance typically displays the lowest average case costs.Finally, available budget is a key enabler of legal aid expenditure and in 2022/23 approved funding was approximately £6 million higher than in 2021/22.  Not only did this facilitate a timelier payment of more bills, but directly reduced the unpaid bills that the Agency would otherwise have carried into 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 6191/22-27, what further action his Department can take following a further blue-green algae outbreak at Moorlough Lake, West Tyrone.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) confirmed, from photographs received on 4 April 2024, the presence of blue-green algae at Moorlough, Strabane. NIEA staff visited the lough on 5 April 2024 and observed no signs of an algae bloom. The behaviour of blooms can be unpredictable and their location within a water body can change quickly throughout the day. Therefore, a bloom that may be visible at one location one day may not be visible the next.My Department is leading a cross departmental Water Quality Steering Group and is in the process of finalising a draft report with a set of evidenced based recommendations to tackle blue-green algae and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland. This report will be brought to the Executive for consideration and discussion.  This will support the actions relevant to improving water quality across Northern Ireland in the draft Environmental Improvement Plan. My Department also plans to commence a Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) scheme that aims to identify viable technical solutions to deal with the immediate effects of Blue-Green Algae in Lough Neagh which may then be able to be used in other waterways.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will publish his Department's new Climate Change Action Plan.
answer_text: Work is currently underway within my Department on the development of a departmental Climate Change Action Plan for 2024/25. I intend to publish this Climate Change Action Plan in June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 7868/22-27, and following consideration by his Department’s casework panel, whether he has an update on the Strabane Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: The business case for the Strabane Public Realm Scheme was considered by my Department’s casework committee on 21 March, with the result that the committee are content to recommend the business case for approval subject to further required amendments and inclusions to the business case.  Once the Council have made the required amendments to the business case, I will give due consideration to funding this project, alongside other capital priorities across my Department.  The business case will also require Department of Finance approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of temporary and agency staff employed by the Education Authority and (ii) the cost of employing these staff, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below contains information on agency and temporary staff from the Education Authority’s audited Annual Report and Accounts.Financial Year2022/2321/2220/2119/2018/19No. of Staff Employed 1Agency18016620412288Temporary16,13115,20413,28811,87111,496Cost of employing staff (£000)£££££Agency5,5094,6705,1055,4092,992Temporary438,884381,165325,666282,453300,812Notes:1 Staffing Figures are Whole Time Equivalent numbers.Includes temporary non-teaching staff and EA HQ Advisory Teachers who are in a temporary substantive posts.   For information on substitute teachers, please see link below to published information on the DE website on teacher substitute days and costs. https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/teacher-workforce-statistics-202223

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department’s review of the Decent Homes Standards will be (i) completed; and (ii) published.
answer_text: My Department initiated a review of the Decent Home Standard in January 2024. Timelines for completion and reporting are yet to be established. Alongside policy development work, officials are monitoring the outcomes of similar reviews in other jurisdictions which may extend timeframes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department’s review of the Disabled Facilities Grant will be (i) completed; and (ii) published.
answer_text: The review of the Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) links into the wider review of (NIHE administered) Private Sector Grants being taken forward by my Department. This review incorporates many varied workstreams which the Department will be progressing over the next year in conjunction with the Housing Executive and the Department of Health.		At this stage, I cannot confirm when any findings of the review will be published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline when will he publish the recommendations of the report from the Planning Appeals Commission on the Public Inquiry on the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to connect the A5 and A6 roads once they have been completed.
answer_text: The Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which may consider such a link road.  At this stage, I have not taken any decisions in relation to the Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to change the law on Community Asset Transfer regulations to bring them more in line with Scotland.
answer_text: Community Asset Transfer is an Executive policy, operated and managed cross-departmentally.  I am aware of the wider powers in Scotland.  Where there are opportunities during this mandate to consider ways to improve policy outcomes in Northern Ireland, I have asked my officials to engage cross-departmentally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he plans to update Credit Union legislation to bring it in line with legislation in Great Britain and the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I have set up a dedicated team to deliver the policies outlined in the recently published Irish League of Credit Unions policy manifesto.  The necessary policies and legislation will be co-designed with both the Irish League of Credit Unions and the Ulster Federation of Credit Unions. The work will be prioritised by my Department. It will proceed at pace and be delivered within the three years remaining of the mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to introduce a specific exemption in discretionary rates relief for Credit Unions, similar to that in Wales.
answer_text: The data LPS holds on its operating system shows that of the 197 ratepayers identified as Credit Unions, 121 are currently in receipt of Small Business Rate Relief. I am aware that the Economy Minister announced that he will be establishing a dedicated team within his Department to grow Credit Unions. It would be for the Department for Economy, as the policy competent Department for Credit Unions, to bring the case for any proposals for additional rate support to the Executive. Such proposals would then be considered in the wider context of aligning the rating system with Executive priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will update guidance on Community Asset Transfers.
answer_text: Community Asset Transfer is an Executive policy, operated and managed cross-departmentally.  There are a number of pieces of guidance in place to support the policy.  My department provides funding to Development Trusts NI to support voluntary and community sector organisations through the CAT process.  Up to date guidance is available, but more importantly, groups considering purchasing an asset are given direct, hands-on support to navigate the nuanced nature of individual projects.  This includes help with the development of business plans.Internal NICS guidance includes: The Community Asset Transfer Policy Framework (published by The Department for Social Development 2014)Disposal of Surplus Public Sector Property in Northern Ireland (published by Land & Property Services 2018)Community Asset Transfer: Guidance for Asset Owners (published by The Department for Communities 2018)Better Business Cases NI-CAT Guidance (published by The Department of Finance 2023)My officials will ensure that the guidance for asset owners is updated to highlight the recently published CAT Business Case guidance.  Where there are opportunities during this mandate to work through other issues impeding the asset transfer process, my officials will ensure that these are realised and that any further updates or policy changes are incorporated into guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7175/22-27, what further progress has been made in discussions with the Minister of Finance in relation to the pay and grading review for Non-Teaching Support Staff in the Education Authority.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 9211/22-27.This answer will be contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 29 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a timescale for the production of the Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: A final review of the current The Ten-Year Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland was published online in September 2023. Developmental work on the successor strategy commenced towards the end of last year, however the recent announcement regarding Northern Ireland’s potential inclusion in the UK Government Tobacco and Vapes Bill has required a diversion of our focus to that work. The current strategy has been extended to allow for development of the successor strategy and we will recommence work on the successor strategy as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will set a smokefree target for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Ten-Year Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland has an overall aim to create a tobacco-free society. A final review of the current strategy was published online in September 2023 and work to develop a new strategy is in the early stages of development. The final review of the Strategy acknowledged the stakeholder support for a smoke-free target date, and this will be further considered as the new strategy is developed. It is important that the setting of such ambitious targets includes considered, resourced and realistic plans as to how to achieve the target. I have recently announced my intention to seek NI Assembly support for inclusion of NI in the UK Government Tobacco and Vapes Bill.  I believe that the measures set out in that Bill have the potential to make a huge impact on smoking prevalence rates here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to increase the cost of MOTs to raise extra revenue for his Department.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency operates as a Trading Fund under the provisions of the Driver & Vehicle Agency Trading Fund (Northern Ireland) Order 2016.  MOT fee income is accounted for within this Trading Fund.  MOT fees are set to recover the costs associated with delivering MOT services, not to raise extra revenue for the Department.  Following consultation MOT fees were increased from 1 October 2023.  This was the first increase for the majority of fees since 2005 and was necessary because of the rising costs of delivering MOT services.  Further fees reviews will be carried out on a more regular basis as necessary and as required under Managing Public Money (NI), to manage the financial position of the DVA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action she is taking to address the doubling of agricultural crime recorded in the Derry and Strabane Policing District.
answer_text: A partnership approach is central to addressing crime, including agricultural crime. The Department of Justice is a member of the Rural Crime Partnership (RCP), along with the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), the Ulster Farmers’ Union, NFU Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Club of Ulster, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DEARA) and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). The partnership shares information on emerging or reoccurring crimes, and will continue to do so, to support a coordinated response and preventative approach to addressing agricultural crime. Notwithstanding, the operational detection and investigation of agricultural crime is however an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the implications of the objectives of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund not being aligned with the objectives of the predecessor EU funds.
answer_text: The Executive is best placed to ensure that funding is prioritised in alignment with our agreed policies and strategies. 	The agreed Executive position is that the British Government should fully replace EU funding and give full control of that funding to the Executive. 	Rather than replicate the activities funded by the EU, we could then ensure delivery against the Executive’s economic strategies, including the key priorities underpinning my Economic Vision.	Failure to do so risks misalignment, duplication, sub-optimal outcomes and poor value for money. This is of particular concern given the currrent challenging budgetary position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will have the costings for all the recommendations made by the Independent Advisory Panel on Welfare Reform Mitigations.
answer_text: My officials are currently taking forward work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Advisory Panel’s Review to help inform decisions on this matter.Any future mitigation package will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years. I will provide further details to the Assembly at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to financially support the Local Labour Market Partnerships beyond the end of the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: As with all departments, the level of funding available for 2024/25 is subject to approval by the NI Executive and yet to be determined for my department.  When the budget position is confirmed, I will be in a position to outline my plans for departmental spend including consideration of financial support for Labour Market Partnerships.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to financially support Jobstart beyond March 2025.
answer_text: I recognise the excellent opportunities and outcomes that the JobStart Scheme achieves.As with all departments, the level of funding available for 2024/25 is subject to approval by the NI Executive and yet to be determined for my department.  When the budget position is confirmed, I will be in a position to outline my plans for departmental spend including consideration of financial support  for the JobStart Scheme for the 2024-25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following comments by the Speaker at the conclusion of Question Time on 12 March 2024, whether he intends to revise or revisit his previous responses to Members.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 7950/22-27, what proportion of applicants were warned of a partner’s previous behaviour.
answer_text: As you will be aware from my answer to your previous question, from its commencement on 26 March 2018 until 31 January 2024, data from the PSNI shows that there have been a total of 1603 applications made to the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme. As you were also advised, applications to the scheme can be made by an individual, or someone they know, who has concerns that they may be at risk of harm from a partner or an ex-partner.  Police officers and staff members can also make an internal application to the scheme.Disclosures (i.e. an individual being warned about a partner’s or ex-partner’s previous behaviour) are made to the individual who may be at risk, unless there are vulnerability issues which means a parent, carer or support worker is best placed to receive the disclosure on the individual’s behalf.Until the end of 31 January 2024, over 294 disclosures had been made regarding the risk posed by a partner or ex-partner. Due to the PSNI’s recording mechanisms, disclosure data for the financial year of 2023/24 will be finalised at the end the year so the figure is likely to be higher.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to amend our debt relief order system to bring it in line with England and Wales.
answer_text: Mr McCrossan has asked:To ask the Minister for the Economy, whether he plans to amend our debt relief order system to bring it in line with England and Wales.	 ANSWER		I will be discussing insolvency legislation with my officials and formulating my approach over the coming weeks.  This will include consideration of my Department’s debt relief scheme. 						CONOR MURPHY MLAMinister for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to increase the budget for Discretionary Support.
answer_text: As part of the 2024-25 Budget exercise, I have sought the funding necessary to continue delivery of my Department’s Discretionary Support scheme in 2024-25. However, it will be for the Executive to decide on the level of Resource and Capital funding allocated to each NI Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s commitment to introduce a GCSE in sign language.
answer_text: My Department has not given a commitment to introduce a GCSE in Sign language, but has previously provided clarification on the process whereby such a qualification, once developed in England, may be made available to Northern Ireland students in due course.Work on the development of a GCSE in British Sign Language is progressing in England. CCEA Regulation, the qualifications regulator for Northern Ireland, is in regular contact with their counterparts in England as they move forward with this work. Northern Ireland operates an open qualifications market and when the development work has been completed and a qualification becomes available in England it can then be accredited for use in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the cost of the Executive Office’s visit to the United States of America for St Patrick's Day.
answer_text: We travelled to Washington DC for a 4-day programme of events over St Patrick’s Week to showcase Northern Ireland, to share best practice on key issues like childcare and to highlight we are open for business in the largest economy in the world. Each Minister travelled with a Special Adviser, their Private Secretary and their Press Officer. A photographer also travelled in support of both Ministers.  A breakdown of the overall costs is provided below.Travel	£38,000Accommodation	£13,000	Other expenses	£3,000

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of why vaccination rates here are lower than in Scotland or Wales.
answer_text: Uptake across all childhood immunisation programmes have been steadily declining in recent years throughout the UK. Uptake rates in Northern Ireland remain higher than the UK-average and also England, however in most cases uptake is lower than in Scotland and Wales. Conversely, uptake rates of Men B, measured at 12 months of age, are higher in Northern Ireland than in Wales.The Public Health Agency (PHA) is a member of the UK forum to facilitate discussion on declining coverage and inequalities, which is chaired by the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA). This groups brings together relevant stakeholders from across the UK nations to explore the likely contributing factors to declining uptake across the routine immunisation programmes; to identify common issues; discuss potential actions to address the issues; and facilitate the sharing of experience and learning across the UK nations.In an effort to tackle the issue of declining uptake in pre-school vaccinations, the Public Health Agency (PHA) has established a multi-disciplinary working group to identify and implement how to improve uptake. Work includes understanding the many factors that may have contributed to declining uptake. These reasons include service pressures at GP and Trust level, the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on public perception of vaccination programmes, perceived limited access to services who provide vaccinations and human behaviours including vaccine hesitancy. Delayed or missing data submissions to the Child Health System (CHS), which is still paper based, may also have an impact on reported uptake rates. Going forward, as we develop digital health records, the aim is that children’s vaccination records will be updated in a timelier manner and also become available to parents through their child’s e-health record.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to increase the vaccination rate up to 95 per cent, as recommended by the World Health Organisation.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) have established a multi-disciplinary working group to identify and implement how to improve childhood immunisation uptake to prevent vaccine-preventable disease and potential outbreaks in Northern Ireland. The remit of this group includes identifying barriers to achieving high vaccine uptake; recommending and implementing targeted evidence-based interventions and improvements; and considering specific campaigns or communications which may improve vaccine uptake.My department is represented on this group, and we will continue to work closely with the PHA to improve uptake rates.  An MMR vaccine catch-up campaign was launched at the beginning of February to improve overall uptake rates for this vaccine by offering vaccination to those up to 25 years of age with less than the required two doses of vaccine. Those aged 26 years and over who remain fully or partially unvaccinated, can request vaccination via their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the recent increase in whooping cough rates is cyclical or due to vaccine hesitancy.
answer_text: It is typical to see cyclical increases in pertussis every 3 to 4 years. This increase is therefore expected, although later than anticipated due to the control measures in place during the pandemic.Due to the fall in vaccine rates in both the infant and maternal programmes it is possible that this has contributed to a higher peak than would be expected. The reasons for the falls in vaccine rates are multifactorial and there is no strong evidence that it is related to increased vaccine hesitancy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to increase his Department’s capital budget, which is the same in cash terms as 17 years ago.
answer_text: It will be for the Executive to decide on the level of Capital funding allocated to each NI Department. However, I will seek to maximise the funding allocated to my Department through my engagement in the 2024-25 Budget Process

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures are in place to ensure that pupils with a statement of special educational needs will receive a school place before the start of term in September 2024.
answer_text: Children with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) follow a different process for securing a school placement other than the general admissions process, one which seeks to ensure their needs as outlined in their statement are met.  Each child is individually placed.Children are referred for SEN assessment and review throughout the year, which means that there is a continual flow of children entering the process. This makes alignment with the general admissions process difficult.Access to appropriate school placements for children with SEN has been the subject of much discussion in recent weeks.  The demand for specialist education provision has increased significantly over the last number of years and the system has struggled to keep pace with the rising demand.  In the last three academic years, the Education Authority (EA) has created 140 additional classes for special schools and 203 new specialist provision classes in mainstream schools (SPiMS).The position for this September is extremely challenging.    Based on the EA’s latest planning assumptions, more than 1,000 additional specialist education places are needed by September 2024.  My immediate priority is to increase the capacity across the education system to ensure sufficient appropriate places for children with SEN for September 2024. The EA is working in collaboration with stakeholders and schools where it knows there are acute pressures to create additional places in both special schools and SPiMS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what progress his Department has made in implementing the recommendations contained in the May 2023 Northern Ireland Audit Office report into pre-school vaccinations.
answer_text: Much progress has been made in implementing the recommendations contained in the May 2023 Northern Ireland Audit Office report into pre-school vaccinations.The Public Health Agency (PHA) had formed a multidisciplinary working group in June 2022, prior to the publication of the report, to address the decline in pre-school vaccine uptake rates and to identify and implement how to improve uptake to prevent vaccine-preventable disease and potential outbreaks in Northern Ireland. The remit of this group, which the department is represented on, includes identifying barriers to achieving high vaccine uptake; recommending and implementing targeted evidence-based interventions and improvements; and considering specific campaigns or communications which may improve vaccine uptake. This group has developed an action plan to support work across five separate themes: data; access to services; communications; operational management; and training and education.The PHA and the Child Health System (CHS), which is used to record vaccination uptake, are working to improve data flow.  The CHS have been providing vaccination uptake data by GP practice, Trust and regional level to identify areas with low uptake. Improved CHS system training has also been developed and offered as part of a wider training plan to GPs, practice managers, health visitors, public health staff nurses and student health visitors.   Promotional activities continue at community level, in schools, and at large scale public events. Wider public communications include social media, face to face, and through literature.  The ‘Living well’ campaign, in collaboration with 500 NI pharmacies, is being used to promote preschool vaccination uptake regionally.Furthermore, an MMR vaccine catch-up campaign was launched at the beginning of February to improve overall uptake rates for this vaccine by offering vaccination to those up to 25 years of age with less than the required two doses of vaccine. Those aged 26 years and over who remain fully or partially unvaccinated, can request vaccination via their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report that highlights (i) a lack of understanding of accountability arrangements; and (ii) a lack of joined-up working in the delivery of the 2016-22 Child Poverty strategy.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering the NIAO’s recent review of the Executive’s Child Poverty Strategy.		As the Child Poverty Strategy was an Executive Strategy, there will be lessons to be learnt for all departments, and I will raise relevant issues with my Ministerial colleagues.		Poverty is a complex and multi-faceted issue and addressing it in all its forms is a key priority for me. I will be working with my Executive colleagues to develop a robust and effective Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and I will ensure any lessons learnt from the report are used to inform this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report that states there has been a lack of significant progress on the main child poverty indicators; and (ii) what he plans he will put in place to address this.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering the NIAO’s recent review of the Executive’s Child Poverty Strategy.As the Child Poverty Strategy was an Executive Strategy, there will be lessons to be learnt for all departments, and I will make sure to raise relevant issues with my Ministerial colleagues.Poverty is a complex and multi-faceted issue and addressing it in all its forms is a key priority for me. I will be working with my Executive colleagues to develop a robust and effective Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and I will ensure any lessons learnt from the report are used to inform this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will bring forward legislation to ban hunting with dogs.
answer_text: I understand that this is an important issue, and much work has been undertaken to date to take forward a ban, most notably, by John Blair, MLA, in the last Assembly mandate.  I am supportive of the need to ban the hunting of wild animals with dogs.However, I need to consider the priority of any relevant legislation against other priorities within the Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency's cautioning of the fall in jobs figures, contained in the Quarterly Employment Survey, were not statistically significant.
answer_text: I have considered the range of labour market statistics released by NISRA on 12th March. I have noted the provisional estimate of 817,780 employee jobs in December 2023 follows the record high of 822,830 employee jobs in September 2023, and that the quarterly change is not statistically significant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will set targets for poverty reduction and a focus on early intervention and preventative actions, as highlighted in the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on the 2016-22 Child Poverty strategy.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering the NIAO’s recent review of the Executive’s Child Poverty Strategy.		As the Child Poverty Strategy was an Executive Strategy, there will be lessons to be learnt for all departments, and I will raise relevant issues with my Ministerial colleagues.		Poverty is a complex and multi-faceted issue and addressing it in all its forms is a key priority for me. I will be working with my Executive colleagues to develop a robust and effective Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and I will ensure any lessons learnt from the report are used to inform this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given his Department’s risk rating for Sports NI is red, what action he is taking to mitigate these risks.
answer_text: As part of the Governance arrangements in place for Arm’s Length bodies, the Department will regularly review its assessment of an organisation based on a range of evidence available to it, including details of accountability meetings, bi-annual assurance statements, internal and external audit reports and ongoing engagement with the organisation’s Board and Senior Management. This approach is applied to Sport NI and regularly reviewed.  The Department continues to work with the Board and the Executive Team at Sport NI to ensure that it operates within an appropriate governance framework to address all levels of risk and to enable the organisation to deliver positive outcomes for sport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of incorporating the European Charter for Regional and Minority Languages into domestic law.
answer_text: To date, I have not assessed incorporating the European Charter for Regional and Minority Languages into domestic law.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to review the personal injury discount rate, similar to what is happening in England and Wales.
answer_text: The Damages (Return on Investment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 amended the Damages Act 1996 to assign the task of reviewing and setting the rate to the Government Actuary, provide a statutory methodology for calculating the rate and establish a timeframe for review of the rate. Under the legislation, a review of the rate must be started by the Government Actuary on 1 July 2024 and be completed within 90 days. In anticipation of that, my Department is presently considering whether any modifications are required to the statutory parameters according to which the rate is set by the Government Actuary and if required, will bring forward draft regulations for approval by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the direct benefits an Irish Language Strategy would bring; and (ii) how these benefits can be (a) monitored; and (b) measured.
answer_text: I have made no assessment of the direct benefits an Irish Language strategy would bring.  The benefits could be monitored and measured by a framework that may be designed for this purpose. However, agreement on a strategy and how it might be monitored and measured will be part of the process taken forward by the Executive when it considers adopting this Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what his Department is doing to reduce the numbers of households in temporary accommodation.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation and has provided the information below.  The Housing Executive has established a Housing Solutions Task and Finish Group which is chaired by the Chief Executive and is considering options for alternative and more financially sustainable options for temporary accommodation. In addressing longer-term goals on temporary accommodation the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation Year 2 Action Plan can be found at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/e95603ed-bad9-47ca-90a2-1aef23659665/Year-2-Actions-Strategic-Action-Plan-for-Temporary-Accommodation-2022-27.pdf and contains 25 actions that support the Housing Executive’s delivery of the following objectives:Minimised need for temporary accommodation;Customers receive appropriate accommodation and support at the point of need; Customers moving on from temporary accommodation sustain their tenancies; andA sustainable supply of good quality, affordable temporary accommodation which is safe, warm and well managed.The Housing Executive is currently developing the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation Year 3 Action Plan which will guide their strategic objectives in relation to temporary accommodation for 2024/25.I have asked the Housing Executive to provide me with a monthly update on the issue of Temporary Accommodation, including progress to address the current position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she will make a statement regarding the £100 million in Barnett consequentials announced by the Chancellor in the spring budget.
answer_text: The Barnett consequentials arising from the Spring Budget will form part of the overall funding available to the Executive to allocate in the 2024-25 financial year.I will make a statement to the Assembly when the Executive agrees its Budget 2024-25 and on any subsequent allocations agreed as part of the in-year monitoring process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's Strategic Plan for Greenways for the West Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: Exercise - Explore - Enjoy: A Strategic Plan for Greenways published in November 2016 sets out the Departments plans to develop a greenway network right across the north. Delivery of greenways are generally council led, with my Department funding up to 50% of the capital cost of delivering approved projects. Officials have been working closely with councils over the past year, including Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and Derry City and Strabane District Council, who share responsibility the West Tyrone area. This engagement allows officials to better understand their 5-year greenway programme and to develop an appropriate grant and support framework for the effective delivery of greenways going forward.Both Councils have identified a number of schemes for West Tyrone.When the relevant Councils develop their plans, officials will be happy to engage with them again. Future delivery will be dependent upon availability of funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what discussions have taken place between his Department and Derry City and Strabane District Council to progress the Strabane to Sion Mills greenway.
answer_text: Delivery of greenways are generally council led, with my Department funding up to 50% of the capital cost of delivering approved projects. Officials have been working closely with councils over the past year, including Derry City and Strabane District Council, to better understand their 5-year greenway programme, and to develop an appropriate grant and support framework for the effective delivery of greenways going forward.A proposed greenway between Strabane and Sion Mills was one of many projects that was discussed with Derry City and Strabane District Council recently. When Council develop their plans, officials will be happy to engage with them again. Future delivery will be dependent upon availability of funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 4655/22-27, how many reports his Department has had of defective Mica blocks being used to build private sector properties.
answer_text: My Department has been made aware of one private property affected by defective Mica blocks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what discussions have taken place between his Department and Derry City and Strabane District Council in relation to homes with defective Mica blocks since they launched their register.
answer_text: On 6th January 2023, the Council’s Director of Environment and Regeneration wrote to the Department’s Permanent Secretary regarding defective Mica blocks.  Officials responded on 19th January 2023 to advise that the issue pertaining to defective Mica blocks is a Reserved Matter that is not within the responsibility of my Department.  There has been no further correspondence with Derry City and Strabane District Council on this issue since the launch of their register.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 7759/22-27, given that there are only three registered Irish Sign Language interpreters registered in NI, whether further actions are needed to increase capacity above the six places being funded by his Department.
answer_text: Qualification as a registered Irish Sign Language (ISL) interpreter in Northern Ireland is a lengthy process compounded by the lack of accredited ISL linguistic courses which limit the supply of students with sufficient linguistic proficiency to access an Interpreter Training Programme (ITP).My officials have engaged with stakeholders to develop a funded pathway for students to gain ISL linguistic qualifications to access an ITP, such as the vocational route programme delivered by the community-based Foyle Deaf Association. Officials are also working with Queen’s University Belfast to develop and provide an academic route to qualification as an ISL interpreter. The availability of both routes to qualification will increase capacity and expertise and will future proof a sustainable supply of ISL interpreters.
