question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the former Strabane Academy site on the Liskey Road, Strabane.
answer_text: The former Strabane Academy site on the Liskey Road remains in Education Authority (EA) ownership and is being held pending the outcomes of a wider review and development of area plans for one of the educational services (EOTAS). A programme has yet to be agreed for the development of the relevant area plans. EA are retaining the entire site until the relevant area plans have been concluded as the site may offer an opportunity for a solution to deliver service needs in the area.Specifically, in relation to the site it can be confirmed that the former school buildings have been demolished apart from the original Milltown House buildings and Gate Lodge. The local community have expressed interest in the future use of the site and Derry City & Strabane District Council have been in contact in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has expressed an interest in acquiring the former Castlederg PSNI station site.
answer_text: The site at the former Castlederg PSNI Station, 5 Castlefin Road, Castlederg was trawled through the Public Sector Surplus Land Trawl on the 24th March 2022. The Housing Executive expressed an interest to develop the site for social housing. Apex Housing Association were subsequently nominated to progress the site and are currently in the process of carrying out their own feasibility studies. Derry City & Strabane District Council have expressed an interest in a potential collaborative approach with Apex Housing for a housing led regeneration plan for the site. Apex are keen to explore this opportunity further and are in the process of arranging meetings with DC&SD Council to discuss the potential for a joint approach. Within the context of the Community Plan the Housing Executive is also working with DC&SD Council to look at options for the redevelopment of the site, this will include the provision of social/affordable housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how his Department is maximising the tourism potential of Newtownstewart.
answer_text: Titanic Belfast and the Giant’s Causeway have been transformational in increasing Northern Ireland’s appeal and driving up visitor numbers. The goal is to build out further from these and create compelling multi-day experiences that entice more people to visit, and experience more of what we have to offer right across Northern Ireland.   To deliver on this objective a new tourism brand, ‘Northern Ireland – Embrace a Giant Spirit’, was launched by Tourism NI in November 2019. Research conducted by Tourism NI suggests that this new brand has the potential to increase by 30% the number of people who may be interested in visiting Northern Ireland. If areas across Northern Ireland, including Newtownstewart and the wider Derry City & Strabane District Council area, are to be successful in securing these additional visitors they need to have clear and compelling reasons for visitors to travel to those areas.Tourism NI is currently working with experience providers across Northern Ireland, to develop their products and services so they can meet the expectations of both domestic and international visitors. This includes several in the Derry City & Strabane District Council area and wider Sperrins area, and includes the Wild Atlantic Distillery, Claudy, near Newtownstewart.   Tourism NI has a close working relationship with each of the eleven local authorities across Northern Ireland. To exploit the potential of the new Embrace a Giant Spirit brand and support regional dispersal, Tourism NI has worked with the local authorities across NI to appoint a tourism brand ambassador within each region, including Derry City & Strabane District Council. Tourism NI now hosts bi-monthly meetings with all local authority brand ambassadors and meets regularly with individual local authorities to identify and work with tourism experiences which are best placed to meet those expectations and create compelling reasons for visitors to travel across Northern Ireland. As part of Tourism NI’s regular marketing campaign, accommodation providers, experiences, and visitor attractions in the Newtownstewart area are encouraged to provide offers to promote and underpin the campaign, which are then displayed on the consumer website: www.discovernorthernireland.com, alongside hosting product listings.Newtownstewart Golf Club is also one of Tourism NI’s accredited and quality assured golf courses and features not only on the discovernorthernireland.com  website but also within Tourism NI’s consumer and trade golf guide publications. As one of Tourism NI’s quality assured courses, Newtownstewart Golf Club featured as part the 2021 “It is officially tee time” campaign, which was delivered via on social media channels. In August 2021, a copy of the latest Tourism NI Golf Marketing Toolkit was sent to all quality assured golf courses, including Newtownstewart, inviting and encouraging golf clubs to work with Tourism NI in promotional opportunities. 	Tourism NI is providing supp

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the upgrading of Seskinore Forest.
answer_text: In my response to your correspondence in November 2021, I advised that you should consider liaising with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council in relation to their discussions and proposals for Seskinore Forest. This was in anticipation of my officials writing to all Councils early in 2022 to offer them a further opportunity to submit an Expression of Interest for enhancement works to Forest Parks in their Council area, which are funded through my Departments Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Programme budget. I can confirm that, whilst no application was received from Fermanagh and Omagh District Council for an enhancement project at Seskinore Forest, FODC did submit two Expression of Interest’s for other projects in the Council area, namely Lough Navar, near Derrygonnelly and Ecclesville, Fintona.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how her Department plans to increase youth engagement in rural parts of West Tyrone.
answer_text: In line with Departmental Youth Policy as set out in the policy document Priorities for Youth, youth service provision is based on assessed need. The Local Assessment of Need 2020 – 2023 and associated Local Development Plans for Fermanagh and Omagh and Strabane and Derry District Council areas can be accessed via the following link –  https://eanifunding.org.uk/local-assessment-of-need Youth engagement and participation is core to youth work and the EA Youth Service (EAYS) has established a formal participation structure ‘Youth Voice’ which allows young people in a specific local Council area to voice their opinions and ideas, participate in consultations and ensure that key issues are brought to the attention of the EAYS and District Councils. A Project Board has recently been established for a Youth Voice within the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council Area.  Mid Ulster and Derry City and Strabane Councils are also participating in the Youth Voice initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how her Department is promoting the sport of skateboarding.
answer_text: Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department, engages with a range of sports governing bodies to support the development of sport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has provided funding for skate parks.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 30087/17-22.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department plans to enhance mental health services in Strabane.
answer_text: As advised in my response to AQW 780/22-27, mental health has been one of my top priorities since becoming Health Minister.  In June 2021, I launched the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 which is aimed at enhancing and ensuring equity of service provision across all areas in Northern Ireland, including Strabane.		My Department’s Mental Health Action Plan, published in 2020, also included key regional investments for the Mental Health Champion, Perinatal services, Managed Care Networks for Forensic Services and CAMHS, as well as a Mental Health Innovation Fund.  		In 2021/22, I also announced funding of up to £16m through the Mental Health Support Fund for those charitable organisations that offer support to people with mental ill health throughout all of Northern Ireland.  A full list of the successful applicants to the Fund, together with the amounts awarded to them, can be found via the following links:Mental Health Support Fund Round 1 Successful ApplicantsMental Health Support Fund Additional Funding and Successful Applicants It should be noted that while organisations may be located in certain constituency areas, many of the funded projects offer services across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is taking action to deal with road surface ponding on the Fyfin Road and Strabane Road, Castlederg.
answer_text: My Department’s current policy is to clean road gullies on the public road network once a year. Some of the gullies on the Fyfin Road were cleaned on 10 February and 16 March 2022 respectively with the remainder of those on Fyfin Road and Strabane Road scheduled to be cleaned over the coming weeks.My officials advise that a programme to clean gully surrounds and outlets is also on-going and will continue subject to the availability of resources and other priorities.My Department also has plans for a scheme on the Fyfin Road in the vicinity of Grove Road to address a localised drainage issue. The timing of commencement of this work will be largely determined by the timing of the award of the interim resurfacing contract for the Strabane area, which it is hoped will take place shortly.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at  LondonderryandStrabane@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department's policy for grass cutting in residential developments.
answer_text: AQW 1242/22-27The budget for grass cutting, and therefore our approach, cannot be confirmed until an overall budget for 2022-23 has been agreed.  However, my Department has commenced a programme of grass cutting that is similar to the previous year which is two cuts across the road network, along with some additional cutting at sightlines for road safety purposes.For clarity, the Department is not responsible for maintaining all verges or grassed areas, particularly those in urban areas. These may be the responsibility of the Housing Executive, Councils or some other private arrangement, such as a management company within a residential development.In support of the All-Ireland Pollinator Plan, which contains a specific action ‘to promote pollinator-friendly management of the national road network in NI’, the Department has been engaged in the ‘Don’t Mow Let It Grow’ pilot project in conjunction with Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council, Northern Ireland Environment Agency and others.  Details of this project can be found at: https://dontmowletitgrow.com/. Going forward I am keen that our grass cutting policy supports this plan more widely.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the cutting of grass verges along roads in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department’s current policy is to cut all grass on roadside verges twice during the growing season. This year’s grass cutting programme is well underway with A and B class roads being dealt with first followed by the unclassified road network.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact the Section Office which covers the relevant section of West Tyrone: FermanaghandOmaghEast@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk or LondonderryandStrabane@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the availability of an out of hours GP in Strabane in each of the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the future of GP provision in Strabane.
answer_text: There are three GP Practices in Strabane.  Two of these practices are located in the Health Centre, Upper Main Street, Strabane and one is located in Mourneside Medical Centre, Ballycolman Avenue.In recent years a new model for primary care Multi-Disciplinary Teams has been developed in a number of practices, including the GP Practices in Strabane. General Practice Pharmacists also work in the practices alongside GPs, nurses and other practice based staff as part of a multidisciplinary team.    With regards to premises, an Outline Business Case is currently being developed by the Western HSC Trust as part of the Derry & Strabane City Deal, for a new primary care health hub in Strabane which will provide an opportunity for improved accommodation for the three GP Practices located in Strabane.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department plans on improving (i) child; and (ii) adult respite services in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) has continued to undertake assessments of need in respect of Short Breaks and has continued to offer both traditional bed-based Short Breaks and Direct Payments in lieu of Short Breaks. The latter particularly being offered in those situations where access to a Short Breaks has not been available. (i) Due to the limited availability of bed based Short Breaks over the last two years the frequency of access to this service has been determined by the social work assessment of need.  The service holds fortnightly panels to allocate places within the Short Breaks service.   Allocation of traditional break beds is carried out as equitable as possible so that where possible carers have some level of short break provision.The WHSCT has 2 Residential short breaks facilities for children, both of which have been unavailable for short breaks recently. This has been due to a need to respond to placement breakdowns by providing urgent residential accommodation in Avalon and an interim placement for a high needs/high risk child in Rosebud. The Trust recognises the stress this places on children with disability and their families and is working on reopening Rosebud for short breaks this month. Work is ongoing to seek alternative long term accommodation for those children currently residing in Avalon with a view to providing residential short breaks at the earliest opportunity.The Trust is actively working on plans to develop capacity for emergency placement breakdowns and for children needing medium to long term residential care which will include developing a hybrid model for short term and long term capacity in one of their facilities as well as developing a new Respite care service similar to Respite Foster care. The Trust is liaising with RQIA and the Department on these plans. Children’s Disability managers have been meeting monthly with Rosebud parents since December 2021 to keep them informed of progress and have also now had two meetings with Avalon parents.(ii) Adult day care and short breaks services are currently below pre-pandemic capacity, therefore I have asked my officials to provide regular updates on service provision levels. Data on day care, short breaks and transport is provided on the Department’s website and will be updated on a monthly basis. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesTo improve the level of provision my officials have worked with the Public Health Agency to develop a Pathway for remobilisation and issued updated public health guidance (also available at the above link), which will enable Trusts to better balance Covid-19 considerations with wider risks to the mental health and wellbeing of service users, families and carers. My officials have agreed to meet a joint All Party Group on Carers and Learning Disability on 14 September 2022, to provide a further update on remobilisation and the work undertaken by the Department to manage Cov

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department will assist domiciliary care workers in the private sector who are facing increasing fuel costs.
answer_text: You will be aware that on 24 November I announced a support package of up to £23m for the independent domiciliary and wider social care sector.  This additional funding has been provided with the expectation that providers will not only maintain service levels, but increase capacity.  Despite a very challenging financial position I have prioritised funding this year to continue supporting this sector.While the Department cannot mandate what levels of pay, or terms and conditions employers should offer, including in regards to mileage or travel costs, I have been clear that the additional funding must be used to stabilise and enhance capacity across the sector to see us through the current challenging period.  The additional funding is being utilised by employers to offer enhanced rates of pay and better terms and conditions, which may also include increased mileage rates, at individual employers’ discretion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current provision of respite services for (i) children; and (ii) adults in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) Due to the limited availability of bed based Short Breaks over the last two years the frequency of access to this service has been determined by the social work assessment of need.  The service holds fortnightly panels to allocate places within the Short Breaks service.  Allocation of traditional break beds is carried out as equitable as possible so that carers have some level of short break provision.At present, the Western Trust is not delivering any residential short breaks for children within Rosebud due to its temporary closure. Unfortunately, there are currently no residential short breaks available within Avalon either due to the Trust responding to emergency situations. Residential short breaks are available to children with complex health care needs within Butterfly Lodge at Altnagelvin. Additionally, the Trust can provide two overnight breaks once a fortnight for two children with complex health care needs in this setting who would usually get this break in Avalon. In place of residential short breaks for children, the Trust is offering outreach to families within community facilities.Provision of Short Break Services for Adults with Learning Disability is detailed below:Types of Short BreaksFacility/ServiceBeds/PlacesStatutory Residential RespiteThe Adult Respite Cottage, LondonderrySpruce House, AltnagelvinBeltany House, Omagh4 beds2 beds3 beds Independent Nursing HomesOwen Mor Nursing Home, LondonderryPrivate Nursing homes within the WHSCT area4 bedsVacant beds can be accessed for short breaks as and when  available and within available fundingIndependent Residential HomesDrumary House, DerrygonnellyPrivate Residential homes within the WHSCT area1 BedVacant beds can be accessed for short breaks as and when  available and within available fundingShort-term Adult Placement ServiceOvernight and day hours are  provided by Host CarersThe Trust has a number of host carers providing this service across the Trust areaDirect PaymentsRecurring and one-off direct payments are made directly to parents/carers within available fundingDomiciliary CareNight Sitting Service – A small number of  families are currently receiving this serviceTo meet the future service needs, the HSCB (now the Department’s Strategic Planning & Performance Group) developed a Regional Framework for Children’s Disability Services, which proposes a new overarching model of care for children with a disability in Northern Ireland. This draft framework outlines a new model of practice based on the principles of: (i) early intervention; (ii) collaboration and multi-agency working; (iii) co-design and decision making with families; and (iv) person centred care.The implementation of the framework will be underpinned by four strategic work streams, which will require significant transformation across Health & Social Care services:Improving our early offer of help and community based supports; Developing additional effective residential short breaks, fostering and intens

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of social workers employed by each Health and Social Care Trust; and how many of these social workers are temporary or agency staff.
answer_text: Each HSC Trust has provided the following current social worker figures for staff directly employed by them.HSC TrustContract TypeHeadcountWhole-time equivalentBelfastPermanent contract1,004960.82Temporary contract4438.96NorthernPermanent contract895836.52Temporary contract5750.19South EasternPermanent contract789740.95Temporary contract2520.66SouthernPermanent contract762706.27Temporary contract3321.29WesternPermanent contract918883.22Temporary contract112104.24Each HSC Trust has provided the following figures for posts that are being covered by social workers employed by agencies to work in the Trusts.HSC TrustCount of social worker posts being covered by agency workers (whole-time equivalent)Belfast60.98Northern36.00South Eastern34.10Southern4.00Western15.00

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he will increase capacity on the National Grid to ensure that both business and domestic properties can benefit from air source heat pumps.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy sets out a vision for Northern Ireland across power, heat and transport to ensure we achieve net zero carbon and affordable energy for all.Network planning and capacity to facilitate increased electrification is a matter for Northern Ireland Electricity Networks (NIEN) and the Utility Regulator (UR).  UR has commenced the next Price Control, RP7, which will set the amount of investment in the network which NIEN is allowed to recover from consumers over the five years from April 2024 and will be crucial in delivering the Energy Strategy. UR has recently consulted its approach to RP7 and published its Forward Work Plan, both of which can be found on UR’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how her Department plans on (i) tackling homelessness among people aged 25 years and under; and (ii) ensuring they are not housed in temporary accommodation for the long-term.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has recently published the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, which, outlines its overall approach to addressing homelessness.  The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 is supported by annual action plans, with a number of actions being directly relevant to those aged 25 years and under.The Housing Executive has also recently published independent research on Experiences of Youth Homelessness, which, will guide further actions in delivering this strategy.  The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 is the draft Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation, which, was subject to public consultation in early 2022.  A final version of the Action Plan is due for publication in the coming months and will outline the Housing Executive’s efforts to support those households who avail of temporary accommodation.The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, it’s Year 1 Action Plan, Experiences of Youth Homelessness and, Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation are all available on the Housing Executive’s website (www.nihe.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she has plans to extend the Sure Start programme into aditional areas that are facing deprivation.
answer_text: The Sure Start programme is delivered via 38 Sure Start projects, in (at least) the 25% most disadvantaged areas in Northern Ireland (NI), as measured by the NI Multiple Deprivation Measure (NIMDM) 2010; research indicates this is where targeted early intervention can be most effective. Provision is universal within the Sure Start catchment areas, therefore all children under four (and their families) in these areas, can access services.Increased Department of Education (DE) funding of £1.56m in 2021/22 and 2022/23 is facilitating  temporary extension of services to an additional 22 Super Output Areas falling within the 25% most disadvantaged areas when applying the latest (2017) NIMDM.To ensure optimum early intervention, DE will take account of the findings of the ongoing Independent Review of Targeted Early Years Interventions (including Sure Start and its geographical scope), which is expected to complete by autumn 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the need to introduce a non-taxable child payment benefit, given the current cost of living crisis.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider a future mitigation package. As part of this work the independent panel were specifically asked to consider the impact of welfare reforms on household income, including on children.		The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the delivery of an Anti-Poverty Strategy with specific and measured child poverty reduction targets.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead a significant programme of work in the co-design of the Executive’s social inclusion strategies. 		The drafting of the strategies is ongoing in readiness for presentation to an incoming Executive

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department is aware of poor mobile phone coverage in Strabane town.
answer_text: From the Office of the Minister 	Gordon Lyons MLA		As you are aware, telecommunications policy is a reserved matter, the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) leads on improvements to mobile connectivity in rural areas across the UK. The Department for the Economy (DfE) maintains regular contact with DCMS on all telecommunications matters ensuring that all local considerations are front and centre of any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of mobile connectivity in Northern Ireland.DfE does not actively monitor the quality of mobile phone signal across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will review the eligibility criteria for school uniform grants given the current cost of living crisis.
answer_text: Uniform Grants are available to families who meet certain qualifying criteria: https://www.eani.org.uk/financial-help/free-school-meals-uniform-grants/free-school-meals-uniform-grant-eligibilityThe Department has commenced a Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The review is considering a number of options for free school meals and uniform grants, including the options for universal free school meal provision for certain year groups or all pupils and the removal/adjustment to current thresholds for Universal Credit/Working Tax Credit as well as the potential to legislate on uniform policy.   My Department continues to work to develop costed options and a consultation is planned to run over the Autumn 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will re-establish the steering group for Phase 2 of the new Omagh Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Health & Social Care Trust has advised that the business case development group is being reformed in order to refresh the 2019 outline business case for resubmission to my Department for review.Membership of this group is made up of Trust representatives from services, Strategic Capital Investment, Finance and Central Procurement Directorate – Health Projects with input from others as required. The project’s Senior Responsible Owner oversees the work of the group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will advance cross border health care initiatives.
answer_text: Cross border health care initiatives are generally advanced through the North South Ministerial Council (NSMC), the Cooperation and Working Together (CAWT) partnership and through local Service Level Agreements between relevant providers in both jurisdictions, where it is demonstrated that there is a clear population health need that could be sustainably met through a collaborative approach and without detriment to current service provision.  The NSMC Health Sectoral work programme focuses on Accident and Emergency Planning; Major emergencies; Co-operation on High Technology Equipment; Cancer Research; Health Promotion and; from 2008 onwards, Child Protection.CAWT is the cross-border health and social care partnership for the Southern and Western Health and Social Care Trusts, my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group, the Public Health Agency and the Health Service Executive in the Republic of Ireland.  CAWT seeks to add value to existing health and social care activity by bringing a cross border dimension to on-going collaboration between the health systems in both jurisdictions and accessing EU funding in support of such activities where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is advancing mental healthcare provision in rural West Tyrone.
answer_text: Mental health has been one of my top priorities since becoming Health Minister.  In June 2021, I launched the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 which is aimed at enhancing and ensuring equity of service provision across all areas in Northern Ireland, including West Tyrone.		My Department’s Mental Health Action Plan, published in 2020, also included key regional investments for the Mental Health Champion, Perinatal services, Managed Care Networks for Forensic Services and CAMHS, as well as a Mental Health Innovation Fund.  		In 2021/22, I also announced funding of up to £16m through the Mental Health Support Fund for those charitable organisations that offer support to people with mental ill health throughout all of Northern Ireland.  A full list of the successful applicants to the Fund, together with the amounts awarded to them, can be found via the following links:Mental Health Support Fund Round 1 Successful ApplicantsMental Health Support Fund Additional Funding and Successful Applicants It should be noted that while organisations may be located in certain constituency areas, many of the funded projects offer services across Northern Ireland.		My Department has made a number of investments that are specific to West Tyrone, including capital funding of £400k in June 2021 towards further improvements to mental health services at the Tyrone and Fermanagh Hospital in Omagh.  This investment was in addition to the £1m that was provided in previous financial years to help improve mental health services at the hospital.  	In addition, I am pleased to note that the Western Trust is currently engaged with the Pathfinder Initiative in relation to the expansion of the Talking Therapies and Well-Being Hub into the southern sector of the Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the North West Cancer Centre.
answer_text: Since opening in November 2016, the North West Cancer Centre (NWCC), has provided  radiotherapy treatment for over 4,300 patients from both Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, with over 1000 patients treated in 2021 and over 400 patients treated year to date (up until end April 2022).   NWCC continues to deliver advanced chemotherapy techniques, provide day care treatment for oncology and haematology patients and provides patients with the newest forms of targeted anti-cancer therapies, including immunotherapy.  These therapies can be more effective and have less associated toxicities than some standard forms of chemotherapy.NWCC has a dedicated oncology/haematology inpatient ward with a dedicated team of on-site consultants and speciality doctors.  In addition, there is a dedicated triage service, which continues to play a critical role in the provision of advice and management to patients across haematology and oncology and reduces the need for patients to go through the Emergency Department for access.NWCC is an example of the benefits of cross border partnership working and continues to be a success story in the delivery of high quality, safe and effective cancer care for patients from both Northern Ireland and from the Republic of Ireland.  Patient and carer feedback continues to echo this message with patients clearly stating the benefit of a service ‘closer to home’ which avoids the need to travel long distance for access to specialist treatments and provides a significant benefit in patients now being close to families and friends while undergoing treatment.NWCC also achieved reference site “centre of excellence” status with Varian Medical Systems in 2018, has previously been a reference site for IBA Dosimetry and is currently entering into a reference site agreement with the Sun Nuclear Corporation. The centre achieved and has subsequently maintained accreditation to CHKS ISO 9001 in November 2018.  Radiotherapy Physics gained accreditation to UKAS MPACE BS 70000 in 2020 and is currently only one of 3 departments in the U.K. to achieve this specific Medical Physics and associated scientific services accreditation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there have been any changes to the estimated costs for delivering the Strabane Public Realm Scheme since it was originally submitted for approval.
answer_text: My Department has received a number of iterations of the Strabane Public Realm Scheme appraisal from the Council, with an increase in the estimated costs for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there have been any design changes to the Strabane Public Realm Scheme since the original application was submitted.
answer_text: Following statutory and public consultations some design changes were made to the original design proposal for the Strabane Public Realm Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the reasons for the delay in approving the Strabane Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: The Department has received a number of iterations of the Strabane Public Realm Scheme appraisal from the Council over the past months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the budget available for the Strabane Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: The absence of a functioning Executive and an agreed budget is resulting in difficulties with Departmental planning in many areas. Once Departmental capital budgets are confirmed, this project will be considered alongside other Departmental responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a timeframe for the delivery of the Strabane Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: The timeframe for the delivery of the Strabane Public Realm Scheme will be dependent on securing the necessary approvals and funding.  The draft timeframe for delivery is scheduled from 2023 to 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the availability of out of hours GP services in Strabane in each month of the last three years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting lists for addiction services in Omagh.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on wait times for addiction services in Omagh. However, it does hold information on wait times for addiction services for the wider Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) area; the wait times are detailed in the table below.Table 1: Number of active waits, by time bands, for addiction services, by WHSCT, as at 30th April 20221.HSC Trust0-3 weeks3-6 weeks6-9 weeks9-13 weeksOver 13 weeksWHSCT118792970Source: Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).1 Figures are taken from management systems and are subject to change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the All Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: In July 2021, ARUP were appointed to take forward the All Island Strategic Rail Review. My officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Transport and ARUP to ensure that the review is progressed whilst ensuring that stakeholders are engaged appropriately with the process. Following the closure of the public consultation, it is anticipated that the review will be completed in the Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Phase 2 development of Omagh Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust submitted a proposal for Phase 2 Development of Omagh (Centre for Mental Health) as part of a recent capital planning review exercise for the period 2022 - 2032. Confirmation of funding and timescales is dependent on Business Case approval and allocation of capital funding by my Department. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care, and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.  However, my ability to strategically plan capital investment is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the expansion of rail services from Derry to Strabane and Omagh.
answer_text: You will be aware of the ongoing work in relation to the All-Island Strategic Rail Review. The Review will allow us to consider our rail network across this island to view how it can be improved for everyone. It will examine the potential for rail connections across the island including how our existing connections could be improved and where new rail links would be best placed. It will also examine how we can connect our rail network to areas such as from Derry to Strabane and Omagh, our international gateways through our ports and airports, the potential for transporting freight using the rail network whilst also considering how we can move to a low or zero emission rail network. It is anticipated that the review will be completed in the autumn of 2022.My Department is also currently developing the new Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan (RSTNTP) which will set out priorities for investment in our road, rail and inter-urban limited stop bus network, including park & ride, through to 2035. This will, in association with Translink, include high level assessments on a variety of potential rail improvements. The RSTNTP will be subject to public consultation and I will be pleased to consider representations on the plan including those in relation to the rail network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of vacant nursing posts in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of registered nursing vacancies actively being recruited to in each Health & Social Care (HSC) Trust at 31 March 2022 is detailed in the table below.HSC TrustRegistered Nursing HSC Vacancies at 31 March 2022Belfast601Northern378South Eastern286Southern391Western291Source: HSC TrustsAs Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.I remain committed to sustained investment in growing the local nursing and midwifery workforce to meet ever increasing demands, for example, this year we have commissioned pre-registration nursing and midwifery training places at a record high level, (1,335). This is alongside a highly successful International Nurse Recruitment campaign through which, 1,023 nurses have been recruited as at 30/04/2022.Whilst recruitment is an operational matter for Health and Social Care Trusts as employers, I can advise that there were 16,772.7 WTE registered nursing & midwifery HSC staff in post at 31st March 2022. This represented an increase of 397 WTE (or 2.4%) from the previous March or an increase of 1,660 WTE (or 11%) from the position at 31st March 2018.Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a priority & the Department has undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures, however, I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on all new build social housing schemes (i) under construction; and (ii) planned, in West Tyrone.
answer_text: In the West Tyrone Parliamentary Constituency: There are currently 9 schemes / 283 social housing units under construction. There are 6 schemes / 62 social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) during the period 2022/23 – 2024/25. The Bidding Round for the new SHDP for the period 2023/24 – 2025/26 is due to take place from June to August 2022, and further schemes may be added in the West Tyrone Parliamentary Constituency in due course.Please note: that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP on an ‘in-year’ basis, through the Competitive Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / or Off-the-Shelf properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he plans on increasing GP numbers, particularly in practices that serve rural communities.
answer_text: I am very aware of the challenges that face General Practice and I recognise that these issues are often particularly felt in rural areas. My Department is continuing to work closely with GP representatives to consider how best to respond to these challenges.  I recently announced that my Department would be increasing the number of GP training places for 2022/23 by 10, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year. This announcement builds on my Department’s continuing work to invest in our GP workforce which has seen the number of GPs trainees increase by over 70% from 2015 levels.  This will help stabilise our GP workforce and ensure it is sustainable for the future.  I recognise that GPs’ experiences during clinical placements can have a significant influence on their future career decisions.  In that regard, the commencement of the Graduate Entry Programme at the Ulster University’s Medical School is a significant development in the provision of medical education in Northern Ireland.  The curriculum of this Graduate Entry Programme places a very significant emphasis on Primary Care placements, with a high concentration of clinical placements in rural settings in the west.  This will help to increase the supply of local students who may be attracted to pursue a career in General Practice in these areas.Similarly, it is important that incoming GPs are reassured that they will be part of a wider primary care team since it has become increasingly evident in recent years that the increase in demand for primary care services cannot be met solely by increasing the number of GPs.  Other elements which my Department continues to work to introduce include the wider roll-out of primary care Multi-disciplinary Teams (MDT), Advanced Nurse Practitioners and additional General Practice Nurses, all of which are making a difference to how services are delivered in primary care and contributing to improved patient outcomes.  I recently announced the further roll out of multi-disciplinary teams to three areas including the south west.  I am committed to extending the MDT model as quickly as possible; however, the speed at which this can be achieved will depend on the availability of appropriate funding (including capital funding) and suitably qualified and experienced staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GP practices that have written to his Department outlining concerns around staffing, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not routinely record this information.  However, the Department is very aware of the staffing pressures, both clinical and administrative, which GP practices are facing.  My Department will continue to work closely with GP representatives to consider how best to respond to the challenges which face practices in Northern Ireland and to ensure that we have a GP workforce that is supported, motivated and sustainable, and which continues to provide quality care to patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of vacant GP posts in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of vacant GP posts is not available.  GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department of Health to provide primary medical services to their patients.  As independent contractors, GPs are responsible for identifying and recruiting the number of GPs and other staff required to deliver those services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the areas in West Tyrone that will benefit from the Shared Rural Network project.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Shared Rural Network (SRN), is a project developed and delivered by the UK’s four mobile network operators (EE, Vodafone, O2 and Three) and UK Government, it will deliver 4G coverage to 95% of the UK, enabling rural businesses and communities to thrive.The SRN, when implemented as planned, will make 4G mobile broadband available to rural communities across Northern Ireland. As part of the legally binding commitments set out within the SRN, mobile coverage in Northern Ireland will reach 98% from at least one operator, and 85% from all four operators compared to 97% and 79% respectively today.The Mobile Network Operators are currently in discussions with relevant Councils with regards to planning applications required to deliver their network improvements. Once these discussions have been completed, they intend to share more information on the locations that will benefit from the Shared Rural Network project. The proposed improvements in Northern Ireland are still currently programmed to be completed by June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the expansion of Ulster University, Magee Campus.
answer_text: Ulster University welcomed its first cohort of students to its Graduate Entry Medical School in August 2021, and will enrol a further intake for Academic Year 2022/23 as well as students on its undergraduate Health Sciences programme which is relocating from the Jordanstown campus.My Department is in discussion with the University in relation to its strategic objectives for growth across all its campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's policy for installing speed humps in residential areas.
answer_text: My Department’s approach to the provision of  traffic calming measures is set out in Policy and Procedure Guide ‘RsPPG E027 – Road Safety Engineering Procedures’ which is publically available at: Road Safety Engineering Procedures RSPPG E027 (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider establishing a resurfacing scheme for unadopted roads.
answer_text: The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, confers legal authority on my Department to carry out works on those public roads that it maintains. This Order does not provide any legal authority to permit my Department to undertake resurfacing works on unadopted or private roads.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on all unadopted roads in residential areas in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information by constituency but rather holds it by Council area. However, we still hold information for the old Council boundary areas of Omagh and Strabane which broadly equates to the West Tyrone constituency area.Listed below are the un-adopted development/private streets sites which are at various stages of completion ranging from planning permission recently granted to largely complete.  Developments qualify for reductions in the bond value of 50%, 70%, Preliminary Certificate (90%) and full adoption, according to what stage the development has reached. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the action his Department is taking to ensure the safe migration of fish in waterways at Sion Mills.
answer_text: DAERA Inland Fisheries Branch is responsible under Section 54 of the Fisheries Act (NI) 1966 to ensure, a fish pass sufficient to permit the free passage through any dam of salmon, trout and eels is provided and maintained by the owner of the dam.  In addition, the Act provides mechanisms for the Department to authorise exemptions from complying with the Act under Section 58, pertaining to the exemption from the requirement of a twenty four hour weekly closure of abstraction sites during the weekend and Section 59, pertaining to the exemption from the requirement to screen abstractions at the point of the intake and outflow from the river.Inland Fisheries Branch is currently considering a renewal application for exemptions under Sections 58 and 59 at the site on the River Mourne at Sion Mills. As part of this process, Inland Fisheries staff have conducted a number of site visits and have had discussions with the operator. The Department has a Technical Advisory Group that assess any exemption applications. Exemption permits have clearly defined and legally enforceable conditions that will ensure fish migration and fish passage at the site. The previous exemption had a condition that required the hydro operator to continue to abstract water and return it to the river unused, during the peak of the smolt run to enhance the downstream passage of smolts at the weir, especially during low flows.DAERA Inland Fisheries Branch will continue to monitor the site and respond to any reports of fish passage issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the flood alleviation scheme for Ballycolman, Strabane.
answer_text: The proposals to alleviate flooding to properties in the Ballycolman Estate comprise two options:The short term option involves the re-profiling of the road in front of the affected properties directing excess flood water away from the houses and temporarily depositing it onto lands owned by Derry City and Strabane District Council at the Melvin Arena; andThe long term option involves the construction of a new large diameter storm drain through the Estate to an outfall to the Mourne River. These works are to be carried out in conjunction with other works to be carried out on behalf of NI Water.Detailed design work and discussions with Council’s technical staff in respect of the short term option have been ongoing over recent months and after agreement on certain issues, was recently achieved and the matter went before the Council's Environment & Regeneration Committee on 11th May 2022 for approval. I am pleased to advise that approval was granted subject to a legal agreement being established between both parties. As soon as the agreement is formally signed off my Department will, subject to availability of funding, be in a position to proceed with the works associated with the short term option.With regard to the longer term option my officials have instructed Amey Consulting Engineers to carry out detailed design work, in conjunction with NI Water, with the intention of initiating the procurement process in late 2023. However I must stress progression of the work associated with the longer term option will be subject to funding being available at that time.I am advised that NI Water’s Designer and Contractor partners are currently gathering the extensive site information required for the proposed tunnelling of a gravity sewer. Given the risks involved with tunnelling in a congested environment, site investigations will involve pit excavations, service diversions, numerous "slit" trenches and extensive CCTV surveys of existing sewers to ensure that no existing underground services are damaged.The Site Investigations stage is expected to be completed by early September 2022 and this will allow detailed design of the scheme to be finalised. Liaison between NI Water and DfI Roads officials will continue to ensure all issues are accounted for in the final proposal.NI Water expects to have details of the costs for delivering this project available by October 2022, however, any NI Water investment would be subject to prioritisation and the availability of funding during the PC21 Price Control period. This potential project is not within NI Water's Price Control 21 Final Determination as agreed with the Utility Regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on rewilding roadside grass verges in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department does not currently have a specific policy relating to rewilding of roadside verges but does work at a local level with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, which has taken the lead in this initiative, to identify areas which can be allowed to grow wild without adversely impacting on road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on all road resurfacing programmes planned for West Tyrone in the 2022/23 financial year.
answer_text: Regrettably the resurfacing programme for the 2022/23 financial year cannot be finalised until the Department’s annual budget has been confirmed. When finalised, details of the programmes for the Fermanagh & Omagh and Derry City & Strabane Council areas will be included within my Department’s respective Council Reports that will be presented to those Councils, then published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on remediation action following the Meenbog landslide.
answer_text: My response to your AQW 28105/17-22 refers, and is still current.While work continues, much of this matter is at present understood to be sub-judice.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will revisit a flood alleviation scheme for Newtownstewart.
answer_text: As you may be aware, following the significant flood event that affected large areas of the Northwest in August 2017, my Department carried out a flood study to assess the flood risk to Newtownstewart from the River Strule.  The aim of the study was to assess the level of flood risk to properties in the town and to identify if there were any viable engineering solutions to alleviate this risk.  Unfortunately the study, even after being reconsidered further, has shown that a flood alleviation scheme at this location would not be cost beneficial and at present there are no proposals to carry out a further review.   However my Department remains committed to carrying out watercourse maintenance and regular screen inspections in the area, to help ensure that there is no increased risk of flooding.In addition domestic property owners can still avail of the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme run by my Department.  Successful applicants receive 90% of the costs (up to £10,000) to modify their properties to make them more resistant to flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) original estimate; and (ii) current estimate of the costs for completing the Strule Shared Education Campus, Omagh.
answer_text: As previously advised on 25th March 2022, AQW 31013/17-22, the estimated total cost of completing the Strule Shared Education Campus in 2016 was £169m. The current estimated cost is £232m which includes the costs of construction, including a contingency for construction price inflation, risk and optimism bias; staffing the Programme; and other running costs, including support being provided to the schools for shared curricular development and implementation.  It should be noted that the 2016 figure did not include an allowance for inflation.Costs will continue to be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the rollout of HEPA air filtration units in schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority Maintenance Service received requests from 59 schools for air filtration units. All 59 schools were assessed on site by maintenance staff, resulting in 46 schools requiring air filtration units. 200 air filtration units have been distributed to date across the Education estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on what action is being taken to address parking concerns at St Conor's Primary School, Omagh.
answer_text: My officials met with Omagh & Fermanagh District Council and St Conor's Primary School in December 2021 and February 2022 to discuss the potential acquisition of land at the front of the school to facilitate a solution to the traffic management issues.  These discussions have however been put on hold as a solution within the school grounds is now being investigated; this has to be ruled out before my Department can proceed with any acquisition of additional land.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on planning application LA11/2018/0186/O, under consideration by his Department.
answer_text: Since the call in of this application by my Department from Derry City and Strabane District Council under Section 29 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (the 2011 Act), officials have determined that the applicant has failed to comply with the necessary requirements of the statutory pre-application community consultation process. The Department wrote to the applicant on 11 May 2022 to advise of this and seek that this be remedied. In accordance with section 50 of 2011 Act the Department has a duty to decline to determine a planning application where, in its opinion, section 27 of the 2011 Act has not been complied with.  Therefore, until such times that the section 27 requirements of the pre application community consultation process has been met, my Department cannot further progress this planning application.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Strule Shared Education Campus, Omagh.
answer_text: The procurement competition for the Main Works construction is continuing following release of the Invitation to Tender on 13 December 2021.On the current programme timetable, subject to all necessary approvals being in place, contract award is planned for early 2023. Assuming successful appointment of a main works contractor and following the pre-construction design period, campus construction is due to begin on site in the spring of 2023 and is currently planned to complete early 2026, with potential partial opening of some facilities in academic year 2025/26 and full campus opening planned for September 2026. The delivery of all remaining construction projects is also progressing, including the Gortin Road/Mountjoy Road scheme which was approved by Fermanagh and Omagh District Council Planning Committee in February 2022.Work is continuing across all other projects and workstreams including detailing the work required to realise the educational benefits and pilot various shared education initiatives prior to go-live, the development of the arrangements for the ownership, governance, funding and management of the Campus and working with existing school site owners to consider how best to plan for and manage the future use and/or disposal of the existing sites following relocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the provision of a pedestrian crossing at St. Columba's Primary School, Clady.
answer_text: Whilst an initial site survey has indicated that demand at this location does not meet the required threshold for provision of a controlled crossing, my officials will continue to monitor the site to see if there are any other suitable measures that could assist pedestrians crossing the road.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on plans to address parking congestion at Sion Mills Primary School.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) have informed me that the technical Feasibility Report has now been received from the Design Team and following a review, which they hope to complete by the end of May 2022, this will inform the development of the Business Case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware of the staff shortages facing the Dromore GP practice; and what his Department will do to ensure the long term future of the practice.
answer_text: I am aware of the situation regarding the Dromore and Trillick GP Practice and appreciate that this is an unsettling time for patients of the practice.  My Department (including officials in the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, previously the Health and Social Care Board) has worked with the practice and the GP Crisis Response Team to ensure the continuation of GMS services at the practice until 30 June 2022.  My Department continues to work with the practice and GP representatives regarding its longer term future.  Patients and local representatives will be kept informed of further developments but in the interim patients should continue to contact the Practice as usual.  My Department is working closely with the Western Local Medical Committee and the South West GP Federation (Fermanagh and Tyrone area) to consider how best to respond to challenges facing General Practice in the Federation area, including Dromore and Trillick.  This is part of a wider approach to supporting rural practices that includes funding for skills mix as well as additional nursing and pharmacy support.  Funding has been made available in 2022/23 to the South West Federation to train four Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs).  These ANPs would be a resource for all practices in the area, including Dromore and Trillick.  In addition, funding has been made available to the Federation to increase the General Practice Pharmacist input to the Dromore and Trillick Practice.Consideration is also being given to the potential for ‘clustering’ of GP Practices in the South West Federation, and in particular the Dromore/Trillick area, as a way of providing the necessary support to ensure the continuation of GMS services in the area.   At a regional level, my Department has continued to invest in our GP workforce and has increased the number of GP trainees by over 70% from 2015 levels.  Work is underway on a review of GP trainee places to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst this work is ongoing, I recently announced that my Department would be increasing of the number of GP training places for 2022/23 by 10, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year.  This will help stabilise our GP workforce and ensure it is sustainable for the future.  Any future increases will be informed by completion of the review and subject to the availability of funding.I recognise that GPs’ experiences during clinical placements can have a significant influence on their future career decisions.  In that regard, the commencement of the Graduate Entry Programme at the Ulster University’s Medical School is a significant development in the provision of medical education in Northern Ireland.  The curriculum of this Graduate Entry Programme places a very significant emphasis on Primary Care placements, with a high concentration of clinical placements in rural settings in the west.  This will help to increase th

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the road safety improvement scheme for the Orchard Road and Bellspark Road junction.
answer_text: I can confirm that purchase of one of the key properties required to facilitate this realignment work has recently been agreed.  As soon as the contractual formalities have been concluded, works to improve the junction visibility will be programmed immediately in advance of the longer term proposal to provide a staggered junction, progress of which is subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on all flood alleviation studies in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department has carried out a number of flood studies to determine if a viable flood alleviation scheme could be taken forward to improve the level of flood protection in Omagh. Unfortunately none of the proposals to date were found to be feasible or economically viable. However, my Department has commissioned a review of the most recent flood study for Omagh Town, which was completed in 2016. The new study, which will take account of climate change commenced in January 2022 and is expected to take 12-18 months to complete.My Department has also completed studies to assess the flood risk to properties in both Newtownstewart and Fintona.  The studies for both locations have concluded that none of the potential options to alleviate flooding are economically viable. However, my Department continues to work with multi-agency partners, including Local Government, to provide support to the local community in both Fintona and Newtownstewart through the work of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG). The RCRG works with communities at risk of flooding to help them prepare for and respond to weather related emergencies and have provided access to community sandbag containers in both these areas. You will be aware my Department is also carrying out feasibility studies at Elm Wood Green, Castlederg, at Sion Mills Village and at the ‘Dry Arch’ located in the Lower Market Street, area of Omagh. The Elm Wood Green and Sion Mills Village studies, which are at an advanced stage, are expected be finalised in the coming weeks. A second draft of the Dry Arch Feasibility Study, which has been received by my Department, has not yet been finalised as officials wish to thoroughly explore all options. It is anticipated at this stage that the study will be complete by 31 July 2022. A feasibility study for the Strabane Significant Flood Risk Area is also programmed for completion this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he plans to progress the A5 Western Transport Corridor Scheme.
answer_text: I am committed to doing all that I can during my time as Minister for Infrastructure to advance this vitally important flagship project.The consultation period on the recently published Environmental Statement Addendum and associated documentation concluded on 6 May 2022, with the Department receiving over 200 written representations. Discussions are ongoing with the PAC and it is hoped that the public inquiry can be reconvened in the autumn of this year. Receipt of the PAC’s final report from the inquiry process should then allow a new formal decision on progression of the scheme during 2023. Subject to this decision and successful completion of the necessary statutory processes and environmental assessments, the construction of phase 1A (New Buildings to North of Strabane) could then commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to resurface the road at Jefferson Court, Strabane.
answer_text: A scheme to resurface the carriageway at Jefferson Court is included within the Department’s two year maintenance programme.  It is currently scheduled to be progressed during the 2023/24 financial year, subject to a term resurfacing contract being in place, and sufficient funding being made available.  In the intervening period my officials will continue to carry out periodic inspections of this development and action for repair any defects which meet the intervention threshold defined in our current maintenance policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Strabane Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: The Strabane Public Realm scheme is progressing through the approval processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to reinstate Area of Natural Constraint payments for farmers.
answer_text: I have no plans to reinstate Area of Natural Constraint payments for farmers. In March, I announced 54 decisions which define future agricultural policy and will allow schemes to be developed that will provide opportunities for all Northern Ireland’s farmers.  Schemes and support are needed to help farmers develop their businesses, no matter where they farm, to become more productive, profitable and resilient, and to maximise the sustainable returns from the assets at their disposal.My recently agreed Climate Change (No 2) Bill will present challenges to all farmers and my future agricultural policy decisions will incentivise farmers to adjust their behaviours and actions to meet these challenges. My policy decisions will provide appropriately designed policy interventions and innovation, without compromising the economic viability of the sector, including in the less favoured areas.  An Area of Natural Constraint Scheme is an old CAP measure that has very little to offer in terms of moving the industry forward.  When last implemented in Northern Ireland, it had to operate under a Ministerial Direction as it could not be shown to represent value for money.  Our farmers deserve better than this.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build youth centre in Strabane town.
answer_text: Following the Department’s approval of the Business Case for the proposed Youth Centre in Strabane on 24 March 2022, the Education Authority have commenced exploring purchasing the preferred site, and appointment of a Design Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department provides public conveniences at the outdoor parks it manages.
answer_text: I can confirm that the Northern Ireland Environment Agency manages 7 country parks on behalf of my Department. With the exception of Redburn Country Park, public conveniences are available at all of these country parks.  While there are no specific facilities provided at Redburn Country Park, use of toilet facilities at the Men’s Shed site, leased by my Department to that organisation, are available for public use.Forest Service manages five forests where public conveniences are available, including Tollymore Forest Park, Glenariff Forest Park, Drum Manor Forest, Portglenone forest and Parkanaur forest.Further to the above, there are public conveniences provided at the following DAERA forests: Hillsborough Forest, Ballysallagh Forest, Florencecourt forest, Gosford Forest Park, Slieve Gullion forest, Castlewellan Forest Park and Gortin Glen Forest Park.  Recreation facilities are managed under partnership arrangements in these forests.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department will progress with the Free School Meals Payment Scheme over the summer.
answer_text: School holidays can create significant financial pressure for parents on low incomes and the ongoing cost of living crisis is causing further strain on household budgets across Northern Ireland. I have therefore decided to allocate £12.6 million funding to provide the families of children entitled to free school meals with additional financial support over the July/August summer holidays 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she plans to review the sustainability criteria for rural schools facing closure.
answer_text: I have no plans at present to commission a full review of the Schools for the Future: A Policy for Sustainable Schools (SSP).  I will revisit this matter when the Independent Review of Education is completed and I have had the opportunity to consider any related implications for the SSP which may emerge from that review. However, I have asked my officials to consider and develop options for a programme to support sustainable education provision in rural areas.  This work is at an early stage.  It will draw on the input from a wide range of stakeholders who attended a Strategic Insights Lab, facilitated by the NI Innovation Lab Team in April 2022.
